topics.title: Approaches to Software Design - Functional Oriented Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT414 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Approaches to Software Design - Functional Oriented Design # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Functional Oriented Design (FOD)**, also called **Procedural / Structured Design**, is a software design methodology in which the system is decomposed into a hierarchy of **functions (procedures/modules)** that operate on shared data structures. The design focuses on *what operations are performed* rather than *what entities the data represents*. Each function is a self-contained, named, parameterized block that accepts input, performs transformation, and returns output. According to KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, FOD belongs to the **classical / traditional** school of software design where: - The **primary unit of decomposition** is the *function*. - **Data** is treated as a passive element that flows *between* functions. - The program is viewed as a **sequence of operations** on data. - Design proceeds using a **Top-Down Refinement** approach (also called *Stepwise Refinement* — popularized by Niklaus Wirth). ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Restaurant Kitchen" > [!IMPORTANT] > **Analogy: A Traditional Restaurant Kitchen** > Imagine a busy restaurant where: > - The **Head Chef** receives an order (input). > - He breaks it into sub-tasks: *chop vegetables, marinate, sauté, plate, serve*. > - Each sub-task is a **function** performed by a sub-chef using shared ingredients from the **pantry (shared data)**. > - The pantry (data) is global — any chef can grab onions or salt whenever needed. > - The dish emerges as a **sequence of operations on the shared pantry**. **Mapping to FOD:** | Kitchen Element | FOD Equivalent | |---|---| | Head Chef | Main Program | | Sub-Chefs | Functions / Procedures | | Recipe Steps | Algorithms inside functions | | Pantry / Ingredients | Global Data Structures | | Order | Input parameters | | Served Dish | Output / Return value | The whole system is "**function-centric**" — change the recipe (function) and the dish (output) changes, even if the pantry (data) stays the same. ## 1.3 Three Foundational Principles of FOD > [!NOTE] > 1. **Top-Down Decomposition** — A complex problem is recursively broken into smaller sub-problems until each sub-problem is solvable by a single function. > 2. **Modularity** — Each function is a logical black box with one well-defined responsibility (*Single Responsibility per function*). > 3. **Structured Programming Constructs** — Only `sequence`, `selection` (`if/else`), and `iteration` (`while/for`) are used. The infamous **GOTO** is banned (Dijkstra, *1968 — "Go To Statement Considered Harmful"*). ## 1.4 Why Study FOD in an OOP Course? Even though Object Oriented Programming is the focus of this paper, the KTU 2024 syllabus requires FOD as the **baseline** to *contrast* with OOD. Understanding FOD limitations directly motivates the **need for OOP**. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Insight:** KTU frequently asks "*Differentiate Functional Oriented Design and Object Oriented Design*" or "*List limitations of FOD*" — so mastering this topic is essential for Module 1 marks. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 The FOD Design Pipeline The Functional Oriented Design lifecycle follows a strict, sequential pipeline: - **Step 1 — Problem Statement:** Define the *what* (e.g., "Compute monthly payroll for 500 employees"). - **Step 2 — Top-Down Decomposition:** Break the problem into sub-problems hierarchically. - **Step 3 — Function Identification:** Identify discrete operations (e.g., `read_inputs()`, `compute_tax()`, `print_slip()`). - **Step 4 — Define Data Structures:** Decide what data exists and how it is stored (typically a single **global record**). - **Step 5 — Specify Interfaces:** Define the **input parameters** and **return values** of each function. - **Step 6 — Refine Algorithms:** Write the *pseudo-code / algorithm* for each function using only structured constructs. - **Step 7 — Integration & Testing:** Combine functions in the calling order and test. ## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step - **Why Top-Down?** — Humans understand complexity by *hiding detail*. Each layer only needs to know the layer immediately below it — this is the principle of **abstraction by layering**. - **Why one global data structure?** — In FOD, data and functions are *separated*. Functions freely read/write the global data, leading to **tight coupling** between functions and data. - **Why structured constructs only?** — Bohm and Jacopini (1966) proved any computable function can be expressed using only `sequence`, `selection`, and `iteration`. This makes programs **provably correct** and easy to test. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Exam Revision Table — Functional Oriented Design at a Glance** | **Aspect** | **Description / Value** | |---|---| | Primary Design Unit | Function / Procedure / Module | | Data Treatment | Passive, shared (usually global) | | Decomposition Strategy | Top-Down (Stepwise Refinement) | | Approach Name | Algorithmic / Procedural / Modular | | Design Focus | *How* the operations are performed | | Control Flow | Explicit, function-calling hierarchy | | Programming Examples | C, Pascal, FORTRAN, COBOL | | Pioneers | Edsger Dijkstra, Niklaus Wirth, C.A.R. Hoare | | Key Strengths | Simple to learn, efficient execution, mature compilers | | Key Weaknesses | Poor reuse, data security, scalability, real-world modeling | | Data-Function Relationship | **Loosely coupled** in code but **tightly coupled** at runtime | | Maintainability | Low — changing data forces many function changes | | Reusability | Low — functions tied to specific global data shape | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Although FOD is "old school," it is **still the engine under the hood** in critical domains: - **Embedded Systems (firmware in ECUs, microcontrollers):** resource-constrained → C-based FOD is the industry standard. - **High-Performance Computing (HPC) and OS kernels (Linux kernel modules):** functional decomposition gives cache-friendly, predictable performance. - **Numerical & Scientific Computing (MATLAB scripts, FORTRAN):** mathematical functions map naturally to procedural functions. - **Compiler Design Passes:** Lexical analysis, parsing, code generation are naturally top-down procedural stages. > [!TIP] > **Real-world lesson:** Modern languages like *Python* and *Java* are *object-oriented* but their standard libraries (e.g., `math.sqrt()`, `Arrays.sort()`) are often **pure functions** — the FOD spirit lives on inside OOP! # 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough — Worked Example ## 3.1 Case Study: Library Management System (FOD Style) > **Problem:** Design a Library Management System using Functional Oriented Design that supports: adding a book, searching a book, issuing a book, and returning a book. ### Step 1 — Top-Down Decomposition We begin at the highest level — a single, abstract function — and keep splitting until each piece is *implementable*. Level 0 (Root): $$ \text{Library\_System()} $$ Level 1 — Split into 4 major operations: $$ \text{Library\_System()} \Rightarrow \begin{cases} \text{add\_book()} \\ \text{search\_book()} \\ \text{issue\_book()} \\ \text{return\_book()} \end{cases} $$ Level 2 — Each operation may need helpers (e.g., `search_book` needs `linear_search` or `binary_search`). $$ \text{search\_book()} \Rightarrow \begin{cases} \text{get\_query\_title()} \\ \text{search\_by\_title()} \\ \text{display\_result()} \end{cases} $$ Level 3 — Eventually each leaf is small enough to code in 5–10 lines of structured C/Pascal. ### Step 2 — Define the Global Data Structure In FOD, **all data is global** and shared. For our library: $$ \text{struct Book} \left\{ \begin{array}{l} \texttt{int id;} \\ \texttt{char title[50];} \\ \texttt{char author[30];} \\ \texttt{int is\_issued;} \\ \texttt{char issued\_to[30];} \\ \right\} $$ $$ \text{struct Book library[MAX\_BOOKS];} \quad \text{(global array)} $$ $$ \text{int total\_books = 0;} \quad \text{(global counter)} $$ ### Step 3 — Function Signatures (Interfaces) Every function takes inputs and returns outputs — they all manipulate the **same global `library` array**. $$ \text{void add\_book(int id, char title[ ], char author[ ])} $$ $$ \text{int search\_book(char query[ ])} \quad \text{(returns index or -1)} $$ $$ \text{int issue\_book(int id, char student[ ])} $$ $$ \text{int return\_book(int id)} $$ ### Step 4 — Pseudo-code for `search_book` $$ \begin{aligned} &\textbf{int search\_book(char query[ ])} \\ &\quad \texttt{int i = 0;} \\ &\quad \texttt{while (i < total\_books)} \{ \\ &\quad\quad \texttt{if (strcmp(library[i].title, query) == 0)} \{ \\ &\quad\quad\quad \texttt{return i;} \quad \text{// index of match found} \\ &\quad\quad \texttt{\}} \\ &\quad\quad \texttt{i = i + 1;} \\ &\quad \texttt{\}} \\ &\quad \texttt{return -1;} \quad \text{// not found} \\ &\texttt{\}} \end{aligned} $$ **Explanation of logic flow:** - Line 1: Function accepts a search string `query[]`. - Line 2: Initialize loop counter to first book. - Line 3: Iterate through every stored book. - Line 4: Use `strcmp` to compare titles (case-sensitive in C). - Line 5: If match → return array index (success exit). - Line 6: Increment counter. - Line 7: End of loop → return `-1` (failure exit). ### Step 5 — Coupling & Cohesion Analysis > [!NOTE] > **KTU High-Yield Concept:** Examiners love asking the difference between *coupling* and *cohesion* in the context of FOD. - **Cohesion** of each function = **HIGH** (each does one job, e.g., `search` only searches). - **Coupling** between functions and `library[]` data = **HIGH and TIGHT**. - Change the data structure (e.g., replace array with linked list) → **all four functions** must be rewritten. - This is the **Achilles' heel of FOD**. ### Step 6 — Complexity & Verification For `search_book` with $n$ books: $$ T(n) = O(n) \quad \text{(linear search — single loop)} $$ Space complexity (auxiliary): $$ S_{\text{aux}}(n) = O(1) \quad \text{(only loop counter i)} $$ If we later upgrade to **binary search** (sorted by title), using divide-and-conquer: $$ T_{\text{BS}}(n) = O(\log_2 n) $$ This still does **not change the FOD nature** of the design — only the *internal algorithm* of one function changes. ## 3.2 Summary of Analytical Insight The walkthrough shows the FOD pipeline in action: - Problem decomposed **top-down**. - Data designed **once, globally**. - Functions designed as **black-box processors** of that data. - Coupling between data and functions is **implicit and global** — making future evolution expensive. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Figure 1 — Top-Down Functional Decomposition (Library System) The following **Mermaid block** renders the recursive tree of functions in our library example, showing how a complex system is broken down via stepwise refinement. ```mermaid graph TD A["Library_System"]:::root --> B["add_book"]:::leaf A --> C["search_book"]:::branch A --> D["issue_book"]:::leaf A --> E["return_book"]:::leaf C --> F["get_query_title"]:::leaf C --> G["search_by_title"]:::branch C --> H["display_result"]:::leaf G --> I["linear_search"]:::leaf G --> J["binary_search"]:::leaf classDef root fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff classDef branch fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef leaf fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000 ``` **How to read this figure:** - The **root node** `Library_System` is at the top. - The **branch nodes** (medium blue) represent intermediate decomposition levels. - The **leaf nodes** (light blue) are terminal functions — small enough to be coded directly. - Arrows indicate the *refinement relationship* (parent is refined into children). ## 4.2 Figure 2 — FOD Architecture: Functions Operating on Shared Global Data The next diagram visualizes the **core FOD weakness** — all functions share one giant global data block. ```mermaid graph LR subgraph Global["SHARED GLOBAL DATA"] DB["library[]
Book Records
total_books"]:::data end F1["add_book"]:::fn --> DB F2["search_book"]:::fn --> DB F3["issue_book"]:::fn --> DB F4["return_book"]:::fn --> DB F5["print_report"]:::fn --> DB DB --> F1 DB --> F2 DB --> F3 DB --> F4 DB --> F5 classDef data fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef fn fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff ``` **Reading guide:** - The **red block** in the centre is the *single, shared* data structure. - **Green nodes** are the five functions. - Arrows go *both ways* (functions read **and** write the global data) — visual proof of **tight coupling**. - In OOD (next module), each function would be *bundled with its own data* inside a class — eliminating the arrows. ## 4.3 Figure 3 — Procedural Call Stack / Control Flow This flowchart shows how the **main function** calls sub-functions sequentially, mimicking the C-runtime call stack. ```mermaid graph TD M["main"]:::root --> I1["init_library"]:::step I1 --> L1["loop: read choice"]:::step L1 -->|choice = 1| A1["add_book"]:::step L1 -->|choice = 2| A2["search_book"]:::step L1 -->|choice = 3| A3["issue_book"]:::step L1 -->|choice = 4| A4["return_book"]:::step L1 -->|choice = 0| X["exit"]:::exit A1 --> L1 A2 --> L1 A3 --> L1 A4 --> L1 classDef root fill:#7030a0,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef step fill:#ffe699,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000 classDef exit fill:#a9d08e,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 ``` **Interpretation:** - `main` initializes and enters a menu loop. - Each user choice dispatches to one function. - After the function returns, control **loops back** to the menu — the classic FOD driver loop. ## 4.4 Diagram Compilation Notes > [!TIP] > All Mermaid nodes use **purely alphanumeric IDs** (`A`, `F1`, `DB`) prefixed with letters. Labels are inside double quotes and contain **only uppercase, plain text + `
` line breaks** — no bold (`**`), no italics (`*`), no embedded tables — ensuring 100% Mermaid renderer compatibility. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]** *Define Functional Oriented Design. List any two of its characteristics.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Definition (2 marks):** Functional Oriented Design is a traditional software design approach in which a system is decomposed into a hierarchy of functions (procedures/modules) that operate on shared data. The design emphasizes *what operations are to be performed* and follows a top-down refinement strategy. > **Any two characteristics (1 mark):** > 1. Functions are the basic building blocks; each performs a single well-defined task. > 2. Data is passive, global, and shared among functions. > 3. Design proceeds through Top-Down Stepwise Refinement. > 4. Uses only structured programming constructs (sequence, selection, iteration). --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** *Differentiate between Functional Oriented Design and Object Oriented Design in terms of basic unit of decomposition and data handling. (3 marks)* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | **Criterion** | **FOD** | **OOD** | |---|---|---| | Basic unit of decomposition | Function / Procedure | Class / Object | | Data handling | Data is global and shared; functions operate on it | Data is encapsulated within objects; functions (methods) are bound to the data | | Binding between data and operations | Loose at design time, tight at runtime | Tight at design time (encapsulation) | *(1 mark for FOD points, 1 mark for OOD points, 1 mark for contrast/tabulation.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice) ### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** **(a) [7 marks]** *Explain the Top-Down Design approach used in Functional Oriented Design. Illustrate with a suitable example.* **Model Answer:** **Definition (2 marks):** Top-Down Design (also called *Stepwise Refinement*) starts with the most abstract description of the program's purpose and progressively breaks the problem into smaller sub-problems until each sub-problem is implementable as a single function or module. The technique was formalized by **Niklaus Wirth (1971)**. **Process (3 marks):** 1. Begin with a single, high-level function representing the entire system. 2. If the function is too complex, split it into 2–4 sub-functions. 3. Continue recursively for each sub-function. 4. Stop when each remaining function performs a single, simple task. **Illustrative Example — "Student Result Processing" (2 marks):** $$ \text{Process\_Results()} \Rightarrow \begin{cases} \text{Read\_Marks()} \\ \text{Calculate\_Total\_Average()} \\ \text{Assign\_Grade()} \\ \text{Print\_Report\_Card()} \end{cases} $$ Each of these can be further refined. For instance, `Assign_Grade()`: $$ \text{Assign\_Grade()} \Rightarrow \begin{cases} \text{if avg} \geq 90: \text{return 'A'} \\ \text{elif avg} \geq 75: \text{return 'B'} \\ \text{elif avg} \geq 60: \text{return 'C'} \\ \text{else: return 'D'} \end{cases} $$ This recursive refinement continues until every leaf function is trivial to code. --- **(b) [7 marks]** *Discuss the major limitations of Functional Oriented Design. How do these limitations motivate the need for Object Oriented Design?* **Model Answer:** **Limitations of FOD (5 marks):** 1. **Poor Real-World Modeling:** Real systems have *entities* (Customer, Account, Invoice) interacting with each other. FOD only models *actions*, not *entities*, leading to unnatural designs. 2. **Data Security Issues:** Since data is global, any function can modify it. There is no way to restrict access → **no encapsulation**. 3. **Low Reusability:** Functions are tied to specific data structures. Reusing `search_book()` in another program requires copying the same `library[]` data structure. 4. **Difficult Maintenance:** A small change in the data structure (e.g., adding a `publisher` field) requires modifying **every** function that touches the data. 5. **No Polymorphism or Inheritance:** FOD cannot naturally express "is-a" or "has-a" relationships. Code duplication is common. 6. **Scalability Bottleneck:** Large FOD programs become "spaghetti" — inter-function dependencies grow exponentially. **Motivation for OOD (2 marks):** - OOD binds *data and operations together* inside **classes** (encapsulation). - It allows **inheritance** (reuse) and **polymorphism** (flexibility). - Real-world entities map directly to **objects**, making design intuitive. - Hence, OOD addresses *every* major FOD limitation — this is the historical reason for its adoption. --- ### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **(a) [7 marks]** *With a neat diagram, explain the modular structure of a program designed using Functional Oriented Design. Use an example of a Banking System.* **Model Answer:** **Modular Structure Concept (2 marks):** A module in FOD is a named, self-contained function that performs a single logical operation. Modules communicate via *parameters* and *return values*, hiding their internal implementation details (a primitive form of information hiding). **Banking System Modules (3 marks):** The system can be decomposed as: - `main()` — driver / menu loop. - `create_account()` — registers a new customer. - `deposit()` — adds money. - `withdraw()` — removes money (with balance check). - `check_balance()` — displays current balance. - `close_account()` — removes a customer record. - `print_statement()` — generates transaction history. **Diagram (2 marks):** ```mermaid graph TD MAIN["main()"]:::r --> M1["create_account()"]:::l MAIN --> M2["deposit()"]:::l MAIN --> M3["withdraw()"]:::l MAIN --> M4["check_balance()"]:::l MAIN --> M5["close_account()"]:::l MAIN --> M6["print_statement()"]:::l M2 --> H1["validate_amount()"]:::s M3 --> H1 M3 --> H2["check_min_balance()"]:::s classDef r fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef l fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef s fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` **Reading the diagram:** `main` is the root; the 6 blue boxes are *Level-1* modules; the light-blue boxes are *helper sub-modules* called by `deposit` and `withdraw`. All these functions operate on a single **global `accounts[]` data structure**. --- **(b) [7 marks]** *Compare Functional Oriented Design and Object Oriented Design across the following parameters: (i) Basic building block, (ii) Data access, (iii) Reusability, (iv) Security, (v) Real-world mapping, (vi) Example languages, (vii) Maintenance.* **Model Answer:** | **Parameter** | **FOD** | **OOD** | |---|---|---| | (i) Basic building block | Function / Procedure | Class / Object | | (ii) Data access | Global, freely accessible | Restricted via access modifiers (public / private / protected) | | (iii) Reusability | Low — functions tied to specific data | High — inheritance and composition | | (iv) Security | Low — no encapsulation | High — encapsulation hides internals | | (v) Real-world mapping | Models actions only | Models both entities and actions | | (vi) Example languages | C, Pascal, FORTRAN, COBOL | Java, C++, Python, C\# | | (vii) Maintenance | Difficult for large systems | Easier due to localized changes | *(1 mark for each well-explained parameter; tabulation earns an extra bonus mark.)* --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Marks-Deduction Pitfalls in FOD Questions** > 1. **Writing code in answer sheets** for 7-mark theory questions → KTU deducts up to 2 marks if pure prose + diagram is expected. Use **pseudo-code / algorithm** only. > 2. **Confusing Top-Down with Bottom-Up** — Top-Down starts from the *problem* and moves to *code*; Bottom-Up starts from *existing modules* and builds up. > 3. **Listing "Object" as the FOD building block** — this is a *fatal* conceptual error. FOD = function; OOD = object. Examiners mark zero if confused. > 4. **Omitting a diagram** in Top-Down questions loses at least 1–2 marks. Always include a **hierarchical tree** of functions. > 5. **Using GOTO in pseudo-code** — KTU explicitly tests structured programming knowledge. Stick to `sequence / selection / iteration`. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Functional Oriented Design** - FOD = **Function-centric** design; data is **passive and global**. - Decomposition strategy = **Top-Down Stepwise Refinement** (Wirth). - Programming paradigm = **Procedural / Modular / Structured**. - Only three control structures are allowed: **Sequence, Selection, Iteration** (Bohm–Jacopini theorem). - Pioneers: **Dijkstra** (GOTO ban), **Wirth** (stepwise refinement), **Hoare** (structured design). - Data and functions are **loosely coupled at code level** but **tightly coupled at runtime**. - Real-world modeling is **weak** — FOD models *actions*, not *entities*. - **No encapsulation, no inheritance, no polymorphism** in pure FOD. - **Strengths:** simple, efficient, mature compilers, ideal for embedded/scientific code. - **Weaknesses:** poor reuse, low security, hard maintenance, weak scalability. - **Languages:** C, Pascal, FORTRAN, COBOL. (Languages that are **not** OOP.) - **Contrast for exams:** FOD vs OOD — the *single most-asked* Module 1 question. - **Always draw a tree diagram** when explaining top-down decomposition. - **Always use `sequence / selection / iteration`** in pseudo-code (no GOTO). - FOD is the **historical baseline** whose limitations directly motivated the invention of **Object Oriented Design**.

topics.title: Object Oriented Design
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Object Oriented Design — Core Foundations

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT414 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Object Oriented Design

---

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Object Oriented Design (OOD)** is the process of planning a system of interacting objects for the purpose of solving a software problem. It is a phase in the software development lifecycle that follows Object Oriented Analysis (OOA) and precedes implementation, wherein the analysis model is transformed into a detailed design model defining system objects, their responsibilities, collaborations, and relationships.

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEECT414)**, Object Oriented Design is the second stage of the **Object Oriented Development (OOD) life cycle**, where the conceptual model built during analysis is refined into an implementable, low-level blueprint using the five core OOP principles:
**Abstraction, Encapsulation, Modularity, Hierarchy, and Persistence.**

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview (Real-World Analogy)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Building a House**

Imagine you are an architect asked to build a house:

1. **Object Oriented Analysis (OOA)** = The client tells you, *"I need a house with 3 bedrooms, 2 bathrooms, a kitchen, and a garden."* — This is gathering **requirements** and identifying **what** the system should do (classes like `Bedroom`, `Kitchen`, `Garden`).
2. **Object Oriented Design (OOD)** = You sit down and decide **how** to build it — where to place doors, how rooms connect, what materials to use, how plumbing runs between rooms. — This is the **design phase** where you decide responsibilities, collaborations, and interfaces.
3. **Implementation** = The actual construction using bricks and cement.

**Key Insight:** OOA answers *"WHAT"* — OOD answers *"HOW"*. The design phase decides which class performs which responsibility, how objects collaborate via messages, and how to structure inheritance and polymorphism.

---

## 1.3 Why Object Oriented Design?

| Aspect | Procedural Approach | Object Oriented Design |
|---|---|---|
| **Focus** | Functions and procedures | Data + Behavior bundled together |
| **Reusability** | Difficult (copy-paste code) | High (inheritance, composition) |
| **Maintainability** | Hard to modify | Easy to extend |
| **Mapping to real world** | Poor | Excellent (objects = real entities) |
| **Modularity** | File-based | Class-based |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** A common 3-mark question asks — *"List the advantages of OOD over procedural design."* The table above gives you a ready-made answer.

---

## 1.4 Object Oriented Design in the OOAD Lifecycle

The complete Object Oriented Analysis and Design (OOAD) process under KTU syllabus follows this sequence:

1. **Object Oriented Analysis (OOA)** — Identify objects, classes, and their relationships from problem statements.
2. **Object Oriented Design (OOD)** — Refine the analysis model, assign responsibilities, define collaborations, optimize class structures, and prepare for implementation.
3. **Implementation** — Translate the design into code (Java, C++, Python, etc.).
4. **Testing** — Verify behavior matches design intent.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Note:** OOA and OOD are NOT the same. OOA = "What objects exist?" OOD = "How will these objects collaborate to deliver functionality?"

---

## 1.5 Core Goals of OOD

The five primary goals that any good OOD must achieve (as per KTU reference framework):

1. **Correctness** — Design accurately reflects analysis requirements.
2. **Robustness** — Design handles invalid inputs and edge cases gracefully.
3. **Flexibility / Extensibility** — New features can be added with minimal changes to existing code (follows **OCP** — Open/Closed Principle).
4. **Reusability** — Classes and components are designed to be reused across projects.
5. **Maintainability** — Code is easy to understand, modify, and debug.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis — OOD Methodology & Principles

---

## 2.1 The OOD Process — Step by Step

Object Oriented Design is not a single activity; it is a structured, iterative process. The KTU syllabus recommends the following workflow:

### Step 1: Refine the Analysis Model
Take the class diagram, use cases, and object diagrams from the OOA phase. Eliminate ambiguities, resolve vague responsibilities, and add implementation-level details.

### Step 2: Design the System Architecture
Decide on subsystem organization:
- **Layered architecture** (Presentation → Business → Data)
- **MVC (Model-View-Controller)** pattern
- **Client-Server** architecture

### Step 3: Identify Classes
Apply three techniques to discover classes:
- **Noun Phrase Identification** — Extract nouns from problem statement.
- **Common Class Patterns** — Look for typical roles: `Controller`, `Boundary`, `Entity`, `Creator`, `Information Expert`.
- **Use-Case Driven Identification** — For each use case, find the participating classes.

### Step 4: Identify Object Responsibilities (GRASP Patterns)
Assign responsibilities using **GRASP** (General Responsibility Assignment Software Patterns). The 9 GRASP patterns are:

| # | GRASP Pattern | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Information Expert** | Assign responsibility to the class that has the information needed to fulfill it. |
| 2 | **Creator** | Assign class B the responsibility to create A if B aggregates, contains, or records A. |
| 3 | **Controller** | Assign responsibility to a class representing the overall system or use case handler. |
| 4 | **Low Coupling** | Reduce dependencies between classes. |
| 5 | **High Cohesion** | Keep related responsibilities together in one class. |
| 6 | **Polymorphism** | Use polymorphic operations to handle type-based variations. |
| 7 | **Pure Fabrication** | Create a class that doesn't represent a domain concept (e.g., helper utilities). |
| 8 | **Indirection** | Introduce an intermediate object to decouple two classes. |
| 9 | **Protected Variations** | Protect elements from variations in other elements. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Frequent Question:** *"Explain GRASP patterns"* or *"Explain any 5 GRASP patterns"* is a frequently asked 7-mark question.

### Step 5: Identify Object Interactions
- Define **method signatures** (interfaces).
- Specify **message passing** sequences (sequence diagrams).
- Establish **cardinality** in associations.

### Step 6: Specify Class Interfaces and Implementations
- Design **public interfaces** (what is exposed).
- Hide **implementation details** (Encapsulation).
- Define **constructors, accessors, mutators**.

### Step 7: Apply OOP Concepts
Map requirements to:
- **Inheritance** (is-a relationships)
- **Composition / Aggregation** (has-a relationships)
- **Polymorphism** (dynamic behavior)
- **Abstract Classes & Interfaces**

---

## 2.2 Coupling and Cohesion — The Twin Pillars of OOD

> [!NOTE]
> These are the **two most important quality metrics** in OOD. Every KTU exam paper has at least one question on these.

### 2.2.1 Coupling
**Coupling** is the measure of **interdependence between modules/classes**. **Low coupling is desirable.**

| Coupling Type | Description | Strength |
|---|---|---|
| **Content Coupling** | One module directly modifies another's internal data | Worst (highest) |
| **Common Coupling** | Modules share global data | High |
| **External Coupling** | Modules share an external data format (e.g., file format) | High |
| **Control Coupling** | One module passes control flags to another | Medium |
| **Stamp Coupling** | Module passes a data structure; receiver uses only part of it | Medium |
| **Data Coupling** | Modules share data via parameters (primitive types) | Low (best) |
| **Message Coupling** | Communication only via message passing (objects) | Lowest (best in OOD) |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Exam:** "C²C-EC²D-M" → Content, Common, Control, Stamp, Data, Message (from worst to best).

### 2.2.2 Cohesion
**Cohesion** is the measure of how strongly the responsibilities of a single module are related. **High cohesion is desirable.**

| Cohesion Type | Description | Strength |
|---|---|---|
| **Coincidental** | Arbitrary grouping of unrelated functions | Worst |
| **Logical** | Logically related tasks selected by a flag | Poor |
| **Temporal** | Tasks executed at the same time (e.g., `initialize()`) | Medium |
| **Procedural** | Tasks in a fixed order | Medium |
| **Communicational** | Tasks use the same input/output data | High |
| **Sequential** | Output of one task is input to the next | High |
| **Functional** | All tasks contribute to a single well-defined function | Best |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Exam:** "CLTPSC-F" → Coincidental, Logical, Temporal, Procedural, Sequential, Communicational, Functional (worst to best).

---

## 2.3 The Five SOLID Principles (OO Design Principles)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 1) explicitly covers these design principles.

| # | Principle | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **S** | **Single Responsibility Principle (SRP)** | A class should have only one reason to change (one responsibility). |
| **O** | **Open/Closed Principle (OCP)** | Classes should be open for extension but closed for modification. |
| **L** | **Liskov Substitution Principle (LSP)** | Subtypes must be substitutable for their base types. |
| **I** | **Interface Segregation Principle (ISP)** | Prefer many small, specific interfaces over one large, general interface. |
| **D** | **Dependency Inversion Principle (DIP)** | Depend on abstractions, not on concrete implementations. |

### 2.3.1 Single Responsibility Principle (SRP) — Deep Dive
A class should have **exactly one responsibility** — one axis of change. If a class has multiple responsibilities, changes in one responsibility may break the other.

**Example:** A `Employee` class that calculates payroll AND saves to database violates SRP. Split into `Employee` (data) + `PayrollCalculator` (computation) + `EmployeeRepository` (persistence).

### 2.3.2 Open/Closed Principle (OCP) — Deep Dive
Software entities should be **open for extension** (you can add new functionality) but **closed for modification** (existing code shouldn't be changed).

**Achieved via:** Abstract classes and polymorphism.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Quick Reference

Since OOD is primarily a design activity, the "formulas" here are **design quality metrics**:

| Metric | Formula / Definition | Ideal Value |
|---|---|---|
| **Coupling (C)** | $C = \text{Number of inter-module connections}$ | Minimize (Lower is better) |
| **Cohesion (H)** | $H = \frac{\text{Strength of internal relationships}}{\text{Module's responsibilities}}$ | Maximize (Higher is better) |
| **Lack of Cohesion (LCOM)** | $LCOM = P - Q$ where P = pairs of methods not sharing instance variables, Q = pairs that do share | LCOM $\leq 1$ (lower is better) |
| **Response Set (RS)** | $RS = \vert \{\text{Methods called by class } C\} \vert$ | Lower is better |
| **CBO (Coupling Between Objects)** | $CBO = \text{Number of classes coupled to class C}$ | Lower is better |
| **RFC (Response For Class)** | $RFC = \vert RS \vert + \vert C \vert$ where C = number of methods in class | Lower is better |
| **DIT (Depth of Inheritance Tree)** | $DIT = \text{Depth from class to root}$ | Moderate (1–3 ideal) |
| **WMC (Weighted Methods per Class)** | $WMC = \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i$ where $c_i$ = complexity of method i | Lower is better |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on LaTeX:** The `|x|` notation has been replaced with `\vert x \vert` in the table to maintain markdown integrity.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Applications of OOD Principles

OOD is the foundation of virtually every modern software system:
- **Enterprise software** (banking, ERP) — uses layered architecture, SOLID principles.
- **Web frameworks** (Spring, Django) — heavy use of Dependency Injection (DIP).
- **Game development** — Entity-Component-System uses composition over inheritance.
- **Mobile apps** (Android) — Activity/Fragment/ViewModel design follows MVC.
- **Microservices** — Each service is a high-cohesion, low-coupling unit.

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough: OOD on a Problem Statement

> [!NOTE]
> Since OOD is design-oriented, this section walks through the **complete OOD process** on a typical KTU-style problem statement, demonstrating how to derive the design from requirements.

---

## 3.1 Problem Statement (KTU-Style)

> *"Design an object-oriented system for an ATM (Automated Teller Machine) that allows users to authenticate using a PIN, check balance, withdraw cash, and print receipts."*

---

## 3.2 Step 1: Identify the Use Cases

From the problem statement, extract use cases:

$$
UC = \{ \text{Authenticate User}, \text{Check Balance}, \text{Withdraw Cash}, \text{Print Receipt} \}
$$

> **Logic:** Each verb-phrase in the problem statement (authenticate, check, withdraw, print) represents a use case — a unit of functionality the system must provide.

---

## 3.3 Step 2: Identify Candidate Classes (Noun Phrase Analysis)

Extract nouns from the problem statement:

$$
\text{Nouns} = \{ \text{ATM}, \text{User}, \text{PIN}, \text{Balance}, \text{Cash}, \text{Receipt}, \text{Account}, \text{Bank}, \text{Card} \}
$$

**Filter the nouns** to keep only domain-relevant, non-redundant classes:

$$
\text{Classes} = \{ \text{ATM}, \text{User}, \text{Account}, \text{Card}, \text{Receipt}, \text{Bank} \}
$$

**Rejected nouns:**
- *PIN* → attribute of `Card` (not a class)
- *Balance* → attribute of `Account`
- *Cash* → abstract concept; handled as a `Transaction`

---

## 3.4 Step 3: Identify Attributes and Methods for Each Class

Using the **Information Expert (GRASP)** pattern, assign attributes and methods to the class that owns the data.

| Class | Attributes | Methods |
|---|---|---|
| **ATM** | `atmId`, `location`, `cashAvailable` | `authenticateUser()`, `processTransaction()` |
| **User** | `userId`, `name`, `phone` | `getUserDetails()` |
| **Account** | `accountNumber`, `balance`, `accountType` | `getBalance()`, `debit(amount)`, `credit(amount)` |
| **Card** | `cardNumber`, `pin`, `expiryDate` | `validatePin(pin)`, `isExpired()` |
| **Receipt** | `transactionId`, `timestamp`, `amount` | `print()` |
| **Bank** | `bankName`, `ifscCode` | `validateCard(cardNumber)` |
| **Transaction** | `transactionId`, `amount`, `type` | `execute()` |

> **Logic for Information Expert:**
> The `Account` class is the **Information Expert** for the `getBalance()` method because it holds the `balance` data. Likewise, `Card` is the expert for `validatePin()` because it stores the PIN.

---

## 3.5 Step 4: Define Relationships (Inheritance, Association, Composition)

Analyze relationships using the **is-a** and **has-a** tests:

$$
\text{Is-A Test} \rightarrow \text{Inheritance}
$$
$$
\text{Has-A Test} \rightarrow \text{Association / Aggregation / Composition}
$$

| Relationship | Type | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| `ATM` **has-a** `Bank` | Aggregation | ATM is associated with one bank but can exist independently |
| `ATM` **has-a** `Account` (current session) | Association | An ATM interacts with an Account during a session |
| `User` **has-a** `Account` | Composition | Account cannot meaningfully exist without a user |
| `User` **has-a** `Card` | Aggregation | User can have multiple cards; cards can be replaced |
| `Account` **has-many** `Transaction` | Composition | Transactions are part of an account's history |
| `Transaction` **is-a** generalization → `Withdrawal`, `Deposit` | Inheritance | Specialization via polymorphism |

---

## 3.6 Step 5: Apply SOLID Principles

### Single Responsibility Principle (SRP) Check

If we had put all methods in a single `ATM` class, it would violate SRP. Instead:
- `Card` handles PIN validation.
- `Account` handles balance operations.
- `ATM` handles the user interface flow.

### Open/Closed Principle (OCP) Check

Define `Transaction` as an **abstract class**:

```java
abstract class Transaction {
    protected double amount;
    public abstract void execute(Account account);
}
```

Now, adding a new transaction type (e.g., `FundTransfer`) doesn't require modifying `ATM`:

```java
class Withdrawal extends Transaction {
    @Override
    public void execute(Account account) {
        if (account.getBalance() >= amount)
            account.debit(amount);
    }
}
```

This is **OCP** — the system is open to extension (new `Transaction` subclasses) but closed for modification (`ATM` class doesn't change).

### Liskov Substitution Principle (LSP) Check

Any subclass of `Transaction` (e.g., `Withdrawal`, `Deposit`, `FundTransfer`) can be used wherever `Transaction` is expected — no surprising behavior, all obey the `execute(Account)` contract.

### Dependency Inversion Principle (DIP) Check

High-level `ATM` class depends on the **abstraction** `Transaction`, not on concrete `Withdrawal` or `Deposit`:

```java
class ATM {
    public void process(Transaction t, Account a) {  // depends on abstraction
        t.execute(a);
    }
}
```

---

## 3.7 Step 6: Assign Responsibilities Using GRASP

| Responsibility | GRASP Pattern | Assigned To |
|---|---|---|
| Knowing the balance of an account | **Information Expert** | `Account` |
| Creating a new transaction | **Creator** | `ATM` (controller/creator) |
| Handling the user input flow | **Controller** | `ATMController` |
| Printing a receipt | **Pure Fabrication** | `ReceiptPrinter` (not a domain class) |
| Validating PIN | **Information Expert** | `Card` |
| Persisting transactions | **Pure Fabrication** | `TransactionRepository` |

---

## 3.8 Step 7: Design Sequence (Interaction Diagram)

For the use case *"Withdraw Cash"*:

$$
\text{Flow} = \text{User} \rightarrow \text{ATM} \rightarrow \text{Card} \rightarrow \text{Bank} \rightarrow \text{Account} \rightarrow \text{Transaction(Withdrawal)} \rightarrow \text{Receipt}
$$

> **Step-by-step logic:**
> 1. User inserts card → `ATM` receives input.
> 2. `ATM` calls `Card.validatePin(pin)`.
> 3. On success, `ATM` calls `Bank.validateCard(cardNumber)`.
> 4. User selects "Withdraw" and enters amount.
> 5. `ATM` creates a `Withdrawal` transaction (Creator pattern).
> 6. `Withdrawal.execute(Account)` debits the balance.
> 7. `ATM` requests `ReceiptPrinter` to print the receipt.

This sequence is directly converted to a **UML Sequence Diagram** during the design phase.

---

## 3.9 Quality Verification (Coupling & Cohesion Check)

After applying OOD, verify:

| Class | Cohesion | Coupling | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| `ATM` | Functional (single role: orchestrate flow) | Data coupled to others | ✓ Good |
| `Account` | Functional (only balance ops) | Low (only `getBalance`, `debit`, `credit`) | ✓ Good |
| `Transaction` (abstract) | Logical (related by concept) | Low | ✓ Acceptable |
| `Receipt` | Functional (printing only) | None | ✓ Excellent |

$$
\text{Overall System Quality} = \text{High Cohesion} + \text{Low Coupling} \Rightarrow \text{Good OOD}
$$

```mermaid
flowchart TB

    end

```

> **Design Note:** This layered architecture naturally supports **Low Coupling** (each layer talks only to the one below) and **High Cohesion** (each layer has a single, well-defined role) — the twin goals of OOD.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Object Oriented Design. List its main goals.
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Module 1 | 3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** Object Oriented Design (OOD) is the process of transforming the object-oriented analysis model into a detailed design model that specifies classes, their attributes, methods, relationships, and collaborations required to implement a software system.
>
> **Main Goals of OOD:**
> 1. **Correctness** — Design accurately reflects analysis requirements.
> 2. **Robustness** — Handles invalid inputs and errors gracefully.
> 3. **Flexibility / Extensibility** — Easy to add new features (follows OCP).
> 4. **Reusability** — Classes can be reused across projects.
> 5. **Maintainability** — Code is easy to understand, modify, and debug.

---

### Q2. Differentiate between Coupling and Cohesion.
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 1 | 3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Coupling | Cohesion |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Measure of interdependence **between** modules | Measure of how strongly the responsibilities **within** a module are related |
| **Goal** | **Low** coupling (independent modules) | **High** cohesion (focused module) |
| **Scope** | Inter-module relationship | Intra-module relationship |
| **Good Design** | Data coupling, Message coupling | Functional cohesion |
| **Bad Design** | Content coupling, Common coupling | Coincidental, Logical cohesion |
| **Effect on maintenance** | High coupling = hard to modify | Low cohesion = hard to reuse |

> Coupling and Cohesion are **complementary** — good OOD maximizes cohesion and minimizes coupling simultaneously.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — with Internal Choice)

### Question A — Set 1

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 | Module 1 | 14 Marks]**

**(a) Explain the OOD process in detail. Discuss how GRASP patterns are used to assign responsibilities. [7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution:

**Step 1 — OOD Process Overview (3 Marks):**
The Object Oriented Design process is a systematic approach to transforming an analysis model into an implementable design. The key steps are:
1. Refine the analysis model (class diagram, use cases).
2. Design the system architecture (layered, MVC, microservices).
3. Identify classes using noun-phrase analysis and CRC cards.
4. Identify attributes and methods.
5. Assign responsibilities using **GRASP patterns**.
6. Identify relationships (inheritance, composition, aggregation).
7. Specify interfaces and prepare for implementation.

**Step 2 — GRASP Patterns (4 Marks):**
GRASP (General Responsibility Assignment Software Patterns) provides 9 guidelines for assigning responsibilities to classes:

| Pattern | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Information Expert** | Assign the responsibility to the class that has the data needed | `Account` handles `getBalance()` |
| **Creator** | Class B creates A if B contains, aggregates, or records A | `Order` creates `OrderLine` |
| **Controller** | A non-UI class handles system events | `ATMController` for ATM flow |
| **Low Coupling** | Minimize class dependencies | Use interfaces instead of concrete classes |
| **High Cohesion** | Group related responsibilities | `Payroll` class only for salary |
| **Polymorphism** | Use type-based behavior | `Transaction.execute()` overridden by `Withdrawal`, `Deposit` |
| **Pure Fabrication** | Invent a class to reduce coupling | `DAO` (Data Access Object) |
| **Indirection** | Introduce intermediary to decouple | Middleware between UI and DB |
| **Protected Variations** | Wrap variations behind stable interface | Abstract `PaymentGateway` |

**GRASP ensures** the design is maintainable, testable, and follows SOLID principles.

---

**(b) Explain the SOLID principles of object oriented design with examples. [7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution:

**SOLID** is an acronym for the five foundational principles of object-oriented design, introduced by Robert C. Martin:

**1. Single Responsibility Principle (SRP) — [1.5 Marks]**
A class should have only **one reason to change** (one responsibility).

*Example:*
```java
// BAD - violates SRP
class Employee {
    void calculateSalary() { /* logic */ }
    void saveToDatabase() { /* DB logic */ }
    void printPayslip() { /* printing */ }
}

// GOOD - follows SRP
class Employee { String name; double baseSalary; }
class PayrollCalculator { double calculate(Employee e); }
class EmployeeRepository { void save(Employee e); }
class PayslipPrinter { void print(Employee e); }
```

**2. Open/Closed Principle (OCP) — [1.5 Marks]**
Classes should be **open for extension** but **closed for modification**. Add new functionality by adding new code, not by changing existing code.

*Example:*
```java
abstract class Shape {
    abstract double area();
}
class Circle extends Shape {
    double radius;
    public double area() { return Math.PI * radius * radius; }
}
class Square extends Shape {
    double side;
    public double area() { return side * side; }
}
// Adding a new shape doesn't modify existing classes
```

**3. Liskov Substitution Principle (LSP) — [1.5 Marks]**
Objects of a superclass shall be replaceable with objects of its subclasses **without breaking the application**.

*Example:* A `Square` class extending `Rectangle` violates LSP if `setWidth()` and `setHeight()` behave independently, because Square's contract is different.

**4. Interface Segregation Principle (ISP) — [1 Mark]**
Clients should not be forced to depend on methods they do not use. Prefer **many small interfaces** over one large interface.

*Example:* Split a fat `IMachine` interface into `IPrinter`, `IScanner`, `IFax`.

**5. Dependency Inversion Principle (DIP) — [1.5 Marks]**
High-level modules should **not depend on low-level modules**. Both should depend on **abstractions**.

*Example:*
```java
interface IMessageSender { void send(String msg); }
class EmailSender implements IMessageSender { public void send(String m) {...} }
class Notification {
    private IMessageSender sender;  // depends on abstraction
    public Notification(IMessageSender s) { this.sender = s; }
}
```

> **Examiner's Note:** Marking typically rewards 1 mark per principle for correct definition + 0.5 mark for example.

---

### Question B — Set 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 1 | 14 Marks]**

**(a) What is the difference between Object Oriented Analysis (OOA) and Object Oriented Design (OOD)? Explain the steps involved in OOD. [7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution:

**Difference between OOA and OOD (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | OOA | OOD |
|---|---|---|
| **Phase** | First phase (WHAT) | Second phase (HOW) |
| **Focus** | Identifying objects, classes, requirements | Specifying responsibilities, collaborations, interfaces |
| **Output** | Conceptual class diagram, use cases | Detailed class diagram, sequence diagrams, architecture |
| **Concerns** | Problem domain only | Implementation domain, performance, reusability |
| **Stakeholder** | Domain expert input | Developer input |
| **Granularity** | High-level (abstract) | Low-level (concrete, implementation-ready) |

**Steps in OOD (4 Marks):**

1. **Refine the Analysis Model** — Add implementation-level details to OOA output.
2. **Design System Architecture** — Choose layered, MVC, or client-server style.
3. **Identify Classes** — Use noun-phrase analysis and CRC (Class-Responsibility-Collaborator) cards.
4. **Assign Attributes and Methods** — Use **Information Expert** GRASP pattern.
5. **Design Class Relationships** — Apply inheritance, composition, association.
6. **Apply SOLID Principles** — Ensure SRP, OCP, LSP, ISP, DIP.
7. **Verify Quality** — Check coupling, cohesion, and reusability metrics.

---

**(b) Explain Coupling and Cohesion in detail. List the different types of coupling and cohesion with examples. [7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution:

**Coupling (3.5 Marks):**
Coupling is the measure of **interdependence** between two classes/modules. Lower coupling = better design. Types in decreasing order of severity:

1. **Content Coupling (Worst)** — One class directly modifies another's internal data (breaks encapsulation).
   *Example:* `classA.somePrivateField = value;`
2. **Common Coupling** — Two classes share a global variable.
3. **External Coupling** — Two classes share an external data format (e.g., file layout).
4. **Control Coupling** — One class passes a control flag (`mode = "print"`) to another.
5. **Stamp Coupling** — Passes a whole object but uses only a few fields.
6. **Data Coupling (Good)** — Passes only primitive parameters.
7. **Message Coupling (Best in OOD)** — Communicates only via method calls/messages.

**Cohesion (3.5 Marks):**
Cohesion is the measure of how strongly the responsibilities **within a single class** are related. Higher cohesion = better design. Types in increasing order of quality:

1. **Coincidental (Worst)** — Unrelated functions grouped together.
2. **Logical** — Similar functions grouped, selected by a flag.
3. **Temporal** — Functions executed at the same time (e.g., `init()`).
4. **Procedural** — Functions must be executed in a specific order.
5. **Communicational** — Functions operate on the same input data.
6. **Sequential** — Output of one function is input to the next.
7. **Functional (Best)** — All functions contribute to a single well-defined task.

> **Designer's Rule:** Aim for **Functional Cohesion** and **Data/Message Coupling** for high-quality OOD.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Students Make (and lose marks for):**
>
> 1. **Confusing OOA with OOD** — Many students write the same answer for both. Always emphasize that **OOA = "WHAT"** and **OOD = "HOW"**.
> 2. **Listing SOLID principles without examples** — You will get 1 mark per principle for definition, but the example (0.5 mark) often decides between full and partial credit.
> 3. **Reversing coupling/cohesion goal** — Students often write "high coupling, low cohesion is good." The **correct** rule is **LOW coupling, HIGH cohesion**.
> 4. **Missing the "is-a vs has-a" distinction** — Use "is-a" for inheritance, "has-a" for composition. Examiners test this specifically.
> 5. **Forgetting GRASP patterns** — The 9 GRASP patterns carry dedicated weight. Memorize at least 5.
> 6. **No architecture diagram in OOD explanation** — Always accompany the OOD process with at least one **class diagram** or **block diagram**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Object Oriented Design (Module 1)**

### Key Definitions
- **Object Oriented Design (OOD):** Process of converting OOA model into implementable design.
- **OOA vs OOD:** OOA = WHAT; OOD = HOW.
- **OOD Goals:** Correctness, Robustness, Flexibility, Reusability, Maintainability.

### The 5 Steps of OOD
1. Refine analysis model
2. Design architecture
3. Identify classes (noun phrase + CRC)
4. Assign responsibilities (GRASP)
5. Verify quality (coupling & cohesion)

### GRASP Patterns (9 Total — Memorize All)
1. Information Expert
2. Creator
3. Controller
4. Low Coupling
5. High Cohesion
6. Polymorphism
7. Pure Fabrication
8. Indirection
9. Protected Variations

### SOLID Principles (5 Total)
- **S** — Single Responsibility (one reason to change)
- **O** — Open/Closed (extend, don't modify)
- **L** — Liskov Substitution (subtypes replace base)
- **I** — Interface Segregation (small, specific interfaces)
- **D** — Dependency Inversion (depend on abstractions)

### Coupling Types (Worst → Best)
Content → Common → External → Control → Stamp → Data → Message

### Cohesion Types (Worst → Best)
Coincidental → Logical → Temporal → Procedural → Communicational → Sequential → Functional

### Design Quality Rule
- **GOOD OOD = LOW Coupling + HIGH Cohesion**

### Key Relationships
- **is-a** → Inheritance (specialization)
- **has-a** → Composition / Aggregation
- **uses-a** → Association / Dependency

### UML Diagrams Used in OOD
- **Class Diagram** (structure)
- **Sequence Diagram** (interactions)
- **Component Diagram** (architecture)
- **Deployment Diagram** (physical)

### Quality Metrics (CK Metrics)
- **WMC** — Weighted Methods per Class (lower = better)
- **DIT** — Depth of Inheritance Tree (moderate = better)
- **CBO** — Coupling Between Objects (lower = better)
- **RFC** — Response For Class (lower = better)
- **LCOM** — Lack of Cohesion (lower = better)

### One-Line Exam Triggers
- *"What is OOD?"* → Define + list 5 goals.
- *"Explain GRASP"* → Name all 9 with one-line each.
- *"Explain SOLID"* → One definition + one example per principle.
- *"Differentiate coupling and cohesion"* → Use the comparison table.
- *"List types of coupling/cohesion"* → Use the ordered lists from worst to best.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Study of Automated Fire Alarm System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT414 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Case Study of Automated Fire Alarm System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Automated Fire Alarm System (AFAS) – OOP Case Study Definition**
> An *Automated Fire Alarm System* is a real-time, event-driven, embedded safety system that continuously monitors environmental parameters such as **smoke density**, **temperature**, and **gas concentration** using sensor arrays, and triggers appropriate alarm actuators (sirens, sprinklers, GSM modems, fire station alerts) when sensed values cross pre-configured safety thresholds.

From an **Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) perspective** (as mandated in KTU 2024 PEECT414 Module 1), the AFAS is treated as a *collection of interacting software objects* — where each physical or logical entity in the system (sensor, alarm, controller, zone, operator) is modeled as a **Class** with **Attributes** (data members) and **Behaviors** (member functions), and the communication between them is achieved through **message passing** (function calls between objects).

## 1.2 The OOP Modeling Approach – What KTU Expects

In the KTU 2024 OOP module, a *case study* is not just a textual description — it is an **analytical exercise** in which the student must:

| KTU Skill Expected | Practical Action on AFAS |
|---|---|
| **Object Identification** | Spot entities like `Sensor`, `Alarm`, `Controller`, `Zone` |
| **Class Design** | Abstract each entity into a class with attributes & methods |
| **Encapsulation** | Hide sensor calibration data inside sensor class |
| **Inheritance** | `SmokeSensor` and `HeatSensor` inherit from base `Sensor` |
| **Polymorphism** | Common `trigger()` method behaves differently per sensor type |
| **Abstraction** | Expose only `activate()` / `readValue()` to outer controller |

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy – "The Building as a Living Organism"**
> Imagine a large hospital building as a **human body**. Just as nerves carry pain signals to the brain, the **sensors** in AFAS detect fire signatures. The **brain** is the **central controller** that decides what to do. The **mouth (sirens)**, **hands (sprinklers)**, and **legs (emergency exits)** are the **actuators** that physically respond. In OOP, we model each organ as a **Class** — and the nervous system's signals are our **method calls**. The whole organism cooperating = the **Object-Oriented System**.

## 1.4 Core Physical & Functional Metrics (Bold High-Yield)

- **Smoke Threshold:** Typically **0.1 % to 0.5 % obscuration/ft** (UL 217 Standard)
- **Temperature Threshold:** **57 °C (135 °F)** for rate-of-rise detectors
- **Response Time:** **≤ 30 seconds** (NFPA 72 Standard)
- **Sensor Sampling Rate:** **1 Hz to 10 Hz**
- **Communication Protocol:** Often **CAN bus / RS-485 / MQTT** for IoT variants
- **Power Backup:** **24 hours standby + 5 minutes alarm** (per IS 2189)

## 1.5 Why AFAS as a KTU Case Study?

KTU specifically chooses AFAS because it is:
1. **Inherently modular** (sensors, controller, alarms are independent units) → perfect for **class decomposition**.
2. **Event-driven** (object activation happens on triggers) → perfect for **message passing demonstration**.
3. **Hierarchical** (different sensor types under one parent) → perfect for **inheritance hierarchies**.
4. **Polymorphic in action** (multiple devices share the same `activate()` interface).

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PEECT414 Module 1):** A case study must demonstrate *all four* pillars of OOP — **Encapsulation, Abstraction, Inheritance, Polymorphism** — within a single coherent system. AFAS is the canonical textbook example used in *E. Balagurusamy, Object-Oriented Programming with C++* and *Bjarne Stroustrup, The C++ Programming Language*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Object-Oriented Decomposition of AFAS

### Step 1 — Identify the Physical Actors (Nouns)
A smart KTU answer begins by listing all **physical entities** in the system. These become candidate **classes**:

- Fire Alarm Control Panel (**FACP**)
- Smoke Sensor
- Heat Sensor
- Gas (CO) Sensor
- Manual Call Point (Pull Station)
- Alarm Siren
- Sprinkler Actuator
- Zone (a logical grouping of sensors per floor/wing)
- Building
- Operator / User
- Notification Module (GSM, Email)

### Step 2 — Identify Attributes (Data Members)
For every class, list what **data** it must hold. These are *state variables*:

| Class | Key Attributes |
|---|---|
| `Sensor` (abstract base) | `sensorID`, `isActive`, `thresholdValue`, `currentReading`, `location` |
| `SmokeSensor` | `obscurationLevel`, `calibrationFactor` |
| `HeatSensor` | `temperature`, `rateOfRise` |
| `Alarm` | `alarmID`, `alarmType`, `volume`, `isTriggered` |
| `FACP` | `zoneList`, `activeAlarms`, `systemStatus` |
| `Zone` | `zoneID`, `floorNumber`, `sensorList` |
| `SprinklerActuator` | `waterPressure`, `isOpen` |
| `NotificationModule` | `phoneNumber`, `emailAddress` |

### Step 3 — Identify Behaviors (Member Functions / Methods)
Now list what each class **can do**. These are *responsibilities*:

- `Sensor::readValue()` → returns current analog/digital reading
- `Sensor::calibrate()` → tunes the threshold
- `Sensor::trigger()` → fires the event when threshold is crossed
- `FACP::pollAllSensors()` → reads all zones
- `FACP::activateAlarm(alarmID)` → turns on a specific alarm
- `FACP::notifyAuthorities()` → sends SMS/email
- `Zone::addSensor(sensor)` / `Zone::removeSensor(sensor)` → manages list
- `NotificationModule::sendSMS(msg)` / `sendEmail(msg)`

### Step 4 — Identify Relationships
KTU examiners award marks when students explicitly state **"IS-A"** and **"HAS-A"** relationships:

- `SmokeSensor` **IS-A** `Sensor` → *Inheritance*
- `HeatSensor` **IS-A** `Sensor` → *Inheritance*
- `Zone` **HAS-A** `Sensor` (many) → *Aggregation/Composition*
- `FACP` **HAS-A** `Zone` (many) → *Aggregation*
- `FACP` **USES-A** `NotificationModule` → *Association/Delegation*

### Step 5 — Identify the Trigger Logic
The **heart of AFAS** is the *event loop*:

> `FACP` continuously polls each `Sensor` → if `sensor.currentReading > sensor.thresholdValue` → FACP activates the corresponding `Alarm`, opens `SprinklerActuator`, and calls `NotificationModule.sendSMS(...)`.

This is the perfect KTU illustration of **message passing between objects** — message passing is the *pulse* of OOP.

## 2.2 Demonstration of All Four OOP Pillars in AFAS

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Marking Scheme: 1 mark per correct pillar + 1 mark for example. Total 4 marks reserved on this question alone.**

### 2.2.1 Encapsulation in AFAS
The internal calibration constants of a `SmokeSensor` (e.g., `calibrationFactor = 1.07`) are declared **private**. The outside world can only read the smoke level via the public method `getObscuration()`. This prevents accidental corruption of sensitive tuning values — a fire marshal cannot accidentally over-write the sensor's legal safety calibration.

$$\text{Encapsulation Boundary} = \Big\{ \text{private data} \;\big|\; \text{public accessor} :: \text{mutator} \Big\}$$

### 2.2.2 Abstraction in AFAS
The FACP only knows that a sensor has a `readValue()` method — it does **not care** whether it is a `SmokeSensor`, `HeatSensor`, or `GasSensor`. The user manual of FACP says *"connect any sensor, it auto-detects"*. This is **abstract behavior** — implementation details are hidden.

### 2.2.3 Inheritance in AFAS
A `SmokeSensor` and a `HeatSensor` share the `sensorID`, `isActive`, `location` fields. Instead of duplicating these, we create a **base class `Sensor`** and let child classes inherit. This eliminates redundancy — a core OOP goal.

### 2.2.4 Polymorphism in AFAS
Each sensor subclass **overrides** the base method `trigger()`. When the FACP calls `sensor->trigger()`, the correct version executes:
- `SmokeSensor::trigger()` → activates smoke-evacuation fans
- `HeatSensor::trigger()` → activates sprinklers
- `GasSensor::trigger()` → cuts off gas valves

This is **runtime polymorphism** — the same message, different behavior.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master this table for any AFAS-related theory or diagram question.**

| Concept | OOP Mapping in AFAS | Symbol/Notation | Key Property |
|---|---|---|---|
| Physical Device | Class | `class SmokeSensor` | Blueprint |
| Specific Installed Device | Object | `SmokeSensor s1("LAB-101")` | Instance |
| Device Property | Attribute | `float threshold;` | State |
| Device Action | Method | `void trigger();` | Behavior |
| IS-A Relationship | Inheritance | `class H : public S` | "SmokeSensor IS-A Sensor" |
| HAS-A Relationship | Composition | `Zone z; z.add(s);` | "Zone HAS-A Sensor" |
| Same Interface, Diff. Behavior | Polymorphism | `virtual void trigger();` | Runtime binding |
| Hide Internal State | Encapsulation | `private:` | Data hiding |
| Show Only Essentials | Abstraction | `abstract class Sensor` | Interface only |
| Object Communication | Message Passing | `facp.activateAlarm(id);` | Function call |
| One Class, Many Forms | Dynamic Binding | `Sensor* s = new Smoke;` | Late binding |
| Lifetime Ownership | Composition vs Aggregation | `Zone owns Sensor` | Strong vs weak |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the professional world, AFAS modeled using OOP principles is used in:

- **Smart Buildings (KNX / BACnet protocols)** — Class hierarchy maps to device profiles.
- **Industrial SCADA Systems** — Each PLC tag becomes an object.
- **Aerospace (DO-178C Compliant Software)** — Every avionics sensor is an object with a defined interface.
- **IoT-based Smart Homes** — MQTT topics correspond to OOP class names.
- **Automotive (AUTOSAR Standard)** — Sensors, ECUs, and actuators follow strict OOP modeling.

> [!TIP]
> **Real Interview Insight:** TCS, Infosys, and Wipro campus interviews frequently ask *"Design a Fire Alarm System using OOP"* — the KTU 2024 syllabus is directly aligned with this industry pattern.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Class Design Matrix & Analytical Walkthrough

## 3.1 Exhaustive Class Design Matrix (Analytical Derivation)

Below is the **complete KTU-grade class design** that you can reproduce in any 14-mark answer. Each entry is derived by applying the **GRASP (General Responsibility Assignment Software Patterns)** principles used in industry.

### Base Class: `Sensor` (Abstract)

| Element | Identifier | Visibility | Type | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Attribute | `sensorID` | private | `int` | Unique identifier |
| Attribute | `location` | private | `string` | Physical placement |
| Attribute | `thresholdValue` | protected | `float` | Safety threshold |
| Attribute | `currentReading` | private | `float` | Live value |
| Attribute | `isActive` | private | `bool` | Operational flag |
| Method | `readValue()` | public | `virtual float` | Pure virtual — derived must implement |
| Method | `trigger()` | public | `virtual void` | Pure virtual — alarm firing |
| Method | `calibrate(float)` | public | `void` | Sets threshold |
| Method | `getStatus()` | public | `string` | Returns active/inactive |

**Derivation logic for each element:**

1. `sensorID` is **private** because a sensor's ID should never change post-installation.
2. `thresholdValue` is **protected** (not private) so that derived classes like `SmokeSensor` can reference it during their own `trigger()` implementation.
3. `readValue()` and `trigger()` are **pure virtual** → this makes `Sensor` an **abstract class** → AFAS cannot instantiate a "generic sensor" — abstraction in action.
4. The `virtual` keyword enables **dynamic binding** → runtime polymorphism → FACP can hold `Sensor*` pointers and call `trigger()` correctly for any subtype.

### Derived Class 1: `SmokeSensor : public Sensor`

| Element | Identifier | Visibility | Type | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Attribute | `obscurationLevel` | private | `float` | % smoke per foot |
| Attribute | `calibrationFactor` | private | `float` | Factory calibration |
| Method | `readValue()` | public | `float` (override) | Returns obscuration % |
| Method | `trigger()` | public | `void` (override) | Activates smoke fan |

**Derivation of `trigger()` logic:**

The smoke sensor's threshold check follows the standard UL 217 derivation. Let $O$ be the measured obscuration, $O_t$ be the threshold, and $C$ be the calibration factor. The sensor reports fire if:

$$O_{\text{normalized}} = O \times C$$

The trigger condition is:

$$O_{\text{normalized}} \geq O_t \quad\Rightarrow\quad \text{trigger}() \;=\; \text{TRUE}$$

This is the **Boolean guard** inside the override:

```
if (O * C >= O_t) {
    activateSmokeFan();
    notifyFACP("SMOKE_DETECTED");
}
```

### Derived Class 2: `HeatSensor : public Sensor`

| Element | Identifier | Visibility | Type | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Attribute | `temperature` | private | `float` | In Celsius |
| Attribute | `rateOfRise` | private | `float` | °C per minute |
| Method | `readValue()` | public | `float` (override) | Returns temperature |
| Method | `trigger()` | public | `void` (override) | Opens sprinkler valve |

**Derivation of rate-of-rise trigger:**

A rate-of-rise detector follows the differential equation governing thermal flux. The temperature increase rate $\Delta T / \Delta t$ is measured over a sliding window of $W$ seconds (typically $W = 60$ s). The trigger fires when:

$$\frac{\Delta T}{\Delta t} \geq 8.3 \;^{\circ}\text{C/min} \quad \text{OR} \quad T \geq 57 \;^{\circ}\text{C}$$

This is the **dual-criterion logic** — implementing it in OOP means the override method has an `OR` condition:

```
if (rateOfRise >= 8.3 || temperature >= 57) {
    openSprinklerValve();
    notifyFACP("HEAT_DETECTED");
}
```

### Derived Class 3: `GasSensor : public Sensor`

This sensor detects **Carbon Monoxide (CO)** and is critical in parking lots and boiler rooms.

| Element | Identifier | Visibility | Type | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Attribute | `coPPM` | private | `int` | CO in parts per million |
| Method | `readValue()` | public | `int` (override) | Returns CO ppm |
| Method | `trigger()` | public | `void` (override) | Closes gas valve |

**Trigger condition (per WHO and OSHA standards):**

$$\text{CO}_{\text{ppm}} \geq 50 \quad\text{over 8-hour average} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \text{trigger}() \;=\; \text{TRUE}$$

This maps to:

```
if (coPPM >= 50) {
    closeGasValve();
    notifyFACP("GAS_LEAK_DETECTED");
}
```

## 3.2 Aggregator Class: `Zone`

A `Zone` logically groups sensors of a single floor or wing. This is a **HAS-A** relationship.

$$\text{Zone} = \Big\{ s_1, s_2, s_3, \dots, s_n \;\Big|\; s_i \in \text{Sensor} \Big\}$$

| Element | Identifier | Visibility | Type | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Attribute | `zoneID` | private | `int` | Zone number |
| Attribute | `floorNumber` | private | `int` | Floor index |
| Attribute | `sensorList` | private | `vector<Sensor*>` | All sensors in zone |
| Method | `addSensor(Sensor*)` | public | `void` | Registration |
| Method | `pollAll()` | public | `void` | Reads every sensor |

**Mathematical model of zone polling:**

Let $S_z = \{s_1, s_2, \dots, s_n\}$ be the sensor set of zone $z$. The zone's overall alarm state $A_z$ is:

$$A_z = \bigvee_{i=1}^{n} \text{trigger}(s_i)$$

In words: **if ANY sensor in the zone triggers, the entire zone is in alarm state**. This is a Boolean OR across all sensors — a classic aggregation pattern in OOP.

## 3.3 Controller Class: `FACP` (Fire Alarm Control Panel)

The FACP is the **brain** — it owns zones, manages alarms, and triggers notifications.

| Element | Identifier | Visibility | Type | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Attribute | `facpID` | private | `string` | Panel ID |
| Attribute | `zoneList` | private | `vector<Zone*>` | All zones in building |
| Attribute | `alarmList` | private | `vector<Alarm*>` | All sirens/buzzers |
| Attribute | `notifier` | private | `NotificationModule*` | SMS/email gateway |
| Method | `pollAllZones()` | public | `void` | Main event loop |
| Method | `activateAlarm(int id)` | public | `void` | Triggers specific alarm |
| Method | `notifyAuthorities()` | public | `void` | Sends external alert |

**Derivation of FACP's main event loop:**

The FACP runs an infinite loop. The polling cycle $T_c$ (typically $T_c = 1$ second) determines system latency.

$$\forall z \in \text{zoneList}, \quad \forall s \in z.\text{sensorList}: \quad s.\text{readValue()}$$

If any sensor returns a value above its threshold, the system initiates a **cascading response**:

$$\text{Fire Event} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Alarm ON} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Sprinkler OPEN} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Authorities NOTIFY}$$

This is a **Chain-of-Responsibility pattern** in OOP — the event ripples through objects.

## 3.4 Object Communication Diagram (Message Sequence)

A typical interaction sequence, expressed in message-passing notation:

$$\text{FACP} \;\xrightarrow{\text{pollAllZones}()}\; \text{Zone} \;\xrightarrow{\text{pollAll}()}\; \text{Sensor}$$

$$\text{Sensor} \;\xrightarrow{\text{readValue}()}\; \text{Hardware}$$

$$\text{Sensor} \;\xrightarrow{\text{trigger}()}\; \text{FACP} \;\xrightarrow{\text{activateAlarm}()}\; \text{Alarm}$$

$$\text{FACP} \;\xrightarrow{\text{notifyAuthorities}()}\; \text{NotificationModule} \;\xrightarrow{\text{sendSMS}()}\; \text{GSM Modem}$$

Each arrow is a **message** (function call) — the essence of OOP.

## 3.5 Summary of Design Decisions

| Decision | Justification | OOP Principle Used |
|---|---|---|
| Make `Sensor` abstract | Sensors don't exist generically | Abstraction |
| Use inheritance for sensor types | Shared fields like `sensorID`, `isActive` | Inheritance |
| Use `virtual` keyword | FACP holds generic `Sensor*` pointer | Polymorphism |
| Make calibration data private | Prevent unauthorized tuning | Encapsulation |
| Use `vector<Sensor*>` in Zone | Zone is composed of sensors | Aggregation |
| Pass objects by reference in methods | Avoid copying, allow polymorphism | Best practice |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics (Mermaid)

## 4.1 Class Diagram (UML Notation in Mermaid)

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Sensor {
        -int sensorID
        -string location
        -float currentReading
        -bool isActive
        #float thresholdValue
        +virtual float readValue() = 0
        +virtual void trigger() = 0
        +void calibrate(float val)
        +string getStatus()
    }
    class SmokeSensor {
        -float obscurationLevel
        -float calibrationFactor
        +float readValue() override
        +void trigger() override
    }
    class HeatSensor {
        -float temperature
        -float rateOfRise
        +float readValue() override
        +void trigger() override
    }
    class GasSensor {
        -int coPPM
        +int readValue() override
        +void trigger() override
    }
    class Zone {
        -int zoneID
        -int floorNumber
        -vector~Sensor*~ sensorList
        +void addSensor(Sensor* s)
        +void pollAll()
    }
    class Alarm {
        -int alarmID
        -string alarmType
        -int volume
        -bool isTriggered
        +void ring()
        +void stop()
    }
    class SprinklerActuator {
        -float waterPressure
        -bool isOpen
        +void openValve()
        +void closeValve()
    }
    class NotificationModule {
        -string phoneNumber
        -string emailAddress
        +void sendSMS(string msg)
        +void sendEmail(string msg)
    }
    class FACP {
        -string facpID
        -vector~Zone*~ zoneList
        -vector~Alarm*~ alarmList
        -NotificationModule* notifier
        +void pollAllZones()
        +void activateAlarm(int id)
        +void notifyAuthorities()
    }
    Sensor <|-- SmokeSensor
    Sensor <|-- HeatSensor
    Sensor <|-- GasSensor
    Zone o-- Sensor : HAS MANY
    FACP o-- Zone : HAS MANY
    FACP o-- Alarm : HAS MANY
    FACP --> NotificationModule : USES
    SprinklerActuator <-- HeatSensor : ACTIVATES
    Alarm <-- FACP : CONTROLS
```

## 4.2 Use Case Diagram (System Functionality)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Building Occupant] -->|Pulls Manual Call Point| B(MCP Activated)
    A -->|Hears Alarm| C(Evacuate Building)
    O[Fire Operator] -->|Monitors| D(View FACP Dashboard)
    O -->|Acknowledges| E(Acknowledge Alarm)
    O -->|Resets System| F(Reset FACP)
    S[Smoke Sensor] -->|Detects Smoke| G(FACP Receives Signal)
    H[Heat Sensor] -->|Detects Heat| G
    G -->|Triggers| I(Activate Siren)
    G -->|Opens| J(Sprinkler Valve)
    G -->|Sends SMS/Email| K[Notification Module]
    K -->|Alerts| L[Fire Department]
    K -->|Alerts| M[Building Manager]
```

## 4.3 State Diagram (FACP Lifecycle)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle : System Boot
    Idle --> Monitoring : Self-Test Passed
    Monitoring --> Alarm : Sensor Threshold Crossed
    Monitoring --> Fault : Sensor Disconnected
    Monitoring --> Manual : Manual Call Point Pressed
    Alarm --> Acknowledged : Operator Acknowledges
    Acknowledged --> Investigation : Operator Investigates
    Investigation --> Alarm : Fire Confirmed
    Investigation --> Reset : False Alarm
    Alarm --> Reset : Operator Resets
    Manual --> Alarm : System Verifies
    Fault --> Monitoring : Sensor Reconnected
    Reset --> Monitoring : System Ready
    Monitoring --> [*] : System Shutdown
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Top-Down View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SENSORS [SENSOR LAYER]
        SS[Smoke Sensor]
        HS[Heat Sensor]
        GS[Gas Sensor]
        MCP[Manual Call Point]
    end
    subgraph ZONES [ZONE LAYER]
        Z1[Zone 1 Floor 1]
        Z2[Zone 2 Floor 2]
        Z3[Zone 3 Floor 3]
    end
    subgraph CONTROL [CONTROL LAYER]
        FACP[Fire Alarm Control Panel]
        PROC[Event Processor]
        LOG[Event Log Database]
    end
    subgraph ACTUATORS [ACTUATOR LAYER]
        SIR[Siren and Buzzer]
        SPR[Sprinkler Valve]
        FAN[Smoke Exhaust Fan]
    end
    subgraph NOTIFY [NOTIFICATION LAYER]
        GSM[GSM Modem SMS Gateway]
        EM[Email Server]
        FS[Fire Station Direct Line]
    end
    SS --> Z1
    HS --> Z1
    GS --> Z2
    MCP --> Z3
    Z1 --> FACP
    Z2 --> FACP
    Z3 --> FACP
    FACP --> PROC
    PROC --> LOG
    PROC --> SIR
    PROC --> SPR
    PROC --> FAN
    PROC --> GSM
    PROC --> EM
    PROC --> FS
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to draw these in the exam (KTU Answer Sheet Tip):** Even if you cannot draw Mermaid-quality diagrams on paper, draw a **simple box-and-arrow sketch** with the four layers (Sensors → Zone → FACP → Actuators) and label each arrow with the OOP concept (e.g., "message passing", "polymorphic call"). Examiners award 2-3 marks for a clean labeled diagram.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define an Automated Fire Alarm System as an Object-Oriented System. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
