> An Automated Fire Alarm System is a real-time, event-driven safety system that detects fire signatures (smoke, heat, gas) via sensors and triggers alarms/sprinklers/notifications. From an OOP perspective, it is modeled as a **collection of cooperating objects** where each physical entity (sensor, alarm, FACP, zone) is represented as a class with attributes (state) and methods (behavior). The system exhibits **encapsulation** (sensor data is hidden), **abstraction** (FACP interacts via generic interfaces), **inheritance** (smoke/heat/gas sensors share a common base), and **polymorphism** (same `trigger()` method behaves differently per sensor type). Message passing between the FACP and its subordinate objects drives the entire detection and response cycle.

---

### Question 2: List any FOUR classes you would identify in an AFAS case study and their key attributes. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Class | Two Key Attributes |
|---|---|
| `Sensor` (abstract) | `sensorID`, `thresholdValue` |
| `FACP` | `zoneList`, `activeAlarms` |
| `Alarm` | `alarmID`, `isTriggered` |
| `Zone` | `zoneID`, `sensorList` |

Each class is a logical unit of the system — `Sensor` is the base abstract class, `FACP` is the controller, `Alarm` is the actuator, and `Zone` is the logical aggregator.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and provides internal choice. You must answer **only one** of the two given options. Both questions below are independent alternatives covering the same Module 1 learning outcome.

### OPTION A (Question A) — 14 Marks

**"Design an Object-Oriented model for an Automated Fire Alarm System. Identify the major classes, their attributes, methods, and relationships. Explain how all four pillars of OOP are applied."** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024]

**Sub-part (a) — 7 Marks: Class Design and Relationships**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Object Identification (1 mark):** The major classes are `Sensor` (abstract base), `SmokeSensor`, `HeatSensor`, `GasSensor`, `Zone`, `Alarm`, `SprinklerActuator`, `NotificationModule`, and `FACP`.

2. **Class Diagram (3 marks):**

| Class | Attributes | Methods |
|---|---|---|
| `Sensor` (abstract) | `sensorID`, `location`, `thresholdValue`, `isActive` | `readValue()`, `trigger()`, `calibrate()` |
| `SmokeSensor` | `obscurationLevel`, `calibrationFactor` | overrides `readValue()`, `trigger()` |
| `HeatSensor` | `temperature`, `rateOfRise` | overrides `readValue()`, `trigger()` |
| `GasSensor` | `coPPM` | overrides `readValue()`, `trigger()` |
| `Zone` | `zoneID`, `floorNumber`, `sensorList` | `addSensor()`, `pollAll()` |
| `FACP` | `facpID`, `zoneList`, `alarmList` | `pollAllZones()`, `activateAlarm()` |
| `Alarm` | `alarmID`, `alarmType`, `isTriggered` | `ring()`, `stop()` |
| `NotificationModule` | `phoneNumber`, `emailAddress` | `sendSMS()`, `sendEmail()` |

3. **Relationships (3 marks):**
   - **IS-A:** `SmokeSensor`, `HeatSensor`, `GasSensor` inherit from `Sensor`.
   - **HAS-A (Composition):** `Zone` contains multiple `Sensor` objects; `FACP` contains multiple `Zone` objects.
   - **USES-A (Association):** `FACP` uses a `NotificationModule` for external alerts.
   - **Cardinality:** One FACP → Many Zones; One Zone → Many Sensors.

**Sub-part (b) — 7 Marks: OOP Pillars Application**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Encapsulation (1.5 marks):** The `thresholdValue` of a sensor and its `calibrationFactor` are declared **private**. External code can only read or modify them via the public `calibrate(float)` method. This prevents accidental or malicious overwriting of safety-critical calibration. Example: `sensor.calibrate(0.15)` is the only legal way to set the smoke threshold.

2. **Abstraction (1.5 marks):** `Sensor` is an **abstract class** with **pure virtual functions** `readValue()` and `trigger()`. The FACP code does not need to know whether it is talking to a `SmokeSensor` or `HeatSensor` — it simply calls `sensor->readValue()`. Implementation details (how the smoke is detected, how the heat is measured) are hidden from the FACP layer.

3. **Inheritance (2 marks):** `SmokeSensor`, `HeatSensor`, and `GasSensor` are derived from the common base class `Sensor`. Common attributes like `sensorID`, `location`, `isActive` are defined once in the base class and reused. New sensor types (e.g., `FlameSensor` in the future) can be added without modifying existing code — this honors the **Open/Closed Principle**.

4. **Polymorphism (2 marks):** The base class declares `virtual void trigger() = 0`. Each derived class provides its own implementation. The FACP holds an array of `Sensor*` pointers and iterates:
   - `for (auto s : sensors) s->trigger();`
   - The correct override is invoked at **runtime** based on the actual object type — this is **dynamic dispatch** and is the textbook example of runtime polymorphism in OOP.

---

### OPTION B (Question B) — 14 Marks

**"With a neat class diagram, explain the IS-A and HAS-A relationships among the various classes in the Automated Fire Alarm System. How does message passing enable the system to function?"** [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model Paper 2024]

**Sub-part (a) — 7 Marks: Class Diagram and Relationships**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Class Diagram (3 marks):** A standard UML class diagram is drawn (as shown in Section 4.1 of these notes) with the following structure:
   - Top: `Sensor` (abstract)
   - Below: `SmokeSensor`, `HeatSensor`, `GasSensor` (children)
   - Side: `Zone` (composed of `Sensor` objects)
   - Bottom: `FACP` (composed of `Zone` and `Alarm` objects), `NotificationModule`

2. **IS-A Relationship (2 marks):** IS-A represents **inheritance**.
   - `SmokeSensor` IS-A `Sensor`
   - `HeatSensor` IS-A `Sensor`
   - `GasSensor` IS-A `Sensor`
   - This is implemented in C++ via `class SmokeSensor : public Sensor`. The derived class inherits all data members and methods (except private ones, which require public accessors).

3. **HAS-A Relationship (2 marks):** HAS-A represents **composition or aggregation**.
   - `Zone` HAS-A `Sensor` (a zone contains multiple sensors)
   - `FACP` HAS-A `Zone` (a control panel manages multiple zones)
   - `FACP` HAS-A `Alarm` (a control panel drives multiple sirens)
   - This is implemented by declaring a member like `vector<Sensor*> sensorList;` inside the `Zone` class.

**Sub-part (b) — 7 Marks: Message Passing and System Functioning**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Definition of Message Passing (1 mark):** In OOP, when one object invokes a method of another object, it is called **message passing**. The calling object is the *sender*, the called object is the *receiver*, and the method name is the *message*. This is the fundamental communication mechanism of OOP.

2. **Message Flow in AFAS (3 marks):**
   - The main controller `facp` starts an infinite loop.
   - Message 1: `facp.pollAllZones()` is invoked.
   - For each `zone` in `facp.zoneList`, Message 2: `zone.pollAll()` is invoked.
   - For each `sensor` in `zone.sensorList`, Message 3: `sensor.readValue()` is invoked.
   - If the returned value exceeds the threshold, Message 4: `sensor.trigger()` is invoked internally, which sends Message 5: `facp.activateAlarm(alarmID)`.
   - Finally, Message 6: `facp.notifyAuthorities()` is invoked, which triggers Message 7: `notifier.sendSMS("Fire at Zone 1")`.

3. **Why Message Passing Matters (2 marks):**
   - It enables **loose coupling** — the FACP does not need to know the internals of any specific sensor.
   - It supports **polymorphic dispatch** — the same `trigger()` message can be sent to different sensor types, each handling it appropriately.
   - It reflects the **real-world event flow** — when smoke is detected, the message "smoke detected" propagates up to the FACP, and the message "sound alarm" propagates down to the siren.
   - It makes the system **modular and extensible** — adding a new sensor type only requires it to understand the same `trigger()` message.

4. **Example of a complete event (1 mark):**
   - `SmokeSensor s1("LAB-101");` → object creation
   - `s1.readValue()` returns `0.6` (60% obscuration) → exceeds `thresholdValue = 0.3`
   - `s1.trigger()` is auto-invoked → activates the smoke fan
   - `FACP` receives notification → calls `alarm.ring()` and `notifier.sendSMS("Fire in Lab 101")`.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on AFAS Questions**
> 1. **❌ Describing the system narratively without identifying CLASSES.** Always explicitly list at least 5-6 class names. A pure narrative answer scores at most 4/14.
> 2. **❌ Forgetting to mention all FOUR OOP pillars.** Examiners allocate nearly 4-5 marks to this. If you skip polymorphism, you lose 1.5 marks.
> 3. **❌ Confusing IS-A and HAS-A.** IS-A is inheritance (parent-child). HAS-A is composition (container-contained). Many students mix these up.
> 4. **❌ Not drawing a class diagram.** Even a rough hand-drawn box diagram earns 2-3 marks. Skipping the diagram loses those marks outright.
> 5. **❌ Not labeling visibility (public/private).** In the class table or diagram, mention `+` for public and `-` for private. KTU examiners check for this.
> 6. **❌ Forgetting message passing.** Always end your answer with a sentence on how objects communicate — this is the *pulse* of OOP and the examiner looks for it.
> 7. **❌ Not mentioning threshold values or trigger conditions.** Adding the real threshold (e.g., 57 °C, 50 ppm CO) shows depth and earns bonus half-marks.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Read this list 5 minutes before the exam — it covers 90% of expected questions.**

- ✅ **AFAS = Automated Fire Alarm System** — a real-world OOP case study.
- ✅ **Minimum 5 classes to identify:** `Sensor`, `SmokeSensor`, `HeatSensor`, `Zone`, `FACP`, `Alarm`.
- ✅ **Base class `Sensor` is ABSTRACT** — pure virtual `readValue()` and `trigger()`.
- ✅ **All four OOP pillars must be demonstrated:**
  - **Encapsulation** → private calibration data + public accessor
  - **Abstraction** → abstract class with pure virtual functions
  - **Inheritance** → SmokeSensor/HeatSensor/GasSensor inherit from Sensor
  - **Polymorphism** → `virtual void trigger()` overridden in each derived class
- ✅ **Two main relationships:**
  - **IS-A** → inheritance → use `: public` syntax
  - **HAS-A** → composition → use container member (e.g., `vector<Sensor*>`)
- ✅ **Standard thresholds to memorize:**
  - Smoke obscuration threshold: ~0.1 to 0.5 %/ft
  - Heat: 57 °C OR 8.3 °C/min rate-of-rise
  - CO: 50 ppm (8-hour average)
- ✅ **Message passing = function call between objects** = the core of OOP.
- ✅ **Always draw a class diagram** — even a simple box-arrow sketch with visibility markers (`+`/`-`).
- ✅ **The FACP is the central controller** — it owns zones, alarms, and notifier.
- ✅ **Zone aggregation logic** — zone is in alarm if **ANY** sensor in it triggers (Boolean OR).
- ✅ **Always end with a one-line conclusion** linking the case study back to OOP principles.
- ✅ **Avoid writing code unless explicitly asked** — KTU Module 1 focuses on **design and analysis**, not implementation.
- ✅ **Key buzzwords for marks:** *encapsulation, abstraction, inheritance, polymorphism, message passing, IS-A, HAS-A, virtual function, abstract class, override, dynamic binding*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Java - Java Buzzwords
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT414 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Introduction to Java - Java Buzzwords # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of Java > [!NOTE] > **Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Standard):** > *Java is a high-level, strongly-typed, general-purpose, class-based, object-oriented programming language* designed by **James Gosling** and his team at **Sun Microsystems** (officially released in **1995**), currently maintained by **Oracle Corporation**. Java applications are typically compiled to **bytecode** that can run on any **Java Virtual Machine (JVM)** regardless of the underlying computer architecture. Java is governed by the principle **"Write Once, Run Anywhere" (WORA)**, meaning compiled Java code (bytecode) is platform-independent and executable on any device equipped with a compatible JVM. ## 1.2 The Concept of "Java Buzzwords" > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition — Java Buzzwords:** > *Java Buzzwords* are the **eleven (11) defining design features** of the Java language, originally enumerated by the designers at Sun Microsystems to summarize the philosophy, strengths, and engineering goals of the language. They serve as the **official descriptive vocabulary** used to characterize Java in academic literature, technical documentation, and university examinations. The 11 official Java Buzzwords are: 1. **Simple** 2. **Object-Oriented** 3. **Distributed** 4. **Robust** 5. **Secure** 6. **Architecture-Neutral** 7. **Portable** 8. **Interpreted** 9. **High-Performance** 10. **Multithreaded** 11. **Dynamic** ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition) > [!TIP] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Universal Travel Adapter":** > Imagine you are an electrical appliance (your Java program) manufactured in one country. To plug into any socket worldwide, you need a *travel adapter* that converts the shape of the plug to fit the local socket. The **JVM (Java Virtual Machine)** is exactly that universal adapter. The **bytecode** is your standardized plug, and the **11 Buzzwords** are the *quality certifications* printed on the adapter (shock-proof, durable, lightweight, fast-charging, etc.). A student of Java must understand these "quality certifications" because KTU examiners test them directly as a measure of language literacy. ### 1.4 Why Java Matters — Key Metrics | Metric | Value | Significance | |---|---|---| | Year of Release | **1995** | Standard historical fact, frequently asked | | Designed By | **James Gosling** | Foundational GK question | | Original Name | **"Oak"** | Renamed due to trademark conflict | | Current Owner | **Oracle Corporation** | Acquired Sun Microsystems in **2010** | | Latest LTS Version | **Java 21 (LTS)** | Knowledge cutoff: January 2026 | | File Extension | **`.java` / `.class`** | Source code vs. compiled bytecode | # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The 11 Java Buzzwords — Conceptual Breakdown > [!IMPORTANT] > **Examination Tip:** KTU expects students to **define each buzzword in one crisp sentence** and then **elaborate with at least one real-world example or Java code snippet**. A bare list of buzzwords without explanation is awarded **zero marks** in the valuation key. ### 2.1.1 Simple - **Why?** Java was designed to be easier than C/C++ by removing complex, error-prone features. - **How?** Eliminates **explicit pointers**, **operator overloading**, and **multiple inheritance** (replaced by interfaces). - **Key Idea:** *Garbage collection* handles memory cleanup automatically, eliminating manual `free()` / `delete()` calls. ### 2.1.2 Object-Oriented - **Why?** Models real-world entities as objects, improving modularity and reusability. - **How?** Built entirely around **classes** and **objects**. Supports all four OOP pillars: **Encapsulation, Abstraction, Inheritance, Polymorphism**. - **Key Idea:** Even the entry point `main()` is wrapped inside a class. ### 2.1.3 Distributed - **Why?** Modern applications run across multiple networked machines. - **How?** Java provides a rich library (`java.net`) for **TCP/IP** socket programming, **RMI (Remote Method Invocation)**, and **URL** handling. - **Key Idea:** Programs can access resources over a network as easily as local files. ### 2.1.4 Robust - **Why?** Programs must fail predictably, not crash the system. - **How?** Achieved through **strong memory management** (no pointers), **exception handling** (`try-catch-finally`), and **strong type-checking** at compile-time and runtime. - **Key Idea:** Eliminates entire categories of bugs (e.g., buffer overflows, dangling pointers). ### 2.1.5 Secure - **Why?** Code from untrusted sources (e.g., the Internet) must not harm the host. - **How?** Programs run inside the **JVM sandbox** (no direct memory access), the **classloader** separates local vs. remote code, and the **SecurityManager** controls runtime permissions. - **Key Idea:** Used in banking, e-commerce, and mobile (Android) ecosystems. ### 2.1.6 Architecture-Neutral - **Why?** Software should not depend on processor specifics (Intel, ARM, RISC-V). - **How?** Java compiles to **bytecode**, an intermediate representation, not native machine code. The **JVM** translates bytecode to the host's native instructions at runtime. - **Key Idea:** Same `.class` file runs on x86, ARM, or SPARC. ### 2.1.7 Portable - **Why?** Code should behave identically across operating systems. - **How?** Primitive types have **fixed sizes** (e.g., `int` is always **32 bits** regardless of OS), and the JVM abstracts OS-specific features. **No implementation-dependent** aspects. - **Key Idea:** Java enforces IEEE 754 for floating-point arithmetic universally. ### 2.1.8 Interpreted - **Why?** A single execution model simplifies deployment. - **How?** The **JVM** is essentially an *interpreter* that reads bytecode instructions. Modern JVMs use **JIT (Just-In-Time) compilation** to convert hot code paths into native machine code for speed. - **Key Idea:** Combines portability of interpretation with near-native performance of compilation. ### 2.1.9 High-Performance - **Why?** Early interpreted languages were considered slow. - **How?** **JIT compiler** in modern JVMs (HotSpot) compiles frequently-executed bytecode to native code. **Garbage collector** algorithms (G1, ZGC) are highly optimized. - **Key Idea:** Benchmarks show Java performance within **~10%** of C++ in many workloads. ### 2.1.10 Multithreaded - **Why?** Modern hardware is multi-core; applications must leverage parallelism. - **How?** Built-in `Thread` class, `Runnable` interface, `synchronized` keyword, and the modern `java.util.concurrent` package. - **Key Idea:** Multiple threads share the same memory space, simplifying concurrent programming. ### 2.1.11 Dynamic - **Why?** Programs should adapt at runtime to evolving requirements. - **How?** Supports **reflection** (`Class.forName()`), **dynamic class loading**, and **runtime type information (RTTI)**. - **Key Idea:** New classes can be loaded over the network and instantiated on the fly. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield "Formula Sheet" — Buzzword Cheat Table > [!NOTE] > This is the single most important table to memorize for the KTU 2024 Scheme University Examination on this topic. | # | Buzzword | One-Line Definition | Key Mechanism | Real-World Use | |:---:|:---|:---|:---|:---| | 1 | **Simple** | Easier than C/C++ | No pointers, GC, no MI | Teaching, beginners | | 2 | **Object-Oriented** | Everything is an object | Classes & Objects | OOP modeling | | 3 | **Distributed** | Built-in networking | `java.net`, RMI, CORBA | Web services | | 4 | **Robust** | Strong error handling | Exception handling, GC | Mission-critical systems | | 5 | **Secure** | Safe in untrusted envs. | Sandbox, SecurityManager | Banking, Android | | 6 | **Architecture-Neutral** | CPU-independent | Bytecode + JVM | Cross-platform apps | | 7 | **Portable** | OS-independent | Fixed primitive sizes | WORA deployment | | 8 | **Interpreted** | Executes bytecode | JVM interpreter + JIT | Runtime flexibility | | 9 | **High-Performance** | Near-native speed | JIT, optimized GC | Enterprise servers | | 10 | **Multithreaded** | Concurrent execution | `Thread`, `synchronized` | Servers, games | | 11 | **Dynamic** | Extensible at runtime | Reflection, class loading | Plugins, frameworks | ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Enterprise Backends** (Banking, E-commerce): Leverage **Robust** + **Secure** + **Multithreaded** features. Example: Trading platforms process millions of transactions per second. - **Android Mobile Development**: Leverages **Secure** (sandboxed apps) + **Portable** (runs on diverse ARM-based devices). - **Big Data & Cloud**: **Hadoop, Spark, Kafka** rely on Java's **High-Performance** + **Distributed** + **Multithreaded** features. - **Embedded & IoT**: **Architecture-Neutral** + **Portable** features allow code to scale from smart cards to servers. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Detailed Walkthrough — From Source Code to Execution (The "Robust + Secure + Architecture-Neutral" Pipeline) > [!NOTE] > This derivation illustrates how three of the most heavily-tested buzzwords (**Architecture-Neutral**, **Portable**, **Interpreted**) interact in practice. KTU examiners frequently pose a 7-mark question on "Explain the Java execution model." ### Step 1 — Source Code Creation A programmer writes human-readable Java code and saves it with the `.java` extension. ```java class Hello { public static void main(String[] args) { System.out.println("Hello KTU"); } } ``` ### Step 2 — Compilation by `javac` The Java compiler (`javac`) translates the `.java` source into **bytecode**, stored in a `.class` file. $$ \text{Hello.java} \xrightarrow{\text{javac compiler}} \text{Hello.class (bytecode)} $$ ### Step 3 — Class Loading The **ClassLoader subsystem** of the JVM loads the `.class` file into memory. $$ \text{Hello.class} \xrightarrow{\text{ClassLoader}} \text{JVM Method Area} $$ ### Step 4 — Bytecode Verification The **Bytecode Verifier** checks the bytecode for **illegal code** (e.g., forged pointers, stack overflows). > [!TIP] > This is what makes Java **Secure**. If verification fails, the class is rejected. Verification guarantees: > - No forged pointers. > - No violation of access controls. > - No stack underflow/overflow. ### Step 5 — Interpretation / JIT Execution The **Execution Engine** interprets the bytecode. Hot code paths are detected and compiled to native code by the **JIT compiler** for **High-Performance**. $$ \text{Bytecode} \xrightarrow{\text{Interpreter / JIT}} \text{Native Machine Code} $$ ### Step 6 — Output The native code executes on the host hardware, producing the output on the screen. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Memory Management (The "Simple + Robust" Derivation) > [!IMPORTANT] > This matrix explains how Java achieves **Simplicity** and **Robustness** simultaneously — a 7-mark KTU favorite. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Manual Memory Management (C/C++)} &= \text{Programmer calls } \texttt{malloc()}/\texttt{free()} \\ \text{Java's Approach} &= \text{Programmer allocates via } \texttt{new} \text{; JVM deallocates via GC} \\ \therefore \quad \text{Bugs Eliminated} &= \{\text{Dangling pointers, Memory leaks, Double free}\} \end{aligned} $$ | Memory Issue in C/C++ | How Java's GC Solves It | Associated Buzzword | |:---|:---|:---| | Dangling Pointer | **No pointer arithmetic** is allowed | Simple, Robust | | Memory Leak | **Garbage Collector** reclaims unreachable objects | Robust | | Wild Pointer | All references must be **type-declared** | Robust | | Double Free | GC tracks each object **once** | Robust | ## 3.3 Step-by-Step — Multithreading Lifecycle (The "Multithreaded" Buzzword) Java threads transition through **four states**, as defined in the `Thread.State` enum: $$ \text{NEW} \rightarrow \text{RUNNABLE} \rightarrow \text{BLOCKED} \rightarrow \text{TERMINATED} $$ | State | Description | Trigger | |:---|:---|:---| | **NEW** | Thread object created, not yet started | `new Thread()` | | **RUNNABLE** | Executing or ready to execute | `t.start()` | | **BLOCKED / WAITING** | Waiting for a monitor lock or signal | `synchronized`, `wait()` | | **TERMINATED** | `run()` method has completed | Normal exit or exception | ```java class MyThread extends Thread { public void run() { System.out.println("Thread running: " + Thread.currentThread().getName()); } } public class Demo { public static void main(String[] args) { MyThread t1 = new MyThread(); MyThread t2 = new MyThread(); t1.start(); // moves thread from NEW -> RUNNABLE t2.start(); } } ``` > [!TIP] > **Why this matters for KTU:** The example above demonstrates the **Multithreaded** buzzword. The `start()` method (not `run()`) is what creates a *new* call stack — a classic KTU trick question. ## 3.4 Reflection Example (The "Dynamic" Buzzword) ```java Class cls = Class.forName("java.util.ArrayList"); Object obj = cls.getDeclaredConstructor().newInstance(); System.out.println("Loaded class: " + obj.getClass().getName()); ``` $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Class.forName("java.util.ArrayList")} &= \text{Dynamically loads class at runtime} \\ \text{newInstance()} &= \text{Creates object without compile-time knowledge} \\ \therefore \quad \text{Java supports dynamic extensibility} \end{aligned} $$ This single snippet validates the **Dynamic** buzzword — at compile time, the program has *no idea* which class it will instantiate. # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Classification of Java Buzzwords > [!NOTE] > The 11 buzzwords can be logically grouped into **four functional categories** for better retention. KTU examiners appreciate such categorizations in 14-mark answers. ```mermaid graph TD A[Java Buzzwords] --> B[Language Design Features] A --> C[Runtime Safety Features] A --> D[Execution Model Features] A --> E[Performance and Concurrency Features] B --> B1[Simple] B --> B2[Object Oriented] B --> B3[Dynamic] C --> C1[Robust] C --> C2[Secure] D --> D1[Architecture Neutral] D --> D2[Portable] D --> D3[Interpreted] E --> E1[High Performance] E --> E2[Multithreaded] E --> E3[Distributed] ``` ## 4.2 Java Execution Architecture (Block-Level Flow) > [!IMPORTANT] > This diagram shows how a Java program is compiled, verified, and executed — covering the **Architecture-Neutral**, **Portable**, **Interpreted**, **Secure**, and **High-Performance** buzzwords in a single visual. ```mermaid flowchart LR SRC[Hello.java
Source Code] --> COMP[javac
Java Compiler] COMP --> BC[Hello.class
Bytecode] BC --> CL[Class Loader
Subsystem] CL --> VERIFY[Bytecode
Verifier] VERIFY --> MEM[Memory Areas
Method, Heap, Stack] MEM --> INT[JVM Interpreter] MEM --> JIT[JIT Compiler
HotSpot Engine] INT --> EXEC[Native Machine
Code Execution] JIT --> EXEC EXEC --> OUT[Program Output] SEC[Security Manager
and Sandbox] -.protects.-> MEM GC[Garbage Collector] -.manages.-> MEM ``` ## 4.3 Buzzword vs. Java Mechanism — Mapping Matrix | Buzzword | Underlying Java Mechanism | Mermaid Node Reference | |:---|:---|:---| | Simple | No pointers, GC, no MI | nodeA | | Object-Oriented | Class, Object, Inheritance | nodeB | | Distributed | `java.net`, RMI, Sockets | nodeC | | Robust | Exception handling, GC | nodeD | | Secure | Sandbox, SecurityManager | nodeE | | Architecture-Neutral | Bytecode + JVM | nodeF | | Portable | Fixed primitive sizes | nodeG | | Interpreted | JVM Interpreter + JIT | nodeH | | High-Performance | JIT, optimized GC | nodeI | | Multithreaded | `Thread`, `synchronized` | nodeJ | | Dynamic | Reflection, class loading | nodeK | ## 4.4 Memory Layout of the JVM (Block Diagram) ```mermaid graph TB subgraph JVM[Java Virtual Machine] subgraph CLS[Class Loader Subsystem] C1[Loading] C2[Linking] C3[Initialization] end subgraph RUNTIME[Runtime Data Areas] MA[Method Area] HEAP[Heap] STK[Java Stack] PC[PC Registers] NM[Native Method Stack] end subgraph ENG[Execution Engine] INT2[Interpreter] JIT2[JIT Compiler] GC2[Garbage Collector] end SECMGR[Security Manager] end ``` > [!TIP] > **Visual Reading Guide:** The `Method Area` and `Heap` are **shared** across threads (multithreaded buzzword), while each thread gets its **own** `Java Stack` and `PC Register`. The `Garbage Collector` operates on the `Heap` (robust + high-performance buzzwords). # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define Java. List any six Java Buzzwords. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** > [!NOTE] > **Java** is a high-level, strongly-typed, general-purpose, object-oriented programming language developed by **James Gosling** at **Sun Microsystems** in 1995, designed with the principle *"Write Once, Run Anywhere"* (WORA) via compilation to platform-independent **bytecode** executed by the **Java Virtual Machine (JVM)**. **Six Java Buzzwords:** 1. **Simple** — Eliminates complex features of C/C++ like pointers and multiple inheritance. 2. **Object-Oriented** — Built entirely on classes and objects, supporting all OOP principles. 3. **Robust** — Strong memory management and exception handling prevent runtime crashes. 4. **Secure** — Programs run inside a JVM sandbox with bytecode verification. 5. **Portable** — Same bytecode runs identically on any OS/hardware with a JVM. 6. **Multithreaded** — Built-in support for concurrent thread execution. *(Any six of the eleven buzzwords are acceptable. 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for the list with brief explanations.)* --- ### Question 2: Differentiate between **Architecture-Neutral** and **Portable** features of Java. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Architecture-Neutral | Portable | |:---|:---|:---| | **Focus** | Independent of **CPU/Processor** (e.g., x86 vs. ARM) | Independent of **Operating System** (e.g., Windows vs. Linux) | | **Achieved by** | Compiling to **bytecode** (no native machine code) | **Fixed-size primitives** and **JVM abstraction** of OS calls | | **Example** | A `.class` file runs on Intel and ARM processors | `int` is always 32 bits in Windows and Linux | | **WORA Component** | Provides the "Run Anywhere" mechanism | Provides the "Write Once" consistency | > [!TIP] > **Key Insight:** Architecture-Neutral refers to **hardware portability**, while Portable refers to **software/environment portability**. Together, they enable WORA. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > Per the KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern, Part B questions are **compulsory with internal choice** — attempt **either** OR. Each 14-mark question typically splits as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a) Explain the following Java Buzzwords with examples: Simple, Object-Oriented, Robust, and Secure. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **1. Simple (2 marks):** Java was designed as a simplified evolution of C++. It removes complex and error-prone features: - **No explicit pointers** (eliminates dangling-pointer bugs). - **No multiple inheritance** (replaced by interfaces to avoid the "diamond problem"). - **Automatic garbage collection** — the JVM reclaims memory of unreachable objects, so programmers never call `free()`. - **Rich standard library** — `java.lang`, `java.util`, etc., reduce the need to write boilerplate code. *Example:* ```java // Java: no pointers, no manual free() String s = new String("KTU"); // GC handles deallocation ``` **2. Object-Oriented (2 marks):** Java is *purely* object-oriented (except for primitive types). Every program is a collection of **classes** and **objects** that interact via **methods**. It supports the four OOP pillars: - **Encapsulation** — data hiding via `private` fields and `public` getters/setters. - **Inheritance** — `class Sub extends Super`. - **Polymorphism** — method overloading and overriding. - **Abstraction** — abstract classes and interfaces. *Example:* ```java class Animal { void sound() { System.out.println("sound"); } } class Dog extends Animal { void sound() { System.out.println("Bark"); } } ``` **3. Robust (1.5 marks):** Robustness is Java's ability to handle erroneous conditions gracefully. Mechanisms: - **Strong type-checking** at compile-time catches many errors early. - **Exception handling** (`try-catch-finally`) prevents abrupt termination. - **No pointers** → no memory corruption. - **Garbage collection** → no memory leaks. **4. Secure (1.5 marks):** Security is critical for networked applications. Mechanisms: - Programs run inside the **JVM sandbox**, isolated from the host OS. - The **Bytecode Verifier** ensures the loaded code does not violate type safety or forge pointers. - **SecurityManager** defines runtime access policies (file, network). - **Public-key encryption** is built into `java.security`. --- **(b) Discuss the features: Architecture-Neutral, Portable, Interpreted, and High-Performance. How do they collectively enable WORA? (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **1. Architecture-Neutral (1.75 marks):** Java compiler (`javac`) produces **bytecode**, an intermediate instruction set, *not* native machine code. The **JVM** is the translator that converts bytecode to host-specific instructions at runtime. This decouples Java from any specific CPU (x86, ARM, RISC-V). **2. Portable (1.75 marks):** Portability extends architecture neutrality to the entire ecosystem: - **Fixed-size primitive types** — `int` is **always 32 bits**, `long` is **always 64 bits**, regardless of the platform. - **JVM abstracts OS-specific calls** — same `System.out.println` works on Windows, macOS, and Linux. - **IEEE 754 floating-point standard** is strictly enforced. - **No implementation-dependent** features are exposed to the programmer. **3. Interpreted (1.75 marks):** The JVM reads and executes bytecode **instruction-by-instruction** via its interpreter. Unlike traditional interpreters, modern JVMs use **Just-In-Time (JIT) compilation**: hot code paths are detected via profiling and compiled to native code for subsequent calls. **4. High-Performance (1.75 marks):** Performance is achieved through: - **JIT compilation** of hot spots (HotSpot engine). - **Optimized garbage collectors** (G1, ZGC, Shenandoah). - **Efficient memory management** with generational heap. - Benchmarks show Java within 10% of C++ in many workloads. **Collective Enablement of WORA:** - *Write Once* — Portable and Architecture-Neutral features guarantee consistent language semantics. - *Run Anywhere* — Interpreted execution + JVM translation make the bytecode universally executable. $$ \text{WORA} = \underbrace{\text{Portable}}_{\text{Write Once}} + \underbrace{\text{Architecture-Neutral + Interpreted}}_{\text{Run Anywhere}} $$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2 `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` **(a) Explain the Distributed and Multithreaded features of Java. Write a Java program to create two threads that print numbers 1 to 5 each. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **1. Distributed Feature (3 marks):** Java was designed with networking built into the core API. It treats network resources the same as local resources. Key libraries: - `java.net` — Provides **Socket** and **ServerSocket** classes for TCP/IP programming. - **RMI (Remote Method Invocation)** — Allows invoking methods on objects running in a *different JVM*, possibly on a different machine. - **URL handling** — Easy access to web resources. - **CORBA support** — Cross-platform distributed object communication (legacy). *Example — A simple URL fetch:* ```java URL u = new URL("https://www.ktu.edu.in"); URLConnection c = u.openConnection(); // distributed resource access ``` **2. Multithreaded Feature (4 marks):** Java has *built-in* support for multithreading, allowing a single program to perform multiple tasks concurrently. A **thread** is the smallest unit of execution within a process. - Threads share the **same memory space** (lightweight). - The `Thread` class and `Runnable` interface provide thread creation. - The `synchronized` keyword ensures **mutual exclusion** for thread safety. - Modern concurrency utilities live in `java.util.concurrent` (ExecutorService, Future, Locks). *Program:* ```java class NumberPrinter extends Thread { public void run() { for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) { System.out.println(Thread.currentThread().getName() + " : " + i); try { Thread.sleep(100); } catch (InterruptedException e) {} } } } public class ThreadDemo { public static void main(String[] args) { NumberPrinter t1 = new NumberPrinter(); NumberPrinter t2 = new NumberPrinter(); t1.setName("Thread-A"); t2.setName("Thread-B"); t1.start(); // creates a new call stack; invokes run() t2.start(); } } ``` **Output (interleaved, order not guaranteed):** ``` Thread-A : 1 Thread-B : 1 Thread-A : 2 Thread-B : 2 ... ``` **Note:** Always call `start()` to begin a new thread; calling `run()` directly executes in the *current* thread — a common KTU trick question. --- **(b) With neat diagrams, explain the Java execution model. Discuss the role of the JIT compiler in achieving high performance. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Java Execution Model (4 marks):** The Java execution model consists of **three major stages**: 1. **Compilation:** The source file `Hello.java` is compiled by `javac` to produce `Hello.class` containing **bytecode**. 2. **Loading & Verification:** The JVM's **ClassLoader** loads the `.class` file. The **Bytecode Verifier** checks it for type-safety and security violations. 3. **Execution:** The **Execution Engine** interprets the bytecode. Hot code paths are compiled to native code by the **JIT compiler**. **Block Diagram of the Java Execution Model:** ``` [Source Code] → [Java Compiler (javac)] → [Bytecode (.class)] ↓ [Native Code] ← [JIT Compiler (Hot code)] ← [Bytecode Verifier] ↑ [ClassLoader] ↓ [Runtime Data Areas] (Method, Heap, Stacks) ``` **Role of JIT Compiler in High Performance (3 marks):** The **Just-In-Time (JIT) compiler** is a critical performance feature: - **Profiling:** The JVM monitors which bytecode instructions are executed most frequently (the "hot spots"). - **Native Compilation:** When a method exceeds a threshold, the JIT compiles it to **native machine code** and caches it in memory. - **Optimization:** The JIT applies advanced optimizations — **inlining, dead-code elimination, loop unrolling, escape analysis** — that are not possible with pure interpretation. - **Result:** The next time the hot method is called, it executes *natively*, achieving **near-C++ performance**. $$ \text{Performance Gain} = \frac{\text{Interpreted Execution Time}}{\text{JIT-Compiled Native Execution Time}} \gg 1 $$ This is why Java is classified as both **Interpreted** *and* **High-Performance** — the two are not contradictory in modern JVMs. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in the KTU Valuation Key:** > 1. **Confusing Architecture-Neutral with Portable** — These are *separate* buzzwords with different scopes. (Lose 1-2 marks) > 2. **Writing only buzzword names without explanation** — A bare list gets **zero marks**. Each buzzword requires a *one-line definition + an example*. > 3. **Stating "Java is compiled" only** — Java is **both compiled and interpreted** (hybrid model). Saying only one is a half-answer. > 4. **Writing `run()` instead of `start()`** in multithreading code — `run()` executes in the *current* thread; only `start()` creates a *new* thread. > 5. **Forgetting the year 1995 / James Gosling** — Often asked as a 1-mark sub-part; missing it loses easy marks. > 6. **Confusing "Robust" with "Secure"** — *Robust* = handles errors gracefully; *Secure* = protected from external threats. They are distinct. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Revision — Memorize These Points Before Entering the Exam Hall:** - ☐ Java was developed by **James Gosling** at **Sun Microsystems** in **1995**; currently owned by **Oracle**. - ☐ The original name was **"Oak"** (renamed to Java due to trademark). - ☐ The phrase **"Write Once, Run Anywhere" (WORA)** encapsulates Java's philosophy. - ☐ Java is **both compiled and interpreted** (hybrid execution model). - ☐ The **11 Java Buzzwords** are: Simple, Object-Oriented, Distributed, Robust, Secure, Architecture-Neutral, Portable, Interpreted, High-Performance, Multithreaded, Dynamic. - ☐ **Architecture-Neutral** → CPU-independent (bytecode). **Portable** → OS-independent (fixed primitive sizes). - ☐ **Robust** = no pointers + GC + exception handling. **Secure** = sandbox + bytecode verifier + SecurityManager. - ☐ **JIT (Just-In-Time) compiler** is the secret behind Java's **High-Performance** despite being interpreted. - ☐ **Multithreaded**: `start()` creates a new thread; `run()` is just an ordinary method call. - ☐ **Dynamic**: Achieved through **reflection** (`Class.forName()`) and **dynamic class loading**. - ☐ The **`.java`** extension holds source code; the **`.class`** extension holds bytecode. - ☐ The JVM consists of: **ClassLoader + Bytecode Verifier + Execution Engine (Interpreter + JIT) + Runtime Data Areas + Garbage Collector**. > **End of Notes — Module 1: Introduction to Java - Java Buzzwords**

topics.title: Java Program Structure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT414 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Java Program Structure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Java Program** is a collection of one or more *classes*, where execution begins from a special method named `main()`. The **Java Program Structure** refers to the standardized, hierarchical organization of source code components — *package declaration, import statements, class definition, members (fields/variables), methods, and statements* — that the Java compiler (`javac`) and the Java Virtual Machine (`JVM`) expect in every valid `.java` source file.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEECT414 – OOP)**, Module 1 establishes this structure as the *scaffolding* upon which object-oriented concepts (classes, objects, encapsulation) are later built. The compiler enforces that any *stand-alone* (independent) Java program must contain a public class with a `public static void main(String[] args)` method signature — this is the **entry point contract** of the JVM.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> *"Object Oriented Programming Concepts – Introduction to Objects, Classes, Methods, Program Structure in Java."*
> The phrase "Program Structure" explicitly demands coverage of comments, identifiers, keywords, data types, variables, operators, control flow, and the anatomy of the smallest runnable Java program.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Recipe Card" 🍳

Imagine a **recipe card** in a professional kitchen:

| Java Element | Kitchen Analogy |
|---|---|
| **File name** (e.g., `Hello.java`) | The name printed on the top of the recipe card |
| **`class` keyword** | The category box (e.g., "Desserts", "Main Course") |
| **`main()` method** | The "Step 1" — where the chef *must* start cooking |
| **Statements** | Individual cooking instructions ("Add 2 cups of flour") |
| **Comments** (`//` or `/* */`) | Chef's personal notes scribbled in the margin |
| **Curly braces `{ }`** | The "BEGIN" and "END" markers of a recipe step |
| **Compiler (`javac`)** | The kitchen supervisor checking that the recipe is valid |
| **JVM** | The actual kitchen environment (gas, oven, utensils) that executes the recipe |

Just as no chef starts cooking from Step 5, the JVM will *always* begin execution at `main()`. The recipe (class) must be *public* (accessible) and its filename must *exactly match* the class name (case-sensitive).

## 1.3 The Five Logical Sections of a Java Program

> [!NOTE]
> While Java is flexible, a professionally written KTU-style program follows this canonical skeleton:

1. **Package Declaration** *(optional, but mandatory in large projects)*
   ```java
   package com.ktu.student.lab1;
   ```
2. **Import Statements** *(optional)*
   ```java
   import java.util.Scanner;
   ```
3. **Class Declaration** *(mandatory)*
   ```java
   public class Hello { ... }
   ```
4. **Class Members** *(fields + methods)*
   ```java
   int marks; void display() { ... }
   ```
5. **`main()` Method** *(mandatory for execution)*
   ```java
   public static void main(String[] args) { ... }
   ```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strict Rule:** The **filename must equal the public class name** with a `.java` extension. Saving `public class Hello` inside `Program.java` is a *compilation error* in Java — this is a very common **KTU viva question**.

## 1.4 Tokens — The Smallest Meaningful Units

A Java compiler breaks source code into **tokens**, the smallest indivisible units. They are:

- **Keywords** — reserved words (`class`, `public`, `static`, `void`, `if`, `else`)
- **Identifiers** — programmer-defined names (class names, variable names, method names)
- **Literals** — fixed values (`100`, `3.14`, `'A'`, `"Hello"`)
- **Operators** — symbols that perform actions (`+`, `-`, `=`, `==`)
- **Separators / Punctuators** — `;`, `,`, `{`, `}`, `(`, `)`, `[`, `]`
- **Comments** — `// single-line`, `/* multi-line */`, `/** documentation */`

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Java Source File — Step-by-Step Breakdown

Let us dissect the canonical *Hello World* program that every KTU lab record begins with:

```java
// File: Hello.java
// Author: Ananya R., Roll No: 45
// Date  : 18-08-2025
// Aim   : Demonstrate basic Java program structure

package com.ktu.lab1;                  // (1) Package declaration

import java.lang.System;               // (2) Import (actually redundant — java.lang is auto-imported)

public class Hello {                   // (3) Public class declaration (filename MUST be Hello.java)

    // ----- Class-level variable (Field) -----
    static String college = "KTU Kerala"; // (4) Static field — single copy shared by all objects

    // ----- Instance method -----
    void greet(String name) {           // (5) Method with one parameter
        System.out.println("Hello, " + name + "!");
    }

    // ----- Program entry point -----
    public static void main(String[] args) { // (6) THE main method
        Hello obj = new Hello();        // (7) Object creation
        obj.greet("Ananya");            // (8) Method call on object
        System.out.println("Welcome to " + college);
    }
}
```

### Step-wise Logical Analysis

- **Step 1 — Package Declaration:**
  A *package* is a namespace that logically groups related classes. The line `package com.ktu.lab1;` places this class inside a folder hierarchy `com/ktu/lab1/`. If omitted, the class belongs to the *default package* (discouraged in production).

- **Step 2 — Import Statement:**
  Tells the compiler to bring in pre-defined classes from other packages. `java.lang.*` is **auto-imported**; everything else must be explicit. Wildcards (`import java.util.*;`) import all classes of that package in a single line.

- **Step 3 — Class Declaration:**
  The keyword `class` introduces a *user-defined type*. The modifier `public` means the class is visible to all other classes anywhere. **One source file may contain at most one `public` class**, and the filename must match it.

- **Step 4 — Field Declaration:**
  A *field* (also called *attribute* or *data member*) holds the state of an object. `static` fields belong to the class, not to any individual object — only **one copy** exists in memory, regardless of how many objects are created.

- **Step 5 — Method Declaration:**
  A *method* defines behavior. Its syntax is:
  `<access-modifier> <return-type> <method-name> (<parameter-list>) { body }`
  The parameter `String name` is a *local variable* — accessible only inside `greet()`.

- **Step 6 — The `main()` Method — JVM's Contract:**
  The signature must **exactly** be:
  `public static void main(String[] args)`
  - `public` — JVM (outside the package) must call it.
  - `static` — JVM can call it **without creating an object**.
  - `void` — it does not return any value to the OS.
  - `String[] args` — receives *command-line arguments*.

- **Step 7 — Object Instantiation:**
  `Hello obj = new Hello();` uses the `new` operator to allocate memory in the **heap** and invokes a constructor. The reference `obj` is stored in the **stack**.

- **Step 8 — Method Invocation:**
  The dot operator (`.`) accesses members through a reference. `obj.greet("Ananya")` passes the literal `"Ananya"` to the `name` parameter.

## 2.2 Why This Specific Structure?

| Component | Why It Exists | Engineering Real-World Mapping |
|---|---|---|
| `class` | Enforces encapsulation from the very first line | A *module* in a software system |
| `public` access | Controls scope of visibility across packages | *Public API* of a microservice |
| `static` | Allows runtime invocation without instantiation | *Utility functions* (e.g., `Math.sqrt`) |
| `main()` | Standardizes the entry point across OS platforms | The *bootstrap function* in embedded C |
| `String[] args` | Enables CLI parameter passing | *argv* in C, `sys.argv` in Python |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:**
> The same structural discipline is what makes Java suitable for **Android development**, **enterprise backend services (Spring Boot)**, and **embedded systems (Java ME)**. The compiler can statically check the entire structure *before* the code ever runs — a major reason for Java's reputation in safety-critical applications like **banking ATMs** and **stock trading platforms**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Java Program Structure

| # | Element | Mandatory? | Syntax Example | Common KTU Pitfall |
|:-:|---|:-:|---|---|
| 1 | Package declaration | No (best practice: Yes) | `package com.ktu.lab1;` | Forgetting the **semicolon** `;` |
| 2 | Import statement | No | `import java.util.Scanner;` | Importing a class that doesn't exist |
| 3 | Public class | **Yes** (for executable) | `public class Hello { }` | Mismatched filename |
| 4 | Class fields | No | `int rollNo;` | Declaring inside `main` as field by mistake |
| 5 | `main()` method | **Yes** (for execution) | `public static void main(String[] args)` | Writing `void main()` (missing `static`) |
| 6 | `String[] args` parameter | **Yes** (for execution) | `String args[]` also accepted | Renaming it causes compilation error |
| 7 | Statements inside `main` | **Yes** | `System.out.println("Hi");` | Missing `;` at end |
| 8 | Comments | No | `// comment` or `/* comment */` | Nested multi-line comments are illegal |
| 9 | Case sensitivity | **Always** | `Main ≠ main` | Writing `Public` instead of `public` |

## 2.4 Java Identifier Naming Conventions (KTU Frequently Tested)

| Identifier Type | Convention | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Class name | PascalCase (capital first letter, every word capitalized) | `StudentRecord`, `HelloWorld` |
| Method name | camelCase (first word lowercase, rest capitalized) | `calculateTotal()`, `getName()` |
| Variable name | camelCase | `rollNumber`, `totalMarks` |
| Constant | ALL_CAPS with underscores | `final int MAX = 100;` |
| Package name | all lowercase, dot-separated | `com.ktu.lab1` |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Alert:** Identifiers **cannot** start with a digit, cannot contain whitespace, cannot use reserved keywords, and **cannot** contain symbols except `_` and `$`. Writing `int 2marks = 50;` is a *compilation error* and a guaranteed **zero mark** in viva.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Walkthrough — From Source Code to Output

Java's famous *Write Once, Run Anywhere* promise is delivered by a **two-step execution model**: **Compilation** followed by **Interpretation**.

### 3.1.1 Compilation Phase

The Java compiler (`javac`) reads `Hello.java` and produces `Hello.class` — a file containing **bytecode** (instructions for the JVM, not for any physical CPU).

Input :  `Hello.java`  (source file — human-readable)

Process:
1. Lexical Analysis — source code is split into tokens (`class`, `Hello`, `{`, `public`, …).
2. Syntax Analysis — tokens are arranged into a parse tree following Java grammar.
3. Semantic Analysis — type checking, scope resolution, identifier binding.
4. Bytecode Generation — produces **platform-independent** `.class` file.

Output: `Hello.class`  (bytecode — JVM-readable)

### 3.1.2 Execution Phase

The JVM loads `Hello.class` and:

1. **Class Loader** reads `.class` into memory.
2. **Bytecode Verifier** checks for illegal operations (security feature).
3. **Interpreter / JIT Compiler** translates bytecode into native machine code.
4. Execution starts at `main()` and proceeds statement by statement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The bytecode is **not** machine code. It is an *intermediate representation*. This is why the same `.class` file runs on Windows, Linux, and macOS without recompilation.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Tracing a Simple Program

Consider the following program (a typical KTU 3-mark derivation question):

```java
public class Trace {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        int a = 5, b = 10;
        int sum = a + b;
        System.out.println("Sum = " + sum);
    }
}
```

### Step-by-Step Trace

**Step 1 — Tokenization**

The compiler recognizes the following tokens:
- Keywords : `public`, `class`, `static`, `void`, `int`
- Identifiers : `Trace`, `main`, `args`, `a`, `b`, `sum`
- Literals  : `5`, `10`
- Operators  : `=`, `+`
- Separators : `(`, `)`, `{`, `}`, `[`, `]`, `;`, `,`

**Step 2 — Variable Allocation in Memory (Stack Frame of `main`)**

Let us walk through the stack frame step by step.

At line `int a = 5, b = 10;` the stack frame contains:

| Variable | Memory Address (conceptual) | Value | Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| `a` | `0x100` | `5` | `int` |
| `b` | `0x104` | `10` | `int` |

At line `int sum = a + b;` the evaluation proceeds as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{sum} &= a + b \\
&= 5 + 10 \\
&= 15
\end{aligned}
$$

> The right-hand side `a + b` is evaluated first, then assigned to `sum`. The stack frame becomes:

| Variable | Memory Address | Value | Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| `a` | `0x100` | `5` | `int` |
| `b` | `0x104` | `10` | `int` |
| `sum` | `0x108` | `15` | `int` |

At line `System.out.println("Sum = " + sum);` the `+` operator performs **string concatenation** when one operand is a `String`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{output} &= \text{"Sum = "} \; \| \; \text{sum} \\
&= \text{"Sum = "} \; \| \; 15 \\
&= \text{"Sum = 15"}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Output on the Console:**

```
Sum = 15
```

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — `main()` Method Modifiers Explained

The phrase `public static void main(String[] args)` contains four critical modifiers. Each has a specific role:

| Modifier | Position | Why It Is Needed | What Happens If Removed |
|---|---|---|---|
| `public` | Access | JVM (outside the class) must be able to call it | `Main method not found in class` runtime error |
| `static` | Non-access | JVM can call it without instantiating the class | `Non-static method cannot be referenced from static context` |
| `void` | Return type | Indicates no value is returned to the OS | If `int`, JVM *ignores* the returned value (warning shown) |
| `String[] args` | Parameter | Receives command-line arguments as a string array | Program still runs, but `args.length == 0` |

## 3.4 Command-Line Argument Processing

When you run:

```
java Trace 10 20 30
```

The JVM constructs the array:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{args[0]} &= \text{"10"} \\
\text{args[1]} &= \text{"20"} \\
\text{args[2]} &= \text{"30"} \\
\text{args.length} &= 3
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial point:** All command-line arguments are received as **Strings**, even if they look like numbers. To add them as integers, you must use `Integer.parseInt(args[0])` — a classic KTU 14-marker question.

**Worked Example — Sum of Two CLI Numbers**

```java
public class AddCLI {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        int x = Integer.parseInt(args[0]);
        int y = Integer.parseInt(args[1]);
        int z = x + y;
        System.out.println("Sum = " + z);
    }
}
```

Execution: `java AddCLI 25 75` → Output: `Sum = 100`

## 3.5 Java vs C/C++ — Structural Differences (High-Yield Comparison)

| Feature | C/C++ Program | Java Program |
|---|---|---|
| File extension | `.c` / `.cpp` | `.java` |
| Compilation | Direct to machine code (`.exe`) | To bytecode (`.class`) |
| Execution | Native OS | Inside JVM |
| Entry point | `int main()` | `public static void main(String[] args)` |
| Header files | `#include <stdio.h>` | `import java.util.*;` |
| Memory management | Manual (`malloc`/`free`) | Automatic (Garbage Collector) |
| Platform dependency | Platform-specific binary | Platform-independent bytecode |
| Pointers | Direct pointer arithmetic | No pointers; only references |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This comparison table is a **favourite KTU question** — both as a 3-mark Part A definition and as a 7-mark Part B sub-question. Memorize the *differences in execution model* and *memory management*.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Anatomy of a Java Source File

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Hello.java Source File] --> B[Package Declaration Line]
    A --> C[Import Statements Block]
    A --> D[Public Class Declaration]
    D --> E[Class Members Block]
    E --> F[Static Fields Section]
    E --> G[Instance Methods Section]
    E --> H[Main Method Block]
    H --> I[Object Creation Statement]
    H --> J[Method Invocation Statement]
    H --> K[Print Statement]
```

> *Figure 4.1 — Hierarchical structure of a typical `.java` source file used in KTU lab programs.*

## 4.2 Process Flow — Compilation and Execution Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Hello.java] -->|javac compiler| B[Hello.class Bytecode]
    B --> C{Platform}
    C -->|Windows JVM| D[Native Win Code]
    C -->|Linux JVM| E[Native Linux Code]
    C -->|MacOS JVM| F[Native Mac Code]
    D --> G[Output: Hello Ananya]
    E --> G
    F --> G
```

> *Figure 4.2 — Java's "Write Once, Run Anywhere" workflow. The same `.class` file executes on any operating system that has a compliant JVM.*

## 4.3 Block Architecture — JVM Internal Subsystems

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph JVM
        L[Class Loader Subsystem]
        M[Bytecode Verifier]
        N[Interpreter and JIT Compiler]
        O[Runtime Data Areas - Stack and Heap]
    end
    L --> M
    M --> N
    N --> O
```

> *Figure 4.3 — Internal architecture of the Java Virtual Machine. The class loader brings the `.class` file into memory; the verifier ensures safety; the interpreter/JIT converts bytecode to native instructions; the runtime data areas hold stack frames and heap objects.*

## 4.4 Class-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    cls[Hello Class] --> fld1[Static Field: college]
    cls --> m1[Method: greet String]
    cls --> m2[Method: main String Array]
    m2 --> obj[Object: obj of type Hello]
    obj --> m1
```

> *Figure 4.4 — Functional mapping of class members, showing how the `main()` method creates an object that, in turn, calls the instance method `greet()`.*

## 4.5 Memory Layout Diagram (Conceptual)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Stack
        S1[args reference]
        S2[obj reference 0x200]
    end
    subgraph Heap
        H1[Object of Hello at 0x200]
        H1 --> H2[college field value KTU Kerala]
    end
```

> *Figure 4.5 — Conceptual memory layout: the `main()` method's stack frame holds a *reference* to the object that actually lives in the heap. This is the foundation for understanding Java's pass-by-reference semantics in later modules.*

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q1. List and briefly explain any four tokens of Java.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Tokens are the smallest individual units in a Java program recognized by the compiler.

1. **Keywords** — Reserved words with predefined meanings in Java. Example: `class`, `public`, `static`, `void`, `if`.
2. **Identifiers** — Names given by the programmer to classes, methods, or variables. Example: `Hello`, `rollNumber`, `calculateTotal`.
3. **Literals** — Constant values assigned to variables. Example: `100` (integer literal), `"KTU"` (string literal), `3.14` (floating literal).
4. **Operators** — Symbols that perform operations. Example: arithmetic `+`, `-`, relational `==`, `>`.
5. **Separators** — Symbols used to structure code. Example: `;`, `{}`, `()`, `,`.
6. **Comments** — Non-executing text for documentation. Example: `// single line`, `/* multi-line */`.

*(Any four explained with examples = 3 marks)*

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q2. What is the significance of the `public static void main(String[] args)` method in Java? Why is each keyword used?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

This method is the **entry point** of every stand-alone Java application. The JVM begins program execution from this method, and without it the program cannot be executed directly.

- `public` — An access specifier that allows the JVM (which lies outside the class) to invoke this method.
- `static` — Enables the JVM to call this method **without creating an object** of the class, saving memory and time at startup.
- `void` — The return type; `main()` does not return any value to the operating system after execution.
- `String[] args` — A parameter that receives **command-line arguments** as an array of strings, allowing external input at runtime.

*(Keyword-by-keyword justification = 3 marks)*

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 — Modified for Practice]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the general structure of a Java program with a neat diagram. List the function of each section. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Write a Java program that accepts three numbers from the command line and prints their average. Show the compilation and execution steps. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) General Structure of a Java Program (7 marks)**

A Java program has the following sections, which must appear in the prescribed order:

| Section | Purpose | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Package Declaration | Groups related classes in a namespace | `package com.ktu.lab1;` |
| Import Statements | Brings in classes from other packages | `import java.util.Scanner;` |
| Class Declaration | Defines a new user-defined type | `public class Hello { }` |
| Variable Declarations | Holds the state of objects | `int roll; String name;` |
| Methods | Define behavior; one must be `main()` | `void display() { }` |
| `main()` Method | Entry point of execution | `public static void main(String[] a) { }` |

**Neat Diagram:**

```
+---------------------------------------+
|  package declaration                  |
+---------------------------------------+
|  import statements                    |
+---------------------------------------+
|  public class ClassName {             |
|      <field declarations>             |
|      <method declarations>            |
|      <main method>                    |
|  }                                    |
+---------------------------------------+
```

**Why each section is needed:**

- The **package** prevents naming conflicts in large projects.
- The **import** avoids fully-qualified class names every time.
- The **class** is the smallest executable unit in Java.
- **Variables and methods** are the data and behavior of the class.
- **`main()`** is mandated by the JVM for execution.

**(b) Program for Average of Three Numbers (7 marks)**

```java
public class Average {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        double a = Double.parseDouble(args[0]);
        double b = Double.parseDouble(args[1]);
        double c = Double.parseDouble(args[2]);
        double avg = (a + b + c) / 3.0;
        System.out.println("Average = " + avg);
    }
}
```

**Compilation Step:**

```
javac Average.java
```

This produces `Average.class` (bytecode).

**Execution Step:**

```
java Average 10 20 30
```

**Output:**

```
Average = 20.0
```

**Explanation of logic:**

- `args[0]`, `args[1]`, `args[2]` are received as **Strings**.
- `Double.parseDouble()` converts each to a `double` value.
- The arithmetic mean is computed as `(a + b + c) / 3.0` — note the `3.0` to force floating-point division.
- The result is concatenated with the label and printed.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Differentiate between **C/C++** and **Java** program structures. List any seven points. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** What are Java identifiers? State the rules for naming identifiers with suitable examples. Write a short note on Java naming conventions. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) C/C++ vs Java Program Structure (7 marks)**

| # | Feature | C/C++ | Java |
|:-:|---|---|---|
| 1 | File extension | `.c` / `.cpp` | `.java` |
| 2 | Compilation output | Native machine code (`.exe`) | Bytecode (`.class`) |
| 3 | Execution | Directly on OS hardware | Inside the JVM |
| 4 | Memory management | Manual (`malloc` / `free` / `new` / `delete`) | Automatic via Garbage Collector |
| 5 | Pointers | Direct pointer arithmetic allowed | No pointers; only safe references |
| 6 | Header / Library inclusion | `#include <stdio.h>` | `import java.util.*;` |
| 7 | Platform dependency | Platform-specific binary | Platform-independent bytecode (WORA) |
| 8 | Entry function | `int main()` | `public static void main(String[] args)` |

*(Any 7 well-explained points = 7 marks)*

**(b) Java Identifiers, Rules, and Naming Conventions (7 marks)**

An **identifier** is a programmer-defined name used to identify a class, method, variable, or label in a Java program.

**Rules for Naming Identifiers:**

1. Identifiers may contain **letters (A–Z, a–z)**, **digits (0–9)**, **underscore `_`**, and **dollar sign `$`**.
2. They **must not begin with a digit**. Example: `2marks` is *invalid*; `marks2` is *valid*.
3. They **cannot be a reserved keyword**. Example: `class`, `int`, `static` cannot be used as identifiers.
4. They are **case-sensitive**. `Roll` and `roll` are two different identifiers.
5. **No whitespace** or special characters (`@`, `#`, `!`, `-`) are allowed.
6. There is **no limit** on the length of an identifier (practical limit ≈ 2 GB).
7. The `$` sign is technically allowed but **discouraged** as it is reserved for machine-generated names.

**Valid Examples:** `rollNumber`, `_count`, `$temp`, `total_marks`
**Invalid Examples:** `2ndYear`, `my-name`, `class`, `int value`

**Java Naming Conventions (Industry Standard):**

- **Classes** → PascalCase : `StudentRecord`, `HelloWorld`
- **Methods/Variables** → camelCase : `getName()`, `rollNumber`
- **Constants** → ALL_CAPS_WITH_UNDERSCORE : `final int MAX = 100;`
- **Packages** → all lowercase : `com.ktu.lab1`
- **Booleans** → start with `is`/`has` : `isActive`, `hasValue`

*(Rules 4 marks + Examples 2 marks + Conventions 1 mark = 7 marks)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Zones in "Java Program Structure" Questions:**
>
> 1. **Filename-Class Mismatch (-2 marks):** Writing `public class Hello` but saving the file as `Program.java` will cause *compilation failure*. Examiners check this implicitly through output.
> 2. **Missing `static` in `main()` (-1 to -2 marks):** Writing `public void main(...)` without `static` is a very common slip.
> 3. **Confusing `String[] args` with `String args[]` semantics:** Both are syntactically valid in declaration, but the first is **preferred style**; the second is C-style and *may be flagged* in viva.
> 4. **Forgetting `Integer.parseInt` for CLI numerics (-1 mark):** Adding `args[0] + args[1]` will produce **string concatenation** (`"10" + "20" = "1020"`), not the integer `30`. This is a classic KTU trap.
> 5. **Writing `print` instead of `println`:** `print` does not add a newline; output may merge with the next prompt.
> 6. **Misspelling keywords:** `Stactic` instead of `static`, `Stirng` instead of `String` — these are *compilation errors*, not warnings.
> 7. **Ignoring case sensitivity:** Writing `Main` (capital M) when the class is `main` — the JVM will *not* find the entry point.
> 8. **Using reserved keywords as identifiers:** Naming a variable `int` will instantly fail compilation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✍️

- A Java program is a **collection of classes**; execution always begins at `main()`.
- The **filename must match** the public class name (case-sensitive), with a `.java` extension.
- The `main()` signature is **strict**: `public static void main(String[] args)`. Any deviation causes a runtime error.
- A `.java` file can have **only one `public` class**, but may contain several non-public classes.
- Java programs are **compiled to bytecode**, not machine code; the **JVM** executes the bytecode.
- **Tokens** are the smallest units: keywords, identifiers, literals, operators, separators, comments.
- **Identifiers** must start with a letter, `_`, or `$`; keywords are reserved and case-sensitive.
- Java is **case-sensitive** — `Main` ≠ `main` ≠ `MAIN`.
- **Command-line arguments** are always received as `String` arrays; use `Integer.parseInt` / `Double.parseDouble` for numeric conversion.
- The **naming conventions** are: PascalCase for classes, camelCase for methods/variables, ALL_CAPS for constants, lowercase for packages.
- A **package** groups related classes; **imports** bring in classes from other packages.
- **Comments** (`//`, `/* */`, `/** */`) are ignored by the compiler and used for documentation.
- **Memory model**: Local variables (including parameters) live on the **stack**; objects live on the **heap**; references connect the two.
- Java supports **automatic garbage collection** — no manual `free()` or `delete`.
- Java's motto: **"Write Once, Run Anywhere (WORA)"** — possible because of platform-independent bytecode.

> **End of Module 1 — Java Program Structure Notes**
> *Prepared per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 (NEP 2020 / 2024 Scheme)*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Java Compiler
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT414 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Java Compiler # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Java Compiler (javac):** A Java compiler is a system software program that translates Java source code (`.java` files) written by a programmer into an intermediate, platform-independent representation known as **Java Bytecode** (`.class` files). The reference implementation shipped with the Java Development Kit (JDK) is called `javac`, developed originally by Sun Microsystems and now maintained under the OpenJDK community. In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme (PEECT414 – Object Oriented Programming), a Java compiler is the **first stage** of the two-stage translation mechanism of the Java platform, the second stage being handled by the **Java Virtual Machine (JVM)** during execution. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Analogy — The Universal Translator** > Imagine a multilingual diplomat (a programmer) who wants to address audiences in 50 different countries. Instead of learning all 50 languages, the diplomat writes a speech in a special *Universal Diplomatic Language* (Java Bytecode). A local translator in each country (the JVM) then converts that universal speech into the local language at the venue. The **Java Compiler** is the office that converts the diplomat's handwritten notes (`.java` source) into the *Universal Diplomatic Language* (`.class` bytecode). This is the famous **"Write Once, Run Anywhere" (WORA)** philosophy of Java. A simpler, engineering-friendly analogy: think of the Java compiler as a **factory assembly line's first robotic arm**. The raw materials (source code) enter, get welded, cut, and stamped (lexical analysis, parsing, semantic analysis, optimization) and roll off the belt as a uniform, semi-finished product (bytecode) — which any downstream machine (JVM) on any platform can finish. ## 1.3 Key Terminology to Remember - **Source Code (`.java`):** Human-readable program text written in Java syntax. - **Bytecode (`.class`):** A compact, **stack-based**, platform-neutral instruction set executed by the JVM. - **`javac`:** The official Java compiler command-line tool bundled in the JDK. - **JIT (Just-In-Time) Compiler:** A sub-component of the JVM that further translates bytecode into native machine code at runtime for performance. - **Platform Independence:** The property that the same `.class` file can run on Windows, Linux, or macOS without recompilation. ## 1.4 Why Java Has a Two-Stage Compilation > [!NOTE] > Traditional compiled languages (C, C++) compile source code **directly** to platform-specific machine code. Interpreted languages (Python, PHP) translate line-by-line at runtime. Java sits in the middle: it is **compiled-then-interpreted**. The first stage (Java Compiler) produces portable bytecode; the second stage (JVM) interprets/JIT-compiles that bytecode. This hybrid model gives Java both **portability** (from compilation) and **runtime flexibility** (from interpretation/JIT).
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 The Java Compilation Pipeline — Phase by Phase When you invoke `javac MyProgram.java`, the compiler performs a sequence of well-defined phases. Each phase transforms an intermediate representation (IR) of the program into a richer one. ### Phase 1 — Lexical Analysis (Tokenization) - The source file is read character by character. - The **lexical analyzer (lexer / scanner)** groups characters into meaningful **lexemes** and assigns them **tokens**. - Example: `int x = 10;` → tokens: `int` (keyword), `x` (identifier), `=` (operator), `10` (integer literal), `;` (separator). - Whitespace and comments are stripped. ### Phase 2 — Syntax Analysis (Parsing) - The token stream is fed to a **parser** which builds a **parse tree** (concrete syntax tree) and then a more abstract **Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)**. - The parser uses Java's **LL(k) / LALR grammar** to verify syntactic correctness. - If the syntax is invalid, a `javac` syntax error is reported (e.g., missing semicolon). ### Phase 3 — Semantic Analysis - The AST is traversed; **symbol tables** are built for variables, methods, and classes. - **Type checking** ensures type compatibility (e.g., you cannot assign a string to an `int`). - **Scope rules** are enforced. - **Constant folding** and minor optimizations occur here. ### Phase 4 — Intermediate Representation (IR) Generation - The validated AST is lowered into an **intermediate representation** (in modern `javac`, this is a typed AST called **JCT (Java Compiler Tree)** with optional bytecode-like IR). - Optimizations like **dead-code elimination** and **inlining** may be applied. ### Phase 5 — Bytecode Generation - The IR is converted to **Java Bytecode** (`.class` file). - Bytecode is a **stack-based instruction set**: each instruction is 1-byte opcode + 0+ operands. - The `.class` file follows a strict **Class File Format** specified in the JVM Specification: - Magic number: `0xCAFEBABE` - Version numbers (major/minor) - Constant pool (literals, class/method/field references) - Access flags - This class, Super class - Interfaces, Fields, Methods, Attributes - Each method's bytecode is stored as a `Code` attribute containing: `max_stack`, `max_locals`, and the actual bytecode array. ### Phase 6 — Writing the `.class` File - The fully assembled `.class` file is written to disk, ready for the JVM to load and execute. ## 2.2 High-Yield Concept: Two Compilers in Java | Aspect | `javac` (Java Compiler) | JIT Compiler (Inside JVM) | |---|---|---| | **Stage** | Pre-execution (Ahead-of-Time) | Runtime (Just-in-Time) | | **Input** | `.java` source file | `.class` bytecode | | **Output** | `.class` bytecode (platform-independent) | Native machine code (platform-specific) | | **Tool** | `javac.exe` (JDK) | `HotSpot C1/C2`, `Graal` (JVM) | | **Optimization Focus** | Compile-time: syntax, type-safety, basic optimizations | Runtime: inlining, escape analysis, loop unrolling | | **Triggered By** | Programmer command | Method being "hot" (called many times) | ## 2.3 Key Components of the JDK / JRE / JVM Ecosystem > [!IMPORTANT] > **JVM (Java Virtual Machine)** — The runtime engine that loads, verifies, and executes bytecode. It contains the **interpreter** and the **JIT compiler**. > > **JRE (Java Runtime Environment)** = JVM + standard class libraries + other runtime files. It is what end-users install to *run* Java applications. > > **JDK (Java Development Kit)** = JRE + development tools (`javac`, `jdb`, `javadoc`, `jar`). It is what *developers* install to *build* Java applications. ## 2.4 KTU Cheat Sheet — Compilation Commands | Command | Purpose | Example | |---|---|---| | `javac FileName.java` | Compile a single source file | `javac Hello.java` | | `javac File1.java File2.java` | Compile multiple files | `javac A.java B.java` | | `javac -d output *.java` | Compile all `.java` files into a specific output directory | `javac -d bin *.java` | | `javac -version` | Display compiler version | `javac -version` | | `java ClassName` | Run the compiled bytecode | `java Hello` | | `javap -c ClassName` | Disassemble a `.class` file to view bytecode | `javap -c Hello` | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Android Development:** Android's `dx`/`d8` compiler evolved from `javac`; understanding Java compilation helps in Android APK analysis. - **Reverse Engineering & Security:** Tools like `javap`, `JD-GUI`, and `CFR decompiler` work on `.class` files. Knowing the class file format aids malware analysis. - **Build Tool Internals:** Tools like `Maven`, `Gradle`, and `Ant` all invoke `javac` underneath; understanding the compiler pipeline is essential for troubleshooting build failures. - **Performance Tuning:** Knowing when JIT kicks in (HotSpot thresholds) helps in writing high-performance server-side Java.
# 3. Step-by-Step Process Walkthrough & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 End-to-End Compilation: A Worked Example Let us trace a complete Java program through the entire compilation pipeline. **Input Source File: `Hello.java`** ```java public class Hello { public static void main(String[] args) { int a = 5; int b = 10; int sum = a + b; System.out.println("Sum = " + sum); } } ``` ### Step 1 — Invocation The developer opens a terminal and executes: ``` javac Hello.java ``` The OS locates `javac` in the JDK's `bin` directory and hands it the file `Hello.java`. ### Step 2 — Lexical Analysis Output The lexer produces the token stream: | # | Token Type | Lexeme | |---|---|---| | 1 | `PUBLIC` | public | | 2 | `CLASS` | class | | 3 | `IDENTIFIER` | Hello | | 4 | `LBRACE` | { | | 5 | `PUBLIC` | public | | 6 | `STATIC` | static | | 7 | `VOID` | void | | 8 | `IDENTIFIER` | main | | 9 | `LPAREN` | ( | | 10 | `LBRACKET` | [ | | 11 | `IDENTIFIER` | String | | 12 | `RBRACKET` | ] | | 13 | `IDENTIFIER` | args | | 14 | `RPAREN` | ) | | ... | ... | ... | > [!NOTE] > Comments and whitespace are discarded.
