| **Use Case** | General logical conditions | When both sides must execute (e.g., method calls) |
| **Performance** | Faster (skips unnecessary work) | Slower in some cases (always does full work) |

**Example illustrating side effects:**

```java
int x = 5;
boolean a = (5 > 10) && (++x > 0);  // x remains 5
boolean b = (5 > 10) &  (++x > 0);  // x becomes 7
```

In the first case, `++x` is skipped due to short-circuiting. In the second, both sides evaluate, causing `x` to increment twice.

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP, 3 Marks]`
**Explain the Boolean wrapper class in Java. Why is it needed when Java already has the primitive `boolean` type?**

**Model Answer:**

The `java.lang.Boolean` wrapper class **encapsulates a primitive `boolean`** inside an object. It is needed because:

1. **Collections Framework:** Java collections like `ArrayList<Boolean>` store **objects only**, not primitives.
2. **Generics:** Type parameters (`<T>`) require reference types, not primitives.
3. **Utility Methods:** Provides static methods like `Boolean.parseBoolean(String)`, `Boolean.valueOf(String)`, and constants `Boolean.TRUE` and `Boolean.FALSE`.
4. **Serialization & Reflection:** Object-based APIs require the wrapper form.

**Auto-boxing/unboxing (Java 5+):**

```java
Boolean obj = true;           // Auto-boxing
boolean prim = obj;           // Auto-unboxing
```

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2 Model QP]`

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain **De Morgan's Laws** in Boolean algebra with truth table proofs. How are these laws applied when simplifying Java conditional expressions? Provide one Java code example.

**(b)** [7 Marks] With a neat diagram, explain the **concept of short-circuit evaluation** of the `&&` and `||` operators in Java. Why is short-circuit evaluation important in real-world programming? Give two scenarios.

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### Part (a) — De Morgan's Laws [7 Marks]

**De Morgan's First Law:** $\neg(A \land B) = \neg A \lor \neg B$

| $A$ | $B$ | $A \land B$ | $\neg(A \land B)$ | $\neg A$ | $\neg B$ | $\neg A \lor \neg B$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| F | F | F | **T** | T | T | **T** |
| F | T | F | **T** | T | F | **T** |
| T | F | F | **T** | F | T | **T** |
| T | T | T | **F** | F | F | **F** |

Since columns 4 and 7 match in all four rows, the law is proved. $\blacksquare$

**De Morgan's Second Law:** $\neg(A \lor B) = \neg A \land \neg B$

| $A$ | $B$ | $A \lor B$ | $\neg(A \lor B)$ | $\neg A$ | $\neg B$ | $\neg A \land \neg B$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| F | F | F | **T** | T | T | **T** |
| F | T | T | **F** | T | F | **F** |
| T | F | T | **F** | F | T | **F** |
| T | T | T | **F** | F | F | **F** |

Since columns 4 and 7 match, the law is proved. $\blacksquare$

**Application in Java:**

De Morgan's laws let you **rewrite negated conditions** for readability. For example:

```java
// Original (hard to read):
if (!(x > 0 && y > 0)) { ... }

// After applying De Morgan's Law 1:
if (x <= 0 || y <= 0) { ... }
```

Both forms are **logically equivalent** (compiler may even optimize them to the same bytecode), but the second is more readable.

#### Part (b) — Short-Circuit Evaluation [7 Marks]

**Concept:** Java's `&&` and `||` operators use **short-circuit evaluation** — the right-hand operand is **not evaluated** if the result is already determined by the left-hand operand.

**Rules:**
- For `&&`: If LHS is `false`, RHS is **skipped** (result must be `false`).
- For `||`: If LHS is `true`, RHS is **skipped** (result must be `true`).

**Diagram:**

```
Expression:  (5 > 10) && (++x > 0)
                  |
                  v
        Evaluate LHS: 5 > 10 = false
                  |
                  v
        Is it false? --YES--> Skip RHS --> Final result: false
                                            (++x never executed)
```

**Real-world importance — two scenarios:**

1. **Preventing NullPointerException:**
   ```java
   if (obj != null && obj.isValid()) { ... }
   ```
   If `obj` is `null`, the second condition `obj.isValid()` is never called, avoiding a crash.

2. **Performance optimization:**
   ```java
   if (expensiveCheck() || simpleCheck()) { ... }
   ```
   If `expensiveCheck()` returns `true`, the simpler check is skipped, saving CPU time.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice):** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2 Model QP]`

**(a)** [7 Marks] Compare and contrast the **primitive `boolean` type** and the **`Boolean` wrapper class** in Java. Explain **auto-boxing and auto-unboxing** with examples. What are the potential pitfalls?

**(b)** [7 Marks] List and explain the **six relational operators** in Java that return Boolean values. For each, give a one-line example and state the result. Also explain **operator precedence** for `!`, `&&`, and `||` with an example.

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### Part (a) — Primitive vs Wrapper Boolean [7 Marks]

| Aspect | `boolean` (Primitive) | `Boolean` (Wrapper) |
|---|---|---|
| **Type** | Primitive (value type) | Reference type (object) |
| **Memory** | 1 bit (JVM-dependent) | 16+ bytes (object overhead) |
| **Default** | `false` | `null` |
| **Null Assignment** | Cannot be `null` | Can be `null` |
| **Usage in Collections** | Not allowed (e.g., `List<boolean>` invalid) | Allowed (e.g., `List<Boolean>` valid) |
| **Generics** | Cannot be type parameter | Can be type parameter |
| **Performance** | Faster (no object overhead) | Slower due to GC pressure |

**Auto-boxing and Auto-unboxing (Java 5+):**

```java
// Auto-boxing: primitive -> object
Boolean wrapper = true;

// Auto-unboxing: object -> primitive
boolean primitive = wrapper;
```

**Common pitfalls:**

1. **NullPointerException during unboxing:**
   ```java
   Boolean b = null;
   if (b) { ... }   // Throws NullPointerException during unboxing
   ```

2. **Object identity vs equality:**
   ```java
   Boolean a = true;
   Boolean b = true;
   System.out.println(a == b);    // true (cached) but == on wrappers is risky
   System.out.println(a.equals(b)); // true (always safe)
   ```

3. **Performance issue in loops:** Excessive auto-boxing in tight loops creates many short-lived objects, increasing garbage collection overhead.

#### Part (b) — Relational Operators and Precedence [7 Marks]

**Six relational operators returning Boolean:**

| Operator | Meaning | Example | Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| `==` | Equal to | `5 == 5` | `true` |
| `!=` | Not equal to | `5 != 3` | `true` |
| `>` | Greater than | `5 > 10` | `false` |
| `<` | Less than | `3 < 5` | `true` |
| `>=` | Greater than or equal | `5 >= 5` | `true` |
| `<=` | Less than or equal | `5 <= 3` | `false` |

**Operator precedence (from highest to lowest):**

1. `!` (unary NOT) — **highest**
2. `&&` (AND) — **middle**
3. `||` (OR) — **lowest**

**Example to demonstrate precedence:**

```java
boolean result = !true && false || true;
```

Step-by-step evaluation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & !\text{true} \text{ is evaluated first (highest precedence)} = \texttt{false} \\
& \text{Expression becomes: } \texttt{false && false || true} \\
\\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \texttt{false && false} \text{ is evaluated next} = \texttt{false} \\
& \text{Expression becomes: } \texttt{false || true} \\
\\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \texttt{false || true} \text{ is evaluated last} = \texttt{true} \\
\\
\therefore \quad & \texttt{result} = \texttt{true}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not** confuse `=` (assignment) with `==` (comparison). This is the **#1 reason for lost marks** in KTU OOP exams.
> 2. **Do not** claim `boolean` and `int` are interchangeable in Java. They are NOT. Statements like `if (1)` will NOT compile in Java.
> 3. **Always** use parentheses in complex Boolean expressions to make precedence explicit — KTU valuators reward clarity.
> 4. When asked about short-circuiting, **always mention side effects** (e.g., increments, method calls) being skipped — this is a 2-mark differentiator.
> 5. In Boolean wrapper questions, **mention auto-boxing gotcha** (NullPointerException on unboxing null) for full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 — Boolean**

- **Boolean** is a primitive data type with exactly two values: `true` and `false`.
- In **Java**, `boolean` is **not interchangeable** with `int` or `char` (unlike C++).
- **Default value** of a `boolean` instance variable is `false`; local variables must be explicitly initialized.
- **Wrapper class** is `java.lang.Boolean` (uppercase B); primitive is `boolean` (lowercase b).
- **Auto-boxing** (primitive → wrapper) and **auto-unboxing** (wrapper → primitive) introduced in Java 5.
- **Short-circuit operators** `&&` and `||` skip RHS evaluation when the result is already determined; `&` and `|` do not.
- **Short-circuiting prevents** `NullPointerException` and avoids unnecessary computation.
- **Relational operators** (`==`, `!=`, `>`, `<`, `>=`, `<=`) all return `boolean`.
- **Logical operators**: `&&` (AND), `||` (OR), `!` (NOT), `^` (XOR).
- **De Morgan's Laws**: $\neg(A \land B) = \neg A \lor \neg B$ and $\neg(A \lor B) = \neg A \land \neg B$.
- **Operator precedence**: `!` > `&&` > `||` (use parentheses for clarity).
- **Boolean Algebra Laws** (Identity, Null, Idempotent, Complement, Commutative, Associative, Distributive) are the foundation of digital logic and CPU design.
- **Ternary operator** `condition ? val1 : val2` is a compact Boolean-driven expression.
- **Common KTU pitfall**: `=` vs `==` — assignment operator vs comparison operator.
- **Boolean** is the **backbone of control flow**: `if`, `while`, `for`, `do-while`, ternary, and `switch` patterns all depend on it.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Literals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT414 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Literals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Literals in Java** are the fixed, constant data values that are written directly into the source code of a program by the programmer, representing themselves exactly as they appear. They are the most basic form of expression in any programming language and form the foundational building blocks for variables, constants, and operations.

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **Object Oriented Programming (PEECT414)**, literals are introduced as part of **Module 2: Java Fundamentals and Language Basics**, where students transition from theoretical programming constructs to actual syntactical elements of the Java language. A literal is *not* a variable, *not* a constant declared via `final`, and *not* a result of a computation — it is a **raw, uncomputed, hard-coded value** supplied verbatim by the source code.

The Java Language Specification (JLS) classifies literals into **six primary categories**:
1. **Integer Literals**
2. **Floating-Point Literals**
3. **Boolean Literals**
4. **Character Literals**
5. **String Literals**
6. **The Null Literal**

Each of these categories has its own syntactic rules, type inference behavior, and implicit casting rules that the Java compiler enforces at compile time. Failure to understand these rules is one of the most common sources of compilation errors in KTU lab examinations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of a literal like a printed price tag in a grocery store. The price tag reads "₹45.00" — that exact value is *fixed*, *visible*, and *unalterable* by the customer. The shopkeeper (Java compiler) reads the tag and assigns it a *type* (rupees, kilograms, liters) based on how the number looks. Just as you cannot have a price tag that says "forty-five" without context, a Java literal must conform to specific formatting rules to be valid.

Another simple way to visualize this:
- A **variable** is a labeled *box* where you can store things and change them later.
- A **literal** is the *actual item* you place into the box — like writing `42`, `'A'`, or `"Hello"` directly into your code.
- The compiler "looks" at the literal and decides its **type** based purely on its appearance (suffixes, decimal points, base prefixes, quotes).

## 1.3 Key Characteristics of Literals

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory Syllabus Highlight:** According to KTU Module 2, students must be able to:
> - Identify the **six types of literals** in Java.
> - Apply **suffixes** (`L`, `F`, `D`, `f`, `d`) to control the inferred type of numeric literals.
> - Use **base prefixes** (`0b`, `0`, `0x`, `0X`) for representing numbers in different numeral systems.
> - Understand **escape sequences** within character and string literals.
> - Recognize the role of the **`null` literal** and its importance in object reference initialization.

### Fundamental Properties:
- Literals are **compile-time constants** — their values are known at compile time, allowing the compiler to perform optimizations.
- They have **no name** (unlike variables or constants).
- They are **typed implicitly** by the compiler based on syntax, not explicitly declared.
- They are **immutable** by definition — a literal value can never change during program execution.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Categories of Java Literals — Structured Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Integer Literals
Integer literals represent whole numbers without any fractional or decimal component. Java supports four representations:

- **Decimal (base 10)**: Digits `0-9`, no prefix. Example: `123`, `0`, `42`.
- **Octal (base 8)**: Prefix `0`, digits `0-7`. Example: `0123` (which equals 83 in decimal).
- **Hexadecimal (base 16)**: Prefix `0x` or `0X`, digits `0-9` and letters `A-F` (case-insensitive). Example: `0xFF` (which equals 255 in decimal).
- **Binary (base 2)**: Prefix `0b` or `0B`, digits `0` and `1`. Example: `0b1010` (which equals 10 in decimal).

> [!NOTE]
> **Inferred Type Rule:** By default, an integer literal is of type `int` if it fits within the 32-bit range [\(-2^{31}\), \(2^{31} - 1\)]. If the value exceeds this range, the compiler raises an error unless the literal is suffixed with `L` or `l` to make it a `long`. The lowercase `l` is **discouraged** because it is visually similar to the digit `1`.

### 2.1.2 Floating-Point Literals
Floating-point literals represent numbers with a fractional part, expressed in either:
- **Standard (decimal) form**: Contains a decimal point. Example: `3.14`, `0.5`, `2.0`.
- **Scientific (exponential) form**: Uses `E` or `e` for the exponent (power of 10). Example: `1.5e3` (which means \(1.5 \times 10^{3} = 1500\)).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inferred Type Rule:** By default, a floating-point literal is of type `double` (64-bit, IEEE 754). To specify a `float` (32-bit), the suffix `F` or `f` must be appended. The suffix `D` or `d` is optional for `double`. The `Double.POSITIVE_INFINITY`, `Double.NEGATIVE_INFINITY`, and `Double.NaN` constants are *not* literals but are predefined static constants — students must **not** confuse these.

### 2.1.3 Boolean Literals
The boolean data type has only **two possible literal values**:
- `true` — represents logical truth.
- `false` — represents logical falsehood.

> [!NOTE]
> Unlike C or C++, Java does **not** permit numeric values (such as `1` or `0`) to be implicitly or explicitly cast to `boolean`. A statement like `boolean flag = 1;` is a **compile-time error** in Java. This is a frequently asked KTU viva question.

### 2.1.4 Character Literals
Character literals represent a **single 16-bit Unicode character** enclosed in **single quotes** (`' '`). They can be expressed in four ways:
- **Direct character**: `'A'`, `'7'`, `'@'`.
- **Escape sequence**: `'\n'` (newline), `'\t'` (tab), `'\\'` (backslash), `'\''` (single quote), `'\"'` (double quote), `'\u0041'` (Unicode for 'A').
- **Octal escape**: `'\101'` (which is 'A' in octal).
- **Unicode escape**: `'\u0041'` — this can even appear **outside** character/string literals (e.g., variable names) since Unicode escapes are processed very early by the compiler.

### 2.1.5 String Literals
String literals represent a **sequence of characters** enclosed in **double quotes** (`" "`). Internally, a String literal is an instance of the `java.lang.String` class. Example: `"Hello, KTU!"`, `"123"`, `""` (empty string).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Concept:** Java maintains a special memory area called the **String Constant Pool (SCP)** inside the heap memory. When the same string literal appears multiple times, the JVM reuses the same object reference to conserve memory. This is why `s1 == s2` can be `true` for two string literals having the same content — but this fails when strings are created using the `new` keyword. This concept is heavily tested in KTU Module 3 but its *foundation* begins here with literal understanding.

### 2.1.6 The Null Literal
The null literal is represented by the keyword `null` (lowercase). It can be assigned to any **reference type** variable (objects, arrays, strings) to indicate that the variable does not currently point to any object in memory. It **cannot** be assigned to a primitive type variable (e.g., `int x = null;` is a compile error).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Literal Category** | **Syntax Pattern** | **Default Inferred Type** | **Suffix to Change Type** | **Example Literals** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Integer (Decimal) | Digits `0-9` | `int` | `L`/`l` for `long` | `42`, `1000L` |
| Integer (Octal) | Leading `0` + digits `0-7` | `int` | `L`/`l` for `long` | `0123`, `077L` |
| Integer (Hex) | `0x` or `0X` + digits/letters | `int` | `L`/`l` for `long` | `0xFF`, `0xCAFE_L` |
| Integer (Binary) | `0b` or `0B` + digits `0/1` | `int` | `L`/`l` for `long` | `0b1010`, `0B1100_L` |
| Floating-Point | Decimal form or `E/e` form | `double` | `F`/`f` for `float`, `D`/`d` for `double` | `3.14`, `1.5e3F`, `2.0D` |
| Boolean | `true` or `false` | `boolean` | Not applicable | `true`, `false` |
| Character | Single char in single quotes | `char` (16-bit Unicode) | Not applicable | `'A'`, `'\n'`, `'\u0041'` |
| String | Chars in double quotes | `java.lang.String` (reference) | Not applicable | `"Hello"`, `""`, `"42"` |
| Null | `null` keyword | Any reference type | Not applicable | `null` |

### 2.2.1 Numeric Range Reference (Mandatory for KTU)

| **Type** | **Size** | **Min Value** | **Max Value** |
|---|---|---|---|
| `byte` | 8 bits | \(-2^{7} = -128\) | \(2^{7} - 1 = 127\) |
| `short` | 16 bits | \(-2^{15} = -32768\) | \(2^{15} - 1 = 32767\) |
| `int` | 32 bits | \(-2^{31} = -2147483648\) | \(2^{31} - 1 = 2147483647\) |
| `long` | 64 bits | \(-2^{63}\) | \(2^{63} - 1\) |
| `float` | 32 bits | `1.4E-45` (approx) | `3.4028235E38` (approx) |
| `double` | 64 bits | `4.9E-324` (approx) | `1.7976931348623157E308` (approx) |

### 2.2.2 Escape Sequence Master Table

| **Escape Sequence** | **Meaning** | **ASCII/Unicode Code** |
|---|---|---|
| `\n` | Newline (Line Feed) | `\u000A` |
| `\t` | Horizontal Tab | `\u0009` |
| `\r` | Carriage Return | `\u000D` |
| `\b` | Backspace | `\u0008` |
| `\f` | Form Feed | `\u000C` |
| `\\` | Backslash | `\u005C` |
| `\'` | Single Quote | `\u0027` |
| `\"` | Double Quote | `\u0022` |
| `\0` | Null character | `\u0000` |

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Application:** Literals are the bedrock of **embedded systems programming**, where hardware register values, sensor calibration constants, and protocol headers are hard-coded as literals. For example, in IoT firmware, a literal like `0xFF` might represent a broadcast address, and a literal like `3.3F` might represent the operating voltage of a microcontroller. In Android development (a Java-based ecosystem), `0xFFFFFFFF` is a common color literal representing pure white with full alpha. Understanding literals correctly prevents overflow bugs, precision loss, and unintended type coercions in mission-critical engineering software.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Conversions & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Converting Between Numeral Systems

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Convert the integer literal `0x2A` to decimal, octal, and binary forms, and verify the result.

### Step 1: Identify the Base
The literal `0x2A` has the prefix `0x`, which means it is in **hexadecimal (base-16)** notation.

### Step 2: Expand Hexadecimal to Decimal
Each digit in hexadecimal represents a power of 16. The rightmost digit is the least significant (position 0), and digits move leftward with increasing powers.

$$\text{Decimal Value} = (2 \times 16^{1}) + (A \times 16^{0})$$

Substituting the value of A (which is 10 in decimal):

$$\text{Decimal Value} = (2 \times 16) + (10 \times 1)$$

$$\text{Decimal Value} = 32 + 10 = 42$$

### Step 3: Convert Decimal 42 to Octal
We repeatedly divide by 8 and record the remainders.

$$42 \div 8 = 5 \text{ remainder } 2$$
$$5 \div 8 = 0 \text{ remainder } 5$$

Reading the remainders from bottom to top: the octal representation is `052`. So the literal `052` in Java represents the same value.

### Step 4: Convert Decimal 42 to Binary
We repeatedly divide by 2 and record the remainders.

$$42 \div 2 = 21 \text{ remainder } 0$$
$$21 \div 2 = 10 \text{ remainder } 1$$
$$10 \div 2 = 5 \text{ remainder } 0$$
$$5 \div 2 = 2 \text{ remainder } 1$$
$$2 \div 2 = 1 \text{ remainder } 0$$
$$1 \div 2 = 0 \text{ remainder } 1$$

Reading the remainders from bottom to top: the binary representation is `101010`. So the literal `0b101010` in Java represents the same value.

### Step 5: Final Verification
All four Java literals represent the same integer value (42):
- `0x2A` (hexadecimal)
- `052` (octal)
- `0b101010` (binary)
- `42` (decimal)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trap:** A common mistake is writing `052` and expecting it to be decimal 52. In Java, any integer literal starting with `0` (but not `0x` or `0b`) is **octal**. So `052` = 42 decimal, not 52. This is a classic KTU 2-mark question.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Type Inference and Suffix Usage

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Determine the inferred type and verify whether each of the following literals will cause a compilation error:
> (a) `100` (b) `100L` (c) `3.14` (d) `3.14F` (e) `0x100000000L` (f) `'A'` (g) `'\u0041'`

### Sub-problem (a): Literal `100`
The literal `100` is a decimal integer that fits within the `int` range. The compiler infers its type as `int`. **No compilation error.**

### Sub-problem (b): Literal `100L`
The suffix `L` forces the compiler to treat the literal as a `long` type. **No compilation error.** Type: `long`.

### Sub-problem (c): Literal `3.14`
The literal `3.14` contains a decimal point, so the compiler infers it as a `double` by default. **No compilation error.** Type: `double`.

### Sub-problem (d): Literal `3.14F`
The suffix `F` forces the compiler to treat the literal as a `float`. The value `3.14` must fit within the `float` precision, which it does. **No compilation error.** Type: `float`.

### Sub-problem (e): Literal `0x100000000L`
This literal is in hexadecimal and equals \(16^{8} = 4294967296\) in decimal, which exceeds the `int` range (\(2^{31} - 1 = 2147483647\)). However, the suffix `L` promotes it to `long`, which can hold this value. **No compilation error.** Type: `long`.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** If the suffix `L` were omitted (i.e., just `0x100000000`), the compiler would raise the error *"integer number too large"* because the default inferred type is `int`, which cannot hold this value.

### Sub-problem (f): Literal `'A'`
This is a character literal containing a single Unicode character. The compiler infers its type as `char`. **No compilation error.**

### Sub-problem (g): Literal `'\u0041'`
This is a Unicode escape sequence representing the character 'A' (Unicode code point U+0041). The compiler infers its type as `char`. **No compilation error.**

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Scientific Notation Conversion

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Evaluate and explain the value of the Java floating-point literal `6.022E23`.

### Step 1: Identify the Components
The literal `6.022E23` has the mantissa `6.022` and the exponent `23` (after the `E`). The base is implicitly 10.

### Step 2: Apply the Scientific Notation Formula
The value is computed as:

$$\text{Value} = \text{mantissa} \times 10^{\text{exponent}}$$

$$\text{Value} = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$$

This is **Avogadro's number**, a fundamental physical constant in chemistry and physics. By default, this literal is inferred as a `double` in Java.

### Step 3: Java Type Assignment
$$\text{Type: } double \text{ (64-bit IEEE 754 representation)}$$

If the suffix `F` were appended (`6.022E23F`), it would be treated as a `float`, which may cause precision loss because the `float` type has only about 7 significant decimal digits of precision, while `6.022 \times 10^{23}\) requires more.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Compile-Time Behavior Table

| **Code Snippet** | **Inferred Type** | **Compiles?** | **Error Message (if any)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| `int a = 100;` | `int` | Yes | None |
| `long b = 100;` | `int` (widened to long) | Yes | None |
| `long c = 100L;` | `long` | Yes | None |
| `long d = 100;` (when value exceeds int) | `int` (literal), but value too large | No | *"integer number too large"* |
| `float e = 3.14;` | `double` (cannot narrow without cast) | No | *"possible lossy conversion from double to float"* |
| `float f = 3.14F;` | `float` | Yes | None |
| `boolean g = 1;` | `int` | No | *"incompatible types: int cannot be converted to boolean"* |
| `char h = 'A';` | `char` | Yes | None |
| `char i = "A";` | `String` | No | *"incompatible types: String cannot be converted to char"* |
| `String j = null;` | `String` (reference) | Yes | None |
| `int k = null;` | `null` (reference) | No | *"incompatible types: <null> cannot be converted to int"* |
| `byte l = 50;` | `int` (literal), narrowed to byte | Yes | None (compile-time constant within byte range) |
| `byte m = 200;` | `int` (literal) | No | *"incompatible types: possible lossy conversion from int to byte"* |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Important Subtlety:** Java allows implicit narrowing from `int` literal to `byte`, `short`, or `char` **only when** the literal is a compile-time constant whose value fits within the target type's range. This is a frequent KTU exam question.

## 3.5 Java Code Demonstration Block (CS-Topic Permitted)

```java
public class LiteralDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Integer literals in different bases
        int decimalVal = 42;           // Decimal: 42
        int octalVal   = 052;          // Octal: 42 (NOT 52!)
        int hexVal     = 0x2A;         // Hex: 42
        int binaryVal  = 0b101010;     // Binary: 42
        long bigVal    = 10000000000L; // Long literal (exceeds int range)

        // Floating-point literals
        double piDouble = 3.14159;     // Default: double
        float  piFloat  = 3.14159F;    // Explicit: float
        double avogadro = 6.022E23;    // Scientific notation: double

        // Boolean literal
        boolean isKtuStudent = true;

        // Character literals
        char letter     = 'K';
        char newline    = '\n';
        char unicodeA   = '\u0041';
        char hexEscape  = '\x41';

        // String literal
        String greeting = "Hello, KTU 2024 Scheme!";

        // Null literal
        String emptyRef = null;

        // Demonstration of narrowing
        byte smallByte = 127;           // Valid: compile-time constant
        // byte invalidByte = 200;      // Error: lossy conversion

        System.out.println("Decimal: " + decimalVal);
        System.out.println("Octal 052 = " + octalVal);
        System.out.println("Hex 0x2A = " + hexVal);
        System.out.println("Binary 0b101010 = " + binaryVal);
        System.out.println("Pi (float) = " + piFloat);
        System.out.println("Letter: " + letter + " Unicode: " + unicodeA);
        System.out.println(greeting);
    }
}
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Output Verification:** The variables `decimalVal`, `octalVal`, `hexVal`, and `binaryVal` all print the value `42`, proving that they are syntactically different but semantically identical. This code will compile and run successfully, illustrating the practical use of literals in a real Java program.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** When two or more string literals in the source code contain the *exact same sequence of characters*, the JVM reuses a single object in the String Constant Pool. Hence `s1 == s2` may return `true` for string literals, but this is **not guaranteed** for strings constructed with the `new` operator. This nuance is heavily tested in KTU university examinations.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a Literal in Java. List its types.
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]`**

**Model Answer:**
A literal in Java is a constant value that is written directly into the source code of a program and represents itself exactly as it appears. Literals are compile-time constants with no associated name. The six types of literals in Java are:
1. Integer Literals (e.g., `42`, `0x2A`, `052`, `0b101010`)
2. Floating-Point Literals (e.g., `3.14`, `1.5e3`)
3. Boolean Literals (e.g., `true`, `false`)
4. Character Literals (e.g., `'A'`, `'\n'`)
5. String Literals (e.g., `"Hello"`, `""`)
6. The Null Literal (e.g., `null`)

---

### Question 2: What is the difference between `052` and `52` as integer literals in Java?
**`[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Expected Question]`**

**Model Answer:**
The literal `52` is a **decimal (base-10)** integer literal, representing the value fifty-two. The literal `052` has a leading zero, which in Java denotes an **octal (base-8)** integer literal. The digits valid in an octal literal are `0-7` only. Therefore, `052` is evaluated as \((0 \times 8^{2}) + (5 \times 8^{1}) + (2 \times 8^{0}) = 0 + 40 + 2 = 42\) in decimal. Hence, `52` and `052` represent different values (52 vs 42) in Java. This is a commonly tested concept to check whether students understand Java's numeral system conventions.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2 Internal Choice]`**

**(a)** Explain the different types of integer literals in Java with suitable examples. Discuss the role of suffixes `L` and `l` in determining the type of an integer literal. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** What are floating-point literals? Differentiate between `float` and `double` literals. Explain the significance of the suffixes `F`, `f`, `D`, and `d` with examples. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

Java supports **four representations** of integer literals:

1. **Decimal (Base-10)**: The default representation using digits 0-9 without any prefix.
   - Example: `int x = 123;` — represents one hundred twenty-three.

2. **Octal (Base-8)**: Uses a leading `0` followed by digits 0-7.
   - Example: `int y = 0123;` — equals \((1 \times 8^{2}) + (2 \times 8^{1}) + (3 \times 8^{0}) = 64 + 16 + 3 = 83\) in decimal.

3. **Hexadecimal (Base-16)**: Uses the prefix `0x` or `0X` followed by digits 0-9 and letters A-F (case-insensitive).
   - Example: `int z = 0xFF;` — equals \(15 \times 16 + 15 = 255\) in decimal.

4. **Binary (Base-2)**: Uses the prefix `0b` or `0B` followed by digits 0 and 1. This was introduced in Java 7.
   - Example: `int w = 0b1010;` — equals 10 in decimal.

**Role of Suffixes `L` and `l`:**
By default, an integer literal is inferred as type `int`, which is a 32-bit signed integer with range \([-2^{31}, 2^{31}-1]\). When an integer literal's value exceeds this range, the compiler throws the error *"integer number too large"*. To represent such large values, the suffix `L` or `l` is used to make the literal of type `long` (64-bit).

Examples:
- `long bigNum = 10000000000L;` — valid, type is `long`.
- `long biggerNum = 0xFFFFFFFFFL;` — valid, equals \(16^{9} - 1\) in decimal.
- `long wrong = 10000000000;` — compile error because the default `int` type cannot hold this value.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Warning:** The lowercase `l` suffix is **not recommended** by Oracle because it is visually indistinguishable from the digit `1` in many fonts. Always use the uppercase `L` for clarity. Students often lose 1 mark in KTU exams by writing ambiguous literals.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Floating-point literals** are numeric literals that contain a fractional component (decimal part). They can be expressed in two forms:

1. **Standard Form**: Contains a decimal point.
   - Example: `3.14`, `0.5`, `2.0`, `.25` (valid — leading decimal point is optional), `5.` (valid — trailing decimal point is optional).

2. **Scientific (Exponential) Form**: Uses the letter `E` or `e` followed by an optionally signed integer exponent.
   - Example: `1.5e3` means \(1.5 \times 10^{3} = 1500\), `2E-4` means \(2 \times 10^{-4} = 0.0002\).

**Differences between `float` and `double` literals:**

| **Aspect** | **float** | **double** |
|---|---|---|
| Size | 32 bits (4 bytes) | 64 bits (8 bytes) |
| Precision | ~7 significant decimal digits | ~15-17 significant decimal digits |
| Default Suffix | `F` or `f` required | None (default) or `D`/`d` |
| Range | `1.4E-45` to `3.4E38` | `4.9E-324` to `1.8E308` |
| Use Case | Memory-critical applications (graphics, embedded) | Scientific and general-purpose calculations |

**Significance of Suffixes:**

- **`F` / `f`**: Forces the literal to be treated as a `float`. Without this suffix, a decimal literal is inferred as `double`, and assigning it to a `float` variable causes a *"possible lossy conversion"* compile error.
  - Example: `float pi = 3.14F;` — valid.
  - Example: `float pi = 3.14;` — **compile error**.

- **`D` / `d`**: Optional suffix that explicitly marks a literal as `double`. Since `double` is the default for floating-point literals, this suffix is rarely used.
  - Example: `double gravity = 9.81D;` — same as `double gravity = 9.81;`.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often write `float f = 1.5;` expecting it to work, but it fails to compile. The literal `1.5` is a `double`, and Java does **not** allow implicit narrowing conversions from `double` to `float`. Always use the `F` suffix. This is a guaranteed KTU 2-mark question pattern.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**`[KTU University Exam - June 2023, Module 2 Internal Choice]`**

**(a)** What are character literals in Java? Explain with examples how escape sequences and Unicode escape sequences are used. List at least six commonly used escape sequences with their meanings. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** Explain the concept of the String Constant Pool. How are string literals stored in memory? Differentiate between string literals created using double quotes and those created using the `new` keyword. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Character Literals** in Java represent a single 16-bit Unicode character and are enclosed in **single quotes** (`' '`). They occupy 2 bytes of memory (because Java uses the Unicode character set, not ASCII) and have the type `char`.

**Methods of expressing character literals:**

1. **Direct Character**: Any printable character placed between single quotes.
   - Examples: `'A'`, `'7'`, `'@'`, `' '` (space).

2. **Escape Sequences**: Special character combinations starting with a backslash (`\`), used to represent non-printable or reserved characters.

3. **Unicode Escape Sequences**: Use the syntax `'\uXXXX'` where `XXXX` is a 4-digit hexadecimal Unicode code point.
   - Example: `'\u0041'` represents the character 'A'.
   - Example: `'\u00A9'` represents the copyright symbol '©'.

**Six Commonly Used Escape Sequences:**

| **Escape Sequence** | **Meaning** |
|---|---|
| `\n` | Newline (Line Feed) — moves the cursor to the next line |
| `\t` | Horizontal Tab — inserts a tab space |
| `\\` | Backslash — inserts a literal backslash character |
| `\'` | Single Quote — inserts a single quote inside a char literal |
| `\"` | Double Quote — inserts a double quote inside a string literal |
| `\r` | Carriage Return — moves the cursor to the beginning of the line |

Additional escape sequences include `\b` (backspace), `\f` (form feed), and `\0` (null character).

**Example Program Snippet:**

```java
char letter = 'K';
char tab = '\t';
char copyright = '\u00A9';
System.out.println("Hello" + tab + "World" + copyright);
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Compiler Trick:** Unicode escapes are processed by the Java compiler *before* tokenization. This means `'\u0041'` is replaced by 'A' at the very first stage of compilation. This is why Unicode escapes can appear in variable names too (e.g., `int \u0061ge = 25;` is valid and creates a variable named `age`).

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**The String Constant Pool (SCP)** is a special memory area inside the **Java heap memory** where the JVM stores string literals to optimize memory usage. When the same string literal appears multiple times in the source code, the JVM ensures that only **one object** is created in the pool, and all references point to that single object.

**How String Literals Are Stored:**
When the Java compiler encounters a string literal such as `"Hello"`, it performs the following steps at runtime:
1. Checks if a string with the same content already exists in the SCP.
2. If yes, the reference points to the existing object.
3. If no, a new `String` object is created in the SCP, and the reference points to it.

**Code Example Demonstrating the Concept:**

```java
String s1 = "Hello KTU";
String s2 = "Hello KTU";        // Same literal — reuses SCP object
String s3 = new String("Hello KTU"); // Forces new object outside SCP

System.out.println(s1 == s2);   // true  — same SCP object
System.out.println(s1 == s3);   // false — different objects
System.out.println(s1.equals(s3)); // true  — same content
```

**Difference Between String Literals and `new` Keyword:**

| **Aspect** | **String Literal** (`"Hello"`) | **`new` Keyword** (`new String("Hello")`) |
|---|---|---|
| Storage Location | String Constant Pool (inside Heap) | Regular Heap memory |
| Object Creation | Reused if already exists in SCP | Always creates a new object |
| Memory Efficiency | High (avoids duplication) | Lower (may create duplicates) |
| `==` Comparison | Returns `true` for identical literals | Returns `false` even for identical content |
| Use Case | Most common, default choice | Explicit object creation (e.g., for mutable wrappers) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** A very common mistake students make in KTU exams is using `==` to compare string contents instead of `.equals()`. While `==` may *coincidentally* work for string literals due to SCP interning, it is **semantically incorrect** and will fail when strings come from user input, file I/O, or the `new` operator. Always use `.equals()` for content comparison. Examiners specifically deduct 2 marks for this mistake.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 — Literals**

- **Six Categories**: Integer, Floating-Point, Boolean, Character, String, Null. Remember all six for viva questions.

- **Integer Bases**:
  - Decimal → no prefix
  - Octal → leading `0` (e.g., `052` = 42 decimal)
  - Hexadecimal → `0x` or `0X`
  - Binary → `0b` or `0B` (Java 7+ feature)

- **Default Numeric Types**:
  - Integers default to `int`; use `L` for `long`.
  - Floating-point defaults to `double`; use `F` for `float`.
  - `D` suffix is **optional** for `double`.

- **Boolean Restriction**: Only `true` and `false` are valid. Integers like `1` and `0` are **not** valid boolean values in Java (unlike C/C++).

- **Character Encoding**: Java uses **16-bit Unicode** (not ASCII) for `char` and string literals. This allows representation of international characters.

- **String Constant Pool (SCP)**: All string literals are interned in the SCP for memory efficiency. Use `.equals()` for content comparison, not `==`.

- **Null Literal**: Can be assigned only to **reference types** (objects, arrays, strings). Assigning `null` to a primitive type (e.g., `int x = null;`) is a **compile-time error**.

- **Underscore in Numeric Literals (Java 7+)**: For readability, underscores can be placed between digits in numeric literals. Example: `1_000_000`, `0xFF_FF_FF_FF`. They are ignored by the compiler. They **cannot** be placed at the start, end, or adjacent to a decimal point.

- **Type Narrowing from Literal**: Implicit narrowing from `int` literal to `byte`/`short`/`char` is allowed **only** when the literal is a compile-time constant whose value fits within the target type's range.

- **Common Pitfalls**:
  - Writing `052` expecting 52 (it's octal 42).
  - Writing `float f = 3.14;` (compile error, needs `F`).
  - Writing `long l = 9999999999;` (compile error, needs `L`).
  - Using `==` to compare string contents.
  - Forgetting to escape the backslash (`\\`).

- **Compiler vs. Runtime**: Literals are resolved at **compile time**. The compiler performs type inference, range checks, and SCP interning during the compilation phase, not at runtime.

- **Escapes Are Early**: Unicode escapes (`\uXXXX`) are processed **before** tokenization, so they can even appear in identifiers — a classic KTU viva trick question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT414 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Variables

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **variable** in Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) is a named storage location in the computer's memory that holds a value which can be modified during the execution of a program. In the context of object-oriented languages such as **Java** (the reference language for KTU PEECT414), a variable is fundamentally a *reference* to a memory address where data of a specified type is stored.

In OOP, variables are tightly coupled to three core principles:
1. **Encapsulation** — variables declared inside a class encapsulate state.
2. **Identity** — each object instance carries its own copy of instance variables.
3. **Lifetime & Scope** — variables exist within a defined visibility region and time window.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** *Variables* are treated as the foundational building block for understanding **classes, objects, constructors, and methods**. Mastery of variable types (local, instance, static, parameter) is a prerequisite for the `this` keyword, constructors, and access modifiers that follow in later modules.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Labelled Box" Model

Imagine a **warehouse with thousands of identical, unlabeled cardboard boxes**. Without labels, you could never find the right box again. A variable is essentially a **labelled box** stuck onto one of these containers:

- The **box itself** is a memory location (a fixed-size slot in RAM).
- The **label written on the box** is the variable's *name* (identifier).
- The **type of item allowed inside** is the *data type* (e.g., `int`, `String`, `double`).
- The **item currently inside the box** is the variable's *value*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy Extension — The OOP Twist:** In a class, every *object* (think of it as a separate *room in the warehouse*) gets its own private set of boxes (**instance variables**), but all objects share a common notice board on the wall with a single box (**static/class variable**) that everyone in the warehouse can read or update.

## 1.3 Classification Snapshot (KTU 2024 Scheme)

Variables in OOP are classified based on **where they are declared** and **who owns them**:

| Category | Declared In | Owned By | Lifetime |
|----------|-------------|----------|----------|
| **Local Variable** | Method, constructor, or block | The method/block | Until block exits |
| **Instance Variable** | Inside a class, outside methods | Each object instance | Until object is garbage-collected |
| **Static (Class) Variable** | Inside a class with `static` keyword | The class itself | Until class is unloaded |
| **Parameter Variable** | Method/constructor signature | The called method | During method call |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Every Variable

Every variable in OOP is governed by three orthogonal axes. Mastering these axes is the single most exam-relevant takeaway from Module 2.

### Pillar 1 — Declaration (Type Binding)

$$ \text{Declaration} = \langle \text{DataType} \rangle \; \langle \text{Identifier} \rangle \; [\text{= initializer}] $$

- A variable **must be declared** with a type before use (Java is *strongly typed*).
- The compiler allocates a typed memory slot at compile time (for primitives) or a reference (for objects).

### Pillar 2 — Scope (Visibility Window)

Scope answers the question: *"From which lines of code can I read/write this variable?"*

- **Local scope**: `{` ... `}` enclosing block.
- **Class scope**: Whole class body.
- **Method scope**: From parameter declaration to closing brace.
- **Block scope**: Any inner `{ }` block (e.g., inside loops, `if`).

### Pillar 3 — Lifetime (Temporal Existence)

- **Local variable lifetime** = method invocation duration.
- **Instance variable lifetime** = `new` → garbage collection.
- **Static variable lifetime** = class load → JVM shutdown.

## 2.2 Detailed Variable Categories

### 2.2.1 Local Variables

- Declared **inside** a method, constructor, or any `{}` block.
- **No default value** — must be *definitely assigned* before use, or compile error occurs.
- Stored on the **stack memory** (fast, LIFO-managed).
- **Not accessible** outside the block where declared.

### 2.2.2 Instance Variables (Non-Static Fields)

- Declared **inside a class but outside any method**.
- Each object gets its **own independent copy**.
- Initialized to **default values** if not explicitly set:
  - `int → 0`, `double → 0.0`, `boolean → false`, `object references → null`.
- Stored on the **heap memory** (along with the object).

### 2.2.3 Static (Class) Variables

- Declared with the `static` keyword inside a class.
- **Only one copy** exists per class, shared by all objects.
- Accessed via `ClassName.variableName` (preferred) or `object.variableName`.
- Created when the class is loaded; destroyed when the class is unloaded.

### 2.2.4 Parameter Variables

- Declared in the **method/constructor header**.
- Receive actual values (or references) during method invocation.
- Scope is the entire method body; lifetime is the method call.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This table is **exam-bible material** for Module 2. Memorize the contrasts.

| Property | Local Variable | Instance Variable | Static Variable | Parameter |
|----------|----------------|-------------------|-----------------|-----------|
| **Declaration Site** | Method/block | Class (not in method) | Class with `static` | Method signature |
| **Memory Region** | Stack | Heap | Method Area (Class region) | Stack |
| **Default Value** | ❌ None (compile error) | ✅ Yes (type-specific) | ✅ Yes (type-specific) | ❌ None |
| **Per-Object Copy?** | N/A | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (shared) | N/A |
| **Access Modifier Allowed?** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| **`this` keyword valid?** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (use `ClassName`) | ❌ No |
| **Visibility Outside Class** | Only its block | Per access modifier | Per access modifier | Only its method |
| **Lifetime Trigger** | Block entry → exit | Object creation → GC | Class load → unload | Method call → return |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Instance variables** model *object state* — every IoT sensor object carries its own `temperatureReading`.
- **Static variables** model *shared state* — a counter tracking active user sessions in a web app.
- **Local variables** model *transient computation* — loop counters, intermediate sums in algorithms.
- **Parameters** model *information flow* — passing authentication tokens across microservices.

In **embedded systems** (a key KTU application area), careful variable choice between `static`, `volatile`, and local directly impacts **memory footprint, determinism, and thread safety** — making this topic highly practical for industry.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Variable Declaration Syntax — Exhaustive Breakdown

The complete Java variable declaration grammar can be expanded as:

$$
\text{VariableDeclaration} \;\rightarrow\; \langle \text{Modifier} \rangle^{*} \; \langle \text{Type} \rangle \; \langle \text{Identifier} \rangle \; [\; = \langle \text{Initializer} \rangle \;]
$$

where:

- $\langle \text{Modifier} \rangle^{*}$ denotes *zero or more* modifiers (e.g., `public`, `private`, `static`, `final`).
- $\langle \text{Type} \rangle$ is a primitive type (`int`, `float`, etc.) or a reference type (`String`, user-defined class).
- $\langle \text{Identifier} \rangle$ follows Java naming rules.
- $\langle \text{Initializer} \rangle$ is an optional expression providing an initial value.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Three Worked Code Skeletons

Because this is a programming topic (CS/PEECT414), the analytical breakdown takes the form of fully annotated code skeletons. Each transition is explained row-by-row to enforce the "no step-skipping" rule.

### 3.2.1 Local Variable Analytical Trace

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \texttt{void demo() \{ } && \text{— Method block begins; a stack frame is pushed.} \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \texttt{int x = 10;} && \text{— Primitive local \texttt{x} created on stack, value 10 stored.} \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \texttt{\{} && \text{— Inner block opened; deeper stack frame possible.} \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \texttt{int y = 20;} && \text{— \texttt{y} exists only inside this inner block.} \\
\text{Step 5:} \quad & \texttt{System.out.println(y);} && \text{— \texttt{y} is visible here; legal access.} \\
\text{Step 6:} \quad & \texttt{\}} && \text{— Inner block ends; \texttt{y} is popped from stack, inaccessible after.} \\
\text{Step 7:} \quad & \texttt{System.out.println(x);} && \text{— \texttt{x} still on stack; still accessible.} \\
\text{Step 8:} \quad & \texttt{\}} && \text{— Method ends; entire stack frame popped; \texttt{x} ceases to exist.}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.2 Instance Variable Analytical Trace

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \texttt{class Student \{ } && \text{— Class definition begins.} \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \texttt{String name;} && \text{— Instance variable, default value \texttt{null}.} \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \texttt{int rollNo;} && \text{— Instance variable, default value \texttt{0}.} \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \texttt{\}} && \text{— Class defined; no memory yet allocated.} \\
\text{Step 5:} \quad & \texttt{Student s1 = new Student();} && \text{— Heap allocates object; \texttt{s1} references it.} \\
\text{Step 6:} \quad & \texttt{s1.name = "Kavya";} && \text{— Modifies \texttt{s1}'s private copy of \texttt{name}.} \\
\text{Step 7:} \quad & \texttt{Student s2 = new Student();} && \text{— Second independent object on heap.} \\
\text{Step 8:} \quad & \texttt{s2.name = "Arjun";} && \text{— \texttt{s1.name} remains \texttt{"Kavya"}; isolation verified.}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.3 Static Variable Analytical Trace

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \texttt{class Counter \{ } && \text{— Class definition.} \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \texttt{static int count = 0;} && \text{— Single shared variable in method area, initialized once.} \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \texttt{Counter() \{ count++; \}} && \text{— Each constructor increments the shared counter.} \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \texttt{\}} && \text{— Class definition closed.} \\
\text{Step 5:} \quad & \texttt{Counter a = new Counter();} && \text{— \texttt{count} becomes 1.} \\
\text{Step 6:} \quad & \texttt{Counter b = new Counter();} && \text{— \texttt{count} becomes 2 (shared!).} \\
\text{Step 7:} \quad & \texttt{Counter c = new Counter();} && \text{— \texttt{count} becomes 3.} \\
\text{Step 8:} \quad & \texttt{System.out.println(Counter.count);} && \text{— Outputs 3; confirms class-level sharing.}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Memory Footprint Derivation

For a class with $n$ instance variables of average size $s_i$ bytes and $k$ static variables of average size $t_j$ bytes, the **per-object memory cost** and **total static memory cost** can be expressed as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MemoryPerObject} &= \text{ObjectHeader} + \sum_{i=1}^{n} s_i \\
\text{StaticMemoryTotal} &= \sum_{j=1}^{k} t_j
\end{aligned}
$$

where `ObjectHeader` typically accounts for the mark word, class pointer, and (if applicable) length field in arrays (commonly **12–16 bytes** in 64-bit JVMs with compressed oops).

**Engineering Insight:** Choosing *local* over *instance* variables reduces heap pressure and improves garbage collection behaviour — a critical concern in **high-throughput backend systems** built with Java/Spring (a common KTU elective pathway).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Variable Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[VARIABLES IN OOP] --> B[Local Variable]
    A --> C[Instance Variable]
    A --> D[Static Class Variable]
    A --> E[Parameter Variable]

    B --> B1[Declared in method or block]
    B --> B2[Stored on stack]
    B --> B3[No default value]
    B --> B4[Lifetime ends with block]

    C --> C1[Declared in class not in method]
    C --> C2[Stored on heap with object]
    C --> C3[Default values assigned]
    C --> C4[One copy per object]

    D --> D1[Declared with static keyword]
    D --> D2[Stored in method area]
    D --> D3[Default values assigned]
    D --> D4[One copy shared by all objects]

    E --> E1[Declared in method signature]
    E --> E2[Stored on stack]
    E --> E3[Receives value at call time]
    E --> E4[Lifetime ends with method]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Memory & Lifetime

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph STACK_REGION["STACK MEMORY"]
        L1[Local variable x]
        L2[Local variable y]
        P1[Parameter a]
        P2[Parameter b]
    end

    subgraph HEAP_REGION["HEAP MEMORY"]
        OBJ1["Object 1: Student s1"]
        OBJ2["Object 2: Student s2"]
        IV1["Instance var: name"]
        IV2["Instance var: rollNo"]
    end

    subgraph METHOD_REGION["METHOD AREA / CLASS REGION"]
        CL1["Class: Student"]
        SV1["Static var: schoolName"]
        SV2["Static var: totalCount"]
    end

    OBJ1 --> IV1
    OBJ1 --> IV2
    OBJ2 --> IV1
    OBJ2 --> IV2
    CL1 --> SV1
    CL1 --> SV2
```

## 4.3 Lifetime Timeline Comparison

```mermaid
graph LR
    T0["t=0: Class Loaded"]
    T1["t=1: new Student()"]
    T2["t=2: method call"]
    T3["t=3: block entry"]
    T4["t=4: block exit"]
    T5["t=5: method return"]
    T6["t=6: object GC"]

    T0 -->|Static vars born| SVL["Static: ALIVE"]
    T1 -->|Instance vars born| IVL["Instance: ALIVE"]
    T2 -->|Local + Params born| LVL["Local + Param: ALIVE"]
    T3 -->|Inner local born| INL["Inner local: ALIVE"]
    T4 -->|Inner local dies| INL
    T5 -->|Local + Params die| LVL
    T6 -->|Instance dies| IVL
    SVL -.->|Continues till JVM exit| TEND["JVM Shutdown: Static dies"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between instance variables and static variables in Java. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

**Instance Variable:**
- Declared inside a class but outside any method, *without* the `static` keyword.
- Each object of the class has its own independent copy.
- Initialized to default values if not explicitly assigned.
- Memory is allocated on the **heap** when an object is created.

**Example:**
```java
class Employee {
    int id;          // instance variable
    String name;     // instance variable
}
```

**Static Variable:**
- Declared inside a class with the `static` keyword.
- Only **one copy** exists per class, shared by all objects.
- Memory is allocated in the **method area** when the class is loaded.

**Example:**
```java
class Employee {
    static String company = "KTU Ltd";   // static variable
    int id;                              // instance variable
}
```

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What is a local variable? Why must it be definitely assigned before use?**

**Model Answer:**

A **local variable** is a variable declared inside a method, constructor, or any block `{ }` in Java. It is created when the block is entered and destroyed when the block exits. Local variables are stored on the **stack**.

It must be *definitely assigned* before use because:
- Local variables **do not receive a default value** (unlike instance/static variables).
- Using an uninitialized local variable leads to a **compile-time error**: *"variable x might not have been initialized"*.
- This is a deliberate Java safety feature to prevent undefined behaviour.

**Example:**
```java
void test() {
    int x;             // declared but not initialized
    System.out.println(x);   // COMPILE ERROR
    x = 5;             // now definitely assigned
    System.out.println(x);   // OK, prints 5
}
```

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]` — 14 Marks

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the different types of variables in Java with suitable examples. Discuss their scope and lifetime.

**Model Answer:**

Java supports four major categories of variables:

**1. Local Variables** — Declared inside methods, constructors, or blocks. Scope is restricted to the block in which they are declared. Lifetime begins when the block is entered and ends when the block is exited. No default value is provided.

```java
void calculate() {
    int sum = 0;        // local variable
    for (int i = 1; i <= 10; i++) {
        sum += i;       // 'i' is local to the for-loop
    }
}
```

**2. Instance Variables** — Declared inside a class but outside any method. Each object gets its own copy. Scope is the entire class (subject to access modifiers). Lifetime is from object creation to garbage collection. Default values are assigned automatically.

```java
class BankAccount {
    double balance;          // instance variable, default 0.0
    String accountHolder;    // instance variable, default null
}
```

**3. Static (Class) Variables** — Declared with the `static` keyword. A single copy is shared by all instances. Lifetime spans the entire program execution (class load to class unload). Should be accessed via `ClassName.variableName`.

```java
class BankAccount {
    static double interestRate = 6.5;   // static variable
}
```

**4. Parameter Variables** — Declared in the signature of a method or constructor. They receive values during method invocation. Scope is the method body; lifetime is the duration of the method call.

```java
void deposit(double amount) {   // 'amount' is a parameter
    balance += amount;
}
```

**Scope and Lifetime Summary Table:**

| Variable Type | Scope | Lifetime |
|---------------|-------|----------|
| Local | Enclosing block | Block entry → exit |
| Instance | Whole class | Object creation → GC |
| Static | Whole class | Class load → unload |
| Parameter | Method body | Method call → return |

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Write a Java program to demonstrate the use of instance and static variables. Explain the output.

**Model Answer:**

```java
class Ticket {
    static int totalTickets = 0;   // static variable
    int ticketNumber;              // instance variable

    Ticket() {
        totalTickets++;
        ticketNumber = totalTickets;
    }

    void display() {
        System.out.println("Ticket No: " + ticketNumber +
                           ", Total issued: " + totalTickets);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Ticket t1 = new Ticket();
        Ticket t2 = new Ticket();
        Ticket t3 = new Ticket();
        t1.display();
        t2.display();
        t3.display();
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Ticket No: 1, Total issued: 3
Ticket No: 2, Total issued: 3
Ticket No: 3, Total issued: 3
```

**Explanation:**
- `totalTickets` is a **static variable** — there is only one copy. It is incremented by every constructor call. After three objects, its value is `3`, and *all* objects see the same value.
- `ticketNumber` is an **instance variable** — each `Ticket` object stores its own unique number (1, 2, 3) because the constructor assigns based on the current shared counter.
- This demonstrates **per-object state (instance)** vs. **class-wide shared state (static)** — a classic KTU viva question.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – Sessional Model Paper]` — 14 Marks

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is the difference between local and instance variables? Discuss with examples what happens when a local variable has the same name as an instance variable. How is it resolved?

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Local Variable | Instance Variable |
|--------|----------------|-------------------|
| Declaration | Inside method/block | Inside class, outside method |
| Memory | Stack | Heap (with object) |
| Default value | None | Type-specific default |
| Access modifier | Not allowed | Allowed |
| Lifetime | Block duration | Object lifetime |

**Name Conflict Scenario — Shadowing:**

When a local variable shares the same name as an instance variable, the local variable **shadows** (hides) the instance variable within that method's scope. The `this` keyword is used to access the shadowed instance variable.

```java
class Student {
    String name = "Default";   // instance variable

    void setName(String name) {   // parameter shadows instance
        this.name = name;          // disambiguates via 'this'
    }

    void show() {
        String name = "Local";    // local variable shadows instance
        System.out.println(name);          // prints "Local"
        System.out.println(this.name);     // prints instance value
    }
}
```

**Resolution Rule (KTU-Key Point):** Within a method body, the **nearest scope wins**. Use `this.fieldName` to forcibly access the instance variable.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of variable scope with reference to block scope, method scope, and class scope. Provide illustrative Java code.

**Model Answer:**

**1. Block Scope:** Variables declared inside any `{}` block (including loops and `if` statements) are visible only within that block.

```java
void blockDemo() {
    int a = 5;
    {
        int b = 10;          // 'b' visible only in this block
        System.out.println(a + b);   // legal, a is outer scope
    }
    System.out.println(b);   // COMPILE ERROR — b not in scope
}
```

**2. Method Scope:** Variables declared as parameters or directly inside a method are visible throughout the method.

```java
int add(int x, int y) {     // x, y in method scope
    int sum = x + y;        // sum in method scope
    return sum;
}
```

**3. Class Scope:** Instance and static variables declared in a class are visible to all methods of that class, subject to access modifiers (`private`, `protected`, `public`).

```java
class Circle {
    private double radius;     // class scope (private)
    static final double PI = 3.14;   // class scope (static, public-ish)

    double area() {
        return PI * radius * radius;   // radius & PI accessible here
    }

    void setRadius(double r) {
        radius = r;   // accessing class-scope instance variable
    }
}
```

**Scope Resolution Order (innermost to outermost):**
1. Local block variables
2. Method parameters
3. Instance variables (via `this`)
4. Class/static variables (via `ClassName`)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing default values:** Forgetting that *local* variables do NOT have defaults (most common error → 1–2 mark deduction).
> 2. **Using `this` inside a `static` method:** Causes compile error. Static context cannot access instance-specific `this` (2 marks lost).
> 3. **Forgetting the `static` keyword when class-wide sharing is needed:** Answer loses marks for not contrasting heap vs. method-area memory.
> 4. **Declaring access modifiers on local variables:** Illegal in Java — loses a mark if you write `public int x = 5;` inside a method.
> 5. **Not mentioning memory regions (Stack/Heap/Method Area):** KTU answer scripts without this term *explicitly* get 1 mark cut.
> 6. **Mixing parameter variables with local variables:** They behave similarly, but parameters are part of the method *signature* — examiners look for this distinction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: Variables**

- 🔹 **Four variable types:** Local, Instance, Static, Parameter.
- 🔹 **Default values exist ONLY for instance and static variables** (not local, not parameter).
- 🔹 **Local variables** live on the **stack**; **instance variables** live on the **heap**; **static variables** live in the **method area**.
- 🔹 **Static variables = one copy per class; instance variables = one copy per object.**
- 🔹 Access modifiers (`public`, `private`, `protected`, `default`) are **not allowed** on local or parameter variables.
- 🔹 The `this` keyword refers to the **current object** and is used to disambiguate shadowed instance variables.
- 🔹 Static methods can access static variables directly but need an object reference for instance variables.
- 🔹 Variable naming must follow Java identifier rules: start with a letter/`$`/`_`, no reserved keywords.
- 🔹 Lifetime of a local variable = block scope; lifetime of an instance variable = object lifetime; lifetime of a static variable = JVM/class lifetime.
- 🔹 **Exam mantra:** "Local = Stack, Instance = Heap, Static = Method Area, Parameter = Stack."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Type Conversion and Casting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT414 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Type Conversion and Casting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the Java programming language (the primary instructional vehicle for **PEECT414 - Object Oriented Programming** under the KTU 2024 Scheme), **Type Conversion** refers to the process of automatically or manually transforming a value of one primitive data type into another data type, or transforming a reference of one class type into another compatible class type within the inheritance hierarchy. This process is fundamentally classified into two principal categories: **Implicit Type Conversion** (also termed **Widening Conversion** or **Type Promotion**) and **Explicit Type Conversion** (also termed **Narrowing Conversion** or **Type Casting**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Concept):**
> Type conversion is the mechanism by which Java handles operations between mixed data types. It occurs implicitly when the destination type can safely hold all possible values of the source type without loss of information. Casting is the explicit instruction provided by the programmer to force a conversion where the compiler cannot guarantee data integrity.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are pouring water from a small **250 mL glass** into a large **1 Litre jug**. The water flows automatically without spilling — this is **Implicit Conversion (Widening)** because the destination container (the `int` variable) is bigger than the source (the `byte` variable).

Now, imagine pouring water back from the **1 Litre jug** into the small **250 mL glass**. You must do this carefully, manually, because the jug holds more water than the glass can contain — water *will spill*. This manual, deliberate action is **Explicit Casting (Narrowing)**. You are telling Java: *"I know what I am doing; truncate or accept potential data loss."*

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Rule of Thumb for Exams:**
> - **Small Box → Big Box** = Automatic (Widening) = Safe.
> - **Big Box → Small Box** = Manual (Casting) = Risky.

### 1.3 Why It Matters in Java

Java is a **strongly-typed (statically-typed) language**. Every variable has a fixed type known at compile-time. Therefore, the compiler must enforce strict rules whenever an expression mixes types. Without conversion rules, even a simple expression like `int + double` would be a compile-time error.

| Keyword | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| `int` | **32-bit** signed integer |
| `double` | **64-bit** IEEE-754 floating point |
| `long` | **64-bit** signed integer |
| `byte` | **8-bit** signed integer |

The table above references standard **JVM Primitive Specifications** which are essential constants for KTU numerical-type problems.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Pillars of Type Conversion

#### Pillar A: Implicit (Widening) Conversion
- **Who performs it?** The Java compiler, automatically.
- **When does it occur?** When a "smaller" type is assigned to a "larger" type, or in mixed-type expressions.
- **Data Loss?** **None.** Guaranteed safe by the Java Language Specification (JLS).
- **Casting Syntax Required?** **No.**

#### Pillar B: Explicit (Narrowing) Conversion
- **Who performs it?** The programmer, manually.
- **When does it occur?** When a "larger" type is forced into a "smaller" type.
- **Data Loss?** **Possible.** May result in truncation or overflow.
- **Casting Syntax Required?** **Yes** — use the target type in parentheses: `(targetType) value`.

### 2.2 The Widening Hierarchy (Ladder of Safety)

Java defines a strict, deterministic order for primitive widening conversions. Conversions are permitted *only* along the directed arrows (and their transitive paths) in the ladder below.

`byte` → `short` → `int` → `long` → `float` → `double`

Additionally, the single lateral widening is permitted:
`char` → `int` → `long` → `float` → `double`

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **`char` → `short` is ILLEGAL even though both are 16-bit.** The compiler treats them as incompatible because `char` is unsigned (range **0 to 65535**) and `short` is signed (range **-32768 to 32767**).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Conversion Cheat Sheet

| Source Type | Target Type | Method | Risk Factor | Example Syntax |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `byte` | `short` | Implicit | **0%** (Safe) | `short s = b;` |
| `short` | `int` | Implicit | **0%** (Safe) | `int i = s;` |
| `int` | `long` | Implicit | **0%** (Safe) | `long l = i;` |
| `long` | `float` | Implicit | *Low* (Precision) | `float f = l;` |
| `float` | `double` | Implicit | **0%** (Safe) | `double d = f;` |
| `double` | `int` | Explicit | **High** (Truncation) | `int i = (int) d;` |
| `int` | `byte` | Explicit | **High** (Overflow) | `byte b = (byte) i;` |
| `long` | `int` | Explicit | **High** (Overflow) | `int i = (int) l;` |
| `double` | `float` | Explicit | **Medium** (Rounding) | `float f = (float) d;` |
| `Object` (Reference) | Subclass | Explicit | **Runtime `ClassCastException`** | `Dog d = (Dog) animal;` |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **Embedded Systems Engineering** (a common KTU lateral pathway), sensor data frequently arrives as `double` from ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter) hardware, but the microcontroller's registers are **8-bit `byte`** registers. Explicit casting is the standard mechanism to pack sensor readings into hardware transmission buffers. In **High-Frequency Trading (HFT)** and **Telecommunications**, the deliberate truncation via narrowing cast is sometimes exploited to enforce fixed-point arithmetic behaviour, trading precision for execution speed.

### 2.5 Reference Type Casting (Beyond Primitives)

In OOP, "casting" also applies to **object references** within an inheritance chain:

1. **Upcasting (Implicit):** Converting a subclass reference to a superclass reference. Always safe.
   * `Animal a = new Dog();` (Dog IS-A Animal)
2. **Downcasting (Explicit):** Converting a superclass reference back to a subclass reference. Risky — requires an `instanceof` guard to avoid `ClassCastException` at runtime.
   * `Dog d = (Dog) a;`
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example: Implicit Widening in Arithmetic Expressions

Consider the following Java statement. We will trace the exact conversion logic step-by-step.

**Problem Code:**
```java
byte  b = 10;
int   i = 20;
double result = b + i * 2.5;
```

**Step 1 — Identify all operands and their types:**
* Operand `b` → type `byte`
* Operand `i` → type `int`
* Operand `2.5` → type `double` (default for floating literals)
* Operator `*` and `+` → binary arithmetic operators

**Step 2 — Evaluate the `*` operator first (higher precedence than `+`):**
The operands are `i` (`int`) and `2.5` (`double`). According to JLS §5.6.2 (Binary Numeric Promotion), if either operand is `double`, the other is promoted to `double`.

$$i_{promoted} = (double)\, 20 = 20.0$$

$$(int)\,20 \;\rightarrow\; (double)\,20.0$$
$$(double)\,20.0 \times (double)\,2.5 = (double)\,50.0$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the `+` operator:**
Now the operands are `b` (`byte`) and the result of Step 2 (`double 50.0`). Again, the `byte` is promoted to `double`.

$$b_{promoted} = (double)\,10 = 10.0$$

$$(double)\,10.0 + (double)\,50.0 = (double)\,60.0$$

**Step 4 — Assignment to `result`:**
`result` is declared as `double`. The right-hand side is already `double 60.0`. Assignment is a **widening assignment** — safe, implicit, and valid.

$$result = 60.0$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Exams:** Notice that `b + i * 2.5` is *not* evaluated as `(b + i) * 2.5`. Operator precedence binds `*` tighter than `+`. This is a classic KTU trick question.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example: Explicit Narrowing with Data Loss

**Problem Code:**
```java
double d = 129.987;
int    i = (int) d;
byte   b = (byte) i;
```

**Step 1 — Cast `double` to `int`:**
The fractional part `.987` is **discarded via truncation towards zero** (not rounding).

$$(int)\,129.987 = 129$$

**Step 2 — Cast `int` to `byte`:**
The value `129` exceeds `byte` maximum (`+127`). Java applies **two's complement modulo 256** arithmetic.

$$129 \pmod{256} = 129$$

Since `129 > 127`, it wraps into the negative range:

$$129 - 256 = -127$$

Therefore:

$$(byte)\,129 = -127$$

**Verification Table for `byte` Overflow:**

| Original `int` | `(byte) value` | Reason |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 127 | 127 | Max positive boundary |
| 128 | -128 | One wrap-around |
| 129 | -127 | Two wraps |
| 255 | -1 | Half cycle |
| 256 | 0 | Full cycle |

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Promotion Rules Cheat-Sheet

| Operand 1 Type | Operand 2 Type | Result Type | Promotion Direction |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `double` | *anything* | `double` | Promote other to `double` |
| `float` | *anything except `double`* | `float` | Promote other to `float` |
| `long` | *anything except `float`/`double`* | `long` | Promote other to `long` |
| `int` | `byte`, `short`, `char` | `int` | Promote smaller to `int` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** Even though `byte + byte` looks symmetric, both operands are individually promoted to `int` BEFORE the addition. Therefore, the expression `byte b = a + c;` (where `a` and `c` are `byte`) **will not compile** without an explicit cast back to `byte`.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Primitive Widening Hierarchy (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeByte["BYTE 8 bit"]
    nodeShort["SHORT 16 bit"]
    nodeChar["CHAR 16 bit unsigned"]
    nodeInt["INT 32 bit"]
    nodeLong["LONG 64 bit"]
    nodeFloat["FLOAT 32 bit IEEE"]
    nodeDouble["DOUBLE 64 bit IEEE"]

    nodeByte -- implicit widening --> nodeShort
    nodeShort -- implicit widening --> nodeInt
    nodeChar -- implicit widening --> nodeInt
    nodeInt -- implicit widening --> nodeLong
    nodeLong -- implicit widening --> nodeFloat
    nodeFloat -- implicit widening --> nodeDouble

    nodeDouble -. explicit cast required .-> nodeFloat
    nodeFloat -. explicit cast required .-> nodeLong
    nodeLong -. explicit cast required .-> nodeInt
    nodeInt -. explicit cast required .-> nodeShort
    nodeInt -. explicit cast required .-> nodeChar
```

### 4.2 Reference Type Casting: Upcasting vs Downcasting

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeAnimal["OBJECT Animal"]
    nodeDog["OBJECT Dog extends Animal"]
    nodePuppy["OBJECT Puppy extends Dog"]
    nodeCat["OBJECT Cat extends Animal"]

    nodePuppy -- implicit upcast --> nodeDog
    nodePuppy -- implicit upcast --> nodeAnimal
    nodeDog -- implicit upcast --> nodeAnimal

    nodeAnimal -. explicit downcast requires instanceof .-> nodeDog
    nodeAnimal -. explicit downcast requires instanceof .-> nodePuppy
    nodeDog -. explicit downcast requires instanceof .-> nodePuppy

    nodeAnimal -. ClassCastException if Cat .-> nodeCat
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Type Conversion Operation

```mermaid
graph TD
    inputA["SOURCE OPERAND raw value"]
    checkType{"CHECK TARGET TYPE SIZE"}
    safePath["IMPLICIT WIDENING PATH auto by compiler"]
    unsafePath["EXPLICIT CASTING PATH programmer invoked"]
    truncate["TRUNCATE OR MODULO 2^N"]
    finalStore["FINAL VARIABLE STORE typed memory cell"]

    inputA --> checkType
    checkType -- target larger or equal --> safePath
    checkType -- target smaller --> unsafePath
    unsafePath --> truncate
    safePath --> finalStore
    truncate --> finalStore
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The "check target type size" gate is the conceptual decision point that the Java compiler (implicit path) or the JVM runtime (explicit path) traverses. The "truncate or modulo" block is where data loss physically occurs in narrow conversions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Differentiate between implicit type conversion and explicit type casting in Java. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks Distribution):**

* **Definition of Implicit Conversion (1 Mark):** Implicit type conversion is the automatic transformation of a smaller data type to a larger data type performed by the Java compiler without any programmer intervention. It is also known as widening conversion and is always safe with no data loss. Example: `int i = 10; long l = i;` — here `int` is implicitly promoted to `long`.
