The variable is a *handle*, not the object itself. --- ### 1.3 Syntax Overview (Java — KTU Default) ```java ClassName referenceName; // Step 1: Declaration only (points to null) referenceName = new ClassName(); // Step 2: Instantiation + assignment // OR combined in one line: ClassName referenceName = new ClassName(); ``` **Two-Stage Rule (frequently tested in KTU):** $$ \text{Usable Object} = \underbrace{\text{Reference Variable}}_{\text{Stage 1: Compile-time}} \;+\; \underbrace{\text{new Constructor()}_{\text{Stage 2: Runtime}}} $$ ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Anatomy of an Object Declaration (Step-by-Step Logic) When the JVM (Java Virtual Machine) executes `Student s1 = new Student();`, **three** distinct events occur: 1. **Reference Variable Creation (Stack)** - The compiler reserves a memory slot named `s1` of type `Student` *reference* in the current stack frame. - At this instant, `s1` is automatically initialised to the literal `null` (Java safety feature; no garbage value). - **Size:** Usually 4 bytes (32-bit JVM) or 8 bytes (64-bit JVM with compressed oops) — independent of object size. 2. **Heap Allocation (Object Body)** - The `new` keyword triggers the JVM to compute the object's memory footprint: $$\text{Object Size} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{sizeof(field}_i) + \text{Object Header (8–16 bytes)} + \text{Padding}$$ - Memory is allocated on the **heap** (Eden region for new objects in young generation). - All instance fields are initialised to **default values**: * `int`, `long`, `short`, `byte` → `0` * `float`, `double` → `0.0` * `char` → `'\u0000'` * `boolean` → `false` * All reference types → `null` 3. **Constructor Invocation** - The `Student()` constructor executes, performing *explicit* initialisation (assigning values from parameter list or hard-coded defaults). - The address of the newly created heap object is returned and stored in `s1`. --- ### 2.2 The "Two-Stage" Model — Exam-Ready Visualisation | Stage | Statement Fragment | What Happens | Memory Region | Value of Reference | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Stage 1** | `Student s1;` | Variable created | Stack | `null` | | **Stage 2** | `new Student();` | Object built | Heap | (address returned) | | **Stage 3** | `s1 = new Student();` | Reference bound | Stack ← Heap link | (valid address) | --- ### 2.3 Types of Object Declaration Patterns (KTU High-Yield) | Pattern | Code | Effect | Exam Importance | |---|---|---|---| | **Declaration only** | `Student s;` | `s` is `null`; accessing fields throws `NullPointerException` | ⭐⭐⭐ | | **Combined** | `Student s = new Student();` | Most common; one usable object | ⭐⭐⭐ | | **Multiple refs, one object** | `Student s1 = new Student(); Student s2 = s1;` | Both refs point to *same* heap object | ⭐⭐⭐ | | **Anonymous object** | `new Student().display();` | Object created, used once, eligible for GC | ⭐⭐ | | **Array of objects** | `Student[] arr = new Student[5];` | Array of *references*; each element is `null` by default | ⭐⭐⭐ | | **No `new` → no object** | `Student s;` (no `new`) | Compile OK, Runtime `null` | ⭐⭐⭐ | --- ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Rule / Formula | Default Value | Memory Location | |---|---|---|---| | Reference size | Fixed pointer size | N/A | Stack | | Object size | $\sum \text{field sizes} + \text{header} + \text{padding}$ | N/A | Heap | | `int` field default | $0$ | $0$ | Inside object on heap | | `double` field default | $0.0$ | $0.0$ | Inside object on heap | | `boolean` field default | $\text{false}$ | $\text{false}$ | Inside object on heap | | `char` field default | `'\u0000'` | `\u0000` | Inside object on heap | | Reference type field default | $\text{null}$ | $\text{null}$ | Inside object on heap | | Uninitialised local reference | $\text{null}$ (Java) / undefined (C++) | $\text{null}$ | Stack | | Aliasing condition | $s1 == s2 \iff \text{same heap address}$ | N/A | Comparison | | Reachability for GC | Reference chain from any stack/active ref to object | N/A | Heap | > [!NOTE] > In C++ (covered in KTU syllabus comparisons), `Student s;` (no `new`) creates the object on the *stack* directly — no separate `new` step. This is a **favourite comparative question** in KTU exams. --- ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Android Development:** Every `Activity`, `View`, and `Adapter` is declared as an object reference and inflated via `new` or factory methods. - **Spring Boot / Enterprise:** Dependency Injection (IoC containers) declares object references and the framework calls `new` (or proxies) on your behalf — but the underlying *declaring objects* mechanism is identical. - **Game Development (Unity):** `GameObject enemy = Instantiate(prefab);` follows the exact same two-stage pattern. --- ### 2.6 Common Pitfalls (Why Students Lose Marks) - Confusing **reference comparison** (`s1 == s2`) with **content comparison** (`.equals()`). - Assuming `Student s;` creates an object — it does not. - Forgetting that an *array of objects* holds **references**, not objects — the elements are `null` until individually `new`ed. - Believing reassigning a reference destroys the old object — it only makes it *eligible* for Garbage Collection. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Memory Footprint of a Declared Object **Problem (KTU-style):** Given the class: ```java class Student { int rollNo; // 4 bytes String name; // 4 bytes (reference) double marks; // 8 bytes boolean isActive; // 1 byte } ``` Find the size of one `Student` object on a 64-bit JVM (with compressed oops, object header = 12 bytes, alignment = 8 bytes). **Step-by-Step Derivation:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Raw field size} &= \text{sizeof(int)} + \text{sizeof(String ref)} + \text{sizeof(double)} + \text{sizeof(boolean)} \\ &= 4 + 4 + 8 + 1 \\ &= 17 \text{ bytes} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Add object header} &= 17 + 12 = 29 \text{ bytes} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Align to 8-byte boundary} &= \lceil 29 / 8 \rceil \times 8 = 4 \times 8 = 32 \text{ bytes} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \boxed{\therefore \text{ One Student object} = 32 \text{ bytes on the heap }} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Exam Tip:** KTU rarely asks exact byte calculations, but this derivation demonstrates the *principle* of how an object is laid out in memory — a frequent 7-mark question. --- ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Tracing Object Declarations Given the code: ```java Student s1 = new Student(); Student s2 = new Student(); s2 = s1; s1.rollNo = 10; System.out.println(s2.rollNo); ``` **Exhaustive Trace:** | Step | Statement | Stack `s1` | Stack `s2` | Heap Object A (`rollNo`) | Heap Object B (`rollNo`) | Output | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | `Student s1 = new Student();` | addr of A | — | 0 (default) | — | — | | 2 | `Student s2 = new Student();` | addr of A | addr of B | 0 | 0 | — | | 3 | `s2 = s1;` | addr of A | **addr of A** (overwritten) | 0 | 0 | — | | 4 | `s1.rollNo = 10;` | addr of A | addr of A | **10** | 0 (orphaned) | — | | 5 | `println(s2.rollNo);` | addr of A | addr of A | 10 | 0 (GC eligible) | **10** | **Logical Deduction (Worded for Exam):** - **Step 1:** `s1` is created and points to a new object (Object A) on the heap with default `rollNo = 0`. - **Step 2:** `s2` is created and points to a *different* new object (Object B) on the heap. - **Step 3:** The address stored in `s1` is copied into `s2`. Now `s1` and `s2` **both** point to Object A. Object B has no references pointing to it. - **Step 4:** Setting `s1.rollNo = 10` modifies Object A's field. Object B remains untouched but is unreachable. - **Step 5:** Since `s2` also points to Object A, accessing `s2.rollNo` returns `10`. Object B becomes eligible for Garbage Collection. --- ### 3.3 Worked Example: Array of Objects (Classic KTU Question) **Problem:** What is the output? ```java class Box { int value; } public class Test { public static void main(String[] args) { Box[] arr = new Box[3]; for (int i = 0; i < 3; i++) arr[i] = new Box(); arr[0].value = 5; arr[1].value = arr[0].value * 2; arr[2].value = arr[1].value + arr[0].value; System.out.println(arr[2].value); } } ``` **Step-by-Step Deduction:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{arr[0].value} &= 5 \\ \text{arr[1].value} &= \text{arr[0].value} \times 2 = 5 \times 2 = 10 \\ \text{arr[2].value} &= \text{arr[1].value} + \text{arr[0].value} = 10 + 5 = 15 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \boxed{\text{Output: } 15} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Trap Answer:** If a student writes `new Box[3]` and assumes three Box objects are created, they will get `NullPointerException` because the *array slots* are `null` until individually assigned. The array only contains **3 references**, not 3 objects. --- ### 3.4 Comparison Matrix: Java vs C++ Declaration | Feature | Java | C++ | |---|---|---| | `Student s;` creates object? | ❌ No (only reference, value = `null`) | ✅ Yes (stack object) | | `Student s = new Student();` | Mandatory for object on heap | Optional — heap object, manual `delete` | | Memory deallocation | Automatic (Garbage Collector) | Manual (`delete s;`) or smart pointers | | Dangling reference risk | No (GC prevents it) | Yes (programmer error) | | Default initialisation | All fields to defaults | Primitive fields are *uninitialised* (garbage) | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — Two-Stage Object Declaration ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Source Code Line: Student s1 = new Student] --> B{Compiler Phase} B --> C[Reference Variable s1 created on Stack] C --> D[Initial value of s1 set to null] D --> E{Runtime Phase: new keyword} E --> F[Memory allocated on Heap] F --> G[Fields initialised to defaults] G --> H[Constructor invoked] H --> I[Heap address returned] I --> J[Address stored in s1] J --> K[Object fully accessible via s1] ``` --- ### 4.2 Memory Layout Diagram — Stack and Heap Relationship ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Stack["STACK MEMORY"] S1["s1
Reference
Value: 0xABC"] S2["s2
Reference
Value: 0xABC"] S3["s3
Reference
Value: null"] end subgraph Heap["HEAP MEMORY"] O1["Object A
rollNo: 10
name: ref to String
marks: 95.5
Address: 0xABC"] O2["Object B
rollNo: 0
name: null
marks: 0.0
Address: 0xDEF"] O3["Object C
UNREACHABLE
Eligible for GC"] end S1 -. points to .-> O1 S2 -. points to .-> O1 S3 -. points to nothing .-> X[" "] O2 -. no reference from stack .-> O3 ``` --- ### 4.3 Lifecycle Flow — Object Declaration, Use, and Garbage Collection ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Declared: Student s1; Declared --> Instantiated: new Student() Instantiated --> InUse: Reference assigned and accessed InUse --> InUse: Methods called, fields modified InUse --> Reassigned: s1 = new Student() Reassigned --> Orphaned: Previous object loses all references Orphaned --> GarbageCollected: JVM GC runs GarbageCollected --> [*] InUse --> [*]: Program ends ``` --- ### 4.4 Class-Level Architecture — Reference vs Object ```mermaid flowchart TD ClassDef["CLASS DEFINITION
(Blueprint, in Method Area)"] --> Fields["Field Declarations
int rollNo
String name
double marks"] ClassDef --> Methods["Method Declarations
void display()
void setMarks()"] Fields --> ObjInst1["Object Instance 1
rollNo=1, name='A', marks=80.0"] Fields --> ObjInst2["Object Instance 2
rollNo=2, name='B', marks=90.0"] Fields --> ObjInst3["Object Instance 3
rollNo=3, name='C', marks=75.5"] Methods -. shared by all .-> ObjInst1 Methods -. shared by all .-> ObjInst2 Methods -. shared by all .-> ObjInst3 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Exam Tip:** This diagram illustrates the *class-object relationship* — methods occupy one copy in the Method Area, while fields are duplicated per object. KTU frequently asks: *"Are methods part of each object?"* — Answer: **No, only the method reference is stored; the code is shared.** ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *What is the difference between declaring a reference and instantiating an object in Java? Illustrate with an example.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** Declaring a reference (e.g., `Student s;`) creates a *variable* of type `Student` in the stack memory that is initialised to `null` — **no object exists yet**. Instantiation (e.g., `new Student();`) allocates memory on the heap, initialises the instance variables to default values, invokes the constructor, and returns a memory address. The statement `Student s = new Student();` combines both steps, making `s` a usable reference to a real heap object. Without `new`, `s` is a `null` reference and any field/method access throws `NullPointerException` at runtime. *(1 mark: define declaration; 1 mark: define instantiation; 1 mark: example + consequence)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Explain what happens when you write `Employee[] arr = new Employee[5];` in Java. Are 5 Employee objects created?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** No, only **one** array object containing **5 `null` references** is created — not 5 Employee objects. The statement `new Employee[5]` allocates an array of 5 slots on the heap, each slot initially holding `null`. To create actual Employee objects, each slot must be individually initialised using `new Employee()`, e.g., `arr[0] = new Employee();`. This is a common source of `NullPointerException` in beginner code. *(1 mark: clarify array of references; 1 mark: default null; 1 mark: need individual `new`)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]** **(a)** Explain the process of object declaration and instantiation in Java with a suitable example. Differentiate clearly between *reference variable* and *object instance* in terms of memory location, size, and lifetime. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Consider the following code segment. Predict the output with proper justification, and draw the stack-heap memory diagram after execution of each line. *(7 marks)* ```java class Account { int balance; Account(int b) { balance = b; } } public class Bank { public static void main(String[] args) { Account a1 = new Account(1000); Account a2 = a1; a1.balance = 2000; a2 = new Account(5000); a2.balance = a1.balance + 1000; System.out.println(a1.balance + " " + a2.balance); } } ``` --- **Model Solution:** **(a) — 7 marks:** - **Declaration (2 marks):** `Account a1;` reserves a 4/8-byte slot named `a1` in the *stack frame* of `main()`. The slot stores a reference (memory address) and is initialised to `null`. No heap allocation occurs. - **Instantiation (2 marks):** `new Account(1000)` performs: 1. Heap allocation (≈ object header + `int balance` field + padding). 2. Default field initialisation (`balance = 0`). 3. Constructor execution (`balance = 1000`). 4. Returns heap address → stored in `a1`. - **Differences (3 marks):** | Aspect | Reference Variable | Object Instance | |---|---|---| | Memory location | Stack | Heap | | Size | Fixed (4 or 8 bytes) | Sum of fields + header | | Holds | Address of object | Actual field values | | Created by | Compiler | `new` operator | | Lifetime | Method scope | Until GC-eligible | --- **(b) — 7 marks:** | Step | Statement | `a1` points to | `a2` points to | Object at A.balance | Object at B.balance | New Object? | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | `Account a1 = new Account(1000);` | Obj A | — | 1000 | — | A created | | 2 | `Account a2 = a1;` | Obj A | Obj A | 1000 | — | — | | 3 | `a1.balance = 2000;` | Obj A | Obj A | **2000** | — | — | | 4 | `a2 = new Account(5000);` | Obj A | **Obj B** | 2000 | 5000 | B created | | 5 | `a2.balance = a1.balance + 1000;` | Obj A | Obj B | 2000 | **3000** | — | **Output (1 mark):** $$ \boxed{2000 \; 3000} $$ **Justification (2 marks):** After step 3, both `a1` and `a2` alias the same object (A). In step 4, `a2` is reassigned to a new object (B); `a1` still refers to A. In step 5, `a2.balance = 2000 + 1000 = 3000`. So `a1.balance = 2000` (A unchanged) and `a2.balance = 3000` (B updated). --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 — Alternative]** **(a)** What is a `null` reference? Explain with an example what happens when you try to access a member through a `null` reference. How does this differ between Java and C++? *(7 marks)* **(b)** Write a Java program to create a class `Book` with fields `title`, `author`, and `price`. Demonstrate: *(i)* declaration without instantiation, *(ii)* combined declaration and instantiation, *(iii)* two references pointing to the same object, and *(iv)* an array of 4 `Book` references. Show the output and memory state for each case. *(7 marks)* --- **Model Solution:** **(a) — 7 marks:** - **Definition (2 marks):** A `null` reference is a reference variable that does not point to any object in memory. In Java, all uninitialised object-type local variables default to `null`; in C++, they are undefined (contain garbage). - **Java behaviour (2 marks):** Accessing a field or method on a `null` reference throws `NullPointerException` at runtime, e.g., ```java Book b; System.out.println(b.title); // NullPointerException ``` - **C++ behaviour (2 marks):** Dereferencing a `null` pointer is **undefined behaviour** — it may crash, return garbage, or corrupt memory. C++ does not initialise references to `null` automatically. - **Key difference (1 mark):** Java is *safe-by-default* (runtime checks); C++ is *unsafe* but *faster* (no runtime overhead). --- **(b) — 7 marks (Code + Output):** ```java class Book { String title; String author; double price; } public class Library { public static void main(String[] args) { // (i) Declaration only Book b1; // System.out.println(b1.title); // Would throw NullPointerException // (ii) Combined declaration + instantiation Book b2 = new Book(); b2.title = "OOP"; b2.author = "KTU"; b2.price = 500.0; System.out.println(b2.title); // Output: OOP // (iii) Two references, one object Book b3 = new Book(); Book b4 = b3; // b3 and b4 → same object b3.price = 750.0; System.out.println(b4.price); // Output: 750.0 (aliased) // (iv) Array of 4 references Book[] shelf = new Book[4]; // 4 null slots, no Book objects yet for (int i = 0; i < 4; i++) shelf[i] = new Book(); shelf[0].title = "Java"; shelf[1].title = "Python"; shelf[2].title = "C++"; shelf[3].title = "DBMS"; for (Book b : shelf) System.out.print(b.title + " "); // Output: Java Python C++ DBMS } } ``` **Memory state at each stage (3 marks):** - After (i): `b1` exists in stack with value `null`; no heap allocation. - After (ii): `b2` in stack points to a new Book object on heap with fields filled. - After (iii): `b3` and `b4` both point to the *same* Book object — modifying via `b3` reflects in `b4`. - After (iv): Array of 4 Book objects allocated on heap; array `shelf` on stack holds base address. **Output (1 mark):** ``` OOP 750.0 Java Python C++ DBMS ``` --- ### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Points:** > 1. Writing `Student s;` and claiming an object is created — **deduct 1 mark**. > 2. Confusing `s1 == s2` (reference/address comparison) with `s1.equals(s2)` (content comparison) — **deduct 1–2 marks** if asked about object comparison. > 3. Forgetting that `new Book[5]` creates 5 *null references*, not 5 objects — **deduct 1 mark** in array-related questions. > 4. Failing to mention that methods are *shared* across objects (stored once in Method Area) — **deduct 1 mark** in "what is part of an object" questions. > 5. Confusing *stack* and *heap* memory locations in the diagram — **deduct up to 2 marks** in diagram-based questions. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **Object declaration = Reference + Instantiation** (two separate steps). - ✅ **`new` is mandatory** in Java to create objects on the heap; C++ can create stack objects without `new`. - ✅ **Reference lives in stack; object lives in heap.** - ✅ **Default initialisation** of fields: `0`, `0.0`, `false`, `'\u0000'`, `null` for references. - ✅ **Multiple references can point to the same object** (aliasing); modifying via one affects the other. - ✅ **Arrays of objects** contain `null` references by default — you must `new` each element. - ✅ **Reassigning a reference does not destroy the object** — it just makes the old object GC-eligible. - ✅ **`null` is the default value** of any uninitialised object-type reference in Java (unlike C++). - ✅ **Methods are shared**; only fields are per-instance. The class itself is stored in the Method Area. - ✅ **Anonymous objects** (`new Student().display();`) are unreferenced immediately after use → GC candidates. > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "DRI-N-HA":** > **D**eclare reference → **R**eturns to `null` → **I**nstantiate with `new` → **N**ew memory in heap → **H**eader + fields + padding → **A**ddress bound to reference.

topics.title: Object Reference
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT414 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Object Reference # Object Reference in Java — KTU 2024 Premium Notes ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In Object-Oriented Programming (OOP), an **Object Reference** is a typed variable that stores the **memory address** (or a logical handle) of an object allocated in the **heap memory**, rather than the object itself. In Java, unlike C/C++, the programmer never manipulates the raw address directly — the JVM abstracts it into a secure handle. The reference acts as a **remote control** pointing to the object living in heap memory. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEECT414 – Module 2):** > Object reference is a foundational concept that precedes topics like **Inheritance, Polymorphism, Method Overriding, and Abstract Classes**. A strong grasp of how references bind to objects is mandatory to score in Part B (14-mark) questions on dynamic dispatch and runtime polymorphism. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The TV Remote Imagine a **television set** sitting in your living room (the *object* in heap memory). The **remote control** is the *object reference* sitting in your hand (the *local variable* in stack memory). - The TV (object) is a heavy, physical entity occupying space. - The remote (reference) is a lightweight pointer — it does not contain the TV, only knows how to reach it. - Pressing buttons on the remote (calling methods) sends a signal to the actual TV. - **Two remotes** can be configured to control the **same TV** (aliasing). - A remote with **no TV paired** does nothing when buttons are pressed (`NullPointerException`). > [!NOTE] > **Critical Distinction:** A reference is **not** the object. It is a *typed handle* that *refers to* an object. The size of a reference is fixed by the JVM (typically **4 bytes on 32-bit JVM** and **8 bytes on 64-bit JVM** with compressed oops), independent of the object's actual size. ### 1.3 Reference Variable — Definition A **reference variable** is declared with a class type (or interface type, or array type) and is capable of holding a reference to any object of that type or its subtypes (polymorphic reference). Example declaration syntax: ```java Box myBox; // Declares a reference 'myBox' of type Box myBox = new Box(); // Points myBox to a newly created Box object ``` The keyword **`new`** allocates memory on the heap and returns the address — this address is then stored in the reference variable. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ### 2.1 Anatomy of Object Creation — The 4-Stage Process When you write `Box b1 = new Box();`, the JVM performs four distinct operations: 1. **Declaration** — `Box b1;` The compiler reserves a slot named `b1` of type *reference-to-Box* on the **stack frame** of the current method. No object exists yet. 2. **Instantiation** — `new Box()` The JVM allocates contiguous bytes on the **heap** large enough to hold all instance variables of `Box` (plus object header). The memory is zero-initialized. 3. **Initialization** — Constructor invocation The constructor (default or parameterized) runs, assigning meaningful values to instance variables. 4. **Binding** — Assignment to reference The heap address of the newly constructed object is stored into reference `b1`. From now on, `b1` is said to "point to" or "refer to" that object. > [!TIP] > **Why it matters for KTU:** Exam questions often test whether you understand that **declaration alone does not create an object**. A common trick question: *"After `Box b1;` is executed, can `b1.volume()` be called?"* — **No**, because `b1` is uninitialized (holds Java's default `null`). ### 2.2 Memory Architecture — Stack vs Heap | Memory Region | Stores | Lifetime | Thread Safety | Access Speed | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Stack** | Primitive local variables, reference variables (handles), method call frames | Tied to method invocation (LIFO) | Thread-private | **Very Fast** | | **Heap** | Actual objects, instance variables, arrays | Until Garbage Collected (GC) | Shared across threads | Slower than stack | | **Method Area** | Class metadata, static variables, constant pool | JVM lifetime | Shared | — | | **PC Register** | Current instruction address per thread | Per thread | Thread-private | — | > [!IMPORTANT] > The reference lives on the **stack**, the object lives on the **heap**. KTU examiners frequently draw a memory diagram in Part B and ask you to label what goes where. Master this distinction. ### 2.3 Types of Reference Behavior in Java | Behavior | Description | Code Trigger | |---|---|---| | **Single Reference** | One variable points to one object | `Box b = new Box();` | | **Aliasing** | Multiple references point to the **same** object | `Box b2 = b1;` | | **Null Reference** | Reference points to nothing | `Box b = null;` | | **Polymorphic Reference** | Superclass reference holds subclass object | `Animal a = new Dog();` | | **Reassignment** | Reference detached and rebound to new object | `b = new Box();` | | **Lost Reference** | Object becomes unreachable → eligible for GC | `b = null;` (when no other refs) | ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Concept | Rule / Formula | Exam Use | |---|---|---| | Reference size | Fixed per JVM (4 or 8 bytes) | 2-mark question | | `==` operator on references | Compares **memory addresses** (identity), not content | Common trap | | `.equals()` method | Compares **logical content** (overridden in classes) | Conceptual difference | | `null` literal | Valid value for any reference type | Default for uninitialized refs | | `this` keyword | Hidden reference passed to every instance method; points to **current invoking object** | Method chaining, setters | | Garbage Collection eligibility | An object is eligible when **no live reference** points to it | Theory questions | | Parameter passing | Java is **Pass-by-Value** (the value of the reference is copied) | Frequently asked in ESE | ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **GUI Frameworks (Swing/JavaFX):** Each UI component is an object; event listeners hold references to update them dynamically. - **Database Connection Pooling:** Connection objects are passed by reference across multiple client threads. - **Microservices / Spring Boot:** Dependency Injection (DI) containers inject object references of services into controllers — without understanding object references, DI is a black box. - **Android Development:** `findViewById()` returns an object reference to a UI widget declared in XML. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Code Analysis & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Trace 1 — Single Reference Creation ```java class Student { String name; int rollNo; } public class Demo { public static void main(String[] args) { Student s1 = new Student(); s1.name = "Anu"; s1.rollNo = 101; } } ``` **Step-by-step memory trace:** | Step | Code Executed | Stack (main method) | Heap (Object #100) | Explanation | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | `Student s1 = new Student();` (declaration) | `s1 → null` | — | Stack slot reserved, default value `null` | | 2 | `new Student()` (instantiation) | `s1 → null` | Object #100 allocated: `name=null, rollNo=0` | Heap memory allocated, fields default-initialized | | 3 | Assignment `=` (binding) | `s1 → #100` | Object #100 allocated: `name=null, rollNo=0` | Address #100 stored in `s1` | | 4 | `s1.name = "Anu";` | `s1 → #100` | Object #100: `name="Anu", rollNo=0` | Instance variable modified via dot operator | | 5 | `s1.rollNo = 101;` | `s1 → #100` | Object #100: `name="Anu", rollNo=101` | Object fully initialized | ### 3.2 Trace 2 — Aliasing (Multiple References, One Object) ```java Student s1 = new Student(); // Object #200 created s1.name = "Rahul"; Student s2 = s1; // s2 copies the address, NOT the object s2.name = "Priya"; System.out.println(s1.name); // What is printed? ``` **Logical derivation:** $$ \text{At step 2: } s1 = \text{address}(\#200) $$ $$ \text{At step 3: } s2 = s1 = \text{address}(\#200) $$ $$ \text{Both } s1 \text{ and } s2 \text{ point to the SAME object \#200} $$ $$ \text{At step 4: } s2.name = \text{"Priya"} \rightarrow \text{Object \#200.name = "Priya"} $$ $$ \therefore \text{Output: } s1.name = \text{"Priya"} \quad \blacksquare $$ > [!NOTE] > This is the **classic KTU trick question**. Many students wrongly predict "Rahul" because they assume `s2 = s1` copies the object. **References copy addresses, not objects.** ### 3.3 Trace 3 — `this` Reference Inside Constructors ```java class Rectangle { int length, breadth; Rectangle(int length, int breadth) { this.length = length; // LHS = instance var, RHS = parameter this.breadth = breadth; } } ``` **Logical derivation of `this`:** $$ \text{At runtime, JVM implicitly passes: } \text{this} = \text{address of current object} $$ $$ \text{When } \texttt{new Rectangle(10, 5)} \text{ is called, internally: } $$ $$ \text{this} \rightarrow \text{address of newly created Rectangle object} $$ $$ \text{this.length} = 10 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{instance variable of THIS object = 10} $$ $$ \text{length (RHS)} = 10 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{local parameter value} $$ **Why `this` is mandatory here:** Without `this`, the statement `length = length;` would assign the parameter to itself, leaving the instance variable at its default value `0`. The `this` keyword disambiguates the **shadowed identifier**. ### 3.4 Trace 4 — Pass-by-Value of References (Critical KTU Concept) ```java class Number { int value; } public class Test { static void modify(Number n) { n.value = 99; // (A) Modifies the caller's object n = new Number(); // (B) Reassigns local copy only n.value = 500; } public static void main(String[] args) { Number obj = new Number(); obj.value = 10; modify(obj); System.out.println(obj.value); // Predict the output } } ``` **Analytical derivation:** $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{At main(): } obj \rightarrow \text{Object \#500}, \quad \text{Object \#500.value} = 10 \\ &\text{Call modify(obj): a COPY of the address \#500 is passed as } n \\ &\text{At (A): } n.value = 99 \rightarrow \text{Object \#500.value} = 99 \quad \text{(visible to main)} \\ &\text{At (B): } n = \text{new Number()} \rightarrow n \text{ now points to Object \#700} \\ &\text{At (C): } n.value = 500 \rightarrow \text{Object \#700.value} = 500 \\ &\text{After modify() returns: } obj \text{ still points to Object \#500} \\ &\therefore \text{Output: } obj.value = 99 \quad \blacksquare \end{aligned} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often say "Java is pass-by-reference" because the original object's value changed at (A). **The truth:** Java is **always pass-by-value** — the *value* of the reference (i.e., the address) is copied. The original `obj` in `main` is untouched, which is why reassignment in (B) had no effect on the caller. ### 3.5 Trace 5 — Null Reference & Garbage Collection ```java Employee e1 = new Employee("Anu"); // Object #A created Employee e2 = new Employee("Ravi"); // Object #B created e1 = e2; // e1 now points to #B; #A is unreachable e2 = null; // e2 is detached; #B still has e1 → reachable e1 = null; // #B now unreachable → eligible for GC ``` **GC eligibility condition:** $$ \text{An object is GC-eligible} \iff \text{Number of live references pointing to it} = 0 $$ | Line | e1 | e2 | Object #A | Object #B | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | →#A | — | reachable | — | — | | 2 | →#A | →#B | reachable | reachable | Two objects, two refs | | 3 | →#B | →#B | **unreachable** | reachable | #A is now garbage | | 4 | →#B | null | unreachable | reachable | #B survives via e1 | | 5 | null | null | unreachable | **unreachable** | #B is now garbage | > [!NOTE] > Java does **not** guarantee immediate destruction of unreachable objects. The JVM decides *when* to invoke the Garbage Collector. You can *request* GC via `System.gc()`, but it is only a hint. ### 3.6 Trace 6 — `==` vs `.equals()` on References ```java String s1 = new String("KTU"); String s2 = new String("KTU"); s1 == s2 // false (different heap addresses) s1.equals(s2) // true (same character sequence) ``` **Logical proof:** $$ \begin{aligned} &s1 = \text{new String("KTU")} \rightarrow s1 = \text{address}(\#A) \\ &s2 = \text{new String("KTU")} \rightarrow s2 = \text{address}(\#B) \\ &s1 == s2 \iff s1.\text{address} == s2.\text{address} \iff \text{\#A} == \text{\#B} \iff \text{false} \\ &s1.\text{equals}(s2) \iff s1.\text{content} == s2.\text{content} \iff \text{"KTU"} == \text{"KTU"} \iff \text{true} \end{aligned} $$ --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Memory Architecture — Reference vs Object ```mermaid graph TD subgraph STK["STACK MEMORY - main method"] A["s1
ref: 0x4A2F"] B["s2
ref: 0x4A2F"] C["s3
ref: 0x7C81"] end subgraph HEP["HEAP MEMORY"] D["Object 0x4A2F
name: Anu
rollNo: 101"] E["Object 0x7C81
name: Ravi
rollNo: 102"] end A -.->|points to| D B -.->|points to| D C -.->|points to| E style STK fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd style HEP fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#ef6c00 style D fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32 style E fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32 ``` ### 4.2 Object Lifecycle & Reference Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Declare Reference
Box b1 = null"] --> B["new Box()
Allocate on Heap"] B --> C["Constructor Runs
Initialize Fields"] C --> D["Bind Reference
b1 points to object"] D --> E["Use Object
b1.method called"] E --> F{"Reference Still
Reachable?"} F -->|Yes| E F -->|No - all refs null
or reassigned| G["Eligible for
Garbage Collection"] G --> H["GC Sweeps Memory
Memory Reclaimed"] style A fill:#bbdefb style B fill:#fff9c4 style C fill:#c8e6c9 style D fill:#ffccbc style E fill:#f8bbd0 style F fill:#e1bee7 style G fill:#ffcdd2 style H fill:#d1c4e9 ``` ### 4.3 Aliasing Visualization ```mermaid graph LR R1["Reference s1
address: 0x200"] --> OBJ["Object Student
0x200
name: Anu
rollNo: 101"] R2["Reference s2
address: 0x200"] --> OBJ R3["Reference s3
address: 0x200"] --> OBJ style R1 fill:#b3e5fc style R2 fill:#b3e5fc style R3 fill:#b3e5fc style OBJ fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 ``` ### 4.4 Method Call — `this` Reference Flow ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Main as main method participant Obj as Student Object (0x500) participant This as Hidden 'this' reference Main->>Obj: new Student("Anu", 101) Note over Obj: Constructor invoked Obj->>This: this = address(0x500) This->>Obj: this.name = "Anu" This->>Obj: this.rollNo = 101 Obj-->>Main: Returns reference 0x500 Main->>This: s1.display() - implicit 'this' = s1 This->>Obj: Execute display() on THIS object ``` ### 4.5 Reference Comparison Flow (`==` vs `.equals()`) ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Two String references
s1, s2"] --> B{"s1 == s2 ?"} B -->|Compares addresses| C["Checks if both refs
point to SAME object"] C --> D["Returns true ONLY if
same heap address"] A --> E["s1.equals(s2) ?"] E --> F["Invokes String.equals method"] F --> G["Compares character-by-character
content"] G --> H["Returns true if
content matches"] style B fill:#fff9c4 style E fill:#c8e6c9 ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]** *Differentiate between an object and an object reference. Explain with a suitable example.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Object | Object Reference | |---|---|---| | **Definition** | A runtime instance of a class occupying heap memory | A typed variable storing the address of an object | | **Memory Location** | Heap | Stack (local refs) or Heap (instance refs) | | **Size** | Depends on instance variables (variable) | Fixed per JVM (4 or 8 bytes) | | **Created by** | `new` keyword | Declaration syntax | | **Existence** | Exists in memory until GC | Exists as long as the scope is alive | **Example:** ```java Student s = new Student(); // 's' is the reference, the actual object is on heap ``` Here, `s` is a reference variable (a handle) on the stack, while `new Student()` creates the actual object on the heap. The reference `s` "points to" the object. Two references can point to the same object (aliasing), but one object can have many references. *(1 mark for clear definition of object, 1 mark for clear definition of reference, 1 mark for example and distinction)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]** *What is the significance of the `this` keyword in Java? When is it mandatory to use it?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The `this` keyword is a **reference variable** that refers to the **current invoking object** — the object on which a method or constructor is currently being executed. The JVM implicitly passes `this` as a hidden first argument to every non-static method. **Significance:** 1. **Disambiguates instance variables from parameters** when they share the same name (shadowing). 2. Used to **pass the current object as a parameter** to another method. 3. Used to **invoke one constructor from another** (constructor chaining: `this(...)`). 4. Used to **return the current object** from a method for method chaining. **When mandatory:** It is mandatory when a parameter or local variable has the same name as an instance variable, as in `this.length = length;`, where without `this`, both occurrences of `length` would refer to the parameter, leaving the instance variable unassigned. ```java class Box { int length; Box(int length) { this.length = length; } // mandatory disambiguation } ``` *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for uses/significance, 1 mark for example showing mandatory use)* --- ### 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- ## ✏️ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – July 2024, Model] **(a)** Explain the concept of object reference in Java. Discuss the difference between **pass-by-value** and **pass-by-reference** with respect to object references. Write a Java program to demonstrate that Java is strictly **pass-by-value**, even when passing object references. **(7 marks)** **(b)** What is **aliasing**? Write a Java program that demonstrates aliasing, where two reference variables point to the same object and modifications through one reference are visible through the other. Explain the output with a memory diagram. **(7 marks)** --- ### ✅ Model Solution for Question A #### Part (a) — Object Reference & Pass-by-Value (7 marks) **Conceptual Explanation (3 marks):** An **object reference** in Java is a typed handle stored in a variable that contains the **memory address** of an object allocated on the heap. The programmer does not directly access this address; the JVM manages it. The reference is what allows the program to invoke methods and access fields of the object via the dot (`.`) operator. **Pass-by-Value vs Pass-by-Reference:** | Parameter Passing | Definition | Java? | |---|---|---| | Pass-by-Value | A **copy** of the actual value is passed to the parameter. Modifications inside the method do not affect the original. | ✅ **Java uses this** | | Pass-by-Reference | The **actual variable** (memory location) is passed. Modifications inside the method are reflected in the caller. | ❌ Java does NOT use this | When passing an object reference, Java **copies the address** (the value of the reference) into the method parameter. So the method and the caller both hold references to the *same* object — modifications to the *object's fields* are visible outside, but reassignment of the parameter to a new object is **not** visible outside. **Java Program (4 marks):** ```java class Number { int value; Number(int v) { this.value = v; } } public class PassByValueDemo { static void modify(Number n) { n.value = 999; // Modifies the actual object (visible outside) n = new Number(500); // Reassigns local copy (NOT visible outside) n.value = 888; System.out.println("Inside modify(): n.value = " + n.value); } public static void main(String[] args) { Number obj = new Number(10); System.out.println("Before modify(): obj.value = " + obj.value); modify(obj); System.out.println("After modify(): obj.value = " + obj.value); } } ``` **Output:** ``` Before modify(): obj.value = 10 Inside modify(): n.value = 888 After modify(): obj.value = 999 ``` **Explanation:** Although `n.value = 999` modifies the original object, the reassignment `n = new Number(500)` only changes the *local copy* of the reference. The caller's `obj` continues to point to the original object. This conclusively proves Java is **pass-by-value**, even for object references. --- #### Part (b) — Aliasing (7 marks) **Definition (2 marks):** **Aliasing** is the phenomenon where **two or more reference variables refer to the same object** in memory. Since they all hold the same address, any modification made to the object through any one of these references is reflected when accessed through any other reference. **Java Program (3 marks):** ```java class BankAccount { String holderName; double balance; BankAccount(String name, double bal) { this.holderName = name; this.balance = bal; } void deposit(double amount) { balance += amount; } void display() { System.out.println("Account[" + holderName + "] Balance: " + balance); } } public class AliasingDemo { public static void main(String[] args) { BankAccount acc1 = new BankAccount("Anu", 5000); BankAccount acc2 = acc1; // ALIASING: both point to same object System.out.println("--- After aliasing ---"); acc1.display(); acc2.display(); acc2.deposit(2500); // Modify via acc2 System.out.println("--- After deposit via acc2 ---"); acc1.display(); // Visible via acc1 too! acc2.display(); } } ``` **Output:** ``` --- After aliasing --- Account[Anu] Balance: 5000.0 Account[Anu] Balance: 5000.0 --- After deposit via acc2 --- Account[Anu] Balance: 7500.0 Account[Anu] Balance: 7500.0 ``` **Memory Diagram (2 marks):** ``` STACK (main method) HEAP +-----------------+ +--------------------------------+ | acc1 → 0xA100 |-------->| BankAccount object @ 0xA100 | | acc2 → 0xA100 |-------->| holderName = "Anu" | +-----------------+ | balance = 7500.0 | +--------------------------------+ ``` **Conclusion:** Both `acc1` and `acc2` are aliases pointing to the *same* BankAccount object. Depositing ₹2500 via `acc2` is reflected when reading the balance via `acc1`, proving that aliasing shares a single underlying object across multiple references. --- ## ✏️ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [Alternative Choice] **(a)** Explain the role of the **`this` keyword** in Java with suitable examples. Discuss at least three distinct uses of `this` in the context of object references. **(7 marks)** **(b)** What is the **Garbage Collector** and how does it relate to object references? Explain with an example how an object becomes eligible for garbage collection when all its references are lost. Differentiate between the `finalize()` method and the modern `Cleaner` API. **(7 marks)** --- ### ✅ Model Solution for Question B #### Part (a) — The `this` Keyword (7 marks) **Definition (2 marks):** In Java, **`this`** is a reference variable that refers to the **current object** — the instance on which a method is currently being invoked. It is an implicit final reference automatically created by the JVM and passed as a hidden first argument to every non-static method and constructor. **Three Key Uses (4 marks):** **Use 1: Disambiguating instance variables from parameters** ```java class Employee { int id; String name; double salary; Employee(int id, String name, double salary) { this.id = id; // LHS = instance var, RHS = parameter this.name = name; this.salary = salary; } } ``` **Use 2: Constructor chaining using `this(...)`** ```java class Point { int x, y; Point() { this(0, 0); // Calls Point(int, int) } Point(int x, int y) { this.x = x; this.y = y; } Point(int value) { this(value, value); // Calls Point(int, int) } } ``` **Use 3: Passing the current object as a parameter** ```java class TreeNode { TreeNode left, right; void traverse() { if (left != null) left.traverse(); // Passing 'this' to another method TreeProcessor.processNode(this); if (right != null) right.traverse(); } } ``` **Use 4: Returning the current object for method chaining** ```java class Counter { int count = 0; Counter increment() { count++; return this; // Returns the current object } public static void main(String[] args) { new Counter().increment().increment().increment(); } } ``` **Conclusion (1 mark):** The `this` reference is fundamental to object-oriented design in Java. It enables clean disambiguation, elegant constructor chaining, fluent APIs, and seamless integration of the current object with external systems. --- #### Part (b) — Garbage Collection & Object References (7 marks) **Concept (2 marks):** The **Garbage Collector (GC)** is a JVM-managed background process that automatically reclaims memory occupied by objects that are **no longer reachable** through any live reference. It eliminates the need for manual `free()` (as in C/C++) and prevents memory leaks when used correctly. **Reachability Rule (2 marks):** An object becomes eligible for garbage collection when **zero live references** point to it — i.e., the reference count (conceptually) drops to zero or the object is part of an unreachable island of references. **Example (2 marks):** ```java class Product { String name; Product(String n) { this.name = n; } public static void main(String[] args) { Product p1 = new Product("Laptop"); // Object A reachable via p1 Product p2 = new Product("Phone"); // Object B reachable via p2 p1 = p2; // Object A is now UNREACHABLE → eligible for GC p2 = null; // Object B is now UNREACHABLE → eligible for GC System.gc(); // Request (hint) to JVM to run GC } } ``` **`finalize()` vs Cleaner API (1 mark):** | Feature | `finalize()` (Deprecated) | `java.lang.ref.Cleaner` (Modern) | |---|---|---| | Introduced | Java 1.0 | Java 9 | | Status | **Deprecated since Java 9**, removed in later versions | **Recommended** replacement | | Threading | Runs on JVM's finalizer thread (single) | Can use a dedicated thread per Cleaner | | Performance | Unpredictable, can cause delays | More efficient and predictable | | Safety | Risky — can resurrect objects | Safer design | ```java // Modern Cleaner usage import java.lang.ref.Cleaner; class Resource implements AutoCloseable { private static final Cleaner CLEANER = Cleaner.create(); private final Cleaner.Cleanable cleanable; Resource() { cleanable = CLEANER.register(this, new CleanupAction()); } public void close() { cleanable.clean(); } static class CleanupAction implements Runnable { public void run() { System.out.println("Resource cleaned up"); } } } ``` --- ### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students lose marks in Object Reference questions:** > > 1. **Confusing "object" with "reference":** Writing *"creating a reference"* when the code actually creates an object via `new`. The reference already exists after declaration; the object is created by `new`. > 2. **Saying Java is "pass-by-reference":** This is the **#1 mistake** KTU examiners penalize. The correct statement is: *"Java is pass-by-value — the value of the reference (i.e., the address) is copied."* > 3. **Confusing `==` with `.equals()`:** Marking `==` as content comparison. **`==` compares references (addresses); `.equals()` compares content (when properly overridden).** > 4. **Forgetting to mention Stack vs Heap:** Any memory-related question demands an explicit mention of **where the reference lives (stack) and where the object lives (heap)**. Omitting this loses 2 marks. > 5. **Assuming immediate garbage collection:** Writing *"the object is destroyed"* — it is only *eligible*; the JVM decides *when* to actually reclaim it. > 6. **Missing `this` in constructors with shadowed parameters:** A constructor `Box(int length) { length = length; }` is a *bug*, not a feature. Examiners specifically test whether you know to use `this.length = length;`. > 7. **Not drawing a memory diagram:** Part B questions on references expect a **labeled diagram** showing stack and heap. Without it, the answer is considered incomplete. --- ### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ An **object reference** is a *typed handle* to an object, **not the object itself**. - ✅ The reference is stored on the **stack**; the object lives on the **heap**. - ✅ `new` is what creates the object; declaration alone just creates a `null` reference. - ✅ **Aliasing** = multiple references, one object. Changes through one are visible through all. - ✅ **`this`** = implicit reference to the current object. Mandatory when parameter names shadow instance variables. - ✅ **Java is strictly pass-by-value** — but the *value* of an object reference is an address, so the underlying object *can* be modified through it. - ✅ An object becomes **garbage** when no live reference points to it. The JVM decides *when* to reclaim it; you can only *request* via `System.gc()`. - ✅ `==` compares **reference identity (addresses)**; `.equals()` compares **logical content**. - ✅ A `null` reference refers to no object — calling a method on it throws `NullPointerException`. - ✅ Reference reassignment (`b = new Box();`) detaches the reference from the old object; if no other reference exists, that object is GC-eligible. - ✅ Polymorphic references (e.g., `Animal a = new Dog();`) are the foundation of **runtime polymorphism** — covered later in Module 3. - ✅ `finalize()` is **deprecated**; use **`java.lang.ref.Cleaner`** in modern Java code. - ✅ Always draw a **stack-heap memory diagram** in Part B answers involving object references — it earns you easy 2–3 marks. ---

topics.title: Access Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT414 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Access Control # 📘 Module 2 — Access Control ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Access Control** in Object-Oriented Programming (specifically Java, as prescribed in the KTU PEECT414 syllabus) is a **mechanism that regulates the visibility and accessibility of classes, methods, constructors, and instance variables** to other classes and packages. It is a fundamental pillar of the OOP principle of **Encapsulation** and is implemented in Java through four **access modifiers**: `private`, default (package-private), `protected`, and `public`. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Access Control in OOP is not merely a syntax rule — it is a **design-level contract** that enforces *information hiding*, ensuring that internal implementation details of a class are shielded from external misuse. The KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to **justify the choice** of an access modifier, not just declare one. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **hospital building**: | Real-World Object | OOP Equivalent | |---|---| | Hospital Reception (open to everyone) | `public` member | | Ward restricted to staff with badges | `protected` member | | Operation Theatre (only doctors of that hospital) | `default` (package-private) member | | Patient's private medical file (only the doctor treating) | `private` member | - A **visitor** (code in any package) can only enter the **Reception** (`public`). - A **doctor from another hospital** (subclass in a different package) can access the **Ward** (`protected`). - A **doctor from the same hospital** (same package) can access the **Ward and the Operation Theatre** (`default` + `protected`). - **Nobody except the treating doctor** (the owning class itself) can read the **medical file** (`private`). This layered visibility is precisely what Java Access Control enforces at the bytecode level via the **JVM Access Flags**. > [!NOTE] > **Why Access Control Matters in Industry:** > - Prevents **accidental coupling** between unrelated modules. > - Enables **API stability** — public interfaces remain stable while private internals can change freely. > - Facilitates **maintainability, refactoring, and team-based development**. --- ### 1.3 The Four Access Modifiers — At a Glance 1. **`private`** — Most restrictive. Accessible **only within the same class**. 2. **Default (package-private)** — No keyword. Accessible **within the same package**. 3. **`protected`** — Accessible within the **same package + subclasses (even in other packages)**. 4. **`public`** — Least restrictive. Accessible **from anywhere**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet ## 2.1 Breakdown of Each Access Modifier ### 🔒 `private` - **Scope:** Same class only. - **Use case:** Instance variables, helper methods that should never be touched externally. - **KTU Design Rule:** *Every instance variable of a well-encapsulated class should be `private`.* ### 📦 Default (Package-Private) - **Scope:** Same package (no modifier keyword is written). - **Use case:** Tightly coupled helper classes within the same package that shouldn't be exposed globally. - **Common pitfall:** Students mistakenly think "default" means "accessible everywhere by default" — it actually means **"no access specified, so package-only."** ### 🛡️ `protected` - **Scope:** Same package **AND** subclasses in any package. - **Use case:** Methods intended to be **overridden by child classes**, but hidden from unrelated classes. - **KTU Hot Topic:** Frequently asked — *"Can a subclass in a different package access a `protected` member via a parent class reference of the same subclass type?"* (Answer involves the infamous **"same package or via inheritance only"** rule.) ### 🌐 `public` - **Scope:** Everywhere — any class in any package. - **Use case:** API methods, constants, entry points like `main()`. - **Design warning:** Overuse of `public` breaks encapsulation. --- ## 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Access Matrix (Cheat Sheet) The matrix below is the **single most important table** for this topic in KTU examinations. Memorize it. | Modifier | Same Class | Same Package (Non-Subclass) | Subclass (Different Package) | Other Package Classes (Non-Subclass) | |---|---|---|---|---| | `private` | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ❌ No | ❌ No | | `default` | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ❌ No | | `protected` | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | | `public` | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | > [!NOTE] > **Class-level access modifiers (Java-specific rule):** > A **top-level class** can only be declared `public` or default (package-private). It **cannot** be `private` or `protected`. > A **nested (inner) class**, however, can be any of the four modifiers. --- ## 2.3 Access Control in the Context of Inheritance When a subclass inherits a member, the inherited member's access level is **NOT** upgraded. For example: - A `protected` field in `Parent` becomes a `protected` field in `Child`. - A `private` field in `Parent` is **NOT inherited** by `Child` (it is hidden, but can be accessed via `public`/`protected` getters). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Method Overriding & Access Modifiers:** When overriding a method, you **CANNOT reduce** the visibility of the parent method. You can only **keep it the same or widen** it. For example, overriding a `public` method as `protected` is a **compilation error**. $$\text{Visibility in Override} \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; V_{\text{child}} \geq V_{\text{parent}}$$ --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Domain | Application | |---|---| | **Banking Software** | Account balance is `private`; withdrawal API is `public`. | | **Android SDK** | Internal lifecycle methods use `protected`; public APIs are `public`. | | **Microservices** | DTOs use `public` getters/setters; internal logic is `private`. | | **Library Design** | Library author exposes only `public` API; internals are `default` or `private`. | # 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Code Walkthroughs ## 3.1 Demonstration 1 — Verifying Access Through a Same-Package Non-Subclass Access **Setup:** Two classes `A` and `B` in the same package `com.ktu.demo`. ### Step 1 — Class A declares members of all four access levels ```java package com.ktu.demo; public class A { private int pvt = 1; int dflt = 2; // default access protected int prt = 3; public int pub = 4; public void display() { System.out.println("Private = " + pvt); System.out.println("Default = " + dflt); System.out.println("Protected = " + prt); System.out.println("Public = " + pub); } } ``` **Logic:** Class `A` is the **owner** of these fields. Inside its own `display()` method, every field is accessible because **access is always granted within the declaring class** — regardless of modifier. ### Step 2 — Class B (same package, NOT a subclass) tries to access A's fields ```java package com.ktu.demo; public class B { public void test() { A obj = new A(); // System.out.println(obj.pvt); // ❌ COMPILE ERROR: pvt has private access in A System.out.println(obj.dflt); // ✅ OK (default → same package) System.out.println(obj.prt); // ✅ OK (protected → same package) System.out.println(obj.pub); // ✅ OK (public → everywhere) } } ``` **Analytical conclusion:** From within the same package: - `private` → ❌ blocked - `default` → ✅ allowed - `protected` → ✅ allowed (behaves like default here) - `public` → ✅ allowed $$\text{Access}(B \rightarrow A) = \begin{cases} \text{Allowed} & \text{if } \text{modifier} \in \{\text{default}, \text{protected}, \text{public}\} \\ \text{Denied} & \text{if } \text{modifier} = \text{private} \end{cases}$$ --- ## 3.2 Demonstration 2 — Subclass in a Different Package **Setup:** Class `Parent` in package `com.ktu.base`. Class `Child` in package `com.ktu.derived` extends `Parent`. ### Step 1 — Parent class ```java package com.ktu.base; public class Parent { private int pvt = 10; int dflt = 20; protected int prt = 30; public int pub = 40; } ``` ### Step 2 — Child class (different package, IS a subclass) ```java package com.ktu.derived; import com.ktu.base.Parent; public class Child extends Parent { public void test() { // System.out.println(pvt); // ❌ private — NOT inherited // System.out.println(dflt); // ❌ default — different package System.out.println(prt); // ✅ protected — inherited & accessible System.out.println(pub); // ✅ public — inherited & accessible } } ``` **Reasoning trace:** - The field `pvt` is **not visible** even via inheritance because `private` members are **never inherited**. - The field `dflt` has **package-only** visibility. Since `Child` is in a different package, access is **denied**. - The field `prt` is **protected** — explicitly designed to grant subclass access across packages. ✅ - The field `pub` is universally accessible. ✅ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Subtle KTU trap:** A `protected` member can only be accessed from a **subclass** via an object of the **subclass type itself** (or its descendant), NOT via a reference of the parent type when the reference resides in a different package. --- ## 3.3 Demonstration 3 — Illegal Override (Reduced Visibility) ```java class Parent { public void show() { System.out.println("Parent"); } } class Child extends Parent { @Override protected void show() { System.out.println("Child"); } // ❌ COMPILE ERROR } ``` **Why this fails:** The compiler enforces the **covariant visibility rule** for overrides: $$V_{\text{Child.show}} \geq V_{\text{Parent.show}} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{protected} < \text{public} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{ILLEGAL}$$ **Correct fix:** Either change the child to `public`, or change the parent to `protected`. --- ## 3.4 Demonstration 4 — Access Control on Classes Themselves ```java // File: TopLevelDemo.java class Helper { } // ✅ default — package-private class public class TopLevelDemo { } // ✅ public class // private class Secret { } // ❌ top-level classes CANNOT be private // protected class Shield { } // ❌ top-level classes CANNOT be protected ``` **Conclusion:** Top-level classes are restricted to `public` or default only. The compiler enforces: $$\text{Top-level class modifier} \in \{\text{public}, \text{default}\}$$ Nested classes, however, follow the full four-modifier rule. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Decision Logic for Access Modifier Selection ```mermaid flowchart TD startA[Designing a new class member] --> qA1{Does the member
represent internal
implementation detail?} qA1 -- YES --> modA1[Use PRIVATE] qA1 -- NO --> qA2{Is the member
meant to be
overridden by
subclasses in
other packages?} qA2 -- YES --> modA2[Use PROTECTED] qA2 -- NO --> qA3{Is the member
shared only within
the same package
as a helper?} qA3 -- YES --> modA3[Use DEFAULT no keyword] qA3 -- NO --> modA4[Use PUBLIC] modA1 --> doneA[Apply modifier] modA2 --> doneA modA3 --> doneA modA4 --> doneA ``` --- ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Visibility Scope of Each Modifier ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph PKG1[Package com ktu base] clsA[Class A] clsB[Class B] end subgraph PKG2[Package com ktu derived] clsC[Class C extends A] clsD[Class D unrelated] end clsA -- private only here --> clsA clsA -- default --> clsB clsA -- protected --> clsB clsA -- protected --> clsC clsA -- public --> clsD clsA -- public --> clsC clsA -- public --> clsB ``` --- ## 4.3 Mermaid Class Hierarchy — Inheritance & Access Interaction ```mermaid classDiagram class Parent { -privateField int ~defaultField int #protectedField int +publicField int +publicMethod void #protectedMethod void } class Child { +childMethod void } class Stranger { +strangerMethod void } Parent <|-- Child : extends Parent <.. Stranger : unrelated class ``` **Reading the diagram:** - `-` denotes `private` - `~` denotes default (package-private) - `#` denotes `protected` - `+` denotes `public` > [!NOTE] > `Child` can access everything in `Parent` **except** `privateField` (not inherited) and `defaultField` (package-bound). `Stranger` can only access `publicField` and `publicMethod`. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` **Differentiate between `private` and `protected` access modifiers in Java with one example each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | `private` | `protected` | |---|---|---| | **Visibility** | Only within the same class | Same package + subclasses (any package) | | **Inheritance** | NOT inherited by subclasses | Inherited and accessible | | **Typical Use** | Data hiding / encapsulation | Methods meant for overriding | | **Example** | `private int balance;` | `protected void onCreate();` | **Mark split (KTU pattern):** Definition (1m) + Comparison (1m) + Example (1m). --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **Can a top-level class be declared `private` or `protected` in Java? Justify your answer.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** No. A top-level class in Java can only be declared either `public` or with **no modifier** (default/package-private). The Java Language Specification restricts top-level classes to these two modifiers because: 1. A `private` top-level class would be inaccessible to *any* other class — including the JVM entry point — making it useless. 2. A `protected` top-level class has no clear semantics outside inheritance, and inheritance requires the parent to be at least package-visible. However, **nested (inner) classes** can be declared with any of the four modifiers. **Mark split:** Yes/No (1m) + Two reasons (1m) + Exception note (1m). --- ## 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### ❓ Question A `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]` — Option 1 **Q. (a)** Explain the four access modifiers in Java with their visibility scope. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Write a Java program demonstrating that a subclass in a different package can access `protected` members of its superclass but NOT `default` (package-private) members. *(7 marks)* --- #### 🔹 Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks **The Four Access Modifiers:** 1. **`private`** *(2 marks)* - Most restrictive modifier. - Accessible **only within the same class** in which it is declared. - Not visible to subclasses, not visible to other classes in the same package. - Used for **data hiding** — instance variables and internal helper methods. 2. **Default (Package-Private)** *(2 marks)* - Declared by writing **no keyword** at all. - Accessible to all classes within the **same package**. - Not accessible to subclasses residing in different packages. - Used for tightly coupled package-level helpers. 3. **`protected`** *(2 marks)* - Accessible within the same package **AND** by subclasses in **any other package**. - Inherited by subclasses but with the **same protection level**. - Used for methods intended for **extension/overriding**. 4. **`public`** *(1 mark)* - Least restrictive — accessible from **anywhere**. - Forms the **public API** of a class. --- #### 🔹 Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks **Java Program Demonstrating Protected vs Default Access Across Packages:** ```java // File: com/ktu/base/Super.java package com.ktu.base; public class Super { int defaultField = 100; // default access protected int protectedField = 200; // protected access void defaultMethod() { // default method System.out.println("Default method in Super"); } protected void protectedMethod() { System.out.println("Protected method in Super"); } } ``` ```java // File: com/ktu/derived/Sub.java package com.ktu.derived; import com.ktu.base.Super; public class Sub extends Super { public void demo() { System.out.println(protectedField); // ✅ Accessible protectedMethod(); // ✅ Accessible // System.out.println(defaultField); // ❌ Compile error // defaultMethod(); // ❌ Compile error } } ``` ```java // File: com/ktu/derived/Main.java package com.ktu.derived; import com.ktu.base.Super; public class Main { public static void main(String[] args) { Sub s = new Sub(); s.demo(); // ✅ Works via inheritance Super sup = new Super(); // sup.defaultField; // ❌ Not accessible (different package) sup.protectedField; // ❌ Accessible only via inheritance path } } ``` **Explanation of outputs and compilation behavior** *(rest of the 7 marks)*: - The `Sub` class successfully accesses `protectedField` and `protectedMethod()` because they are declared `protected` in `Super`, and `Sub` is a subclass (even though in a different package). - The lines attempting to access `defaultField` and `defaultMethod()` produce **compile-time errors**, confirming that default access is bound to the package and is **NOT** extended to subclasses outside the package. --- ### ❓ Question B `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` — Option 2 **Q. (a)** What is encapsulation? How is it achieved using access control? *(7 marks)* **(b)** Explain with a Java program why you cannot reduce the visibility of an inherited method when overriding it. *(7 marks)* --- #### 🔹 Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks **Encapsulation** is the OOP principle of **bundling data and the methods that operate on that data into a single unit (class)** and **restricting direct external access** to some of the object's components. **How Access Control Achieves Encapsulation** *(breakdown for marks)*: 1. **Hiding data with `private`** *(2 marks)* - Instance variables are declared `private`, making them inaccessible from outside the class. - This prevents **unauthorized modification** and enforces **data integrity**. 2. **Controlled access via `public` getters/setters** *(2 marks)* - `public` accessor (getter) and mutator (setter) methods provide **validated** access to private fields. - Allows the class to enforce invariants (e.g., age must be ≥ 0). 3. **Restricting helper logic to package or protected scope** *(2 marks)* - Internal helper methods can be `default` or `private`, ensuring only intended collaborators can invoke them. 4. **Exposing only the public API** *(1 mark)* - The `public` methods form a **stable contract** that other classes depend on, while internals remain free to evolve. --- #### 🔹 Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks **Java Program Demonstrating Visibility Restriction in Overriding:** ```java class BankAccount { public double getBalance() { // PUBLIC method return 1000.0; } } class SavingsAccount extends BankAccount { @Override protected double getBalance() { // ❌ COMPILE ERROR return 2000.0; } } ``` **Explanation** *(for the remaining marks)*: When `SavingsAccount` tries to override `getBalance()` with reduced visibility (`protected` instead of `public`), the compiler raises an error: ``` error: getBalance() in SavingsAccount cannot override getBalance() in BankAccount attempting to assign weaker access privileges; was public ``` **Why?** The Java compiler enforces the **Liskov-Substitution-friendly** rule: $$V_{\text{child method}} \;\geq\; V_{\text{parent method}}$$ Reducing visibility would **break polymorphism** — code written to call `getBalance()` on a generic `BankAccount` reference would suddenly lose access when the actual object is a `SavingsAccount`. This violates the **substitution principle** underlying OOP inheritance. **Correct version:** Change the child method to `public`, OR keep both as `protected`. ```java @Override public double getBalance() { // ✅ Same or wider visibility — OK return 2000.0; } ``` --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Access Control Questions:** > 1. **Confusing `default` with `public`** — Students often write *"default means accessible everywhere if no modifier is specified"*. This is WRONG. Default = package-only. > 2. **Believing `private` members are inherited** — They are NOT inherited. A subclass does not see them, but can access them via `public`/`protected` getters of the parent. > 3. **Reducing visibility while overriding** — Instant zero for that sub-question if not justified. > 4. **Forgetting that `protected` ≠ `public` for non-subclasses in other packages** — A non-subclass in another package CANNOT access `protected` members. > 5. **Mixing class-level and member-level rules** — Top-level classes ≠ nested classes for access modifier applicability. --- ## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ✅ There are **four** access modifiers in Java: `private`, default, `protected`, `public`. - ✅ **`private`** → Same class only. - ✅ **Default** → Same package only. - ✅ **`protected`** → Same package **+** subclasses in any package. - ✅ **`public`** → Everywhere. - ✅ Top-level classes can only be `public` or default — **not** `private` or `protected`. - ✅ Nested classes can use **all four** modifiers. - ✅ `private` members are **not inherited** by subclasses. - ✅ Overriding methods **cannot reduce visibility** — they can only keep or widen it. - ✅ `protected` members in a different package are accessed only via the **subclass type**, not via a parent reference held in the unrelated package. - ✅ Always prefer `private` fields with `public` getters/setters (encapsulation). - ✅ Use `protected` **only** when you intentionally want subclasses to extend behavior. - ✅ Use `public` sparingly — every public method is part of the **permanent API contract**.

topics.title: Introduction to Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT414 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **method** in Java is a named, reusable block of statements that performs a specific, well-defined task. Methods are members of a class (or interface) and form the fundamental unit of modularization in Object-Oriented Programming. They encapsulate behavior, promote code reusability, and enable top-down program decomposition through the **Divide and Conquer** paradigm.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** A *method* is a member of a class that implements a computation or action that can be performed by an object or by the class itself. Every Java method has a **signature**, a **return type**, an **access modifier**, and a **body** containing the executable statements.

In Java terminology (and thus the KTU OOP module), what many other languages call a *function* or *subroutine* is called a **method**, because in pure OO design, all executable code must belong to a class.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Vending Machine" Model

Imagine a **vending machine** in your college canteen:

- The machine itself is the **object (instance)**.
- The labelled slot *“Insert ₹10 → Press B3 → Get a Kit-Kat”* is the **method**.
- **Inputs (money + button press)** = *arguments / parameters*.
- **The chocolate that drops out** = the *return value*.
- The **instruction sheet printed inside the door** = the *method body*.
- The **slot the chocolate falls into** = the *return mechanism (return statement)*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** You don’t need to know *how* the vending machine works internally (gears, springs, motors) to use it. You only need to know its **interface** — what to put in, what comes out. A method is exactly the same: it hides implementation details behind a clean, well-defined **interface** (its signature). This is the **Encapsulation of Behavior** principle.

## 1.3 Why Methods Exist — The Three Pillars

| Pillar | Meaning | Benefit |
|--------|---------|---------|
| **Modularity** | Each method solves *one* logical sub-problem | Easier to read, debug, and reason about |
| **Reusability** | One method can be called N times from many places | No copy-paste, less duplication |
| **Abstraction** | Caller knows *what* — not *how* | Clean separation of interface and implementation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Emphasis:** The 2024 scheme examiners specifically reward answers that mention **reusability, modularity, and parameterization** when a 14-mark question is asked on methods. Always quote these three advantages in long-answer questions.

## 1.4 Categories of Methods (Static vs. Instance vs. Constructors)

- **Instance method** — invoked on an *object*; has implicit access to `this`.
- **Static method** — belongs to the *class*, invoked via the class name; cannot access instance variables directly.
- **Constructor** — special method used to *initialize* new objects; no return type (not even `void`).

```java
public class Calculator {
    // Instance method
    public int add(int a, int b) { return a + b; }

    // Static method
    public static double pi() { return 3.14159; }

    // Constructor
    public Calculator() { /* initialization */ }
}
```

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Java Method — The Seven Mandatory & Optional Parts

A Java method declaration is parsed left-to-right by the compiler. Each part has a precise syntactic slot:

```
[access-modifier] [static] [final] [abstract] return-type method-name( [parameter-list] ) [throws clause] { method-body }
```

### 2.1.1 Breakdown of Each Component

1. **Access Modifier** — Controls visibility:
   - `public` — visible everywhere
   - `protected` — visible in same package + subclasses
   - *default* (package-private) — same package only
   - `private` — same class only

2. **`static` keyword** — Makes the method a *class method* (shared by all objects, allocated once).
   - **Memory cost:** `**1**` copy in the Method Area of JVM.
   - **Calling cost:** No `this` reference overhead.

3. **`final` keyword** — Disables *method overriding* in subclasses (a key OOP design contract).

4. **`abstract` keyword** — Declares the method without a body; *must* be overridden in a concrete subclass.

5. **Return Type** — The data type of the value the method hands back.
   - Primitive (`int`, `double`, `char`, `boolean`, …)
   - Reference (`String`, `Student`, `int[]`, …)
   - **`void`** — method returns nothing.

6. **Method Name** — Identifier following Java naming conventions (*camelCase*, verb-based: `calculateTax`, `printReport`).

7. **Parameter List** — Comma-separated `type name` pairs enclosed in parentheses. May be empty `()`.

8. **`throws` Clause** — Lists *checked exceptions* the method may propagate to the caller.

9. **Method Body** — Block of statements enclosed in `{ }`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Constructor vs Method:** A constructor has *no return type* — not even `void`. It is invoked by the `new` operator, never by an explicit call statement. Examiners frequently set this as a trick question.

## 2.2 Method Signature — The Most-Tested Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** A Java method **signature** is the **method name + parameter list (types and order)**. It **does NOT include** the return type, access modifier, or `throws` clause.

$$\text{Signature}(m) \;=\; \big( \text{name}(m),\; \text{orderedTuple}(\text{paramTypes}(m)) \big)$$

**Examples:**

| Method Declaration | Signature |
|--------------------|-----------|
| `void show(int x)` | `show(int)` |
| `double area(double r)` | `area(double)` |
| `int sum(int a, double b)` | `sum(int, double)` |
| `int sum(double a, int b)` | `sum(double, int)` ← **Different from above!** |

## 2.3 Formal Parameters vs. Actual Arguments

- **Formal Parameters (Parameters)** — Variables listed in the method’s declaration. They are *local* to the method body. Think of them as **empty boxes waiting to be filled**.
- **Actual Arguments (Arguments)** — Real values/expressions passed by the caller at the invocation site.

$$\underbrace{a,\;b}_{\text{formal parameters}} \longleftarrow \underbrace{5,\;3.14}_{\text{actual arguments}}$$

## 2.4 Return Statement Mechanics

The `return` statement performs two simultaneous actions:
1. **Terminates** the method immediately (no further statements execute).
2. **Hands back** the evaluated expression value to the caller.

A method declared `void` uses `return;` (no value) just to *exit early*.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Every code path in a non-`void` method MUST return a value. Otherwise the compiler throws: *"missing return statement"*. If a method has an `if` without an `else`, add a `return` after the `if`.

## 2.5 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Rule / Equation | Exam Tip |
|---------|----------------|----------|
| Method signature length | $\lvert S \rvert = 1 + k$ where $k$ = #parameters | Don't count the return type! |
| #overloaded methods possible (n params of t types) | $t^{n}$ | Useful for MCQs |
| Static call cost (clock cycles, conceptual) | $C_{\text{static}} < C_{\text{instance}}$ | No `this` pointer dereference |
| Memory: static method | **1** copy per class | Stored in Method Area (JVM) |
| Memory: instance method | **1** copy per class (code), called on **N** objects | Code shared; only data is per-object |
| Return statement value | $v = \text{expr}$ evaluated in callee frame | Passed back via operand stack |
| Formal vs Actual params count | Must match *exactly* | Java does NOT default-fill missing args |

> [!NOTE]
