# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the `this` keyword in Java. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The `this` keyword in Java is a **reference variable** that refers to the **current object** — the instance on which a method or constructor is currently being invoked.

**Key Points to Mention for Full Marks:**

1. It is an implicit, **final** reference automatically passed by the JVM.
2. It distinguishes instance variables from local/parameter variables when both share the same name (resolves **variable shadowing**).
3. It is **not available** in static contexts (static methods or static blocks).
4. It is primarily used in **six** canonical ways as per the KTU syllabus.

---

### Question 2: Can `this` keyword be used inside a `static` method? Justify. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**No**, the `this` keyword **cannot be used** inside a `static` method in Java.

**Justification:**

- A `static` method belongs to the **class**, not to any specific object instance.
- Since there is no current object in a static context, the `this` reference has no value to point to.
- The compiler raises the error: *"non-static variable this cannot be referenced from a static context"*.
- **However**, `this` **can** be passed **as a parameter** to a static method from an instance context.

---

---

## 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each carries 14 marks (typically split as **part (a) = 7 marks** and **part (b) = 7 marks**).

---

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q:** Explain the six different uses of the `this` keyword in Java with suitable code examples. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]

**Model Solution:**

**Use 1: Referring to current instance variable (Shadowing Resolution)**

```java
class Employee {
    int id;
    String name;

    Employee(int id, String name) {
        this.id = id;
        this.name = name;
    }
}
```

**Explanation:** Here, parameters `id` and `name` shadow the instance variables. `this.id` explicitly refers to the object's field.

**Use 2: Invoking current class method explicitly**

```java
class Calculator {
    void add(int a, int b) {
        System.out.println("Add: " + (a + b));
        this.sub(a, b);
    }

    void sub(int a, int b) {
        System.out.println("Sub: " + (a - b));
    }
}
```

**Explanation:** `this.sub(a, b)` explicitly invokes the current object's `sub()` method.

**Use 3: Invoking current class constructor (Constructor Chaining)**

```java
class Vehicle {
    String type;
    int wheels;

    Vehicle() {
        this("Car", 4);
    }

    Vehicle(String type, int wheels) {
        this.type = type;
        this.wheels = wheels;
    }
}
```

**Explanation:** The no-arg constructor calls the parameterized one via `this()`, eliminating code duplication.

**Use 4: Passing `this` as an argument**

```java
class Helper {
    void process(Student s) {
        System.out.println("Processing: " + s.name);
    }
}

class Student {
    String name = "Anu";

    void trigger() {
        Helper h = new Helper();
        h.process(this);
    }
}
```

**Use 5: Returning `this` for method chaining**

```java
class Builder {
    Builder setA() { System.out.println("A set"); return this; }
    Builder setB() { System.out.println("B set"); return this; }
}
```

**Use 6: Reassignment of `this` — NOT ALLOWED**

```java
class Demo {
    void test() {
        this = new Demo(); // COMPILE ERROR
    }
}
```

**Conclusion:** The `this` keyword is a powerful, multi-purpose reference that enhances code clarity, supports constructor chaining, and enables fluent API design in Java.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q:** With a neat diagram, illustrate how the `this` reference is stored in the JVM stack frame when an instance method is invoked. Differentiate between `this` and `super` keywords. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Model Solution:**

**JVM Stack Frame Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HEAP["HEAP"]
        OBJ["Demo Object\nfield1, field2"]
    end

    subgraph STACK["STACK FRAME: instanceMethod"]
        S0["Slot 0: this → OBJ"]
        S1["Slot 1: parameterA"]
        S2["Slot 2: parameterB"]
    end

    S0 -.implicit.-> OBJ

    classDef heapStyle fill:#ffe5b4,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px
    classDef stackStyle fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003d99,stroke-width:2px
    class OBJ heapStyle
    class S0,S1,S2 stackStyle
```

**Explanation:** When `instanceMethod()` is called, the JVM creates a stack frame with `this` occupying **slot 0** of the Local Variable Array, pointing to the object on the heap.

**Comparison Table — `this` vs `super`:**

| Feature | `this` | `super` |
|---------|--------|---------|
| Refers to | Current object | Immediate parent class object |
| Constructor call | `this(args)` | `super(args)` |
| Used for | Resolving shadowing, chaining constructors | Accessing overridden methods/hidden fields |
| Default behavior | Implicit | Implicit (compiler inserts no-arg `super()` if none written) |
| Static context | ❌ Forbidden | ❌ Forbidden |

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q:** Demonstrate constructor chaining using the `this()` keyword with a complete Java program. Explain why `this()` must be the first statement in a constructor. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Model Solution:**

```java
class Rectangle {
    int length, breadth;

    Rectangle() {
        this(10, 5);
        System.out.println("Default Rectangle created");
    }

    Rectangle(int side) {
        this(side, side);
        System.out.println("Square Rectangle created");
    }

    Rectangle(int l, int b) {
        this.length = l;
        this.breadth = b;
        System.out.println("Rectangle: " + l + " x " + b);
    }

    int area() {
        return this.length * this.breadth;
    }
}

class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Rectangle r1 = new Rectangle();
        Rectangle r2 = new Rectangle(7);
        Rectangle r3 = new Rectangle(4, 6);

        System.out.println("Area r1 = " + r1.area());
        System.out.println("Area r2 = " + r2.area());
        System.out.println("Area r3 = " + r3.area());
    }
}
```

**Output Trace:**

```
Rectangle: 10 x 5
Default Rectangle created
Rectangle: 7 x 7
Square Rectangle created
Rectangle: 4 x 6
Area r1 = 50
Area r2 = 49
Area r3 = 24
```

**Why `this()` must be the first statement:**

1. **Object Initialization Guarantee:** Java requires that the parent's constructor runs before the child's constructor body executes. The `this()` call must therefore be placed before any other statements to ensure proper initialization order.
2. **Compile-Time Enforcement:** The compiler verifies that `this()` appears at **line 1** of the constructor body. Any subsequent use results in error: *"call to this must be first statement in constructor"*.
3. **Prevents Partial Initialization:** Allowing statements before `this()` would risk the object being used in an uninitialized state if the chained constructor throws an exception.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q:** Write a Java program to implement a **fluent interface** using the `this` keyword for method chaining in a `ShoppingCart` class with methods `addItem()`, `applyDiscount()`, and `checkout()`. Explain each step. [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**Model Solution:**

```java
class ShoppingCart {
    double total = 0;
    double discount = 0;

    ShoppingCart addItem(double price) {
        this.total += price;
        System.out.println("Item added: " + price);
        return this;
    }

    ShoppingCart applyDiscount(double percent) {
        this.discount = percent;
        System.out.println("Discount applied: " + percent + "%");
        return this;
    }

    void checkout() {
        double finalAmount = this.total - (this.total * this.discount / 100);
        System.out.println("Final Amount: " + finalAmount);
    }
}

class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new ShoppingCart()
            .addItem(500)
            .addItem(300)
            .applyDiscount(10)
            .checkout();
    }
}
```

**Output:**

```
Item added: 500.0
Item added: 300.0
Discount applied: 10.0%
Final Amount: 720.0
```

**Step-by-step Explanation:**

1. `new ShoppingCart()` creates an object on the heap.
2. `.addItem(500)` invokes the method with `this` = the new cart; `total` becomes 500; returns `this` (same cart).
3. `.addItem(300)` chains immediately — `this` is still the same cart; `total` becomes 800; returns `this`.
4. `.applyDiscount(10)` updates `discount = 10`; returns `this`.
5. `.checkout()` performs final calculation; since it returns `void`, the chain terminates.

**Mathematical Verification:**

$$
\text{Final Amount} = \text{total} - \left(\text{total} \times \frac{\text{discount}}{100}\right)
$$

$$
= 800 - \left(800 \times \frac{10}{100}\right) = 800 - 80 = 720
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Deduction Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Forgetting `final` nature:** Writing `this = null;` or `this = new ClassName();` — the examiner will deduct **2 marks** and question your fundamental understanding.
> 2. **Misplacing `this()`:** Placing `this(args)` as the **second statement** in a constructor — **3 marks deducted** as it is a hard compile-time error.
> 3. **Confusing `this()` with `super()`:** These are **not interchangeable**. `this()` chains within the same class; `super()` invokes the parent's constructor.
> 4. **Using `this` in static context:** Writing `this.x` inside a `main()` method — examiner deducts **2 marks** for this fundamental violation.
> 5. **Incomplete constructor chaining examples:** Showing only one constructor with `this()` without tracing the execution order loses **2 marks**.
> 6. **Ignoring shadowing explanation:** Simply writing `this.name = name;` without explaining **why** `this` is needed loses **1-2 marks**.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — `this` Keyword**

- ✅ `this` = reference to the **current invoking object** (instance)
- ✅ It is an **implicit, final** reference injected by the JVM into every instance method/constructor
- ✅ Resides in **Local Variable Array slot 0** of the method's stack frame
- ✅ **Cannot** be used in `static` methods, `static` blocks, or `main()` (which is static)
- ✅ **Six canonical uses:** field disambiguation, method call, constructor chaining, argument passing, return value, (forbidden: reassignment)
- ✅ `this(args)` **must be the first statement** in a constructor body
- ✅ Cyclic `this()` chaining → **StackOverflowError** at runtime
- ✅ Reassigning `this` → **Compile-time error** (final reference)
- ✅ Method chaining works because each method **returns `this`**
- ✅ `this` ≠ `super`: `this` = current object; `super` = parent object
- ✅ Common use cases in real engineering: **Builder Pattern**, **Fluent APIs**, **Event Listeners**, **Observer Pattern**
- ✅ KTU weightage: ~**3-7 marks** per question; appears in Part A frequently, Part B as sub-part

**Memory Aid (Mnemonic — "FIMCRP"):**
- **F**ield disambiguation
- **I**nvoke own method
- **M**ultiple constructor chaining via `this()`
- **C**an be passed as argument
- **R**eturn value for chaining
- **P**rotected from reassignment (final)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Method Overloading
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT414 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Method Overloading

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Method Overloading** (also termed *Compile-Time Polymorphism* or *Static Polymorphism*) is a core feature of Object-Oriented Programming in Java that allows a class to declare **multiple methods** with the **same name** but **different parameter signatures** within the same class (or across a class and its subclass). The compiler distinguishes between these methods at compile time by analyzing the method's *signature*, which consists of the method name, the number of parameters, the data types of parameters, and the order of parameter types.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**: *Method Overloading is the mechanism of defining two or more methods within the same class that share the same name but differ in the number, type, or order of parameters. The return type alone does NOT qualify a method as overloaded.*

Mathematically, the signature of an overloaded method `M` can be expressed as:

$$S(M) = \{N(M), P(M), T(P), O(P)\}$$

Where:
- **N(M)** = Name of the method (must be identical for all overloaded versions)
- **P(M)** = Number of parameters (arity)
- **T(P)** = Data types of parameters
- **O(P)** = Order of parameter types

The compiler invokes **binding logic** as follows:

$$M_{\text{resolved}} = \arg\max_{i} \, \text{match}(S(M_i), S(M_{\text{call}}))$$

The version whose signature best matches the call-site argument list is selected.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **coffee vending machine** in a college canteen. The machine has a single big red button labeled "**Make Coffee**". When you press it, you can:
- Insert **1 coin** → get a small cup
- Insert **2 coins** → get a medium cup
- Insert **3 coins** + choose "**sugar**" or "**no sugar**" → get a customized large cup

The *action* (make coffee) is the **same name**. What *changes* is the **input** (number of coins, type of choice). The machine handles all variations under the same button — this is **method overloading**. The vending machine (compiler) decides *at the time you press the button* (compile time) which coffee variant to dispense based on your inputs.

## 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Signature** | The combination of method name + parameter list (NOT return type) |
| **Compile-Time Binding** | The compiler resolves which overloaded method to call before program execution |
| **Static Polymorphism** | Another name for method overloading — the binding decision is "static" (fixed at compile time) |
| **Type Promotion** | Automatic conversion of smaller data types to larger ones when an exact match is not found |
| **Ambiguity** | A compile-time error occurring when the compiler cannot uniquely determine which overload to invoke |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters in KTU exams**: Method Overloading is a **2-mark / 3-mark favourite** in Part A and frequently appears as a **7-mark sub-question** in Part B under Module 2 (OOP Principles & Java Basics).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Method Overloading

For two methods `M1` and `M2` to be considered **validly overloaded**, **at least ONE** of the following three conditions must hold true. If none holds, they are treated as **duplicate method declarations** and the code fails to compile.

### Pillar 1 — Variation in Number of Parameters (Arity)

The two methods must accept a **different count** of arguments.

```java
void display(int a)              // 1 parameter
void display(int a, int b)       // 2 parameters  → Valid overload
```

### Pillar 2 — Variation in Data Types of Parameters

The parameter lists must have the **same number** of arguments but **different data types** in at least one position.

```java
void calculate(int x)            // int parameter
void calculate(double x)         // double parameter → Valid overload
```

### Pillar 3 — Variation in Order of Parameter Types

The parameter lists must have the **same number** of arguments but **different sequences** of types.

```java
void register(int id, String name)
void register(String name, int id)   → Valid overload
```

## 2.2 Rules and Restrictions (High-Yield for KTU)

| # | Rule | Valid? |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Different number of parameters | ✅ YES |
| 2 | Different types of parameters | ✅ YES |
| 3 | Different order of parameter types | ✅ YES |
| 4 | Only return type differs | ❌ NO — Compile Error |
| 5 | Only access modifier differs | ❌ NO — Compile Error |
| 6 | Only exception type differs | ❌ NO — Compile Error |
| 7 | Both static and non-static with same signature | ❌ NO — Compile Error |
| 8 | `main(String[] args)` overloaded with `main(int[] args)` | ✅ YES |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Signature Identity**: The compiler's resolution engine treats `int method()` and `double method()` as having **identical signatures** if their parameter lists match. Hence they cause a *Duplicate Method* compilation error.

## 2.3 Type Promotion Hierarchy (Critical for Exam)

When an exact parameter match is not found, Java performs **automatic type promotion** following this strict widening hierarchy:

$$byte \rightarrow short \rightarrow int \rightarrow long \rightarrow float \rightarrow double$$

$$char \rightarrow int$$

$$char \rightarrow int \rightarrow long \rightarrow float \rightarrow double$$

The formal promotion rule is:

$$T_{\text{promoted}} = \min\{\, T_{\text{target}} \,:\, T_{\text{target}} \geq T_{\text{source}} \,\}$$

This means if you call `display(5)` and there is no `display(int)`, the compiler promotes `int` → `long` → `float` → `double` in that order until a match is found.

## 2.4 Ambiguity Errors

Ambiguity occurs when the compiler's resolution algorithm cannot uniquely select one method because **multiple matches are equally specific**.

### Common Ambiguity Scenarios

**Scenario A: `null` argument with multiple reference-type overloads**

```java
void print(String s)        // Match 1
void print(Object o)       // Match 2
print(null);               // Compiler ERROR — ambiguous
```

**Scenario B: Implicit promotion to two equally valid types**

```java
void show(int x, float y)   // Match 1 (int → float)
void show(long x, double y) // Match 2 (int → long, float → double)
show(10, 10.5);             // Compiler ERROR — ambiguous
```

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Use Case of Method Overloading |
|---|---|
| **Android Development** | `setText(String)`, `setText(int resourceId)` in TextView |
| **Java Standard Library** | `System.out.println(int)`, `println(float)`, `println(String)` |
| **API Design** | Constructors with varying argument sets for flexible object creation |
| **Math Libraries** | `Math.max(int,int)`, `Math.max(double,double)`, `Math.max(float,float)` |
| **Compiler Construction** | Static dispatch tables use signature matching for early binding |

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Rule / Formula | Exam Priority |
|---|---|---|
| Signature = | Name + Parameter List (NOT return type) | **HIGH** |
| Overloading requires | Δ in **number**, **type**, or **order** of params | **HIGH** |
| Promotion chain | `byte → short → int → long → float → double` | **HIGH** |
| Return-type only overload | ❌ Not allowed | **HIGH** |
| Binding time | Compile time (Static / Early Binding) | **HIGH** |
| Polymorphism type | Compile-Time / Static Polymorphism | **HIGH** |
| Ambiguity trigger | Multiple equally-specific matches | **MEDIUM** |
| Varargs `(int... x)` | Treated as `int[] x` — lowest priority in matching | **MEDIUM** |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Compiler Resolution Algorithm (Formal Derivation)

The Java compiler applies a **3-phase matching algorithm** to resolve which overloaded method to invoke when a call is encountered.

### Phase 1 — Exact Match Search

Given a method call `M(a₁, a₂, …, aₙ)` and a candidate overload `M'(b₁, b₂, …, bₙ)`:

$$\text{Match}_{\text{exact}} = \bigwedge_{i=1}^{n} \Big[\, \text{type}(a_i) \equiv \text{type}(b_i) \,\Big]$$

If exactly one overload satisfies this, it is selected and binding terminates.

### Phase 2 — Widening Primitive Conversion Match

If Phase 1 fails, the compiler attempts type promotion:

$$\text{Match}_{\text{widening}} = \bigwedge_{i=1}^{n} \Big[\, \text{type}(b_i) \geq \text{type}(a_i) \,\Big]$$

using the promotion chain. The overload with the **smallest cumulative widening distance** is preferred.

$$\Delta_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{rank}(\text{type}(b_i)) - \text{rank}(\text{type}(a_i))$$

Where `rank(byte)=1, rank(short)=2, rank(int)=3, rank(long)=4, rank(float)=5, rank(double)=6`.

### Phase 3 — Autoboxing / Varargs Match

If Phases 1 and 2 fail, the compiler tries **autoboxing** (`int` ↔ `Integer`) and finally **varargs**.

$$\text{Match}_{\text{varargs}} = \text{Array}_{\text{type}}(b_i) \text{ compatible with } a_i$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Priority hierarchy**: `Exact Match` > `Widening Primitive` > `Autoboxing` > `Varargs`. A wider match in a *higher* tier always beats a narrower match in a *lower* tier.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Complete Trace

**Code under analysis:**

```java
class Demo {
    void test(int a, long b)  { System.out.println("int-long"); }
    void test(long a, int b)  { System.out.println("long-int"); }
    void test(int a, int b)   { System.out.println("int-int");   }
}

Demo d = new Demo();
d.test(10, 20);
```

### Step-by-Step Resolution Trace

**Step 1** — Compiler scans all `test()` overloads registered in the **method table** of class `Demo`:

$$\text{Table} = \{\, (int, long),\ (long, int),\ (int, int) \,\}$$

**Step 2** — Call arguments are `(int=10, int=20)`. Apply Phase 1 (exact match):

$$\text{Check } (int, int) : \ \text{type}(10) \equiv int \ \wedge \ \text{type}(20) \equiv int \ = \textbf{TRUE}$$

**Step 3** — Exact match found with `test(int, int)`. Binding terminates.

**Output**: `int-int`

**Final binding decision**:

$$M_{\text{resolved}} = test(int, int) \quad \text{via Phase 1, exact match}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Scenario Tests

### Test Case A: Type Promotion Trace

```java
class Calc {
    void show(int x)    { System.out.println("int");    }
    void show(double x) { System.out.println("double"); }
}
Calc c = new Calc();
c.show(100);    // Which version executes?
```

**Resolution Trace:**

| Phase | Candidate | Argument `100` (int) | Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| Phase 1 — Exact | `show(int)` | `int ≡ int` | ✅ **MATCH FOUND** |

$$M_{\text{resolved}} = show(int) \implies \text{Output: "int"}$$

### Test Case B: Promotion When Exact Fails

```java
class Calc {
    void show(long x)   { System.out.println("long");   }
    void show(float x)  { System.out.println("float");  }
    void show(double x) { System.out.println("double"); }
}
Calc c = new Calc();
c.show(100);    // int literal — no exact (int) overload exists
```

**Resolution Trace:**

| Phase | Candidate | Distance | Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| Phase 2 — Widening | `show(long)` | $\Delta = 4 - 3 = 1$ | Lowest |
| Phase 2 — Widening | `show(float)` | $\Delta = 5 - 3 = 2$ | Higher |
| Phase 2 — Widening | `show(double)` | $\Delta = 6 - 3 = 3$ | Highest |

$$M_{\text{resolved}} = \arg\min_i \Delta_i = show(long) \implies \text{Output: "long"}$$

### Test Case C: Ambiguity Demonstration

```java
class Calc {
    void show(int x, float y)   { }
    void show(long x, double y) { }
}
c.show(10, 10.5f);    // float → float  OR  float → double ?
```

**Resolution Trace:**

| Candidate | Promotion path for arg 2 (`10.5f`) | Δ |
|---|---|---|
| `show(int, float)` | `float → float` (exact) | 0 |
| `show(long, double)` | `int → long`, `float → double` | $1 + 1 = 2$ |

→ `show(int, float)` wins ⇒ Output: matches `int-float` version.

But the following produces **ambiguity**:

```java
c.show(10, 10.5);    // double literal
```

Because now `int → long (Δ=1)` AND `double → double (exact, Δ=0)` competes with `int → int (Δ=0), double → float (loss of precision, invalid)` — leading to **two equally-valid matches**.

## 3.4 Summary Decision Matrix

| Call Argument Type | No `int` overload | No `long` overload | No `double` overload |
|---|---|---|---|
| `byte` / `short` / `char` | Promotes to `int` | Promotes to `long` | Promotes to `double` |
| `int` literal | **Exact Match** | Promotes to `long` | Promotes to `double` |
| `float` literal | Error (loss of precision) | Error | Promotes to `double` |
| `long` literal | **Exact Match** | — | Promotes to `double` |
| `double` literal | Error | Error | **Exact Match** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Compiler Method Resolution Pipeline

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **decision pipeline** the Java compiler traverses when resolving an overloaded method call.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Method Call Encountered"] --> B{"Phase 1: Exact Match Available?"}
    B -- Yes --> C["Bind to Exact Match"]
    B -- No --> D{"Phase 2: Widening Primitive Conversion?"}
    D -- Yes --> E{"Multiple Widening Matches?"}
    E -- No --> F["Bind to Widening Match"]
    E -- Yes --> G["AMBIGUITY ERROR - Compile Fails"]
    D -- No --> H{"Phase 3: Autoboxing Match?"}
    H -- Yes --> I["Bind to Autoboxed Match"]
    H -- No --> J{"Phase 4: Varargs Match?"}
    J -- Yes --> K["Bind to Varargs Match"]
    J -- No --> L["COMPILE ERROR - No Method Found"]
    C --> M["Compilation Succeeds"]
    F --> M
    I --> M
    K --> M
```

## 4.2 Type Promotion Ladder Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["byte"] --> P2["short"]
    P2 --> P3["int"]
    P3 --> P4["long"]
    P4 --> P5["float"]
    P5 --> P6["double"]
    P7["char"] --> P3
```

> **Reading the diagram**: Arrows point from *smaller / lower-precision* types toward *larger / higher-precision* types. Java promotes arguments only **upward** along this ladder — never downward.

## 4.3 Overload Class Structure

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Calculator {
        +add(int a, int b) int
        +add(double a, double b) double
        +add(int a, int b, int c) int
        +add(String a, String b) String
    }
    class Client {
        +main() void
    }
    Client --> Calculator : calls overloaded add
```

## 4.4 Three Pillars Decision Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R["Two methods same name"] --> Q{"Are parameter LISTS different?"}
    Q -- No --> X["COMPILE ERROR: Duplicate Method"]
    Q -- Yes --> S{"How do they differ?"}
    S --> T1["PILLAR 1: Different NUMBER of parameters"]
    S --> T2["PILLAR 2: Different DATA TYPES of parameters"]
    S --> T3["PILLAR 3: Different ORDER of parameter types"]
    T1 --> Y["VALID OVERLOAD"]
    T2 --> Y
    T3 --> Y
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**

**Q: Define method overloading in Java. List any two rules that must be satisfied for valid method overloading.**

**Model Answer:**

Method overloading in Java is the process of defining multiple methods within the same class that share the **same name** but differ in their **parameter lists**. The compiler resolves which version to execute at compile time, making it a form of **compile-time (static) polymorphism**.

**Two essential rules:**
1. The method **name must be identical**, but at least one of the following must differ: *number of parameters*, *data types of parameters*, or *order of parameter types*.
2. The **return type alone cannot** differentiate two overloaded methods — only the parameter list constitutes the signature.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary Exam]**

**Q: What is type promotion in the context of method overloading? Explain with an example.**

**Model Answer:**

Type promotion is the **automatic conversion** performed by the Java compiler when an exact parameter match for an overloaded method is not available. The argument's data type is promoted to a **larger compatible type** following a strict hierarchy:

$$byte \rightarrow short \rightarrow int \rightarrow long \rightarrow float \rightarrow double$$

**Example:**

```java
void display(int x)    { System.out.println("int");    }
void display(double x) { System.out.println("double"); }

display(10);    // No exact int? Actually int overload exists -> "int"
display('A');   // char promotes to int -> calls display(int) -> "int"
```

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — **[14 Marks]**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q: Explain the concept of method overloading in Java with a suitable example. Discuss any three different ways of achieving method overloading.**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** Method overloading allows a class to have multiple methods with the same name but different signatures. The binding decision is made by the compiler before execution (compile-time polymorphism).

**Three Ways to Achieve Method Overloading:**

**Way 1 — Varying the number of parameters:**

```java
class Addition {
    int sum(int a, int b)              { return a + b; }
    int sum(int a, int b, int c)       { return a + b + c; }
}
```

**Way 2 — Varying the data types of parameters:**

```java
class Printer {
    void print(int x)     { System.out.println("int: " + x); }
    void print(double x)  { System.out.println("double: " + x); }
    void print(String x)  { System.out.println("String: " + x); }
}
```

**Way 3 — Varying the order of parameter types:**

```java
class User {
    void register(int id, String name)         { /* save user */ }
    void register(String name, int id)         { /* alt save */ }
}
```

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q: What is type promotion in Java? With the help of a program, demonstrate how automatic type promotion resolves method overloading when no exact match is found.**

**Model Solution:**

When no method matches the exact data type of the argument, Java's compiler automatically **promotes** the argument to a larger compatible type as per the hierarchy:

$$byte \rightarrow short \rightarrow int \rightarrow long \rightarrow float \rightarrow double$$

**Program demonstrating type promotion:**

```java
class PromoDemo {
    void show(long x)   { System.out.println("long version: "   + x); }
    void show(float x)  { System.out.println("float version: "  + x); }
    void show(double x) { System.out.println("double version: " + x); }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        PromoDemo obj = new PromoDemo();
        obj.show(100);      // int literal → promotes to long → "long version"
        obj.show(100.5f);   // float literal → exact float match → "float version"
        obj.show('A');      // char → promotes to int → promotes to long → "long version"
    }
}
```

**Output:**

```
long version: 100
float version: 100.5
long version: 65
```

The `int` literal `100` has no exact `show(int)` overload. The compiler promotes `int → long` (smallest widening), so `show(long)` is invoked.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — **[14 Marks]**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q: What is ambiguity in method overloading? Explain with two different scenarios where ambiguity errors occur. Also state the Java compiler's resolution rules for overloaded methods.**

**Model Solution:**

**Ambiguity in method overloading** is a *compile-time error* that occurs when the Java compiler cannot uniquely determine which overloaded method to invoke because **two or more candidates match the call arguments with equal specificity**.

**Scenario 1 — Promotion to two equally-valid types:**

```java
class Test {
    void compute(int x, long y)   { }
    void compute(long x, int y)   { }
}
Test t = new Test();
t.compute(10, 20);    // AMBIGUITY: int→long for arg1 conflicts with int→int for arg2
                      // Both overloads require one promotion — equal specificity
```

**Scenario 2 — `null` literal with multiple reference-type overloads:**

```java
class Test {
    void print(String s)   { }
    void print(Object o)   { }
}
Test t = new Test();
t.print(null);   // AMBIGUITY: Both String and Object can accept null — compiler cannot choose
```

**Compiler Resolution Rules (priority order):**

| Priority | Matching Strategy |
|---|---|
| 1 (Highest) | Exact type match (no conversion) |
| 2 | Widening primitive conversion |
| 3 | Autoboxing / Unboxing |
| 4 (Lowest) | Varargs |

A method matched in a higher priority tier always wins over one matched in a lower tier.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q: Write a Java program to create a class `ShapeArea` that overloads a method `area()` to compute the area of (i) a square, (ii) a rectangle, and (iii) a circle. Demonstrate the invocation of each overloaded method from `main()`.**

**Model Solution:**

```java
class ShapeArea {

    // (i) Area of square: side x side
    double area(double side) {
        return side * side;
    }

    // (ii) Area of rectangle: length x breadth
    double area(double length, double breadth) {
        return length * breadth;
    }

    // (iii) Area of circle: PI x radius x radius
    double area(float radius) {
        return Math.PI * radius * radius;
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        ShapeArea shape = new ShapeArea();

        double squareArea   = shape.area(5.0);
        double rectArea     = shape.area(4.0, 6.0);
        double circleArea   = shape.area(3.0f);

        System.out.println("Area of Square   = " + squareArea);
        System.out.println("Area of Rectangle = " + rectArea);
        System.out.println("Area of Circle   = " + circleArea);
    }
}
```

**Output:**

```
Area of Square   = 25.0
Area of Rectangle = 24.0
Area of Circle   = 28.274333882308138
```

**Explanation of Overload Resolution:**

| Call | Overload Selected | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| `shape.area(5.0)` | `area(double)` — square | 1 double arg → exact match |
| `shape.area(4.0, 6.0)` | `area(double, double)` — rectangle | 2 double args → exact match |
| `shape.area(3.0f)` | `area(float)` — circle | float literal → exact float match |

---

## 5.1 Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Returning "method overloading changes only return type"** — This is **WRONG**. The compiler treats `int foo()` and `double foo()` as duplicates. KTU examiners specifically deduct 1–2 marks for this misconception.
> 2. **Confusing method overloading with method overriding** — Overloading = same class, different signature, compile-time. Overriding = parent-child classes, same signature, run-time. Mixing them up costs 3+ marks.
> 3. **Forgetting the promotion chain** — Students often write `byte → int → double` skipping `short` and `long`. Memorize the full chain for full marks.
> 4. **Assuming `main()` cannot be overloaded** — Wrong! `main(String[])` and `main(int[])` are perfectly valid overloads, though only `main(String[])` is the JVM entry point.
> 5. **Not stating "compile-time polymorphism" explicitly** — Examiners expect the phrase *"compile-time / static polymorphism"* in the first sentence of any overloading answer.

## 5.2 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ Method Overloading = **same method name, different parameter list** within the **same class**.
- ✅ It implements **compile-time (static) polymorphism** — binding is decided by the compiler.
- ✅ Three pillars: **Δ in number of params**, **Δ in types of params**, **Δ in order of params**.
- ❌ Return type alone **CANNOT** differentiate overloaded methods.
- ✅ Type promotion hierarchy: `byte → short → int → long → float → double` and `char → int`.
- ✅ Resolution priority: **Exact Match > Widening > Autoboxing > Varargs**.
- ⚠️ Ambiguity errors arise when **multiple overloads match equally** — e.g., `null` or competing promotions.
- ✅ `main()` **can** be overloaded legally; only `public static void main(String[] args)` is the JVM entry point.
- 📌 **Constructor overloading** is a direct application of the same rules applied to constructors.
- 📌 KTU exams test overloading through: definition (2 marks), code trace questions (5 marks), and program-writing tasks (7 marks).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inheritance - Super Class
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT414 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Inheritance - Super Class

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Object Oriented Programming (OOP)** under the KTU 2024 Scheme, a **Super Class** (also referred to as a **Parent Class**, **Base Class**, or **Superclass**) is the class from which another class (**subclass** or **derived class**) inherits its features (attributes and behaviors).

Formally, according to the KTU 2024 OOP module specification:

> A **super class** is a generalized class that defines a common set of attributes and methods which are then inherited and possibly specialized by one or more **sub classes**. The `super` keyword in Java is a reference variable that is used to refer to the immediate parent class object — enabling access to parent class fields, methods, and constructors from within the subclass.

The relationship is **"is-a"**: A subclass **is a** specialized form of its super class. For example, a `Car` **is-a** `Vehicle`, a `SavingsAccount` **is-a** `BankAccount`.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP] **Think of it like a Family Tree**
> Imagine a **Family Tree** of biological inheritance. Your **parents (super class)** pass down certain traits to you (sub class) — like eye color, height tendencies, or last name. You *inherit* these features automatically, but you can also *override* them (your personality is uniquely yours) or *extend* them (you may learn skills they did not have). In Java, the **super class** plays the role of the parent, and the **sub class** is the child who automatically receives — and may customize — the inherited properties.

Another intuitive picture: A **Super Class** is like a **generic blueprint** for a house (rooms, doors, roof). A **Sub Class** is a *specialized* version of that blueprint — say, a Villa blueprint that inherits all generic house features but adds a swimming pool and a garden.

### 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Super Class** | The class being inherited **from** (parent/base) |
| **Sub Class** | The class that inherits (child/derived) |
| **`super` keyword** | Java reference to the **immediate parent class** |
| **Inheritance** | Mechanism of acquiring features from a parent class |
| **Method Overriding** | Redefining a parent method in the child class |
| **Constructor Chaining** | Sequential calling of constructors from child up to Object |

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Highlight**
> The `super` keyword in Java is the **only** mechanism that allows a subclass to explicitly refer to the **non-static** and **non-private** members of its **immediate** super class. It cannot refer to grandparent class members directly — it always refers to the *direct* parent.

### 1.4 Why is Super Class Concept Important?

1. **Code Reusability** — Common code is written once in the super class.
2. **Polymorphism Support** — Enables dynamic method dispatch (runtime polymorphism).
3. **Method Overriding** — Forms the foundation of overriding behavior.
4. **Maintainability** — Centralized changes in the super class propagate downward.
5. **Real-world Modeling** — Captures hierarchical "is-a" relationships in problem domains (e.g., `Employee → Manager`, `Shape → Circle`).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The `super` Keyword — Three Core Uses

In Java, the `super` keyword serves **three distinct purposes** within a subclass. Every KTU 2024 exam question on this topic will revolve around at least one of these three uses.

#### Use 1: `super.variable` — Accessing Hidden Super Class Fields

When a subclass declares a field with the **same name** as a field in the super class, the subclass field **shadows** (hides) the parent's field. To explicitly access the parent's field, we use `super.variableName`.

**Operational Logic:**
1. Compiler first looks in the **current (sub) class** scope.
2. If the variable is found locally, it is used.
3. If the variable name is shadowed, the parent's version is accessed via `super.variableName`.
4. This is resolved at **compile time** (static binding).

#### Use 2: `super.methodName()` — Invoking Overridden Super Class Method

When a subclass overrides a method from the super class, the **subclass version** is invoked by default. To explicitly call the **parent's overridden version**, use `super.methodName(arguments)`.

**Operational Logic:**
1. JVM looks up the method in the subclass first.
2. If the method is overridden, the subclass version executes.
3. `super.methodName()` bypasses the override and executes the parent's version.
4. This is essential when the subclass wants to **extend** behavior rather than completely replace it.

#### Use 3: `super()` or `super(arguments)` — Invoking Super Class Constructor

The most critical use. Every constructor in a subclass **implicitly** calls `super()` as its first statement. If the super class has a parameterized constructor with no default constructor, the subclass **must** explicitly call `super(args)`.

**Operational Logic:**
1. The very first executable statement in any Java constructor is either `this()` (another constructor in same class) or `super()` (a parent constructor).
2. If neither is written, the compiler **automatically inserts** `super()` (a no-argument call to the parent's default constructor).
3. If the parent has **no default constructor** (i.e., only parameterized constructors exist), the compiler error occurs unless the subclass **explicitly** writes `super(args)`.
4. This creates a **constructor chain** ending at `java.lang.Object`.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE] **High-Yield Quick Reference Table**

| Concept | Syntax | Purpose | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Access super class field | `super.fieldName` | Read/write parent field hidden by subclass | When subclass field **shadows** parent field |
| Access super class method | `super.methodName(args)` | Call parent's overridden method | When subclass overrides a method but wants parent's behavior |
| Call super class constructor | `super(args)` | Invoke specific parent constructor | As the **first statement** in a subclass constructor |
| Implicit constructor call | *compiler inserts `super()`* | Calls parent's no-arg constructor | When no explicit `super()` or `this()` is written |
| Reference to immediate parent | `super` (no dot) | Refers to parent object | In `instanceof` or method binding |

### 2.3 The Three Golden Rules of `super`

1. **`super` must refer to the IMMEDIATE parent only** — it cannot skip levels (cannot access grandparent's hidden members directly).
2. **`super()` must be the FIRST statement** in a constructor — cannot appear anywhere else in the constructor body.
3. **`super` cannot be used in a `static` context** — it is an instance reference, not a class reference. Using `super` inside a `static` method or static block causes a compile-time error.

### 2.4 Constructor Chaining — The Complete Flow

When an object of a subclass is created, the following sequence occurs:

1. JVM allocates memory for the entire object (including inherited fields).
2. Default initialization of all fields (numeric to 0, boolean to false, references to null).
3. The subclass constructor begins execution.
4. The first statement (implicit or explicit `super(args)`) invokes the parent constructor.
5. The chain continues up to `java.lang.Object`'s constructor.
6. Execution returns down the chain — the parent's body finishes first, then the subclass body runs.

This is why **parent fields are initialized before child fields** — a fundamental KTU 2024 exam point.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **GUI Frameworks (JavaFX, Swing):** `Button extends Control extends Region extends Node`. Customizing button behavior often uses `super.paint()`.
- **Exception Handling:** Custom exceptions extend `Exception` and call `super(message)`.
- **Collections Framework:** `ArrayList extends AbstractList extends AbstractCollection`.
- **Enterprise Java (Spring/Hibernate):** Service layer classes inherit from base services, calling `super.init()`.
- **Android Development:** Activity lifecycle methods like `onCreate()` frequently call `super.onCreate(savedInstanceState)`.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT] **Exhaustive Walkthrough Mandate**
> Every code line and execution step is explained explicitly — no skipping, no assumptions.

### 3.1 Case Study 1: Accessing Hidden Fields with `super.variable`

**Problem Statement:** Demonstrate the use of `super` to access a parent's variable when the subclass shadows it.

**Java Code:**

```java
class Animal {
    String name = "Generic Animal";
    int legs = 4;
}

class Dog extends Animal {
    String name = "Buddy";  // shadows parent's 'name'
    int legs = 4;
    
    void display() {
        System.out.println("Subclass name: " + name);          // Buddy
        System.out.println("Superclass name: " + super.name);  // Generic Animal
        System.out.println("Subclass legs: " + legs);          // 4
        System.out.println("Superclass legs: " + super.legs);  // 4
    }
}

public class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Dog d = new Dog();
        d.display();
    }
}
```

**Step-by-Step Analytical Trace:**

1. `Dog d = new Dog();` — JVM allocates memory containing both `Animal` fields (`name`, `legs`) and `Dog` fields (`name`, `legs`).
2. `d.display();` is called.
3. Line `System.out.println("Subclass name: " + name);` — Compiler resolves `name` in current scope → finds `Dog.name = "Buddy"`. Output: `Subclass name: Buddy`.
4. Line `System.out.println("Superclass name: " + super.name);` — Explicit `super` prefix forces lookup in parent class → finds `Animal.name = "Generic Animal"`. Output: `Superclass name: Generic Animal`.
5. Same logic applies for `legs`.
6. Final Output:
   ```
   Subclass name: Buddy
   Superclass name: Generic Animal
   Subclass legs: 4
   Superclass legs: 4
   ```

### 3.2 Case Study 2: Invoking Overridden Methods with `super.method()`

**Problem Statement:** A subclass overrides a method but needs to extend (not replace) the parent's behavior.

**Java Code:**

```java
class Vehicle {
    int maxSpeed = 120;
    
    void displayInfo() {
        System.out.println("Vehicle max speed: " + maxSpeed + " km/h");
    }
}

class Car extends Vehicle {
    int maxSpeed = 200;  // shadowing
    
    @Override
    void displayInfo() {
        super.displayInfo();  // call parent's version first
        System.out.println("Car max speed: " + maxSpeed + " km/h");
        System.out.println("Additional feature: Air conditioning");
    }
}

public class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Car c = new Car();
        c.displayInfo();
    }
}
```

**Step-by-Step Analytical Trace:**

1. `Car c = new Car();` creates a `Car` object.
2. `c.displayInfo();` — JVM resolves method via dynamic dispatch.
3. Since `Car` overrides `displayInfo()`, the **subclass version** executes.
4. Inside subclass `displayInfo()`, the first line is `super.displayInfo();` — this forces execution of the parent's version.
5. Parent's `displayInfo()` prints `Vehicle max speed: 120 km/h`.
6. Control returns to subclass `displayInfo()`.
7. Subclass prints `Car max speed: 200 km/h`.
8. Subclass prints `Additional feature: Air conditioning`.

**Final Output:**
```
Vehicle max speed: 120 km/h
Car max speed: 200 km/h
Additional feature: Air conditioning
```

### 3.3 Case Study 3: Constructor Chaining with `super()`

**Problem Statement:** A subclass must pass data to the parent's parameterized constructor.

**Java Code:**

```java
class Person {
    String name;
    int age;
    
    Person(String name, int age) {
        this.name = name;
        this.age = age;
        System.out.println("Person constructor called");
    }
}

class Student extends Person {
    int rollNo;
    String course;
    
    Student(String name, int age, int rollNo, String course) {
        super(name, age);  // explicit call to parent's parameterized constructor
        this.rollNo = rollNo;
        this.course = course;
        System.out.println("Student constructor called");
    }
    
    void display() {
        System.out.println("Name: " + name + ", Age: " + age);
        System.out.println("Roll No: " + rollNo + ", Course: " + course);
    }
}

public class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Student s = new Student("Arjun", 20, 101, "Computer Science");
        s.display();
    }
}
```

**Step-by-Step Analytical Trace:**

1. `new Student("Arjun", 20, 101, "Computer Science")` — JVM allocates memory.
2. `Student` constructor begins. First statement: `super(name, age)` → calls `Person("Arjun", 20)`.
3. `Person` constructor executes:
   - `this.name = "Arjun";`
   - `this.age = 20;`
   - Prints `Person constructor called`.
4. Control returns to `Student` constructor.
5. `this.rollNo = 101;`
6. `this.course = "Computer Science";`
7. Prints `Student constructor called`.
8. `s.display()` is called, printing all four fields.

**Final Output:**
```
Person constructor called
Student constructor called
Name: Arjun, Age: 20
Roll No: 101, Course: Computer Science
```

**Analytical Matrix — Why `super()` is Mandatory Here:**

$$
\text{If super not called explicitly} \implies \text{Compiler inserts } super() \text{ automatically}
$$

$$
\text{Parent has NO no-arg constructor} \implies \text{Compile-time error: implicit super() undefined}
$$

$$
\therefore \text{Subclass MUST write super(args) explicitly when parent lacks no-arg constructor}
$$

### 3.4 Case Study 4: Constructor Chain in Multilevel Inheritance

**Java Code:**

```java
class A {
    A() { System.out.println("A constructor"); }
}
class B extends A {
    B() { System.out.println("B constructor"); }
}
class C extends B {
    C() { System.out.println("C constructor"); }
}

public class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        C obj = new C();
    }
}
```

**Step-by-Step Execution Order:**

$$
\text{Output sequence} = A \rightarrow B \rightarrow C
$$

**Derivation of Execution Order:**

1. `new C()` is invoked.
2. `C()` constructor's first implicit statement is `super()` → calls `B()`.
3. `B()` constructor's first implicit statement is `super()` → calls `A()`.
4. `A()` constructor's first implicit statement is `super()` → calls `Object()`.
5. `Object()` constructor completes.
6. `A()` body executes → prints `A constructor`.
7. `B()` body executes → prints `B constructor`.
8. `C()` body executes → prints `C constructor`.

> [!NOTE] **Key Insight**
> The constructor invocation order is from **most-derived (child) to least-derived (root)** during the call, but the **execution order is from root to child** — a classic KTU 2024 exam favorite.

### 3.5 Case Study 5: `this()` vs `super()` — A Subtle Distinction

**Analytical Comparison:**

| Aspect | `this()` | `super()` |
|---|---|---|
| Refers to | Another constructor in **same class** | Constructor in **parent class** |
| Position | Must be **first statement** | Must be **first statement** |
| Mutual exclusivity | Cannot use both `this()` and `super()` in same constructor | Same — only one allowed |
| Default insertion | If neither written, compiler inserts `super()` | Cannot be inserted if `this()` is explicitly written |
| Use case | Constructor overloading within same class | Passing values to parent's constructor |

**Critical Rule:**

$$
\exists \text{ only one of } \{\text{this()},\ \text{super()}\} \text{ in any given constructor as the first statement}
$$

If you write `this(args)` in a constructor, the **other constructor** (which `this()` redirects to) will handle the `super()` call. The chain ultimately reaches the parent.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Inheritance Hierarchy Diagram

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates a typical **multilevel inheritance** structure with super class — sub class relationships, a common KTU 2024 exam scenario.

```mermaid
graph TD
    objectA[Object] --> animalA[Animal]
    animalA --> dogA[Dog]
    animalA --> catA[Cat]
    dogA --> pupA[Puppy]
    catA --> kittenA[Kitten]
    
    objectA -.->|super of Animal| animalA
    animalA -.->|super of Dog and Cat| dogA
    dogA -.->|super of Puppy| pupA
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- `Object` is the **root super class** of all Java classes (implicitly extended).
- `Animal` is a sub class of `Object` and the **super class** of `Dog` and `Cat`.
- `Dog` is a sub class of `Animal` and the **super class** of `Puppy`.
- Dotted arrows represent the `super` reference direction — a subclass holds a `super` pointer to its **immediate** parent.

### 4.2 Constructor Chaining Flow Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    startA[new SubClass invoked] --> call1A[SubClass constructor first statement: super args]
    call1A --> call2A[ParentClass constructor executes]
    call2A --> call3A[GrandparentClass constructor executes]
    call3A --> call4A[Object constructor executes]
    call4A --> return4A[Object body completes]
    return4A --> return3A[Grandparent body executes]
    return3A --> return2A[Parent body executes]
    return2A --> return1A[SubClass body executes]
    return1A --> endA[Object fully constructed]
    
    call1A -.->|implicit if not written| auto1A[Compiler inserts super]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **call chain** (solid arrows) goes **downward**: from sub class → parent → grandparent → Object.
- The **execution chain** (dotted return arrows) goes **upward**: from Object → grandparent → parent → sub class.
- This is the **LIFO (Last-In-First-Out)** nature of constructor execution.

### 4.3 The Three Uses of `super` — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    superA[SUPER KEYWORD] --> use1A[super variable]
    superA --> use2A[super method]
    superA --> use3A[super args]
    
    use1A --> desc1A[Access parent field hidden by subclass]
    use2A --> desc2A[Invoke parent method overridden by subclass]
    use3A --> desc3A[Call parent constructor as first statement]
    
    desc1A --> example1A[super name / super age]
    desc2A --> example2A[super displayInfo / super toString]
    desc3A --> example3A[super / super name age]
```

### 4.4 Method Resolution and `super.method()` Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    callA[Object reference calls method] --> checkA{Is method overridden?}
    checkA -->|No| execute1A[Execute parent method directly]
    checkA -->|Yes| dynamic1A[JVM uses dynamic dispatch]
    dynamic1A --> execute2A[Execute subclass method by default]
    execute2A --> superFlowA[Inside subclass, super.method call]
    superFlowA --> execute3A[Execute parent method explicitly]
    execute3A --> returnFlowA[Return to subclass method]
    returnFlowA --> continueA[Continue subclass method body]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*What is a super class in Java? Explain the three uses of the `super` keyword with suitable examples.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **super class** (also called a **parent class** or **base class**) in Java is the class whose features (fields and methods) are inherited by another class known as the **sub class**. The `super` keyword in Java is a reference variable used to refer to the immediate parent class object. It has three primary uses:

1. **`super.variableName`** — Used to access the data members (fields) of the super class when they are hidden (shadowed) by the subclass. Example: `super.name` accesses the `name` field of the parent class.

2. **`super.methodName()`** — Used to invoke a method of the super class that has been overridden in the subclass. Example: `super.displayInfo()` calls the parent's `displayInfo()` method.

3. **`super(arguments)`** — Used to explicitly call a constructor of the super class from the subclass constructor. It must be the first statement in the constructor. Example: `super(name, age)` invokes the parameterized parent constructor.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Why must `super()` be the first statement in a constructor? What happens if the super class does not have a no-argument constructor?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The `super()` call (or any `super(args)` call) must be the **first statement** in a Java subclass constructor because it ensures that the **parent class's state is fully initialized** before the subclass constructor begins executing its own initialization logic. This guarantees that no inherited member is accessed before being properly constructed, maintaining the integrity of the object construction process.

If the super class does **not** have a no-argument (default) constructor — meaning it only defines parameterized constructors — then the compiler **cannot implicitly insert** a call to `super()`. In such a scenario, the subclass constructor **must explicitly** call `super(args)` with the appropriate arguments as its first statement; otherwise, a **compile-time error** is raised with the message *"implicit super constructor is undefined; must explicitly invoke another constructor"*.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of a super class and sub class in Java with a real-world analogy. Write a Java program to demonstrate the use of the `super` keyword to access:
- (i) A hidden field of the super class
- (ii) An overridden method of the super class
*(7 marks)*

**(b)** What is constructor chaining? Write a Java program demonstrating multilevel inheritance with constructor chaining using the `super()` keyword, and explain the order of execution. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Real-World Analogy (2 marks):**

A super class can be thought of as a **generic blueprint for a vehicle** that defines common features like `speed`, `fuelType`, and methods like `start()` and `stop()`. A sub class like `Car` or `Motorcycle` inherits these features and specializes them — for example, `Car` may add `airConditioner()` and `Motorcycle` may add `kickStart()`. This is exactly how Java inheritance works: the super class provides generic behavior, and the sub class extends or overrides it.

**Java Program (5 marks):**

```java
// Super Class
class Vehicle {
    int speed = 60;  // field that will be hidden
    
    void display() {  // method to be overridden
        System.out.println("Vehicle speed: " + speed + " km/h");
    }
}

// Sub Class
class Car extends Vehicle {
    int speed = 180;  // hides parent's speed
    
    @Override
    void display() {  // overrides parent's display
        System.out.println("Subclass field speed: " + speed);
        System.out.println("Superclass field speed: " + super.speed);  // super.variable
        super.display();  // super.method() to invoke parent version
        System.out.println("Car-specific feature: Sunroof enabled");
    }
}

// Driver class
public class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Car myCar = new Car();
        myCar.display();
    }
}
```

**Expected Output:**
```
Subclass field speed: 180
Superclass field speed: 60
Vehicle speed: 60 km/h
Car-specific feature: Sunroof enabled
```

**Explanation of Key Lines:**
- `super.speed` accesses the parent's `speed` field (60) hidden by child's `speed` (180).
- `super.display()` calls the parent's overridden `display()` method, printing `Vehicle speed: 60 km/h`.
- This demonstrates both `super.variable` and `super.method()` usage.

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Definition (2 marks):**

**Constructor chaining** is the mechanism by which a constructor in a subclass automatically (or explicitly) invokes a constructor of its super class, which in turn may invoke its own super class constructor, forming a chain that always terminates at the `java.lang.Object` constructor. This ensures that the entire object hierarchy is properly initialized in a top-down manner.

**Java Program (3 marks):**

```java
class Electronics {
    Electronics() {
        System.out.println("Electronics class constructor");
    }
}

class Computer extends Electronics {
    Computer() {
        super();  // explicit call (also implicit)
        System.out.println("Computer class constructor");
    }
}

class Laptop extends Computer {
    Laptop() {
        super();  // explicit call to Computer constructor
        System.out.println("Laptop class constructor");
    }
}

public class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Laptop l = new Laptop();
    }
}
```

**Expected Output:**
```
Electronics class constructor
Computer class constructor
Laptop class constructor
```

**Order of Execution Explanation (2 marks):**

1. `new Laptop()` is called → enters `Laptop()` constructor.
2. First statement `super()` calls `Computer()`.
3. First statement of `Computer()` is `super()` → calls `Electronics()`.
4. First statement of `Electronics()` is `super()` → calls `Object()`.
5. `Object()` constructor completes silently.
6. `Electronics()` body executes → prints `Electronics class constructor`.
7. `Computer()` body executes → prints `Computer class constructor`.
8. `Laptop()` body executes → prints `Laptop class constructor`.

The call order is **Laptop → Computer → Electronics → Object**, but the **execution (printing) order is reversed**: **Object → Electronics → Computer → Laptop**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Differentiate between `this()` and `super()` in Java constructors. Can both be used in the same constructor? Justify your answer with an example. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Write a Java program to create a class `BankAccount` (super class) with fields `accountNumber`, `balance`, and methods `deposit()` and `display()`. Create a sub class `SavingsAccount` that extends `BankAccount`, adds a field `interestRate`, overrides `display()` to also print interest, and uses `super` to call the parent's `display()` and constructor. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Feature | `this()` | `super()` |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Calls another constructor in the **same class** | Calls a constructor of the **parent class** |
| Scope | Intra-class constructor chaining | Inter-class (sub class to super class) chaining |
| Position | Must be the **first statement** | Must be the **first statement** |
| Default behavior | If not written, compiler inserts `super()` | If not written, compiler inserts `super()` |
| Example | `this(name, 0)` | `super(name, age)` |

**Can both be used in the same constructor? (2 marks):**

**No.** Both `this()` and `super()` **cannot be used in the same constructor** because each must be the **first statement** of the constructor, and a constructor can have only **one first statement**. The Java compiler will throw an error: *"call to this must be first statement in constructor"* or *"call to super must be first statement in constructor"*.

However, the **indirect chaining** is possible: if a constructor uses `this(args)`, it redirects to another constructor in the same class, and **that other constructor** can then use `super(args)`. Thus, the chain `this() → this() → super()` is valid across multiple constructors but not within a single one.

**Example (2 marks):**

```java
class Box {
    int length, breadth;
    
    Box() {
        this(10, 10);  // calls Box(int, int)
        System.out.println("Default Box constructor");
    }
    
    Box(int l, int b) {
        super();  // calls Object
        this.length = l;
        this.breadth = b;
        System.out.println("Parameterized Box constructor");
    }
}

class ColoredBox extends Box {
    String color;
    
    ColoredBox(String color) {
        super(20, 30);  // calls Box(int, int), which itself calls super()
        this.color = color;
        System.out.println("ColoredBox constructor, color: " + color);
    }
}

public class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        ColoredBox cb = new ColoredBox("Red");
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Parameterized Box constructor
ColoredBox constructor, color: Red
```

Note how `super(20, 30)` in `ColoredBox` triggers `Box(int, int)`, which contains its own `this(10, 10)` and `super()` — demonstrating indirect chaining.

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Java Program (7 marks):**

```java
// Super Class
class BankAccount {
    long accountNumber;
    double balance;
    
    BankAccount(long accountNumber, double balance) {
        this.accountNumber = accountNumber;
        this.balance = balance;
    }
    
    void deposit(double amount) {
        balance += amount;
        System.out.println("Deposited: " + amount + ", New Balance: " + balance);
    }
    
    void display() {
        System.out.println("Account Number: " + accountNumber);
        System.out.println("Balance: " + balance);
    }
}

// Sub Class
class SavingsAccount extends BankAccount {
    double interestRate;
    
    SavingsAccount(long accountNumber, double balance, double interestRate) {
        super(accountNumber, balance);  // explicit super(args) call
        this.interestRate = interestRate;
    }
    
    @Override
    void display() {
        super.display();  // call parent's display() first
        System.out.println("Interest Rate: " + interestRate + "%");
        System.out.println("Account Type: Savings");
    }
}

// Driver
public class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        SavingsAccount sa = new SavingsAccount(123456789L, 5000.0, 4.5);
        sa.deposit(2000);
        sa.display();
    }
}
```

**Expected Output:**
```
Deposited: 2000.0, New Balance: 7000.0
Account Number: 123456789
Balance: 7000.0
Interest Rate: 4.5%
Account Type: Savings
```

**Code Walkthrough (highlight key lines):**
- `super(accountNumber, balance)` in `SavingsAccount` constructor — passes values to the parent `BankAccount` parameterized constructor. Mandatory because `BankAccount` has no default constructor.
- `super.display()` inside overridden `display()` — calls the parent's `display()` to print `accountNumber` and `balance` before printing the subclass-specific `interestRate`.
- `@Override` annotation — ensures the compiler verifies that `display()` actually overrides a parent method (compile-time safety).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams**
>
> 1. **Forgetting `super(args)` when parent has no default constructor** — This is the **#1 cause of compile-time errors** in KTU OOP exams. If the parent has only a parameterized constructor, the **subclass MUST** explicitly invoke `super(args)`. Marks are deducted even if the rest of the code is logically correct.
>
> 2. **Placing `super()` not as the first statement** — Writing any executable statement before `super()` in a constructor causes a compile error. Forgetting this is a frequent mark-loss.
>
> 3. **Confusing `super` with `this`** — Students often write `super()` when they mean `this.fieldName` or vice versa. Remember: `super` always refers to the **parent**, `this` always refers to the **current object**.
>
> 4. **Assuming `super` can access grandparent** — `super` refers only to the **immediate** parent. To access grandparent, you must use `super.super.X` is **illegal in Java** — you must go through the immediate parent's public/protected methods.
>
> 5. **Forgetting the output order in constructor chaining** — Many students incorrectly state that the subclass constructor runs first. The correct order is **parent-first execution, child-first invocation** — this is a guaranteed 2-mark question in every KTU exam.
>
> 6. **Not using `@Override` annotation** — While not a compile error, KTU examiners expect professional code. Omitting `@Override` may cost you the "code style" mark.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid Revision Checklist**

- **Super Class** = parent class = base class — the class being inherited **from**.
- **`super` keyword** is a **reference variable** to the **immediate** parent class object.
- **Three uses of `super`:** `super.variable`, `super.method()`, `super(args)`.
- **`super()` must be the FIRST statement** in a constructor — no exceptions.
- **Compiler auto-inserts `super()`** if no explicit `this()` or `super()` is written — but only works if the parent has a **no-arg constructor**.
- **Constructor chaining:** Invocation flows from **child to parent**, execution flows from **parent to child**.
- **Final chain always ends at `java.lang.Object`** constructor.
- **`this()` and `super()` cannot coexist** in the same constructor as the first statement.
- **`super` cannot be used in `static` methods, `static` blocks, or `main()`** (if `main` were not static — but `main` is static, so `super` is illegal there).
- **`super` cannot refer to private members** of the parent class (use `protected` or `public`).
- **Method overriding + `super.method()`** = way to extend (not just replace) parent behavior.
- **Field shadowing + `super.field`** = way to disambiguate fields with the same name.
- **Multilevel inheritance** → `super` always points to the **direct** parent, not the root.
- **Practical applications:** GUI frameworks, Exception classes, Collections, Android lifecycle methods, Enterprise Java.
- **Exam tip:** Always trace the constructor call chain **top-to-bottom** in your answer — examiners love step-by-step execution traces.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT414 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sub Class

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Sub Class — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of Object Oriented Programming under the KTU 2024 Scheme, a **Sub Class** (also called a **Derived Class**, **Child Class**, or **Extended Class**) is a class that inherits attributes (fields) and behaviors (methods) from another class, referred to as its **Super Class** (or **Base Class** / **Parent Class**). The mechanism that enables this relationship is called **Inheritance**, which is one of the four foundational pillars of OOP (alongside Encapsulation, Abstraction, and Polymorphism).

The KTU syllabus defines a sub class as a class declared using the `extends` keyword in Java (or `:` in C++), which establishes a strict **"IS-A"** relationship between the child and the parent. A sub class is permitted to:
- **Reuse** the non-private members of the super class.
- **Extend** the functionality by adding new fields and methods.
- **Override** existing methods to provide specialized behavior (Runtime Polymorphism).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Sub classes form the structural backbone of **Inheritance** and are the primary enabler of **dynamic polymorphism** in Java. The exam frequently tests your understanding of how sub classes interact with super class constructors, `super` keyword usage, and method overriding rules.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Real-World" Intuition

Imagine a **biological family tree**.

A **Parent** (Super Class) has certain traits — eye color, surname, height tendencies, native language. A **Child** (Sub Class) is *born into* this family and automatically inherits all these traits without any extra paperwork. The child **does not need to redefine** "I have a surname" — it's already there.

However, the child can:
- **Add** new traits unique to themselves (e.g., a unique hobby).
- **Specialize** inherited traits (e.g., the child may be *better* at cricket than the parent, so they "override" the parent's sports ability with their own superior version).

In software terms: just as a child inherits genetic code from parents, a `Car` sub class can inherit the `startEngine()` method from a `Vehicle` super class — but it can also provide its own specialized `startElectricMotor()` for an EV variant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Aid:** Think **Sub Class = Specialized Class**. A sub class is *always* a more *specific* version of its super class. If `Animal` is the super class, then `Dog` is a more specific kind of `Animal`.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Reference Table

| Term | Synonym | Role in Relationship |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Sub Class** | Derived / Child / Extended Class | The *inheriting* class |
| **Super Class** | Base / Parent / Super Class | The *being inherited from* class |
| **Inheritance** | Generalization/Specialization | The *mechanism* connecting them |
| **`extends` keyword** | (Java) Inheritance operator | Establishes the IS-A link |
| **`super` keyword** | Parent reference | Accesses hidden parent members |
| **Method Overriding** | Dynamic Binding | Redefining inherited methods |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — How a Sub Class Works

A sub class functions through five distinct operational mechanics, all of which are high-yield for KTU examinations:

### Step 1: Declaration via `extends`
In Java (the prescribed language for KTU PEECT414), a sub class is created using the `extends` keyword. This single keyword establishes the inheritance link at **compile time**.

```java
class Vehicle {           // Super Class
    void start() { System.out.println("Vehicle starts"); }
}
class Car extends Vehicle {   // Sub Class — Car IS-A Vehicle
    void honk() { System.out.println("Car honks"); }
}
```

### Step 2: Member Inheritance (The "Free Pass")
Upon compilation, the sub class **automatically receives** copies of all *accessible* members of the super class. The access is governed by Java's four access modifiers:

| Access Modifier | Same Class | Same Package | Sub Class (diff package) | Other Classes |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| `private` | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |
| `default` | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ |
| `protected` | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ |
| `public` | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Rule:** `private` members of the super class are **NOT inherited** by the sub class. They physically exist in the parent's memory layout, but the child has no syntactic access to them. This is the single most common source of confusion in exam answers.

### Step 3: Constructor Chaining via `super()`
Constructors are **never inherited**, but they **must be executed** before the sub class object is fully constructed. Java enforces this by inserting a hidden `super()` call as the *very first line* of every sub class constructor.

### Step 4: Method Overriding (Runtime Polymorphism)
A sub class may provide a *new implementation* of an inherited method, provided the signature matches exactly. This is decided at **runtime** based on the actual object type, not the reference type — this is **Dynamic Method Dispatch**.

### Step 5: IS-A and HAS-A Distinction
- **IS-A** (Inheritance): `Car IS-A Vehicle` → use `extends`.
- **HAS-A** (Composition): `Car HAS-A Engine` → use an instance field.

## 2.2 Types of Inheritance (KTU High-Yield)

Java supports **four** of the five classic inheritance patterns (multiple inheritance is *not* supported between classes, but is achieved through interfaces):

| Type | Structure Diagram | Java Support |
| :--- | :--- | :---: |
| **Single** | A → B | ✅ |
| **Multilevel** | A → B → C | ✅ |
| **Hierarchical** | A → B, A → C | ✅ |
| **Multiple** | A, B → C | ❌ (Only via interfaces) |
| **Hybrid** | Combination | ❌ (Partial via interfaces) |

## 2.3 KTU Formula & Syntax Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The "formulas" below are the **syntactic rules** that govern sub class behavior in Java. Memorize them verbatim for the 14-mark derivations.

| # | Rule | Formal Expression / Syntax |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Sub class declaration | `class SubName extends SuperName { }` |
| 2 | Constructor chaining | `super(arg1, arg2)` must be the **first statement** |
| 3 | Accessing parent field | `super.fieldName` |
| 4 | Accessing parent method | `super.methodName(args)` |
| 5 | Overriding precondition | Method signature must be **identical** (name + parameters) |
| 6 | Overriding access rule | Overriding method **cannot be more restrictive** |
| 7 | Final classes | `final class X` → **cannot be extended** |
| 8 | Final methods | `final void m()` → **cannot be overridden** |
| 9 | Abstract sub class | Must implement all abstract methods OR itself be abstract |
| 10 | Single root | Every Java class implicitly extends `java.lang.Object` |

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

The sub class concept is foundational in virtually every Java enterprise framework:
- **Spring Framework**: Beans are configured through class hierarchies.
- **Android SDK**: Every `Activity` is a sub class of `android.app.Activity`.
- **GUI Toolkits**: `JButton` is a sub class of `AbstractButton`, which is a sub class of `JComponent`, which is a sub class of `Container`, which is a sub class of `Component` — a 5-level deep inheritance chain.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Code Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Demonstration: Constructor Chaining in Sub Classes

This is the **#1 most tested sub class topic** in KTU exams. We will derive the exact execution order step-by-step.

### The Code Being Analyzed

```java
class Super {
    Super() {
        System.out.println("1. Super Class Default Constructor");
    }
    Super(int x) {
        System.out.println("1b. Super Class Parameterized: " + x);
    }
}

class Sub extends Super {
    int data;
    Sub() {
        this(50);  // Calls Sub(int) constructor
    }
    Sub(int x) {
        super(x);  // Explicitly calls Super(int)
        this.data = x;
        System.out.println("2. Sub Class Constructor with data = " + data);
    }
}

public class Demo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Sub obj = new Sub();
    }
}
```

### Step-by-Step Execution Trace

When `new Sub()` executes, the JVM walks the call stack as follows:

**Step 1:** `main()` encounters `new Sub()` → invokes `Sub()` (no-arg constructor).

**Step 2:** `Sub()` body's *first line* is `this(50);` → delegates to `Sub(int)`.

**Step 3:** `Sub(int x)` body's *first line* is `super(x);` → delegates to `Super(int)`.

**Step 4:** `Super(int)` executes its body → prints `"1b. Super Class Parameterized: 50"`.

**Step 5:** Control returns to `Sub(int)`, which sets `this.data = 50` and prints `"2. Sub Class Constructor with data = 50"`.

**Step 6:** Control returns to `Sub()`, which is now finished.

### Final Console Output

```
1b. Super Class Parameterized: 50
2. Sub Class Constructor with data = 50
```

### The Golden Rule (Mathematically Expressed)

Let the execution order be defined as:

$$
\text{Order}(obj) = \text{SuperClass}() \;\rightarrow\; \text{SubClass}()
$$

In general, for a chain $A \rightarrow B \rightarrow C$:

$$
\text{Order} = C_{super}() \;\rightarrow\; B_{super}() \;\rightarrow\; A()
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
