> **Key Insight:** The super class constructor **always** runs before the sub class constructor body. This is a **non-negotiable JVM invariant** — you cannot write any statement before `super()` or `this()` inside a constructor.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Method Overriding vs Method Overloading

Students frequently confuse these two. Here is the definitive comparison:

| Property | Overriding (Sub Class) | Overloading (Same Class) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Where** | Across sub class & super class | Within the *same* class |
| **Method Signature** | Must be **identical** | Must **differ** in parameters |
| **Return Type** | Same or *covariant* (subtype) | Can be anything |
| **Access Modifier** | Cannot be more restrictive | Independent of original |
| **Binding Time** | **Runtime** (Dynamic Dispatch) | **Compile time** (Static Binding) |
| **`final` keyword** | Forbids overriding | No effect on overloading |
| **`@Override` annotation** | Recommended (compiler check) | Not applicable |

### Code Proof of Runtime Polymorphism

```java
class Shape {
    void draw() { System.out.println("Drawing generic Shape"); }
}
class Circle extends Shape {
    @Override
    void draw() { System.out.println("Drawing Circle with radius"); }
}
class Square extends Shape {
    @Override
    void draw() { System.out.println("Drawing Square with 4 sides"); }
}

public class PolyDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Shape ref;             // Reference of type Shape
        ref = new Circle();    // Points to Circle object
        ref.draw();            // Calls Circle's draw() — NOT Shape's!
        ref = new Square();
        ref.draw();            // Calls Square's draw() — NOT Shape's!
    }
}
```

### Execution Trace

$$
\text{Output} = \begin{cases} \text{"Drawing Circle with radius"} & \text{if } ref \rightarrow Circle \\ \text{"Drawing Square with 4 sides"} & \text{if } ref \rightarrow Square \end{cases}
$$

**The compiler only knows `ref` is of type `Shape`.** It is the **JVM at runtime** that inspects the actual object and dispatches the correct overridden method. This is **Dynamic Method Dispatch** — the very heart of runtime polymorphism.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: `this` vs `super` Keyword

| Context | `this` | `super` |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Refers to | Current object | Parent (super) class object |
| Accessing field | `this.field` | `super.field` |
| Accessing method | `this.method()` | `super.method()` |
| Constructor call | `this(args)` — same class | `super(args)` — parent class |
| Position rule | First statement of constructor | First statement of constructor |
| Mutual exclusion | Cannot use both `this()` and `super()` in same constructor | (same) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Class Hierarchy Diagram — Inheritance Tree

```mermaid
classDiagram
    direction LR
    class Object {
        <<root>>
        +getClass()
        +equals(Object)
        +hashCode()
        +toString()
    }
    class Vehicle {
        #String brand
        #int speed
        +start()
        +stop()
    }
    class Car {
        -int numDoors
        +honk()
        +start()
    }
    class ElectricCar {
        -int batteryCapacity
        +charge()
        +start()
    }
    class SportsCar {
        -int topSpeed
        +turboBoost()
    }
    Object <|-- Vehicle
    Vehicle <|-- Car
    Car <|-- ElectricCar
    Car <|-- SportsCar
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the diagram above, every class **implicitly** extends `java.lang.Object` — even `Vehicle` itself. This is why every Java object has methods like `toString()` and `equals()`.

## 4.2 Object Creation & Constructor Chaining Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[main: new SportsCar] --> B[SportsCar constructor invoked]
    B --> C{Implicit or Explicit super call?}
    C -->|Implicit super| D[Car constructor invoked]
    C -->|Explicit super args| E[Car constructor with args]
    D --> F{Vehicle super call?}
    F -->|Implicit| G[Vehicle constructor invoked]
    F -->|Explicit| H[Vehicle constructor with args]
    G --> I[Object constructor invoked]
    I --> J[Object init complete]
    J --> K[Vehicle init complete]
    K --> L[Car init complete]
    L --> M[SportsCar init complete]
    M --> N[Object returned to main]
```

## 4.3 Dynamic Method Dispatch Architecture

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Main
    participant Reference as Shape ref
    participant Object as Actual Object (Circle)
    Main->>Reference: ref = new Circle()
    Main->>Reference: ref.draw()
    Note over Reference: Compiler binds ref.draw() to Shape.draw()
    Reference->>Object: Look up actual object type at runtime
    Object->>Object: Found Circle.draw() override
    Object-->>Main: Executes Circle's draw() method
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: Conceptual Definition `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define a sub class in Java. What keyword is used to declare a sub class, and what is the relationship called between the sub class and its super class?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **sub class** is a class that inherits the attributes and methods of another class, known as the super class. In Java, the `extends` keyword is used to declare a sub class. The relationship between a sub class and its super class is called the **"IS-A" relationship**, which is a form of **inheritance** that promotes code reuse and establishes a hierarchical classification. For example, `class Car extends Vehicle` makes `Car` a sub class of `Vehicle`, meaning every `Car` IS-A `Vehicle`.

*(Mark distribution: 1 mark for definition + 1 mark for keyword + 1 mark for relationship name.)*

### Question A2: Concept Application `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Explain why constructors are not inherited by a sub class in Java. How is the super class constructor invoked from a sub class?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Constructors are not inherited because they are responsible for initializing a **specific** object, and a sub class has its own initialization requirements that differ from the super class. If constructors were inherited, the sub class would inherit a constructor with the *super class's* name, which is syntactically impossible since the constructor name must match the class name.

The super class constructor is invoked using the **`super()`** call (with appropriate arguments matching a super class constructor). This call **must be the first statement** inside the sub class constructor. If no explicit `super()` call is provided, the compiler **automatically inserts** a call to the super class's no-argument (default) constructor.

*(Mark distribution: 2 marks for "why not inherited" + 1 mark for `super()` invocation.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** In 14-mark Part B questions, students frequently lose **2–3 marks** for:
> 1. Forgetting that `private` members are NOT inherited.
> 2. Placing statements *before* `super()` (compilation error).
> 3. Confusing method **overriding** with method **overloading**.
> 4. Not using the `@Override` annotation, which demonstrates best practice.
> 5. Failing to mention Dynamic Method Dispatch in polymorphism derivations.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): Comprehensive Inheritance Design `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**Design a Java program with a super class `Employee` and sub class `Manager`. The `Employee` class should have fields for `name` (String), `id` (int), and `basicSalary` (double), with a constructor and a method `displayDetails()`. The `Manager` sub class should add a field `bonus` (double), override `displayDetails()` to also print the bonus, and demonstrate constructor chaining. Write the complete program and explain the output. (14 marks)**

#### Part (a): Code Implementation (7 marks)

```java
class Employee {
    protected String name;
    protected int id;
    protected double basicSalary;

    // Super class constructor
    Employee(String name, int id, double basicSalary) {
        this.name = name;
        this.id = id;
        this.basicSalary = basicSalary;
        System.out.println("Employee constructor called");
    }

    void displayDetails() {
        System.out.println("Name: " + name);
        System.out.println("ID: " + id);
        System.out.println("Basic Salary: " + basicSalary);
    }
}

class Manager extends Employee {
    private double bonus;

    // Sub class constructor using super() for chaining
    Manager(String name, int id, double basicSalary, double bonus) {
        super(name, id, basicSalary);  // Calls Employee constructor
        this.bonus = bonus;
        System.out.println("Manager constructor called");
    }

    // Overriding displayDetails() to include bonus
    @Override
    void displayDetails() {
        super.displayDetails();  // Reuse parent's display logic
        System.out.println("Bonus: " + bonus);
    }
}

public class CompanyApp {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Manager mgr = new Manager("Alice", 101, 75000.0, 15000.0);
        mgr.displayDetails();
    }
}
```

#### Part (b): Output Explanation & Concepts (7 marks)

**Console Output:**

```
Employee constructor called
Manager constructor called
Name: Alice
ID: 101
Basic Salary: 75000.0
Bonus: 15000.0
```

**Explanation of Output:**

1. When `new Manager(...)` is executed, the **first line** of the `Manager` constructor is `super(name, id, basicSalary)`. This immediately transfers control to the `Employee` constructor.
2. The `Employee` constructor initializes the three `protected` fields and prints `"Employee constructor called"`.
3. Control returns to the `Manager` constructor, which initializes `bonus` and prints `"Manager constructor called"`.
4. The object is now fully constructed.
5. When `mgr.displayDetails()` is called, **Dynamic Method Dispatch** invokes the *overridden* `Manager` version, which in turn calls `super.displayDetails()` to reuse the parent class's printing logic before adding the bonus line.

**Key Concepts Demonstrated:**

| Concept | Where it appears |
| :--- | :--- |
| Inheritance (`extends`) | `class Manager extends Employee` |
| `protected` access | `name`, `id`, `basicSalary` accessible in `Manager` |
| Constructor chaining | `super(name, id, basicSalary)` |
| Method overriding | `displayDetails()` in `Manager` |
| `super` method call | `super.displayDetails()` |
| Dynamic Method Dispatch | Overridden method chosen at runtime |

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Alternative — Polymorphism & Abstract Sub Classes `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**Explain the concept of a sub class with respect to abstract classes in Java. Write a program using an abstract super class `Shape` with an abstract method `area()`. Create two sub classes `Circle` and `Rectangle` that implement `area()`. Demonstrate polymorphism by storing both in an array of `Shape` references and computing total area. (14 marks)**

#### Part (a): Conceptual Explanation of Abstract Sub Classes (7 marks)

An **abstract class** is a class declared with the `abstract` keyword that **cannot be instantiated** — it serves as a *blueprint* for sub classes. An abstract class can contain:
- **Abstract methods**: declared but not implemented (no body).
- **Concrete methods**: fully implemented and inherited normally.
- **Fields and constructors**: used by sub classes.

A sub class of an abstract class must follow this rule:

$$
\text{SubClass} = \begin{cases} \text{Concrete (Valid)} & \text{if ALL abstract methods are implemented} \\ \text{Abstract (Still Valid)} & \text{if at least one abstract method is unimplemented} \\ \text{Compilation ERROR} & \text{if abstract methods are partially implemented} \end{cases}
$$

The primary purpose of forcing sub classes to implement abstract methods is to **enforce a contract**: every sub class *must* provide its own specialized behavior for the inherited abstract operations.

#### Part (b): Code Implementation with Polymorphic Array (7 marks)

```java
abstract class Shape {
    protected String color;
    Shape(String color) { this.color = color; }

    abstract double area();  // Abstract method - no body

    void displayColor() {    // Concrete method
        System.out.println("Color: " + color);
    }
}

class Circle extends Shape {
    private double radius;
    Circle(String color, double radius) {
        super(color);
        this.radius = radius;
    }
    @Override
    double area() {
        return Math.PI * radius * radius;
    }
}

class Rectangle extends Shape {
    private double length, width;
    Rectangle(String color, double length, double width) {
        super(color);
        this.length = length;
        this.width = width;
    }
    @Override
    double area() {
        return length * width;
    }
}

public class ShapeApp {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Polymorphic array — Shape references holding different sub class objects
        Shape[] shapes = new Shape[3];
        shapes[0] = new Circle("Red", 5.0);
        shapes[1] = new Rectangle("Blue", 4.0, 6.0);
        shapes[2] = new Circle("Green", 3.0);

        double totalArea = 0.0;
        for (Shape s : shapes) {
            s.displayColor();
            double currentArea = s.area();   // Dynamic dispatch
            System.out.println("Area: " + currentArea);
            totalArea += currentArea;
        }
        System.out.println("Total Area: " + totalArea);
    }
}
```

**Sample Output:**

```
Color: Red
Area: 78.53981633974483
Color: Blue
Area: 24.0
Color: Green
Area: 28.274333882308138
Total Area: 130.81415022205296
```

**Explanation of Polymorphism in Action:**

The loop iterates over `Shape[]` references, but each reference points to either a `Circle` or `Rectangle` object. When `s.area()` is called, the JVM uses **Dynamic Method Dispatch** to invoke the *correct* overridden `area()` method based on the actual object type — not the reference type. This is the practical power of sub classes: **one interface (`Shape`), many implementations (`Circle`, `Rectangle`)**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section and pin it to your study wall before the exam.**

### ✅ Sub Class — Must-Know Facts

- [x] A **sub class** is declared using `extends` in Java.
- [x] Inheritance creates an **"IS-A"** relationship (sub class *is a kind of* super class).
- [x] `private` members of the super class are **NOT** inherited (cannot be accessed in sub class).
- [x] `protected` members are accessible in sub classes (even in different packages).
- [x] **Constructors are not inherited**, but the sub class constructor **must call** a super class constructor using `super()` as the first statement.
- [x] If no explicit `super()` is written, Java auto-inserts a call to the **no-arg super constructor**.
- [x] **Method overriding** requires an **identical signature** and a return type that is the same or a *covariant* subtype.
- [x] An overriding method **cannot reduce visibility** (e.g., cannot override `public` with `protected`).
- [x] **`super.fieldName`** accesses a parent class field hidden by a same-named sub class field.
- [x] **`super.methodName()`** calls the parent class version of an overridden method.
- [x] **`final` classes cannot be extended**; **`final` methods cannot be overridden**.
- [x] A sub class of an `abstract` class must implement **all** abstract methods, or itself be declared abstract.
- [x] **Dynamic Method Dispatch** is the JVM mechanism that invokes the correct overridden method at runtime.
- [x] Multiple inheritance between classes is **not** allowed in Java (use interfaces instead).
- [x] Every Java class implicitly extends `java.lang.Object`.
- [x] Use the **`@Override`** annotation to get compiler verification of overriding.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Keyword super
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT414 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** The Keyword super

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In Java, **`super`** is a **reserved keyword** that acts as a **reference variable** used to refer to the **immediate parent class (superclass)** of the current object. It provides a direct, unambiguous pathway for a subclass to access, invoke, and inherit the members (variables, methods, and constructors) of its direct superclass.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2 – Inheritance & Polymorphism):**
> The `super` keyword is one of the **three foundational pillars of inheritance-based polymorphism**, alongside method overriding and dynamic method dispatch. A solid understanding of `super` is non-negotiable for solving KTU exam questions on inheritance hierarchies.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **family business** where a son (subclass) inherits a shop from his father (superclass). The shop already has:
- A **cash register** (parent class variable)
- A **billing process** (parent class method)
- A **shop setup routine** (parent class constructor)

Now, the son wants to:
1. **Check the old cash register** value → `super.variable`
2. **Use the father's billing process but add GST** → `super.methodName()` then add his own logic
3. **Ensure the father's shop is set up before he adds his own modern décor** → `super()` in constructor

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** Just as a child carries the surname of the father but builds his own identity, the subclass inherits the superclass's behavior but uses `super` to explicitly access the parent's original implementation.

### 1.3 The Three Core Usages of `super`

The keyword `super` can be used in **three distinct contexts** within a Java program:

1. **To call the parent class constructor** → `super()` or `super(arguments)`
2. **To call the parent class method** → `super.methodName()`
3. **To access the parent class data member (instance variable)** → `super.variableName`

### 1.4 Scope and Lifetime Characteristics

| Property | Specification |
|---|---|
| **Keyword Type** | Reserved Literal (cannot be used as identifier) |
| **Reference Type** | Implicit, compiler-managed reference |
| **Scope** | Limited to instance context (non-static) |
| **Lifetime** | Tied to the current object lifetime |
| **Memory Footprint** | Zero overhead (compiler-optimized) |
| **Default Behavior** | If omitted, compiler inserts `super()` as first statement |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Contexts — Exhaustive Operational Logic

#### Context A: `super` to Invoke Parent Class Constructor

**Operational Rule Chain:**

1. When a subclass object is created, the **JVM must construct the entire inheritance chain** from top to bottom.
2. The compiler **automatically inserts a call `super()`** as the **first statement** of every subclass constructor — IF the developer does not explicitly write one.
3. `super()` must always be the **first executable statement** inside a constructor body.
4. If the parent class has **no default (no-argument) constructor**, the compiler will throw an error if explicit `super(args)` is not provided.
5. The chaining continues recursively — if grandparent has a parameterized constructor, the chain must be explicitly maintained.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Writing a statement before `super(...)`. This causes a compile-time error: *"Constructor call must be the first statement in a constructor."*

#### Context B: `super` to Invoke Parent Class Method (Method Overriding)

**Operational Rule Chain:**

1. When a subclass **overrides** a parent method, the subclass version **shadows** the parent's version.
2. To invoke the **overridden parent method's original logic** from within the subclass, use `super.methodName()`.
3. This is commonly used in the **Template Method Design Pattern** and to **extend** (not replace) parent behavior.
4. `super.method()` follows standard dynamic dispatch rules when calling an overridden method that itself calls another overridden method.

#### Context C: `super` to Access Parent Class Data Members

**Operational Rule Chain:**

1. When a parent and child class share an instance variable with the **same name**, the child's variable **hides** the parent's (this is called **variable shadowing**, not overriding).
2. To access the parent's hidden variable, use `super.variableName`.
3. This works **only for non-static, accessible (non-private)** parent members.
4. `super` **cannot** be used to access `private` members of the parent class — the encapsulation barrier still holds.

### 2.2 KTU Cheat Sheet — Master Reference Table

> [!NOTE]
> The table below is a **high-yield rapid-revision matrix** expected in KTU Module 2 questions. Memorize the columns!

| Context | Syntax | Placement Rule | Can be chained? | Accesses Private Parent? | Required When? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Constructor Call | `super()` or `super(args)` | Must be **first statement** of subclass constructor | Yes (constructor chaining) | N/A (constructors are not private access) | Parent has no default constructor |
| Method Call | `super.methodName(args)` | Anywhere inside instance method | No (use parent of parent) | No (private methods are not inherited) | Extending (not replacing) parent behavior |
| Variable Access | `super.variableName` | Anywhere inside instance context | No | **No** (compile-time error) | Variable shadowing scenario |
| In Static Context | **Not allowed** | Compile-time error if used | N/A | N/A | Never |
| In `main()` method | **Not allowed** | Compile-time error | N/A | N/A | Never |

### 2.3 Critical Compiler-Injected Rules

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Ironclad Rules that KTU Examiners Frequently Test:**

**Rule 1 — The Implicit `super()` Insertion:**
The Java compiler **automatically inserts** a no-argument `super()` call as the first statement of every constructor **unless** the developer explicitly writes `super()` or `this()`. This guarantees complete object construction across the inheritance hierarchy.

**Rule 2 — `this()` and `super()` are Mutually Exclusive:**
A constructor **cannot** call both `this()` and `super()` because **both must be the first statement**. Choosing one precludes the other. If you write `this(args)`, then the **delegated constructor** is responsible for calling `super()`.

**Rule 3 — `super` is Instance-Scoped, Not Class-Scoped:**
`super` is **resolved at runtime** based on the actual object type (for methods) and refers to the **immediate parent's static type** at compile time. It **cannot** be used in `static` methods, `static` blocks, or as a standalone expression.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The `super` keyword is fundamental in building **enterprise-grade Java applications**:

- **Spring Framework** — The `AbstractController` pattern uses `super` to extend base controller behavior.
- **JUnit Testing** — `setUp()` methods often call `super.setUp()` to inherit test fixtures.
- **JavaFX / Swing** — Custom UI components extend `javafx.scene.control.Control` and call `super(...)` to initialize the visual base.
- **Hibernate ORM** — Custom entity interceptors use `super` to preserve framework-managed lifecycle methods.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Code Derivations

### 3.1 Derivation Trace 1 — Constructor Chaining via `super()`

**Problem Setup:** Demonstrate how `super()` constructs the inheritance chain from top-most ancestor downward.

**Java Code:**
```java
class Grandparent {
    Grandparent() {
        System.out.println("Grandparent constructor");
    }
}

class Parent extends Grandparent {
    Parent() {
        super();  // Explicit call to Grandparent()
        System.out.println("Parent constructor");
    }
}

class Child extends Parent {
    Child() {
        super();  // Explicit call to Parent()
        System.out.println("Child constructor");
    }
}

public class Demo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Child c = new Child();
    }
}
```

**Step-by-Step Execution Trace:**

| Execution Step | Action | Output | Stack State |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `new Child()` called | — | `main()` active |
| 2 | `Child()` constructor invoked | — | `main()` + `Child()` |
| 3 | `super()` → calls `Parent()` | — | + `Parent()` |
| 4 | `Parent()` invokes `super()` → `Grandparent()` | — | + `Grandparent()` |
| 5 | `Grandparent()` body executes | `Grandparent constructor` | — |
| 6 | `Grandparent()` returns to `Parent()` | — | `Parent()` resumes |
| 7 | `Parent()` body executes | `Parent constructor` | — |
| 8 | `Parent()` returns to `Child()` | — | `Child()` resumes |
| 9 | `Child()` body executes | `Child constructor` | — |

**Final Output:**
```
Grandparent constructor
Parent constructor
Child constructor
```

**Mathematical Representation of the Construction Order:**

$$
\text{Construction Order} = \text{TopDown}(C_1 \to C_2 \to \cdots \to C_n)
$$

where $C_1$ is the top-most ancestor and $C_n$ is the most derived class. This is the **reverse** of the `instanceof` traversal direction.

### 3.2 Derivation Trace 2 — `super` in Method Overriding (Behavioral Extension)

**Problem Setup:** A `SavingsAccount` extends `BankAccount`. The `calculateInterest()` method must use the parent's base rate logic but add a bonus multiplier.

**Java Code:**
```java
class BankAccount {
    double balance = 10000;
    
    double calculateInterest() {
        return balance * 0.04;  // 4% base interest
    }
}

class SavingsAccount extends BankAccount {
    double balance = 20000;  // Shadowing: hides parent's balance
    
    @Override
    double calculateInterest() {
        double baseInterest = super.calculateInterest();   // Uses parent's logic
        double bonus = super.balance * 0.01;              // Uses parent's balance
        return baseInterest + bonus;
    }
}

public class BankDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        SavingsAccount sa = new SavingsAccount();
        System.out.println("Total Interest: Rs." + sa.calculateInterest());
    }
}
```

**Step-by-Step Numerical Evaluation:**

Let the savings account object be instantiated. The execution proceeds as follows.

$$
\text{baseInterest} = \text{super.calculateInterest()}
$$

Since `super` refers to the parent portion of the object, the parent's `balance` (10000) is used.

$$
\text{baseInterest} = 10000 \times 0.04 = 400
$$

$$
\text{bonus} = \text{super.balance} \times 0.01 = 10000 \times 0.01 = 100
$$

$$
\text{Total Interest} = \text{baseInterest} + \text{bonus} = 400 + 100 = 500
$$

**Final Output:**
```
Total Interest: Rs.500.0
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** Without `super`, the `this.balance` would resolve to the child's `20000`, producing different values. The `super` keyword disambiguates which `balance` is used.

### 3.3 Derivation Trace 3 — `super` with Parameterized Constructors

**Problem Setup:** Parent has no default constructor; subclass must explicitly invoke parameterized parent constructor.

**Java Code:**
```java
class Vehicle {
    String type;
    int wheels;
    
    Vehicle(String type, int wheels) {  // Only parameterized constructor
        this.type = type;
        this.wheels = wheels;
    }
}

class Car extends Vehicle {
    String brand;
    
    Car(String brand, int wheels) {
        super("Car", wheels);  // Must call parameterized super
        this.brand = brand;
    }
    
    void display() {
        System.out.println("Type: " + super.type);     // Accesses parent's type
        System.out.println("Wheels: " + super.wheels);  // Accesses parent's wheels
        System.out.println("Brand: " + this.brand);
    }
}

public class VehicleDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Car myCar = new Car("Honda", 4);
        myCar.display();
    }
}
```

**Execution Trace:**

$$
\text{Object State After super("Car", 4):}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{type} &= \text{"Car"} \\
\text{wheels} &= 4
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\text{Object State After this.brand = brand:}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{type} &= \text{"Car"} \\
\text{wheels} &= 4 \\
\text{brand} &= \text{"Honda"}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Output:**
```
Type: Car
Wheels: 4
Brand: Honda
```

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use `super`

| Scenario | Use `super`? | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Parent has no default constructor | **Yes (Mandatory)** | Compiler cannot auto-insert `super()` |
| Extending parent method behavior | **Yes (Recommended)** | Preserves parent logic, adds child logic |
| No variable shadowing exists | **No (Optional)** | Plain name suffices; `super` adds noise |
| Accessing private parent member | **No (Impossible)** | Encapsulation violation — use protected or getter |
| Inside `static` method | **No (Compile Error)** | `super` requires instance context |
| Constructor calls another constructor of same class | **No — use `this()`** | `super()` and `this()` cannot coexist |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Memory Architecture — Object Layout with `super` Reference

The following diagram illustrates how a single object in memory contains both its own fields and (conceptually) the inherited superclass fields, with `super` providing a logical pointer to the parent portion.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    objMem["Child Object in Heap Memory"]
    parentPortion["Parent Portion (super reference)"]
    childPortion["Child Portion (this reference)"]
    
    parentVar1["parentVar1 (inherited)"]
    parentVar2["parentVar2 (inherited)"]
    parentMethod["parentMethod() (inherited)"]
    
    childVar1["childVar1 (declared)"]
    childVar2["childVar2 (declared)"]
    childMethod["childMethod() (declared)"]
    
    superPtr["super pointer (logical link)"]
    thisPtr["this pointer (implicit self-ref)"]
    
    objMem --> parentPortion
    objMem --> childPortion
    parentPortion --> parentVar1
    parentPortion --> parentVar2
    parentPortion --> parentMethod
    childPortion --> childVar1
    childPortion --> childVar2
    childPortion --> childMethod
    
    superPtr -.points to.-> parentPortion
    thisPtr -.points to.-> objMem
```

### 4.2 Constructor Chaining Flow — Top-Down Construction

This diagram models the **execution flow** when a multi-level inheritance hierarchy is instantiated, showing the `super()` call chain.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode(["main calls new Child()"])
    childCtor["Child() constructor begins"]
    superC1["super() invoked from Child"]
    parentCtor["Parent() constructor begins"]
    superC2["super() invoked from Parent"]
    gpCtor["Grandparent() constructor begins"]
    gpBody["Grandparent body executes"]
    gpReturn["Grandparent returns"]
    parentBody["Parent body executes"]
    parentReturn["Parent returns"]
    childBody["Child body executes"]
    childReturn["Child returns"]
    endNode(["Object fully constructed"])
    
    startNode --> childCtor
    childCtor --> superC1
    superC1 --> parentCtor
    parentCtor --> superC2
    superC2 --> gpCtor
    gpCtor --> gpBody
    gpBody --> gpReturn
    gpReturn --> parentBody
    parentBody --> parentReturn
    parentReturn --> childBody
    childBody --> childReturn
    childReturn --> endNode
```

### 4.3 Decision Tree — `super` vs `this` vs Plain Identifier

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startQ{"What do you need to access?"}
    
    q1{"Same class variable/method?"}
    q2{"Parent class variable/method?"}
    q3{"Different constructor of same class?"}
    q4{"Parent class constructor?"}
    
    a1["Use plain name or this.x"]
    a2["Use super.x or super.method()"]
    a3["Use this() or this(args)"]
    a4["Use super() or super(args)"]
    
    startQ --> q1
    startQ --> q2
    startQ --> q3
    startQ --> q4
    
    q1 --> a1
    q2 --> a2
    q3 --> a3
    q4 --> a4
```

### 4.4 Method Override Resolution — With and Without `super`

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    caller["Object.methodCall()"]
    checkOver{"Is method overridden?"}
    superCall["super.methodCall() forces parent"]
    thisCall["this.methodCall() uses overridden version"]
    
    parentImpl["ParentClass.methodCall() executes"]
    childImpl["ChildClass.methodCall() executes"]
    
    caller --> checkOver
    checkOver -- "Yes, want parent version" --> superCall
    checkOver -- "Yes, want child version" --> thisCall
    checkOver -- "Not overridden" --> childImpl
    
    superCall --> parentImpl
    thisCall --> childImpl
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
> **What is the purpose of the `super` keyword in Java? List its uses.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The `super` keyword in Java is a **reference variable** that refers to the **immediate parent class** of the current object. It is used in the following three contexts:

1. **`super()`** — To invoke the parent class constructor (must be the first statement in a subclass constructor).
2. **`super.methodName()`** — To call the overridden parent class method from within the subclass.
3. **`super.variableName`** — To access the parent class data member that has been shadowed by a same-named variable in the subclass.

> *(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for constructor usage, 1 mark for method and variable usage combined.)*

---

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
> **Can `super` and `this` be used in the same constructor? Justify your answer.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**No, `super()` and `this()` cannot be used in the same constructor.** This is because both calls must be the **first executable statement** of the constructor body. Since a constructor can have only one "first statement," invoking one precludes the other. If `this(args)` is used to delegate to another constructor of the same class, that delegated constructor is responsible for invoking `super()`.

> *(Award 2 marks for the rule, 1 mark for the justification about first-statement placement.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the three uses of the `super` keyword in Java with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program to demonstrate the use of `super` to call the parent class constructor and to access a parent class variable that is shadowed in the subclass. Explain the output. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Use 1 — Calling Parent Class Constructor (3 marks):**

```java
class Animal {
    Animal() {
        System.out.println("Animal constructor called");
    }
}

class Dog extends Animal {
    Dog() {
        super();  // Calls parent constructor
        System.out.println("Dog constructor called");
    }
}
```

The `super()` call must appear as the first statement. If the parent has only a parameterized constructor, `super(args)` is mandatory.

**Use 2 — Calling Parent Class Method (2 marks):**

```java
class Parent {
    void display() {
        System.out.println("Parent display");
    }
}

class Child extends Parent {
    @Override
    void display() {
        super.display();  // Calls parent version
        System.out.println("Child display");
    }
}
```

This is used to **extend** rather than **replace** the parent's behavior.

**Use 3 — Accessing Parent Class Variable (2 marks):**

```java
class A {
    int x = 10;
}

class B extends A {
    int x = 20;
    
    void printBoth() {
        System.out.println("Child x = " + x);        // 20
        System.out.println("Parent x = " + super.x); // 10
    }
}
```

This disambiguates shadowed variables.

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Complete Java Program (5 marks):**

```java
class Employee {
    String name = "General Employee";
    double salary = 30000;
    
    Employee(String name, double salary) {
        this.name = name;
        this.salary = salary;
        System.out.println("Employee constructor: " + name);
    }
}

class Manager extends Employee {
    String name = "Default Manager";  // Shadowing parent variable
    double bonus;
    
    Manager(String name, double salary, double bonus) {
        super(name, salary);  // Calls parent constructor
        this.bonus = bonus;
        System.out.println("Manager constructor: " + name);
    }
    
    void displayDetails() {
        System.out.println("Manager name (this): " + this.name);
        System.out.println("Manager name (super): " + super.name);
        System.out.println("Salary: " + super.salary);
        System.out.println("Bonus: " + bonus);
    }
}

public class Company {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Manager m = new Manager("Alice", 80000, 15000);
        m.displayDetails();
    }
}
```

**Output Explanation (2 marks):**

The output is:
```
Employee constructor: Alice
Manager constructor: Alice
Manager name (this): Default Manager
Manager name (super): Alice
Salary: 80000.0
Bonus: 15000.0
```

The first two lines confirm constructor chaining via `super(name, salary)`. The `displayDetails()` method demonstrates that `this.name` refers to the child's default value, while `super.name` retrieves the value set by the parent constructor.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a)** With an example, explain how the `super` keyword can be used to invoke an overridden method of the parent class. Why is this useful? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between `super` and `this` keywords in Java with examples. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Example (5 marks):**

```java
class Shape {
    void draw() {
        System.out.println("Drawing a generic shape");
    }
}

class Circle extends Shape {
    @Override
    void draw() {
        super.draw();  // Calls parent's draw() first
        System.out.println("Drawing a circle with radius");
    }
}

class ColoredCircle extends Circle {
    @Override
    void draw() {
        super.draw();  // Calls Circle's draw()
        System.out.println("Applying color to circle");
    }
}

public class ShapeDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        ColoredCircle cc = new ColoredCircle();
        cc.draw();
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Drawing a generic shape
Drawing a circle with radius
Applying color to circle
```

**Why is this useful? (2 marks):**
1. It enables **behavioral extension** — the subclass can add new functionality without duplicating the parent's code.
2. It supports the **Open/Closed Principle** of OOP — classes are open for extension but closed for modification.
3. It is the foundation of the **Template Method Design Pattern**.

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

| Comparison Aspect | `super` keyword | `this` keyword |
|---|---|---|
| **Refers to** | Immediate **parent class** | **Current class** |
| **Constructor usage** | `super()` calls parent constructor | `this()` calls another constructor of same class |
| **Variable resolution** | Resolves shadowed parent variables | Resolves current class variables (disambiguates parameter vs. field) |
| **Method usage** | Calls overridden parent method | Calls current class method (rarely needed, but disambiguates) |
| **Static context** | **Not allowed** | **Not allowed** |
| **Inheritance required?** | **Yes** | No (works in any class) |
| **Compiler default** | Compiler auto-inserts `super()` if no `this()` is written | Not auto-inserted |
| **Usage frequency** | Used in inheritance hierarchies | Used in nearly every class |

**Example (2 marks):**

```java
class Demo {
    int value = 50;
    
    Demo(int value) {
        this.value = value;  // 'this.value' = instance field, 'value' = parameter
    }
    
    void show() {
        System.out.println("Instance value: " + this.value);
    }
}
```

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Deductions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to call `super(args)` when parent has no default constructor** — *up to 2 marks deducted* (compile-time error in exam scenarios).
> 2. **Confusing variable shadowing with method overriding** — Variable access via `super` is **not** overriding. State this distinction explicitly.
> 3. **Placing statements before `super()`** — Code-positioning errors. The first statement rule is inviolable.
> 4. **Using `super` in `static` context** — Will be marked as a fundamental conceptual error. Always state: *"super requires an instance."*
> 5. **Not mentioning constructor chaining** — Many students discuss `super` only for methods. KTU examiners specifically test constructor chaining scenarios.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Every KTU Exam:**

- The `super` keyword is a **reference to the immediate parent class object portion**.
- It has **exactly three uses**: constructor call, method call, and variable access.
- `super()` or `super(args)` **must be the first statement** of a constructor.
- The compiler **automatically inserts** `super()` if no `this()` or explicit `super()` is written.
- `super` and `this()` **cannot coexist** in the same constructor.
- `super` **cannot** be used in `static` methods, `static` blocks, or `main()`.
- `super` **cannot** access `private` members of the parent class.
- Variable access via `super` is **shadowing resolution**, **not** overriding.
- Method access via `super` is used for **behavioral extension**, not replacement.
- Construction proceeds **top-down** through the inheritance chain.
- `super` is **instance-scoped** and resolved at runtime for overridden methods.
- The `super` keyword is essential for the **Open/Closed Principle** and the **Template Method Pattern**.
- If the parent class has **only parameterized constructors**, the subclass **must** explicitly call `super(args)`.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: protected Members
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT414 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** protected Members # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In **Object Oriented Programming (OOP)**, the `protected` keyword is an **access modifier** (also called a **visibility specifier**) that governs the accessibility of class members (attributes and methods) across the inheritance hierarchy. A `protected` member occupies a specific, intermediate position in the access control spectrum — it is **more restrictive than `public`** but **less restrictive than `private`**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition** > A `protected` member is accessible: > 1. **Within the same class** in which it is declared. > 2. **Within subclasses (derived classes)** — even if those subclasses reside in a **different package/namespace** (in Java) or are derived via any inheritance mode (in C++). > 3. **Within the same package** as the class containing the member (Java-specific behavior, equivalent to package-private + subclass access). In **C++**, `protected` members are accessible: - Within the class itself. - Within its **friend functions** and **friend classes**. - Within any **derived class** — but **only through a pointer/reference of the derived class type** (a subtle but examinable rule). In **Java**, the visibility matrix for `protected` is wider: it merges *package-level access* with *subclass access*, making it the most commonly misunderstood modifier in board examinations. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Family Vault" Model Imagine a **family vault** (the class) that holds: - A **public display room** (`public` members) — anyone in the world can enter and see the exhibits. - A **private safe** (`private` members) — only the head of the family (the owning class) has the combination. - A **protected family archive room** (`protected` members) — only **immediate family members** (the class itself) and **descendants** (subclasses) can enter, even if those descendants live in another country (different package). > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Summary**: Think of `protected` as a *"bloodline-only"* pass — it bypasses geographical boundaries (packages) but enforces biological boundaries (inheritance relationships). ## 1.3 The Access Control Spectrum The complete access hierarchy in OOP, ordered from **most restrictive → least restrictive**, is: 1. `private` — Class-only access. 2. `default` / `package-private` (Java) / no modifier (C++ defaults to `private` in `class`) — Package-or-class-cluster access. 3. `protected` — Package + Inheritance access. 4. `public` — Universal access. ## 1.4 Standard Metrics and Engineering Constants > [!IMPORTANT] > **Industry-Standard Practice Metrics** > - **Inheritance Depth Recommendation**: A protected member should ideally not propagate more than **3 levels** deep in an inheritance hierarchy to avoid the *"fragile base class problem"*. > - **Encapsulation Threshold**: According to most OOP design guidelines (e.g., Robert C. Martin's *Clean Code*), at least **60–70%** of a class's members should be `private`, with `protected` reserved strictly for **template-method** or **state-sharing** design patterns. > - **Java Language Specification Reference**: The `protected` keyword is formally defined in **JLS §6.6.2.1** (Java Language Specification, detailing the rules of protected access). # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Rules — The Logic Stack The behavior of `protected` members is governed by a set of nested access rules. Each rule is examined in detail below. ### Rule 1 — Self-Access (Always Permitted) A class **C** can always access its own `protected` members, irrespective of any qualifier or location. This is the unconditional baseline. ### Rule 2 — Same-Container Access (Permitted) - **In Java**: Any other class **D** residing in the **same package** as **C** can access **C's** `protected` members. - **In C++**: A `protected` member is **not accessible** to other classes in the same translation unit unless they are friends or derived classes. (C++ does not have a "package" concept.) ### Rule 3 — Subclass Access via Inheritance (Permitted with Constraints) - **In Java**: A subclass **S** (even in a different package) can access a `protected` member **M** of its superclass **C**, **but only if the access is made through a reference of type `S` (or its subclass)**, not through a reference of type `C` or any other sibling class. - **In C++**: A derived class **D** can access `protected` members of base **B** only through a `D*` or `D&` reference. Accessing them through a `B*` is **not allowed**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU High-Yield Trap**: The reference-type restriction in Rule 3 is the **#1 question** asked in KTU exams regarding `protected`. Many students mistakenly assume that any subclass instance (even via a base-class pointer) can access protected members — **this is false**. ### Rule 4 — Cross-Hierarchy Access (Denied) A **non-related** class (i.e., one that does not inherit from **C** and does not share a package with **C**) **cannot** access `protected` members. This is identical to `private` behavior in this scenario. ## 2.2 Comparison Matrix — `private` vs. `default` vs. `protected` vs. `public` The following table encapsulates the entire access-control logic in a single glance — a high-yield revision artifact for KTU ESE. | Visibility Context | `private` | `default` (Java) / `private` (C++ class) | `protected` | `public` | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Same class** | ✅ Allowed | ✅ Allowed | ✅ Allowed | ✅ Allowed | | **Same package, non-subclass (Java)** | ❌ Denied | ✅ Allowed | ✅ Allowed | ✅ Allowed | | **Different package, subclass (Java)** | ❌ Denied | ❌ Denied | ✅ Allowed (with ref-type constraint) | ✅ Allowed | | **Different package, non-subclass (Java)** | ❌ Denied | ❌ Denied | ❌ Denied | ✅ Allowed | | **Derived class (C++)** | ❌ Denied | ❌ Denied | ✅ Allowed (via `D*`/`D&` only) | ✅ Allowed | | **Unrelated class (C++)** | ❌ Denied | ❌ Denied | ❌ Denied | ✅ Allowed | | **Friend (C++ only)** | ✅ Allowed | ✅ Allowed | ✅ Allowed | ✅ Allowed | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Formula for Accessibility Decision** > > $$\text{Access}(m) = \begin{cases} \text{GRANTED} & \text{if } (\text{SameClass}) \lor (\text{SamePackage} \land \text{NotPrivate}) \lor (\text{IsSubclass} \land \text{RefType} = \text{SubclassOrBelow}) \\ \text{DENIED} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ > > where: > - *SameClass* refers to the declaring class itself. > - *SamePackage* is Java-specific. > - *IsSubclass* checks inheritance relationship. > - *RefType* enforces the reference-type constraint (the famous KTU trap). ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The `protected` modifier is the **backbone of the Template Method design pattern** (Gang of Four) and is essential in: - **GUI Frameworks** (e.g., JavaFX, Swing): Base classes expose protected `layoutX`, `layoutY` properties to subclasses for custom rendering. - **Game Engines** (e.g., Unity, Unreal): `protected` fields like `health`, `mana` allow derived `Enemy`, `Player` classes to modify state without exposing them to the outside world. - **Web Frameworks** (e.g., Django, Spring): `protected` model fields enable ORM-level access by framework internals while preserving API-level encapsulation. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Java `protected` Across Packages **Problem Statement (Modeled after KTU University Exam, June 2022)**: Demonstrate the accessibility of `protected` members when a subclass resides in a **different package** than its superclass. Write a complete Java program and explain the reference-type constraint. ### Step 1 — Define the Superclass (Package `pkg1`) ```java package pkg1; public class Super { protected int value = 100; protected void display() { System.out.println("Super.value = " + value); } } ``` **Textual Explanation**: We declare `value` as a `protected int`, meaning it is hidden from the world but open to subclasses and same-package classes. ### Step 2 — Define the Subclass in a Different Package (Package `pkg2`) ```java package pkg2; import pkg1.Super; public class Sub extends Super { public void accessProtected() { // Direct access through 'this' reference (a Sub object) — ALLOWED System.out.println("Direct access: " + this.value); this.display(); } } ``` **Textual Explanation**: The subclass `Sub` can access `value` and `display()` directly **because it is accessing them through its own instance** (`this`). This satisfies the reference-type constraint. ### Step 3 — Demonstrate the Reference-Type Trap ```java package pkg2; import pkg1.Super; public class TestTrap { public static void main(String[] args) { Sub s = new Sub(); s.accessProtected(); // ✅ ALLOWED Super ref = new Sub(); // base-class reference to derived object // System.out.println(ref.value); // ❌ COMPILE-TIME ERROR } } ``` **Textual Explanation**: Even though the *actual object* is a `Sub`, the **reference type** is `Super`. The compiler checks access based on the **reference type**, not the runtime object type. Hence `ref.value` is denied. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Compiler Logic Walkthrough** > > $$\text{Decision}_{compile} = f(\text{ReferenceType}, \text{AccessModifier}, \text{Inheritance})$$ > > For `ref.value`: > - *ReferenceType* = `Super` > - *AccessModifier* = `protected` > - *Inheritance* = `TestTrap` is **not** a subclass of `Super` > - **Conclusion** = `DENIED` at compile time. ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — C++ `protected` with Multiple Inheritance **Problem Statement**: Show how `protected` members behave in a **diamond inheritance** scenario in C++. ### Step 1 — Base Class Definition ```cpp class Base { protected: int coreData; public: Base(int d) : coreData(d) {} }; ``` **Textual Explanation**: `coreData` is `protected` — hidden from outside, accessible to derived classes. ### Step 2 — Two Intermediate Derived Classes ```cpp class DerivedA : public Base { public: DerivedA(int d) : Base(d) {} void modifyA(int x) { coreData = x; } // ✅ Allowed }; class DerivedB : public Base { public: DerivedB(int d) : Base(d) {} void modifyB(int x) { coreData = x; } // ✅ Allowed }; ``` **Textual Explanation**: Both `DerivedA` and `DerivedB` can freely modify `coreData` because they access it through their own `this` pointer. ### Step 3 — Diamond Tip Class ```cpp class Diamond : public DerivedA, public DerivedB { public: Diamond(int d) : DerivedA(d), DerivedB(d) {} void show() { // std::cout << coreData; // ❌ AMBIGUOUS — which Base::coreData? std::cout << DerivedA::coreData; // ✅ Disambiguated access } }; ``` **Textual Explanation**: The `Diamond` class faces **ambiguity** when accessing `coreData` because it inherits two copies (one from each intermediate parent). Explicit disambiguation is required. > [!NOTE] > **Diamond Resolution Form** > > $$\text{Ambiguity} = \begin{cases} \text{Resolvable} & \text{with explicit } \text{Path::Member syntax} \\ \text{Permanent} & \text{without virtual inheritance} \end{cases}$$ ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Visibility Decisions in Inheritance Chains The following table maps every possible access scenario to its decision outcome. This is the **definitive reference matrix** for KTU 2024 Scheme exam preparation. | Declaring Class | Accessing Class | Relationship | Reference Type | Modifier | Decision | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | `A` | `A` | Self | `A` | `protected` | ✅ Granted | | `A` | `B` | Same package, no inheritance | `B` | `protected` | ✅ Granted (Java only) | | `A` | `C` | Subclass, same package | `C` | `protected` | ✅ Granted | | `A` | `C` | Subclass, different package | `C` | `protected` | ✅ Granted (Java) | | `A` | `C` | Subclass, different package | `A` (base ref) | `protected` | ❌ Denied (Java) | | `A` | `D` | Sibling, same package | `D` | `protected` | ❌ Denied (unless same pkg) | | `A` | `E` | Unrelated, different package | `E` | `protected` | ❌ Denied | ## 3.4 Algorithmic Derivation — When to Choose `protected` over `private` The decision flow below encapsulates professional OOP design judgment: $$ \text{ChooseModifier}(m) = \begin{cases} \text{private} & \text{if } m \text{ is implementation detail, never inherited} \\ \text{protected} & \text{if } m \text{ must be shared with subclasses only} \\ \text{package-private} & \text{if } m \text{ must be shared with package collaborators} \\ \text{public} & \text{if } m \text{ is part of the stable API contract} \end{cases} $$ **Textual Explanation**: This decision tree is a direct application of the **Liskov Substitution Principle** and **Information Hiding** (Parnas, 1972). Choosing `protected` is a deliberate trade-off — it weakens encapsulation in exchange for inheritance flexibility. # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Visibility Decision Flowchart The following Mermaid flowchart visualizes the complete decision algorithm that a Java/C++ compiler follows when resolving access to a `protected` member. ```mermaid flowchart TD start[Start: Access Request for Member m] --> checkSelf{Is accessing class
same as declaring class?} checkSelf -- Yes --> grant1[GRANTED Access] checkSelf -- No --> checkPackage{Is accessing class
in the same package?} checkPackage -- Yes --> grant2[GRANTED Access
Java Only] checkPackage -- No --> checkSubclass{Is accessing class
a subclass of declaring class?} checkSubclass -- No --> deny1[DENIED Access] checkSubclass -- Yes --> checkRefType{Is the reference type
subclass or below?} checkRefType -- Yes --> grant3[GRANTED Access] checkRefType -- No --> deny2[DENIED Access
Reference Type Trap] deny1 --> endNode[End] deny2 --> endNode grant1 --> endNode grant2 --> endNode grant3 --> endNode ``` **Diagram Reading Guide**: - The flowchart begins with an access request. - The first gate (`checkSelf`) is the unconditional baseline. - The second gate (`checkPackage`) is Java-specific. - The third gate (`checkSubclass`) tests inheritance. - The fourth gate (`checkRefType`) is the **most-asked KTU trap** — it ensures the reference type is the subclass itself, not the base class. ## 4.2 Class Hierarchy Inheritance Map The following Mermaid class diagram illustrates a practical 3-level inheritance chain where `protected` is used to share state across generations. ```mermaid classDiagram class Vehicle { #int maxSpeed #String model #void startEngine() } class Car { +int numDoors +void honk() } class ElectricCar { +int batteryCapacity +void charge() } Vehicle <|-- Car Car <|-- ElectricCar ``` **Diagram Reading Guide**: - The `#` symbol denotes `protected` members in UML notation. - `Vehicle` declares `protected` state (`maxSpeed`, `model`) and behavior (`startEngine`). - `Car` (subclass) can access all three `protected` members. - `ElectricCar` (sub-subclass) inherits this access **transitively**. - The `+` symbol denotes `public` members (e.g., `numDoors`, `honk`). ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — The Protection Layers The following block diagram maps the conceptual layers of access protection in an OOP system. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph OUTER[Outside World Layer] extClient[External Client Code] end subgraph PUBLIC[Public API Layer] publicMethods[Public Methods and Fields] end subgraph PROTECTED[Protected Family Layer] protectedMembers[Protected Members
Inheritance Only] end subgraph PRIVATE[Private Core Layer] privateMembers[Private Members
Class Encapsulation] end extClient -->|reads calls| publicMethods publicMethods -->|invokes internally| protectedMembers protectedMembers -->|delegates to| privateMembers privateMembers -->|state changes bubble up| protectedMembers protectedMembers -->|exposed via| publicMethods ``` **Diagram Reading Guide**: - The **Outside World** can only touch the **Public API Layer**. - The **Public API** invokes **Protected Members** internally. - **Protected Members** are the bridge between the public interface and the private core. - **Private Members** form the deepest encapsulation layer. - This layered architecture mirrors the **Onion Architecture** pattern, with `protected` as the inner-ring connector to subclasses. # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Q: Differentiate between `private` and `protected` access specifiers in C++ with respect to inheritance.** **Model Answer**: The `private` access specifier restricts member visibility to the **declaring class only** — even derived classes cannot access private members of the base class directly. In contrast, the `protected` access specifier allows derived classes to access the member, **provided** the access is made through a pointer or reference of the derived class type (not the base class type). Additionally, `protected` members are accessible to friend functions and friend classes of the declaring class, whereas `private` members are limited strictly to the class and its friends. In essence, `protected` is a **relaxation** of `private` that opens access to the inheritance hierarchy. ### Question 2 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2023]** **Q: Can a `protected` member of a class be accessed from a non-related class in the same package in Java? Justify your answer.** **Model Answer**: **Yes**, a `protected` member of a class can be accessed from a non-related class in the **same package** in Java. This is a Java-specific behavior arising from the fact that `protected` access **subsumes package-level (default) access**. Therefore, any class within the same package — whether it inherits from the declaring class or not — can access the `protected` member. However, this allowance does **not** extend to non-related classes in **different packages**; such classes are denied access just as they would be for `private` members. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of `protected` access specifier in Java. Discuss its visibility with respect to: - (i) The same class - (ii) Same package, non-subclass - (iii) Different package, subclass - (iv) Different package, non-subclass **(b) [7 Marks]** Write a Java program demonstrating that a `protected` member of a superclass is **not accessible** through a base-class reference, even when the actual object is of the derived class type. Justify the output with the reference-type constraint rule. --- #### Model Solution to Question A(a) **Definition**: In Java, the `protected` access specifier provides a **hybrid access level** — combining package-level access with subclass access. It is the second-most permissive modifier after `public`. **Visibility Analysis**: 1. **(i) Same class**: A class can always access its own `protected` members. This is unconditional and forms the baseline of the access hierarchy. 2. **(ii) Same package, non-subclass**: ✅ **Allowed**. Java's `protected` includes package-level access. Any class in the same package can read or invoke the `protected` member. 3. **(iii) Different package, subclass**: ✅ **Allowed (with constraint)**. A subclass in a different package can access the `protected` member, **but only if the reference used for access is of the subclass type** (or a type further down the inheritance chain). Access via a base-class reference is **denied** at compile time. 4. **(iv) Different package, non-subclass**: ❌ **Denied**. A class that neither inherits from the declaring class nor shares its package has no access to `protected` members. To such classes, the member behaves as if it were `private`. --- #### Model Solution to Question A(b) **Java Program**: ```java // File: pkg1/Super.java package pkg1; public class Super { protected int secret = 42; protected void reveal() { System.out.println("Super.reveal() called, secret = " + secret); } } // File: pkg2/Sub.java package pkg2; import pkg1.Super; public class Sub extends Super { public void showViaThis() { System.out.println("Via this: " + this.secret); this.reveal(); } } // File: pkg2/Demo.java package pkg2; import pkg1.Super; public class Demo { public static void main(String[] args) { Sub s = new Sub(); s.showViaThis(); // Access via base-class reference: Super baseRef = new Sub(); // System.out.println(baseRef.secret); // COMPILE-TIME ERROR // baseRef.reveal(); // COMPILE-TIME ERROR } } ``` **Justification**: - The lines commented out at the end (`baseRef.secret` and `baseRef.reveal()`) cause **compile-time errors** with the message *"secret has protected access in pkg1.Super"*. - The Java compiler resolves access based on the **reference type** (`Super`), not the **runtime object type** (`Sub`). - Since `Demo` is **not** a subclass of `Super` (it merely *uses* `Super` as a reference type), it cannot invoke the `protected` members of `Super`. - This is the **reference-type constraint**, formalized in **JLS §6.6.2.1**. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice* **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the behavior of `protected` members in C++ during **single inheritance**. Provide a code example showing: - (i) A base class with a `protected` data member. - (ii) A derived class accessing the `protected` member through its own pointer. - (iii) An attempt to access the `protected` member through a base-class pointer (this should fail). **(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **diamond inheritance problem** in C++ with respect to `protected` members. Show how **virtual inheritance** resolves the ambiguity. Provide a complete, compilable code example. --- #### Model Solution to Question B(a) **Code Example**: ```cpp #include using namespace std; class Base { protected: int id; public: Base(int i) : id(i) {} }; class Derived : public Base { string name; public: Derived(int i, string n) : Base(i), name(n) {} void show() { cout << "ID: " << id << ", Name: " << name << endl; // ✅ ALLOWED } }; int main() { Derived d(101, "Alice"); d.show(); Base* bp = &d; // cout << bp->id; // ❌ COMPILE-TIME ERROR: 'id' is protected return 0; } ``` **Explanation**: - In the `Derived` class method `show()`, the `protected` member `id` is accessed directly — this is **allowed** because the access is via a `Derived*` (`this`). - In `main()`, `bp` is a `Base*` pointer. Accessing `bp->id` is **denied** by the C++ compiler because the access is being attempted through a base-class pointer, even though the runtime object is a `Derived`. > [!NOTE] > **C++ vs. Java Subtle Difference** > > $$\text{C++ Rule}: \text{Access via } B^* \text{ to } B\text{'s protected} \Rightarrow \text{DENIED}$$ > > $$\text{Java Rule}: \text{Access via } B \text{ ref to } B\text{'s protected} \Rightarrow \text{DENIED if accessor not a subclass}$$ > > The mechanisms differ in syntax but the **principle is identical**: access is gated by both the access modifier and the reference/pointer type. --- #### Model Solution to Question B(b) **The Diamond Problem with `protected` Members**: ```cpp #include using namespace std; class Base { protected: int data; public: Base(int d) : data(d) {} }; class Left : public Base { public: Left(int d) : Base(d) {} }; class Right : public Base { public: Right(int d) : Base(d) {} }; // Virtual inheritance resolves the diamond ambiguity class Bottom : virtual public Left, virtual public Right { public: Bottom(int d) : Left(d), Right(d), Base(d) {} void show() { cout << "Data: " << data << endl; // ✅ NO AMBIGUITY (virtual inheritance) } }; int main() { Bottom b(202); b.show(); return 0; } ``` **Explanation**: - Without `virtual` inheritance, `Bottom` would inherit **two copies** of `Base::data` (one via `Left`, one via `Right`), causing **ambiguity** when accessing `data`. - With `virtual` inheritance, the compiler ensures a **single shared instance** of `Base`, eliminating ambiguity. - The constructor of `Bottom` now directly initializes `Base` (not just `Left` and `Right`) — this is a mandatory requirement of virtual inheritance. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > > - **Pitfall #1 (3 marks lost)**: Students often confuse `protected` with `private` when the question asks about *subclass access* in C++. Always clarify that `protected` is **accessible** to derived classes. > - **Pitfall #2 (2 marks lost)**: In Java, students forget that `protected` includes **package-level access**. Marking it as "subclass-only" loses partial credit. > - **Pitfall #3 (4 marks lost — most critical)**: The **reference-type constraint** in Java and the **pointer-type constraint** in C++ are commonly ignored. Always mention that the access must be made through a reference/pointer of the **subclass type**, not the base type. > - **Pitfall #4 (2 marks lost)**: When asked about `protected` in **diamond inheritance**, students forget to show the `virtual` keyword in the inheritance declaration. Always include it. > - **Pitfall #5 (3 marks lost)**: In code-writing questions, students often forget the **`super` / base class constructor call** in the derived class constructor's initializer list. This is a frequent deduction. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for `protected` Members** > > - **`protected` is a hybrid access modifier** — it bridges `private` and `public`, granting access to subclasses and (in Java) same-package classes. > > - **Java's `protected`** = **package access + subclass access**. > > - **C++'s `protected`** = **derived-class access + friend access** (no package concept). > > - **The Reference-Type Constraint (THE KTU GOLDEN RULE)**: A `protected` member can only be accessed through a reference/pointer of the **subclass type** (or below), not the base class type. The compiler checks the **static type**, not the **runtime type**. > > - **Diamond inheritance in C++** requires `virtual` inheritance to disambiguate `protected` members inherited from a common ancestor. > > - **`protected` is NOT a substitute for `public`**. Once a member is exposed as `protected`, it becomes part of the inheritance contract — changing it later breaks subclasses (the *fragile base class problem*). > > - **Best Practice**: Use `protected` sparingly. Reserve it for fields/methods that subclasses **must** override or directly manipulate (e.g., the Template Method pattern). > > - **Default access in C++ classes** is `private`; in C++ `struct`, it is `public`. Java classes have no default — you must declare a modifier or use package-private. > > - **Exam Tip**: When asked to "explain `protected`," always cover **all four access scenarios** (same class, same package/non-subclass, different package/subclass, different package/non-subclass) — this is the most common marking scheme. > > - **Compiler vs. Runtime**: The access check happens at **compile time** based on the **reference type**. Even if the runtime object is a subclass, an illegal reference-type access fails to compile. > > - **Friend Functions in C++** can access `protected` members of any class that declares them as friends — this is a parallel but distinct mechanism from inheritance. > > - **`protected` in Interfaces (Java 9+)**: Interfaces can have `private` methods (since Java 9), but `protected` methods in interfaces are implicitly `public` — a subtle but examinable detail.

topics.title: Method Overriding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT414 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Method Overriding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 2 — METHOD OVERRIDING

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Board Standard):** **Method Overriding** is a feature of Object-Oriented Programming that allows a **subclass (child class)** to provide a **specific implementation** of a method that is **already defined** in one of its **superclasses (parent classes)**. The overridden method in the subclass must have the **exact same name, return type (or covariant return type), and parameter list** as the method in the parent class.

It is the primary mechanism by which Java achieves **Runtime Polymorphism (Dynamic Method Dispatch)** — a core pillar of the OOP paradigm covered in KTU Module 2.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PEECT414 Module 2):** Method Overriding falls under the unit on *Inheritance and Polymorphism*. It is **invariably tested** in KTU End Semester Examinations (ESE), typically as a **Part B (14-mark)** programming/analytical question worth **7 marks per sub-part**.

---

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy — "The Manager Inheriting the Employee Role"

Imagine a corporate organization:

- The parent class `Employee` defines a generic method `calculateBonus()` that returns a **standard 10% bonus** for any employee.
- Now, the child class `Manager` inherits from `Employee` but needs a **different rule** — say, a **20% bonus** plus a fixed performance incentive.

Instead of creating a brand-new method (which would break the contract and force every caller to know the exact type), `Manager` simply **re-defines** the `calculateBonus()` method with its own logic.

From the perspective of the HR system, it still calls `calculateBonus()` — but the **actual behaviour executed depends on whether the employee is a regular worker or a manager**. This is *Method Overriding* in its purest business form.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Lock:** The *signature* (method name + parameters) is the **contract**. The *body* is the **implementation**, which each subclass is free to rewrite. The caller never needs to know which subclass is at the other end.

---

## 1.3 Why Method Overriding Matters (Engineering Relevance)

| Domain | Use Case |
|---|---|
| **GUI Frameworks** (JavaFX/Swing) | `paint()` is overridden by every custom component |
| **Plugin Architectures** | Base class defines hooks; plugins override them |
| **Template Method Pattern** | Algorithm skeleton in parent, steps overridden in children |
| **Frameworks like Spring/Struts** | Lifecycle methods (`init()`, `service()`) are overridden |
| **Collections API** | `equals()`, `hashCode()`, `toString()` are routinely overridden |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Rule Sheet

## 2.1 The Core Mechanism — How the JVM Resolves an Overridden Call

When a call `obj.someMethod()` is compiled, the **compiler** checks only the **reference type** (compile-time type) to confirm the method exists. However, the **JVM at runtime** looks at the **actual object on the heap** (runtime type) to decide *which* version of the overridden method to invoke. This process is called **Dynamic Method Dispatch**.

### Operational Steps (in execution order):

1. **Compile-time check** — The compiler verifies that the method signature exists in the reference type (parent class). If not, compilation fails.
2. **Object creation** — At runtime, an object of the subclass is created and its reference is stored in a parent-class variable.
3. **Method binding** — The JVM binds the call to the **most specific (lowest-in-hierarchy) overridden version** available for the actual object.
4. **Execution** — The chosen method body executes on the live object.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Concept:** The choice of which overridden method to execute is made at **runtime**, not at compile time. This is the literal definition of **Runtime Polymorphism**.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Rule Sheet (Cheat-Sheet Table)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** The Rules Table below is the single most-tested component in Part A (3-mark) questions. **Memorize every row verbatim** — examiners award 0 marks for partially correct rules.

| # | Rule | Permitted? | KTU Board Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| R1 | Method name **must be identical** in parent & child | ✅ Yes | Strict requirement |
| R2 | Parameter list **must be identical** (no new params) | ✅ Yes | Otherwise it becomes *overloading*, not overriding |
| R3 | Return type **same** OR a **covariant return type** (subclass) | ✅ Yes | Covariance introduced in Java 5 |
| R4 | Access modifier in child **must not be more restrictive** | ✅ Yes | Can broaden (e.g., `protected` → `public`) |
| R5 | **Cannot override `static` methods** — they are *hidden* instead | ❌ No | This is **method hiding**, not overriding |
| R6 | **Cannot override `final` methods** | ❌ No | `final` = no modification allowed |
| R7 | **Cannot override `private` methods** | ❌ No | Private methods are not inherited |
| R8 | **Constructors cannot be overridden** | ❌ No | They belong to their declaring class |
| R9 | Subclass **may use `super.methodName()`** to invoke parent version | ✅ Yes | Common in extended functionality |
| R10 | **Instance methods only** — overriding applies to objects | ✅ Yes | Static/primitive-bound methods follow different rules |

---

## 2.3 The `super` Keyword in Overriding Context

When a subclass overrides a method but still wants to *reuse* the parent's logic as a base, the `super` keyword is used.

$$\text{Execution Order} = \text{Parent body (via super)} \rightarrow \text{Child body (extension)}$$

This is the foundation of the **Template Method Design Pattern**, which KTU frequently references in Module 5.

---

## 2.4 Method Overriding vs Method Overloading (High-Yield Comparison)

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **favourite 3-mark KTU question**: *"Differentiate between method overloading and method overriding."* Students frequently confuse the two — see the comparison below.

| Parameter | Method Overloading | Method Overriding |
|---|---|---|
| **Class scope** | Within a **single class** | Across **parent-child classes** |
| **Parameter list** | **Must differ** (type/count/order) | **Must be identical** |
| **Return type** | Can differ (no constraints) | Must be same or covariant |
| **Binding time** | **Compile-time** (Static binding) | **Runtime** (Dynamic binding) |
| **Polymorphism type** | Compile-time polymorphism | Runtime polymorphism |
| **Access modifier** | No restrictions | Cannot be more restrictive in child |
| **`static` methods** | Can be overloaded | **Cannot** be overridden (only hidden) |
| **`final` methods** | Can be overloaded | **Cannot** be overridden |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough with Code

## 3.1 Canonical Java Example — The Bank Account Hierarchy

```java
// File: BankDemo.java
class Account {
    double balance;

    Account(double balance) {
        this.balance = balance;
    }

    // Method to be overridden
    double calculateInterest() {
        return balance * 0.04;  // Base 4% interest
    }
}

class SavingsAccount extends Account {
    SavingsAccount(double balance) {
        super(balance);
    }

    // Overriding the parent's calculateInterest()
    @Override
    double calculateInterest() {
        return balance * 0.05;  // Savings gets 5%
    }
}

class FixedDeposit extends Account {
    FixedDeposit(double balance) {
        super(balance);
    }

    // Overriding again with a different rate
    @Override
    double calculateInterest() {
        return balance * 0.07;  // FD gets 7%
    }
}
```

### Step-by-Step Trace of a Polymorphic Call

```java
Account ref;            // Compile-time type: Account

ref = new SavingsAccount(10000);
System.out.println(ref.calculateInterest());   // LINE A

ref = new FixedDeposit(10000);
System.out.println(ref.calculateInterest());   // LINE B
```

**Trace LINE A:**

1. **Compile phase:** Compiler sees `ref` is of type `Account`, finds `calculateInterest()` there → ✅ compiles.
2. **Runtime phase:** JVM checks actual object → it is `SavingsAccount` → jumps to `SavingsAccount.calculateInterest()`.
3. **Execution:** $10000 \times 0.05 = 500.00$ → prints `500.0`.

**Trace LINE B:**

1. **Compile phase:** Same as before.
2. **Runtime phase:** Actual object is now `FixedDeposit` → JVM binds to `FixedDeposit.calculateInterest()`.
3. **Execution:** $10000 \times 0.07 = 700.00$ → prints `700.0`.

> [!NOTE]
> The reference variable `ref` is **the same**, but the **method that executes differs**. This is runtime polymorphism demonstrated live.

---

## 3.2 Covariant Return Type — Worked Example

$$\text{Parent return type} = \text{Object} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Child return type} = \text{String (subclass of Object)}$$

```java
class Animal {
    Object getInfo() {
        return "Generic Animal";
    }
}

class Dog extends Animal {
    @Override
    String getInfo() {            // Covariant return: String IS-A Object
        return "This is a Dog";
    }
}
```

**Why it works:** Java's type system permits a subclass to *narrow* the return type because a `String` *is-a* `Object` — the contract of "returning an Object" is satisfied, only more specifically.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Covariance applies to **return types only** — *not* to parameter types. Subclass method parameters must match **exactly**.

---

## 3.3 The `super` Keyword — Augmenting Parent Behaviour

```java
class Vehicle {
    void start() {
        System.out.println("Vehicle started");
    }
}

class Car extends Vehicle {
    @Override
    void start() {
        super.start();                          // Reuse parent logic
        System.out.println("Car engine warmed up");
        System.out.println("Ready to drive");
    }
}
```
