**Output upon calling `new Car().start()`:**

```
Vehicle started
Car engine warmed up
Ready to drive
```

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. JVM binds `start()` to `Car.start()` (overriding).
2. `super.start()` invokes parent `Vehicle.start()` — prints "Vehicle started".
3. Execution returns to `Car.start()` — prints the two remaining lines.

---

## 3.4 Why `static` Methods Cannot Be Overridden — Conceptual Proof

```java
class Parent {
    static void display() {
        System.out.println("Parent static");
    }
}

class Child extends Parent {
    static void display() {          // This HIDES, it does NOT override
        System.out.println("Child static");
    }
}
```

```java
Parent p = new Child();
p.display();        // Output: "Parent static"  ← NOT "Child static"!
```

**Mathematical/Logical Proof of binding:**

$$\text{Static methods} \Rightarrow \text{Binding decided by REFERENCE TYPE (compile-time)}$$

$$\text{Instance methods} \Rightarrow \text{Binding decided by OBJECT TYPE (runtime)}$$

Since `p` is declared as `Parent`, Java binds `display()` to `Parent.display()` at compile time. The `Child` version is essentially *invisible* through parent references. This phenomenon is called **Method Hiding**.

---

## 3.5 The `@Override` Annotation — A Safety Net

The `@Override` annotation is **not mandatory** for overriding to work, but it is **strongly recommended** by KTU board standards because:

- The compiler verifies that a method with the **same signature exists in the parent class**.
- If the parent method is **renamed or removed**, compilation fails immediately — protecting against silent bugs.
- It improves **code readability** for other developers.

```java
@Override
double calculateInterest() {   // Compiler will catch any mismatch
    return balance * 0.05;
}
```

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Class Hierarchy & Method Override Map

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Account {
        +double balance
        +calculateInterest() double
        +display() void
    }
    class SavingsAccount {
        +calculateInterest() double
    }
    class FixedDeposit {
        +calculateInterest() double
    }
    class CurrentAccount {
        +calculateInterest() double
    }
    Account <|-- SavingsAccount : extends
    Account <|-- FixedDeposit : extends
    Account <|-- CurrentAccount : extends
    note for SavingsAccount "Overrides calculateInterest() — 5 percent"
    note for FixedDeposit "Overrides calculateInterest() — 7 percent"
    note for CurrentAccount "Overrides calculateInterest() — 3 percent"
```

**Reading the diagram:** The `Account` superclass declares `calculateInterest()`. Each subclass **overrides** it with its own rate. The arrow (`<|--`) denotes inheritance (`extends`).

---

## 4.2 Dynamic Method Dispatch — Runtime Resolution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source code: ref.calculateInterest] --> B{Compile phase}
    B --> C{Does method exist in ref type?}
    C -- No --> D[Compilation ERROR]
    C -- Yes --> E[Code compiles successfully]
    E --> F{Runtime phase: actual object type}
    F --> G[Object is SavingsAccount?]
    F --> H[Object is FixedDeposit?]
    F --> I[Object is CurrentAccount?]
    F --> J[Object is Account itself?]
    G -- Yes --> K[Execute SavingsAccount.calculateInterest]
    H -- Yes --> L[Execute FixedDeposit.calculateInterest]
    I -- Yes --> M[Execute CurrentAccount.calculateInterest]
    J -- Yes --> N[Execute Account.calculateInterest]
    K --> O[Return interest value]
    L --> O
    M --> O
    N --> O
```

**Block-Level Interpretation:**
- The **left column** represents the **compile-time** checks (compile-time polymorphism / method resolution).
- The **right column** represents the **runtime** lookup of the most specific implementation (dynamic dispatch).
- The diamond blocks (`{}`) are **decision points** where the JVM branches based on the actual object type.

---

## 4.3 The `super.method()` Call Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Caller
    participant CarObj as Car object
    participant VehicleParent as Vehicle (parent)
    Caller->>CarObj: start()
    activate CarObj
    CarObj->>VehicleParent: super.start()
    activate VehicleParent
    VehicleParent-->>CarObj: prints Vehicle started
    deactivate VehicleParent
    CarObj-->>Caller: prints Car engine warmed up
    CarObj-->>Caller: prints Ready to drive
    deactivate CarObj
```

**Reading the sequence:** The caller invokes `start()` on a `Car` reference. The `Car` implementation delegates the first part to its parent `Vehicle` via `super.start()`, then completes its own additional logic.

---

## 4.4 Overriding vs Overloading — Visual Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Two methods with same name] --> Q1{Classes involved?}
    Q1 -- Same class --> OL[Method Overloading]
    Q1 -- Parent and Child --> Q2{Same parameter list?}
    Q2 -- No --> OL
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Binding time?}
    Q3 -- Compile time --> OL2[NOT possible static]
    Q3 -- Runtime --> OVR[Method Overriding]
    OL --> OLNote[Compile time polymorphism]
    OVR --> OVRNote[Runtime polymorphism]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define method overriding. List any four rules that must be followed while overriding a method in Java.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 mark):** Method overriding is the process by which a subclass provides a specific implementation of a method that is already defined in its superclass. The method in the subclass must have the same name, return type (or covariant return type), and parameter list as in the parent class.

**Any four rules (½ mark each = 2 marks):**

1. The method name and parameter list must be **identical** to the parent class method.
2. The return type must be the **same** or a **covariant subtype** of the parent's return type.
3. The access level **cannot be more restrictive** than the parent (e.g., cannot override a `public` method as `protected`).
4. **Static, final, and private** methods cannot be overridden. Constructors cannot be overridden.
5. The `@Override` annotation should be used to ensure compile-time correctness.

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is the difference between method overloading and method overriding? Mention the type of polymorphism each implements.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Method Overloading | Method Overriding |
|---|---|---|
| Class relationship | Within the **same class** | Between **parent and child class** |
| Parameter list | **Must differ** | **Must be the same** |
| Polymorphism type | **Compile-time** (static binding) | **Runtime** (dynamic binding) |
| Return type | Can differ freely | Must be same or covariant |
| Access modifier | No constraint | Cannot be more restrictive in child |

> **Type of Polymorphism (1 mark):** Overloading → *Compile-time polymorphism*; Overriding → *Runtime polymorphism* (achieved via dynamic method dispatch).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Module-2 questions on Overriding usually offer an **internal choice**. The two alternative questions below model the actual exam structure — **each choice is a full 14-mark question with two 7-mark sub-parts**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Question]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the concept of **method overriding** with a suitable Java program. Demonstrate how **runtime polymorphism** is achieved using a parent class reference to call an overridden method.

**(b) [7 marks]** Discuss the rules of method overriding in detail. What are **covariant return types**? Provide a Java code example that uses the `super` keyword to invoke the overridden method of the parent class.

---

#### **Model Solution — Q A (a) [7 marks]**

**Concept Explanation (3 marks):**

Method overriding allows a subclass to redefine a method inherited from its superclass. The version of the method that executes is determined at **runtime** based on the actual type of the object, not the type of the reference variable. This is called **runtime polymorphism** (or dynamic method dispatch).

**Java Program (3 marks):**

```java
class Shape {
    void draw() {
        System.out.println("Drawing a generic shape");
    }
}

class Circle extends Shape {
    @Override
    void draw() {
        System.out.println("Drawing a circle");
    }
}

class Square extends Shape {
    @Override
    void draw() {
        System.out.println("Drawing a square");
    }
}

public class TestPolymorphism {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Shape s;                       // Parent reference

        s = new Circle();
        s.draw();                      // Calls Circle's draw()

        s = new Square();
        s.draw();                      // Calls Square's draw()
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Drawing a circle
Drawing a square
```

**Explanation (1 mark):** Even though `s` is declared as `Shape`, the JVM binds the call to the actual object's overridden method — proving that the decision happens at **runtime**, not compile time.

---

#### **Model Solution — Q A (b) [7 marks]**

**Rules of Method Overriding (4 marks):**

1. Method name and parameter list must match exactly.
2. Return type must be the same or a covariant subtype.
3. Access modifier cannot be more restrictive (e.g., `public` → `private` is illegal).
4. Cannot override `static`, `final`, or `private` methods.
5. Constructors cannot be overridden.
6. The `@Override` annotation is recommended for safety.

**Covariant Return Types (1 mark):**

A covariant return type allows the overriding method to return a **subclass** of the return type declared in the parent method — provided the returned object is still a valid instance of the parent's return type.

**Java Code with `super` keyword (2 marks):**

```java
class Employee {
    String name = "John";
    void work() {
        System.out.println(name + " is working generically");
    }
}

class Developer extends Employee {
    @Override
    void work() {
        super.work();                     // Invokes parent version
        System.out.println(name + " is writing Java code");
    }
}

public class TestSuper {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new Developer().work();
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
John is working generically
John is writing Java code
```

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Question]`

**(a) [7 marks]** With a suitable example, explain **dynamic method dispatch** in Java. How is it different from **method hiding** in the case of static methods?

**(b) [7 marks]** Write a Java program to create a class `Vehicle` with a method `fuelType()`. Create subclasses `Car` and `Bike` that override this method. Show how a single array of `Vehicle` references can be used to invoke the appropriate overridden method at runtime.

---

#### **Model Solution — Q B (a) [7 marks]**

**Definition (2 marks):** Dynamic Method Dispatch is the mechanism by which a call to an overridden method is resolved at runtime, based on the actual type of the object being referred to (not the reference variable's declared type).

**Example (3 marks):**

```java
class Animal {
    void sound() { System.out.println("Animal sound"); }
}
class Dog extends Animal {
    @Override
    void sound() { System.out.println("Bark"); }
}
class Cat extends Animal {
    @Override
    void sound() { System.out.println("Meow"); }
}

public class Dispatch {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Animal a = new Dog();
        a.sound();          // "Bark" — runtime dispatch
        a = new Cat();
        a.sound();          // "Meow" — runtime dispatch
    }
}
```

**Difference from Method Hiding (2 marks):**

| Dynamic Method Dispatch | Method Hiding |
|---|---|
| Applies to **instance** methods | Applies to **static** methods |
| Binding is at **runtime** | Binding is at **compile-time** |
| Depends on **object type** | Depends on **reference type** |
| Achieves runtime polymorphism | Does not achieve runtime polymorphism |

---

#### **Model Solution — Q B (b) [7 marks]**

```java
class Vehicle {
    void fuelType() {
        System.out.println("Generic fuel");
    }
}

class Car extends Vehicle {
    @Override
    void fuelType() {
        System.out.println("Car uses petrol/diesel");
    }
}

class Bike extends Vehicle {
    @Override
    void fuelType() {
        System.out.println("Bike uses petrol");
    }
}

public class TestVehicle {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Vehicle[] vehicles = new Vehicle[3];
        vehicles[0] = new Vehicle();
        vehicles[1] = new Car();
        vehicles[2] = new Bike();

        for (Vehicle v : vehicles) {
            v.fuelType();   // Polymorphic call
        }
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Generic fuel
Car uses petrol/diesel
Bike uses petrol
```

**Explanation (in code comments — 1 mark for stating the principle):** A single `Vehicle[]` array holds references of parent type. Iterating with a `for-each` loop invokes the correct overridden method at runtime — a textbook example of polymorphism via method overriding.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (read carefully):**
>
> 1. **Writing "overriding can change parameter list"** — this is wrong; it becomes *overloading* if the parameter list changes. KTU examiners deduct **1 full mark** for this confusion.
> 2. **Forgetting the `@Override` annotation** — not a compilation error, but examiners in Module-2 programming questions often **deduct 0.5 mark** for poor practice.
> 3. **Confusing method hiding with overriding** — writing `static` methods and calling them overriding is a **3-mark killer**. Be precise.
> 4. **Omitting the runtime polymorphism demonstration** — in 14-mark questions, examiners expect a *trace* or *output*. Writing only the code without showing which version executes when loses 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Writing covariant return types as "any return type"** — covariance has a strict IS-A relationship. Mark loss: 1 mark.
> 6. **Not invoking `super.methodName()` correctly** — the syntax is `super.methodName()`, not `super().methodName()`. The latter is a constructor call, completely different context.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Pin this to your wall before the ESE.**

- ✅ Method overriding = **same signature, different body**, across parent–child classes.
- ✅ Enables **runtime polymorphism** (dynamic method dispatch).
- ✅ Method name + parameter list = **must be identical**.
- ✅ Return type = **same OR covariant subtype**.
- ✅ Access modifier = **cannot be more restrictive** in the child.
- ✅ **Cannot override**: `static`, `final`, `private` methods, and constructors.
- ✅ Use `@Override` annotation for compile-time safety.
- ✅ Use `super.methodName()` to reuse the parent implementation.
- ✅ **Overloading ≠ Overriding** — overloading is compile-time; overriding is runtime.
- ✅ Static method in subclass with same signature as parent = **method hiding**, NOT overriding.
- ✅ Real-world relevance: GUI frameworks, design patterns (Template Method), plugin systems, Spring lifecycle methods.
- ✅ A parent reference variable holding a child object is the **standard polymorphic setup** — the JVM resolves the overridden call at **runtime**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Packages - Defining Package
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT414 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Packages - Defining Package

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📦 Packages in Object Oriented Programming — Defining a Package

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Standard):**
> A **Package** in Java (and analogous OOP languages) is a **namespace mechanism** that organizes a set of related *classes*, *interfaces*, *enumerations*, and *sub-packages* into a single logical unit. It functions as both a **container** for type grouping and an **access control boundary** that enforces encapsulation at the directory level.

In the KTU 2024 OOP syllabus (PEECT414, Module 3), the topic "Packages — Defining Package" specifically focuses on the **declaration syntax**, **naming conventions**, **directory mapping rules**, and the **two principal categories** of packages:

1. **Built-in (Predefined / API) Packages** — Provided by the JDK (e.g., `java.lang`, `java.util`, `java.io`).
2. **User-defined (Custom) Packages** — Created by the programmer to group their own classes.

The act of *defining* a package is accomplished through the **`package` keyword**, which must appear as the **first executable statement** of a source file.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Office Filing Cabinet**
> Imagine a large corporate office with thousands of files. If every document were thrown into one giant room, finding anything would be impossible. The office solves this by installing **filing cabinets**, each labeled with a category:
> - Cabinet **"Finance"** contains invoices, balance sheets.
> - Cabinet **"HR"** contains resumes, payslips.
> - Cabinet **"Engineering"** contains blueprints, code modules.
>
> A **Java Package works exactly like this filing cabinet**:
> - The **cabinet label** = the package name (e.g., `com.company.finance`).
> - The **folders inside** = the classes and interfaces.
> - The **physical cabinet location in the building** = the directory path on disk.
> - The **rule that you must go through the labeled cabinet** = the `import` statement.

When you *define* a package, you are essentially **labeling a folder** so the compiler and JVM can locate, organize, and protect your classes.

---

## 1.3 Why Packages Matter — KTU High-Yield Highlights

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Five Core Benefits of Defining Packages (Frequently asked in KTU 2-mark and 3-mark questions):**
> 1. **Namespace Management** — Prevents class-name collisions (two classes can share the same name if they live in different packages).
> 2. **Logical Organization** — Groups related types together (e.g., all `List`, `Map`, `Set` interfaces live in `java.util`).
> 3. **Access Protection** — Adds a fourth access level (`default`/package-private) that restricts visibility to same-package classes only.
> 4. **Reusability** — Packages can be distributed as JAR files and reused across projects.
> 5. **Encapsulation at Scale** — Hides internal implementation classes behind package boundaries.

---

## 1.4 Standard Package Metrics & Conventions

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU-Relevant Naming Standards (from Java Language Specification §6.1):**
> - Package names are written in **all lowercase** to avoid conflict with class names.
> - Companies use **reverse domain name notation** (e.g., `in.ac.ktu.oop` for KTU's OOP department).
> - The keyword **`package`** is **reserved**, **lowercase**, and must be the first statement.
> - **Only one `package` statement** is allowed per source file.
> - A source file without a `package` statement belongs to the **default (unnamed) package**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🧠 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Package Definition Statement

A package is defined in a Java source file using the following grammar:

```java
package <fully.qualified.package.name>;
```

### Structural Components:

| Component | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **`package`** keyword | Reserved Java keyword | `package` |
| **Dotted Identifier** | Period-separated hierarchical name | `com.ktu.oop.graphics` |
| **Semicolon** | Mandatory statement terminator | `;` |
| **Position** | Must be the **first non-comment, non-whitespace** line | Line 1 (after `//` or `/* */` comments) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Rule:** If the `package` statement is placed **after** `import` or `class` declarations, the code will produce a **compile-time error: *Class or interface declaration expected***. This is one of the most common mistakes penalized in KTU valuations.

---

## 2.2 Directory-to-Package Mapping Rule (The Golden Rule)

> [!NOTE]
> **The Correspondence Law (Most Critical for KTU 14-mark Questions):**
> The **fully qualified package name** must **exactly mirror the directory structure** (folder hierarchy) on the file system, where each dot (`.`) in the package name represents **one level of subdirectory**.

### Example Mapping:

| Package Declaration | Required Directory Path |
|---|---|
| `package mypack;` | `project_root/mypack/` |
| `package com.ktu.oop;` | `project_root/com/ktu/oop/` |
| `package a.b.c;` | `project_root/a/b/c/` |

If the directory structure does **not** match the package name, the compiler will throw:
```
error: cannot find symbol
  class MyClass
  location: package com.ktu.oop
```

---

## 2.3 CLASSPATH — The Search Compass

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition — CLASSPATH:**
> The **CLASSPATH** is an **environment variable** (or `-cp` JVM argument) that tells the Java compiler (`javac`) and the Java Virtual Machine (`java`) **where to look for root package directories** (i.e., the parent directory that contains the top-level package folder).

### How CLASSPATH Resolves a Package:

1. Suppose CLASSPATH = `C:\projects\myapp\` (or `/home/user/myapp/` on Linux).
2. You compile a class declared as `package com.ktu.utils;`.
3. The compiler expects the file at:
   - Windows: `C:\projects\myapp\com\ktu\utils\MyClass.class`
   - Linux: `/home/user/myapp/com/ktu/utils/MyClass.class`

### CLASSPATH Setting Syntax:

| OS | Command |
|---|---|
| Windows (CMD) | `set CLASSPATH=.;C:\myapp` |
| Windows (PowerShell) | `$env:CLASSPATH=".;C:\myapp"` |
| Linux/macOS | `export CLASSPATH=.:/home/user/myapp` |
| Compile-time override | `javac -cp . MyClass.java` |
| Run-time override | `java -cp . com.ktu.utils.MyClass` |

> [!WARNING]
> **The dot (`.`) in CLASSPATH is NOT a typo** — it represents the **current working directory**. Forgetting it is the #1 reason KTU students get *"class not found"* errors during lab viva.

---

## 2.4 Two Types of Packages — Comparative Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Question Pattern:** *"Differentiate between built-in and user-defined packages."* (Typically a 7-mark sub-question.)

| Parameter | Built-in (API) Packages | User-defined Packages |
|---|---|---|
| **Source** | Provided by JDK / JRE | Created by the programmer |
| **Examples** | `java.lang`, `java.util`, `java.io`, `java.sql` | `mypack`, `com.ktu.oop.graphics` |
| **Import required?** | `java.lang` is auto-imported; others need explicit `import` | Always requires `import` or fully qualified name |
| **Location** | Inside the JDK's `rt.jar` (or `modules` file in Java 9+) | User's project directory or JAR file |
| **Modification** | Cannot be modified (read-only) | Fully editable |
| **Primary purpose** | Provide standard functionality (collections, I/O, networking) | Group application-specific classes |

---

## 2.5 Java's Complete Access Control Spectrum (Packages Context)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked:** *"How do packages extend access control?"*

| Access Modifier | Same Class | Same Package (Subclass) | Same Package (Non-Subclass) | Different Package (Subclass) | Different Package (Non-Subclass) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `private` | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |
| `default` (package-private) | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ |
| `protected` | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ |
| `public` | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |

> The `default` (no modifier) access level **exists ONLY because of packages**. Without packages, Java would have only 3 access levels.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet — Defining a Package

| Element | Syntax / Rule | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Package declaration | `package name1.name2.name3;` | First statement of file |
| Sub-package separator | `.` (dot) | Maps to subdirectory `/` |
| Maximum packages per file | **1** | Cannot declare multiple packages in one file |
| Comments before `package` | Allowed | `//` or `/* */` is fine |
| Import before `package` | **Not allowed** | Compile error |
| Class declaration before `package` | **Not allowed** | Compile error |
| Valid identifier rules | Cannot start with digit, no reserved words | Same as variable naming |
| Naming convention | All lowercase, reverse domain | e.g., `org.apache.commons` |
| File extension required | `.java` | Must match public class name (if any) |
| CLASSPATH root | Points to **parent** of top-level package | Use `.` for current directory |

---

## 2.7 Engineering Utility — Where Packages Are Used in Industry

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Deployment Context:**
> - **Maven / Gradle Projects** — Every Java enterprise project organizes its code into packages like `com.company.project.controller`, `com.company.project.service`, `com.company.project.repository`.
> - **Android Development** — Package names are tied to the app's unique application ID on the Google Play Store.
> - **Microservices** — Each service's API is exported as a packaged JAR with a unique namespace.
> - **Open-Source Libraries** — Apache Commons, Spring Framework, and Google Guava all use the reverse-domain package convention.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🔬 Step-by-Step Procedural Analysis — How to Define a Package

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — The Simplest Custom Package

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Lab Question Style:** *"Create a class `Hello` inside a package named `mypack` and demonstrate how to use it."*

### Step 1 — Plan the Directory Structure

We want the package `mypack`. The directory structure must be:

```
project_root/
│
└── mypack/
    └── Hello.java
```

### Step 2 — Write the Class File with Package Declaration

**File: `project_root/mypack/Hello.java`**

```java
// Step (a): The very FIRST line defines the package
package mypack;

// Step (b): A public class so other packages can access it
public class Hello {
    
    // Step (c): A public method callable from outside
    public void display() {
        System.out.println("Hello from mypack!");
    }
}
```

**Explanation of each line:**

| Line | Purpose | Why it matters |
|---|---|---|
| `package mypack;` | Tells the compiler this class belongs to namespace `mypack` | Without this line, the class is in the *default package* |
| `public class Hello` | Exposes the class to other packages | If omitted, the class is package-private (default access) |
| `public void display()` | Exposes the method | Required because methods default to package-private too |

### Step 3 — Compile the Class

Open a terminal inside `project_root` and run:

```
javac mypack/Hello.java
```

This produces `Hello.class` inside the `mypack` directory.

### Step 4 — Use the Class from Another Package

**File: `project_root/TestHello.java`**

```java
// Step (a): Import the packaged class
import mypack.Hello;

// Step (b): The TestHello class is in the DEFAULT (unnamed) package
public class TestHello {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Step (c): Instantiate and call
        Hello h = new Hello();
        h.display();
    }
}
```

### Step 5 — Compile and Run

```
javac TestHello.java
java TestHello
```

### Step 6 — Expected Output

```
Hello from mypack!
```

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Multi-Level Hierarchical Package

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Pattern (Common 14-mark sub-question):** *"Explain how sub-packages are created and accessed."*

### Step 1 — Define the Sub-Package Class

**File: `project_root/com/ktu/graphics/Shape.java`**

```java
package com.ktu.graphics;   // First statement

public class Shape {
    public void draw() {
        System.out.println("Drawing a generic shape.");
    }
}
```

**Directory tree:**

```
project_root/
└── com/
    └── ktu/
        └── graphics/
            └── Shape.java
```

### Step 2 — Use the Sub-Package Class

**File: `project_root/MainApp.java`**

```java
// Option 1: Import a specific class
import com.ktu.graphics.Shape;

public class MainApp {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Shape s = new Shape();
        s.draw();
        
        // Option 2: Use fully qualified name WITHOUT import
        com.ktu.graphics.Shape s2 = new com.ktu.graphics.Shape();
        s2.draw();
    }
}
```

### Step 3 — Compilation Logic

```
javac com/ktu/graphics/Shape.java
javac -cp . MainApp.java
java -cp . MainApp
```

### Expected Output

```
Drawing a generic shape.
Drawing a generic shape.
```

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Access Control Across Package Boundaries

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Favorite Test:** *"Can a subclass in a different package access a `default` (package-private) member of its parent class?"*

| Scenario | Superclass Member | Subclass Location | Accessible? | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `public void m1()` | Different package | ✅ Yes | `public` is universal |
| 2 | `protected void m2()` | Different package (subclass) | ✅ Yes | `protected` allows inheritance access |
| 3 | `protected void m2()` | Different package (non-subclass) | ❌ No | `protected` requires inheritance |
| 4 | `void m3()` *(default)* | Different package | ❌ No | `default` is package-only |
| 5 | `private void m4()` | Different package | ❌ No | `private` is class-only |

---

## 3.4 Procedural Rules for `package` Statement — Decision Flow

Below is the **logical decision tree** every KTU student must memorize:

```
IF source file has a class/interface declaration:
│
├── Is the FIRST non-comment line a `package` statement?
│   │
│   ├── YES → Class belongs to the named package
│   │         └── Directory MUST match package name exactly
│   │
│   └── NO  → Class belongs to the DEFAULT (unnamed) package
│             └── Located in the current working directory
│
└── Are there MULTIPLE `package` statements in one file?
    │
    └── YES → COMPILE-TIME ERROR
              "class, interface, or enum expected"
```

---

## 3.5 Numerical Example — CLASSPATH Resolution (Analytical Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Numerical-Reasoning Variant:** *"Given the CLASSPATH and package declaration, find the absolute file path."*

**Given:**
- CLASSPATH = `D:\JavaProjects\Lab3\`
- Package declaration: `package edu.ktu.cs.oop.lab3;`
- Class name: `Student.java`

**Find:** The full path the compiler will search for.

### Step-by-Step Resolution:

| Step | Logic | Result |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Start with CLASSPATH root | `D:\JavaProjects\Lab3\` |
| 2 | Append sub-folder for `edu` | `D:\JavaProjects\Lab3\edu\` |
| 3 | Append sub-folder for `ktu` | `D:\JavaProjects\Lab3\edu\ktu\` |
| 4 | Append sub-folder for `cs` | `D:\JavaProjects\Lab3\edu\ktu\cs\` |
| 5 | Append sub-folder for `oop` | `D:\JavaProjects\Lab3\edu\ktu\cs\oop\` |
| 6 | Append sub-folder for `lab3` | `D:\JavaProjects\Lab3\edu\ktu\cs\oop\lab3\` |
| 7 | Append the class file | `D:\JavaProjects\Lab3\edu\ktu\cs\oop\lab3\Student.class` |

**Final Resolved Path:**

$$
\text{FullPath} \;=\; \text{CLASSPATH} \;+\; \bigcup_{i=1}^{n}\left(\text{packageLevel}_{i} \;+\; \text{``/''}\right) \;+\; \text{ClassName} \;+\; \text{``.class''}
$$

Where $n$ is the number of dots in the package name.

---

## 3.6 Common Compilation Errors — The "Valuation Penalty" Table

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Note:** The following errors each carry a **1 to 2 mark deduction** in KTU evaluations.

| # | Mistake | Resulting Error | KTU Marks Lost |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `package` statement *after* `import` | *"class, interface, or enum expected"* | 1 |
| 2 | Two `package` statements in one file | *"class, interface, or enum expected"* | 1 |
| 3 | Folder name ≠ package name | *"cannot find symbol"* | 2 |
| 4 | Forgetting `.` in CLASSPATH | *"class not found"* (runtime) | 1 |
| 5 | Using `Package` (capital P) | *"cannot find symbol — Package"* | 1 |
| 6 | Class declared `public` but file name differs | *"class Hello is public, should be declared in a file named Hello.java"* | 2 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
