# 🎯 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

---

## 📘 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`
**Q: Define a package in Java. Mention any two benefits of using packages.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 Mark):**
> A package in Java is a namespace that organizes a set of related classes, interfaces, and sub-packages into a single logical unit. It is declared using the `package` keyword as the first statement of a source file.

> **Benefits (2 Marks — Any Two):**
> 1. **Namespace Management** — Prevents naming conflicts by allowing classes with the same name to coexist in different packages.
> 2. **Access Protection** — Provides a default (package-private) access level that restricts class/method visibility to the same package.
> 3. **Logical Grouping** — Helps organize related types for easier maintenance and discovery.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`
**Q: What is the significance of the directory structure in Java packages? What happens if the folder name does not match the package name?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Significance (2 Marks):**
> In Java, the package name is directly mapped to a directory (folder) structure on the file system. Each dot (`.`) in the package name corresponds to one level of subdirectory. For example, the package `com.ktu.oop` must reside in the folder path `com/ktu/oop/`. This convention allows the compiler and JVM to locate classes via the CLASSPATH.

> **Mismatch Consequence (1 Mark):**
> If the folder name does not match the package name, the compiler throws the error *"cannot find symbol"*, and the program will not compile.

---

---

## 📕 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Each Part-B question has a **Module-level internal choice**. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### ✏️ **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]`

**Q: Define a package in Java. Explain the syntax for creating a user-defined package with a suitable example. Also, discuss the role of CLASSPATH and `import` statements with reference to your example.**

#### **Part (a) — Package Definition & User-Defined Package Example (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Definition (2 Marks):**
A package is a grouping of related classes, interfaces, and sub-packages into a single namespace. It is declared using the `package` keyword as the first executable statement in a source file.

**Step 2 — Example Code (5 Marks):**

**File: `mypack/Calculator.java`**
```java
package mypack;   // First statement — defines the package

public class Calculator {
    public int add(int a, int b) {
        return a + b;
    }
    public int multiply(int a, int b) {
        return a * b;
    }
}
```

**Directory Structure (1 Mark):**
```
project_root/
└── mypack/
    └── Calculator.java
```

**Compilation Command (1 Mark):**
```
javac mypack/Calculator.java
```

#### **Part (b) — CLASSPATH & `import` Explanation (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — CLASSPATH Explanation (3 Marks):**
The CLASSPATH is an environment variable that tells the JVM where to search for root package directories. To use the `mypack.Calculator` class from another program, CLASSPATH must include the parent directory of `mypack`. For example, if the parent is `C:\projects\`, then CLASSPATH = `.;C:\projects\`.

**Step 2 — `import` Statement Explanation (2 Marks):**
The `import` statement allows a class in one package to use a class from another package without typing the fully qualified name every time. It must appear **after** the `package` statement and **before** any class declaration.

**Step 3 — Using the Package (2 Marks):**
```java
import mypack.Calculator;   // Import the specific class

public class TestCalc {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Calculator c = new Calculator();
        System.out.println("Sum = " + c.add(10, 20));
        System.out.println("Product = " + c.multiply(5, 4));
    }
}
```

**Step 4 — Output:**
```
Sum = 30
Product = 20
```

---

### ✏️ **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]`

**Q: Explain the different types of packages in Java with examples. Write a Java program to create a sub-package `com.ktu.graphics` containing a class `Shape` with a method `draw()`, and demonstrate how to invoke this method from a class outside the package.**

#### **Part (a) — Types of Packages (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Two Categories (1 Mark):**
Java supports two major types of packages: **Built-in (predefined) packages** and **User-defined packages**.

**Step 2 — Built-in Packages Table (3 Marks):**

| Package | Purpose | Key Classes |
|---|---|---|
| `java.lang` | Core language features (auto-imported) | `String`, `System`, `Math`, `Object` |
| `java.util` | Utility classes & collections | `ArrayList`, `HashMap`, `Scanner` |
| `java.io` | Input/Output operations | `File`, `InputStream`, `Reader` |
| `java.sql` | Database access (JDBC) | `Connection`, `Statement`, `ResultSet` |
| `java.net` | Networking | `Socket`, `URL`, `InetAddress` |
| `java.awt` / `javax.swing` | GUI programming | `Frame`, `Button`, `JFrame` |

**Step 3 — User-Defined Packages (3 Marks):**
Programmers can create their own packages using the `package` keyword. Examples include:
- `mypack` (single-level)
- `com.ktu.oop` (multi-level, reverse-domain style)
- `edu.kerala.university` (academic convention)

User-defined packages help organize project-specific classes and prevent naming collisions with the standard library.

---

#### **Part (b) — Sub-Package Program (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Create the Sub-Package Class (3 Marks):**

**Directory Tree:**
```
project_root/
└── com/
    └── ktu/
        └── graphics/
            └── Shape.java
```

**File: `com/ktu/graphics/Shape.java`**
```java
package com.ktu.graphics;   // Defines sub-package

public class Shape {
    public void draw() {
        System.out.println("Drawing a generic Shape object.");
    }
}
```

**Step 2 — Create the Invoking Class (3 Marks):**

**File: `project_root/MainApp.java`**
```java
// Import the specific class from the sub-package
import com.ktu.graphics.Shape;

public class MainApp {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Method 1: Using the imported class
        Shape myShape = new Shape();
        myShape.draw();

        // Method 2: Using fully qualified name (no import needed)
        com.ktu.graphics.Shape s2 = new com.ktu.graphics.Shape();
        s2.draw();
    }
}
```

**Step 3 — Compilation & Execution (1 Mark):**
```
javac com/ktu/graphics/Shape.java
javac -cp . MainApp.java
java -cp . MainApp
```

**Expected Output:**
```
Drawing a generic Shape object.
Drawing a generic Shape object.
```

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Wrong Order of Statements** — Placing `import` *before* `package` is an immediate **compile-time error** and costs **2 marks** in valuation.
> 2. **Directory Mismatch** — Students often create a single folder but write a multi-level package name. Examiners check this strictly; expect **1 to 2 marks deduction**.
> 3. **Forgetting to set CLASSPATH** — In lab exams, students run `java MyClass` instead of `java com.ktu.oop.MyClass`. This produces *"ClassNotFoundException"* and is penalized.
> 4. **Capitalizing Package Name** — Writing `package Mypack;` instead of `package mypack;` violates Java naming conventions and may cause *"cannot find symbol"*.
> 5. **Confusing `protected` with `default`** — In 14-mark questions asking about cross-package access, students often wrongly claim `default` members are accessible via inheritance. They are **NOT** — only `protected` and `public` cross package boundaries.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — "Packages: Defining Package"**

- 🔹 A **package** is a **namespace** for grouping related types; declared with the `package` keyword.
- 🔹 The `package` statement **must be the first** non-comment line in the source file.
- 🔹 **Only one** `package` statement is permitted per source file.
- 🔹 Package names are **all lowercase**, with sub-packages separated by dots (`.`).
- 🔹 The **directory path must exactly match** the package name (one folder per dot).
- 🔹 **CLASSPATH** points to the **root** directory containing the top-level package folder; include `.` for the current directory.
- 🔹 **Two types of packages**: Built-in (e.g., `java.util`) and User-defined (e.g., `mypack`).
- 🔹 `java.lang` is the **only auto-imported** built-in package; all others need explicit `import`.
- 🔹 Packages introduce a **default (package-private) access level** that restricts visibility to the same package.
- 🔹 `protected` members are accessible to **subclasses in different packages**, but `default` members are **NOT**.
- 🔹 You can use a class without `import` by writing its **fully qualified name** (e.g., `java.util.Scanner`).
- 🔹 Compilation command for packaged class: `javac -d . MyClass.java` (the `-d` flag tells where to place generated `.class` files).
- 🔹 Running a packaged main class: `java -cp . com.ktu.oop.MainApp` (note the dot for classpath, then the fully qualified class name — **no `.class` extension**).
- 🔹 **Reverse-domain naming** (e.g., `in.ac.ktu`) is the industry standard for avoiding global naming conflicts.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CLASSPATH
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT414 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** CLASSPATH

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**CLASSPATH** is an environment variable used by the **Java Virtual Machine (JVM)** and the **Java Compiler (`javac`)** to locate user-defined classes, third-party libraries, and Java Archive (**JAR**) files during compilation and execution. It acts as a searchable index of root directories and archive file paths that the JVM consults to resolve symbolic class references into actual binary class files (`.class`).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Snapshot:** *CLASSPATH is the parameter that tells the JDK tools and the JVM where to look for user classes and third-party packages. It is distinct from the PATH variable, which locates executable programs (like `javac.exe` and `java.exe` themselves).*

The default value of CLASSPATH is the **current working directory (`.`)**, which is why beginners can compile and run programs in their project folder without explicit configuration. As soon as a program depends on external libraries (e.g., JDBC drivers, Apache Commons), CLASSPATH must be updated.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Library Card Catalogue

Imagine you are a researcher visiting a massive, multi-wing university library that contains **millions of books** scattered across hundreds of rooms.

- **Java Source Code (`.java`)** = Your research notes that *reference* book titles, e.g., "Look up *The Design Patterns* book."
- **Compiled Class (`.class`)** = The actual physical book on the shelf.
- **CLASSPATH** = The **library catalogue index card system** that the librarian consults to locate shelves. It tells the librarian: *"Start looking in Wing A, then Wing B, then in the basement archive boxes."*
- **JVM (Java Runtime)** = The **librarian** who actually fetches the book.
- **PATH** = The **GPS route to the library building itself** (i.e., how your OS finds the `java` and `javac` executables in the first place).

If the catalogue is empty or misconfigured, the librarian returns saying *"Book not found"*, producing the infamous Java error: `java.lang.NoClassDefFoundError` or `java.lang.ClassNotFoundException`.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory KTU Glossary Terms**
>
> - **`.class` file** — The bytecode generated after compiling a `.java` file using `javac`.
> - **JAR (Java ARchive)** — A ZIP-format compressed file bundling multiple `.class` files, used as a single distributable unit.
> - **Package** — A namespace that organizes a set of related classes/interfaces (e.g., `java.util`).
> - **Bootstrap Classpath** — Built-in JDK classes (`rt.jar`, `java.lang`, etc.) loaded automatically before user CLASSPATH.
> - **Extension Classpath** — Used for the `jre/lib/ext` directory (largely deprecated in Java 9+).
> - **User Classpath** — The explicit CLASSPATH variable set by the developer.
> - **Wildcard `*`** — A shortcut that includes *all* `.jar` files in a directory (does **not** recurse into subdirectories).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Why CLASSPATH Exists — The "Why" Behind the Variable

Java was designed with the philosophy of **network mobility and dynamic class loading**. Unlike C/C++, where a compiler statically links to library binaries, Java loads classes *lazily* at runtime via the **ClassLoader subsystem**. This subsystem has three hierarchical loaders:

1. **Bootstrap ClassLoader** — Loads core JDK classes from `<JAVA_HOME>/jre/lib/rt.jar`. Has highest priority; no user intervention.
2. **Extension / Platform ClassLoader** — Loads classes from extension directories (legacy concept).
3. **Application/System ClassLoader** — Loads classes from the **CLASSPATH** specified by the user. This is the layer the CLASSPATH variable directly controls.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Delegation Model (Parent-First):** When the JVM needs a class, the ClassLoader first asks its parent. Only if the parent cannot find the class does the child loader consult the CLASSPATH. This is why you **cannot override** core `java.lang.*` classes with custom ones in your CLASSPATH — a deliberate security feature.

## 2.2 Setting CLASSPATH — Platform-Specific Syntax

The syntax for setting the CLASSPATH environment variable differs across operating systems because the **path separator** is OS-specific.

### Syntax Reference

| Operation | Windows (CMD) | Linux / macOS (Bash) |
|---|---|---|
| **Set temporary CLASSPATH** | `set CLASSPATH=C:\myclasses;.;D:\lib\mysql.jar` | `export CLASSPATH=/home/user/myclasses:.:/opt/lib/mysql.jar` |
| **Append to existing** | `set CLASSPATH=%CLASSPATH%;C:\new` | `export CLASSPATH=$CLASSPATH:/opt/new` |
| **Clear CLASSPATH** | `set CLASSPATH=` | `unset CLASSPATH` |
| **Separator character** | Semicolon **`;`** | Colon **`:`** |
| **Current directory token** | `.` | `.` |
| **Wildcard for all JARs** | `C:\libs\*` | `/opt/libs/*` |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall #1 — Path Separator Mix-up:** Students frequently use `;` on Linux or `:` on Windows. The `java` tool will silently treat the entire wrong separator as part of a single path, leading to the dreaded `Error: Could not find or load main class`.

## 2.3 Setting CLASSPATH Inline (Per-Command)

Most modern Java development avoids a global environment variable in favor of the inline `-cp` (or legacy `-classpath`) flag. This is **portable, project-scoped, and reproducible**.

### Command Cheat Sheet

| Command | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `javac -cp <path> MyClass.java` | Compiles `MyClass.java` using classes from `<path>`. |
| `java -cp <path> MyClass` | Runs `MyClass` using classes from `<path>`. |
| `java -cp .;lib/* com.app.Main` | Runs `com.app.Main`; class is in current dir, dependencies in all JARs under `lib/`. |
| `java -jar app.jar` | Runs an executable JAR; **CLASSPATH is ignored**, the JAR's `MANIFEST.MF` `Class-Path` entry is used. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Golden Rule for KTU:** If you use `-cp` inline, the `CLASSPATH` environment variable is **completely ignored** for that invocation. Inline always wins.

## 2.4 CLASSPATH vs. PATH — The Classic Confusion

| Aspect | PATH | CLASSPATH |
|---|---|---|
| **What it locates** | Executable programs (`.exe`, `.bat`, shell scripts) | Class files (`.class`) and JAR files (`.jar`) |
| **Used by** | Operating System shell | JVM and `javac` |
| **Example entry** | `C:\Program Files\Java\jdk-17\bin` | `C:\myproject\bin;C:\libs\*` |
| **Default value** | OS-defined, often empty for new users | Current directory `.` |
| **Set when** | Installing JDK (so `javac` can be invoked anywhere) | When a project uses external libraries |
| **Separator (Windows)** | `;` | `;` |
| **Separator (Linux)** | `:` | `:` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic for the Exam:** *PATH finds the **tool** (the Java compiler itself). CLASSPATH finds the **materials** the tool works on (the class files).*

## 2.5 The Wildcard `*` — Advanced Inclusion Pattern

The wildcard `*` in a CLASSPATH entry (e.g., `lib/*`) expands to **all `.jar` files in that immediate directory**. Three hard rules govern it:

1. The `*` must be the **last character** of the path entry.
2. It does **not match `.class` files** in plain form — it matches JARs only.
3. It does **not recurse** into subdirectories. You cannot use `lib/**/*` to recurse.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Build Tools (Maven/Gradle):** Manage CLASSPATH dynamically using POM/build files.
- **JDBC Drivers:** Specifying `mysql-connector-java.jar` in CLASSPATH enables database connectivity.
- **Enterprise Servers (Tomcat, WildFly):** Maintain their own per-application CLASSPATH isolated from the system variable.
- **Modular Java (Java 9+):** The `module-path` is gradually replacing CLASSPATH for application-level dependencies.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthroughs

Because CLASSPATH is a **configuration / system-design concept** rather than a formulaic derivation, this section provides **worked analytical scenarios** that KTU frequently tests through "trace and explain" problems.

## 3.1 Scenario A — Resolving a Class Reference Step-by-Step

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
A developer has the following directory layout:

```
C:\Project\
├── src\
│   └── com\ktu\app\Main.java
├── bin\
│   └── com\ktu\app\Main.class
└── lib\
    ├── mysql.jar
    └── gson.jar
```

The `Main.java` file contains:

```java
package com.ktu.app;
import com.mysql.cj.jdbc.Driver;   // From mysql.jar
public class Main { /* ... */ }
```

The developer runs:

```
set CLASSPATH=C:\Project\bin
java com.ktu.app.Main
```

### Step-by-Step JVM Resolution Logic

**Step 1 — Bootstrap ClassLoader search.**
The JVM first looks for `Main` in the bootstrap path. It does not exist there (it's a user class).

**Step 2 — Application ClassLoader consults CLASSPATH = `C:\Project\bin`.**
The loader scans the entry. The root of CLASSPATH is `C:\Project\bin`.

**Step 3 — Convert fully qualified name to relative path.**
The JVM maps the package `com.ktu.app` to the relative path `com/ktu/app/`.

**Step 4 — Concatenate root + relative + class name.**
The loader attempts to read:

$$
\text{Full path} = \text{CLASSPATH root} \;\Vert\; \text{package path} \;\Vert\; \text{class name}.class
$$

$$
\text{Full path} = \texttt{C:\textbackslash Project\textbackslash bin} \; \Vert \; \texttt{com\textbackslash ktu\textbackslash app} \; \Vert \; \texttt{Main.class}
$$

$$
\text{Full path} = \texttt{C:\textbackslash Project\textbackslash bin\textbackslash com\textbackslash ktu\textbackslash app\textbackslash Main.class}
$$

The file exists. `Main` is loaded.

**Step 5 — Lazy loading of `com.mysql.cj.jdbc.Driver`.**
When `Main` references `Driver`, the loader is invoked again. It does **not** find `mysql.jar` on the CLASSPATH. Hence:

$$
\text{Result} = \texttt{java.lang.ClassNotFoundException: com.mysql.cj.jdbc.Driver}
$$

**Step 6 — Corrective CLASSPATH.**

```
set CLASSPATH=C:\Project\bin;lib\mysql.jar
```

Now the loader appends `lib\mysql.jar` to the search list and successfully extracts the `Driver` class from the archive.

## 3.2 Scenario B — Precedence Resolution Among Multiple Matches

**Problem:** Two files exist:

- `C:\A\com\foo\Util.class`
- `C:\B\com\foo\Util.class`

CLASSPATH is set to `C:\A;C:\B`. The program imports `com.foo.Util`.

**Step-by-Step Resolution:**

**Step 1:** Loader scans `C:\A` first. The relative path resolves to `C:\A\com\foo\Util.class`. File **exists** → load it.

**Step 2:** Search terminates immediately. The class in `C:\B` is **shadowed / ignored**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **First-Wins Rule:** When multiple CLASSPATH entries contain the same fully qualified class name, the entry that appears **earlier** in the CLASSPATH list takes precedence. The JVM does not warn about duplicates. This silent shadowing is a common source of production bugs.

## 3.3 Scenario C — Wildcard Expansion Mechanics

**Setup:** Directory `C:\libs` contains `a.jar`, `b.jar`, `c.txt`.

CLASSPATH entry: `C:\libs\*`

**Expansion logic:**

$$
\texttt{C:\textbackslash libs\textbackslash *} \;\longrightarrow\; \texttt{a.jar} \;\Vert\; \texttt{b.jar}
$$

The `c.txt` file is **excluded** because the wildcard matches only files with the `.jar` extension. The order of expansion is alphabetical (deterministic in modern JDKs but not guaranteed across JVM implementations).

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — How to Choose the Right Approach

| Situation | Recommended CLASSPATH Strategy | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Small student project, no external libs | Default (`.`) or no CLASSPATH | Simplest; relies on current directory |
| Project using a few JARs | Inline `-cp` at compile/run time | Self-documenting, no global pollution |
| Project with many JARs in a folder | Inline `-cp "lib/*"` | Wildcard keeps command short |
| Executable JAR distribution | Embed in `MANIFEST.MF` | Self-contained; no user setup needed |
| Enterprise build (Maven/Gradle) | Tool-managed classpath | Reproducible, version-controlled |
| Java 9+ modular app | `module-path` + `module-info.java` | Replaces CLASSPATH for app modules |

## 3.5 Common KTU Numerical / Trace Type Sub-Question

**Question Pattern:** *"Given CLASSPATH = `.;/home/ktu/libs/*:/opt/x.jar`, list the order in which the JVM searches for class `org.demo.Worker`."*

**Model Answer Logic:**

**Step 1:** Parse the CLASSPATH into discrete entries separated by `:` (Linux convention):
1. `.` (current directory)
2. `/home/ktu/libs/*` (all JARs in `/home/ktu/libs/`)
3. `/opt/x.jar` (single JAR)

**Step 2:** Form the relative package path: `org/demo/`.

**Step 3:** The search order is:

$$
\text{Order} = \bigl[\texttt{./org/demo/Worker.class}\bigr] \;\rightarrow\; \bigl[\texttt{/home/ktu/libs/any.jar!org/demo/Worker.class}\bigr] \;\rightarrow\; \bigl[\texttt{/opt/x.jar!org/demo/Worker.class}\bigr]
$$

The `!` denotes JAR internal entry separator. The **first match wins**; if none found, `ClassNotFoundException` is thrown.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The ClassLoader Hierarchy & CLASSPATH Placement

This block-level diagram illustrates the **three-tier delegation model** of the JVM ClassLoader and the precise role of the user-defined CLASSPATH.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    JVMReq["JVM Request: load class com.ktu.app.Main"] --> BootCheck{Bootstrap ClassLoader}
    BootCheck -->|"Found in rt.jar"| BootUse["Class Loaded - Return"]
    BootCheck -->|"Not Found"| ExtCheck{Extension / Platform ClassLoader}
    ExtCheck -->|"Found in jre/lib/ext"| ExtUse["Class Loaded - Return"]
    ExtCheck -->|"Not Found"| AppCheck{Application ClassLoader}
    AppCheck -->|"Scan CLASSPATH entry 1: dot (.)"| Scan1["Search current directory for class"]
    Scan1 -->|"Match found"| AppUse["Class Loaded - Return"]
    Scan1 -->|"No match"| ScanJar["Scan CLASSPATH entry 2: libs wildcard"]
    ScanJar -->|"Match found"| AppUse
    ScanJar -->|"No match"| Scan3["Scan CLASSPATH entry 3: explicit JAR"]
    Scan3 -->|"Match found"| AppUse
    Scan3 -->|"No match"| ThrowErr["Throw java.lang.ClassNotFoundException"]

    classDef success fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745,color:#155724
    classDef failure fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#721c24
    classDef neutral fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#007bff,color:#004085
    class BootUse,ExtUse,AppUse success
    class ThrowErr failure
    class BootCheck,ExtCheck,AppCheck,Scan1,ScanJar,Scan3 neutral
```

## 4.2 CLASSPATH Search Resolution Pipeline

The diagram below maps the **declarative-to-physical** path resolution pipeline when the JVM encounters a class import.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Source code: import com.app.Worker"] --> B["Fully qualified name: com.app.Worker"]
    B --> C["Convert dots to slashes: com/app/Worker.class"]
    C --> D["Iterate CLASSPATH entries in order"]
    D --> E1["Entry: dot (.)"]
    D --> E2["Entry: /opt/libs/*"]
    D --> E3["Entry: /home/x.jar"]
    E1 --> F1{File exists?}
    E2 --> F2{ZIP entry exists?}
    E3 --> F3{ZIP entry exists?}
    F1 -->|Yes| LOAD["Class Loaded"]
    F1 -->|No| NEXT1[Move to next entry]
    F2 -->|Yes| LOAD
    F2 -->|No| NEXT2[Move to next entry]
    F3 -->|Yes| LOAD
    F3 -->|No| ERR["ClassNotFoundException"]

    classDef ok fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745,color:#155724
    classDef bad fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#721c24
    classDef proc fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#6c757d,color:#383d41
    class LOAD ok
    class ERR bad
    class A,B,C,D,E1,E2,E3,F1,F2,F3,NEXT1,NEXT2 proc
```

## 4.3 PATH vs. CLASSPATH Functional Separation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph UserShell["User types 'javac MyApp.java' on command line"]
        OS1["Operating System Shell"]
    end
    OS1 -->|"Lookup PATH for 'javac' executable"| PATHVAR["PATH variable"]
    PATHVAR -->|"Locate javac.exe / javac"| JDKBIN["JDK /bin directory"]
    JDKBIN -->|"Invoke compiler"| COMPILE["Compilation process begins"]

    COMPILE -->|"Compiler needs external classes"| CLASSPATHVAR["CLASSPATH variable"]
    CLASSPATHVAR -->|"Locate user classes & JARs"| USERLIB["User libraries / project bin"]
    USERLIB -->|"Supply .class files"| COMPILE

    classDef pathbox fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#856404
    classDef classbox fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#17a2b8,color:#0c5460
    class PATHVAR,JDKBIN pathbox
    class CLASSPATHVAR,USERLIB classbox
```

## 4.4 Wildcard `*` Expansion Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["CLASSPATH contains 'libs/*'"] --> ScanDir["Open directory 'libs/'"]
    ScanDir --> ListFiles["Enumerate all files"]
    ListFiles --> Filter{"File extension == .jar?"}
    Filter -->|"Yes (a.jar)"| AddA["Add a.jar to search list"]
    Filter -->|"Yes (b.jar)"| AddB["Add b.jar to search list"]
    Filter -->|"No (readme.txt)"| Skip["Ignore file"]
    Filter -->|"No (subdir/docs/)"| NoRecurse["Do not recurse"]
    AddA --> FinalList["Final list: a.jar, b.jar"]
    AddB --> FinalList
    Skip --> FinalList
    NoRecurse --> FinalList

    classDef inc fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745,color:#155724
    classDef exc fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#721c24
    classDef proc fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#6c757d,color:#383d41
    class AddA,AddB inc
    class Skip,NoRecurse exc
    class Start,ScanDir,ListFiles,Filter,FinalList proc
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**
*Define CLASSPATH. Why is it necessary in Java?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** CLASSPATH is an environment variable that specifies the locations (directories and JAR files) where the Java compiler (`javac`) and JVM should search for user-defined classes and third-party libraries.
- **Necessity – Reason 1 (1 mark):** It allows the JVM to locate and load `.class` files at runtime, enabling modular development and use of external libraries without hard-coding absolute paths.
- **Necessity – Reason 2 (1 mark):** It supports Java's dynamic class-loading architecture, where classes are loaded on demand from any location accessible to the JVM.

### Question A2
**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**
*Differentiate between PATH and CLASSPATH environment variables.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Attribute | PATH | CLASSPATH |
|---|---|---|
| Used by | Operating system | JVM and `javac` |
| Purpose | Locates executable programs (e.g., `java.exe`) | Locates `.class` files and `.jar` archives |
| Default | OS-defined (often no entry for Java) | Current directory `.` |

(1 mark for correct usage distinction, 1 mark for purpose, 1 mark for default value.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question B1 — Choice A

**`[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3]`**
**(a)** Explain the concept of CLASSPATH in Java. Discuss how the Java Virtual Machine uses CLASSPATH to locate classes, with a neat diagram of the ClassLoader delegation model. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following directory structure. The CLASSPATH is set as:
`set CLASSPATH=.;C:\myproject\libs\*;C:\myproject\bin`

```
C:\myproject\
├── src\
│   └── com\ktu\demo\Main.java
├── bin\
│   └── com\ktu\demo\Main.class
└── libs\
    ├── a.jar      (contains com.ktu.demo.Helper)
    ├── b.jar      (contains com.ktu.demo.Helper - duplicate)
    └── c.jar      (contains com.ktu.demo.Util)
```

The `Main` class imports both `Helper` and `Util`. Explain the order in which the JVM resolves each class. What happens when both `a.jar` and `b.jar` define `Helper`? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) CLASSPATH Concept and ClassLoader Delegation (7 marks):**

1. **Definition (1 mark):** CLASSPATH is an environment variable that informs the Java compiler and JVM about the locations of user-defined classes and external libraries (JAR files). It is essentially a list of root paths that the ClassLoader searches sequentially.

2. **ClassLoader Delegation Model (4 marks):** The JVM uses a hierarchical, parent-first delegation model:
   - **Bootstrap ClassLoader** — Loads core JDK classes (e.g., `java.lang.*`) from `rt.jar`. Highest priority.
   - **Platform/Extension ClassLoader** — Loads JDK extension classes.
   - **Application ClassLoader** — Loads classes from the user-defined CLASSPATH. This is the only loader influenced by the CLASSPATH variable.
   - When a class is requested, the request propagates from the application loader **upward** to the parent loaders. Only if no parent can find the class does the application loader consult the CLASSPATH.

3. **Search Mechanics (2 marks):** For a class `com.ktu.demo.Main`:
   - The JVM converts the package to a relative path: `com/ktu/demo/`.
   - For each CLASSPATH entry, the JVM constructs the full path: `entryPath + com/ktu/demo/Main.class`.
   - The first match is loaded; subsequent entries with the same class are ignored.

   *(Refer to Section 4.1 diagram for the schematic.)*

**(b) Resolution Order and Duplicate-Handling (7 marks):**

1. **CLASSPATH Parsing (1 mark):** The CLASSPATH contains three entries:
   - Entry 1: `.` (current directory)
   - Entry 2: `C:\myproject\libs\*` (all JARs in `libs/` — expands to `a.jar`, `b.jar`, `c.jar` in alphabetical order)
   - Entry 3: `C:\myproject\bin` (root for compiled classes)

2. **Resolution of `Main` (2 marks):** The class `com.ktu.demo.Main` is **not** in the current directory (`.`). It is **not** in any JAR. It is found at `C:\myproject\bin\com\ktu\demo\Main.class`. The loader reads this file and loads `Main`.

3. **Resolution of `Util` (2 marks):** `Util` is not in `.`. The loader opens `a.jar` — not found. Opens `b.jar` — not found. Opens `c.jar` — found! The entry `com/ktu/demo/Util.class` exists inside `c.jar`. Loaded successfully.

4. **Resolution of `Helper` and Duplicate Conflict (2 marks):** `Helper` is not in `.`. The loader opens `a.jar` first (alphabetical priority). The entry `com/ktu/demo/Helper.class` is found. **`a.jar`'s version of `Helper` is loaded.** The duplicate in `b.jar` is **shadowed / silently ignored** by the **First-Wins Rule**. The JVM does **not** merge or warn; it simply uses the first match encountered.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Choice A):**
> - **Don't** say "the JVM merges both Helper classes" — that is wrong; only the first match loads.
> - **Don't** say "the more recent JAR wins" — CLASSPATH order, not file modification time, determines priority.
> - **Don't** forget to mention that the wildcard `*` does not recurse into subdirectories.

### Question B1 — Choice B (Alternative)

**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]`**
**(a)** What is the role of CLASSPATH in Java? Explain the difference between setting CLASSPATH as an environment variable versus using the inline `-cp` flag. List the syntax for setting CLASSPATH on both Windows and Linux. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write the complete commands to compile and run a Java program `com.ktu.app.Main` whose source is in `C:\proj\src` and compiled classes go to `C:\proj\bin`. The program uses an external library `C:\proj\extlib\json.jar`. Also explain what happens if you mistakenly use the colon `:` separator on Windows. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Role of CLASSPATH and Setting Methods (7 marks):**

1. **Role (2 marks):** CLASSPATH instructs the JVM and `javac` where to find classes outside the JDK. Without a correctly configured CLASSPATH, user programs and external libraries cannot be located, resulting in `ClassNotFoundException` or `NoClassDefFoundError`.

2. **Environment Variable vs. Inline `-cp` (3 marks):**

   | Aspect | Environment Variable | Inline `-cp` |
   |---|---|---|
   | Scope | Global, affects all Java invocations | Per-command invocation |
   | Persistence | Persists across sessions (if set permanently) | Only for that single command |
   | Override | Inline `-cp` overrides the env variable | Cannot be overridden further by env |
   | Portability | Low (machine-specific) | High (script-friendly) |
   | Recommendation | Avoid in modern development | Preferred for projects |

3. **Syntax on Both Platforms (2 marks):**
   - **Windows:** `set CLASSPATH=C:\myclasses;.;C:\libs\*`
   - **Linux/macOS:** `export CLASSPATH=/home/user/myclasses:.:/opt/libs/*`

**(b) Compile/Run Commands and Separator Mistake (7 marks):**

1. **Compilation Command (2 marks):**
   ```
   javac -cp C:\proj\extlib\json.jar -d C:\proj\bin C:\proj\src\com\ktu\app\Main.java
   ```
   - `-cp` provides the external dependency.
   - `-d` specifies the destination directory for compiled `.class` files.

2. **Execution Command (2 marks):**
   ```
   java -cp C:\proj\bin;C:\proj\extlib\json.jar com.ktu.app.Main
   ```
   - Both the project `bin` and the external JAR must be on the classpath.
   - The fully qualified class name (with dots) is given as the argument to `java`.

3. **Mistaken Colon Separator on Windows (3 marks):** If a developer accidentally sets:
   ```
   set CLASSPATH=C:\proj\bin:C:\proj\extlib\json.jar
   ```
   the JVM interprets the entire string `C:\proj\bin:C:\proj\extlib\json.jar` as a **single path** (because `:` is not a recognized separator on Windows). The system tries to find a directory or file with that literal name, fails, and throws:

   ```
   Error: Could not find or load main class com.ktu.app.Main
   Caused by: java.lang.ClassNotFoundException: com.ktu.app.Main
   ```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Choice B):**
> - Students often forget that `java` takes the **fully qualified class name** (with dots) — not the path with slashes.
> - Many students omit the project `bin` directory from the runtime `-cp`, leading to `Main` not being found even though it compiles fine.
> - Mixing up `;` and `:` is a high-frequency mistake; mention the OS-specific separator explicitly in your answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- **CLASSPATH** = an environment variable / inline flag that tells the JVM and `javac` where to find user classes and JARs.
- **PATH** finds Java tools; **CLASSPATH** finds the materials those tools need.
- **Default CLASSPATH** is the current working directory `.` — represented by a single dot.
- **Path separator:** Semicolon `;` on Windows, Colon `:` on Linux/macOS.
- **Wildcard `*`** matches all JARs in a directory; it does **not** match `.class` files and does **not** recurse.
- **Inline `-cp` flag** overrides the environment variable for that command.
- **First-Wins Rule:** when multiple CLASSPATH entries contain the same class, the earliest entry in the list takes precedence; duplicates are silently ignored.
- **Three-tier ClassLoader hierarchy:** Bootstrap → Platform → Application. CLASSPATH only influences the **Application ClassLoader**.
- **ClassNotFoundException** is thrown at runtime when a class is not on the CLASSPATH; **NoClassDefFoundError** is thrown when a class was present at compile time but missing at runtime.
- **JAR files** use the internal `!/` separator; e.g., `libs/json.jar!/com/json/Object.class`.
- **Executable JARs** ignore the system CLASSPATH and rely on the `MANIFEST.MF` `Class-Path` entry.
- **Java 9+ Modules:** The `module-path` option is the modern alternative to CLASSPATH for application-level modular dependencies.
- **Setting CLASSPATH permanently:** Windows → *System Properties → Environment Variables*; Linux → add `export CLASSPATH=...` to `~/.bashrc`.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Importing Packages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT414 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Importing Packages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Package (Java Definition):** A package in Java is a *namespace* that organizes a set of related classes, interfaces, enumerations, and sub-packages. It functions as a *container* or *logical folder* used to prevent naming conflicts, enforce access protection, and provide a clear hierarchical structure for modular programming.

> **Importing Packages:** The act of making classes, interfaces, or static members from another package accessible to the current compilation unit *without* using their Fully Qualified Name (FQN). The `import` keyword instructs the Java compiler to search a specific package during the symbol resolution phase.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Office Filing Cabinet"

Imagine your college office:

- The **Office** is the JVM (Java Virtual Machine).
- Each **Drawer** in the cabinet is a **Package** (e.g., `java.util`, `java.io`).
- Each **File Folder** inside a drawer is a **Class** (e.g., `Scanner`, `ArrayList`).
- **Importing a package** is like telling the office clerk: *"For today's work, I need access to the 'Mathematics' drawer. Please pull it out and place it on my desk."* After this, you can directly refer to any file (class) inside that drawer by its short name instead of spelling out the full path every time.

### 1.3 Why Packages Exist — The Core Problem They Solve

Without packages, every Java programmer on Earth could create a class named `List`, `Date`, or `Vector`, and the compiler would have no way to distinguish between them. Packages provide:

1. **Namespace Management** — Two developers can each have a class named `Helper` as long as they live in different packages (e.g., `com.tcs.Helper` vs `com.infosys.Helper`).
2. **Access Control** — Classes marked `default` (package-private) are visible *only* to other classes in the same package.
3. **Modular Organization** — Related functionality is grouped logically (e.g., all database classes in `java.sql`, all collection classes in `java.util`).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Module 3 of PEECT414 explicitly targets "Packages and Interfaces." A frequent board question asks students to *write a program that uses a user-defined package*. You must understand importing fluently to answer this.

### 1.4 Two Flavors of Packages

| Package Type | Examples | Created By |
|---|---|---|
| **Built-in (API) Packages** | `java.lang`, `java.util`, `java.io`, `java.sql`, `java.awt`, `javax.swing` | JDK Developers (Sun/Oracle) |
| **User-defined Packages** | `mypack`, `com.ktu.student`, `org.department.utility` | The programmer |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Auto-Imported Rule:** The package `java.lang` is **implicitly and automatically** imported into *every* Java program. This is why you can use `String`, `System`, `Math`, and `Object` without writing any `import` statement. No other package enjoys this privilege.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Forms of the `import` Statement

Java provides exactly **three syntactic forms** to import external entities. Understanding the differences is a KTU favorite.

#### Form 1 — Explicit Single-Type Import (Most Recommended)

```java
import java.util.Scanner;
import java.util.ArrayList;
```

- Brings **one specific class** into scope.
- Compiler already knows the exact location of `Scanner` → faster compilation, better readability, no ambiguity.
- **KTU Examiner's Preference:** This form is considered the most professional and is preferred in board examinations unless wildcard is specifically required.

#### Form 2 — Wildcard (Star) Import

```java
import java.util.*;
```

- Brings **every public class and interface** from the `java.util` package into scope.
- Does **NOT** import sub-packages (e.g., `java.util.regex` is *not* auto-included by `java.util.*`).
- May cause **Naming Ambiguity** if two imported packages contain classes with the same name. The compiler will throw an error and force you to use the FQN.

#### Form 3 — Static Import

```java
import static java.lang.Math.sqrt;
import static java.lang.Math.*;
import static java.lang.System.out;
```

- Introduced in **Java 5.0**.
- Allows direct access to **static members** (fields and methods) of a class *without* qualifying them with the class name.
- Example: After `import static java.lang.Math.*;`, you can write `sqrt(25)` instead of `Math.sqrt(25)`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Compiler Internals:** The `import` statement is *not* a runtime instruction. It is purely a **compile-time convenience** for the developer. At runtime, the JVM uses the **Fully Qualified Name (FQN)** internally to locate `.class` files via the classpath. This is a classic 2-mark KTU question.

### 2.2 The Package Directory Structure — A Mandatory Contract

Java enforces a strict rule: **The package name must map directly to a directory structure on the file system**.

$$\text{package declaration} \longleftrightarrow \text{physical folder structure}$$

| Package Declaration | Required Directory |
|---|---|
| `package mypack;` | `mydir/mypack/MyClass.class` |
| `package com.ktu.cse;` | `com/ktu/cse/MyClass.class` |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall #1:** Students frequently declare `package com.ktu.cse;` at the top of their file but save the file inside a *plain* folder. The compiler then refuses to find the class. The directory hierarchy **must mirror** the dotted package name.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Key Rule | Typical Marks |
|---|---|---|
| `java.lang` auto-import | Always available, no statement needed | 2 |
| Wildcard scope | Imports classes, **not sub-packages** | 2 |
| `import` is compile-time | JVM uses FQN at runtime | 2 |
| Package ↔ Folder mapping | Mandatory strict mapping | 3 |
| Static import | Imports static members only | 3 |
| Naming conflict resolution | Use FQN for the ambiguous class | 3 |
| `package` statement position | Must be the **first** line (before `import`) | 2 |
| `CLASSPATH` | Tells JVM/compiler where to find `.class` files | 3 |
| Default access modifier | Visible only within the **same package** | 3 |
| Access modifiers in package | `public` for all, `default` for package-only | 3 |

### 2.4 The Compilation & Resolution Lifecycle (Theory)

The following sequence occurs whenever a `.java` file references a non-local class:

1. The programmer writes an `import` statement.
2. The **compiler** consults the `import` to expand short names into FQNs.
3. The compiler searches the **classpath** for a matching `.class` file.
4. If found, the compiled `.class` file contains FQNs (e.g., `java/util/Scanner`).
5. At runtime, the **ClassLoader** (Bootstrap → Extension → Application) loads the bytecode using FQNs.
6. Static linking establishes method calls between classes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** This same package-and-classloader model is the foundation of enterprise frameworks like **Spring Boot**, **Maven**, and **OSGi**. Every JAR file in a Maven project is essentially a packaged module. Understanding Java packages is your gateway to understanding modern software architecture.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Creating and Importing a User-Defined Package

This is a **classic 14-mark KTU problem**. The examiner will expect you to:
1. Create a package with at least one class.
2. Create another class in a *different* source file that imports the first.
3. Compile and run, explaining each step.

#### Step 1 — Create the package class

Save the following file as `Addition.java` inside the folder `mypack/`.

```java
// File: mypack/Addition.java
package mypack;

public class Addition {
    public int sum(int a, int b) {
        return a + b;
    }
}
```

**Explanation of each line:**

- `package mypack;` — Declares that this class logically belongs to the package `mypack`. This *must* be the first statement in the file (excluding comments).
- `public class Addition` — The class must be marked `public` so that classes in *other* packages can import and instantiate it.
- `public int sum(...)` — The method is `public` so it is accessible from outside the package.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule Derived:** A class that wishes to be *imported* by other packages **must be declared `public`**. Otherwise, it remains package-private and invisible to outsiders.

#### Step 2 — Save with the correct path

$$\text{Project Root} \rightarrow \text{mypack} \rightarrow \text{Addition.java}$$

#### Step 3 — Compile the package

Open the terminal at the **project root** and execute:

```bash
javac mypack/Addition.java
```

**What happens internally:**

$$
\text{javac} \rightarrow \text{reads } \texttt{mypack/Addition.java} \rightarrow \text{generates } \texttt{mypack/Addition.class}
$$

The directory structure remains `mypack/Addition.class` after compilation, because the package name is part of the class's identity.

#### Step 4 — Write the importing class

Save the following file as `Test.java` at the **project root** (NOT inside `mypack`).

```java
// File: Test.java (in the default/unnamed package)
import mypack.Addition;  // Explicit single-class import

public class Test {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Addition obj = new Addition();
        int result = obj.sum(10, 20);
        System.out.println("Sum = " + result);
    }
}
```

**Line-by-line logical breakdown:**

- `import mypack.Addition;` — Tells the compiler: "If you see the short name `Addition` in this file, resolve it to `mypack.Addition`."
- `Addition obj = new Addition();` — Creates an instance. The compiler expands `Addition` → `mypack.Addition` internally.
- `obj.sum(10, 20)` — Calls the public method and stores the return value.
- `System.out.println(...)` — Uses `System` and `out` from the auto-imported `java.lang` package.

#### Step 5 — Compile and run the test class

```bash
javac Test.java
java Test
```

**Output:**
```
Sum = 30
```

#### Full Lifecycle (Mathematical/Logical Notation)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Source Phase:} \quad & \texttt{mypack/Addition.java} \xrightarrow{\text{javac}} \texttt{mypack/Addition.class} \\
\text{Symbol Resolution:} \quad & \texttt{import mypack.Addition;} \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{Compiler binds short name} \\
\text{Compilation:} \quad & \texttt{Test.java} \xrightarrow{\text{javac}} \texttt{Test.class} \quad \text{(contains FQN: mypack/Addition)} \\
\text{Runtime:} \quad & \text{AppClassLoader} \rightarrow \text{reads CLASSPATH} \rightarrow \text{loads } \texttt{mypack/Addition.class} \rightarrow \text{links} \\
\text{Execution:} \quad & \texttt{java Test} \rightarrow \text{main()} \rightarrow \texttt{sum(10,20)} \rightarrow \texttt{30}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Wildcard vs Explicit Import (Symbol Resolution)

Consider two packages each containing a class named `Date`:

- `java.util.Date` (utility date)
- `java.sql.Date` (SQL date)

#### Case A — Wildcard Imports Both

```java
import java.util.*;
import java.sql.*;
```

**Result:** Compilation **fails** with the error:
```
reference to Date is ambiguous
    both class java.util.Date in java.util and class java.sql.Date in java.sql match
```

**Resolution:** The programmer must use the FQN at the point of conflict:

```java
java.util.Date d1 = new java.util.Date();   // FQN disambiguates
java.sql.Date d2 = new java.sql.Date(0L);
```

**Logic step:**
$$\text{Compiler cannot pick} \rightarrow \text{Error} \rightarrow \text{Programmer supplies FQN} \rightarrow \text{No ambiguity}$$

#### Case B — Explicit Imports

```java
import java.util.Date;     // Imports ONLY java.util.Date
import java.sql.*;         // Imports everything from java.sql EXCEPT the conflict
```

Here there is no conflict because `java.util.Date` is imported explicitly, and `java.sql.*` does not import `java.util.*`.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall #2:** Many students believe that `import java.util.*;` automatically imports `java.util.regex`, `java.util.concurrent`, etc. **It does not.** The wildcard is strictly **one level deep**. This is a tested 2-mark concept.

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Static Import in Action

Without static import, the `Math` class requires verbose calls:

```java
public class CircleArea {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        double r = 5.0;
        double area = Math.PI * Math.pow(r, 2);
        double root = Math.sqrt(area);
        System.out.println("Area = " + area);
        System.out.println("Root = " + root);
    }
}
```

After applying **static import**, the code becomes concise:

```java
import static java.lang.Math.PI;
import static java.lang.Math.pow;
import static java.lang.Math.sqrt;
import static java.lang.System.out;

public class CircleArea {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        double r = 5.0;
        double area = PI * pow(r, 2);
        out.println("Area = " + area);
        out.println("Root = " + sqrt(area));
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Area = 78.53981633974483
Root = 8.862939241877357
```

**Derivation of values:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Area} & = \pi \cdot r^2 \\
& = 3.141592653589793 \times (5.0)^2 \\
& = 3.141592653589793 \times 25.0 \\
& = 78.53981633974483 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Root} & = \sqrt{\text{Area}} \\
& = \sqrt{78.53981633974483} \\
& \approx 8.862939241877357 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Overuse Warning:** Static imports are popular in **JUnit testing** (`import static org.junit.Assert.assertEquals;`) and **mathematical code**, but overuse in production code harms readability. KTU expects you to mention this trade-off in descriptive answers.

### 3.4 The CLASSPATH Mechanism (Detailed)

The classpath is the **search path** the JVM and compiler use to find `.class` files. It can be set in three ways:

1. **Environment variable** — `set CLASSPATH=.;C:\mylibs`
2. **Command-line flag** — `javac -classpath .;C:\mylibs *.java`
3. **Manifest file** (for JARs) — `Class-Path: lib/dependency.jar` inside `META-INF/MANIFEST.MF`

**Search Order Algorithm (for `java.util.Scanner`):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Check JRE's } \texttt{rt.jar} \text{ (bootstrap classes) — contains java.lang only} \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Check JRE's } \texttt{jdk/extensions} \text{ directory} \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Check the user-defined CLASSPATH, in order} \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{If not found → } \texttt{ClassNotFoundException} \text{ at runtime} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Importing Strategies Compared

| Strategy | Syntax | Imports Sub-packages? | Readability | Performance | Risk of Ambiguity |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Explicit single import | `import p.A;` | No | ★★★★★ | Fastest compile | None |
| Wildcard import | `import p.*;` | No | ★★★☆☆ | Slightly slower | High |
| Fully qualified name (no import) | `p.A obj = new p.A();` | N/A | ★★☆☆☆ | Fastest | None |
| Static import (single) | `import static p.C.m;` | N/A | ★★★★☆ | Normal | Low |
| Static import (wildcard) | `import static p.C.*;` | N/A | ★★☆☆☆ | Normal | Very High |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*What is a package in Java? Why is it necessary to use packages?*

**Model Answer:**

A package in Java is a namespace that groups a set of related classes, interfaces, and sub-packages into a single logical unit, physically represented as a folder structure.

Packages are necessary for the following reasons:

1. **To avoid naming conflicts** — Two different organizations can define a class with the same name (e.g., `Date`) and place them in different packages, making both uniquely identifiable by their FQN.
2. **To provide access control** — Classes with default (package-private) visibility are accessible only within the same package, enabling encapsulation at the package level.
3. **To organize code modularly** — Related classes (e.g., all database-related classes) are kept together for easier maintenance and discovery.
4. **To enable reusability** — A well-designed package can be distributed as a JAR file and reused across multiple projects.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Explain the difference between `import p.ClassName;` and `import p.*;`. State one advantage and one disadvantage of each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | `import p.ClassName;` | `import p.*;` |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Imports a single specific class | Imports all public types in the package |
| **Sub-packages** | Does not affect sub-packages | Does not affect sub-packages either |
| **Compilation Speed** | Faster — compiler knows exact location | Slightly slower — compiler must scan the package |
| **Ambiguity Risk** | Zero | Possible if two packages share class names |

**Advantage of explicit import:** Higher clarity; reader sees exactly which class is being used.

**Disadvantage of explicit import:** Verbose if many classes from the same package are needed.

**Advantage of wildcard:** Concise; useful when several classes from one package are used.

**Disadvantage of wildcard:** Hides which classes are actually used, and may cause name conflicts.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**

> *(a)* Explain the concept of user-defined packages in Java. Write a Java program to create a package named `geometry` containing a class `Circle` with a method `area(double r)` that returns the area of a circle. **[7 Marks]**
>
> *(b)* Write a Java program in a different file that imports the `geometry.Circle` class and uses it to compute and display the area of three circles with radii 2.5, 5.0, and 7.5. Show the directory structure, compilation steps, and output. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Concept:** A user-defined package is a package created by the programmer (not provided by the JDK) to group their own related classes. The first line of every Java file that belongs to a package must contain the `package` statement. The class must be declared `public` if it is to be accessed from outside the package.

**Directory structure required:**

```
ProjectRoot/
└── geometry/
    └── Circle.java
```

**Code — `geometry/Circle.java`:**

```java
// File: geometry/Circle.java
package geometry;

public class Circle {
    public double area(double r) {
        return Math.PI * r * r;
    }
}
```

**Compilation step (run from ProjectRoot):**

```bash
javac geometry/Circle.java
```

This generates `geometry/Circle.class`. The package name `geometry` is encoded in the bytecode.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Code — `TestCircle.java` (saved in ProjectRoot, NOT inside `geometry`):**

```java
// File: TestCircle.java
import geometry.Circle;  // Explicit single-class import

public class TestCircle {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Circle c = new Circle();
        double[] radii = {2.5, 5.0, 7.5};

        System.out.println("--- Circle Area Calculator ---");
        for (double r : radii) {
            double a = c.area(r);
            System.out.println("Radius = " + r + "  ->  Area = " + a);
        }
    }
}
```

**Compilation & Execution:**

```bash
javac TestCircle.java
java TestCircle
```

**Output:**
```
--- Circle Area Calculator ---
Radius = 2.5  ->  Area = 19.634954084936208
Radius = 5.0  ->  Area = 78.53981633974483
Radius = 7.5  ->  Area = 176.71458676442586
```

**Step-by-step derivation of the first area:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_1 & = \pi \cdot r^2 \\
& = 3.141592653589793 \times (2.5)^2 \\
& = 3.141592653589793 \times 6.25 \\
& = 19.634954084936208 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

The same formula applied for $r = 5.0$ and $r = 7.5$ produces the other two outputs.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**

> *(a)* With suitable code examples, explain the three types of import statements in Java: explicit, wildcard, and static import. Mention one limitation of each. **[7 Marks]**
>
> *(b)* What is the `CLASSPATH` environment variable? Explain how the JVM uses it to locate a class at runtime. What happens if `CLASSPATH` is not set or is set incorrectly? **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Type 1 — Explicit Import:**

```java
import java.util.ArrayList;

public class Demo1 {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        ArrayList<String> list = new ArrayList<>();  // Short name works
    }
}
```

*Limitation:* Verbose when many classes from the same package are required.

**Type 2 — Wildcard Import:**

```java
import java.util.*;

public class Demo2 {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);  // No need to import Scanner separately
        HashMap<Integer, String> map = new HashMap<>();
    }
}
```

*Limitation:* Does not import sub-packages (e.g., `java.util.regex.Pattern` is not imported by `java.util.*`). Also, can cause naming ambiguity.

**Type 3 — Static Import:**

```java
import static java.lang.Math.sqrt;
import static java.lang.System.out;

public class Demo3 {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        out.println("Square root of 81 = " + sqrt(81));  // Direct access
    }
}
```

*Limitation:* Overuse can produce unreadable code because the class source of a method/field is no longer visible at the call site. Should be restricted to constant classes (e.g., `Math`) and test frameworks (JUnit).

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Definition of CLASSPATH:**

> The **CLASSPATH** is an environment variable (or a command-line option `-cp`) that tells the Java compiler (`javac`) and the Java Virtual Machine (`java`) **where to look for user-defined and third-party `.class` files and JAR archives**.

**How the JVM uses it (search algorithm):**

When a Java program needs a class (e.g., `mypack.Addition`), the **Application ClassLoader** performs the following steps in order:

1. Checks the **Bootstrap classpath** (`jre/lib/rt.jar`) for core Java API classes.
2. Checks the **Extension classpath** (`jre/lib/ext/`) for optional packages.
3. Checks the **User classpath** (the `CLASSPATH` variable or `-cp` flag) in left-to-right order, looking in each directory for a matching folder structure.
4. For each entry, the loader reconstructs the path: `mypack/Addition.class`.

**What happens if CLASSPATH is not set or is incorrect:**

- If **not set** and the current directory `.` is not included, the JVM throws `ClassNotFoundException` or `NoClassDefFoundError` for any user class.
- If **set to a wrong path** (e.g., pointing to `C:\wrongfolder` instead of the project root), the classloader cannot locate the `.class` file and the same exception is thrown.
- The default behavior (when CLASSPATH is unset) varies between JDK versions: older JDK 1.x used to include `.` by default; modern JDK (9+) does **not** include `.` automatically, so you must either set `CLASSPATH=.;C:\myclasses` or use `java -cp . MyClass`.

**Demonstration:**

```bash
# Correct way to run Test.java when Addition.class is in ./mypack/
java -cp . Test

# Or equivalently:
set CLASSPATH=.
java Test
```

If you wrongly set `CLASSPATH=C:\windows`, then:
```
Exception in thread "main" java.lang.NoClassDefFoundError: mypack/Addition
```

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Importing Packages Questions:**
>
> 1. **Missing `public` modifier on the class or method** — A class intended for import across packages that is not declared `public` will be invisible to the importer. Examiners deduct **2 marks** for this oversight.
> 2. **Wrong directory placement** — Saving `Addition.java` directly in the project root (without the `mypack/` folder) makes compilation fail with `cannot find symbol`. Always match the folder name to the package name *exactly* (case-sensitive).
> 3. **Forgetting the `package` statement** — A class file inside the `mypack/` folder but without a `package mypack;` declaration is considered part of the *default (unnamed) package*, and `import mypack.Addition;` will fail.
> 4. **Confusing `import` with `#include`** — C/C++ programmers often wrongly assume `import` physically copies code. It does **not**. It is a compile-time directive only. The JVM uses FQNs at runtime.
> 5. **Trying to import sub-packages** — Writing `import java.util.*;` and expecting `java.util.regex.Pattern` to work fails. The wildcard is non-recursive.
> 6. **Forgetting to compile the package first** — If you compile `Test.java` before `Addition.java`, the compiler will report `cannot find symbol` even though `Addition.java` exists. **Always compile the dependency first.**

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Importing Packages**

- A **package** is a *namespace* that groups related Java types and maps to a directory structure.
- The `package` declaration must be the **first non-comment, non-blank statement** in a `.java` file.
- **Built-in packages** include `java.lang`, `java.util`, `java.io`, `java.sql`, `java.net`, `java.awt`, `javax.swing`.
- `java.lang` is **auto-imported** into every Java program; do not write `import java.lang.*;`.
- Three import forms: **explicit (`import p.A;`)**, **wildcard (`import p.*;`)**, **static (`import static p.C.m;`)**.
- Wildcard imports do **NOT** import sub-packages.
- `import` is a **compile-time** directive; at runtime the JVM uses **FQNs** via the **ClassLoader**.
- The **CLASSPATH** tells the compiler and JVM where to find `.class` files; it can be an environment variable, a `-cp` flag, or a JAR manifest entry.
- A class must be declared `public` to be visible outside its package.
- Members have four access levels: `private` (class only), `default` (package only), `protected` (package + subclasses), `public` (everywhere).
- A fully qualified name (FQN) can always be used in place of an `import` to disambiguate conflicting class names.
- **Static import** is most useful for constants (`Math.PI`) and JUnit assertions; avoid overusing it in production code.
- The directory structure must mirror the package name: `package com.ktu.cse;` → `com/ktu/cse/YourClass.java`.
- Compilation: `javac -d . package/Class.java` places the `.class` file in the correct folder structure under the current directory.
- Runtime class-loading order: **Bootstrap → Platform → Application ClassLoader**.
- A common runtime error is `ClassNotFoundException` (class missing on classpath) or `NoClassDefFoundError` (class was present at compile time but not at runtime).
- **Best practice:** Prefer explicit imports for clarity; use wildcard only when importing 4+ classes from the same package.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exception Handling - Checked Exceptions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT414 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Exception Handling - Checked Exceptions # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the Java programming language, an **Exception** is an event that disrupts the normal flow of a program's execution during runtime. The Java language classifies exceptions into a strict, three-tier hierarchy rooted at the `java.lang.Throwable` class. A **Checked Exception** (also called a *compile-time exception* or *checked exception type*) is formally defined as: > An exception that is **not** a subclass of `java.lang.RuntimeException` or `java.lang.Error`, and is therefore subject to **compile-time verification** by the Java compiler (Javac). The compiler enforces that any method which may throw such an exception must either *handle* it using a `try-catch` block or *declare* it in the method's `throws` clause; otherwise, compilation fails. In the KTU 2024 OOP syllabus, checked exceptions represent the foundational pillar of Java's robust, type-safe error-handling philosophy. They are predominantly used to model **recoverable conditions** that occur due to external environmental factors outside the programmer's direct control — such as missing files, network failures, or invalid database queries. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Under the KTU 2024 Scheme (PEECT414), Module 3 expects students to differentiate between checked, unchecked, and errors, and to fluently apply `try`, `catch`, `finally`, `throw`, and `throws` constructs in programs involving checked exception types. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Airport Security Checkpoint Imagine you are boarding a flight from Kochi (COK) to Dubai (DXB). Before you are allowed to step onto the aircraft, the airline **mandatorily** verifies two things at the security gate: 1. **You possess a valid passport and visa** (the *visa officer* does the check before you even reach the gate). 2. **Your luggage does not contain prohibited items** (the *X-ray scanner* does the check). If either check fails, you are *not allowed to fly* until the issue is resolved. This is precisely how the **Java compiler** treats a checked exception: | Airport Checkpoint | Java Compiler Analogy | |---|---| | The visa officer examines documents *before* you board | The compiler examines your code *before* it runs | | You must have a valid visa OR a valid catch by a connecting airline | You must `catch` the exception OR `throws`-declare it | | Without either, the journey is forbidden | Without either, the `.java` file will not compile to `.class` | | The security gate = **Compile-time gate** | The `javac` tool = **Compile-time gate** | A **checked exception**, therefore, is the compiler forcing you to "show your visa papers" (handle the exception) before your program is "allowed to take off" (run). > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction:** The word "checked" refers to **compile-time checking**, NOT runtime checking. The compiler checks whether you have acknowledged the *possibility* of such an exception — the actual exception may or may not occur when the program runs. ## 1.3 Classification Metrics at a Glance - **Mandatory Handling:** **Yes** (enforced by `javac`). - **Detected At:** **Compile Time**. - **Parent Class in Hierarchy:** `java.lang.Exception` (but **not** a subclass of `RuntimeException`). - **Typical Cause:** External resources (I/O, network, database, filesystem). - **Recovery Strategy:** Usually recoverable — caller is expected to act. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Java Exception Class Hierarchy The Java standard library defines a strict class hierarchy for all throwable objects. Understanding this tree is **the single most important concept** in this module. $$ \text{java.lang.Object} \;\rightarrow\; \text{java.lang.Throwable} \;\rightarrow\; \begin{cases} \text{java.lang.Error} \\ \text{java.lang.Exception} \begin{cases} \text{java.io.IOException} \\ \text{java.sql.SQLException} \\ \text{java.lang.ClassNotFoundException} \\ \text{java.text.ParseException} \\ \text{java.io.FileNotFoundException} \\ \text{java.net.MalformedURLException} \\ \quad\vdots \\ \text{java.lang.RuntimeException} \begin{cases} \text{ArithmeticException} \\ \text{NullPointerException} \\ \text{ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException} \\ \text{NumberFormatException} \\ \quad\vdots \end{cases} \end{cases} \end{cases} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Golden Rule of Checked vs Unchecked:** If a class is a direct or indirect subclass of `java.lang.RuntimeException`, it is **unchecked**. Otherwise, if it sits under `java.lang.Exception` (and not under `Error`), it is **checked**. `Error` and its subclasses are neither checked nor unchecked — they represent fatal JVM-level conditions (e.g., `OutOfMemoryError`). ## 2.2 Structural Logic: The Five Core Mechanisms The Java language provides five interlocked mechanisms to deal with checked exceptions. Each serves a distinct role in the *handling chain*. ### 2.2.1 The `try` Block — The Watchtower - A `try` block **encloses** code that is "risky" — i.e., code that *may* throw a checked exception. - A `try` block **must** be followed by at least one `catch` block **or** one `finally` block (or both). - **Why?** It tells the compiler: "The code inside might fail. If it does, hand control to a catch block." ### 2.2.2 The `catch` Block — The Rescue Team - A `catch` block is an **exception handler** that catches a specific exception type (or its supertype). - Java requires the `catch` parameter to be an object of type `java.lang.Throwable` or any subclass. - **How?** When a checked exception is thrown, the JVM walks up the call stack looking for the first matching `catch` block. The first match (based on inheritance) wins. ### 2.2.3 The `finally` Block — The Cleanup Crew - A `finally` block contains code that **always executes** — whether an exception was thrown, caught, or not. - Used for releasing resources (closing file handles, network sockets, database connections). - The only cases where `finally` does NOT execute are `System.exit()` calls or JVM crashes. ### 2.2.4 The `throws` Keyword — The Declaration Sign - Used in a method's **signature** to declare that the method *may* throw one or more checked exception types. - **Why?** It transfers the responsibility of handling the exception to the **caller** of the method. - A method can `throws` multiple exceptions, separated by commas. ### 2.2.5 The `throw` Keyword — The Alarm Bell - Used *inside* a method body to **manually trigger** (raise) an exception. - The expression following `throw` must be a `Throwable` object. - Syntax: `throw new IOException("File missing");` ## 2.3 The "Handle OR Declare" Rule (HoD Rule) This is the **single most-tested rule** in KTU examinations on this topic. The compiler enforces it as follows: > For any checked exception that a method may throw, the method must satisfy **at least one** of the following two conditions: > > 1. **HANDLE** — Enclose the throwing statement within a `try` block and provide a matching `catch` block. > 2. **DECLARE** — Append a `throws` clause listing the exception type in the method signature. > > Failure to do either causes: `error: unreported exception ; must be caught or declared to be thrown`. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Construct | Mandatory Syntax Element | Compiler Verdict | When Required | |---|---|---|---| | `try` | At least one `catch` or `finally` | Hard compile-time | Wraps code that may throw | | `catch(ExceptionType e)` | `ExceptionType` must be `Throwable` subclass | Hard compile-time | Handles thrown exception | | `finally` | Optional but recommended | None | Always runs (cleanup) | | `throws X, Y` | Must list checked types | Hard compile-time | Delegates to caller | | `throw new X(...)` | Must construct a `Throwable` | Hard compile-time | Manually raise exception | | `try-with-resources` | Resource must implement `AutoCloseable` | Compile-time check | Auto-close I/O resources | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Checked exceptions are the cornerstone of **defensive programming** in mission-critical enterprise systems: - **Banking Software:** A `SQLException` when deducting a transfer fee is checked; the application is *forced* to either retry, rollback, or notify the user — never silently crash. - **Embedded Systems (IoT):** A `FileNotFoundException` when loading a firmware config forces the engineer to code a fallback path, preventing the device from entering an undefined state. - **Aerospace & Avionics:** Compiler-enforced exception handling reduces the probability of unhandled fault paths, aligning with DO-178C software safety guidelines. - **Web Servers (Tomcat, Spring):** Servlets declare `throws IOException, ServletException` precisely because the framework mandates it — the servlet container (`HttpServlet.service()`) is the eventual catcher. In all these domains, the **compile-time guarantee** that exception paths have been considered is invaluable — it shifts the burden of correctness from runtime testing to static analysis. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the "Handle OR Declare" Logic The Java Language Specification (JLS) §11.2 defines the precise rule. We can express it logically: $$ \text{For a method } M \text{ containing statement } S \text{ that may throw checked exception } E: $$ $$ \text{CompilationPass}(M) = \begin{cases} \text{TRUE} & \text{if } \exists\, \text{catch } C \text{ in } M \text{ such that } C.\text{param} \;\supseteq\; E \\ \text{TRUE} & \text{if } E \in \text{throws}(M) \\ \text{FALSE} & \text{otherwise (compile error)} \end{cases} $$ where $\supseteq$ denotes the inheritance "is-a" relationship (i.e., the catch parameter is $E$ itself, a superclass of $E$, or the interface that $E$ implements). **Transition Logic Explanation:** - A `try-catch` block satisfies the rule if the exception type caught is the same as, or a supertype of, the exception thrown. - A `throws` clause satisfies the rule by propagating the responsibility upward through the call stack to the caller of $M$. - If neither is present, the compiler raises an *unreported exception* error and refuses to produce a `.class` file. ## 3.2 Analytical Walkthrough — Three Scenarios We will analyze three program fragments to determine whether they compile, and *why*. ### Scenario 1: Handling via `try-catch` ```java import java.io.*; class Demo { public static void main(String[] args) { try { FileReader fr = new FileReader("data.txt"); // throws FileNotFoundException fr.read(); // throws IOException fr.close(); } catch (FileNotFoundException e) { System.out.println("File missing: " + e.getMessage()); } catch (IOException e) { System.out.println("I/O error: " + e.getMessage()); } } } ``` **Step-by-step evaluation:** $$ \text{Step 1: } \text{new FileReader("data.txt")} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{throws } \text{FileNotFoundException} $$ $$ \text{Step 2: } \text{fr.read()} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{throws } \text{IOException} $$ $$ \text{Step 3: Compiler checks each statement against the HoD rule} $$ $$ \text{Step 4: } \text{Both } \text{FileNotFoundException} \text{ and } \text{IOException} \text{ are subclasses of } \text{Exception} $$ $$ \text{Step 5: } \text{Both have matching catch handlers present} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{COMPILE PASS} $$ ### Scenario 2: Declaration via `throws` ```java import java.io.*; class Demo { public static void main(String[] args) throws FileNotFoundException, IOException { FileReader fr = new FileReader("data.txt"); fr.read(); fr.close(); } } ``` **Step-by-step evaluation:** $$ \text{Step 1: Risky statements are present (no try block).} $$ $$ \text{Step 2: Signature contains } \text{throws FileNotFoundException, IOException} $$ $$ \text{Step 3: Caller of } \text{main} \text{ is the JVM — JVM does not "handle" them but accepts the contract} $$ $$ \text{Step 4: Compiler satisfies the DECLARE branch of HoD rule} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{COMPILE PASS} $$ ### Scenario 3: Neither Handle Nor Declare (Failure Case) ```java import java.io.*; class Demo { public static void main(String[] args) { FileReader fr = new FileReader("data.txt"); // compile-time error here } } ``` **Step-by-step evaluation:** $$ \text{Step 1: } \text{new FileReader("data.txt")} \text{ can throw } \text{FileNotFoundException} $$ $$ \text{Step 2: No enclosing } \text{try-catch} \text{ exists} $$ $$ \text{Step 3: No } \text{throws} \text{ clause in method signature} $$ $$ \text{Step 4: HoD rule is violated (both branches FALSE)} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{COMPILE FAIL} $$ $$ \text{Compiler output: error: unreported exception FileNotFoundException;} \;\text{must be caught or declared to be thrown} $$ ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Checked vs Unchecked vs Error | Property | Checked Exception | Unchecked Exception | Error | |---|---|---|---| | Inherits from | `java.lang.Exception` (NOT `RuntimeException`) | `java.lang.RuntimeException` | `java.lang.Error` | | Detected at | **Compile time** | Runtime | Runtime | | Compiler forces handling? | **Yes** | No | No | | Recoverable? | Usually yes | Sometimes | Usually no | | Examples | `IOException`, `SQLException`, `ClassNotFoundException` | `NullPointerException`, `ArithmeticException` | `OutOfMemoryError`, `StackOverflowError` | | KTU typical question weight | **High** (Module 3 focus) | Medium | Low | | Caused by | External environment | Programmer logic | JVM/system failure | ## 3.4 Common Checked Exception Classes — Quick Reference | Class | Package | Typical Trigger | |---|---|---| | `IOException` | `java.io` | General I/O failure | | `FileNotFoundException` | `java.io` | Specified file does not exist | | `EOFException` | `java.io` | End-of-file reached unexpectedly | | `ClassNotFoundException` | `java.lang` | Class not found during dynamic loading | | `CloneNotSupportedException` | `java.lang` | Object does not implement `Cloneable` | | `InterruptedException` | `java.lang` | Thread was interrupted while waiting | | `InstantiationException` | `java.lang` | Abstract class instantiation attempted via reflection | | `SQLException` | `java.sql` | Database access error | | `MalformedURLException` | `java.net` | Invalid URL format | | `ParseException` | `java.text` | String parsing failure (e.g., date format) | ## 3.5 Step-by-Step — Creating a Custom Checked Exception For exam purposes, students must know how to **define** a checked exception type. **Step 1:** Create a class extending `java.lang.Exception`. **Step 2:** Provide at least two constructors: a default no-arg constructor and a one-arg constructor accepting a `String message`. **Step 3:** Use the `throw` keyword to raise the exception where the exceptional condition is detected. **Step 4:** Use `try-catch` or `throws` in the calling method. ```java // Step 1 & 2: Defining a custom checked exception class InsufficientBalanceException extends Exception { public InsufficientBalanceException() { super("Insufficient balance in account"); } public InsufficientBalanceException(String message) { super(message); } } // Step 3 & 4: Throwing and handling class BankAccount { double balance = 1000.0;
