public void withdraw(double amount) throws InsufficientBalanceException { if (amount > balance) { throw new InsufficientBalanceException( "Requested: " + amount + ", Available: " + balance); } balance -= amount; } } ``` $$ \text{Inheritance Chain: } \text{Object} \rightarrow \text{Throwable} \rightarrow \text{Exception} \rightarrow \text{InsufficientBalanceException} $$ $$ \text{Compiler enforcement: } \text{withdraw()} \text{ MUST be called within try-catch OR caller must add throws} $$ # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Java Exception Class Hierarchy ```mermaid graph TD A[Object] --> B[Throwable] B --> C[Error] B --> D[Exception] C --> C1[OutOfMemoryError] C --> C2[StackOverflowError] D --> E[IOException] D --> F[SQLException] D --> G[ClassNotFoundException] D --> H[ParseException] D --> I[InterruptedException] D --> J[RuntimeException] J --> J1[ArithmeticException] J --> J2[NullPointerException] J --> J3[ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException] E --> E1[FileNotFoundException] E --> E2[EOFException] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** Everything under `Exception` **but not** under `RuntimeException` is a **checked** exception. The shaded branches under `Error` and `RuntimeException` are the unchecked categories. ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Flow of `try-catch-finally` Execution ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Program enters try block] --> S2{Does exception occur?} S2 -- No --> S3[Execute remaining try code] S3 --> S4[Skip all catch blocks] S4 --> S5[Execute finally block] S5 --> S6[Continue program after try-catch-finally] S2 -- Yes --> S7[Stop try at exception point] S7 --> S8{Matching catch found?} S8 -- Yes --> S9[Execute matching catch block] S9 --> S5 S8 -- No --> S10[Propagate exception to caller] S10 --> S5 S5 --> S6 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > The `finally` block executes **in every path** — success, handled exception, propagated exception, or even an explicit `return` inside `try` or `catch`. ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — `throws` Propagation Up the Call Stack ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Main as main method participant Alpha as methodA participant Beta as methodB participant IO as FileReader constructor Main->>Alpha: call methodA() Alpha->>Beta: call methodB() Beta->>IO: new FileReader(data.txt) IO-->>Beta: throws FileNotFoundException Note over Beta: No catch here Beta-->>Alpha: throws (propagates upward) Note over Alpha: No catch here Alpha-->>Main: throws (propagates upward) Note over Main: Caller of main is JVM
JVM terminates with stack trace ``` > [!NOTE] > **Takeaway:** If no method in the call chain handles the checked exception, the `main` method is the last Java-level point of recovery. If `main` also does not handle it, the JVM takes over and prints a stack trace. ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — The Handle OR Declare Decision Logic ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Method body contains a statement that throws a checked exception] --> B{Is the statement inside a try block?} B -- Yes --> C{Is there a matching catch block?} C -- Yes --> D[HANDLE branch satisfied - COMPILES] C -- No --> E[Compile Error - must add catch or finally] B -- No --> F{Does the method signature contain throws?} F -- Yes --> G[DECLARE branch satisfied - COMPILES] F -- No --> H[Compile Error - must catch or declare] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]` **What is a checked exception in Java? Give two examples.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A checked exception is an exception that is checked by the Java compiler at compile time. It is any subclass of `java.lang.Exception` that does **not** inherit from `java.lang.RuntimeException`. The compiler enforces that such exceptions must either be caught using a `try-catch` block or declared in the method's `throws` clause; otherwise, the program will not compile. *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for two valid examples)* **Examples:** 1. `java.io.IOException` — raised when an input/output operation fails. 2. `java.sql.SQLException` — raised when a database access error occurs. 3. `java.lang.ClassNotFoundException` — raised when a class is not found during dynamic loading. ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Sample Paper]` **Differentiate between checked and unchecked exceptions in Java.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | Checked Exception | Unchecked Exception | |---|---|---| | Compiler verification | Checked at compile time | Not checked at compile time | | Inherits from | `java.lang.Exception` (not `RuntimeException`) | `java.lang.RuntimeException` | | Handling | Mandatory (catch or declare) | Optional | | Caused by | External resources (files, network) | Program logic errors | | Example | `IOException`, `SQLException` | `ArithmeticException`, `NullPointerException` | *(1 mark each for three correct differentiating points)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module 3 Internal Choice ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Explain the five keywords used for exception handling in Java with suitable examples. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Write a Java program that reads an integer from the user and performs division. The program must demonstrate the use of `try`, `catch`, `finally`, and `throw` for handling a custom checked exception `NonZeroDenominatorException`. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks The five keywords are `try`, `catch`, `finally`, `throw`, and `throws`. *(1 mark for listing)* - **`try`:** Encloses code that may raise an exception. *(1 mark)* - **`catch`:** Catches and handles an exception of a specific type. *(1 mark)* - **`finally`:** Always executes, used for cleanup (e.g., closing files). *(1 mark)* - **`throw`:** Used to explicitly raise an exception inside a method. *(1 mark)* - **`throws`:** Declares exceptions in a method signature to delegate responsibility. *(1 mark)* - Example: a code snippet showing all five in use. *(1 mark)* ```java void readFile(String path) throws FileNotFoundException { FileReader fr = null; try { fr = new FileReader(path); if (fr.read() == -1) throw new EOFException("Unexpected end of file"); } catch (FileNotFoundException e) { System.out.println("File missing"); } catch (IOException e) { System.out.println("Read error"); } finally { try { if (fr != null) fr.close(); } catch (IOException e) { } } } ``` #### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks **Step 1 (1 mark):** Define the custom checked exception. ```java class NonZeroDenominatorException extends Exception { public NonZeroDenominatorException(String message) { super(message); } } ``` **Step 2 (1 mark):** This class extends `Exception` directly, so it is a **checked** exception. **Step 3 (1 mark):** Implement the main class with the division logic and `throw`. ```java import java.util.Scanner; class DivisionDemo { public static double divide(int num, int den) throws NonZeroDenominatorException { if (den == 0) { throw new NonZeroDenominatorException("Denominator cannot be zero"); } return (double) num / den; } public static void main(String[] args) { Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in); try { System.out.print("Enter numerator: "); int n = sc.nextInt(); System.out.print("Enter denominator: "); int d = sc.nextInt(); double result = divide(n, d); System.out.println("Result = " + result); } catch (NonZeroDenominatorException e) { System.out.println("Custom Exception: " + e.getMessage()); } catch (Exception e) { System.out.println("Other error: " + e.getMessage()); } finally { sc.close(); System.out.println("Program execution completed."); } } } ``` **Step 4 (1 mark):** The `divide()` method declares `throws NonZeroDenominatorException` — satisfying the **DECLARE** branch. **Step 5 (1 mark):** The `main` method uses `try-catch-finally` — satisfying the **HANDLE** branch. **Step 6 (1 mark):** Explanation of the flow: if user enters 0, `throw` is executed, the corresponding `catch` block runs, then `finally` block runs to close the Scanner. **Step 7 (1 mark):** Sample output (for the case of 0 denominator): ``` Custom Exception: Denominator cannot be zero Program execution completed. ``` --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Sample Paper]` **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the Java exception class hierarchy. Distinguish between checked and unchecked exceptions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Write a Java program to read the contents of a file named `"input.txt"`. Use a checked exception handling mechanism with `try-catch-finally` and `throws` clause. Explain the role of the compiler in enforcing the handling of checked exceptions. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks **Step 1 (2 marks):** Draw/describe the hierarchy (refer to Section 4.1 of these notes). **Step 2 (1 mark):** `Object` → `Throwable` is the root. - `Throwable` has two main branches: `Error` and `Exception`. **Step 3 (1 mark):** `Error` represents fatal, unrecoverable JVM conditions (`OutOfMemoryError`, `StackOverflowError`). **Step 4 (1 mark):** `Exception` has two main sub-branches: - `IOException`, `SQLException`, etc. → **Checked** (compile-time checked). - `RuntimeException` and its subclasses → **Unchecked** (e.g., `ArithmeticException`, `NullPointerException`). **Step 5 (2 marks):** Differentiation table (same as Section 3.3 of these notes, abbreviated): | Aspect | Checked | Unchecked | |---|---|---| | Detected at | Compile time | Runtime | | Inherits from | `Exception` (not `RuntimeException`) | `RuntimeException` | | Compiler enforces handling? | **Yes** | No | #### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks **Step 1 (1 mark):** Program structure with method `readFile()` declaring `throws IOException`. ```java import java.io.*; class FileReadDemo { static void readFile(String filename) throws FileNotFoundException, IOException { FileReader fr = null; try { fr = new FileReader(filename); int ch; System.out.println("File contents:"); while ((ch = fr.read()) != -1) { System.out.print((char) ch); } } finally { if (fr != null) { fr.close(); System.out.println("\nFile closed successfully."); } } } public static void main(String[] args) { try { readFile("input.txt"); } catch (FileNotFoundException e) { System.out.println("Error: File not found - " + e.getMessage()); } catch (IOException e) { System.out.println("Error: I/O failure - " + e.getMessage()); } } } ``` **Step 2 (1 mark):** `readFile()` declares `throws FileNotFoundException, IOException` — using the **DECLARE** mechanism. **Step 3 (1 mark):** The `try-finally` ensures the file is closed regardless of whether `read()` throws an exception. **Step 4 (1 mark):** The `main()` method uses `try-catch` with two specific handlers — the **HANDLE** mechanism. **Step 5 (1 mark):** Role of the compiler: it verifies that both `FileNotFoundException` and `IOException` (both checked) are either caught or declared. If either is missing, `javac` produces the error *"unreported exception ... must be caught or declared to be thrown"*. **Step 6 (1 mark):** If `main` had not included the try-catch, the compiler would have demanded `main` itself include a `throws` clause. If `main` neither handled nor declared, the program would simply fail to compile. **Step 7 (1 mark):** Note: `FileNotFoundException` is a subclass of `IOException`. Java requires the more specific catch (`FileNotFoundException`) to appear **before** the more general one (`IOException`), otherwise the compiler reports *"exception FileNotFoundException has already been caught"*. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Marks-Losing Mistakes in KTU Valuation:** > > 1. **Forgetting the `throws` clause on a checked exception (–2 to –3 marks):** Many students write code that throws `IOException` but forget to add `throws IOException` to the method signature. The code will not compile — the examiner deducts heavily. > > 2. **Catch order violation (–1 to –2 marks):** Placing `catch (Exception e)` *before* `catch (IOException e)` causes a compile error: *"exception java.io.IOException has already been caught"*. Always list **more specific** exceptions first. > > 3. **Confusing `throw` and `throws` (–1 mark):** `throw` is a statement used inside a method body to raise an exception. `throws` is part of the method signature to declare exceptions. Spelling confusion is a frequent deduction. > > 4. **Writing `catch` without `(ExceptionType e)` parameter (–1 mark):** A `catch` block must have a parameter; otherwise it is a syntax error. > > 5. **Omitting the `finally` resource cleanup (–1 mark):** When asked to read a file in the KTU exam, you are expected to close the file in the `finally` block. Examiners explicitly check for this. > > 6. **Forgetting that custom exceptions must extend `Exception` (not `Throwable` directly) for checked behaviour (–1 mark):** Extending `Throwable` works at compile time but is poor practice. Always extend `Exception` for a checked custom exception. > > 7. **Writing checked-exception code inside a `catch` block without re-handling (–1 mark):** If a `try` with `catch (IOException e)` is followed by code that also throws `IOException`, students often forget the *new* code is *not* protected by the previous catch. They must add another try-catch or a `throws`. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Checked Exceptions (Module 3)** - A **checked exception** is a subclass of `java.lang.Exception` but **NOT** of `java.lang.RuntimeException`. - Checked exceptions are **enforced at compile time** by the `javac` compiler. - The **Handle OR Declare (HoD) Rule** is the central principle: every method that may throw a checked exception must either `try-catch` it or `throws`-declare it. - The five keywords are: **`try`**, **`catch`**, **`finally`**, **`throw`**, **`throws`**. - `try` *must* be followed by at least one `catch` or `finally`. - `finally` always executes (cleanup), unless `System.exit()` is called or the JVM crashes. - The `throws` keyword is used in the **method signature**; `throw` is used **inside the method body**. - Multiple `catch` blocks must be ordered from **most specific** to **most general** exception type. - A `catch (Exception e)` clause is the most general and must come **last**. - Custom checked exceptions are created by extending `java.lang.Exception`. - Common checked exceptions: `IOException`, `FileNotFoundException`, `SQLException`, `ClassNotFoundException`, `InterruptedException`, `ParseException`, `CloneNotSupportedException`. - `Error` and `RuntimeException` are **NOT** checked — the compiler does not enforce their handling. - A checked exception can propagate up the **call stack** if no method in the chain handles it; the JVM terminates the program if `main` does not handle it. - **`try-with-resources`** is a JDK 7+ feature that auto-closes resources implementing `AutoCloseable` — highly preferred for I/O in KTU practical exams.

topics.title: Unchecked Exceptions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT414 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Unchecked Exceptions # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition An **Unchecked Exception** in Java is any exception that extends `java.lang.RuntimeException` (or `java.lang.Error`), and therefore is **NOT verified by the compiler** during the compile-time phase. The Java compiler does not force the programmer to either *catch* the exception using a `try-catch` block or *declare* it in the method signature using the `throws` keyword. Such exceptions manifest only when the program is actually executed and an exceptional condition is triggered at runtime. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEECT414 – Module 3):** > The OOP module classifies exceptions into three families: > 1. **Checked Exceptions** (Compile-time) — e.g., `IOException`, `SQLException` > 2. **Unchecked Exceptions** (Runtime) — e.g., `ArithmeticException`, `NullPointerException` > 3. **Errors** — e.g., `OutOfMemoryError`, `StackOverflowError` > > Unchecked exceptions are *subclasses* of `RuntimeException`, which itself extends `Exception`. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are driving a car on a highway: - A **Checked Exception** is like a *traffic signal that turns red before you reach the intersection* — the compiler (traffic system) warns you ahead of time, and you *must* stop (handle it) or announce you will stop later (`throws`). - An **Unchecked Exception** is like a *sudden pothole on the road* — the road-construction authority (compiler) did not mark it, and you only discover it when your car tire hits it at runtime. You *may* choose to wear a seatbelt (`try-catch`) or drive carefully (`input validation`), but no one legally forces you to. > [!NOTE] > **Simple Definition (Board-Exam Ready):** > *"An unchecked exception is a runtime exception that the Java compiler does not require to be caught or declared. It typically indicates programming bugs such as logic errors, invalid arguments, or illegal operations."* ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Categories | Metric / Property | Value / Category | |---|---| | **Base Class** | `java.lang.RuntimeException` | | **Detection Phase** | Runtime (JVM) | | **Compiler Enforcement** | **None** (no `try-catch` or `throws` mandate) | | **Origin** | Usually programmer logic errors | | **Recoverability** | Often non-recoverable; indicates code must be fixed | | **Package** | `java.lang` (most common subclasses) | --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Exception Class Hierarchy (Theory) The Java exception framework follows a strict class hierarchy rooted at `java.lang.Throwable`. The `Throwable` class has two direct descendants: 1. **`Error`** — Represents serious, abnormal conditions (e.g., `OutOfMemoryError`, `VirtualMachineError`) that an application **should not** attempt to catch. These are unchecked. 2. **`Exception`** — Represents conditions that a reasonable program *might* want to catch. It has two main sub-branches: - **Checked branch** → `IOException`, `ClassNotFoundException`, `SQLException`, etc. (subclasses of `Exception` directly). - **Unchecked branch** → `RuntimeException` and all its subclasses. ### Why "Unchecked"? The term *unchecked* is defined by the **Java Language Specification (JLS)** with respect to compile-time analysis: - A Java compiler must perform **reachability and checked-exception analysis** on every method body. - If a statement can throw a **checked exception**, the compiler mandates that the method either enclose it in a `try-catch` block or declare it in the `throws` clause. - For **RuntimeException and its subclasses**, this analysis is **skipped** — hence the name "unchecked." ## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" — Operational Logic **Why do unchecked exceptions exist?** - They represent **programming errors** that a well-written program should *prevent*, not *handle*. - Forcing every potential division-by-zero or null dereference to be wrapped in `try-catch` would clutter valid code with boilerplate. - The JVM detects them at runtime because they depend on **dynamic data** (e.g., actual divisor value, actual array length). **How does the JVM handle them?** - When an unchecked exception is thrown and no surrounding `try-catch` block catches it, the exception **propagates up the call stack**. - If it reaches the main thread unhandled, the JVM **terminates the thread** and prints a stack trace to `System.err`. ## 2.3 Common Unchecked Exception Classes (KTU High-Yield List) The following are the subclasses of `RuntimeException` that appear most frequently in KTU question papers: | Exception | Cause | Example Trigger | |---|---|---| | `ArithmeticException` | Invalid arithmetic operation | `int x = 10 / 0;` | | `NullPointerException` (NPE) | Accessing method/field on a null reference | `String s = null; s.length();` | | `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` | Array index ≥ length or < 0 | `a[5]` on array of size 5 | | `IndexOutOfBoundsException` | Invalid index for list/string | `list.get(10)` on size-3 list | | `StringIndexOutOfBoundsException` | Invalid index on a String | `"hello".charAt(10)` | | `ClassCastException` | Invalid type cast | `(Integer) obj` where `obj` is a `String` | | `IllegalArgumentException` | Method received an unacceptable argument | `Thread.sleep(-5);` | | `NumberFormatException` | String-to-number conversion failure | `Integer.parseInt("abc");` | | `UnsupportedOperationException` | Operation not supported | Modifying an immutable collection | | `IllegalStateException` | Method invoked at wrong time | Starting a stopped thread | > [!NOTE] > **Important Distinction:** > `NumberFormatException` is a subclass of `IllegalArgumentException`, which is itself a subclass of `RuntimeException`. All are **unchecked**. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Rule / Formula | |---|---| | **Mandatory catch rule** | NOT required for unchecked exceptions | | **Mandatory throws rule** | NOT required for unchecked exceptions | | **Base class** | `RuntimeException` | | **Detection** | Runtime (JVM) | | **Programmer responsibility** | Prevent via input validation / assertions | | **Optional handling** | Can be caught, but optional | | **Top-level superclass** | `java.lang.Throwable` | | **Common trigger types** | `null` reference, divide-by-zero, bad index, bad cast | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Utility in Engineering:** > In production-grade software (banking systems, embedded controllers, IoT firmware), unchecked exceptions often signal **logic flaws** that should be caught during unit testing. Modern frameworks like **Spring Boot** use global exception handlers (`@ControllerAdvice`) to translate unchecked exceptions into proper HTTP error responses (e.g., 400 Bad Request for `IllegalArgumentException`). --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the "Unchecked" Property — Logical Proof The Java compiler's checking procedure for a method `M` can be formalized as follows: **Step 1 — Identify all statements in `M` that may throw an exception.** The compiler analyses each expression and statement, identifying reachable exception-throwing operations (method calls, divisions, array accesses, casts, etc.). **Step 2 — Classify each potentially thrown exception.** For each exception `E` that *may* be thrown: - If `E` is a subclass of `RuntimeException` or `Error` → **unchecked** → skip Step 3. - Otherwise (i.e., `E` is a checked exception) → proceed to Step 3. **Step 3 — Verify handling or declaration (for checked exceptions only).** For each checked exception `E`: - Either there must exist an enclosing `try` block whose corresponding `catch` clause catches `E` (or a superclass of `E`). - **OR** the method `M` must declare `throws E` in its signature. **Step 4 — Compilation decision.** - If all checked exceptions are handled or declared, **compilation succeeds**. - Otherwise, the compiler emits an **error** (e.g., *"unreported exception IOException; must be caught or declared to be thrown"*). **Conclusion (Logical Result):** Since unchecked exceptions are filtered out at Step 2, neither catching nor declaring them is required. This is the formal justification for the term *unchecked*. $$ \text{Exception Verdict} = \begin{cases} \text{Checked} \rightarrow \text{Require catch} \lor \text{throws} \\ \text{Unchecked (Runtime/Error)} \rightarrow \text{No requirement} \end{cases} $$ ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — When Each Unchecked Exception is Triggered Let us define the exception-verdict function for an operation `op` with inputs $I_1, I_2, \dots, I_n$: $$ E(op) = \begin{cases} \text{ArithmeticException} & \text{if } op = \text{division and } \text{denominator} = 0 \\ \text{NullPointerException} & \text{if } op \text{ dereferences a null reference} \\ \text{ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException} & \text{if } \text{index} \notin [0, \text{length}-1] \\ \text{ClassCastException} & \text{if } \text{dynamic type} \neq \text{target cast type} \\ \text{NumberFormatException} & \text{if } \text{parseInt/parseDouble fails} \\ \text{IllegalArgumentException} & \text{if } \text{input violates precondition} \end{cases} $$ ### Worked Example: Triggering `ArithmeticException` **Program code:** ```java public class Demo { public static void main(String[] args) { int a = 10; int b = 0; int c = a / b; // Line triggers exception System.out.println(c); } } ``` **Step-by-step evaluation:** **Step 1:** The JVM reaches the expression `a / b`. **Step 2:** It evaluates the operands: `a = 10`, `b = 0`. **Step 3:** It performs integer division. Detects that the right operand equals 0. **Step 4:** The JVM constructs a new `ArithmeticException("/ by zero")` object and **throws** it. **Step 5:** Since there is no surrounding `try-catch`, the exception propagates up to `main()`, then to the JVM. **Step 6:** The JVM prints the stack trace: $$ \text{Exception in thread "main" java.lang.ArithmeticException: / by zero} $$ **Step 7:** The program terminates abnormally. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** This code **compiles successfully** even though it crashes at runtime. This is the defining property of an unchecked exception. ## 3.3 Comparison Matrix: Checked vs. Unchecked Exceptions | Property | Checked Exception | Unchecked Exception | |---|---|---| | **Base class** | `Exception` (not `RuntimeException`) | `RuntimeException` | | **Compile-time check** | Yes — **mandatory** | No | | **Runtime check** | Yes | Yes | | **Typical cause** | External resources (file, network, DB) | Programmer logic error | | **Recoverable?** | Usually yes | Often no — fix the code | | **Examples** | `IOException`, `SQLException`, `ClassNotFoundException` | `ArithmeticException`, `NPE`, `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` | | **KTU exam weightage** | High (Module 3) | High (Module 3) | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Java Exception Class Hierarchy ```mermaid graph TD A[java.lang.Throwable] --> B[java.lang.Exception] A --> C[java.lang.Error] B --> D[java.lang.RuntimeException] B --> E[Checked Exceptions
IOException, SQLException, ClassNotFoundException] C --> F[OutOfMemoryError] C --> G[StackOverflowError] D --> H[ArithmeticException] D --> I[NullPointerException] D --> J[ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException] D --> K[ClassCastException] D --> L[NumberFormatException] D --> M[IllegalArgumentException] M --> N[NumberFormatException] style A fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff style B fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000,color:#fff style C fill:#c62828,stroke:#000,color:#fff style D fill:#ef6c00,stroke:#000,color:#fff style E fill:#6a1b9a,stroke:#000,color:#fff style F fill:#b71c1c,stroke:#000,color:#fff style G fill:#b71c1c,stroke:#000,color:#fff style H fill:#f9a825,stroke:#000 style I fill:#f9a825,stroke:#000 style J fill:#f9a825,stroke:#000 style K fill:#f9a825,stroke:#000 style L fill:#f9a825,stroke:#000 style M fill:#f9a825,stroke:#000 style N fill:#f9a825,stroke:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Runtime Exception Propagation Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Method A executes
division operation] --> P2{Is divisor zero?} P2 -- Yes --> P3[JVM throws
ArithmeticException] P2 -- No --> P4[Normal execution continues] P3 --> P5{Is there a
try-catch block?} P5 -- Yes --> P6[Catch block executes
exception handled] P5 -- No --> P7[Exception propagates
to caller Method B] P7 --> P8{Does Method B
have try-catch?} P8 -- Yes --> P6 P8 -- No --> P9[Propagates to main method] P9 --> P10{Does main have
try-catch?} P10 -- Yes --> P6 P10 -- No --> P11[JVM prints stack trace
and terminates thread] style P3 fill:#c62828,stroke:#000,color:#fff style P6 fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000,color:#fff style P11 fill:#b71c1c,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Block-Level Architecture: Compile-Time vs Runtime Check ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph CompileTime[COMPILE PHASE - javac] C1[Source Code .java] --> C2[Parser and Type Checker] C2 --> C3{Throws Checked
Exception?} C3 -- Yes --> C4{Catch or declare
via throws?} C4 -- No --> C5[COMPILE ERROR] C4 -- Yes --> C6[Bytecode .class generated] C3 -- No --> C6 C2 --> C7[Unchecked Exception
NOT analysed] C7 --> C6 end subgraph RunTime[RUNTIME PHASE - JVM] C6 --> R1[Classloader loads class] R1 --> R2[Bytecode execution begins] R2 --> R3[Runtime condition occurs
e.g. divide by zero] R3 --> R4[Throws unchecked exception] R4 --> R5{Catch block
present?} R5 -- Yes --> R6[Handled gracefully] R5 -- No --> R7[Stack trace printed
Thread terminates] end style C5 fill:#c62828,stroke:#000,color:#fff style R4 fill:#ef6c00,stroke:#000,color:#fff style R7 fill:#b71c1c,stroke:#000,color:#fff style R6 fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Define unchecked exceptions. List any four common unchecked exceptions in Java.** **Model Answer:** An **unchecked exception** is a runtime exception that the Java compiler does not require to be caught or declared. It is a subclass of `java.lang.RuntimeException` and is detected only at runtime by the JVM. Four common unchecked exceptions: 1. `ArithmeticException` — e.g., division by zero 2. `NullPointerException` — e.g., accessing a method on a null reference 3. `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` — e.g., invalid array index 4. `NumberFormatException` — e.g., converting a non-numeric string to integer --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **Why are unchecked exceptions called "unchecked"? How do they differ from checked exceptions?** **Model Answer:** Unchecked exceptions are called **"unchecked"** because the Java compiler does **not verify (check)** at compile time whether they are handled or declared. The compiler skips its checked-exception analysis for any subclass of `RuntimeException` or `Error`. **Key Differences:** | Aspect | Checked | Unchecked | |---|---|---| | Base class | `Exception` | `RuntimeException` | | Compiler enforcement | Mandatory catch/throws | Not enforced | | Detection | Compile-time + runtime | Runtime only | | Typical cause | External resources | Programmer logic errors | | Example | `IOException` | `ArithmeticException` | --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Format ### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the Java exception class hierarchy with a neat diagram. Distinguish between checked and unchecked exceptions. **(b) [7 Marks]** Write a Java program that demonstrates the occurrence of `ArithmeticException`, `NullPointerException`, and `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`. Use a single `try-catch` block with multiple `catch` clauses to handle each. #### Model Solution for (a): The Java exception hierarchy is rooted at `java.lang.Throwable`, which has two direct subclasses: `Exception` and `Error`. - **`Exception`** is the superclass for all exceptions a program should handle. - **Checked exceptions** (subclasses of `Exception` but NOT `RuntimeException`): `IOException`, `ClassNotFoundException`, `SQLException`. The compiler forces handling. - **Unchecked exceptions** (subclasses of `RuntimeException`): `ArithmeticException`, `NullPointerException`, `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`. No compiler enforcement. - **`Error`** represents critical, unrecoverable problems like `OutOfMemoryError` and `StackOverflowError`. Programs should not catch these. **Distinction Table:** | Property | Checked | Unchecked | |---|---|---| | Base class | `Exception` | `RuntimeException` | | Compiler check | Yes | No | | Example | `IOException` | `ArithmeticException` | #### Model Solution for (b): ```java public class UncheckedDemo { public static void main(String[] args) { try { int a = 10 / 0; // ArithmeticException String s = null; System.out.println(s.length()); // NullPointerException int[] arr = new int[3]; System.out.println(arr[5]); // ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException } catch (ArithmeticException e) { System.out.println("Caught ArithmeticException: " + e.getMessage()); } catch (NullPointerException e) { System.out.println("Caught NullPointerException: " + e.getMessage()); } catch (ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException e) { System.out.println("Caught ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException: " + e.getMessage()); } } } ``` **Output (Step-by-Step Execution):** **Step 1:** The JVM enters the `try` block. **Step 2:** It evaluates `10 / 0`. Detects division by zero. Constructs and throws `ArithmeticException("/ by zero")`. **Step 3:** Control jumps to the first matching `catch` block: `catch (ArithmeticException e)`. **Step 4:** Message is printed. The remaining statements inside `try` are **not** executed (control has left the `try` block). **Final Output:** $$ \text{Caught ArithmeticException: / by zero} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Why don't the other two exceptions execute?** > Once an exception is thrown inside a `try` block, the JVM **immediately** transfers control to the matching `catch`. The remaining statements in the `try` block are skipped. To demonstrate all three exceptions, the code must be structured as **three separate try-catch blocks**, or each risky statement must be isolated. ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2** **[KTU University Exam – June 2023]** **(a) [7 Marks]** What is a `NullPointerException`? Write a Java program to demonstrate how it can be prevented using input validation. **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain with examples how `NumberFormatException` and `ClassCastException` occur. How are they classified in terms of checked vs unchecked? #### Model Solution for (a): A **`NullPointerException` (NPE)** is an unchecked exception thrown by the JVM when an application attempts to use a null reference where an object is required — for example, calling a method, accessing a field, or checking the length of a null array. **Prevention via Input Validation:** ```java import java.util.Scanner; public class NPEPrevent { public static void main(String[] args) { Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in); System.out.print("Enter a string (or press Enter for null): "); String input = sc.nextLine(); String name; if (input == null || input.isEmpty()) { name = "DefaultUser"; } else { name = input; } if (name != null) { System.out.println("Length of name: " + name.length()); } else { System.out.println("Name is null — cannot compute length."); } } } ``` **Explanation:** - **Step 1:** Read user input. - **Step 2:** If input is null or empty, assign a safe default. - **Step 3:** Before calling `.length()`, verify the string is non-null using an `if` guard. - **Step 4:** This preventive check eliminates the risk of `NullPointerException`. #### Model Solution for (b): **`NumberFormatException` — Example:** ```java public class NFEExample { public static void main(String[] args) { String s = "abc"; int n = Integer.parseInt(s); // Throws NumberFormatException } } ``` **Step-by-step:** **Step 1:** `Integer.parseInt("abc")` attempts to convert the string "abc" to an integer. **Step 2:** The method scans the characters and finds that 'a' is not a valid digit. **Step 3:** It throws `NumberFormatException("For input string: \"abc\"")`. **`ClassCastException` — Example:** ```java public class CCEExample { public static void main(String[] args) { Object obj = "Hello World"; Integer n = (Integer) obj; // Throws ClassCastException } } ``` **Step-by-step:** **Step 1:** Variable `obj` is declared as `Object` but holds a `String` reference at runtime. **Step 2:** The cast `(Integer) obj` instructs the JVM to check the runtime type. **Step 3:** The actual type is `String`, which is **not** assignable to `Integer`. **Step 4:** The JVM throws `ClassCastException`. **Classification:** Both `NumberFormatException` and `ClassCastException` are **unchecked** exceptions because: - `NumberFormatException` extends `IllegalArgumentException` → which extends `RuntimeException`. - `ClassCastException` directly extends `RuntimeException`. Therefore, neither is checked at compile time. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing `NumberFormatException` with `IOException`.** Many students wrongly classify it as checked. *Remember:* it extends `RuntimeException` → **unchecked**. > 2. **Forgetting that `try-catch` is OPTIONAL for unchecked exceptions.** Writing a `try-catch` is *allowed*, but the compiler will *not force* you to. Do not claim it is mandatory. > 3. **Wrong hierarchy in diagrams.** `RuntimeException` extends `Exception` (NOT `Throwable` directly). Marks are deducted for wrong arrows. > 4. **Spelling errors** in class names like `NullPointerExecption` or `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundException` cost marks. > 5. **Not mentioning that `Error` and its subclasses (e.g., `OutOfMemoryError`) are also technically "unchecked."** A complete answer must include this. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Unchecked Exception** = any subclass of `java.lang.RuntimeException` (and `Error`). - The Java compiler **does NOT** enforce catching or declaring unchecked exceptions. - Unchecked exceptions are detected **only at runtime** by the JVM. - They usually represent **programmer errors** — fix the code rather than catch the exception. - **Key hierarchy:** `Throwable` → `Exception` → `RuntimeException` → specific subclasses. - **Most-tested unchecked exceptions in KTU exams:** - `ArithmeticException` (divide by zero) - `NullPointerException` (NPE) - `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` - `NumberFormatException` - `ClassCastException` - `IndexOutOfBoundsException` - `NumberFormatException` is a subclass of `IllegalArgumentException`, which is a subclass of `RuntimeException` → all **unchecked**. - `Error` and its subclasses (`OutOfMemoryError`, `StackOverflowError`) are technically unchecked but **should not be caught** in application code. - **Best Practice:** Prevent unchecked exceptions using: - Input validation (check `null`, check array bounds, check divisor ≠ 0). - Use `Optional` to avoid nulls in modern Java. - Use `try-catch` only at boundaries where recovery is meaningful. - **Compile-time vs Runtime:** Checked = compile-time + runtime. Unchecked = runtime only. - The `throws` clause is **optional** for unchecked exceptions; the compiler will not complain if it is omitted.

topics.title: try Block and catch Clause
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT414 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** try Block and catch Clause

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) under the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, **Exception Handling** is a robust mechanism that allows a program to detect, intercept, and recover from **runtime anomalies (exceptions)** without abrupt termination. The two foundational pillars of this mechanism in **C++** are:

1. **The `try` Block** — A guarded region of code in which the programmer *anticipates* the possibility of an exceptional condition. Any statement that may throw an exception is enclosed within a `try` block.
2. **The `catch` Clause (Handler)** — A specialized function-like block that *catches and processes* an exception thrown from its corresponding `try` block. Each `catch` handler is identified by a parameter list specifying the **type of exception** it can handle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (Verbatim Expectation):**
> *"A `try` block is a compound statement preceded by the keyword `try` that encloses code which may generate exceptions. A `catch` clause is a handler that declares the type of exception it can intercept, allowing the program to maintain control flow even after an abnormal event occurs."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Safety Net" of a Circus

Imagine a **trapeze artist** performing high above the ground.

- The `try` block is the **trapeze act itself** — the risky performance where something *could* go wrong (a slip, a miss).
- The `catch` block is the **safety net** stretched below. It does *nothing* while the artist performs flawlessly, but the moment something goes wrong (an exception is thrown), the net instantly activates and catches the performer.
- The **type of net** (the parameter in `catch`) matters — a net designed for a human won't catch a falling bicycle. Hence, catch handlers are **type-specific**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The `try` block does **NOT** specify what exception to catch. It only marks the *region* under surveillance. The `catch` clause is what *identifies* and *processes* the exception. The pairing happens by **proximity and type matching**, not by explicit naming.

### 1.3 The Three-Token Vocabulary (Prerequisite Building Blocks)

| Token | Role | What It Does NOT Do |
|---|---|---|
| `try` | Marks the **guarded region** | Does not catch anything |
| `throw` | **Raises** an exception | Does not handle anything |
| `catch` | **Handles** the thrown exception | Does not raise anything |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Favorite Line:** *"Without `throw`, there is nothing for `catch` to catch. Without `try`, there is no region to throw from. Without `catch`, a thrown exception becomes an **uncaught exception**, leading to program termination."*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The `try` Block — Structural Anatomy

A `try` block is syntactically a **compound statement** (a block of code enclosed in `{ }`) prefixed by the keyword `try`.

```cpp
try {
    // Statement 1
    // Statement 2 (may throw)
    // Statement 3
}
```

**Operational Characteristics:**
- It is the **only place** where the C++ compiler implicitly links to exception-handling machinery.
- The `try` block itself is **passive** — it does not filter, catch, or react to errors.
- A `try` block must be immediately followed by **at least one `catch` handler**. The C++ compiler will reject code with a `try` but no `catch`.
- `try` blocks can be **nested** — an inner `try` may be enclosed within an outer `try`.

### 2.2 The `catch` Clause — Structural Anatomy

```cpp
catch (ExceptionType1 e1) {
    // Recovery code for type 1
}
catch (ExceptionType2 e2) {
    // Recovery code for type 2
}
```

**Operational Characteristics:**
- The **single parameter** in the catch declaration is the **type signature** the handler responds to.
- When an exception is thrown, the runtime searches the `catch` chain **sequentially (top-to-bottom)** and invokes the **first matching handler**.
- A `catch(...)` (catch-all handler) is a **universal handler** that matches any exception type. It must be placed **last** in the chain.
- A `catch` parameter can be a **reference** (e.g., `catch (MyException &e)`) to avoid object slicing — a KTU favorite concept.

### 2.3 Control Flow Mechanics — The Five-Stage Lifecycle

1. **Normal Flow:** Program executes statements inside the `try` block linearly.
2. **Exception Detection:** Either the runtime (e.g., `throw new int;` or built-in `throw`) or the program's own logic (`throw MyException("error");`) raises an exception.
3. **Stack Unwinding Begins:** The runtime **destroys all local objects** in the `try` block (from the point of throw to the end of the try) — invoking destructors of any constructed objects in reverse order.
4. **Handler Search:** The runtime scans the `catch` handlers associated with the `try` block. Matching is based on **type compatibility** (exact match or base class match).
5. **Handler Execution & Resumption:** The matching `catch` block executes. Control then transfers to the statement **immediately after the last `catch` handler** in the chain.

### 2.4 The Critical Rule — "Catch by Reference"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Rule:** Always catch exceptions **by reference** (`catch (Base &e)`), never by value (`catch (Base e)`), to prevent **object slicing** when polymorphic types are involved.

**Object Slicing Illustrated:**
- If a `Derived` exception is thrown but caught by value as `Base`, the derived portion of the object is **sliced off**, losing polymorphic behavior.
- Catching by reference preserves the full object.

### 2.5 KTU Formula / Concept Sheet

| Concept | Rule / Syntax | KTU Board Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Try block syntax | `try { /* code */ }` | Marks guarded region |
| Catch syntax | `catch (Type name) { /* handler */ }` | Type-specific handler |
| Catch-all | `catch (...) { /* universal */ }` | Must be last; catches any throw |
| Matching rule | First match wins (top-down) | Order of catch matters |
| Parameter passing | `catch (Type &e)` preferred | Prevents object slicing |
| Re-throwing | `throw;` (no operand) | Forwards same exception up |
| Nested try | `try { try { } catch { } } catch { }` | Inner catch can re-throw to outer |
| Stack unwinding | Automatic destructor calls | Critical for resource management (RAII) |
| Uncaught exception | Program calls `std::terminate()` | Leads to abnormal termination |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Exception handling with `try`/`catch` is the **backbone of fault-tolerant systems**:
- **Embedded systems** in aerospace (e.g., flight control software) use try/catch to recover from sensor glitches without rebooting.
- **Banking software** catches `InsufficientFundsException` to prevent transactions and log errors.
- **Network servers** wrap I/O operations in try/catch to handle connection drops gracefully, keeping the server alive.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Division by Zero Handler

Let us derive the complete behavior of a `try`/`catch` block using a **division-by-zero** scenario. We will trace the control flow step-by-step.

**Source Code:**

```cpp
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

int main() {
    int a = 10, b = 0, result;
    
    try {
        if (b == 0)
            throw "Division by zero error!";  // throws a const char*
        result = a / b;
        cout << "Result: " << result << endl;
    }
    catch (const char* msg) {
        cout << "Exception Caught: " << msg << endl;
    }
    catch (...) {
        cout << "Unknown exception occurred." << endl;
    }
    
    cout << "Program continues..." << endl;
    return 0;
}
```

**Step-by-Step Execution Trace:**

| Step | Action | State |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `int a = 10, b = 0;` | Variables initialized |
| 2 | Enter `try` block | Control inside guarded region |
| 3 | Evaluate `if (b == 0)` | Condition is `true` |
| 4 | `throw "Division by zero error!";` | Exception of type `const char*` raised |
| 5 | Stack unwinding begins | No local objects to destroy in this try |
| 6 | Runtime scans catch handlers | Handler 1: `catch (const char* msg)` |
| 7 | Type match? `const char* == const char*` | **YES — exact match** |
| 8 | `msg` bound to the thrown string literal | Handler executes |
| 9 | Print "Exception Caught: ..." | Handler body completes |
| 10 | Skip all subsequent catch handlers | Control jumps to line after last catch |
| 11 | Print "Program continues..." | Normal flow resumes |
| 12 | `return 0;` | Program terminates normally |

**Final Output:**

```
Exception Caught: Division by zero error!
Program continues...
```

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Multi-Type Catch Chain

This example demonstrates the **first-match rule** and the role of the **catch-all handler**.

```cpp
#include <iostream>
#include <stdexcept>
using namespace std;

void test(int x) {
    try {
        if (x == 1) throw 100;              // throws int
        if (x == 2) throw "String error";   // throws const char*
        if (x == 3) throw runtime_error("Runtime issue");  // throws STL exception
        cout << "No exception for x = " << x << endl;
    }
    catch (int e) {
        cout << "Integer exception: " << e << endl;
    }
    catch (const char* e) {
        cout << "String exception: " << e << endl;
    }
    catch (exception& e) {
        cout << "STL exception: " << e.what() << endl;
    }
    catch (...) {
        cout << "Catch-all: unknown exception" << endl;
    }
}
```

**Analytical Matrix — Behavior for Each Input:**

$$
\text{Handler Resolution} = f(x) =
\begin{cases}
\text{catch (int e)} & \text{if } x = 1 \\[6pt]
\text{catch (const char* e)} & \text{if } x = 2 \\[6pt]
\text{catch (exception\& e)} & \text{if } x = 3 \\[6pt]
\text{No exception path} & \text{if } x \notin \{1, 2, 3\} \\[6pt]
\text{catch (...)} & \text{for any other unlisted type}
\end{cases}
$$

**Resolution Order Justification:**
- For $x = 1$: The thrown object is `int`. The **first** catch matching `int` is `catch (int e)`. It fires.
- For $x = 2$: The thrown object is `const char*`. The first catch matching it is `catch (const char* e)`. It fires.
- For $x = 3$: `runtime_error` inherits from `exception`. The first catch matching it is `catch (exception& e)` (by reference, no slicing). It fires.
- For $x = 4$: No exception thrown. The `try` block completes normally; all `catch` blocks are skipped.

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Nested try/catch with Re-throw

```cpp
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

int main() {
    try {                                    // OUTER try
        try {                                // INNER try
            throw 50;
        }
        catch (int e) {
            cout << "Inner catch: " << e << endl;
            throw;                           // RE-THROW the same exception
        }
    }
    catch (int e) {
        cout << "Outer catch: " << e << endl;
    }
    return 0;
}
```

**Execution Trace:**

1. Outer `try` entered.
2. Inner `try` entered.
3. `throw 50;` raises an `int` exception.
4. Inner `catch (int e)` matches → prints "Inner catch: 50".
5. `throw;` (bare throw) **re-throws the same exception** ($e = 50$) to the outer scope.
6. The inner `try/catch` block is **exited** (no further inner code runs).
7. Outer `catch (int e)` matches → prints "Outer catch: 50".

**Output:**

```
Inner catch: 50
Outer catch: 50
```

### 3.4 The Matching Algorithm — Formal Specification

The C++ runtime's catch-matching algorithm can be expressed as a decision function:

$$
\text{Match}(T_{\text{throw}}, T_{\text{catch}_i}) =
\begin{cases}
1 & \text{if } T_{\text{throw}} = T_{\text{catch}_i} \quad \text{(exact type match)} \\[4pt]
1 & \text{if } T_{\text{catch}_i} \text{ is an unambiguous public base of } T_{\text{throw}} \\[4pt]
1 & \text{if } T_{\text{catch}_i} = T_{\text{throw}}* \text{ and throw is a pointer} \\[4pt]
0 & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

The first $i$ for which $\text{Match} = 1$ wins. The `catch(...)` handler has an implicit $\text{Match} = 1$ for all $T_{\text{throw}}$ and therefore must be placed **last**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The try/catch Control Flow — Top-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Program Start]) --> B[Enter try Block]
    B --> C{Statement Executes}
    C -->|No Exception| D[Continue to Next Statement]
    C -->|Exception Thrown| E[Stack Unwinding]
    E --> F{Scan catch Handlers}
    F -->|Handler 1 Match| G[Execute Handler 1]
    F -->|Handler 2 Match| H[Execute Handler 2]
    F -->|No Match Found| I[Propagate to Outer try]
    I -->|Outer try Exists| F
    I -->|No Outer try| J[std::terminate Called]
    G --> K([Code After Last catch])
    H --> K
    D --> K
    K --> L([Program Continues])
    J --> M([Abnormal Termination])
```

### 4.2 Sequential Catch Resolution Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0([Exception of Type T Thrown]) --> P1{catch T1 e1}
    P1 -->|Match| P2A[Handler 1 Executes]
    P1 -->|No Match| P3{catch T2 e2}
    P3 -->|Match| P4A[Handler 2 Executes]
    P3 -->|No Match| P5{catch Base e}
    P5 -->|T isDerivedFrom Base| P6A[Handler 3 Executes]
    P5 -->|No Match| P7{catch ellipsis}
    P7 -->|Universal Match| P8A[Catch-All Executes]
    P7 -->|Always Matches| P8A
    P2A --> P9([Skip Remaining])
    P4A --> P9
    P6A --> P9
    P8A --> P9
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Nested Exception Handling

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Outer[Outer try Block] --> Inner[Inner try Block]
    Inner --> Code1[Risky Statement 1]
    Inner --> Code2[Risky Statement 2]
    Code2 --> ThrowOp{Exception Raised?}
    ThrowOp -->|Yes| Unwind[Stack Unwinding in Inner Scope]
    Unwind --> InnerCatch[Inner catch Handler]
    InnerCatch --> Rethrow{Re-throw?}
    Rethrow -->|Yes throw| OuterUnwind[Propagate to Outer Scope]
    Rethrow -->|No| OuterResume[Resume in Outer]
    OuterUnwind --> OuterCatch[Outer catch Handler]
    OuterCatch --> OuterResume
    ThrowOp -->|No| InnerDone[Inner Block Completes]
    InnerDone --> OuterDone[Outer Block Completes]
    OuterResume --> FinalCode[Code After All Handlers]
    OuterDone --> FinalCode
```

### 4.4 Object Slicing Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DerivedObj["Derived Object (Thrown)"]
        D1[Base Part]
        D2[Derived Part]
    end
    DerivedObj -->|Caught by Value: catch Base e| SlicedObj["Sliced Object"]
    SlicedObj --> S3[Base Part Only]
    SlicedObj -.->|Derived Part LOST| X[Discarded]
    DerivedObj -->|Caught by Reference: catch Base and e| FullObj["Complete Object Preserved"]
    FullObj --> F1[Base Part]
    FullObj --> F2[Derived Part]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> **Q1. Differentiate between a `try` block and a `catch` clause in C++ exception handling.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | `try` Block | `catch` Clause |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Marks the region of code under surveillance | Handles the exception thrown from the `try` block |
| **Keyword** | Begins with `try` | Begins with `catch` |
| **Parameter** | Takes no parameter | Takes an exception type (or `...`) as parameter |
| **Role** | Passive — does not catch anything | Active — intercepts and processes the exception |
| **Count** | One per guarded region | One or more per `try` block |
| **Execution** | Runs during normal program flow | Runs only when a matching exception is thrown |

**(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for tabular difference, 1 mark for concluding statement.)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **Q2. What is the significance of `catch(...)` in C++? Where should it be placed in a catch chain?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- The `catch(...)` clause is a **catch-all handler** that matches **any type of exception** not caught by the preceding specific handlers. (1 mark)
- It acts as a **safety net** for unanticipated exceptions, preventing program termination due to uncaught exceptions. (1 mark)
- It **must be placed last** in the catch chain because the C++ runtime matches handlers sequentially (first match wins). If placed first, it would intercept all exceptions, making subsequent handlers unreachable. (1 mark)

---

### Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question as per 2024 Scheme]**
>
> **Question A (14 Marks):**
> **(a)** Explain the concept of exception handling in C++ with a neat diagram showing the `try`, `throw`, and `catch` mechanism. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Write a C++ program that defines a custom exception class `NegativeNumberException`. The program should read an integer from the user, throw the exception if the number is negative, and catch it to display an appropriate error message. Demonstrate what happens when the exception is NOT caught. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — Concept and Diagram (7 Marks):**

Exception handling in C++ is a mechanism to handle **runtime errors** gracefully, allowing the program to continue execution rather than terminating abruptly. The three keywords involved are:

- **`try`**: Encloses the code that may generate an exception.
- **`throw`**: Raises an exception when an error condition is detected.
- **`catch`**: Receives and processes the exception.

**Diagram:**

```
   +-------------------+
   |   try block       |
   |  {                |
   |   ...code...      |---- throw exception ---->
   |  }                |                    |
   +-------------------+                    v
                                    +-------------------+
                                    |  Stack Unwinding  |
                                    |  (destructors     |
                                    |   called)         |
                                    +-------------------+
                                              |
                                              v
   +-------------------+      +-------------------+
   |  catch (Type1 e)  |  OR  |  catch (Type2 e)  |  OR  | catch(...)   |
   |  { ...handle... } |      |  { ...handle... } |      | { ...all...} |
   +-------------------+      +-------------------+      +--------------+
                                              |
                                              v
                                  Code After All catch Blocks
```

**Control Flow Description (3 marks for explanation):**
1. Code inside `try` executes normally.
2. If an abnormal condition arises, `throw` is executed.
3. The runtime performs **stack unwinding**, destroying local objects.
4. The thrown object is matched against catch handlers in order.
5. The first matching `catch` executes; remaining handlers are skipped.
6. Control resumes after the last `catch` block.

**Part (b) — C++ Program (7 Marks):**

```cpp
#include <iostream>
#include <exception>
using namespace std;

// Custom exception class inheriting from std::exception
class NegativeNumberException : public exception {
    string message;
public:
    NegativeNumberException(const string& msg) : message(msg) {}
    const char* what() const throw() {
        return message.c_str();
    }
};

int main() {
    int num;
    cout << "Enter an integer: ";
    cin >> num;
    
    // ===== DEMO 1: Exception IS caught =====
    try {
        if (num < 0)
            throw NegativeNumberException("Negative numbers are not allowed!");
        cout << "You entered: " << num << endl;
    }
    catch (NegativeNumberException& e) {
        cout << "Exception Caught: " << e.what() << endl;
    }
    
    // ===== DEMO 2: Exception is NOT caught =====
    // Uncomment the block below to see std::terminate being called.
    /*
    if (num < 0)
        throw NegativeNumberException("This exception is uncaught!");
    */
    
    cout << "Program ended normally." << endl;
    return 0;
}
```

**Sample Outputs:**

For input `-5` (exception caught):
```
Enter an integer: -5
Exception Caught: Negative numbers are not allowed!
Program ended normally.
```

For input `10` (no exception):
```
Enter an integer: 10
You entered: 10
Program ended normally.
```

For the uncaught case (Demo 2 with `-5`): The runtime calls **`std::terminate()`**, leading to **abnormal program termination** with a message like `"terminate called after throwing an instance of 'NegativeNumberException'"`. (1 mark for explaining the uncaught behavior.)

**Mark Distribution Breakdown:**
- (2 marks) Class definition with `what()` override
- (2 marks) `try`/`throw`/`catch` usage in `main`
- (2 marks) Correct output demonstration
- (1 mark) Explanation of uncaught exception behavior

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**
> **(a)** What is stack unwinding? Explain its role in C++ exception handling with an example showing destructor invocation. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the concept of **re-throwing an exception** using the `throw;` statement. Write a C++ program demonstrating a nested `try`/`catch` where the inner catch re-throws an exception to be handled by the outer catch. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — Stack Unwinding (7 Marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Stack unwinding is the process by which the C++ runtime **destroys all automatic (local) objects** that were constructed inside the `try` block, from the point where the exception was thrown up to the point where it is caught. This ensures proper cleanup of resources.

**Role (2 marks):** It guarantees that destructors of all local objects are called, enabling **RAII (Resource Acquisition Is Initialization)** — a critical pattern for resource management (memory, file handles, locks).

**Example Program (3 marks):**

```cpp
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

class Resource {
    int id;
public:
    Resource(int i) : id(i) { cout << "Resource " << id << " acquired." << endl; }
    ~Resource() { cout << "Resource " << id << " released (destructor)." << endl; }
};

void trigger() {
    Resource r1(1);
    Resource r2(2);
    Resource r3(3);
    throw "Error in trigger()!";    // Exception thrown
    Resource r4(4);                 // Never constructed
}

int main() {
    try {
        trigger();
    }
    catch (const char* msg) {
        cout << "Caught: " << msg << endl;
    }
    return 0;
}
```

**Output:**
```
Resource 1 acquired.
Resource 2 acquired.
Resource 3 acquired.
Resource 3 released (destructor).
Resource 2 released (destructor).
Resource 1 released (destructor).
Caught: Error in trigger()!
```

Note: `r4` is **never constructed** (code after `throw` is unreachable), but `r1`, `r2`, `r3` are all **destroyed in reverse order** of construction (LIFO). This is stack unwinding.

**Part (b) — Re-throwing Exceptions (7 Marks):**

**Concept (3 marks):** A `catch` handler can re-throw the same exception it received using a **bare `throw;` statement** (with no operand). This forwards the exception to an **enclosing (outer) `try`/`catch` block**, allowing different levels of the program to apply different handling logic. The outer handler can perform higher-level recovery (e.g., logging, user notification), while the inner handler performs partial cleanup.

**Program (4 marks):**

```cpp
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

void processData(int value) {
    try {
        if (value < 0)
            throw value;          // Inner throw
        cout << "Valid value: " << value << endl;
    }
    catch (int e) {
        cout << "[Inner] Partial handling for value: " << e << endl;
        throw;                    // Re-throw to outer
    }
}

int main() {
    try {
        processData(-5);
    }
    catch (int e) {
        cout << "[Outer] Full recovery for value: " << e << endl;
    }
    cout << "Program continues normally." << endl;
    return 0;
}
```

**Output:**
```
[Inner] Partial handling for value: -5
[Outer] Full recovery for value: -5
Program continues normally.
```

**Key Distinction (KTU 2024):**
- `throw e;` → throws a **copy** of `e` (loses original type information if slicing).
- `throw;` → re-throws the **original exception object** preserving its dynamic type.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the catch parameter type** — Writing `catch (e)` without specifying the type. This is a **compile-time error** in C++. Always include the type: `catch (int e)`. **(−1 mark)**
> 2. **Placing `catch(...)` before specific handlers** — The catch-all will swallow all exceptions, making specific handlers unreachable. **(−2 marks)**
> 3. **Confusing `throw;` with `throw e;`** — The bare `throw` preserves the original object; `throw e` throws a copy. In polymorphic scenarios, only the bare form prevents slicing during re-throw. **(−2 marks)**
> 4. **Assuming `try` block catches errors** — It does NOT. Many students write "the `try` block catches the exception" in definitions. The `catch` block catches; the `try` block merely encloses. **(−1 mark)**
> 5. **Missing `#include <exception>` or `<stdexcept>`** — Required when using `std::exception` or its derived classes. **(−1 mark for output)**
> 6. **Forgetting to make custom exception classes inherit from `std::exception`** — While not mandatory, KTU expects it for the `what()` interface. **(−1 mark)**
> 7. **Confusing "stack unwinding" with "stack overflow"** — These are entirely different concepts. Stack unwinding is destructor invocation during exception propagation. **(−1 mark)**

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — `try` Block and `catch` Clause:**

- 🔹 The `try` block **marks** the guarded region; it does **not** catch exceptions.
- 🔹 The `catch` clause **handles** exceptions based on **type matching**.
- 🔹 Every `try` must be followed by **at least one `catch`**.
- 🔹 Catch handlers are matched **sequentially** (top-down); **first match wins**.
- 🔹 `catch(...)` is the **catch-all handler** and must be placed **last**.
- 🔹 **Catch by reference** (`catch (T& e)`) to prevent **object slicing** in polymorphic hierarchies.
- 🔹 A **bare `throw;`** re-throws the original exception; **`throw e;`** throws a copy.
- 🔹 **Stack unwinding** guarantees destructor calls for all constructed local objects during exception propagation.
- 🔹 An **uncaught exception** triggers **`std::terminate()`**, causing abnormal program termination.
- 🔹 A `throw` statement **skips** all remaining code in the `try` block (unreachable code after `throw`).
- 🔹 Exceptions in C++ are **unchecked** by default — no `throws` clause declaration is needed (unlike Java).
- 🔹 The `what()` method of `std::exception` returns a `const char*` description of the error.
- 🔹 **Order of catch blocks matters** — place **derived** (specific) exception types **before** **base** (general) types.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 One-Liner Exam Punchlines (Memorize These):**
> - *"A `try` without a `catch` is like a question without an answer."*
> - *"`catch(...)` placed first is a compiler's silent disaster."*
> - *"Stack unwinding is C++'s promise — no resource left behind."*
> - *"Catch by reference, or lose the polymorphism."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT414 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 3
