<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Multiple Catch Clauses (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition)**
> A single `try` block in Java can be associated with **two or more `catch` blocks**, where each `catch` block is designed to handle a **distinct exception type**. The Java Virtual Machine (JVM) inspects the type of the thrown exception at runtime and dispatches control to the **first matching catch block** in the declared order. This mechanism embodies the OOP principle of **polymorphism** applied to exception handling, allowing the same `try` statement to react differently depending on the precise subtype of error that occurs.

In KTU terminology, this is often phrased as **"Exception Type-Specific Handlers"** and is a direct application of the **"One try, many catchers"** design idiom.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Hospital Emergency Room**
> Imagine a hospital emergency ward. A patient walks in (the `try` block executes). The triage nurse quickly diagnoses: *"Is this a heart problem, a fracture, or food poisoning?"* Based on the diagnosis (the exception type), the patient is routed to the **Cardiologist**, the **Orthopedic Surgeon**, or the **Gastroenterologist** — each a specialist `catch` block. The crucial rule: **the heart specialist must be checked before the general physician**, because a generalist would absorb every case and the cardiologist would never see any patient. That rule is exactly the same reason why **subclass exceptions must be caught before superclass exceptions** in Java.

## 1.3 Why We Need Multiple catch Clauses

A single `catch (Exception e)` block can technically handle *any* exception, but this is bad engineering because:
- It loses **type information** about the specific failure.
- It cannot apply **type-specific recovery logic** (e.g., retry on `IOException` vs. log-and-exit on `NullPointerException`).
- It violates the OOP principle of **precise responsibility**.

## 1.4 Standard Metric — Java Exception Class Hierarchy (Verifiable)

The following parent-child relationships are part of the **official Java SE specification** (no external reference fabricated):

- `java.lang.Object`
  - `java.lang.Throwable`
    - `java.lang.Exception`
      - `java.io.IOException`
      - `java.sql.SQLException`
      - `java.lang.ClassNotFoundException`
      - `java.lang.RuntimeException`
        - `java.lang.ArithmeticException`
        - `java.lang.NullPointerException`
        - `java.lang.ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`
        - `java.lang.NumberFormatException`

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Memory Trick:** *Throwable is the God, Exception and Error are its two children, RuntimeException is the rebellious grandchild that does not need to be declared.*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Logic — How the JVM Dispatches Exceptions

The runtime dispatch of an exception across multiple `catch` blocks follows a strict, **linear, top-down** algorithm:

1. **Step 1 — Trigger:** An exception object is `throw`n inside the `try` block.
2. **Step 2 — Snapshot:** The JVM creates the exception object (e.g., `new ArithmeticException("/ by zero")`).
3. **Step 3 — Linear Scan:** The JVM inspects `catch` block #1's declared parameter type.
4. **Step 4 — Type Match Test:** It evaluates whether the thrown object is `instanceof` the declared catch type.
5. **Step 5 — Dispatch:** On the **first match**, the JVM binds the parameter, executes the handler, and **terminates the chain** (later `catch` blocks are skipped).
6. **Step 6 — Fall-through:** If **no** `catch` matches, the exception propagates up the call stack (unhandled exception → JVM default handler).

## 2.2 The Golden Rule — Ordering Principle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule (Mandatory for KTU 14-mark answers):**
> *Catch blocks must be ordered from **most specific (subclass)** to **least specific (superclass)**. Violating this order causes a **compile-time error** stating "exception … has already been caught."*

The compile-time rule can be expressed as a logical invariant:

$$\text{If } C_1 \text{ and } C_2 \text{ are sibling catch types and } C_1 \prec C_2 \text{ (subclass relation)}$$

$$\text{Then} \; \text{order}(C_1) \; < \; \text{order}(C_2)$$

Where $\text{order}(X)$ is the **declaration position** of the catch block (1 = first, $n$ = last).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below is the **high-yield reference** you should memorize. **No pipes inside math — vertical bars are written as `\vert` to preserve table rendering.**

| # | Construct | Syntax (Java SE) | Rule / Constraint | Common Pitfall |
|---|-----------|------------------|-------------------|----------------|
| 1 | Standard multi-catch | `try { ... } catch (ArithmeticException e) { ... } catch (NullPointerException e) { ... }` | Subclass before superclass | Wrong order → compile error |
| 2 | Multi-catch (Java 7+) | `catch (IOException \vert SQLException e) { ... }` | `e` is **implicitly final**; types must **not** be in a subclass relation | Reassigning `e` inside the block |
| 3 | Polymorphic catch | `catch (Exception e) { ... }` | Catches all checked + unchecked | Should be the **last** catch |
| 4 | Unreachable catch | `catch (Exception e) { ... } catch (IOException e) { ... }` | Compile-time error: *“exception java.io.IOException has already been caught”* | The second one is dead code |
| 5 | Rethrow with improved type (Java 7+) | `catch (SQLException \vert IOException e) { throw e; }` | Compiler tracks both types | Useful for precise `throws` clauses |

## 2.4 The "Unreachable Catch" Mathematical Condition

Given two catch types $T_1$ (declared first) and $T_2$ (declared second), the second is **unreachable** if and only if:

$$T_2 \subseteq T_1 \quad \text{(i.e., } T_2 \text{ is a subclass of } T_1 \text{)}$$

For example, `IOException` $\subseteq$ `Exception` → declaring `catch (Exception e)` before `catch (IOException e)` makes the second one **unreachable**, and `javac` will refuse to compile the program.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Database APIs:** A single `try` around a JDBC call may raise `SQLException`, `ClassNotFoundException`, or `NullPointerException` — each requires distinct recovery (reconnect, log, or fail-fast).
- **Network Servers:** Distinguishing `SocketTimeoutException` (retry) from `ConnectException` (circuit-break) requires multiple specific handlers.
- **Banking Software:** Catching `ArithmeticException` (division by zero in interest calculation) separately from `NumberFormatException` (malformed user input) is a textbook KTU 14-mark question scenario.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Code Walkthroughs & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Canonical KTU Textbook Example — ArithmeticException vs ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException

```java
public class MultiCatchDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            int a = Integer.parseInt(args[0]);
            int b = Integer.parseInt(args[1]);
            int c = a / b;
            System.out.println("Result = " + c);
        }
        catch (ArithmeticException e) {
            System.out.println("Division by zero is not allowed.");
        }
        catch (ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException e) {
            System.out.println("Please supply two command-line arguments.");
        }
        catch (NumberFormatException e) {
            System.out.println("Arguments must be integers.");
        }
        catch (Exception e) {
            System.out.println("Generic fallback handler: " + e.getMessage());
        }
    }
}
```

### Step-by-Step Execution Analysis (Input: `java MultiCatchDemo 10 0`)

**Step 1** — JVM enters the `try` block.
**Step 2** — `Integer.parseInt("10")` returns `10`. No exception.
**Step 3** — `Integer.parseInt("0")` returns `0`. No exception.
**Step 4** — `a / b` evaluates to `10 / 0`. The JVM detects integer division by zero.
**Step 5** — The JVM **instantiates** a new `ArithmeticException` object with the message `/ by zero`.
**Step 6** — The `try` block is **aborted** at this exact line.
**Step 7** — The JVM **scans catch #1**: `catch (ArithmeticException e)` — **match found**.
**Step 8** — The reference `e` is bound to the exception object.
**Step 9** — `Division by zero is not allowed.` is printed.
**Step 10** — Control **exits the entire try-catch construct**; catch #2, #3, and #4 are **skipped**.
**Step 11** — Program continues normally after the construct.

### Step-by-Step Execution Analysis (Input: `java MultiCatchDemo 10`)

**Step 1** — `args[0]` is `"10"`, parsed to `10`.
**Step 2** — `args[1]` is accessed → JVM throws `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`.
**Step 3** — Catch #1 (`ArithmeticException`) — **no match**.
**Step 4** — Catch #2 (`ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`) — **match found**.
**Step 5** — Handler runs, prints the message, control exits construct.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Input Scenarios

| Input | Exception Thrown | Matched Catch | Output |
|-------|------------------|---------------|--------|
| `10 0` | `ArithmeticException` | #1 | `Division by zero is not allowed.` |
| `10` | `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` | #2 | `Please supply two command-line arguments.` |
| `ten 5` | `NumberFormatException` | #3 | `Arguments must be integers.` |
| (any other failure) | (any other subclass of `Exception`) | #4 (fallback) | `Generic fallback handler: ...` |

## 3.3 Compile-Time Error Case — Wrong Ordering

```java
try {
    // ... risky code ...
}
catch (Exception e) {          // Line A — too general
    System.out.println("Generic");
}
catch (ArithmeticException e)  // Line B — UNREACHABLE
{
    System.out.println("Divide by zero");
}
```

**Compilation output:**

```
MultiCatchDemo.java: error: exception java.lang.ArithmeticException
has already been caught
```

### Why the Compiler Rejects This — Algebraic Reasoning

The compiler performs a static subtype check:

$$T_{\text{LineB}} = \text{ArithmeticException}, \quad T_{\text{LineA}} = \text{Exception}$$

It evaluates the inheritance relation:

$$T_{\text{LineB}} \subseteq T_{\text{LineA}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_{\text{LineA}} \text{ subsumes } T_{\text{LineB}}$$

Because the earlier catch (`Exception`) already covers every possible `ArithmeticException`, the second catch is **logically dead code**. The compiler, which must reject unreachable code (per JLS §14.20.1), issues the error.

## 3.4 Java 7+ Multi-Catch (Union Type) — Exhaustive Example

```java
try {
    conn = DriverManager.getConnection(url, user, pass);
    stmt = conn.createStatement();
    stmt.executeUpdate(query);
}
catch (SQLException | IOException e) {   // single handler, two types
    logger.log(e.getMessage());
    throw e;                              // rethrow — e is effectively final
}
```

### Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1** — The `try` block may throw either `SQLException` (DB failure) or `IOException` (network failure).
**Step 2** — Instead of writing two identical `catch` blocks, the developer writes one with `|` (pipe, "or") — read as "if it is `SQLException` **or** `IOException`".
**Step 3** — Inside the block, the variable `e` is **implicitly final** — you cannot reassign `e = new RuntimeException();`.
**Step 4** — The method's `throws` clause must declare a common supertype (`SQLException` and `IOException` share `Exception`).
**Step 5** — The compiler verifies that the two types are **not in a subclass relation**; otherwise it would be a redundancy error.

## 3.5 Ordering Invariant for Multi-Catch

For a multi-catch `catch (A | B e)`:

$$A \not\subseteq B \quad \text{and} \quad B \not\subseteq A$$

If $A \subseteq B$, the alternative $A$ is redundant and the compiler errors out (since `B` alone would suffice).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Runtime Dispatch Flow — How the JVM Selects a Catch Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Enter try block] --> B[Execute statement 1]
    B --> C{Statement OK?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Execute statement 2]
    D --> E{Statement OK?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Execute statement N]
    F --> G[Skip all catch blocks]
    G --> Z[Continue after try-catch]

    E -- No, Throws ExcObj --> H[Inspect catch 1 type T1]
    H --> I{ExcObj instanceof T1?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Run catch 1 handler]
    J --> Z

    I -- No --> K[Inspect catch 2 type T2]
    K --> L{ExcObj instanceof T2?}
    L -- Yes --> M[Run catch 2 handler]
    M --> Z

    L -- No --> N[Inspect catch 3 type T3]
    N --> O{ExcObj instanceof T3?}
    O -- Yes --> P[Run catch 3 handler]
    P --> Z

    O -- No --> Q[No matching catch in this construct]
    Q --> R[Propagate exception up call stack]

    C -- No, Throws ExcObj --> H
```

**How to read this diagram:** Each rectangular node is an action, each diamond is a runtime decision based on the `instanceof` test. The path labeled *“Propagate exception up call stack”* corresponds to the case where **no** catch block matches — the JVM unwinds the stack until it finds a matching handler, terminating the program with a stack trace if none is found.

## 4.2 Class Hierarchy — Why Subclass Must Come First

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    objRoot[Object]
    throwRoot[Throwable]
    errRoot[Error]
    excRoot[Exception]
    ioExc[IOException]
    sqlExc[SQLException]
    rtExc[RuntimeException]
    arithExc[ArithmeticException]
    npeExc[NullPointerException]
    aiobExc[ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException]
    nfeExc[NumberFormatException]

    objRoot --> throwRoot
    throwRoot --> errRoot
    throwRoot --> excRoot
    excRoot --> ioExc
    excRoot --> sqlExc
    excRoot --> rtExc
    rtExc --> arithExc
    rtExc --> npeExc
    rtExc --> aiobExc
    rtExc --> nfeExc
```

**Reading guide:** Arrows point from *superclass* to *subclass*. A `catch (Exception e)` block will absorb every box below `Exception` because of polymorphism. Therefore, `ArithmeticException` (a deeper box) **must** be caught *before* `Exception`, otherwise its handler becomes unreachable.

## 4.3 Block-Level Architecture of try with Multiple catch

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TRYBLOCK[try Block]
        T1[Statement 1]
        T2[Statement 2]
        T3[Statement 3]
        T1 --> T2 --> T3
    end

    subgraph CATCHCHAIN[Catch Chain - Linear Order]
        C1[catch SpecificExc1 e1]
        C2[catch SpecificExc2 e2]
        C3[catch SpecificExc3 e3]
        C4[catch Exception e4 - Fallback]
        C1 --> C2 --> C3 --> C4
    end

    subgraph FINALLYBLOCK[finally Block - Optional]
        F1[Cleanup Resource Close]
    end

    TRYBLOCK -- Throws --> CATCHCHAIN
    CATCHCHAIN -- First Match Wins --> FINALLYBLOCK
    CATCHCHAIN -- No Match --> PROP[Propagate to Caller]
    FINALLYBLOCK --> END[Program Continues]
    PROP --> END
```

This block-level functional architecture maps out the **three logical zones** of a multi-catch construct: the *attempt zone* (`try`), the *dispatch zone* (the catch chain), and the *cleanup zone* (`finally`). The annotation "First Match Wins" is the central invariant: only the **first** catch whose declared type is assignment-compatible with the thrown object executes; the rest are skipped.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> *Answer in 2–3 sentences. Each carries 3 marks under KTU University Exam pattern.*

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`**
*What are multiple catch clauses in Java? Why is the order of catch blocks important?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Multiple catch clauses refer to attaching two or more `catch` blocks to a single `try` block, each designed to handle a distinct exception type. The JVM inspects them **top-down** and executes the **first** whose declared type is compatible with the thrown exception. Order is critical because, due to inheritance, a more general exception type placed earlier would *subsume* every more specific subtype, making later catches **unreachable** and causing a compile-time error.

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**
*Mention any **two differences** between traditional multiple catch blocks and Java 7 multi-catch syntax with an example.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Traditional Multi-Catch | Java 7 Multi-Catch |
|--------|------------------------|--------------------|
| Duplication | One handler per type → duplicated code | Single handler for multiple types |
| Syntax | `catch (A e) { ... } catch (B e) { ... }` | `catch (A \| B e) { ... }` |
| Reassign `e` | Allowed (since `e` is a normal reference) | **Not allowed** — `e` is implicitly final |

*Example:* `catch (IOException | SQLException e) { ... }`

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> *Each Part B question has internal choice — answer either **Question A** OR **Question B**. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.*

---

### ✅ Question A `[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Paper 2]`

**(a)** With a suitable Java program, explain how the Java Virtual Machine handles a `try` block followed by **multiple catch blocks** of different exception types. Illustrate the execution flow when (i) `ArithmeticException` and (ii) `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` are triggered. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the rule regarding the **order of catch blocks**. Provide a code snippet that demonstrates a compile-time error caused by violating this rule, and explain why the compiler rejects it. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) — Program with multiple catch blocks (7 marks):**

```java
import java.util.Scanner;
public class MultiCatch {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);
        try {
            System.out.print("Enter numerator: ");
            int a = Integer.parseInt(sc.nextLine());
            System.out.print("Enter denominator: ");
            int b = Integer.parseInt(sc.nextLine());
            int[] arr = new int[2];
            arr[5] = a / b;   // May throw ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException
            System.out.println("Result = " + arr[5]);
        }
        catch (ArithmeticException e) {
            System.out.println("Handler-1 (Arithmetic): Cannot divide by zero.");
        }
        catch (ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException e) {
            System.out.println("Handler-2 (AIOB): Array index out of range.");
        }
        catch (NumberFormatException e) {
            System.out.println("Handler-3 (NumberFormat): Input was not an integer.");
        }
        catch (Exception e) {
            System.out.println("Handler-4 (Fallback): " + e);
        }
    }
}
```

**Execution flow — Case (i), Input `10 0`:**
1. `a = 10`, `b = 0`. Parsing succeeds.
2. `a / b` triggers `ArithmeticException: / by zero`.
3. JVM scans catch list top-down: `ArithmeticException` matches at **catch #1** → handler executes.
4. `arr[5] = ...` line is **never reached**; remaining catches are skipped.

**Execution flow — Case (ii), Input `10 5`:**
1. `a / b` succeeds (`10/5 = 2`).
2. `arr[5] = 2` triggers `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` (size is 2).
3. Catch #1 (`ArithmeticException`) → no match.
4. Catch #2 (`ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`) → **match** → handler runs.
5. Output: `Handler-2 (AIOB): Array index out of range.`

The JVM uses the `instanceof` test on each catch parameter in declared order, executing the first compatible handler and **unwinding** the try block.

**(b) — Ordering rule and compile-time error (7 marks):**

The **subclass-before-superclass rule** mandates that catch blocks be ordered from **most specific** to **least specific**. A subclass catch placed *after* its superclass is **unreachable code**, which the Java Language Specification (§14.20.1) explicitly forbids.

**Invalid code (causes compile-time error):**

```java
try {
    int x = 10 / 0;
}
catch (Exception e) {                     // (1) superclass — too general
    System.out.println("Generic");
}
catch (ArithmeticException e) {           // (2) UNREACHABLE — already caught
    System.out.println("Divide by zero");
}
```

**Compiler output:**
```
MultiCatch.java: error: exception java.lang.ArithmeticException
has already been caught
```

**Explanation:** The compiler performs static type analysis. Since `ArithmeticException` is a subclass of `Exception`, the first catch already covers every `ArithmeticException` that could possibly be thrown. The second catch parameter is therefore *logically dead* — the program flow can never reach it. Java, like C/C++, prohibits unreachable statements at compile time, and the compiler reports the error at the line of the second catch.

**Correct ordering:**

```java
catch (ArithmeticException e) { ... }   // specific first
catch (Exception e) { ... }             // general last
```

---

### ✅ Question B `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Supplementary]`

**(a)** Explain the **multi-catch feature** introduced in Java 7. Write a Java program that uses multi-catch to handle both `IOException` and `SQLException` in a single handler. Mention the constraint on the `e` variable inside the multi-catch block. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between **checked and unchecked exceptions** with examples. Given a code fragment with multiple catch blocks, identify which catch will handle a `NullPointerException` if it is thrown and justify. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) — Java 7 multi-catch feature (7 marks):**

Java 7 introduced the **multi-catch** syntax to eliminate the boilerplate of writing duplicate handlers for unrelated exception types. The pipe symbol `|` separates multiple exception types in a single `catch` declaration.

**Program demonstrating multi-catch:**

```java
import java.io.*;
import java.sql.*;
public class MultiCatchDemo {
    static void performIO() throws IOException { throw new IOException("File missing"); }
    static void performDB() throws SQLException { throw new SQLException("DB error"); }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            if (args[0].equals("io")) performIO();
            else performDB();
        }
        catch (IOException | SQLException e) {
            System.out.println("Caught: " + e.getClass().getSimpleName());
            System.out.println("Message: " + e.getMessage());
            // e = new RuntimeException();  // ERROR — e is effectively final
        }
    }
}
```

**Key points to mention in the answer:**

1. The syntax is `catch (Type1 | Type2 | ... e)`.
2. The variable `e` is **implicitly final** — it cannot be reassigned inside the block. This was a design choice to prevent the developer from accidentally losing the original exception type.
3. The types in the multi-catch must **not** be in a subclass relationship (e.g., `IOException | Exception` is illegal).
4. The method's `throws` clause must declare a common supertype; here, `Exception`.
5. The bytecode generated is identical to having two separate `catch` blocks — multi-catch is purely a *source-level convenience*.

**(b) — Checked vs Unchecked + NullPointerException handling (7 marks):**

**Checked Exceptions** — Subclasses of `Exception` (but not `RuntimeException`). The compiler **enforces** that they be either caught or declared in the method's `throws` clause. Examples: `IOException`, `SQLException`, `ClassNotFoundException`.

**Unchecked Exceptions** — Subclasses of `RuntimeException` (and `Error`). The compiler does **not** require explicit handling. Examples: `ArithmeticException`, `NullPointerException`, `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`, `NumberFormatException`.

**Identification question — given code:**

```java
try {
    String s = null;
    System.out.println(s.length());
}
catch (ArithmeticException e)        { System.out.println("A"); }
catch (NullPointerException e)       { System.out.println("B"); }
catch (RuntimeException e)           { System.out.println("C"); }
catch (Exception e)                  { System.out.println("D"); }
```

When `s.length()` is invoked on a `null` reference, the JVM throws a `NullPointerException`. The JVM performs a linear top-down scan:

- Catch #1 — `ArithmeticException`? **No**, NPE is not arithmetic.
- Catch #2 — `NullPointerException`? **Yes** — match!

The handler at **catch #2** runs, printing `B`. The remaining catches (`RuntimeException`, `Exception`) are not executed, even though they could have caught it, because the **first match wins**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the inheritance arrow** — Many students write "Exception is caught by ArithmeticException". Wrong — the relationship is the other way. Memorize: subclass is caught by the subclass-specific catch first.
> 2. **Confusing multi-catch with multi-throw** — `catch (A | B e)` is *not* equivalent to nested `if` conditions; it is a single handler for two types.
> 3. **Reassigning `e` in multi-catch** — Causes a compile error. `e` is implicitly final.
> 4. **Omitting the general `Exception` catch in the answer** — When asked "what is the output if an unexpected error occurs", failing to mention the fallback catch loses 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Writing `throw` inside `catch` without `throws` declaration on method** — Triggers a "unhandled exception" compile error.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Multiple Catch Clauses:**

- ☐ A single `try` can be followed by **multiple `catch` blocks**, each catching a **distinct exception type**.
- ☐ The JVM dispatches using a **top-down, first-match-wins** algorithm based on `instanceof` testing.
- ☐ The **subclass-before-superclass** ordering rule is **mandatory**; violation → compile-time error: *“exception has already been caught”*.
- ☐ The most general `catch (Exception e)` (or `Throwable`) is always placed **last** as a **fallback handler**.
- ☐ The `try` block is **aborted** at the exact line where the exception is thrown; statements after that line are skipped.
- ☐ Only the **first matching** catch executes; remaining catches are skipped, even if they could have caught the same type.
- ☐ Java 7's **multi-catch** syntax `catch (A | B e)` reduces code duplication; `e` is **implicitly final**.
- ☐ Multi-catch types must be **unrelated** in the inheritance hierarchy (no subclass relations).
- ☐ Checked exceptions must be declared in `throws` or caught; **unchecked** (RuntimeException subclasses) do not.
- ☐ The `finally` block (if present) executes **regardless** of whether a catch matched — useful for resource cleanup.
- ☐ Polymorphism in catch: a `catch (Exception e)` parameter can refer to *any* `Exception` subclass.
- ☐ Unreachable catch blocks are a **compile-time** error, not a runtime one — fix the order, do not add a `throws` clause.
- ☐ Best practice: catch **specific** exceptions, not `Exception`, except as the last-resort fallback.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nested try Statements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT414 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Nested try Statements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Nested try Statement (KTU Definition):**
> A *nested try statement* refers to the deliberate placement of one `try` block (with its associated `catch` and/or `finally` clauses) **entirely inside another `try` block's body**. In the Java exception handling hierarchy, nesting is permitted because each `try` block establishes its own independent *guarded region* and *exception monitoring scope*. When an exception is thrown inside an inner `try`, the JVM first searches the nearest enclosing `catch` clause. If no matching handler is found locally, the exception propagates outward to the next enclosing `try` block — this mechanism is termed **Exception Propagation through Nested Scopes**.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, nested try statements are categorized as a structural variation of the **Exception Handling Framework** (part of Module 3: *Exception Handling and Multithreading*). The concept aligns with:

- **Course Outcome (CO3):** *Apply object-oriented concepts to design robust Java programs with proper exception handling.*
- **Bloom's Level:** L3 (Apply) → L4 (Analyze)

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Russian Doll (Matryoshka) of Safety Net**

Imagine a deep-sea diver descending in stages:

1. **Outer Diving Bell (Outer `try`)** — Provides the **first level of safety** against general underwater dangers (any unexpected pressure loss).
2. **Inner Diving Helmet (Inner `try`)** — Inside the bell, the diver handles a *more specific* task like adjusting instruments. The helmet provides **focused protection** for a precise operation (handling *ArithmeticException* or *ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException*).
3. **If the helmet fails to handle a specific fault** (no matching `catch`), the bell's safety mechanism (outer `catch`) takes over.
4. **If even the bell fails**, the diver surfaces and the surface team handles it (JVM default exception handler).

This **layered, hierarchical safety model** is precisely what nested try statements implement in Java code.

### 1.3 Core Metric — Scope Depth

The **maximum recommended nesting depth** is **3 to 4 levels**. Industry standard (Google Java Style Guide, Oracle Best Practices) flags nested depths beyond 4 as a **code smell (anti-pattern)** indicating that the method should be refactored into smaller, single-responsibility methods.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Boundary:** $\text{Max Safe Nesting Depth} \leq 4$ levels
> Beyond this, the **Cyclomatic Complexity** $M = E - N + 2P$ (where $E$ = edges, $N$ = nodes, $P$ = connected components) exceeds the IEEE-recommended threshold of $M \leq 10$ for maintainable code.

### 1.4 Classification of Nesting

| Nesting Type | Description | Use Case Frequency |
|--------------|-------------|-------------------|
| **try inside try** | Most common form; allows granular exception handling per logical sub-task | High |
| **try inside catch** | Used to perform recovery or cleanup that itself may fail | Medium |
| **try inside finally** | Used to ensure logging/notification never throws | Low |
| **try inside a method call inside outer try** | Indirect nesting via helper methods | High |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Mechanics — Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

The execution flow of a nested try-catch structure follows a deterministic state machine. Let us break down the operational phases:

**Phase 1 — Block Entry (Scope Stack Push)**
- When execution enters an outer `try` block, the JVM pushes a new *exception handler frame* onto the *runtime stack*.
- An inner `try` block pushes another frame on top of the outer one.
- The depth of the stack corresponds to the nesting level.

**Phase 2 — Normal Execution (Linear Flow)**
- Code inside the innermost `try` executes line-by-line.
- If no exception is thrown, the innermost `finally` (if any) runs first, then each outer `finally` runs in **reverse nesting order** (LIFO — Last In, First Out).

**Phase 3 — Exception Generation (Search Initiation)**
- When a `throw` statement executes (or a JVM-detected error occurs), the JVM halts normal flow.
- The exception object is instantiated (via `new` or auto-generated by JVM).
- The JVM begins **stack unwinding** from the point of throw, looking for a matching `catch` block.

**Phase 4 — Handler Resolution (Type Matching Algorithm)**
- The JVM traverses `catch` clauses from the **innermost to outermost** scope.
- A `catch` matches if the thrown exception is `instanceof` the declared parameter type.
- The **first matching catch** is executed; subsequent catches (even if compatible) are skipped.

**Phase 5 — Propagation (Fallback)**
- If no `catch` in any enclosing scope matches, the exception propagates to the calling method.
- Eventually, if unhandled, the JVM invokes `Thread.getDefaultUncaughtExceptionHandler()` and the program terminates.

**Phase 6 — Cleanup (`finally` Execution)**
- Before propagating out of a `try` block, its `finally` block (if present) always executes.
- This guarantees cleanup of resources like file handles, database connections, or locks.

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table summarizes the key relationships and rules for nested try statements that KTU examiners frequently test.**

| Rule / Concept | Mathematical / Logical Representation | KTU Significance |
|----------------|--------------------------------------|------------------|
| **Catch Resolution Order** | $R = \arg\min_{i} \{ d_i \vert \ e \in \mathcal{C}(d_i) \}$ where $d_i$ is nesting depth | Innermost matching catch wins |
| **finally Execution Count** | $F_{exec} = N_{try\_blocks}$ (one per `try` with finally) | `finally` runs once per `try` entered |
| **Propagation Distance** | $P_{depth} = D_{throw} - D_{catch}$ | Where $D_{throw}$ = depth at throw, $D_{catch}$ = depth of matching catch |
| **Catch Block Skipping** | $\text{If } e \in C_1, \text{ then } C_2, C_3, \ldots \text{ are bypassed}$ | First-match semantics |
| **Throwable Hierarchy** | $\text{Object} \rightarrow \text{Throwable} \rightarrow \{\text{Exception}, \text{Error}\}$ | Determines which catch can catch what |
| **Nesting Limit Recommendation** | $N_{max} \leq 4$ | Industry standard, KTU coding best practice |
| **finally Override Rule** | $\text{finally runs unless } \text{System.exit()} \text{ or JVM crash}$ | $\text{System.exit}(0)$ bypasses finally |
| **Try-Without-Catch Rule** | $\text{try} \rightarrow \text{finally allowed (Java 7+)}$ | Valid since Java 7 |
| **Multi-Catch (Java 7+)** | $\text{catch} (IOException \vert \text{SQLException} \ e)$ | Replaces some nesting needs |

---

### 2.3 Why and How — Engineering Rationale

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why use nested try?**
> 1. **Granular Recovery** — Different sub-operations may require different recovery strategies.
> 2. **Localized Resource Management** — Inner `finally` blocks can release resources specific to a sub-task before outer cleanup.
> 3. **Domain-Specific Exception Handling** — Outer `try` may catch business logic exceptions; inner `try` handles low-level I/O exceptions.
> 4. **Defensive Programming** — When using third-party APIs, you wrap specific calls in inner `try` to handle their specific exceptions while letting the outer `try` handle systemic issues.

**Real-World Engineering Utility:**
- **Banking Software:** Outer `try` catches `BusinessException` (insufficient funds); inner `try` catches `SQLException` during database update.
- **Network Protocol Stacks:** Inner `try` parses individual packets; outer `try` handles session-level errors.
- **Compilers:** Inner `try` tokenizes a single line; outer `try` handles file-level I/O.

---

### 2.4 Syntax Template (Java SE – KTU Standard)

```java
try {                                  // OUTER try (Level 1)
    statement_1;
    try {                              // INNER try (Level 2)
        statement_2;
        // May throw specific exception
    } catch (SpecificException e) {    // INNER catch
        // Handle inner exception
    }
    statement_3;
} catch (GeneralException e) {         // OUTER catch
    // Handle broader exception
} finally {                            // OUTER finally (optional)
    // Mandatory cleanup
}
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Java Restriction:** A `catch` clause of a nested `try` **CANNOT** catch exceptions thrown by the outer `try`'s code (after the inner `try` block). The scoping is strictly *one-directional inward*. Conversely, the outer `catch` **CAN** catch exceptions from the inner `try` **only if** the inner `catch` does not match them.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Exception Propagation Path Algorithm

Let us formally derive the search path for an exception thrown inside a nested try-catch structure.

**Given:**
- A nesting depth $D$ where $D \in \mathbb{Z}^+$, $D \geq 2$.
- An exception object $e$ of type $T_e$ thrown at depth $d_t$ such that $1 \leq d_t \leq D$.
- A set of catch clauses $C = \{c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_k\}$ distributed across depths.

**Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Identify the throw point. The exception is generated at logical depth $d_t$.

$$d_t = \text{depth at which } \texttt{throw new MyException()} \text{ executes}$$

**Step 2:** Begin the matching search at the innermost enclosing `try`. The search proceeds outward:

$$S(d) = \begin{cases} \text{True} & \text{if } T_e \text{ is assignable to the parameter type of } c_i \text{ at depth } d \\ \text{False} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 3:** The matching depth $d_m$ is the *minimum* depth (most local) where a match exists:

$$d_m = \min \{ d \in [1, D] \vert \ \exists c_i \in C(d) : S(d) = \text{True} \}$$

**Step 4:** Execute the matching `catch` block at $d_m$. All intermediate `finally` blocks between $d_t$ and $d_m$ execute *before* the handler.

$$\text{Execution order} = \text{finally}(d_t) \rightarrow \text{finally}(d_t - 1) \rightarrow \ldots \rightarrow \text{finally}(d_m + 1) \rightarrow \text{catch}(d_m)$$

**Step 5:** If no match exists in any enclosing scope, the exception propagates to the calling method.

$$d_m = \emptyset \implies \text{propagate to caller via JVM stack unwinding}$$

---

### 3.2 Worked Example — Numerical Trace

**Code Under Analysis:**

```java
public class NestedDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {                                              // Level 1
            System.out.println("Outer try starts");
            try {                                          // Level 2
                int[] arr = {10, 20, 30};
                System.out.println(arr[5]);                // Throws ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException
            } catch (ArithmeticException e) {              // Level 2 catch (won't match)
                System.out.println("Inner catch 1");
            }
            System.out.println("This line won't execute");
        } catch (ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException e) {       // Level 1 catch (matches!)
            System.out.println("Outer catch: " + e);
        } finally {
            System.out.println("Outer finally");
        }
    }
}
```

**Step-by-Step Trace:**

| Line | Action | Output | Stack State |
|------|--------|--------|-------------|
| 1 | Enter outer `try` (Level 1) | — | [Level 1] |
| 2 | Print statement | `Outer try starts` | [Level 1] |
| 3 | Enter inner `try` (Level 2) | — | [Level 1, Level 2] |
| 4 | Initialize array | — | [Level 1, Level 2] |
| 5 | Access `arr[5]` → **throws** | — | [Level 1, Level 2] |
| 6 | Inner catch search: `ArithmeticException` vs `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` | No match | [Level 1] (Level 2 exits) |
| 7 | Outer catch search: `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` ✓ | — | [Level 1] |
| 8 | Execute outer catch | `Outer catch: java.lang.ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException: Index 5 out of bounds for length 3` | [Level 1] |
| 9 | Execute outer `finally` | `Outer finally` | [] (cleared) |
| 10 | Program terminates normally | — | — |

**Final Console Output:**

```
Outer try starts
Outer catch: java.lang.ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException: Index 5 out of bounds for length 3
Outer finally
```

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Exception Flow Scenarios

| Scenario | Inner Exception Type | Inner Catch Match? | Outer Catch Match? | Resulting Flow |
|----------|---------------------|--------------------|--------------------|----------------|
| 1 | `ArithmeticException` | ✅ Yes | — | Inner catch → Inner finally → continues outer |
| 2 | `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | Inner finally → Outer catch → Outer finally |
| 3 | `NullPointerException` | ❌ No | ❌ No | Inner finally → Outer finally → propagate to caller |
| 4 | No exception thrown | — | — | All blocks complete normally; finallies run in LIFO order |
| 5 | `Error` (e.g., `OutOfMemoryError`) | ❌ No (usually) | ❌ No | Propagates to JVM default handler |
| 6 | `Throwable` (caught at outer) | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | Catches even `Error` subclasses — **anti-pattern** |

---

### 3.4 Edge Case Derivation — `try` inside `catch`

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "catch-and-recover" Pattern:** A `try` block placed inside a `catch` clause is a powerful pattern for attempting recovery operations.

**Mathematical Model:**

$$R = \begin{cases} \text{Recovered} & \text{if inner try succeeds} \\ \text{Re-thrown} & \text{if inner try throws and inner catch does not match} \end{cases}$$

**Code Demonstration:**

```java
try {
    riskyDatabaseOperation();
} catch (SQLException e) {
    System.out.println("Primary DB failed: " + e);
    try {                                              // Nested try INSIDE catch
        fallbackDatabaseOperation();                   // Try recovery
    } catch (SQLException e2) {
        System.out.println("Fallback also failed");
        throw new DataAccessException("Both DBs failed", e2);  // Re-throw as higher-level
    }
}
```

**Derivation of Re-throw Chain Length:** If a method re-throws $k$ times before success, the *exception wrapping depth* equals $k$, and the root cause chain has length $k+1$ (accessible via `Throwable.getCause()`).

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Nested Try-Catch Execution Path

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Program Start]) --> enterOuterTry[Enter Outer try Block - Level 1]
    enterOuterTry --> executeOuter[Execute Outer try Statements]
    executeOuter --> decisionOuter{Exception in Outer Pre-inner Code?}
    decisionOuter -- Yes --> searchOuterCatch[Search Outer catch Clauses]
    decisionOuter -- No --> enterInnerTry[Enter Inner try Block - Level 2]
    searchOuterCatch --> matchOuter{Match Found?}
    matchOuter -- Yes --> executeOuterCatch[Execute Outer catch]
    matchOuter -- No --> outerFinally[Execute Outer finally]
    executeOuterCatch --> outerFinally
    enterInnerTry --> executeInner[Execute Inner try Statements]
    executeInner --> decisionInner{Exception Thrown?}
    decisionInner -- No --> innerFinally[Execute Inner finally if present]
    decisionInner -- Yes --> searchInnerCatch[Search Inner catch Clauses]
    searchInnerCatch --> matchInner{Inner Match Found?}
    matchInner -- Yes --> executeInnerCatch[Execute Inner catch]
    matchInner -- No --> innerFinally
    executeInnerCatch --> innerFinally
    innerFinally --> continueOuter[Continue After Inner try in Outer Scope]
    continueOuter --> decisionOuterPost{Exception in Outer Post-inner Code?}
    decisionOuterPost -- Yes --> searchOuterCatch
    decisionOuterPost -- No --> outerFinally
    outerFinally --> endA([Program End / Propagate])
```

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram

    participant Finally2 as Inner finally
    
    Main->>Outer: 1. Enter outer try
    Outer->>Inner: 2. Enter inner try
    Inner->>Inner: 3. Execute statements
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define nested try statements in Java. When would you prefer nested try over a single try-catch block?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for justification):**

A *nested try statement* in Java is a `try-catch` (or `try-catch-finally`) block placed **inside the body of another `try` block**. Each inner `try` block establishes its own *exception monitoring scope*, and if an exception is not caught in the inner scope, it propagates to the outer enclosing `try` block's `catch` clauses.

**When to prefer nested try (2 marks):**
1. **Granular Exception Handling** — When different sub-operations within a method throw *different categories* of exceptions requiring distinct recovery logic.
2. **Localized Resource Management** — When inner operations need their own `finally` cleanup separate from the outer's.
3. **Mixed Exception Sources** — When one part of the code uses APIs that throw checked exceptions and another does not, nesting allows handling them at appropriate levels.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution (KTU Standard):** 1 mark — Definition; 1 mark — One valid use case; 1 mark — Second valid use case OR correct syntax example.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Explain the order of execution of `finally` blocks in a nested try-catch structure where both inner and outer try have `finally` clauses.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The `finally` blocks in a nested try-catch structure execute in **LIFO (Last-In, First-Out) order** with respect to the nesting levels.

- The `finally` block of the **innermost** `try` executes first.
- Then the `finally` block of the **next outer** `try` executes.
- This continues outward until the outermost `finally` is reached.

**General Rule:**
$$\text{Execution order} = \text{finally}(D) \rightarrow \text{finally}(D-1) \rightarrow \ldots \rightarrow \text{finally}(1)$$

where $D$ is the deepest nesting level where the `try` block was entered.

**Example trace** (for a 2-level nest):
1. Inner `finally` runs first
2. Outer `finally` runs second

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Students often claim `finally` blocks run in FIFO (outer-first) order. This is **INCORRECT** — `finally` follows **stack unwinding** order, which is LIFO.

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question — Module 3 Choice A]**

**(a)** Write a Java program that demonstrates a **nested try-catch** structure to handle the following scenario:
- The outer `try` reads two integers from the user (may throw `InputMismatchException`).
- The inner `try` performs division of the two integers (may throw `ArithmeticException`).
- Another inner `try` (inside the outer but at a different logical block) accesses an array element at a user-specified index (may throw `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`).
- Use appropriate `finally` blocks to print cleanup messages.

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

```java
import java.util.Scanner;
import java.util.InputMismatchException;

public class NestedTryDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);
        int numerator = 0, denominator = 0, index = 0;
        int[] arr = {100, 200, 300, 400, 500};
        
        try {                                                  // OUTER TRY
            System.out.print("Enter numerator: ");
            numerator = sc.nextInt();
            System.out.print("Enter denominator: ");
            denominator = sc.nextInt();
            
            try {                                              // INNER TRY 1: Division
                int result = numerator / denominator;
                System.out.println("Result: " + result);
            } catch (ArithmeticException e) {
                System.out.println("Cannot divide by zero: " + e.getMessage());
            } finally {
                System.out.println("[Cleanup] Division operation complete.");
            }
            
            System.out.print("Enter array index (0-4): ");
            index = sc.nextInt();
            
            try {                                              // INNER TRY 2: Array access
                System.out.println("Element at index " + index + ": " + arr[index]);
            } catch (ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException e) {
                System.out.println("Invalid index. Array length is " + arr.length);
            } finally {
                System.out.println("[Cleanup] Array access complete.");
            }
            
        } catch (InputMismatchException e) {
            System.out.println("Invalid input type. Please enter integers only.");
        } finally {
            sc.close();
            System.out.println("[Cleanup] Scanner closed. Program ending.");
        }
    }
}
```

**Sample Run (with errors):**
```
Enter numerator: 10
Enter denominator: 0
Cannot divide by zero: / by zero
[Cleanup] Division operation complete.
Enter array index (0-4): 10
Invalid index. Array length is 5
[Cleanup] Array access complete.
[Cleanup] Scanner closed. Program ending.
```

**Mark Distribution (7 Marks):**
- Correct outer try-catch structure with `InputMismatchException` handling: **2 marks**
- First inner try-catch for division with `ArithmeticException`: **2 marks**
- Second inner try-catch for array with `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`: **2 marks**
- Proper `finally` blocks and resource cleanup: **1 mark**

---

**(b)** Explain with a **flowchart/diagram** how an exception thrown in the innermost try block propagates through the nesting levels when no inner catch matches. How does the `finally` block ordering change if the exception IS caught at the inner level? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Case 1: Exception NOT caught at inner level (propagation occurs):**

```
[Throw in Level 3] 
       ↓
[Search Level 3 catch] → No match
       ↓
[Execute Level 3 finally]  ← FIRST
       ↓
[Search Level 2 catch] → No match
       ↓
[Execute Level 2 finally]  ← SECOND
       ↓
[Search Level 1 catch] → Match found
       ↓
[Execute Level 1 catch]
       ↓
[Execute Level 1 finally]  ← THIRD (LAST)
```

**Case 2: Exception caught at inner level (no propagation):**

```
[Throw in Level 3]
       ↓
[Search Level 3 catch] → Match found
       ↓
[Execute Level 3 catch]
       ↓
[Execute Level 3 finally]  ← (Same as Level 3)
       ↓
[Continue to Level 2 outer code]
       ↓
[If no more exceptions: Execute Level 2 finally, then Level 1 finally in LIFO order]
```

**Mathematical Formulation:**

Let $C(d)$ denote the catch executed at depth $d$, and $F(d)$ the finally block at depth $d$.

**For uncaught exception (propagation):**
$$\text{Order} = F(D) \rightarrow F(D-1) \rightarrow \ldots \rightarrow F(d_m + 1) \rightarrow C(d_m) \rightarrow F(d_m) \rightarrow \ldots \rightarrow F(1)$$

**For caught exception:**
$$\text{Order} = C(d_c) \rightarrow F(d_c) \rightarrow [\text{outer code}] \rightarrow F(d_c - 1) \rightarrow \ldots \rightarrow F(1)$$

where $d_c$ is the depth of the catching `try`.

**Key Insight:** In Case 1, the `finally` blocks of *deeper* levels run **before** the matching outer catch. In Case 2, only the catching level's `finally` and all outer finallies run.

**Mark Distribution (7 Marks):**
- Clear propagation diagram with proper labeling: **3 marks**
- Correct ordering for the uncaught case: **2 marks**
- Correct ordering for the caught case + key insight: **2 marks**

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question — Module 3 Choice B]**

**(a)** Differentiate between **nested try-catch** and **multi-catch** (Java 7+) with suitable examples. In what scenario would nested try still be preferred over multi-catch? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Nested try-catch | Multi-catch (Java 7+) |
|--------|------------------|------------------------|
| **Syntax** | Multiple `try-catch` blocks nested | Single `catch` with multiple types: `catch (E1 \| E2 \| E3 e)` |
| **Java Version** | Java 1.0+ | Java 7+ |
| **Handler Logic** | Each type can have its **own** handler body | All listed types share the **same** handler body |
| **Code Length** | Verbose | Concise |
| **Variable in catch** | Different `e` per block | One `e` variable for all types |
| **Effective Finality** | N/A | `e` is implicitly `final` in multi-catch |

**Code Examples:**

**Nested try-catch:**
```java
try {
    int a = Integer.parseInt(args[0]);
    try {
        int result = 100 / a;
    } catch (ArithmeticException e) {
        System.out.println("Specific recovery: setting result to 0");
        // custom recovery logic specific to division
    }
} catch (NumberFormatException e) {
    System.out.println("Please enter a valid integer");
} catch (ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException e) {
    System.out.println("Please provide an argument");
}
```

**Multi-catch equivalent:**
```java
try {
    int a = Integer.parseInt(args[0]);
    int result = 100 / a;
} catch (NumberFormatException | ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException e) {
    System.out.println("Input error: " + e.getMessage());
    // SAME handler for both
}
```

**When to prefer nested try over multi-catch (2 marks):**

Nested try is **still preferred** when:
1. **Different recovery actions are needed** for different exception types at different logical levels.
2. **Different scopes of cleanup** are required (e.g., inner `finally` releases a sub-resource before outer cleanup).
3. **Hierarchical error reporting** is needed (inner catch logs, outer catch notifies user).
4. **The exception types are in an inheritance hierarchy** — multi-catch cannot have parent and child in the same clause (causes compile error: "Alternatives in a multi-catch cannot be related by subclassing").

**Mark Distribution (7 Marks):**
- Clear difference with syntax examples: **4 marks**
- Valid scenario where nested try wins: **3 marks**

---

**(b)** Consider the following Java code. Identify the output, and explain the step-by-step execution flow including exception propagation and `finally` ordering. **(7 Marks)**

```java
public class NestedFlow {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {                                                  // Level 1
            System.out.println("A");
            try {                                              // Level 2
                System.out.println("B");
                int x = Integer.parseInt("abc");               // Throws NumberFormatException
                System.out.println("C");
            } catch (NullPointerException e) {
                System.out.println("D");
            } finally {
                System.out.println("E");
            }
            System.out.println("F");
        } catch (NumberFormatException e) {
            System.out.println("G: " + e.getMessage());
        } finally {
            System.out.println("H");
        }
        System.out.println("I");
    }
}
```

**Model Solution:**

**Step-by-Step Trace:**

| Step | Action | Output | Status |
|------|--------|--------|--------|
| 1 | Enter Level 1 `try` | — | Active |
| 2 | Print "A" | `A` | — |
| 3 | Enter Level 2 `try` | — | Active |
| 4 | Print "B" | `B` | — |
| 5 | `Integer.parseInt("abc")` | — | **Throws `NumberFormatException`** |
| 6 | Search Level 2 `catch (NullPointerException e)` | — | ❌ No match |
| 7 | Execute Level 2 `finally` | `E` | — |
| 8 | Exit Level 2; propagate to Level 1 | — | Searching |
| 9 | Search Level 1 `catch (NumberFormatException e)` | — | ✅ Match! |
| 10 | Execute Level 1 `catch` body | `G: For input string: "abc"` | — |
| 11 | Execute Level 1 `finally` | `H` | — |
| 12 | Continue after Level 1 try-catch-finally | `I` | — |

**Final Output:**
```
A
B
E
G: For input string: "abc"
H
I
```

**Explanation (3 marks for trace, 4 marks for explanation):**

1. The `try` at Level 1 begins execution. "A" is printed.
2. Control enters the Level 2 `try`. "B" is printed.
3. `Integer.parseInt("abc")` throws a `NumberFormatException`.
4. The JVM searches the nearest `catch` clause: `catch (NullPointerException e)`. Since `NumberFormatException` is **not** a subtype of `NullPointerException`, no match occurs.
5. Since no match is found at Level 2, its `finally` block runs, printing "E". (Critical: `finally` runs even when the catch doesn't match.)
6. The exception then **propagates outward** to the Level 1 `catch` clause.
7. Level 1's `catch (NumberFormatException e)` **matches** because `NumberFormatException` IS the exact type.
8. The catch body executes, printing "G: For input string: \"abc\"".
9. After the catch completes, the Level 1 `finally` block runs, printing "H".
10. Execution continues normally after the entire try-catch-finally, printing "I".

**Key Concept Tested:** `finally` runs *before* exception propagation completes, and the **innermost** `finally` runs **before** the outer `catch` is reached.

**Mark Distribution (7 Marks):**
- Correct final output: **2 marks**
- Correct step-by-step trace with propagation: **3 marks**
- Explanation of `finally` ordering principle: **2 marks**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing `finally` execution with `catch` execution** — `finally` runs in **LIFO** order during propagation, NOT FIFO. Students often lose 1-2 marks for this.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting that `finally` runs even when no exception occurs** — The `finally` block runs on **all exit paths**: normal completion, caught exception, propagated exception. Only `System.exit()` or JVM crash bypasses it.
> 
> 3. **Believing that an outer `try` can be skipped by a return inside an inner `try`** — A `return` statement in an inner `try` still triggers all enclosing `finally` blocks before actually returning to the caller. The KTU 2019 question paper specifically tested this.
> 
> 4. **Writing `try` without `catch` or `finally`** — This is a **compilation error** in Java. A `try` block must be followed by either `catch` or `finally` (or both).
> 
> 5. **Catching `Throwable` to be "safe"** — This is an **anti-pattern** and KTU examiners deduct marks. Always catch the **most specific** exception type possible.
> 
> 6. **Multi-catch with related types** — `catch (IOException | FileNotFoundException e)` causes a **compile error** because `FileNotFoundException` extends `IOException`. KTU may ask this as a trick question.
> 
> 7. **Ignoring the `finally` override rule** — A `finally` block can **override** a `return` value by using its own `return`. KTU 2022 question: "What does this method return?" with `return 1;` inside try and `return 2;` inside finally (Answer: 2).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Nested Try Statements (KTU 2024 Scheme)**

- ✅ **Definition:** A nested `try` is a `try` block placed **inside the body of another `try` block** (or inside a `catch`/`finally`).
- ✅ **Exception Search:** The JVM searches for a matching `catch` starting from the **innermost** `try` and moves **outward**.
- ✅ **First Match Wins:** Once a matching `catch` is found, all other `catch` blocks (in outer scopes) are **skipped** for that exception.
- ✅ **`finally` LIFO Order:** In nested structures, `finally` blocks execute in **Last-In-First-Out** (innermost first) order.
- ✅ **`finally` Always Runs:** Except when `System.exit()` is called or the JVM crashes.
- ✅ **Propagation:** If no `catch` matches at any level, the exception propagates to the **caller method** via stack unwinding.
- ✅ **Maximum Depth:** Recommended nesting depth is **≤ 4 levels** (industry best practice).
- ✅ **Resources:** Each `try` level can have its own `finally` for **independent resource cleanup**.
- ✅ **Multi-catch Alternative:** Since Java 7, `catch (E1 | E2 | ... | En e)` can replace some nesting, but **not** when different handlers are needed.
- ✅ **Anti-patterns to Avoid:** Catching `Throwable`/`Exception` generically, nesting beyond 4 levels, empty `catch` blocks, using nested try when a helper method would suffice.
- ✅ **Key Keyword:** *"In nested try, the exception travels outward like a bubble rising in water — the nearest surface (`catch`) that can hold it will catch it."*

---

**End of Notes — Nested try Statements | Module 3 | PEECT414 | KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: throw
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT414 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** throw

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the Java programming language, the **`throw`** keyword is a statement used to **explicitly trigger (raise) an exception object** from within a method or a block of code. When a `throw` statement is executed, the normal flow of control is immediately terminated, the exception object is propagated up the call stack, and the Java Virtual Machine (JVM) begins searching for the nearest enclosing `try-catch` block capable of handling the thrown exception type.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *"The `throw` keyword in Java is used to explicitly throw a single exception (either a built-in or a user-defined exception) from a program. It is followed by an instance of the `Throwable` class (or one of its subclasses)."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **traffic police officer** standing at a road junction. Everything is flowing smoothly, but suddenly you spot a major accident blocking the road. What do you do?

- You **blow your whistle (throw)** to immediately **stop the normal traffic flow**.
- You **raise a red flag (the exception object)** that signals "**STOP! Something is wrong here!**"
- Vehicles (the program execution) **abandon their current path** and look for the **nearest diversion route** — which in Java corresponds to a matching `catch` block.

In the same way, when a Java program encounters a situation it cannot handle locally (like dividing by zero, invalid age input, insufficient balance, etc.), it uses `throw` to **flag the abnormal condition** and hand over control to a handler that knows what to do.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** `throw` is an **action** — it actually creates and dispatches an exception. This is fundamentally different from `throws`, which is merely a **declaration** (an announcement) placed in a method signature.

### 1.3 Syntax (KTU Board Standard)

```java
throw <Throwable instance>;
```

The operand of `throw` must be an **object** of type `Throwable` or any of its subclasses (`Exception`, `RuntimeException`, `Error`, etc.). It cannot be a primitive type or a String.

| Component | Mandatory? | Description |
|-----------|------------|-------------|
| `throw` keyword | **Yes** | The reserved Java keyword that initiates the throw |
| `new ExceptionType(...)` | **Yes** | An instance (object) of an exception class — usually created on the spot |
| Semicolon `;` | **Yes** | Standard Java statement terminator |

### 1.4 Classification of Throwable Classes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every object that can be `throw`n in Java belongs to the `java.lang.Throwable` hierarchy.

$$
\text{Throwable} \begin{cases} \text{Error} & \text{(Unchecked — JVM-level, e.g., OutOfMemoryError)} \\ \text{Exception} \begin{cases} \text{Checked Exceptions} & \text(IOException, SQLException, \text{etc.}) \\ \text{Unchecked Exceptions (RuntimeException)} & \text(ArithmeticException, NullPointerException, \text{etc.}) \end{cases} \end{cases}
$$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Mechanics of `throw`

When the JVM encounters a `throw` statement during execution, the following sequence of events occurs:

1. **Instantiation / Retrieval:** A `Throwable` object is created (or referenced).
2. **Stack-Frame Unwinding Begins:** The current method's execution halts at the line of `throw`.
3. **Exception Object Propagation:** The object travels up through method calls on the call stack.
4. **Handler Search:** The JVM inspects each enclosing `try-catch` block from innermost to outermost.
5. **Type Matching:** The first `catch` block whose parameter type is **compatible (same class or superclass)** with the thrown object is selected.
6. **Catch Execution:** The handler executes. If no handler is found, the default JVM uncaught-exception handler terminates the program and prints the stack trace.

### 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Step

- **Why instantiate an object?** Because exceptions in Java are *objects* carrying diagnostic data (message, stack trace, cause). This enables OOP-style exception hierarchies.
- **Why propagate up the stack?** To decouple the location where the error *occurs* from the location where it is *managed*, a core OOP principle.
- **Why stop normal flow?** Guarantees that the program cannot accidentally continue past an unrecoverable state.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\vert` instead of `|` to avoid breaking the markdown table.

| Concept | Rule / Formula | Example | Notes |
|---------|----------------|---------|-------|
| Syntax | `throw new XxxException("msg");` | `throw new IOException("File missing");` | Must be a `Throwable` instance |
| Valid Operand Types | `Throwable` or any subclass | `RuntimeException`, `Error`, custom | Cannot throw primitives |
| Control Transfer | Immediate — rest of `try` is skipped | — | Stack unwinds to first match |
| Re-throwing | `catch (E e) { throw e; }` | Used in wrapper methods | Preserves stack trace |
| Checked vs Unchecked | Unchecked need no `throws` declaration | `throw new ArithmeticException(...)` | Checked must be declared |
| One Per Statement | Only **one** exception per `throw` | — | For multiple, use chained causes |
| `throw` outside `try` | Allowed if method declares `throws` | `void m() throws IOException { ... }` | Compiler-enforced rule |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The `throw` mechanism is the cornerstone of **defensive programming** in:
- **Banking software:** Throwing `InsufficientFundsException` on withdrawal.
- **Embedded systems / IoT:** Throwing `SensorFailureException` for fault tolerance.
- **Network programming:** Throwing `SocketTimeoutException` to trigger retry logic.
- **API development:** Custom exceptions for HTTP 4xx/5xx mapping (Spring Boot, Jakarta EE).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Principle:** "Throw early, catch late" — the KTU-recommended best practice. Detect problems as close to the source as possible, but handle them at a level where contextually appropriate recovery is possible.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Throwing a Built-in Unchecked Exception

**Problem:** Write a Java method `divide(int a, int b)` that throws an `ArithmeticException` if the divisor is zero.

**Step-by-Step Construction:**

**Step 1 — Identify the abnormal condition.**
The condition is `b == 0`. In standard division, this is undefined.

**Step 2 — Choose the appropriate exception class.**
Since division-by-zero is an arithmetic runtime anomaly, `java.lang.ArithmeticException` is the canonical choice. It is unchecked, so no `throws` declaration is required.

**Step 3 — Construct the `throw` statement.**
We use the `new` operator to instantiate the exception, optionally passing a descriptive message.

**Step 4 — Place it inside an `if` guard** so it triggers only on the bad case.

```java
public class Divider {
    public static double divide(int a, int b) {
        if (b == 0) {
            // Step 4a: Create the exception object
            ArithmeticException ex = new ArithmeticException("Divisor cannot be zero");
            // Step 4b: Throw it explicitly
            throw ex;
        }
        return (double) a / b;
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        System.out.println(divide(10, 2));   // Normal path
        System.out.println(divide(10, 0));   // Abnormal path -> exception
    }
}
```

**Step 5 — Trace the control flow:**

$$
\text{divide(10, 0)} \rightarrow \text{condition true} \rightarrow \text{throw ex} \rightarrow \text{main() has no catch} \rightarrow \text{JVM default handler} \rightarrow \text{terminate}
$$

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Throwing a User-Defined (Custom) Exception

**Problem:** Define a custom checked exception `InvalidAgeException` and throw it from a `setAge(int age)` method if the age is outside `[0, 150]`.

**Step 1 — Define the custom exception class.**

```java
class InvalidAgeException extends Exception {
    public InvalidAgeException(String message) {
        super(message);
    }
}
```

> [!NOTE]
> Extending `Exception` (not `RuntimeException`) makes it a **checked exception**, forcing the caller to either handle it with `try-catch` or declare it with `throws`.

**Step 2 — Write the method that throws it.**

```java
public class Person {
    private int age;

    public void setAge(int age) throws InvalidAgeException {  // Mandatory declaration
        if (age < 0 || age > 150) {
            throw new InvalidAgeException("Age " + age + " is out of valid range [0, 150]");
        }
        this.age = age;
    }
}
```

**Step 3 — Call it from a method that handles the exception.**

```java
public class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Person p = new Person();
        try {
            p.setAge(25);    // OK
            p.setAge(200);   // Throws InvalidAgeException
        } catch (InvalidAgeException e) {
            System.out.println("Caught: " + e.getMessage());
        }
    }
}
```

**Step 4 — Dry-run analysis:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{p.setAge(25)} &\rightarrow \text{condition false} \rightarrow \text{normal assignment} \rightarrow \text{returns} \\
\text{p.setAge(200)} &\rightarrow \text{condition true} \rightarrow \text{throw new InvalidAgeException} \\
&\rightarrow \text{propagates to setAge's caller (main)} \\
&\rightarrow \text{main's catch matches} \rightarrow \text{prints "Caught: Age 200..."}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Re-throwing an Exception

**Problem:** Demonstrate how `throw` can be used inside a `catch` block to re-raise the same (or a wrapped) exception.

```java
public class RethrowDemo {
    public static void readFile(String path) throws Exception {
        try {
            java.io.FileReader fr = new java.io.FileReader(path);
            // ... read operations ...
        } catch (java.io.FileNotFoundException e) {
            System.out.println("Logging the error: " + e.getMessage());
            throw e;     // Re-throw the same exception to the caller
        }
    }
}
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why re-throw?** A method may want to perform partial recovery (e.g., logging, closing resources) but still signal failure to its caller. The `throw e;` statement inside `catch` enables this pattern.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — `throw` vs `throws` vs `Throwable`

| Property | `throw` | `throws` | `Throwable` |
|----------|---------|----------|-------------|
| **Type** | Keyword / Statement | Keyword / Clause | Class |
| **Purpose** | To actually throw an exception | To declare possible exceptions | Superclass of all exceptions/errors |
| **Location** | Inside method body | In method signature | Type used in `throw` or `catch` |
| **Number of exceptions** | Exactly **one** per statement | Can list **multiple**, comma-separated | N/A |
| **Mandatory `new`?** | Usually yes | N/A | Must extend it |
| **Checked exception rule** | Caller must handle OR method must `throws` | Method signature must list it | N/A |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Flowchart: Execution Path of a `throw` Statement

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Program enters try block] --> B{Condition for error?}
    B -- No --> C[Execute normal statements]
    C --> D[End of try block reached]
    D --> E[Skip catch blocks]
    E --> F[Continue after try-catch]
    B -- Yes --> G[Execute throw new XxxException]
    G --> H[Create Throwable object on heap]
    H --> I[Terminate current statement sequence]
    I --> J{JVM searches for matching catch}
    J -- Match found --> K[Bind exception object to catch parameter]
    K --> L[Execute catch block body]
    L --> F
    J -- No match --> M[Propagate to outer try-catch or caller]
    M --> N{Handler in caller?}
    N -- Yes --> K
    N -- No --> O[JVM default uncaught exception handler]
    O --> P[Print stack trace and terminate program]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Call-Stack Unwinding on `throw`

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph stack1[Call Stack at Time of Throw]
        direction TB
        m3[main method - executing p.setAge 200]
        m2[setAge method - condition true, throw new InvalidAgeException]
        m1[InvalidAgeException object sitting on heap]
        m3 --> m2
        m2 -.throws to.-> m3
        m1 -.propagated to.-> m3
    end
    m3 --> handler{Matching catch in main?}
    handler -- Yes --> cb[catch InvalidAgeException block executes]
    handler -- No --> term[Program terminates via default handler]
```

### 4.3 Exception Class Hierarchy (for `throw` targets)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[java.lang.Object] --> B[java.lang.Throwable]
    B --> C[java.lang.Error]
    B --> D[java.lang.Exception]
    C --> C1[OutOfMemoryError]
    C --> C2[StackOverflowError]
    D --> E[java.lang.RuntimeException]
    D --> F[java.io.IOException]
    D --> G[java.sql.SQLException]
    E --> E1[ArithmeticException]
    E --> E2[NullPointerException]
    E --> E3[ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException]
    E --> E4[NumberFormatException]
    F --> F1[FileNotFoundException]
    F --> F2[EOFException]
    G --> G1[SQLSyntaxErrorException]
```

> [!NOTE]
> Any node on this tree (Error, Exception, RuntimeException, IOException, or a custom user-defined class extending one of these) is a **legal operand for `throw`**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is the purpose of the `throw` keyword in Java? How does it differ from the `throws` keyword?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **`throw`** keyword in Java is used to **explicitly raise (trigger) an exception** at runtime. It is followed by an instance of `Throwable` (or its subclass) and causes the normal program flow to halt, propagating the exception object up the call stack to the nearest matching `catch` block.

**Differences from `throws`:**

| Aspect | `throw` | `throws` |
|--------|---------|----------|
| Nature | An action/statement | A declaration/clause |
| Placement | Inside method body | In method signature |
| Purpose | Actually dispatches the exception | Declares exceptions the method might pass up |
| Count | One exception per statement | Can declare multiple, comma-separated |

*(Self-explanatory tabular form fetches full 3 marks in KTU valuation.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Can we throw multiple exceptions using a single `throw` statement in Java? Justify your answer.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**No**, a single `throw` statement in Java can throw **only one exception object at a time**. The syntax requires exactly one `Throwable` instance as its operand:

```java
throw new ArithmeticException("Error");   // Valid
throw new IOException(), new SQLException();  // INVALID — compile-time error
```

If multiple errors need to be conveyed, the recommended approach is to use **exception chaining** — wrap the secondary exception as a *cause*:

```java
throw new RuntimeException("Primary error", new IllegalStateException("Root cause"));
```

This single `throw` can then carry diagnostic information about multiple failure points, satisfying KTU's expectation of clean, idiomatic code.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the syntax and operational rules of the `throw` keyword in Java with a suitable example. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Write a Java program that defines a custom checked exception `InsufficientBalanceException`. Use it in a `BankAccount` class where the `withdraw(double amount)` method throws this exception if the balance is insufficient. Demonstrate handling in `main`. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Syntax and Rules of `throw` (7 marks):**

**Syntax:**
```java
throw <throwable_instance>;
```

**Key Operational Rules (state each for 1 mark; example fetches remaining marks):**

1. The operand must be an object of type `Throwable` or its subclass — never a primitive.
2. Only **one** exception can be thrown per `throw` statement.
3. After `throw` executes, control immediately transfers out of the current block.
4. A `throw` statement causes stack unwinding until a matching `catch` is found.
5. If `throw` is used for a **checked** exception outside a `try` block, the enclosing method must declare it using `throws`.
6. `throw` can be used inside a `catch` block to re-throw or wrap the caught exception.

**Illustrative Example:**

```java
public void setSpeed(int speed) {
    if (speed < 0 || speed > 200) {
        throw new IllegalArgumentException("Speed out of range: " + speed);
    }
    this.speed = speed;
}
```

---

**(b) — Custom Exception Program (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Define the custom checked exception.**

```java
class InsufficientBalanceException extends Exception {
    public InsufficientBalanceException(String message) {
        super(message);
    }
}
```

**Step 2: Implement the `BankAccount` class.**

```java
class BankAccount {
    private String holder;
    private double balance;

    public BankAccount(String holder, double balance) {
        this.holder = holder;
        this.balance = balance;
    }

    public void withdraw(double amount) throws InsufficientBalanceException {
        if (amount <= 0) {
            throw new InsufficientBalanceException("Withdrawal amount must be positive");
        }
        if (amount > balance) {
            throw new InsufficientBalanceException(
                "Insufficient balance. Available: " + balance + ", Requested: " + amount
            );
        }
        balance -= amount;
        System.out.println("Withdrawal successful. New balance: " + balance);
    }

    public double getBalance() { return balance; }
}
```

**Step 3: Demonstrate handling in `main`.**

```java
public class BankApp {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        BankAccount acc = new BankAccount("Anand", 5000.0);
        try {
            acc.withdraw(2000);   // Success path
            acc.withdraw(4000);   // Fails — throws exception
        } catch (InsufficientBalanceException e) {
            System.out.println("Transaction failed: " + e.getMessage());
        }
        System.out.println("Final balance: " + acc.getBalance());
    }
}
```

**Expected Output:**
```
Withdrawal successful. New balance: 3000.0
Transaction failed: Insufficient balance. Available: 3000.0, Requested: 4000.0
Final balance: 3000.0
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Custom Exception Questions:**
> 1. Forgetting the `throws` clause in `withdraw` — the **checked** exception rule mandates this. Loss: **1 mark**.
> 2. Extending `RuntimeException` instead of `Exception` — the question asked for a *checked* exception. Loss: **1 mark**.
> 3. Not calling `super(message)` in the custom exception constructor — the message is lost. Loss: **0.5–1 mark**.
> 4. Putting business logic inside the exception class — exception classes should remain lean. Loss: **1 mark**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** Discuss the difference between checked and unchecked exceptions in Java. Give one example of each and explain how `throw` behaves differently for them. *(7 marks)*
