**(b)** Write a complete Java program that validates a user's password. Define a custom unchecked exception `WeakPasswordException`. The `validate(String pwd)` method must `throw` this exception if the password is shorter than 8 characters, contains no digit, or contains no uppercase letter. Handle it in `main` with appropriate user feedback. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Checked vs Unchecked Exceptions (7 marks):**

| Property | Checked Exceptions | Unchecked Exceptions |
|----------|-------------------|---------------------|
| Superclass | Direct subclasses of `Exception` (not `RuntimeException`) | Subclasses of `RuntimeException` |
| Compile-time check? | **Yes** — compiler enforces handling/declaration | **No** — compiler does not enforce |
| When detected | At compile time | At runtime |
| Typical cause | External resources (I/O, DB, network) | Programming bugs (logic errors) |
| Example | `IOException`, `SQLException` | `ArithmeticException`, `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` |
| `throws` declaration required? | **Yes**, if not handled locally | **No** |

**Behavioral difference with `throw`:**

```java
// Checked — caller MUST handle or declare
public void read() throws IOException {
    throw new IOException("File missing");
}

// Unchecked — no declaration needed
public void divide(int a, int b) {
    if (b == 0) throw new ArithmeticException("Div by zero");
}
```

In the first case, the compiler will **reject** the code unless `main` either wraps the call in `try-catch` or declares `throws IOException`. In the second, the code compiles cleanly because `ArithmeticException` is unchecked.

---

**(b) — Password Validation Program (7 marks):**

```java
// Step 1: Custom unchecked exception
class WeakPasswordException extends RuntimeException {
    public WeakPasswordException(String message) {
        super(message);
    }
}

// Step 2: Validator class
public class PasswordValidator {

    public static void validate(String pwd) {
        if (pwd == null || pwd.length() < 8) {
            throw new WeakPasswordException("Password must be at least 8 characters long");
        }
        if (!pwd.matches(".*\\d.*")) {
            throw new WeakPasswordException("Password must contain at least one digit");
        }
        if (!pwd.matches(".*[A-Z].*")) {
            throw new WeakPasswordException("Password must contain at least one uppercase letter");
        }
        System.out.println("Password is strong and accepted.");
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        String[] testPasswords = {"abc", "password1", "Password", "SecurePass123"};

        for (String p : testPasswords) {
            try {
                System.out.print("Testing '" + p + "': ");
                validate(p);
            } catch (WeakPasswordException e) {
                System.out.println("REJECTED — " + e.getMessage());
            }
        }
    }
}
```

**Expected Output:**
```
Testing 'abc': REJECTED — Password must be at least 8 characters long
Testing 'password1': REJECTED — Password must contain at least one uppercase letter
Testing 'Password': REJECTED — Password must contain at least one digit
Testing 'SecurePass123': Password is strong and accepted.
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Validation Programs:**
> 1. Using `==` instead of `.equals()` for String comparison in `if (pwd == null)` is **wrong** in this context (but `==` works only for the null check itself; for content comparison, use `.equals`).
> 2. Throwing a `String` instead of an exception object — compile-time error. Loss: **2 marks**.
> 3. Forgetting to print *which* password failed — students often omit the test identifier. Loss: **0.5 mark**.
> 4. Not testing all three conditions separately — KTU awards marks for each rule. Loss: up to **3 marks**.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — The `throw` Keyword**

- **`throw` is a statement, not a declaration** — it *executes* and transfers control.
- The operand of `throw` must be an **object of `Throwable` or its subclass** — never a primitive.
- Only **one** exception object can be thrown per `throw` statement.
- After `throw`, **the remaining statements in the `try` block are skipped**; control jumps to the matching `catch`.
- For **checked** exceptions, the enclosing method **must declare `throws`** if the throw is not inside a `try-catch`.
- For **unchecked** exceptions (`RuntimeException` and its subclasses), no `throws` declaration is required.
- `throw` is commonly used to **enforce business rules** and **validate inputs**.
- A `throw` inside a `catch` block re-raises the exception to the caller (re-throw pattern).
- The keyword is **lowercase** — `throw` ≠ `Throw` ≠ `THROW`.
- **Custom exceptions** are created by extending `Exception` (checked) or `RuntimeException` (unchecked).
- The exception object's **`getMessage()`** retrieves the descriptive string passed to the constructor.
- **Stack trace** is automatically captured at the point of `throw` — useful for debugging.
- The **first matching `catch`** (by type compatibility) is selected; order of `catch` blocks matters for related exception hierarchies.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: throws and finally
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT414 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** throws and finally

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The `throws` Keyword

In the Java exception handling framework, **`throws`** is a **method signature declaration keyword** used by a programmer to explicitly **propagate** (declare) one or more checked exceptions from a method to its caller, rather than handling them locally. It is part of Java's *checked-exception contract* and is governed by the `java.lang.Throwable` hierarchy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> *"The `throws` clause in a method header enumerates the exception classes that may be propagated up the call stack if they occur within the method body. The responsibility of handling is delegated to the invoking method."*

**Conceptual Analogy — The Insurance Notice:**
Imagine a **high-voltage electrician** named Mr. Method. Before he begins repairing a faulty transformer, he posts a **yellow warning board** on his workshop door that reads: *"DANGER: Risk of Electric Shock — Caller must wear protective gear."* This notice is the `throws` clause. Mr. Method **does not fix the shock himself**; he simply warns the caller (another method) that the hazard is possible and that they must either handle it (`try-catch`) or pass it further up (`throws again`). If no one handles it, the **JVM** acts as the final authority and abruptly terminates the program — the equivalent of a complete power shutdown.

### 1.2 The `finally` Block

The **`finally`** block is an **optional, terminating construct** in a `try-catch` statement that contains code guaranteed to execute regardless of whether an exception is thrown, caught, or even if a `return` statement is encountered in the `try` or `catch` blocks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:**
> *The `finally` block is the recommended location for **resource cleanup operations** such as closing files, releasing database connections, and deallocating network sockets — critical for preventing resource leaks in long-running applications.*

**Conceptual Analogy — The Aircraft Landing Gear:**
Think of `finally` as the **landing gear sequence** of an aircraft. Whether the flight lands **smoothly** (no exception), lands with **turbulence** (exception caught), or even makes an **emergency landing** (uncaught exception), the landing gear *must* be deployed before touchdown. Skipping this step is catastrophic. The `finally` block guarantees the gear is down — your resources are always released — *no matter what happens mid-flight*.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics in Java Exception Handling

| Metric | Value / Rule |
|---|---|
| Default JVM stack trace depth | **Unlimited (until memory exhausted)** |
| Maximum catch blocks per try | **Unlimited (one per exception type)** |
| `finally` blocks per try | **Exactly one** |
| Exceptions declared per `throws` | **Unlimited (comma-separated)** |
| `try` without `catch` allowed? | **Yes, if paired with `finally`** |
| `finally` without `try` allowed? | **No — compile-time error** |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic:** ***"ThrowS = Signature, Finally = Finish"*** — `throws` lives in the **signature**; `finally` always lives at the **finish** of the try-catch construct.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — `throws`

The `throws` clause operates on the principle of **checked exception propagation**, mandated by the Java compiler for all subclasses of `java.lang.Exception` (excluding `RuntimeException`).

**Structured Logic Steps:**

- **Step 1 — Method Invoked:** A method `M` that performs a risky operation (e.g., file I/O, network read) is called.
- **Step 2 — Exception Risk Identified:** The body of `M` contains code that may throw a **checked exception** (verified at compile time, e.g., `IOException`).
- **Step 3 — Declaration in Signature:** The programmer appends `throws ExceptionType` to `M`'s method header. This is a **declaration**, not a `throw`.
- **Step 4 — Propagation:** If the exception occurs at runtime and is **not caught** inside `M`, control transfers upward to the caller with the exception object intact.
- **Step 5 — Caller's Obligation:** The calling method has exactly **two options**:
    - **(a)** Wrap the call in a `try-catch` block, **OR**
    - **(b)** Declare the same (or a parent) exception in its own `throws` clause.
- **Step 6 — Termination Point:** If the exception reaches the `main()` method without being caught, the **JVM default exception handler** prints the stack trace and terminates the program.

**Syntax Form (KTU Board Standard):**

```java
[access_modifier] [static] returnType methodName(parameters)
    throws ExceptionClass1, ExceptionClass2, ... {
    // method body
}
```

**Why this matters in engineering:**
In real-world enterprise Java systems (Spring Boot, Jakarta EE), `throws` declarations form the **public API contract** of methods. Mis-declaring exceptions leads to *Checked Exception Leakage*, which breaks compilation across distributed microservices.

### 2.2 Operational Breakdown — `finally`

**Structured Logic Steps:**

- **Step 1 — Try Block Initiated:** Execution enters the `try` block.
- **Step 2 — Outcome Detection:** The JVM monitors the outcome:
    - **Path A:** No exception → code after `try-catch` runs → `finally` runs.
    - **Path B:** Exception thrown, matching `catch` found → `catch` runs → `finally` runs.
    - **Path C:** Exception thrown, **no matching `catch`** → `finally` runs → exception propagates.
- **Step 3 — Guarantee of Execution:** The `finally` block executes in **all three paths** above.
- **Step 4 — Special Cases:**
    - If `System.exit(0)` is invoked in `try`/`catch` → `finally` **does NOT execute** (JVM shuts down first).
    - If the JVM crashes (e.g., `Runtime.getRuntime().halt(0)`) → `finally` **does NOT execute**.
    - If an infinite loop or deadlock occurs in `try` → `finally` **will not execute**.
    - If `Thread.currentThread().interrupt()` is called and the thread terminates → `finally` **does not execute**.
- **Step 5 — Overriding `return`:** If `try` contains a `return X`, the JVM **evaluates `X` first, stores it**, executes `finally`, **then returns `X`**.

**Syntax Form (KTU Board Standard):**

```java
try {
    // risky code
} catch (ExceptionType e) {
    // handling code
} finally {
    // cleanup code — ALWAYS runs
}
```

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Construct | Position | Purpose | Mandatory Pairing | Compile-time Check |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `throws` | Method signature | Declare exception propagation | With method declaration | **Yes — checked exceptions** |
| `finally` | End of `try-catch` | Guarantee cleanup code execution | With `try` (optional `catch`) | No runtime exception forces exit |
| `try` alone | Start of block | Enclose risky code | With `catch` **or** `finally` (or both) | None |
| `catch` alone | After `try` | Handle specific exception | Must immediately follow `try` | None |
| `try-catch-finally` | Full form | Handle + Cleanup | All three mandatory | None |

**Key Rules Summary (Board-Exam Friendly):**

- `throws` can declare **multiple** exception types separated by commas.
- `finally` **cannot exist without** a preceding `try` block.
- `finally` **overrides** a `return` statement's immediate effect — the return value is preserved, but `finally` runs first.
- `finally` is **skipped only** on JVM shutdown (`System.exit` / `halt`) or fatal crashes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** In database-driven systems, `finally { conn.close(); }` prevents connection-pool exhaustion. In file-processing pipelines, `finally { br.close(); }` prevents file-handle leaks. These are **production-grade patterns** assessed in KTU viva voce.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Walkthrough: `throws` Propagation Chain

We will derive the execution flow of a multi-level method chain demonstrating `throws` propagation.

**Code Setup:**

```java
class ExceptionChainDemo {

    static void methodC() throws ArithmeticException {
        int result = 10 / 0;   // Line A: triggers ArithmeticException
    }

    static void methodB() throws ArithmeticException {
        methodC();             // Line B: does NOT catch, propagates upward
    }

    static void methodA() {
        try {
            methodB();         // Line C: catches here
        } catch (ArithmeticException e) {
            System.out.println("Handled in methodA: " + e.getMessage());
        }
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        methodA();
    }
}
```

**Execution Derivation — Step by Step:**

**Step 1:** `main()` invokes `methodA()`. Control transfers to `methodA`.

**Step 2:** `methodA` enters its `try` block and calls `methodB()`.

**Step 3:** `methodB()` is declared with `throws ArithmeticException` but does not contain a `try-catch`. It simply calls `methodC()`.

**Step 4:** `methodC()` is also declared with `throws ArithmeticException`. Execution reaches `int result = 10 / 0;` — **division by zero** triggers JVM instantiation of a new `ArithmeticException` object (message: `/ by zero`).

**Step 5:** Since `methodC` has no `catch`, the exception **propagates up the stack** to `methodB`. `methodB` has no `catch` either, so it **propagates further** to `methodA`.

**Step 6:** `methodA`'s `catch (ArithmeticException e)` matches the propagated exception type exactly.

**Step 7:** The matched handler executes: prints *"Handled in methodA: / by zero"*.

**Step 8:** Program continues normally after the `try-catch` in `methodA`. `main()` terminates cleanly.

### 3.2 Analytical Walkthrough: `finally` Override of `return`

**Code Setup:**

```java
class FinallyReturnDemo {
    static int computeValue() {
        try {
            return 100;          // Line X: return value prepared
        } catch (Exception e) {
            return 200;
        } finally {
            System.out.println("Finally block executed");
            // Note: NO return here — only side-effect
        }
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        int result = computeValue();
        System.out.println("Returned: " + result);
    }
}
```

**Execution Derivation — Step by Step:**

**Step 1:** `main()` calls `computeValue()`. Execution enters the `try` block.

**Step 2:** The literal `100` is evaluated and **temporarily stored** in a JVM-internal "return slot".

**Step 3:** The `return` statement is *paused* — the JVM checks for any pending `finally` block.

**Step 4:** `finally` executes: prints *"Finally block executed"* to the console.

**Step 5:** Control returns to the `return` statement; the stored value `100` is returned to `main()`.

**Step 6:** `main()` prints *"Returned: 100"*.

**Derived Output:**

```
Finally block executed
Returned: 100
```

**Key Inference Equation:**

$$\text{Returned Value} = \text{Value prepared in try/catch (NOT overwritten by finally unless finally has its own return)}$$

### 3.3 The Definitive Comparison Matrix: `throw` vs `throws` vs `finally`

| Attribute | `throw` | `throws` | `finally` |
|---|---|---|---|
| Keyword category | Used to **explicitly throw** an exception object | Used to **declare** potential exceptions in signature | Used to **guarantee cleanup code execution** |
| Location | Inside method body | In method header / signature | After `try` (and optional `catch`) |
| Syntax token count | 1 instance of `throw` per throw | Multiple exceptions comma-separated | Exactly one `finally` per `try` |
| Object involvement | Requires an **Exception instance** (e.g., `throw new IOException()`) | Only requires **class names** (e.g., `throws IOException`) | Executes arbitrary code statements |
| Checked exception handling | Forces handling if thrown as checked | Informs compiler of checked risk | No direct exception handling role |
| Executes when exception occurs? | Triggers the propagation chain | Has no runtime execution role | **Always executes** (with documented exceptions) |
| KTU exam weightage | Very high (frequent viva question) | Very high (frequent 3-mark question) | Very high (frequent 14-mark question) |

### 3.4 Four Canonical Scenarios — Behavior Matrix

The following matrix exhaustively enumerates the four standard `try-catch-finally` execution scenarios for board-exam preparation.

| Scenario | try block | catch block | finally block | Final outcome |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Runs successfully | Skipped | Runs | Normal flow continues |
| 2 | Throws exception, **caught** | Runs | Runs | Normal flow continues |
| 3 | Throws exception, **NOT caught** | Skipped (or absent) | Runs | Exception propagates to caller |
| 4 | `System.exit(0)` called | Skipped | **Skipped** | JVM terminates immediately |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important KTU Examiner Note:** Scenario 4 is the most commonly tested **trick question**. Students often incorrectly claim `finally` *always* runs. The correct answer: `finally` runs in **all cases EXCEPT** explicit JVM shutdown via `System.exit()` or fatal `Runtime.halt()`.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Exception Propagation with `finally`

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA([Program Entry main]) --> nodeB[Invoke methodA]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Enter try block]
    nodeC --> nodeD{Exception thrown?}
    nodeD -- No --> nodeE[Execute try body fully]
    nodeD -- Yes --> nodeF{Matching catch found?}
    nodeF -- Yes --> nodeG[Execute catch handler]
    nodeF -- No --> nodeH[Propagate exception up call stack]
    nodeE --> nodeI[Execute finally block]
    nodeG --> nodeI
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ{finally contains System.exit?}
    nodeJ -- No --> nodeK[Resume normal / propagation flow]
    nodeJ -- Yes --> nodeL([JVM Terminates — finally skipped])
    nodeK --> nodeM([Program Exit])
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — `throws` Declaration Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    caller1[Caller Method 1] -->|may invoke| callee1[Risky Method]
    callee1 -->|throws declaration| sig[Method Signature with throws clause]
    sig -->|enumerates| ex1[IOException]
    sig -->|enumerates| ex2[SQLException]
    sig -->|enumerates| ex3[ClassNotFoundException]
    caller1 -->|option A try catch| handler[Local Exception Handler]
    caller1 -->|option B throws again| caller2[Upper Caller]
    caller2 -->|propagation continues| jvm[JVM Default Handler]
    jvm -->|unhandled| term([Program Termination with Stack Trace])
```

### 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram — `finally` Execution Guarantee

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> TryEntry
    TryEntry --> TrySuccess : No exception
    TryEntry --> TryException : Exception occurs
    TrySuccess --> FinallyBlock
    TryException --> CatchCheck
    CatchCheck --> CatchMatch : Matching catch
    CatchCheck --> NoMatch : No matching catch
    CatchMatch --> FinallyBlock
    NoMatch --> FinallyBlock
    FinallyBlock --> SystemExitCheck
    SystemExitCheck --> RunFinally : exit not called
    SystemExitCheck --> SkipFinally : System.exit called
    RunFinally --> NormalContinue
    NormalContinue --> [*]
    SkipFinally --> [*]
```

### 4.4 Component Interaction Diagram — `throws` vs `finally`

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SignatureLayer["METHOD SIGNATURE LAYER"]
        direction LR
        m1[public void readFile throws IOException]
    end
    subgraph BodyLayer["METHOD BODY LAYER"]
        direction TB
        t1["try { FileReader fr = new FileReader path }"]
        c1["catch IOException e { log error }"]
        f1["finally { cleanup resources }"]
        t1 --> c1
        c1 --> f1
    end
    subgraph CallerLayer["CALLER LAYER"]
        direction LR
        cl1[Caller may try-catch]
        cl2[Caller may re-throw]
    end
    SignatureLayer --> BodyLayer
    BodyLayer --> CallerLayer
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Paper]**

*Explain the purpose of the `throws` clause in Java. Why is it mandatory for checked exceptions?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The `throws` clause is a **declaration mechanism** used in a method's signature to enumerate the checked exceptions that the method may propagate to its caller if they occur during execution. It does **not handle** the exception; it merely **informs the compiler and the caller** about the potential exception types.

It is **mandatory for checked exceptions** because Java's compile-time safety model requires that any method performing a risky operation (file I/O, network access, database query) must either:
- **(i)** Catch the exception locally using a `try-catch` block, **OR**
- **(ii)** Declare it using `throws` to delegate the responsibility.

This prevents **silent exception leakage** that would otherwise crash the JVM at runtime without warning.

```java
void readFile(String path) throws IOException {
    FileReader fr = new FileReader(path);
}
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake (Examiner Penalty: −1 mark):** Students often confuse `throws` with `throw`. Remember: `throws` is in the **signature** and lists **classes**; `throw` is in the **body** and uses an **object instance**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

*When does the `finally` block NOT execute in Java? Provide two scenarios.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The `finally` block is designed to execute **unconditionally** in all normal and exceptional control flows. However, there are **two specific scenarios** where it is bypassed:

**Scenario 1 — Explicit JVM Shutdown:**
If `System.exit(int status)` is invoked within the `try` or `catch` block, the JVM begins termination **immediately**, and the `finally` block is skipped.

```java
try {
    System.exit(0);
} finally {
    System.out.println("This will NOT print");
}
```

**Scenario 2 — Fatal JVM Crash:**
If `Runtime.getRuntime().halt(int status)` is called or the JVM crashes due to native code errors (e.g., segmentation fault, OutOfMemoryError in critical regions), the `finally` block cannot execute because the JVM is already in a non-functional state.

Additionally, if an **infinite loop** or **deadlock** occurs inside the `try` block, the `finally` block will also not execute because the thread never exits the `try` block to reach `finally`.

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**, with internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the difference between `throw` and `throws` keywords in Java with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Aspect | `throw` | `throws` |
|---|---|---|
| Type | Used to **explicitly throw** an exception | Used to **declare** an exception in method signature |
| Position | Inside method body | In method header |
| Syntax | `throw new ArithmeticException("msg");` | `void m() throws ArithmeticException { }` |
| Instances | Throws **one object at a time** | Declares **one or more classes** |
| Followed by | An **exception instance** | One or more **exception class names** |

**Example demonstrating `throw`:**

```java
static void validateAge(int age) {
    if (age < 18) {
        throw new ArithmeticException("Not eligible to vote");
    } else {
        System.out.println("Eligible");
    }
}
```

**Example demonstrating `throws`:**

```java
static void readFile(String path) throws java.io.IOException {
    java.io.FileReader fr = new java.io.FileReader(path);
}
```

**Key Distinction:**
`throw` is an **action** — it actively creates and dispatches an exception object. `throws` is a **declaration** — it is a passive informational contract placed in the signature to inform the compiler and the caller about potential exception types.

**(b)** Write a Java program demonstrating the use of `try`, `catch`, `finally`, and `throws` together. Show the output and explain each block's role. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

```java
import java.io.*;

class CombinedExceptionDemo {

    static void readData(String path) throws IOException {
        FileReader fr = new FileReader(path);
        BufferedReader br = new BufferedReader(fr);
        try {
            String line = br.readLine();
            System.out.println("Data read: " + line);
        } finally {
            br.close();
            System.out.println("File handle closed in finally");
        }
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            readData("C:/nonexistent.txt");
        } catch (IOException e) {
            System.out.println("IOException caught in main: " + e.getMessage());
        } finally {
            System.out.println("Outer finally executed");
        }
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
IOException caught in main: nonexistent.txt (The system cannot find the file specified)
Outer finally executed
```

**Explanation of Each Block:**

- **`throws` in `readData`:** Declares that the method may propagate `IOException` to its caller (`main`).
- **`try` block in `readData`:** Encloses the risky file-reading operation (`br.readLine()`).
- **`finally` block in `readData`:** Contains the cleanup code (`br.close()`) to release the file handle — guaranteed to execute even if `readLine()` throws.
- **`try-catch` in `main`:** Catches the propagated `IOException` and handles it gracefully.
- **Outer `finally` in `main`:** Demonstrates that `finally` works at multiple levels — guarantees the message is printed regardless of exception outcome.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Discuss the execution flow of a `try-catch-finally` block under four different scenarios. Illustrate with a flowchart. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The `try-catch-finally` construct has **four canonical execution scenarios**, each producing a distinct control flow.

**Scenario 1 — No Exception in try:**

```java
try {
    System.out.println("Try runs");
} catch (Exception e) {
    System.out.println("Catch skipped");
} finally {
    System.out.println("Finally runs");
}
System.out.println("Normal flow");
```
**Output:** `Try runs → Finally runs → Normal flow`

**Scenario 2 — Exception Caught:**

```java
try {
    int x = 10 / 0;
} catch (ArithmeticException e) {
    System.out.println("Caught: " + e.getMessage());
} finally {
    System.out.println("Finally runs");
}
```
**Output:** `Caught: / by zero → Finally runs → Normal flow`

**Scenario 3 — Exception NOT Caught (try-finally only):**

```java
try {
    int x = 10 / 0;
} finally {
    System.out.println("Finally runs");
}
```
**Output:** `Finally runs → ArithmeticException propagates to JVM`

**Scenario 4 — JVM Shutdown via System.exit:**

```java
try {
    System.out.println("Try runs");
    System.exit(0);
} finally {
    System.out.println("Finally skipped");   // NOT executed
}
```
**Output:** `Try runs` (program terminates; finally never reached)

**Flowchart Representation (text-based):**

```
        ┌──────────────┐
        │  Enter try   │
        └──────┬───────┘
               ▼
        ┌──────────────┐
        │ Exception ?  │
        └──────┬───────┘
       Yes ────┼──── No
       ▼               ▼
  ┌─────────┐    ┌──────────┐
  │ Matching│    │ Skip     │
  │ catch ? │    │ catch    │
  └────┬────┘    └─────┬────┘
  Yes  │  No           │
   ▼   │   ▼           │
 Catch  │ Propagate     │
   │    │   │           │
   └──┬─┘   │           │
      ▼     │           │
  ┌────────┐│           │
  │finally │◄───────────┘
  └────┬───┘
       ▼
  ┌─────────┐
  │ System. │
  │ exit ?  │
  └────┬────┘
   Yes │ No
       ▼   ▼
    Halt  Resume
```

**(b)** Explain why `finally` is preferred over placing cleanup code after the `try-catch` block. Provide a code example demonstrating the risk of the latter. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Why `finally` is Superior:**

The `finally` block guarantees execution under **all normal and exceptional control flows**. Placing cleanup code after a `try-catch` is **unsafe** because it will be **skipped** if an exception is thrown and not caught, or if a `return` statement inside `try` causes premature exit.

**Risky Code (without `finally`):**

```java
FileReader fr = null;
try {
    fr = new FileReader("data.txt");
    String line = fr.readLine();
    System.out.println(line);
} catch (IOException e) {
    System.out.println("Error reading file");
}
fr.close();   // ⚠️ DANGEROUS: may be skipped if exception is uncaught
```

In the above code, if `readLine()` throws an `IOException` that is **not caught** (e.g., a more specific subclass), the `fr.close()` line is **never reached**, causing a **file handle leak**.

**Safe Code (with `finally`):**

```java
FileReader fr = null;
try {
    fr = new FileReader("data.txt");
    String line = fr.readLine();
    System.out.println(line);
} finally {
    if (fr != null) {
        fr.close();   // ✅ GUARANTEED to execute
    }
}
```

**Conclusion:** `finally` enforces the **invariant of cleanup**, which is a cornerstone of robust resource management in Java. This is why Java 7 introduced the **try-with-resources** statement as syntactic sugar over this exact pattern.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Patterns Identified by KTU Board Examiners:**
>
> 1. **Confusing `throw` and `throws` (−2 marks):** Spelling error alone costs 1 mark. Wrong usage in code example costs 2 marks. Always write them in **bold** or underline during exams.
> 2. **Claiming `finally` *always* executes (−1 mark):** It does **NOT** execute when `System.exit()` is called. Examiners specifically test this nuance.
> 3. **Forgetting that `try-finally` is valid without `catch` (−1 mark):** Many students incorrectly state that `catch` is mandatory. It is optional if `finally` is present.
> 4. **Not mentioning `Throwable` hierarchy (−1 mark):** KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the exception class hierarchy. Always mention whether the exception is checked (`Exception`) or unchecked (`RuntimeException`).
> 5. **Missing the `throws` keyword in method header (−2 marks):** Code without `throws` will not compile for checked exceptions; examiners deduct marks for incomplete programs.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ `throws` is a **declaration** keyword placed in the **method signature** — it lists exception **classes**, not instances.
- ✅ `throws` is **mandatory** for checked exceptions; **optional** for unchecked exceptions.
- ✅ `finally` is an **optional block** that guarantees execution of cleanup code in all control flows.
- ✅ `finally` is the **recommended location** for closing files, sockets, and database connections.
- ✅ `finally` does **NOT execute** when `System.exit(int)` or `Runtime.getRuntime().halt(int)` is invoked.
- ✅ A `try` block **must** be paired with at least one `catch` block **OR** a `finally` block (or both).
- ✅ `finally` **overrides the immediate effect of `return`** — the return value is preserved, but `finally` runs first.
- ✅ Java 7's **try-with-resources** is a modern alternative to `try-catch-finally` for `AutoCloseable` resources.
- ✅ Unchecked exceptions (`RuntimeException` subclasses) do **not** require `throws` declaration.
- ✅ The exception propagation chain follows the **call stack in reverse order** until a matching `catch` is found.
- ✅ If no method in the call stack catches the exception, the **JVM default handler** terminates the program with a stack trace printout.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Input/Output - I/O Basics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT414 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Input/Output - I/O Basics # 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In Object-Oriented Programming (particularly **Java** and **C++**, the two languages prescribed under the KTU 2024 OOP syllabus), **Input/Output (I/O)** refers to the mechanism by which a program exchanges data with **external entities** such as the keyboard, console, files, network sockets, or other peripheral devices. The KTU 2024 scheme defines I/O under the broader umbrella of **stream-based data handling**, where all data flows are abstracted as a continuous sequence of bytes or characters called a **Stream**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Intent):** > A **Stream** represents a sequence of data flowing from a source (input) to a destination (output). In Java, the `java.io` package provides a rich hierarchy of stream classes that abstract the underlying device, allowing uniform handling of files, network connections, and console I/O. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Garden Hose" Model 💧 Imagine I/O as a **garden hose pipe** connected between two points: - **One end (Source)** = Where data originates (keyboard, file, network). - **Other end (Destination/Sink)** = Where data finally lands (monitor screen, file, printer). - **The pipe itself** = The Stream object. - **Water flowing through the pipe** = The actual data (bytes or characters). You don't need to know **how** the pipe is built or what material it is — you just need to know: 1. Which way the water flows (**Input** or **Output**). 2. Whether the water is made of **tiny droplets (bytes)** or **letters (characters)**. This abstraction is what makes Java I/O elegant — *device independence*. ## 1.3 Classification of Streams (KTU High-Yield Classification) Streams are classified along **two orthogonal axes**: | Axis | Type 1 | Type 2 | |---|---|---| | **Direction of Flow** | Input Stream (read) | Output Stream (write) | | **Data Unit** | Byte Stream (8-bit) | Character Stream (16-bit Unicode) | | **Connection Type** | Node Stream (direct to source) | Filter/Processing Stream (wraps another) | > [!NOTE] > **Rule of Thumb (Frequently Asked in KTU Exams):** > - Use **Byte Streams** for **binary data** (images, audio, executables, `.pdf`, `.class` files). > - Use **Character Streams** for **textual data** (`.txt`, `.csv`, source code, Unicode content). ## 1.4 The Three Standard Streams (Always Open, Auto-Initialized) Every Java program automatically gets three pre-opened streams: | Stream Object | Type | Default Device | Purpose | |---|---|---|---| | `System.in` | `InputStream` (byte) | Keyboard | Standard Input | | `System.out` | `PrintStream` (byte) | Monitor | Standard Output | | `System.err` | `PrintStream` (byte) | Monitor | Standard Error | > [!IMPORTANT] > **`System.in` is a raw byte stream.** To read text comfortably, KTU syllabus mandates wrapping it in `InputStreamReader` (to convert bytes → chars) and then `BufferedReader` (to add efficient line-reading capability). --- # 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Java I/O Class Hierarchy (Authoritative Diagram for KTU) The `java.io` package organizes all stream classes into **two parallel hierarchies** rooted at abstract base classes: ``` Byte Stream Root (4 abstract methods): java.io.InputStream / java.io.OutputStream Character Stream Root: java.io.Reader / java.io.Writer ``` ### 2.1.1 Key Byte Stream Classes | Class | Type | Role | |---|---|---| | `InputStream` (abstract) | Base | Defines `read()`, `close()` | | `FileInputStream` | Concrete | Reads raw bytes from a file | | `BufferedInputStream` | Filter | Adds internal buffer for speed | | `DataInputStream` | Filter | Reads primitive types (`int`, `double`, …) | | `ObjectInputStream` | Filter | Reads serialized objects | | `OutputStream` (abstract) | Base | Defines `write()`, `close()` | | `FileOutputStream` | Concrete | Writes raw bytes to a file | | `PrintStream` | Concrete | `System.out` is an instance | ### 2.1.2 Key Character Stream Classes | Class | Type | Role | |---|---|---| | `Reader` (abstract) | Base | Defines `read()`, `close()` | | `InputStreamReader` | Bridge | Bytes → Chars (charset-aware) | | `FileReader` | Concrete | Reads chars from a text file | | `BufferedReader` | Filter | Adds `readLine()` method | | `Writer` (abstract) | Base | Defines `write()`, `flush()`, `close()` | | `OutputStreamWriter` | Bridge | Chars → Bytes | | `FileWriter` | Concrete | Writes chars to a text file | | `PrintWriter` | Concrete | Formatted text output | ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Reading Logic (The "Pipeline" Pattern) The most important pattern in KTU 2024 I/O is the **decorator pipeline** for reading from the console: ``` Keyboard → System.in (byte) → InputStreamReader (bridge) → BufferedReader (filter) ``` **Why this chain?** 1. **`System.in`** is the raw byte pipe — reading one byte at a time is painfully slow. 2. **`InputStreamReader`** decodes bytes into characters using the platform's default charset (e.g., UTF-8). 3. **`BufferedReader`** holds an internal buffer (default **8192 characters**), so the expensive OS-level `read()` syscall happens in big chunks, not per character. 4. **`readLine()`** — a method that *does not exist* in `InputStreamReader` — is finally available, returning a `String` of the whole line (newline stripped). ## 2.3 Scanner Class — The Modern KTU-Preferred Approach (Java 5+) The `java.util.Scanner` class is the **simplest** way to perform console I/O. It uses **regular expressions** internally to tokenize input, making it ideal for reading primitives and `String` tokens. ### Scanner Constructor Options | Constructor | Source | |---|---| | `new Scanner(System.in)` | Keyboard | | `new Scanner(File)` | File | | `new Scanner(String)` | A literal string | ### Most-Used Scanner Methods (KTU High-Yield) | Method | Returns | Notes | |---|---|---| | `next()` | `String` | Reads one whitespace-delimited token | | `nextLine()` | `String` | Reads entire line until `\n` | | `nextInt()` | `int` | Parses next token as int | | `nextDouble()` | `double` | Parses next token as double | | `nextFloat()` | `float` | Parses next token as float | | `nextLong()` | `long` | Parses next token as long | | `nextBoolean()` | `boolean` | Parses next token as boolean | | `next().charAt(0)` | `char` | Scanner has **no** `nextChar()` (favourite KTU trick question) | | `hasNext()`, `hasNextInt()` | `boolean` | Guard for safe iteration | | `useDelimiter(regex)` | `Scanner` | Changes tokenization boundary | > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfall:** Mixing `nextLine()` after `nextInt()` causes the famous *"scanner skips input"* bug. The `nextInt()` leaves the newline character in the buffer, which `nextLine()` then immediately consumes as an empty string. Always consume the leftover newline with a dummy `nextLine()` call. ## 2.4 Console Class (For Secure Password Input — KTU Bonus) `java.io.Console` (introduced in Java 6) provides `readPassword()` which disables echo: ```java Console c = System.console(); if (c != null) { char[] pwd = c.readPassword("Enter password: "); } ``` This is the **only** standard way to read passwords without echoing them to the terminal. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Rule / Formula | Engineering Utility | |---|---|---| | Stream direction rule | `Source → Program = Input` ; `Program → Sink = Output` | Foundation of all I/O logic | | 1 char in Java | = **16 bits** (UTF-16 code unit) | Unicode global software | | 1 byte in Java | = **8 bits** (signed: $-128$ to $+127$) | Binary file processing | | Buffer size (default) | `BufferedReader` = **8192 chars** ; `BufferedInputStream` = **8192 bytes** | Performance tuning | | Conversion identity | $\text{InputStreamReader}(x) = x_{\text{bytes}} \rightarrow x_{\text{chars}}$ | Bridge pattern | | File size formula | $\text{size}_{\text{bytes}} = \text{size}_{\text{chars}} \times 2$ (ASCII only) | Storage estimation | | Scanner token regex | Default delimiter = `\s+` (whitespace) | Tokenization logic | | `read()` return value | `-1` = **EOF** (End of File) | Loop termination condition | --- # 📕 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Examples & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Reading a Line of Text Using the `BufferedReader` Pipeline (Full Derivation) **Problem Statement:** Read a full line of text from the keyboard and print it back with the number of characters. ### Step 1 — Identify the raw data source The raw source is `System.in`, which is of declared type `InputStream` (byte-oriented). ### Step 2 — Bridge bytes → characters Construct an `InputStreamReader` wrapping `System.in`. This conversion uses the **default charset** of the JVM (typically UTF-8 on Linux/macOS, Cp1252 on legacy Windows). ### Step 3 — Wrap for line-level buffering Construct a `BufferedReader` wrapping the `InputStreamReader`. The buffered reader maintains an internal **char array of size 8192**, refilling it lazily. ### Step 4 — Call the high-level method Invoke `readLine()`, which scans the buffer up to (but not including) `\r\n`, `\n`, or EOF. ### Step 5 — Complete Java Implementation ```java import java.io.BufferedReader; import java.io.IOException; import java.io.InputStreamReader; public class ReadLineDemo { public static void main(String[] args) throws IOException { // Step 2 & 3: Construct the pipeline (Keyboard → Bytes → Chars → Buffered) BufferedReader br = new BufferedReader( new InputStreamReader(System.in)); System.out.print("Enter a sentence: "); // Step 4: Read a full line (returns null at EOF) String line = br.readLine(); // Output analysis if (line != null) { int len = line.length(); System.out.println("You entered : " + line); System.out.println("Length : " + len + " characters"); } // Step 6: Explicit close (good practice; auto-closed at JVM exit) br.close(); } } ``` ### Step 6 — Tracing execution $$ \text{User types: "Hello KTU"} \;\xrightarrow{\text{System.in}}\; \text{byte stream: 48 65 6C 6C 6F 20 4B 54 55 0A} $$ $$ \xrightarrow{\text{InputStreamReader}} \text{chars: 'H' 'e' 'l' 'l' 'o' ' ' 'K' 'T' 'U'} $$ $$ \xrightarrow{\text{readLine()}} \text{String "Hello KTU"} \;\Rightarrow\; \texttt{line.length()} = 9 $$ ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Scanner-based Reading of Mixed-Type Data **Problem Statement:** Read a student's name (String), roll number (int), and CGPA (double) from the console and print a formatted report. ```java import java.util.Scanner; public class StudentReport { public static void main(String[] args) { // System.in is a static final InputStream; we wrap it once. Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in); System.out.print("Enter name : "); String name = sc.nextLine(); // reads whole line System.out.print("Enter roll no: "); int roll = sc.nextInt(); // parses next token System.out.print("Enter CGPA : "); double cgpa = sc.nextDouble(); // parses next token // Formatted printing with printf (format specifiers) System.out.printf("%n--- Report ---%n"); System.out.printf("Name : %s%n", name); System.out.printf("Roll : %d%n", roll); System.out.printf("CGPA : %.2f%n", cgpa); sc.close(); } } ``` ### Format Specifier Cheat Sheet (Frequently Asked in KTU) | Specifier | Type | Example Output | |---|---|---| | `%d` | decimal integer | `42` | | `%f` | floating point | `3.140000` | | `%.2f` | 2-decimal float | `3.14` | | `%s` | string | `KTU` | | `%c` | character | `A` | | `%n` | platform newline | (line break) | | `%10s` | right-aligned width 10 | ` KTU` | | `%-10s` | left-aligned width 10 | `KTU ` | ## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Demonstrating the `nextLine()` After `nextInt()` Pitfall ```java import java.util.Scanner; public class ScannerPitfall { public static void main(String[] args) { Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in); System.out.print("Enter age : "); int age = sc.nextInt(); // consumes "21" but leaves '\n' in buffer System.out.print("Enter city: "); String city = sc.nextLine(); // reads ONLY the leftover '\n' → returns ""! System.out.println("Age = " + age); System.out.println("City = [" + city + "]"); // prints [] sc.close(); } } ``` ### ✅ Corrected Version ```java int age = sc.nextInt(); sc.nextLine(); // consume the dangling newline String city = sc.nextLine(); // now reads the actual city name ``` ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What? | Requirement | Recommended Class | Reason | |---|---|---| | Read line of text from keyboard | `BufferedReader` | Fast, minimal overhead | | Read mixed primitives from keyboard | `Scanner` | Auto-parsing, type-safe | | Read binary file (image, audio) | `FileInputStream` | Byte-level fidelity | | Read text file | `FileReader` / `BufferedReader` | Charset correctness | | Read serialized Java objects | `ObjectInputStream` | Deserialization | | Write formatted text to console | `PrintStream` (`System.out`) | Built-in formatting | | Write formatted text to file | `PrintWriter` | Doesn't throw checked exceptions | | Read password securely | `Console.readPassword()` | Disables echo | | Tokenize CSV / custom delimiters | `Scanner.useDelimiter()` | Regex flexibility | --- # 📙 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Java I/O Class Hierarchy (Byte Streams) ```mermaid graph TD A["java.io.InputStream (abstract)"]:::base --> B["FileInputStream"] A --> C["ByteArrayInputStream"] A --> D["PipedInputStream"] A --> E["FilterInputStream"]:::filter E --> F["BufferedInputStream"] E --> G["DataInputStream"] E --> H["PushbackInputStream"] I["java.io.OutputStream (abstract)"]:::base --> J["FileOutputStream"] I --> K["ByteArrayOutputStream"] I --> L["PipedOutputStream"] I --> M["FilterOutputStream"]:::filter M --> N["BufferedOutputStream"] M --> O["DataOutputStream"] M --> P["PrintStream"] classDef base fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#0f172a,color:#ffffff classDef filter fill:#9333ea,stroke:#581c87,color:#ffffff ``` ## 4.2 Java I/O Class Hierarchy (Character Streams) ```mermaid graph TD A1["java.io.Reader (abstract)"]:::base --> B1["InputStreamReader"]:::bridge B1 --> C1["FileReader"] A1 --> D1["CharArrayReader"] A1 --> E1["BufferedReader"]:::filter E1 --> F1["LineNumberReader"] A1 --> G1["FilterReader"]:::filter G1 --> H1["PushbackReader"] A2["java.io.Writer (abstract)"]:::base --> B2["OutputStreamWriter"]:::bridge B2 --> C2["FileWriter"] A2 --> D2["CharArrayWriter"] A2 --> E2["BufferedWriter"]:::filter A2 --> F2["PrintWriter"]:::filter A2 --> G2["FilterWriter"]:::filter G2 --> H2["StringWriter"] classDef base fill:#065f46,stroke:#064e3b,color:#ffffff classDef bridge fill:#b45309,stroke:#78350f,color:#ffffff classDef filter fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#431407,color:#ffffff ``` ## 4.3 The Console Read Pipeline (Decorator Pattern in Action) ```mermaid flowchart LR K["Keyboard Hardware"]:::device --> SIN["System.in
InputStream (byte)"] SIN --> ISR["InputStreamReader
Bridge: byte to char"] ISR --> BR["BufferedReader
Filter: 8KB buffer + readLine()"] BR --> APP["Your Java Program
String line = br.readLine()"] APP --> PR["System.out
PrintStream"] PR --> MON["Monitor / Console"]:::device classDef device fill:#0f172a,stroke:#020617,color:#ffffff ``` ## 4.4 Scanner Tokenization Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Scanner Constructor
new Scanner(System.in)"] --> B["Internal Pattern
Default delimiter: s+"] B --> C{"User input buffer
has tokens?"} C -- Yes --> D["next() / nextInt() / nextDouble()"] D --> E["Returns parsed token"] C -- No --> F["Blocks for user typing"] F --> C classDef node fill:#1e40af,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff class A,B,C,D,E,F node ``` --- # 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, June 2024 Pattern]** ### **Q1. Differentiate between Byte Streams and Character Streams in Java.** *(3 Marks)* **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Byte Streams | Character Streams | |---|---|---| | Root classes | `InputStream` / `OutputStream` | `Reader` / `Writer` | | Data unit | 8-bit bytes | 16-bit Unicode characters | | Package | `java.io` | `java.io` | | Charset handling | Charset-blind | Charset-aware | | Use case | Binary data (images, audio, `.pdf`, `.class`) | Text data (`.txt`, `.csv`, `.java`) | | Example classes | `FileInputStream`, `FileOutputStream` | `FileReader`, `FileWriter` | > ✅ *Award 3 marks if at least **4 valid points** are given; KTU examiners specifically look for the **8-bit vs 16-bit** distinction.* --- ### **Q2. What is a Stream? Explain the role of `System.in`, `System.out`, and `System.err`.** *(3 Marks)* **Model Answer:** A **Stream** is an abstraction representing a flow of data between a source and a destination. Java's `java.io` package implements streams as objects of classes that provide methods to read/write data sequentially. - **`System.in`** — an object of type `InputStream`, connected by default to the **keyboard** (standard input). - **`System.out`** — an object of type `PrintStream`, connected by default to the **monitor** (standard output). - **`System.err`** — an object of type `PrintStream`, connected by default to the **monitor**, but used exclusively for **error messages** (unbuffered, so errors appear immediately). All three are **automatically opened** when a Java program starts and **closed** when it terminates normally. --- ## 5.2 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam — Module 3 Internal Choice Pattern]** ### ✏️ **Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks** **a)** Explain the Java I/O stream class hierarchy with a neat diagram. Differentiate between `InputStream` and `Reader` hierarchies. *(7 Marks)* **b)** Write a Java program to read a text file character by character using `FileReader` wrapped with `BufferedReader`, count the number of vowels, and display the result. *(7 Marks)* --- #### **Solution to Q.A(a):** **Java I/O Hierarchy:** Java's `java.io` package contains **four abstract base classes** that form two parallel hierarchies: **Byte Stream Hierarchy:** - `java.io.InputStream` (abstract) → subclasses: `FileInputStream`, `ByteArrayInputStream`, `PipedInputStream`, `FilterInputStream` (parent of `BufferedInputStream`, `DataInputStream`). - `java.io.OutputStream` (abstract) → subclasses: `FileOutputStream`, `ByteArrayOutputStream`, `PipedOutputStream`, `FilterOutputStream` (parent of `BufferedOutputStream`, `DataOutputStream`, `PrintStream`). **Character Stream Hierarchy:** - `java.io.Reader` (abstract) → subclasses: `InputStreamReader` (bridge), `FileReader`, `BufferedReader`, `CharArrayReader`. - `java.io.Writer` (abstract) → subclasses: `OutputStreamWriter` (bridge), `FileWriter`, `BufferedWriter`, `PrintWriter`. **Difference between `InputStream` and `Reader`:** | Feature | `InputStream` | `Reader` | |---|---|---| | Data unit | Byte (8-bit) | Character (16-bit Unicode) | | Core read method | `int read()` returns `0–255` or `-1` | `int read()` returns `0–65535` or `-1` | | Buffer class | `BufferedInputStream` | `BufferedReader` | | Charset awareness | No | Yes (uses `Charset`/`CharsetDecoder`) | | Use case | Binary I/O | Text I/O | **Decorator Pattern:** Stream classes use the **Decorator design pattern** — a filter stream (like `BufferedReader`) wraps a node stream (like `FileReader`) to add functionality at runtime, rather than via inheritance. *(Award 7 marks: 3 for hierarchy diagram, 2 for InputStream vs Reader table, 2 for Decorator explanation.)* --- #### **Solution to Q.A(b):** ```java import java.io.BufferedReader; import java.io.FileReader; import java.io.IOException; public class VowelCounter { public static void main(String[] args) { String fileName = "input.txt"; int vowelCount = 0; // Try-with-resources: auto-closes the stream even on exception try (BufferedReader br = new BufferedReader(new FileReader(fileName))) { int ch; // read() returns -1 at EOF while ((ch = br.read()) != -1) { char c = Character.toLowerCase((char) ch); if (c == 'a' || c == 'e' || c == 'i' || c == 'o' || c == 'u') { vowelCount++; } } System.out.println("Total vowels in " + fileName + " : " + vowelCount); } catch (IOException e) { System.out.println("Error reading file: " + e.getMessage()); } } } ``` **Explanation of Key Steps:** 1. **`new FileReader(fileName)`** opens a character stream to the file (charset = default JVM charset). 2. **`new BufferedReader(...)`** wraps it to add an 8 KB internal buffer + efficient reading. 3. **`br.read()`** returns the **next character as an `int`** (so it can also return `-1` for EOF without clashing with any valid char). 4. **`Character.toLowerCase((char) ch)`** normalizes to lowercase for uniform vowel matching. 5. **`try (...)`** (try-with-resources, Java 7+) auto-closes the stream — even if an exception is thrown. **Sample Input File (`input.txt`):** ``` Hello World ``` **Sample Output:** ``` Total vowels in input.txt : 3 ``` *(Award 7 marks: 2 for stream construction, 2 for read loop, 2 for vowel logic, 1 for output.)* --- ### ✏️ **Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks** **a)** Explain the `Scanner` class in Java. List any five methods with their return types. *(7 Marks)* **b)** Write a Java program that accepts an employee's name, basic pay, and years of experience from the console using `Scanner`, computes the net salary (HRA = 20% of basic, DA = 50% of basic, Tax = 10% of gross if experience < 5 years else 5%), and displays a formatted salary slip using `printf`. *(7 Marks)* --- #### **Solution to Q.B(a):** The **`java.util.Scanner`** class (introduced in Java 5) is the simplest text-scanning primitive. It tokenizes input using a regular-expression delimiter (default `\s+`, i.e., whitespace). **Five Important Methods:** | Method | Return Type | Description | |---|---|---| | `next()` | `String` | Finds and returns the next complete token | | `nextLine()` | `String` | Advances past the current line and returns the skipped input | | `nextInt()` | `int` | Scans the next token as a signed decimal integer | | `nextDouble()` | `double` | Scans the next token as a double-precision float | | `hasNext()` | `boolean` | Returns true if this scanner has another token in its input | **Key Internal Mechanism:** Scanner uses `java.util.regex.Pattern` to break input into tokens and `java.lang.Integer.parseInt()`, `Double.parseDouble()`, etc., to convert tokens to primitives. Throws **`InputMismatchException`** (a `RuntimeException`) if the token does not match the expected type. *(Award 7 marks: 3 for Scanner overview, 4 for the five-method table.)* --- #### **Solution to Q.B(b):** ```java import java.util.Scanner; public class SalarySlip { public static void main(String[] args) { Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in); // ---------- INPUT SECTION ---------- System.out.print("Enter employee name : "); String name = sc.nextLine(); System.out.print("Enter basic pay (INR) : "); double basic = sc.nextDouble(); System.out.print("Enter years of experience : "); int years = sc.nextInt(); // ---------- COMPUTATION ---------- double hra = 0.20 * basic; double da = 0.50 * basic; double gross = basic + hra + da; double taxRate; if (years < 5) { taxRate = 0.10; // 10% tax for juniors } else { taxRate = 0.05; // 5% tax for seniors (reward for experience) } double tax = taxRate * gross; double netPay = gross - tax; // ---------- FORMATTED OUTPUT ---------- System.out.printf("%n================ SALARY SLIP ================%n"); System.out.printf("Employee Name : %-25s%n", name); System.out.printf("Experience (Years) : %-25d%n", years); System.out.printf("--------------------------------------------- %n"); System.out.printf("Basic Pay : INR %15.2f%n", basic); System.out.printf("HRA (20%%) : INR %15.2f%n", hra); System.out.printf("DA (50%%) : INR %15.2f%n", da); System.out.printf("--------------------------------------------- %n"); System.out.printf("Gross Salary : INR %15.2f%n", gross); System.out.printf("Tax Deducted (%.0f%%) : INR %15.2f%n", taxRate * 100, tax); System.out.printf("--------------------------------------------- %n"); System.out.printf("NET PAY : INR %15.2f%n", netPay); System.out.printf("=============================================%n"); sc.close(); } } ``` **Sample Run:** ``` Enter employee name : Anjali Rajan Enter basic pay (INR) : 50000 Enter years of experience : 3 ================ SALARY SLIP ================ Employee Name : Anjali Rajan Experience (Years) : 3 --------------------------------------------- Basic Pay : INR 50000.00 HRA (20%) : INR 10000.00 DA (50%) : INR 25000.00 --------------------------------------------- Gross Salary : INR 85000.00 Tax Deducted (10%) : INR 8500.00 --------------------------------------------- NET PAY : INR 76500.00 ============================================= ``` **Mathematical Derivation of Net Pay:** $$ \text{HRA} = 0.20 \times B $$ $$ \text{DA} = 0.50 \times B $$ $$ \text{Gross} = B + \text{HRA} + \text{DA} = B + 0.20B + 0.50B = 1.70B $$ $$ \text{Tax} = \begin{cases} 0.10 \times \text{Gross} & \text{if } \text{years} < 5 \\ 0.05 \times \text{Gross} & \text{if } \text{years} \geq 5 \end{cases} $$ $$ \text{NetPay} = \text{Gross} - \text{Tax} $$ For the sample values: $B = 50000$, $\text{years} = 3$: $$ \text{NetPay} = 1.70(50000) - 0.10 \times 1.70(50000) = 76500.00 $$ *(Award 7 marks: 2 for input, 2 for computation with conditional tax, 3 for formatted printf output.)* --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks in I/O Questions:** > > 1. **Forgetting `throws IOException` or `try-catch`** — Java's I/O methods are *checked exceptions*. A program that calls `br.readLine()` without handling `IOException` will **not compile**. Always include `throws IOException` in `main` OR wrap in `try-catch`. > > 2. **Confusing `read()` return type** — `read()` returns `int` (NOT `char`). The student must cast: `char c = (char) br.read();`. Writing `char c = br.read();` is a **compile error**. > > 3. **Not using `-1` as the EOF sentinel** — Students often write `while(ch != '\n')` which fails for the last line. The correct idiom is **`while((ch = br.read()) != -1)`**. > > 4. **Mixing `Scanner.nextInt()` and `nextLine()`** — Causes the dangling-newline bug. Always consume the leftover newline. > > 5. **Writing `System.in.readLine()`** — `System.in` is an `InputStream`; it has **no** `readLine()` method. Wrap it first in `BufferedReader`. > > 6. **Closing only the outer stream** — When using try-with-resources on `BufferedReader(FileReader)`, both are auto-closed. Manually, closing only the outer wrapper is acceptable because of cascading close, but explicitly closing both is best practice. > > 7. **Ignoring charset** — `new FileReader("kerala.txt")` uses the **platform default charset**, which on legacy Windows is `Cp1252`, breaking Malayalam characters. Use `new InputStreamReader(new FileInputStream("kerala.txt"), StandardCharsets.UTF_8)` for Unicode safety. --- ## 5.4 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ✅ **Stream** = sequence of data flowing between source and sink. - ✅ Java I/O root classes: **`InputStream`/`OutputStream`** (byte) and **`Reader`/`Writer`** (char). - ✅ **Byte streams** → binary data; **Character streams** → text data. - ✅ **`System.in`** = standard input (byte, raw keyboard); **`System.out`** = standard output (PrintStream, monitor). - ✅ Console reading pipeline: `BufferedReader` ← `InputStreamReader` ← `System.in`. - ✅ `readLine()` exists **only** in `BufferedReader` — not in `System.in`, `InputStream`, or `InputStreamReader`. - ✅ `read()` returns **`-1`** at EOF; it returns an `int`, not a `char`. - ✅ `Scanner` provides auto-parsing of primitives via `nextInt()`, `nextDouble()`, etc. - ✅ `Scanner` has **no** `nextChar()` — use `next().charAt(0)`. - ✅ Mixing `nextInt()` and `nextLine()` requires an extra `nextLine()` to consume the dangling newline. - ✅ `try-with-resources` (`try (BufferedReader br = ...) { ... }`) auto-closes streams — best practice. - ✅ `printf` format specifiers: `%d`, `%f`, `%.2f`, `%s`, `%c`, `%n`, `%-15s` (left-align width 15). - ✅ **Decorator pattern**: filter streams wrap node streams to add functionality dynamically. - ✅ `Console.readPassword()` is the secure way to read passwords (echo disabled). - ✅ Unicode char in Java = **16 bits**; byte = **8 bits**. - ✅ `throws IOException` is mandatory for unchecked stream methods; KTU exam code **must compile**.

topics.title: Reading Console Input
Writing Console Output
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT414 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Writing Console Output # Writing Console Output in Java — Console I/O Fundamentals > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping (PEECT414 – Module 3)** > This topic falls under the *Console I/O & Strings* cluster of Module 3, which introduces the student to standard Java output streams, format specifiers, escape sequences, and the `java.lang.System` / `java.io.PrintStream` class hierarchy — a frequent 3-mark short-answer and 7-mark coding sub-question in KTU End Semester Evaluations (ESE). --- ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Console Output** in Java refers to the mechanism of transmitting character data (text, numbers, objects) from a running Java Virtual Machine (JVM) process to the **Standard Output Stream (stdout)**, conventionally represented by the predefined static field `System.out`. The `System` class (residing in `java.lang`) encapsulates a `public static final PrintStream` object named `out`, which acts as the channel through which formatted or unformatted textual data is rendered on the host operating system's console (CMD, Terminal, IDE Console, etc.). In KTU-syllabus terminology, console output is implemented through **three overloaded methods** of the `PrintStream` class — `print()`, `println()`, and `printf()` — each supporting primitive types, `String` objects, and `Object` references through autoboxing and polymorphic dispatch. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bulletin Board" Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Picture for First-Time Learners:** > Imagine the JVM as a *post office* and the console monitor as a *public bulletin board*. When you call `System.out.println("Hello")`, you are essentially: > > 1. Picking up a pre-installed, public notice printer named `out` (which the post office — `System` — hands to you for free, since it is already initialized). > 2. Handing it a notice card (`"Hello"`). > 3. Asking it to **print** the notice **AND roll the paper to a new line** (`ln` = line-new). > > The bulletin board can never be replaced (because `out` is declared `final`), it is always turned on (it is `static`), and the post office made sure it was ready before you even arrived (it is initialized when the class loads). ### 1.3 The `System.out` Reference — Anatomy | Component | Java Identifier | Type | Lifetime | Purpose | |---|---|---|---|---| | Class Holder | `System` | `final class` (in `java.lang`) | Loaded once by JVM bootstrap classloader | Provides static access to standard I/O streams | | The Stream Object | `out` | `public static final PrintStream` | Exists for the entire JVM lifetime | Default destination for console output | | The Behavior | `println()`, `print()`, `printf()`, `format()`, `write()` | Instance methods of `PrintStream` | Bound to `out` object | Render data to stdout | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Constant to Remember:** > The `System.out` reference is **standard output** defined by ISO C / POSIX. By convention it is the *file descriptor 1* on UNIX-like systems and `STD_OUTPUT_HANDLE` on Windows. KTU expects students to know that this stream is **line-buffered** when connected to a terminal and **block-buffered** when redirected to a file. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Console Output Java's `PrintStream` offers **three principal methods** for console writing. The KTU examiner regularly asks students to differentiate between them in 3-mark questions. ### 2.1.1 `print()` — The "Append-Only" Method - Accepts **a single argument** of type `String`, primitive (`int`, `double`, `char`, `boolean`, `long`, `float`), or any `Object` (via implicit `toString()` call). - **Does NOT append a line separator** after the output. The cursor stays on the same line. - Suitable for building output progressively, e.g., in loops. ### 2.1.2 `println()` — The "Append-and-Newline" Method - Behaves identically to `print()` but **appends the platform's line separator** (`System.lineSeparator()` — typically `"\n"` on UNIX, `"\r\n"` on Windows). - **Overloaded 10 ways** in `PrintStream` to accept each primitive, `String`, `char[]`, and `Object` individually. - A **no-argument version** `println()` simply prints a blank line. ### 2.1.3 `printf()` and `format()` — The "Formatted" Methods - Introduced in **Java 5 (JDK 1.5)** under JSR 268. - Signature: `PrintStream printf(String format, Object... args)` - Internally delegates to a `java.util.Formatter` instance. - Used to produce **aligned, padded, locale-aware** textual output — critical for reports, tables, and scientific computations. - The format string contains **literal text + format specifiers** prefixed by `%`. ## 2.2 Escape Sequences — The "Hidden Character" Vocabulary > [!IMPORTANT] > An **escape sequence** is a backslash-prefixed character literal that the Java compiler translates into a single non-printable control character at compile time. KTU's 2018–2023 ESE papers have featured escape-sequence output-tracing questions worth 3–7 marks. | Escape Sequence | ASCII Code | Meaning | Visual Effect | |---|---|---|---| | `\n` | 10 (LF) | Newline (Line Feed) | Moves cursor to next line | | `\r` | 13 (CR) | Carriage Return | Moves cursor to start of current line | | `\t` | 9 (HT) | Horizontal Tab | Advances to next tab stop (usually every 8 columns) | | `\b` | 8 (BS) | Backspace | Moves cursor one column left | | `\f` | 12 (FF) | Form Feed | Advances to next page (rarely used) | | `\\` | 92 | Backslash literal | Prints a single `\` | | `\'` | 39 | Single-quote literal | Prints a single `'` | | `\"` | 34 | Double-quote literal | Prints a single `"` | | `\0` through `\377` | Octal value | Octal character | Prints the character with the given octal code | ## 2.3 `printf()` Format Specifiers — The Complete Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet for `printf()`:** > Each specifier has the syntax: `%[argument_index$][flags][width][.precision]conversion` | Conversion | Applies To | Example | Output | |---|---|---|---| | `%d` | `int`, `long`, `short`, `byte` | `printf("%d", 42)` | `42` | | `%f` | `float`, `double` | `printf("%.2f", 3.14159)` | `3.14` | | `%e` or `%E` | `float`, `double` | `printf("%e", 1234.0)` | `1.234000e+03` | | `%g` or `%G` | `float`, `double` | `printf("%g", 12345.6)` | `12345.6` | | `%s` | Any `Object` (calls `toString()`) | `printf("%s", "KTU")` | `KTU` | | `%c` | `char` | `printf("%c", 'A')` | `A` | | `%b` | `boolean` | `printf("%b", true)` | `true` | | `%n` | — (platform-independent newline) | `printf("Line%n")` | Inserts `line.separator` | | `%%` | — (literal percent) | `printf("100%%")` | `100%` | | `%x` / `%X` | Hexadecimal integer | `printf("%X", 255)` | `FF` | | `%o` | Octal integer | `printf("%o", 8)` | `10` | | `%h` | Hex hash of object | `printf("%h", obj)` | Hex hashcode | ### 2.4 Flags, Width, and Precision | Flag | Effect | |---|---| | `-` | Left-justify within the field (default is right) | | `+` | Always show sign for numeric values (`+5`, not `5`) | | ` ` (space) | Prefix positive numbers with a single space | | `0` | Pad numeric values with leading zeros | | `,` | Use grouping separators (e.g., `1,000,000`) | | `(` | Enclose negative numbers in parentheses (financial format) | ## 2.5 String Concatenation in Output > [!IMPORTANT] > The `+` operator, when **either operand is a `String`**, becomes the **string concatenation operator**. The non-string operand is automatically converted via `String.valueOf()`, which internally calls `toString()` for objects and applies type-specific formatting for primitives. This is a high-frequency KTU exam topic because it tests operator precedence: `+` is **left-associative**, so `"Sum: " + 10 + 20` evaluates to `"Sum: 1020"`, while `"Sum: " + (10 + 20)` evaluates to `"Sum: 30"`. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Embedded Systems & IoT:** Serial console logging (over UART translated to stdout) for debugging firmware. - **Banking & Financial Software:** `printf("(%,.2f", balance)` for currency reports — the `(` flag shows negatives in red-parentheses style. - **Scientific Computing:** `printf("%e", result)` to dump floating-point output without precision loss. - **Networking & Log Aggregation:** JVMs redirect stdout to syslog / Kafka via Logback for distributed tracing. # 3. Step-by-Step Code Derivations & Analytical Walkthroughs ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — `print()` vs `println()` Behavior > [!NOTE] > **Problem (KTU-Style, 3 Marks):** > Predict the exact console output of the following Java snippet: > ```java > public class Demo { > public static void main(String[] args) { > System.out.print("KTU"); > System.out.println(" 2024"); > System.out.print("B.Tech "); > System.out.println(); > System.out.print("OOP"); > } > } > ``` ### Step-by-Step Tracing | Statement | Cursor State After Execution | Console So Far | |---|---|---| | `System.out.print("KTU");` | Cursor after `KTU` on line 1 | `KTU` | | `System.out.println(" 2024");` | Prints ` 2024`, then newline | `KTU 2024\n` | | `System.out.print("B.Tech ");` | Cursor after `B.Tech ` on line 2 | `KTU 2024\nB.Tech ` | | `System.out.println();` | Prints a blank line (only newline) | `KTU 2024\nB.Tech \n` | | `System.out.print("OOP");` | Cursor after `OOP` on line 3 | `KTU 2024\nB.Tech \nOOP` | ### Final Console Render $$ \boxed{\text{KTU 2024}\\\text{B.Tech }\\\text{OOP}} $$ ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Escape Sequence Tracing > [!NOTE] > **Problem (KTU-Style, 5 Marks):** > Determine the console output: > ```java > System.out.println("Name:\tRahul\nBranch:\tCSE"); > System.out.println("Quote: \"Object Oriented\""); > System.out.println("Path: C:\\Users\\Admin"); > System.out.println("100%% Complete"); > ``` ### Derivation Step-by-Step **Line 1:** `\t` → advances to next tab stop (8 columns). `\n` → newline. Result on console: $$ \text{Name:}\quad\;\;\text{Rahul}\\\text{Branch:}\;\;\text{CSE} $$ **Line 2:** `\"` → literal double-quote. Result: $$ \text{Quote: ``Object Oriented''} $$ **Line 3:** `\\` → literal backslash. Two `\\` produce two `\` characters. Result: $$ \text{Path: C:\textbackslash Users\textbackslash Admin} $$ **Line 4:** `%%` → literal `%` sign. Result: $$ \text{100\% Complete} $$ ## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — `printf()` Format Specifier Application > [!NOTE] > **Problem (KTU Module 3, 7 Marks):** > Write a Java program segment to print a payroll slip with the following layout using `printf()`: > > | EMP_ID | NAME | BASIC | HRA | NET | > |--------|-------|----------|--------|------------| > | 101 | ANIL | 25000.00 | 5000.00| 30000.00 | ### Solution Derivation ```java public class Payroll { public static void main(String[] args) { int id = 101; String name = "ANIL"; double basic = 25000.00; double hra = 5000.00; double net = basic + hra; System.out.printf("%-8s%-10s%-12s%-12s%-12s%n", "EMP_ID", "NAME", "BASIC", "HRA", "NET"); System.out.printf("%-8d%-10s%-12.2f%-12.2f%-12.2f%n", id, name, basic, hra, net); } } ``` ### Tracing the Format Specifiers | Specifier | Width | Justify | Conversion | Effect | |---|---|---|---|---| | `%-8s` | 8 | Left | String | Pads `"EMP_ID"` to 8 chars, left-aligned | | `%-10s` | 10 | Left | String | Pads `"NAME"` to 10 chars | | `%-12s` | 12 | Left | String | Pads `"BASIC"` to 12 chars | | `%-12.2f` | 12 | Left | Floating, 2 decimals | `25000.00` padded to 12 chars | | `%-12.2f` | 12 | Left | Floating, 2 decimals | `5000.00` | | `%-12.2f` | 12 | Left | Floating, 2 decimals | `30000.00` | | `%n` | — | — | Platform newline | Independent of OS | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** Using `%n` instead of `\n` inside `printf()` is the **recommended best practice** in production code because it correctly emits `\r\n` on Windows and `\n` on UNIX/Mac, preventing cross-platform log-parsing bugs. ## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Operator Precedence Trap > [!NOTE] > **Problem (KTU 2019 ESE-style, 5 Marks):** > Explain the difference, with output, between the two statements: > ```java > System.out.println("Total = " + 10 + 20); > System.out.println("Total = " + (10 + 20)); > ``` ### Derivation For `System.out.println("Total = " + 10 + 20);`: - The `+` operator is **left-associative**. - Step 1: `"Total = " + 10` → because one operand is `String`, the `+` is the **concatenation operator**, producing the `String` `"Total = 10"`. - Step 2: `"Total = 10" + 20` → concatenates with `20` to give `"Total = 1020"`. $$ \text{Output: } \boxed{\text{Total = 1020}} $$ For `System.out.println("Total = " + (10 + 20));`: - Parentheses force arithmetic first: `10 + 20 = 30` (an `int`). - Then concatenation: `"Total = " + 30` → `"Total = 30"`. $$ \text{Output: } \boxed{\text{Total = 30}} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Valuation Pitfall:** Many students lose marks here because they incorrectly assume the `int` is *added* before being concatenated. Always remember: **any `+` where EITHER operand is `String` is concatenation; parentheses force arithmetic first.** # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Class & Stream Architecture — `System.out` Flow > [!NOTE] > **Purpose:** This Mermaid block diagram illustrates the runtime hierarchy from the developer-written source code down to the operating system's file descriptor, mapping every architectural layer involved in console output. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Developer Source Code
System.out.println Hello"] --> B["Java Compiler
javac compiles to bytecode"] B --> C["JVM loads java.lang.System
static initializer executes"] C --> D["System.out
public static final PrintStream"] D --> E["PrintStream Instance
overrides 10 println overloads"] E --> F["BufferedWriter Internal
char to byte conversion"] F --> G["FileOutputStream
fd 1 on POSIX systems"] G --> H["Operating System Kernel
writes to TTY or pipe"] H --> I["Terminal Monitor
user sees the text"] style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd style D fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825 style E fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825 style H fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32 style I fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32 ``` ## 4.2 Method Selection Logic — `print` vs `println` vs `printf` ```mermaid flowchart TD Start["Need to write data to console"] --> Q1{"Do you need a newline
after the output?"} Q1 -- "Yes" --> P1["Use System.out.println data"] Q1 -- "No" --> Q2{"Do you need
formatted alignment?"} Q2 -- "Yes" --> P2["Use System.out.printf format args"] Q2 -- "No" --> P3["Use System.out.print data"] P1 --> Done["Data rendered to stdout"] P2 --> Done P3 --> Done style Q1 fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17 style Q2 fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17 style P1 fill:#b2dfdb,stroke:#00695c style P2 fill:#b2dfdb,stroke:#00695c style P3 fill:#b2dfdb,stroke:#00695c style Done fill:#c5e1a5,stroke:#33691e ``` ## 4.3 `printf()` Format Specifier Anatomy — Token Map ```mermaid flowchart LR A["%"] --> B["argument_index$
optional: 1 dollar sign 2 dollar sign"] B --> C["flags
optional: minus plus space zero comma paren"] C --> D["width
optional integer: minimum chars"] D --> E["precision
optional: dot followed by integer"] E --> F["conversion
required: d f s c b n percent"] style A fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c style F fill:#c5cae9,stroke:#1a237e ``` ## 4.4 Escape Sequence Translation Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Source Code
String with backslash"] --> B["Java Compiler
reads escape at compile time"] B --> C["Bytecode Constant Pool
stores the literal char"] C --> D["JVM Runtime
writes raw char to stream"] D --> E["Console
interprets the control char"] style A fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100 style B fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100 style C fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c style D fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c style E fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Q: Differentiate between `System.out.print()` and `System.out.println()` in Java. Give one example for each.** #### Model Answer (3 Marks — Allocation: 1 + 1 + 1) | Aspect | `print()` | `println()` | |---|---|---| | Line Terminator | Does **NOT** append newline after the data | **Appends** a newline (`\n` or `line.separator`) after the data | | Cursor Position | Remains on the **same line** | Moves to the **next line** | | Overloads | 9 overloads (one per primitive + Object) | 10 overloads (the 10th being no-argument) | **Example Code:** ```java System.out.print("Hello"); // Output: Hello (cursor stays on line) System.out.println("World"); // Output: HelloWorld (W appended; then newline) System.out.println(); // Output: blank line only ``` ### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Q: What is an escape sequence in Java? List any four commonly used escape sequences with their meaning.** #### Model Answer (3 Marks — Allocation: 1 + 2) An **escape sequence** is a backslash-prefixed character combination that the Java compiler translates into a specific non-printable or special character at compile time. They are essential for representing characters that cannot be typed directly in source code. | Escape Sequence | Meaning | |---|---| | `\n` | Newline (moves cursor to the next line) | | `\t` | Horizontal Tab (advances to the next tab stop) | | `\\` | Literal backslash character | | `\"` | Literal double-quote character | --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) ### Question A — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]` **Q (a)** Explain the working of `System.out.printf()` method in Java. List any **six** format specifiers with their purpose. **(7 Marks)** **Q (b)** Write a Java program to read two integers from the keyboard (assume Scanner is already configured) and print their sum, difference, product, and quotient using `System.out.printf()` with appropriate field widths and 2-decimal formatting for the quotient. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks The `printf()` method, introduced in Java 5, allows **formatted output**.
