Its signature is: $$ \text{public PrintStream printf(String format, Object... args)} $$ Internally, it delegates to a `java.util.Formatter` instance which parses the format string. A format string consists of **literal text** and **format specifiers** beginning with `%` and ending with a **conversion character**. **Six Format Specifiers (1 mark each ≈ 6 marks; 1 mark for explanation):** | Specifier | Purpose | Example | |---|---|---| | `%d` | Decimal integer | `printf("%d", 25)` → `25` | | `%f` | Floating-point number | `printf("%.2f", 3.14)` → `3.14` | | `%s` | String | `printf("%s", "KTU")` → `KTU` | | `%c` | Character | `printf("%c", 'A')` → `A` | | `%n` | Platform-independent newline | Inserts `\r\n` on Windows, `\n` on UNIX | | `%b` | Boolean | `printf("%b", 5>3)` → `true` | #### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks ```java import java.util.Scanner; public class ArithmeticDemo { public static void main(String[] args) { Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in); System.out.print("Enter first integer : "); int a = sc.nextInt(); System.out.print("Enter second integer: "); int b = sc.nextInt(); int sum = a + b; int diff = a - b; int prod = a * b; double quot = (b != 0) ? (double)a / b : 0.0; System.out.println("===================================="); System.out.printf("%-15s : %10d%n", "Sum", sum); System.out.printf("%-15s : %10d%n", "Difference", diff); System.out.printf("%-15s : %10d%n", "Product", prod); System.out.printf("%-15s : %10.2f%n", "Quotient", quot); System.out.println("===================================="); } } ``` **Output (Sample, a=20, b=3):** $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{====================================}\\ &\text{Sum} \;\;\;\; : \;\;\;\;\;\;\; 23\\ &\text{Difference} : \;\;\;\;\;\;\; 17\\ &\text{Product} \;\;\; : \;\;\;\;\;\;\; 60\\ &\text{Quotient} \;\; : \;\;\;\;\;\;\; 6.67\\ &\text{====================================} \end{aligned} $$ **Step-by-step explanation (for valuation):** 1. `%n` is used instead of `\n` for **cross-platform newline** — 1 mark. 2. `%-15s` left-aligns the label within a 15-character field — 1 mark. 3. `%10d` right-aligns the integer in a 10-character field — 1 mark. 4. `%10.2f` formats the double with **2 decimal places** within a 10-character field — 1 mark. 5. Cast `(double)a` prevents integer division truncation — 1 mark. 6. Division-by-zero guard `b != 0` is good practice — 1 mark. 7. Final output traced and verified — 1 mark. --- ### Question B — `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]` (Alternative Choice) **Q (a)** Explain the concept of **escape sequences** in Java. List **eight** commonly used escape sequences with their ASCII codes and write a program that uses each of them. **(7 Marks)** **Q (b)** Explain string concatenation in `System.out.println()`. What is the role of the `+` operator when one operand is a `String`? Illustrate with an example where the result would be ambiguous without parentheses. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks > [!IMPORTANT] > An **escape sequence** is a two-character literal in Java source code, starting with a backslash (`\`), that the compiler translates into a single character (often a control character) during compilation. **Eight Escape Sequences (8 × 0.75 ≈ 6 marks; 1 mark for the program):** | Escape | ASCII | Purpose | |---|---|---| | `\n` | 10 (LF) | Newline | | `\r` | 13 (CR) | Carriage return | | `\t` | 9 (HT) | Horizontal tab | | `\b` | 8 (BS) | Backspace | | `\f` | 12 (FF) | Form feed | | `\\` | 92 | Backslash literal | | `\'` | 39 | Single-quote literal | | `\"` | 34 | Double-quote literal | **Program:** ```java public class EscapeDemo { public static void main(String[] args) { System.out.println("Line1\nLine2"); System.out.println("Col1\tCol2"); System.out.println("Back\bspace"); System.out.println("Quote: \"Hello\""); System.out.println("Apostrophe: \'A\'"); System.out.println("Path: C:\\Windows\\System32"); } } ``` #### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks When **either operand** of the `+` operator is a `String`, the `+` is treated as the **string concatenation operator** rather than arithmetic addition. The non-string operand is converted to its string representation via `String.valueOf()` before joining. **Ambiguity Example (5 marks for illustration + 2 marks for explanation):** ```java System.out.println("Result: " + 5 + 5); // Prints: Result: 55 System.out.println("Result: " + (5 + 5)); // Prints: Result: 10 ``` - **Line 1:** Left-to-right evaluation. `"Result: " + 5` → `"Result: 5"`. Then `"Result: 5" + 5` → `"Result: 55"`. **Concatenation both times**. - **Line 2:** Parentheses force arithmetic first: `5 + 5 = 10` (int). Then `"Result: " + 10` → `"Result: 10"`. **One concatenation, one addition**. --- ## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks in Console Output Questions:** > 1. **Confusing `\n` and `%n`:** `\n` always emits `\n` (LF only), which on Windows Notepad displays the next line without proper line-break spacing. `%n` is the platform-correct choice inside `printf()` — but **never use `%n` with `println()`**; that is a syntax error. > 2. **Forgetting that `out` is `static final`:** Students sometimes attempt `System.out = null;` — this is a **compile-time error** because `out` is `final`. > 3. **Mixing `+` precedence:** `"Age: " + 18 + 2` yields `"Age: 182"`, not `"Age: 20"`. Always wrap arithmetic in parentheses. > 4. **Octal confusion with `\0` to `\377`:** `\08` is **invalid** because `8` is not an octal digit; students often write this in theory questions. > 5. **Format width vs precision misuse:** `%.2f` is a **precision** specifier (2 decimals), while `%10.2f` is **width 10, precision 2** — confusing these loses 1–2 marks per question. > 6. **Escape sequences in `char` literals vs `String` literals:** `'\n'` is a `char` (2 bytes in JVM); `"\n"` is a `String` of length 1. Both behave identically in output, but their types differ — a frequent viva question. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - ✅ `System.out` is a **`public static final PrintStream`** declared in the `java.lang.System` class. - ✅ `print()` → no newline; `println()` → newline; `printf()` → formatted. - ✅ `System.out.println()` is **overloaded 10 times** to accept every primitive + `String` + `Object`. - ✅ The `+` operator becomes the **concatenation operator** if **either** operand is a `String`. - ✅ Use `%n` inside `printf()` for **platform-independent** line breaks. - ✅ `%d` (decimal), `%f` (float), `%s` (string), `%c` (char), `%b` (boolean), `%n` (newline), `%%` (literal `%`). - ✅ Width specifier `%10d` reserves 10 columns (right-aligned by default); `%-10d` left-aligns. - ✅ Precision specifier `%.2f` rounds to 2 decimal places. - ✅ Escape sequences are **resolved at compile time**, not runtime. - ✅ Common escape sequences: `\n`, `\t`, `\r`, `\b`, `\f`, `\\`, `\'`, `\"`. - ✅ `System.out.write()` is also available (from `OutputStream`), but `print`/`println`/`printf` are the preferred high-level APIs. - ✅ In Java 7+, `System.out.printf(locale, format, args)` allows locale-specific number formatting. - ✅ `PrintStream` does **NOT throw `IOException`** for console output (it sets an internal error flag instead). - ✅ For exam answers, always **trace line-by-line** when predicting console output.

topics.title: Print Writer Class
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT414 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Print Writer Class

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**PrintWriter** is a character-based output stream class in the `java.io` package that provides convenient methods to print formatted representations of various data types to a text-output stream. It implements the `Appendable`, `Closeable`, and `Flushable` interfaces and is the character-stream counterpart of the byte-based `PrintStream` class.

```java
public class PrintWriter extends Writer
```

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PEECT414 - Module 3: Stream Based I/O)**, PrintWriter is studied as the recommended character-stream alternative to `System.out.println()` style operations when writing to files, sockets, or any user-supplied `Writer` destination.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** PrintWriter is the **text-formatting engine** of the Java I/O hierarchy. Unlike raw `OutputStreamWriter`, it adds the convenience methods `print()`, `println()`, and `printf()` to a character stream, eliminating manual buffer flushing for individual writes.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a `PrintWriter` as a **smart multilingual secretary** who sits between your Java program and an output device (a file, a network socket, or the console).

- The **raw paper** the secretary writes on is the underlying `Writer` (e.g., `FileWriter`, `BufferedWriter`, `OutputStreamWriter`).
- The **ink** is the data being written (strings, integers, doubles, objects).
- The **secretary** automatically handles the messy tasks: converting any Java primitive (int, double, boolean) into its **human-readable text form**, handling the line separator appropriate for the OS, and optionally **flushing** the buffer after every `print()` call.

Without `PrintWriter`, to write the number `42` as text, you would have to convert it to `"42"` manually, write the characters one by one, and remember to call `flush()`. With `PrintWriter`, you just say `pw.println(42)` and everything else is automatic.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** PrintWriter is a **decorator-friendly** writer. You can wrap virtually any `Writer` inside it, layering formatting behavior on top of existing I/O capabilities (buffering, charset conversion, network transmission).

## 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Character Stream** | Streams that handle 16-bit Unicode characters (Reader/Writer classes) |
| **Auto-flush** | Optional feature that flushes the underlying writer after every `print()`, `println()`, or `printf()` call |
| **Decorating** | Wrapping an existing stream to add new behaviors (classic GoF Decorator pattern) |
| **Line Separator** | Platform-independent newline, obtained via `System.lineSeparator()` |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Class Hierarchy & Position in `java.io`

```
java.lang.Object
   └── java.io.Writer
           └── java.io.PrintWriter
```

`PrintWriter` directly extends `java.io.Writer`, making it a peer of `BufferedWriter`, `FileWriter`, `OutputStreamWriter`, and `StringWriter` — but it carries **formatting superpowers** none of the others have.

## 2.2 Constructor Catalogue

`PrintWriter` offers **six constructors**, all of which are examinable:

| Constructor | Behaviour |
|---|---|
| `PrintWriter(File file)` | Creates a PrintWriter over a `File` (auto-flush disabled, default charset) |
| `PrintWriter(File file, String csn)` | Same, but with an explicit charset name (e.g., `"UTF-8"`) |
| `PrintWriter(OutputStream out)` | Bridges byte-stream to character-stream, **no auto-flush** |
| `PrintWriter(OutputStream out, boolean autoFlush)` | Byte-stream bridge with **optional auto-flush** |
| `PrintWriter(Writer wr)` | Wraps an existing character writer, **no auto-flush** |
| `PrintWriter(Writer wr, boolean autoFlush)` | Wraps a character writer, **optional auto-flush** |
| `PrintWriter(String fileName)` | Opens a file by its path string |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Auto-flush is OFF by default** in all constructors. This is the **single most common examination trap**. The student must explicitly pass `true` if flush-after-every-write is desired.

## 2.3 Method Catalogue — The PrintWriter Toolkit

### 2.3.1 Data-Output Methods (the `print` family)

| Method Signature | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `void print(boolean b)` | Prints the string `"true"` or `"false"` |
| `void print(char c)` | Prints a single character |
| `void print(int i)` | Prints the decimal integer form |
| `void print(long l)` | Prints the decimal long form |
| `void print(float f)` | Prints the float using `Float.toString` |
| `void print(double d)` | Prints the double using `Double.toString` |
| `void print(char[] s)` | Prints an array of characters |
| `void print(String s)` | Prints a string (null prints as `"null"`) |
| `void print(Object obj)` | Prints `obj.toString()` |

### 2.3.2 Line-Terminator Methods (the `println` family)

Every `print` method has a matching `println` variant. `println()` with no arguments simply terminates the current line using `line.separator` system property.

### 2.3.3 Formatted Output

| Method | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `PrintWriter printf(String format, Object... args)` | C-style formatted output (returns `this`) |
| `PrintWriter format(String format, Object... args)` | Same as `printf` |
| `PrintWriter format(Locale l, String format, Object... args)` | Locale-sensitive formatting |

### 2.3.4 Error and State Methods

| Method | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `boolean checkError()` | Returns `true` if an error occurred; **flushes the stream** |
| `protected void setError()` | Sets the internal error flag to `true` |
| `void flush()` | Flushes the underlying writer |
| `void close()` | Flushes and then closes the underlying writer |

## 2.4 The Auto-Flush Contract

The auto-flush flag (when `true`) causes `PrintWriter` to invoke `flush()` on the underlying writer after every invocation of `print`, `println`, or `printf`. This is **critical** for scenarios such as:

- Logging applications that must persist every line immediately (e.g., crash logs).
- Network responses where the client is waiting for streamed data.
- Interactive programs that must display output without buffering.

```text
autoFlush = true   →  print() → write to buffer → flush()   (every call)
autoFlush = false  →  print() → write to buffer           (manual flush required)
```

## 2.5 PrintWriter vs PrintStream — The Definitive Comparison

| Feature | `PrintWriter` | `PrintStream` |
|---|---|---|
| Base class | `Writer` (character stream) | `OutputStream` (byte stream) |
| Data handled | 16-bit Unicode chars | 8-bit bytes |
| Default encoding | Configurable (charset) | Platform default (often broken) |
| `System.out` type | **No** | **Yes** |
| Recommended for files/networks | **Yes** | Avoid |
| Auto-flush default | Off | Off |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why prefer PrintWriter?** Because it does not suffer from the notorious platform-encoding bug of the old `PrintStream`, and it is the only character-stream class with built-in `print`/`println` convenience methods.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Server-side logging:** Web frameworks wrap the HTTP response `Writer` in a `PrintWriter` so that servlets can use `out.println("<html>")`.
- **Configuration file generation:** Tools that emit `.properties`, `.csv`, or `.xml` files use `PrintWriter` for portable text output.
- **Report generation:** Billing systems print formatted invoices with `printf("%-20s %10.2f", item, price)`.
- **Migration utilities:** ETL tools use `PrintWriter` to write character-clean extract files regardless of the OS locale.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

Since PrintWriter is a programming construct, the "derivations" below walk through the **logical flow of bytes/chars, state transitions, and buffering behavior** with full analytical rigour.

## 3.1 Exhaustive Walkthrough — What Happens When You Call `pw.println(42)`?

Assume the following setup:

```java
PrintWriter pw = new PrintWriter(new FileWriter("out.txt"), true);
pw.println(42);
```

### Step 1 — Method Resolution

The compiler resolves `println(int)` to the `PrintWriter.println(int x)` method (overloaded for every primitive type and `String`).

### Step 2 — String Conversion

The `int` value `42` is converted to its textual representation using `String.valueOf(42)`, producing the character sequence `"42"`.

$$ \text{String.valueOf}(42) \rightarrow \text{"42"} $$

### Step 3 — Delegation to Underlying Writer

`PrintWriter` then calls the inherited `Writer.write(String str)` method, which in turn calls `write(str, 0, str.length())`. Internally, this delegates to the wrapped `FileWriter`.

### Step 4 — Line Separator Appended

After the data, the **platform-specific line separator** is written. This is obtained from `System.getProperty("line.separator")`.

$$ \text{line.separator} = \begin{cases} \text{"\textbackslash n"} & \text{on Linux/macOS} \\ \text{"\textbackslash r\textbackslash n"} & \text{on Windows} \end{cases} $$

So the bytes written to the file are: `"42" + line.separator` → `42\n` (on Unix) or `42\r\n` (on Windows).

### Step 5 — Auto-Flush Trigger

Because we passed `true` in the constructor, `println` finishes by invoking `flush()` on the underlying `FileWriter`, which pushes characters through the `FileOutputStream` to disk.

### Step 6 — State Update

The internal `trouble` flag (a `protected boolean` in `PrintWriter`) is checked. If no `IOException` was thrown, the flag remains `false`. Otherwise, it is set to `true` via `setError()`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** Unlike most Java I/O classes, `PrintWriter` does **not throw `IOException`** from its `print`/`println` methods. Errors are silently captured and later retrievable through `checkError()`. This is a deliberate design choice to keep text output code uncluttered, but it is a favourite exam question.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Auto-Flush Behaviour

| Constructor Used | After `print("A")` Buffer Holds | After `println("B")` Buffer Holds | Disk State |
|---|---|---|---|
| `new PrintWriter(fw)` | `"A"` | `"A\nB"` | Empty (not yet flushed) |
| `new PrintWriter(bw)` | `"A"` (in `bw`) | `"A\nB"` (in `bw`) | Empty |
| `new PrintWriter(fw, true)` | Empty (flushed) | Empty (flushed) | `"A"` then `"A\nB"` |
| `new PrintWriter(bw, true)` | Empty (flushed from `bw`) | Empty (flushed from `bw`) | `"A"` then `"A\nB"` |

## 3.3 Exhaustive Code Trace — Decorator Stacking

```java
PrintWriter pw = new PrintWriter(
                     new BufferedWriter(
                         new OutputStreamWriter(
                             new FileOutputStream("data.txt"), "UTF-8")), true);
```

The **stack of wrappers** from the outside in is:

1. **`PrintWriter`** — adds `print`, `println`, `printf`, auto-flush.
2. **`BufferedWriter`** — adds an in-memory 8192-char buffer to reduce disk I/O.
3. **`OutputStreamWriter`** — converts chars to bytes using the `UTF-8` charset.
4. **`FileOutputStream`** — actually writes bytes to the file on disk.

**Data flow per call to `pw.println("Hi")`:**

$$ \text{"Hi"} \xrightarrow{\text{PrintWriter}} \text{Buffer} \xrightarrow{\text{BufferedWriter}} \text{Convert to UTF-8 bytes} \xrightarrow{\text{OutputStreamWriter}} \text{Disk file} $$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — printf Format Specifiers

| Specifier | Type | Example Input | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| `%d` | Integer | `printf("%d", 42)` | `42` |
| `%f` | Floating point | `printf("%.2f", 3.14159)` | `3.14` |
| `%s` | String | `printf("%s", "Hi")` | `Hi` |
| `%n` | Platform newline | `printf("A%nB")` | `A\nB` (Unix) |
| `%10s` | Width-padded string | `printf("%10s", "Hi")` | `        Hi` |
| `%-10s` | Left-aligned | `printf("%-10s", "Hi")` | `Hi        ` |
| `%05d` | Zero-padded | `printf("%05d", 42)` | `00042` |

> [!WARNING]
> **Use `%n`, not `\n`, in `printf`!** Inside a format string, `%n` is the **platform-independent** newline. Using `\n` works on most systems but is technically not guaranteed by the spec, and KTU examiners love to deduct a mark here.

## 3.5 Logical Derivation — Why PrintWriter Does Not Throw IOException

| Question | Answer |
|---|---|
| What does `print(int)` declare in its `throws` clause? | **Nothing** — it does not declare any checked exception. |
| How are errors then reported? | Internally, the `setError()` flag is set when an exception occurs. |
| How can a user check for an error? | By calling `boolean checkError()` after writing. |
| Why was this design chosen? | To keep formatting code (`pw.println(...)`) free of `try/catch` clutter. |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Class Hierarchy Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    objA["java.lang.Object"]
    writB["java.io.Writer"]
    buffW["java.io.BufferedWriter"]
    fileW["java.io.FileWriter"]
    oswC["java.io.OutputStreamWriter"]
    strW["java.io.StringWriter"]
    printD["java.io.PrintWriter"]

    objA --> writB
    writB --> buffW
    writB --> fileW
    writB --> oswC
    writB --> strW
    writB --> printD
    buffW -.wraps.-> writB
    printD -.wraps.-> writB
```

## 4.2 Decorator Stack for a Typical File-Writing PrintWriter

```mermaid
graph TD
    progA["User Code pw.println data"]
    pwB["PrintWriter Format Layer"]
    bwC["BufferedWriter 8192 char buffer"]
    oswD["OutputStreamWriter Charset Conversion UTF-8"]
    fosE["FileOutputStream Raw byte writer"]
    diskF["Physical Disk out.txt"]

    progA --> pwB
    pwB --> bwC
    bwC --> oswD
    oswD --> fosE
    fosE --> diskF
```

## 4.3 State Machine for Auto-Flush Mode

```mermaid
graph LR
    idleA["IDLE Writer open"]
    wroteB["WROTE print or println called"]
    bufC["BUFFERED chars in memory"]
    flushD["FLUSHED bytes on disk"]
    closedE["CLOSED writer released"]

    idleA --> wroteB
    wroteB --> bufC
    bufC --> flushD
    flushD --> idleA
    idleA --> closedE
    bufC --> closedE
```

**Legend of states:**

- **IDLE** — The writer is open and ready.
- **WROTE** — A `print`/`println` invocation just executed.
- **BUFFERED** — Characters are waiting in the buffer (in-memory).
- **FLUSHED** — Characters were pushed to the underlying byte stream.
- **CLOSED** — The writer is permanently shut; further writes raise an error.

## 4.4 Method-Resolution Flow for `pw.println(42)`

```mermaid
graph TD
    callA["pw.println int value 42"]
    resB["Method resolution: PrintWriter.println int x"]
    convC["Convert int to String via String.valueOf"]
    writD["Call write str on underlying Writer"]
    sepE["Append line separator from System property"]
    flushF["If autoFlush true call flush"]
    errG["Catch IOException set trouble flag true"]

    callA --> resB
    resB --> convC
    convD["write str to underlying writer"] --> writD
    convC --> writD
    writD --> sepE
    sepE --> flushF
    flushF --> errG
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 2]**
*What is the PrintWriter class in Java? Why is it preferred over PrintStream for writing text to files?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** `PrintWriter` is a character-based output stream class in `java.io` that extends `Writer` and provides convenient methods like `print()`, `println()`, and `printf()` to write formatted text representations of primitive types and objects.
- **Reason 1 (1 mark):** It is a **character stream** (16-bit Unicode), so it handles international text correctly, whereas `PrintStream` works on raw bytes and depends on the platform's default encoding.
- **Reason 2 (1 mark):** It is **stateless with respect to exceptions** (errors are captured in an internal flag), making text output code cleaner, and it is the only character-stream writer with built-in formatting methods, avoiding the need for manual `String.valueOf()` conversions.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Explain the concept of auto-flush in PrintWriter with a suitable constructor example.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Auto-flush is an optional boolean flag passed to the `PrintWriter` constructor. When set to `true`, it causes the underlying writer to be flushed automatically after every `print()`, `println()`, or `printf()` call.
- **Constructor (1 mark):** `PrintWriter(Writer wr, boolean autoFlush)` is the standard signature; for example, `new PrintWriter(new FileWriter("log.txt"), true)` enables auto-flush.
- **Use case (1 mark):** Auto-flush is essential in **logging** and **network communication** scenarios where every line must be persisted immediately and cannot be lost in the event of a crash or session timeout.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> Both sub-questions carry **7 marks each**. The internal choice follows the **KTU 2024 ESE pattern**: answer EITHER the whole of Choice A OR the whole of Choice B.

### Choice A — Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the class hierarchy of `PrintWriter` in Java. List any **six** constructors of the class with their purpose. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The class hierarchy (2 marks):

```text
java.lang.Object
     └── java.io.Writer
             └── java.io.PrintWriter
```

`PrintWriter` directly extends `java.io.Writer`, making it a peer of `BufferedWriter`, `FileWriter`, `OutputStreamWriter`, and `StringWriter`. It implements the `Appendable`, `Closeable`, and `Flushable` interfaces.

Six constructors (½ mark each = 3 marks):

1. `PrintWriter(OutputStream out)` — wraps a byte stream as a character stream, no auto-flush.
2. `PrintWriter(OutputStream out, boolean autoFlush)` — same, with optional auto-flush.
3. `PrintWriter(Writer wr)` — wraps an existing character writer, no auto-flush.
4. `PrintWriter(Writer wr, boolean autoFlush)` — same, with optional auto-flush.
5. `PrintWriter(File file)` — opens a `File` directly, default charset, no auto-flush.
6. `PrintWriter(String fileName)` — opens a file by path string, no auto-flush.

Constructors and uses (2 marks): `PrintWriter` is used in file writing, network socket writing, servlet HTTP responses, and report generation. It is preferred because it decouples formatting concerns (print/println) from the underlying transport (file/socket/buffer).

**(b)** Write a Java program to read a list of integers from the keyboard and write them to a file `nums.txt` using `PrintWriter`. The program should also display how many numbers were written. Use `BufferedReader` for input. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

```java
import java.io.*;

public class IntFileWriter {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws IOException {
        BufferedReader br = new BufferedReader(new InputStreamReader(System.in));
        PrintWriter pw = new PrintWriter(new FileWriter("nums.txt"), true);

        System.out.println("Enter integers (type 'stop' to end):");
        String line;
        int count = 0;

        while (!(line = br.readLine()).equalsIgnoreCase("stop")) {
            try {
                int n = Integer.parseInt(line);
                pw.println(n);   // writes the integer as text
                count++;
            } catch (NumberFormatException e) {
                System.out.println("Not a valid integer, skipped.");
            }
        }

        System.out.println("Total numbers written: " + count);
        pw.close();
        br.close();
    }
}
```

**Step-by-step explanation (7 marks split):**

- **Imports and main setup (1 mark):** `BufferedReader` is used to read keyboard input line-by-line, and `PrintWriter` wraps a `FileWriter` with auto-flush enabled (`true`) so every number is written immediately.
- **Reading loop (2 marks):** A `while` loop reads each line until the user types `"stop"`. Each line is parsed using `Integer.parseInt()`; invalid entries are caught and reported without terminating the program.
- **Writing and counting (2 marks):** For each valid integer, `pw.println(n)` converts the integer to its string form and appends the line separator. A `count` variable tracks the total.
- **Cleanup and output (2 marks):** After the loop, the total is printed to the console, and both `pw` and `br` are closed to release the file handle and keyboard stream.

### Choice B — Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Differentiate between `PrintWriter` and `PrintStream` in Java. Why is `PrintWriter` recommended for character output? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | `PrintWriter` | `PrintStream` |
|---|---|---|
| Parent class | `Writer` (character) | `OutputStream` (byte) |
| Data unit | 16-bit Unicode chars | 8-bit bytes |
| Default encoding | Configurable via `csn` | Platform default (often wrong) |
| `System.out` type | No | Yes |
| Auto-flush default | Off | Off |
| `print/println/printf` | Yes | Yes |
| Use case | Text files, network, logs | `System.out` console only |

**Why `PrintWriter` is recommended for character output (4 marks):**

1. **Encoding control (1 mark):** `PrintWriter` constructors accept a `String csn` (charset name) parameter, allowing explicit specification of UTF-8, ISO-8859-1, etc.
2. **Portability (1 mark):** It does not depend on the host operating system's default encoding, ensuring the same file looks identical on Windows, Linux, and macOS.
3. **No platform-encoding bug (1 mark):** Unlike `PrintStream`, it does not silently corrupt non-ASCII characters on systems whose default encoding is not UTF-8.
4. **Cleaner semantics (1 mark):** Because it is a true character stream, `PrintWriter` integrates naturally with other character streams like `BufferedReader`, `FileReader`, and `Scanner`.

**(b)** Explain `printf` and `format` methods of `PrintWriter` with an example that prints an employee payslip. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The `printf` and `format` methods of `PrintWriter` produce formatted output using a **format string** and an **argument list** (1 mark). They are **identical** in behaviour — `printf` is a convenience alias for `format` (1 mark). Both return the `PrintWriter` itself, allowing **method chaining** (1 mark).

```java
import java.io.*;

public class Payslip {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws IOException {
        PrintWriter pw = new PrintWriter(new FileWriter("payslip.txt"), true);

        String name = "Anjali";
        int empId = 1042;
        double basic = 45000.00, hra = 18000.00, tax = 5400.00;
        double net = basic + hra - tax;

        pw.printf("EMPLOYEE PAYSLIP%n");
        pw.printf("Name      : %-20s%n", name);
        pw.printf("Emp ID    : %010d%n", empId);
        pw.printf("Basic     : %10.2f%n", basic);
        pw.printf("HRA       : %10.2f%n", hra);
        pw.printf("Tax       : %10.2f%n", tax);
        pw.printf("Net Salary: %10.2f%n", net);

        pw.close();
    }
}
```

**Step-by-step explanation (4 marks):**

- **Format specifier breakdown (2 marks):** `%-20s` left-aligns the name in a 20-char field; `%010d` zero-pads the employee ID to 10 digits; `%10.2f` prints a floating-point number right-aligned in 10 chars with 2 decimal places.
- **Line separator choice (1 mark):** The format string uses `%n` for the newline, which is **platform-independent** — preferred over `\n` in formatted output.
- **Auto-flush benefit (1 mark):** With `true` passed to the constructor, each `printf` call is immediately flushed to the file, ensuring the payslip is persisted even if the program crashes mid-execution.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting to close the PrintWriter (–1 mark):** If the student never calls `pw.close()` and uses no auto-flush, the file may end up empty. Always close the writer.
> 2. **Using `\n` instead of `%n` in `printf` (–1 mark):** The format specifier `%n` is the only **guaranteed portable** newline. Examiners specifically test this.
> 3. **Confusing `print` with `println` (–1 mark):** Forgetting that `print` does not terminate the line is a frequent mistake; the next write will continue on the same line.
> 4. **Expecting `print` to throw `IOException` (–1 mark):** It does not. Errors are stored internally and reported only by `checkError()`.
> 5. **Passing the wrong constructor parameter (–1 mark):** Many students pass `autoFlush` as the **first** argument or omit it entirely when it is required.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **PrintWriter** is a character-stream writer in `java.io` that extends `Writer` and provides `print`, `println`, and `printf` methods.
- It is the **character-stream counterpart** of `PrintStream`, and is the **preferred** class for writing formatted text to files, sockets, and other character destinations.
- It supports **constructor-level auto-flush** via the `boolean autoFlush` parameter — when `true`, the underlying writer is flushed after every `print`/`println`/`printf` call.
- It has **seven standard constructors** covering `OutputStream`, `Writer`, `File`, and `String fileName` parameters, with optional charset and auto-flush flags.
- Unlike most I/O classes, its `print`/`println` methods **do not throw `IOException`**; errors are reported through the internal `trouble` flag and the `checkError()` method.
- `printf` and `format` are **aliases**; both support format specifiers like `%d`, `%f`, `%s`, `%n`, width flags (`%10s`), and alignment flags (`%-10s`).
- Always use `%n` (not `\n`) inside a `printf` format string for **platform-independent** line termination.
- It can be **decorator-stacked** with `BufferedWriter` (for I/O efficiency) and `OutputStreamWriter` (for charset control).
- The default state for **auto-flush is OFF** — students must pass `true` explicitly when required.
- Closing a `PrintWriter` automatically flushes its buffer, so `close()` is mandatory at the end of every writing block.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Working with Files
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT414 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Working with Files

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Working with Files** in Object Oriented Programming (Java) refers to the mechanism of **persistent data storage and retrieval** using the **java.io** and **java.nio** packages. A *file* is a named collection of information stored on a secondary storage device, and *streams* are the logical channels that act as conduits for transporting data between a program and an external file/device.

In Java (the prescribed language for KTU PEECT414), file handling is built on the concept of **Streams** — an abstraction that represents a flow of data from a *source* (input) to a *destination* (output). The `java.io` package provides two principal hierarchies:

- **Byte Streams** → `InputStream` / `OutputStream` (handle raw binary data, **8-bit** bytes)
- **Character Streams** → `Reader` / `Writer` (handle **16-bit Unicode** characters)

The `java.io.File` class (legacy) and `java.nio.file.Path` / `Files` class (modern NIO.2) are used to represent file and directory pathnames abstractly.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Pipeline" Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition Box — Think of Streams as Water Pipes**

Imagine a **water tap connected to an overhead tank**:

| Real World Object | Java Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Overhead Tank (Source) | File on disk / Keyboard |
| Tap (Faucet) | Stream object (`FileInputStream`, `FileReader`, etc.) |
| Water flowing | Data (bytes/chars) moving |
| Bucket (Destination) | Program's variable / Console / Another file |
| Closing the tap after use | Calling `.close()` to release OS resources |

Just as you must **open** a tap, **let data flow**, and **close** the tap to save water and prevent leaks, every Java file operation must:
1. **Open** the stream (constructor)
2. **Read/Write** the data (`.read()` / `.write()`)
3. **Close** the stream (`.close()`) — usually in a `finally` block or try-with-resources

A **buffer** is like a **large bucket placed under the tap** — instead of carrying one cup at a time, you fill the bucket and carry many cups in one trip. This dramatically reduces I/O trips to the disk.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):** Students must study `File`, `FileInputStream`, `FileOutputStream`, `FileReader`, `FileWriter`, `BufferedReader`, `BufferedWriter`, `PrintWriter`, `RandomAccessFile`, and **Object Serialization** (`ObjectInputStream` / `ObjectOutputStream`, `Serializable` interface, `transient` keyword).

## 1.3 Physical & Standard Constants

- **Byte Size:** **1 byte = 8 bits** (range: `-128` to `+127`)
- **Unicode Character Size:** **2 bytes = 16 bits** (UTF-16 internal encoding)
- **EOF Marker:** `-1** (returned by `read()` when end of stream is reached)
- **Default Buffer Size:** **8192 bytes (8 KB)** for `BufferedInputStream`/`BufferedOutputStream` and **8192 chars** for `BufferedReader`/`BufferedWriter`
- **File Separator:** `/` (forward slash) on Unix/Linux/macOS, `\\` (double backslash in Java string) on Windows
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Stream Classification (Hierarchy)

Java divides all I/O into two broad families based on **data unit size**:

```
                  java.io
                    │
        ┌───────────┴───────────┐
        │                       │
   Byte Streams           Character Streams
   (8-bit)                (16-bit Unicode)
        │                       │
   ┌────┴────┐             ┌────┴────┐
   │         │             │         │
InputStream OutputStream  Reader    Writer
   │         │             │         │
FileInputStream FileOutputStream  FileReader  FileWriter
   │         │             │         │
BufferedInputStream BufferedOutputStream  BufferedReader  BufferedWriter
   │         │             │
DataInputStream DataOutputStream  PrintWriter
   │
ObjectInputStream  ObjectOutputStream
```

## 2.2 Why Two Different Stream Families?

| Aspect | Byte Streams | Character Streams |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Unit** | 1 byte (8 bits) | 1 char (16 bits) |
| **Best for** | Binary files (images, audio, `.class`, `.exe`, `.pdf`) | Text files (`.txt`, `.csv`, `.json`, `.xml`) |
| **Encoding** | None — raw bytes | Automatic Unicode ↔ Local charset conversion |
| **Abstract Base** | `InputStream` / `OutputStream` | `Reader` / `Writer` |
| **KTU Preference** | Used for serialization & binary I/O | Preferred for text-based file reading |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Golden Rule:** *Always use Character Streams (`Reader`/`Writer`) for text and Byte Streams (`InputStream`/`OutputStream`) for binary.* Mixing them up is a guaranteed mark loser in the exam.

## 2.3 Key Constructor Reference Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> This table is a **high-yield KTU cheat sheet**. Memorize the constructor signatures and what happens when you omit the second argument.

| Class | Constructor | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| `FileInputStream` | `FileInputStream(String name)` | Opens for **reading** — file **must exist** |
| `FileOutputStream` | `FileOutputStream(String name)` | Opens for **writing** — **creates** if absent, **overwrites** if present |
| `FileOutputStream` | `FileOutputStream(String name, boolean append)` | If `append=true`, **appends** to existing file |
| `FileReader` | `FileReader(String name)` | Character stream for reading |
| `FileWriter` | `FileWriter(String name)` | Character stream for writing (overwrite mode) |
| `FileWriter` | `FileWriter(String name, boolean append)` | Append mode writer |
| `BufferedReader` | `BufferedReader(Reader r)` | Wraps any Reader with buffering |
| `BufferedWriter` | `BufferedWriter(Writer w)` | Wraps any Writer with buffering |
| `PrintWriter` | `PrintWriter(String name)` | Wraps a `FileWriter` internally, supports `println()` |
| `ObjectOutputStream` | `ObjectOutputStream(OutputStream o)` | For serialization |
| `ObjectInputStream` | `ObjectInputStream(InputStream i)` | For deserialization |
| `RandomAccessFile` | `RandomAccessFile(String name, String mode)` | `"r"`, `"rw"`, `"rws"`, `"rwd"` modes |

## 2.4 The `File` Class — Path Abstraction

The `File` class (in `java.io`) is **not** a stream. It is a *pathname* abstraction used to query or manipulate file/directory metadata.

**Commonly tested methods:**

| Method | Returns / Action |
|---|---|
| `boolean exists()` | True if file/dir exists on disk |
| `boolean isFile()` | True if path is a file (not directory) |
| `boolean isDirectory()` | True if path is a directory |
| `long length()` | File size in **bytes** |
| `String[] list()` | Names of files/dirs inside a directory |
| `boolean canRead()` / `canWrite()` | Permission check |
| `boolean mkdir()` | Creates **one** directory |
| `boolean mkdirs()` | Creates **directory path** (multiple nested dirs) |
| `boolean createNewFile()` | Creates a new empty file |
| `boolean delete()` | Deletes the file or empty directory |
| `String getName()` | Returns last name in path (e.g., `test.txt`) |
| `String getAbsolutePath()` | Returns the absolute path string |
| `boolean renameTo(File dest)` | Renames/moves a file |

## 2.5 Object Serialization Theory

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Serialization** is the process of converting a **Java object's state** into a **byte stream** so it can be saved to a file, sent over a network, or stored in a database. **Deserialization** is the reverse.

**Three pillars of Serialization:**

1. **Implement `java.io.Serializable`** — a *marker interface* (no methods to implement). Tells the JVM that this class *can* be serialized.
2. **Use `ObjectOutputStream.writeObject(obj)`** — performs the conversion.
3. **Use `ObjectInputStream.readObject()`** — performs the reverse, returns `Object` (must be cast back).

**The `transient` keyword** — fields marked `transient` are **excluded** from serialization. Used for sensitive data (passwords) or non-serializable fields.

**The `serialVersionUID`** — a `static final long` used for **version control** of serialized classes. If the UID changes between save and load time, `InvalidClassException` is thrown.

### 2.5.1 Why Serialization Matters in Engineering

- **Distributed systems** (RMI, EJB) use serialization to pass objects between JVMs.
- **Caching frameworks** (Redis, Memcached) serialize objects before storing.
- **Android** uses `Parcelable` (faster alternative) for inter-process communication.
- **Big Data** (Hadoop, Spark) serializes objects to send them across cluster nodes.

## 2.6 Random Access File Theory

Unlike sequential streams (which read/write from beginning to end), `RandomAccessFile` allows **jumping to any byte position** in the file using `seek(long position)` and `getFilePointer()`. It supports both **reading and writing** simultaneously through the mode string.

$$\text{Position} = \text{Offset from byte 0} \quad (0 \leq \text{position} \leq \text{file.length()})$$

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Complete File-Reading Operational Sequence

We derive the canonical 5-step file reading process used in KTU model answers.

**Step 1: Create the `File` reference (optional but recommended for safety checks).**
```java
File f = new File("data.txt");
if (!f.exists()) { System.out.println("File missing"); return; }
```
*Logic:* The `File` object is *purely a path name*; it does **not** open the file. We use it to verify existence **before** attempting to open streams, preventing `FileNotFoundException`.

**Step 2: Open the byte-level stream.**
```java
FileInputStream fis = new FileInputStream("data.txt");
```
*Logic:* This constructor translates the string path into a system file handle. The OS allocates a kernel-level **file descriptor** (an integer index into the open-file table). The `FileInputStream` object now holds this descriptor.

**Step 3: Wrap with a buffer (recommended for performance).**
```java
BufferedInputStream bis = new BufferedInputStream(fis);
```
*Logic:* The buffer (default **8192 bytes**) reduces physical disk reads. If the program requests 1 byte, the buffer fetches up to 8192 bytes from disk in one syscall, then serves subsequent bytes from RAM.

**Step 4: Read the data — loop until EOF.**
```java
int ch;
while ((ch = bis.read()) != -1) {
    System.out.print((char) ch);
}
```
*Logic:* The `.read()` method returns:
- An **unsigned byte** converted to `int` (range $0$ to $255$)
- The sentinel value $\mathbf{-1}$ when stream is exhausted (EOF)

This is the **canonical KTU pattern** — using `int` (not `byte`) for the read variable to distinguish valid data from EOF.

**Step 5: Close the streams (reverse order of construction).**
```java
bis.close();   // closes fis too (cascading close)
fis.close();
```
*Logic:* Closing the outermost buffered stream automatically propagates the close to underlying streams. **Always close** to release OS resources and flush buffers to disk.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Best Practice — Try-With-Resources (Java 7+):**
> ```java
> try (BufferedReader br = new BufferedReader(new FileReader("data.txt"))) {
>     String line;
>     while ((line = br.readLine()) != null) {
>         System.out.println(line);
>     }
> } // br.close() called automatically here
> ```
> This is the **modern, preferred** KTU pattern — no `finally` block needed.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Class

| Scenario | Recommended Class | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Read a text file **line by line** | `BufferedReader` + `FileReader` | `readLine()` method, efficient buffering |
| Write text with `println()` convenience | `PrintWriter` | Built-in `print()`, `println()`, `printf()` |
| Read/write raw binary (images, audio) | `FileInputStream` / `FileOutputStream` | Byte-level fidelity, no charset translation |
| Append data to an existing log file | `FileWriter(name, true)` or `FileOutputStream(name, true)` | Constructor's `append` flag |
| Save a Java object's state | `ObjectOutputStream` | Serialization support |
| Read a serialized object back | `ObjectInputStream` | Deserialization support |
| Read AND modify mid-file (e.g., seek to byte 100) | `RandomAccessFile` | `seek()`, `getFilePointer()` |
| Read primitive types (int, double) directly | `DataInputStream` / `DataOutputStream` | `readInt()`, `readDouble()`, `writeUTF()` |

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation of a Serialization-Deserialization Round Trip

**Step 1: Define a serializable class.**
```java
import java.io.Serializable;
public class Student implements Serializable {
    private static final long serialVersionUID = 1L;   // Version control
    private String name;
    private int rollNo;
    private transient String password;                // Will NOT be serialized
    
    public Student(String n, int r, String p) {
        this.name = n; this.rollNo = r; this.password = p;
    }
    public String toString() {
        return name + " | " + rollNo + " | " + password;
    }
}
```
*Logic:* Implementing `Serializable` is a **declaration only** — no methods to override. The `transient` `password` field is excluded from the byte stream; upon deserialization, it will be `null`.

**Step 2: Serialize (write the object to a file).**
```java
Student s1 = new Student("Anu", 42, "secret123");
try (ObjectOutputStream oos =
        new ObjectOutputStream(new FileOutputStream("stu.ser"))) {
    oos.writeObject(s1);
}
```
*Logic:* `writeObject()` performs these internal steps:
1. Checks if the class implements `Serializable` (else `NotSerializableException`).
2. Writes the class's `serialVersionUID` to identify the class version.
3. Recursively serializes all non-`transient`, non-`static` fields.
4. Writes an EOF marker and closes.

**Step 3: Deserialize (read the object back).**
```java
Student s2;
try (ObjectInputStream ois =
        new ObjectInputStream(new FileInputStream("stu.ser"))) {
    s2 = (Student) ois.readObject();   // cast required!
}
System.out.println(s2);
```
*Logic:* `readObject()` returns `Object` and must be cast. Output will be:
```
Anu | 42 | null
```
The `password` is `null` because it was `transient` — **proving the transient keyword works**.

## 3.4 Reading a CSV File — Line-by-Line Derivation

Consider a file `marks.txt`:
```
Anu,85,90
Rahul,78,88
Priya,92,95
```

**Step 1: Wrap with `BufferedReader` for line-level efficiency.**

**Step 2: Read each line using `readLine()`** — returns `null` at EOF.

**Step 3: Split each line on the comma delimiter** using `String.split(",")` or `StringTokenizer`.

**Step 4: Convert tokens** as needed (`Integer.parseInt` for marks).

The complete derivation is captured in the model answer to Question B below.

## 4.2 File I/O Operational Flow (Try-With-Resources)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Program needs file] --> B{File exists?}
    B -- No --> Z1[Throw FileNotFoundException]
    B -- Yes --> C[Construct FileInputStream / FileReader]
    C --> D[Wrap in BufferedReader for efficiency]
    D --> E[Open try-with-resources block]
    E --> F[Loop: call readLine / read]
    F --> G{EOF reached?}
    G -- No --> H[Process the line/chunk]
    H --> F
    G -- Yes --> I[Auto close via try-with-resources]
    I --> J[End: File handle released]
    Z1 --> K[Catch block: handle exception]
    K --> J
```

## 4.3 Serialization Round-Trip Architecture

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant App as Java Application
    participant OOS as ObjectOutputStream
    participant File as .ser File on Disk
    participant OIS as ObjectInputStream

    Note over App: Student s1 = new Student(Anu, 42, secret)
    App->>OOS: new ObjectOutputStream(FileOutputStream)
    App->>OOS: writeObject(s1)
    OOS->>OOS: Check implements Serializable
    OOS->>OOS: Write serialVersionUID
    OOS->>OOS: Skip transient fields
    OOS->>File: Convert object to byte stream
    File-->>OOS: Bytes written to disk
    OOS-->>App: Success
    Note over App,File: -- Time passes / different JVM --
    App->>OIS: new ObjectInputStream(FileInputStream)
    App->>OIS: readObject()
    OIS->>File: Read byte stream
    File-->>OIS: Bytes returned
    OIS->>OIS: Verify serialVersionUID match
    OIS->>OIS: Reconstruct object (transient = null)
    OIS-->>App: Student s2 = (Student) obj
    Note over App: password field is null
```

## 4.4 Buffered vs Unbuffered Stream Performance Block

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Application] -->|1 byte request| B[Buffered Stream 8KB RAM Buffer]
    B -->|1 syscall fetches 8192 bytes| C[Disk]
    C -->|8192 bytes returned| B
    B -->|1 byte delivered| A

    D[Application] -->|1 byte request| E[Unbuffered FileInputStream]
    E -->|1 syscall per byte| F[Disk]
    F -->|1 byte returned| E
    E -->|1 byte delivered| D

    style B fill:#2e7d32,color:#fff
    style E fill:#c62828,color:#fff
    style C fill:#1f3a5f,color:#fff
    style F fill:#1f3a5f,color:#fff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** For reading 1 MB of data, the unbuffered stream makes **1,048,576 syscalls**; the buffered stream makes only **128 syscalls** — an **~8000×** reduction in kernel-level overhead.

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]**

### Q1. What is a stream in Java? Differentiate between byte streams and character streams.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **stream** is a logical channel that represents a flow of data between a source and a destination. In Java's `java.io` package, streams are classified into two families:

| Byte Stream | Character Stream |
|---|---|
| Operates on **8-bit bytes** | Operates on **16-bit Unicode characters** |
| Base classes: `InputStream`, `OutputStream` | Base classes: `Reader`, `Writer` |
| Used for binary data (images, audio) | Used for text data (`.txt`, `.csv`) |
| Examples: `FileInputStream`, `FileOutputStream` | Examples: `FileReader`, `FileWriter` |

*(1 mark for stream definition + 2 marks for the differentiation table)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Set B]**

### Q2. What is serialization? Explain the role of the `Serializable` interface and the `transient` keyword.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Serialization** is the process of converting a Java object's state into a byte stream, so it can be saved to a file or transmitted over a network.

- **`Serializable` interface** — It is a *marker interface* (in `java.io`) with no methods. A class must `implements Serializable` to allow its instances to be serialized. If a class does not implement it, `NotSerializableException` is thrown by `ObjectOutputStream.writeObject()`. *(1.5 marks)*

- **`transient` keyword** — Fields declared as `transient` are **excluded** from the serialization process. When the object is deserialized, transient fields are restored to their **default values** (`null` for objects, `0` for numerics, `false` for booleans). It is used to skip sensitive data like passwords. *(1.5 marks)*

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3, Set A]**

#### (a) Explain the different `FileInputStream` and `FileOutputStream` constructors with examples. Write a Java program to copy the contents of one file to another using byte streams. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Constructors of `FileInputStream`:**
1. `FileInputStream(String name)` — opens file by name
2. `FileInputStream(File file)` — opens file using a `File` object
3. `FileInputStream(FileDescriptor fdObj)` — uses existing file descriptor

**Constructors of `FileOutputStream`:**
1. `FileOutputStream(String name)` — opens for **writing** (creates new / **overwrites** existing)
2. `FileOutputStream(String name, boolean append)` — opens in **append** mode if `append=true`
3. `FileOutputStream(File file)` — uses `File` object

**Java Program — File Copy using Byte Streams:**
```java
import java.io.*;

public class FileCopyDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws IOException {
        FileInputStream  fis = null;
        FileOutputStream fos = null;
        try {
            fis = new FileInputStream("source.txt");
            fos = new FileOutputStream("destination.txt", false);
            int ch;
            while ((ch = fis.read()) != -1) {
                fos.write(ch);
            }
            System.out.println("File copied successfully.");
        } catch (FileNotFoundException e) {
            System.out.println("Source file not found: " + e.getMessage());
        } finally {
            if (fis != null) fis.close();
            if (fos != null) fos.close();
        }
    }
}
```
**Explanation of key points (to write in answer):**
- `fis.read()` returns the next byte as `int` (0–255) or `-1` at EOF.
- The `int` is passed to `fos.write()` which automatically truncates to the lower 8 bits.
- `try-catch-finally` ensures streams are closed even if an exception occurs.
- The `throws IOException` declaration handles checked exceptions at the compiler level.

*(2 marks constructors + 3 marks program + 2 marks explanation)*

#### (b) With a neat diagram, explain the `java.io` class hierarchy of byte streams. List any **four** methods of `DataInputStream`. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Hierarchy Diagram:**
```
java.io.InputStream  (abstract)
        │
        ├── FileInputStream
        ├── ByteArrayInputStream
        ├── FilterInputStream
        │       ├── BufferedInputStream
        │       ├── DataInputStream
        │       └── PushbackInputStream
        └── ObjectInputStream

java.io.OutputStream (abstract)
        │
        ├── FileOutputStream
        ├── ByteArrayOutputStream
        ├── FilterOutputStream
        │       ├── BufferedOutputStream
        │       ├── DataOutputStream
        │       └── PrintStream
        └── ObjectOutputStream
```

**Four methods of `DataInputStream`:**
1. `int readInt()` — reads 4 bytes and returns an `int`
2. `double readDouble()` — reads 8 bytes and returns a `double`
3. `String readUTF()` — reads a UTF-8 encoded string
4. `boolean readBoolean()` — reads one byte and returns `true`/`false`
5. (Bonus) `void readFully(byte[] b)` — reads bytes until buffer is full

**Why use `DataInputStream`?** — It allows reading **primitive Java data types** in a **machine-independent** way (uses big-endian byte order), which is essential for cross-platform data exchange.

*(3 marks diagram + 2 marks methods listing + 2 marks use-case explanation)*

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3, Set B]**

#### (a) What is meant by object serialization? Write a complete Java program to serialize and deserialize a `Employee` object (with fields `id`, `name`, `salary`) to/from a file `emp.ser`. Explain the significance of `serialVersionUID`. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Object Serialization** is the mechanism of converting a Java object's state into a byte stream so that it can be stored on disk or transmitted across a network. **Deserialization** is the reverse process — reconstructing the object from the byte stream.

**Complete Java Program:**
```java
import java.io.*;

class Employee implements Serializable {
    // Significance: ensures version compatibility between
    // serialized data and the current class definition.
    private static final long serialVersionUID = 1L;
    private int id;
    private String name;
    private double salary;
    
    public Employee(int id, String name, double salary) {
        this.id = id;
        this.name = name;
        this.salary = salary;
    }
    public String toString() {
        return "Employee [id=" + id + ", name=" + name + ", salary=" + salary + "]";
    }
}

public class SerializationDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // -------- SERIALIZATION --------
        try (ObjectOutputStream oos =
                 new ObjectOutputStream(new FileOutputStream("emp.ser"))) {
            Employee e1 = new Employee(101, "Anu", 55000.50);
            oos.writeObject(e1);
            System.out.println("Object serialized: " + e1);
        } catch (IOException e) {
            System.out.println("Serialization error: " + e.getMessage());
        }

        // -------- DESERIALIZATION --------
        try (ObjectInputStream ois =
                 new ObjectInputStream(new FileInputStream("emp.ser"))) {
            Employee e2 = (Employee) ois.readObject();   // explicit cast required
            System.out.println("Object deserialized: " + e2);
        } catch (IOException | ClassNotFoundException e) {
            System.out.println("Deserialization error: " + e.getMessage());
        }
    }
}
```

**Significance of `serialVersionUID`:**
- It is a **unique identifier** for a serializable class, computed from class structure (fields, methods, etc.).
- During deserialization, the JVM compares the `serialVersionUID` of the loaded class with the one stored in the byte stream.
- If they **match** → deserialization proceeds normally.
- If they **differ** → `InvalidClassException` is thrown, preventing corruption from incompatible class versions.
- Manually declaring it (as `private static final long serialVersionUID = 1L;`) is a **best practice** because the auto-generated UID changes if even a small modification is made to the class.

*(2 marks serialization definition + 3 marks program + 2 marks serialVersionUID explanation)*

#### (b) Write a Java program that uses `BufferedReader` to read a text file `marks.txt` containing student names and three subject marks (comma-separated), and computes the total and average for each student. Display the result on the console. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Assumed `marks.txt` content:**
```
Anu,85,90,78
Rahul,88,76,92
Priya,95,98,99
```

**Java Program:**
```java
import java.io.*;

public class MarksProcessor {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // try-with-resources automatically closes the reader
        try (BufferedReader br = new BufferedReader(new FileReader("marks.txt"))) {
            String line;
            int recordCount = 0;
            
            System.out.printf("%-10s %-8s %-8s %-8s %-8s %-8s%n",
                              "Name", "M1", "M2", "M3", "Total", "Average");
            System.out.println("-------------------------------------------------------------");
            
            while ((line = br.readLine()) != null) {
                String[] parts = line.split(",");
                String name   = parts[0].trim();
                int m1        = Integer.parseInt(parts[1].trim());
                int m2        = Integer.parseInt(parts[2].trim());
                int m3        = Integer.parseInt(parts[3].trim());
                int total     = m1 + m2 + m3;
                double avg    = total / 3.0;
                
                System.out.printf("%-10s %-8d %-8d %-8d %-8d %-8.2f%n",
                                  name, m1, m2, m3, total, avg);
                recordCount++;
            }
            System.out.println("\nTotal records processed: " + recordCount);
            
        } catch (FileNotFoundException e) {
            System.out.println("File not found: marks.txt");
        } catch (IOException e) {
            System.out.println("I/O error: " + e.getMessage());
        } catch (NumberFormatException e) {
            System.out.println("Invalid number format in file: " + e.getMessage());
        }
    }
}
```

**Expected Output:**
```
Name       M1       M2       M3       Total    Average
-------------------------------------------------------------
Anu        85       90       78       253      84.33
Rahul      88       76       92       256      85.33
Priya      95       98       99       292      97.33

Total records processed: 3
```

**Explanation of Key Points:**
- `BufferedReader.readLine()` returns `null` at EOF — the canonical loop condition.
- `String.split(",")` tokenizes each line on the comma delimiter.
- `Integer.parseInt()` converts string tokens to integers; `NumberFormatException` is caught.
- `try-with-resources` ensures the file handle is released even if exceptions occur.
- `printf` with `%-10s` left-aligns strings within a 10-character field for clean tabular output.

*(2 marks algorithm + 3 marks program + 2 marks output/justification)*

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in "Working with Files" Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to cast** `readObject()` return value — `ObjectInputStream.readObject()` returns `Object`, **NOT** the original type. Failing to cast to `Student`/`Employee` etc. loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Using `byte` instead of `int`** for the read variable — `byte` cannot hold `-1` (EOF sentinel), causing an **infinite loop** with all values 0xFF. Always use `int ch;`.
> 3. **Not closing streams** in `catch` blocks — `finally` block must close streams, OR use try-with-resources. Loss: 1 mark for resource leak awareness.
> 4. **Confusing `FileWriter(name)` with `FileWriter(name, true)`** — the former *overwrites*, the latter *appends*. Examiner tests this distinction.
> 5. **Declaring `throws Exception` blindly** — should declare specific checked exceptions: `IOException`, `FileNotFoundException`, `ClassNotFoundException`.
> 6. **Forgetting `implements Serializable`** — without it, `NotSerializableException` is thrown at runtime. Always mention this in design.
> 7. **Not handling `ClassNotFoundException`** in deserialization — it is checked, so the compiler will demand a catch/throws.
> 8. **Mixing `Reader` with `InputStream`** in code — `BufferedReader br = new BufferedReader(new FileInputStream("..."));` **will not compile** because they belong to different hierarchies. Must use `InputStreamReader` as a bridge.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- 📂 A **File** is a persistent storage abstraction; a **Stream** is the data-flow channel.
- 🌊 **Two stream families**: `InputStream`/`OutputStream` (byte, 8-bit) vs `Reader`/`Writer` (character, 16-bit Unicode).
- 📖 **Use Character Streams for text**; **Byte Streams for binary** — this is the single most-tested KTU rule.
- 🔢 **`read()` returns `int`** (0 to 255) or **`-1` at EOF** — never use `byte` for the read variable.
- 🏗️ **`File` class is *not* a stream** — it represents pathnames and file metadata only.
- 🪣 **Always buffer** with `BufferedReader`/`BufferedInputStream` for performance (default buffer = **8192 bytes/chars**).
- 🧬 **Serialization** requires `implements Serializable` (marker interface, no methods).
- 🔒 **`transient` fields are skipped** during serialization → default values on deserialization.
- 🆔 **`serialVersionUID`** ensures version compatibility; declare it manually to avoid auto-regeneration issues.
- ⏪ **`readObject()` returns `Object`** → must cast to the original type.
- 🔁 **`RandomAccessFile`** supports both read/write via `seek()` and `getFilePointer()`.
- ➕ **`FileWriter(name, true)` / `FileOutputStream(name, true)`** → append mode (commonly tested!).
- 🧹 **Try-With-Resources** (Java 7+) is the preferred, modern KTU pattern for auto-closing.
- 🪪 **Checked exceptions to remember**: `IOException`, `FileNotFoundException`, `EOFException`, `ClassNotFoundException`.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Swings Fundamentals - Swing Key Features
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT414 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Swings Fundamentals - Swing Key Features

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Java Swing? — Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Swing** is a GUI (Graphical User Interface) **widget toolkit** for Java, forming the lightweight component layer of the **Java Foundation Classes (JFC)**. It is built on top of the older AWT (Abstract Window Toolkit) and provides a rich, **pluggable**, **100% pure Java** set of UI components beginning with the prefix `J` (e.g., `JButton`, `JFrame`, `JLabel`).

Every Swing component is part of the package `javax.swing` and is rendered entirely using **Java 2D graphics primitives** (in the `java.awt.Graphics2D` API), rather than relying on the host operating system's native peer components.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Furniture" Metaphor

Imagine a workshop:

- **AWT** is like **fixed, heavy wooden furniture** that is built directly by the carpenter (the operating system). Every chair looks different on every house, and you cannot re-paint or re-style it easily — it is **heavyweight** and tied to the house's native wood.
- **Swing** is like **modular, lightweight, ready-made furniture** assembled in the workshop itself. You can choose any **finish (Look and Feel)** — modern, classic, futuristic — by simply plugging in a different "skin." The furniture is made entirely inside the workshop, and the OS just provides the floor and the paintbrush.

This modularity is the essence of Swing: components are *painted*, not *peered*.

## 1.3 Key Architectural Pillars (At a Glance)

> [!NOTE]
> **The Four Pillars of Swing Identity:**
> 1. **Pure Java implementation** — no native peer code.
> 2. **Lightweight components** — drawn on the canvas of a heavyweight ancestor.
> 3. **Pluggable Look And Feel (PLAF)** — same code, different appearance.
> 4. **MVC-based component design** — separates data, look, and control.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Terminology

| Term | Standard Value / Definition |
|---|---|
| Package name | `javax.swing` |
| Component prefix | `J` (e.g., `JButton`, `JPanel`) |
| Top-level container count | **4** (`JFrame`, `JApplet`, `JDialog`, `JWindow`) |
| Foundation class set | Java Foundation Classes (JFC) = AWT + Swing + Accessibility + Java 2D |
| Look-and-Feel classes | `Metal`, `Nimbus`, `Motif`, `Windows`, `GTK` |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Feature Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Swing Feature

### 2.1.1 Feature 1 — Lightweight Components

- **Why?** AWT components were **heavyweight**: each component (e.g., a `Button`) wrapped a native OS peer (a `java.awt.peer.ButtonPeer`). This caused platform-specific bugs, memory leaks, and the infamous "**gray-rect**" bug on Windows.
- **How?** Swing's lightweight components **inherit from `java.awt.Container` / `java.awt.Component`** but do **not** create native peers. They draw themselves using `paintComponent(Graphics g)` over the canvas of a single heavyweight ancestor (a `JFrame` or `JApplet`).

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb:** *Swing is drawn. AWT is delegated.* The top-level container is the only heavyweight entity in a Swing app.

### 2.1.2 Feature 2 — Pluggable Look And Feel (PLAF)

- **Why?** Enterprises wanted a single Java codebase to look like a *Windows* app on Windows, a *Mac* app on macOS, and a *Linux* app on Linux — without rewriting UI code.
- **How?** Every Swing component delegates its rendering to a `javax.swing.LookAndFeel` object. By calling `UIManager.setLookAndFeel(...)`, the entire UI is "re-skinned" at runtime.

### 2.1.3 Feature 3 — MVC (Model-View-Controller) Architecture

- **Why?** Mixing data (state) and visuals in one class (as AWT did) makes it impossible to attach the *same* UI to *multiple* data sources (e.g., one `JList` showing two different arrays).
- **How?** Each Swing component uses a **delegate UI object** that separates:
  - **Model** — data (e.g., `ButtonModel`, `ListModel`).
  - **View** — rendering (`ButtonUI`, `BasicButtonUI`).
  - **Controller** — input handling (mouse/keyboard listeners).

### 2.1.4 Feature 4 — Rich, Extended Component Set

Swing adds dozens of components AWT never had: `JTable`, `JTree`, `JTabbedPane`, `JSlider`, `JProgressBar`, `JOptionPane`, `JColorChooser`, `JFileChooser`, `JMenuBar`, `JToolBar`, `JTextPane`, etc.

### 2.1.5 Feature 5 — Double Buffering & Custom Painting

- **Why?** To eliminate flicker during repaint operations.
- **How?** Swing paints into an **off-screen image buffer**, then atomically copies the final image to the screen in a single paint cycle.

### 2.1.6 Feature 6 — Border, Icon, and Tooltip Support

- **Why?** Visual richness without subclassing.
- **How?** `JComponent.setBorder(Border)`, `setIcon(Icon)`, `setToolTipText(String)`. Borders include `LineBorder`, `TitledBorder`, `EtchedBorder`, `BevelBorder`, `EmptyBorder`.

### 2.1.7 Feature 7 — Keybinding & Undo/Redo Framework

Swing decouples input from components using `InputMap` and `ActionMap`, and provides `javax.swing.undo.UndoManager` for built-in undo/redo.

### 2.1.8 Feature 8 — Accessibility (Java Accessibility API)

Every Swing component implements `Accessible`, providing screen-reader and assistive-device support out of the box.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Feature Comparison Table

| Feature | AWT (Heavyweight) | Swing (Lightweight) |
|---|---|---|
| Package | `java.awt` | `javax.swing` |
| Class prefix | No prefix (`Button`) | Prefix `J` (`JButton`) |
| Native peer | Yes (one per component) | No (only top-level) |
| Look-and-Feel | Fixed by OS | Pluggable (PLAF) |
| Architecture | Single class | MVC with UI delegates |
| Components | ~14 basic | 40+ rich components |
| Double buffering | Not built-in | Built-in |
| Performance on slow CPUs | Fast (native) | Slower (pure Java) |
| Memory footprint | Lower per component | Higher (each holds UI delegate) |
| Flicker | Common | Rare (off-screen buffer) |
| Inheritance from | `Component` | `JComponent` → `Container` → `Component` |
| Event delegation model | Yes (and also 1.1+) | Yes (Java 1.1+ model) |

## 2.3 Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Swing is still the canonical framework used in:
> - **University-level Java curricula** (KTU, AICTE labs).
> - **NetBeans IDE** (built using Swing).
> - **IntelliJ IDEA** (early versions).
> - **Cross-platform enterprise tools** (e.g., JasperReports iReport, SQL Developer).
>
> Understanding Swing reinforces **MVC**, **Event-Driven Programming**, and **Composition over Inheritance** — three pillars of professional OOP.

## 2.4 The Swing Class Hierarchy (Conceptual Tree)

```
java.lang.Object
 └── java.awt.Component
      └── java.awt.Container
           └── javax.swing.JComponent      ← root of all Swing widgets
                ├── JLabel, JButton, JToggleButton
                ├── JTextField, JTextArea, JEditorPane
                ├── JScrollBar, JSlider
                ├── JPanel, JScrollPane, JTabbedPane
                ├── JTable, JTree, JList
                └── (40+ more)
```

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown & Code-Driven Walkthrough

## 3.1 Demonstrating Key Swing Features — A Single Annotated Program

Below is an exhaustive, line-by-line justified Java program that exercises **six** of the eight key features covered above. This is the typical KTU Part-B "Write a Swing program" question.

```java
import javax.swing.*;                  // Step 1: Import the Swing API
import javax.swing.border.*;           // Step 2: Import border classes (Feature 6)
import java.awt.*;                     // Step 3: Import AWT layout & event classes
import java.awt.event.*;               // Step 4: Import AWT event listeners

public class SwingDemo extends JFrame implements ActionListener {
    // Step 5: JFrame = top-level heavyweight container (only one per app)
    private JButton clickBtn, exitBtn;
    private JLabel  statusLbl;
    private JPanel  mainPanel;

    public SwingDemo() {
        // ===== Feature 2: PLAF =====
        try {
            // Step 6: Force the cross-platform "Nimbus" Look and Feel
            UIManager.setLookAndFeel("javax.swing.plaf.nimbus.NimbusLookAndFeel");
        } catch (Exception e) {
            System.out.println("PLAF not available: " + e);
        }

        // ===== Feature 5: Top-level container setup =====
        setTitle("KTU Swing Demo");
        setSize(400, 200);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);

        // ===== Feature 4: Rich component set =====
        mainPanel  = new JPanel(new FlowLayout(FlowLayout.CENTER, 10, 10));
        clickBtn   = new JButton("Click Me");
        exitBtn    = new JButton("Exit");
        statusLbl  = new JLabel("Status: waiting...");

        // ===== Feature 6: Border + Tooltip =====
        clickBtn.setBorder(BorderFactory.createBevelBorder(BevelBorder.RAISED));
        clickBtn.setToolTipText("Click to increment counter");
        statusLbl.setBorder(BorderFactory.createEtchedBorder(EtchedBorder.LOWERED));

        // ===== Feature 7: Event handling (Controller part of MVC) =====
        clickBtn.addActionListener(this);
        exitBtn.addActionListener(this);

        // ===== Feature 1 & 5: Lightweight composition =====
        mainPanel.add(clickBtn);
        mainPanel.add(exitBtn);
        mainPanel.add(statusLbl);
        add(mainPanel); // JFrame adds the lightweight panel to its native peer

        setVisible(true); // Triggers Swing's double-buffered paint cycle
    }

    // ===== Feature 7: Controller logic =====
    @Override
    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        if (e.getSource() == clickBtn) {
            statusLbl.setText("Status: Clicked at " + System.currentTimeMillis());
        } else if (e.getSource() == exitBtn) {
            System.exit(0);
        }
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Step 7: Always launch Swing apps on the Event Dispatch Thread (EDT)
        SwingUtilities.invokeLater(() -> new SwingDemo());
    }
}
```

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Analytical Justification

### Step 1 — Importing the Package

- **Action:** `import javax.swing.*;`
- **Reason:** All Swing classes live in the `javax.swing` package (the `javax` prefix historically denoted "Java extensions"). Without this import, the compiler cannot resolve `JFrame`, `JButton`, etc.
- **Why not AWT?** AWT uses `java.awt`; mixing imports is allowed but AWT components are heavyweight and should be avoided in pure-Swing GUIs.

### Step 2 — Setting the Pluggable Look and Feel (Feature 2)

- **Action:** `UIManager.setLookAndFeel(String className)`
- **Reason:** This installs a `LookAndFeel` object into a static `UIDefaults` table. Every subsequent Swing component consults this table when constructing its UI delegate.
- **Key Insight:** Changing the L&F **after** components are created requires calling `SwingUtilities.updateComponentTreeUI(window)` on every visible window.

### Step 3 — Top-Level Heavyweight Container (Feature 1)

- **Action:** `extends JFrame`
- **Reason:** `JFrame` is one of the **four** top-level Swing containers (`JFrame`, `JDialog`, `JApplet`, `JWindow`). Only these four create a native peer. Everything added *inside* a `JFrame` is lightweight.
- **Mathematical Analogy:** If the `JFrame` is the canvas, the `JButton` is the brush stroke — the stroke is "lightweight" because it has no peer of its own.

### Step 4 — Event Dispatch Thread (EDT) — Critical Rule

- **Action:** `SwingUtilities.invokeLater(() -> new SwingDemo());`
- **Reason:** Swing is **not thread-safe**. All UI construction and updates must occur on the **Event Dispatch Thread**, a single dedicated thread managed by the JVM. Violating this rule causes race conditions, deadlocks, and random repaint failures.
- **Examiner Note:** Marks are awarded for explicitly mentioning EDT in a KTU answer.

### Step 5 — MVC Mapping Inside the Program

| Component | Model | View | Controller |
|---|---|---|---|
| `JButton` | `ButtonModel` (holds pressed/armed/selected state) | `ButtonUI` (paints the button) | `BasicButtonListener` (handles mouse) |
| `JLabel` | `LabelUI` (text + icon) | `BasicLabelUI` | None (non-interactive) |
| `JFrame` | None (container) | `RootPaneUI` | `WindowListener` |

### Step 6 — Double Buffering Justification

- **Action:** Implicit in `setVisible(true)`.
- **Reason:** `JComponent` overrides `update(Graphics g)` to clear the off-screen buffer, then `paintComponent` draws into it, and finally the buffer is blitted to the screen — eliminating flicker.

### Step 7 — Border, Icon, Tooltip (Feature 6)

- **Action:** `setBorder(...)`, `setIcon(...)`, `setToolTipText(...)`
- **Reason:** `JComponent` exposes these as standard bean properties, supporting the **JavaBeans contract** and enabling GUI-builder tools (like NetBeans Matisse) to discover them via introspection.

## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix — When to Use Swing vs AWT vs JavaFX

| Criterion | AWT | Swing | JavaFX |
|---|---|---|---|
| Year introduced | 1995 (Java 1.0) | 1997 (Java 1.1/1.2) | 2008 (JavaFX 1.0) |
| Weight | Heavy | Light | Light |
| Skinnable | No | Yes (PLAF) | Yes (CSS) |
| Modern UI patterns | No | No | Yes (FXML, MVVM) |
| Current KTU syllabus focus | Foundational | **Primary** | Mentioned as successor |
| Status | Legacy | Maintained, legacy | Removed from JDK 11+ (now external) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Swing Architecture (PLAF + MVC Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Application main method] --> B[SwingUtilities.invokeLater]
    B --> C[Construct JFrame top level container]
    C --> D[UIManager setLookAndFeel]
    D --> E[UIDefaults Table Updated]
    E --> F[Create JButton JLabel JPanel]
    F --> G[JComponent instantiated]
    G --> H[LookAndFeel creates ButtonUI delegate]
    H --> I[ButtonUI references ButtonModel]
    I --> J[Component added to Container hierarchy]
    J --> K[setVisible true]
    K --> L[EDT triggers paintComponent]
    L --> M[Off screen image buffer painted]
    M --> N[Buffer blitted to screen]
    N --> O[User clicks button]
    O --> P[ActionListener.actionPerformed invoked]
    P --> Q[Model state updated]
    Q --> L
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Swing Class Inheritance (Functional Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Object] --> B[Component AWT]
    B --> C[Container AWT]
    C --> D[JComponent Swing root]
    D --> E1[JLabel]
    D --> E2[JButton]
    D --> E3[JTextField]
```
