```mermaid
    D --> E4[JPanel]
    D --> E5[JTable]
    D --> E6[JTree]
    D --> E7[JScrollPane]
    C --> F1[JFrame top level]
    C --> F2[JDialog top level]
    C --> F3[JWindow top level]
    C --> F4[JApplet top level]
    E4 --> G1[custom sub panels]
    E2 --> H1[ButtonModel data]
    E2 --> H2[ButtonUI view]
    E2 --> H3[ButtonListener controller]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Lightweight vs Heavyweight Component Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HOST[Host Operating System]
        OS1[Windows GDI]
        OS2[macOS Cocoa]
        OS3[Linux GTK]
    end
    OS1 --> P1[JFrame native peer]
    OS2 --> P1
    OS3 --> P1
    P1 --> L1[JPanel lightweight]
    P1 --> L2[JButton lightweight]
    P1 --> L3[JLabel lightweight]
    L1 --> L4[JTable lightweight]
    L1 --> L5[JTree lightweight]
    L2 --> D1[ButtonModel]
    L2 --> D2[ButtonUI delegate]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Event Dispatch Thread Lifecycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> MainThread: main method invoked
    MainThread --> EDT: SwingUtilities.invokeLater
    EDT --> ConstructUI: build component tree
    ConstructUI --> Idle: await user input
    Idle --> ProcessEvent: mouse or key event
    ProcessEvent --> Dispatch: to registered listeners
    Dispatch --> UpdateModel: mutate Model
    UpdateModel --> Repaint: repaint request enqueued
    Repaint --> Idle: next frame
    Idle --> [*]: window closed
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between heavyweight and lightweight components in Java. Why is Java Swing called lightweight?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Heavyweight components** (AWT) rely on the host operating system's native peer to render themselves. Each component owns a peer object (e.g., `ButtonPeer`), making them platform-dependent, memory-heavy, and prone to platform-specific bugs such as the "gray-rect" issue.
- **Lightweight components** (Swing) have **no native peer**. They inherit from `javax.swing.JComponent` and render themselves using Java 2D drawing primitives (`paintComponent(Graphics g)`) over the canvas of a single heavyweight ancestor (`JFrame` / `JApplet`). This makes them platform-independent, skinnable via PLAF, and flicker-free due to built-in double buffering.
- Hence, Swing is called **lightweight** because it avoids the per-component native peer overhead and draws entirely in pure Java.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is Pluggable Look And Feel (PLAF) in Swing? Name any two built-in L&F classes.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **PLAF** is Swing's mechanism that decouples a component's *behavior* from its *appearance*. Every Swing component delegates its rendering to a `LookAndFeel` object stored in `UIManager`, allowing the entire UI to be re-skinned at runtime by a single call: `UIManager.setLookAndFeel(className)`.
- **Two built-in L&F classes:** `MetalLookAndFeel` (cross-platform default) and `NimbusLookAndFeel` (modern flat look introduced in Java SE 6u10). Other examples include `WindowsLookAndFeel` and `MotifLookAndFeel`.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module 4 Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Explain the MVC architecture used by Swing components. Discuss how the **Model**, **View**, and **Controller** are organized with a suitable example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a complete Java Swing program to create a simple calculator GUI with two `JTextField` inputs, four `JButton`s (+, −, ×, ÷) and a `JLabel` that displays the result. Use `BorderLayout` and handle `ActionEvent`. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a) MVC in Swing (7 marks)

**1. Why MVC?**
The classical problem in AWT was that a component's *data* (its label text, selected state, list contents) and *visual representation* were fused inside a single class. This prevented:
- Attaching the same UI to multiple data sources.
- Re-skinning without recompiling.
- Testing UI logic independently.

**2. Swing's MVC Implementation**
Swing uses a **separable model architecture** with **UI delegates**:

| Layer | Implementation | Example for `JButton` |
|---|---|---|
| **Model** | An interface (e.g., `ButtonModel`) plus a default impl (`DefaultButtonModel`) | Holds `isPressed`, `isArmed`, `isSelected` |
| **View + Controller** | A `ComponentUI` subclass (e.g., `BasicButtonUI`) | Paints the button + routes input |
| **Glue** | The Swing component itself (`JButton`) | Forwards calls to Model and UI |

**3. Code Mapping (7-mark answer)**

```java
JButton btn = new JButton("OK");
ButtonModel model = btn.getModel();          // Access Model
btn.setUI((ButtonUI)UIManager.getUI(btn));   // Swap View
btn.addActionListener(e -> {                 // Add Controller
    if (model.isPressed()) System.out.println("Pressed");
});
```

**4. Exam Tip:** Emphasize that **Swing is "MVC-like"** — it uses a *compound* UI delegate combining View and Controller, rather than three completely independent classes.

#### Model Solution — (b) Calculator GUI (7 marks)

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class CalcGUI extends JFrame implements ActionListener {
    JTextField t1, t2;
    JLabel resultLbl;

    public CalcGUI() {
        setTitle("Mini Calculator");
        setSize(320, 220);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setLayout(new BorderLayout(10, 10));

        // ----- North: inputs -----
        JPanel north = new JPanel(new GridLayout(2, 2, 5, 5));
        t1 = new JTextField(); t2 = new JTextField();
        north.add(new JLabel("Number 1:")); north.add(t1);
        north.add(new JLabel("Number 2:")); north.add(t2);

        // ----- Center: buttons -----
        JPanel center = new JPanel(new GridLayout(1, 4, 5, 5));
        String[] ops = {"+", "-", "*", "/"};
        for (String op : ops) {
            JButton b = new JButton(op);
            b.addActionListener(this);
            center.add(b);
        }

        // ----- South: result -----
        resultLbl = new JLabel("Result: ", SwingConstants.CENTER);
        resultLbl.setBorder(BorderFactory.createEtchedBorder());

        add(north, BorderLayout.NORTH);
        add(center, BorderLayout.CENTER);
        add(resultLbl, BorderLayout.SOUTH);

        setVisible(true);
    }

    @Override
    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        try {
            double a = Double.parseDouble(t1.getText());
            double b = Double.parseDouble(t2.getText());
            String op = ((JButton) e.getSource()).getText();
            double r = switch (op) {
                case "+" -> a + b;
                case "-" -> a - b;
                case "*" -> a * b;
                case "/" -> a / b;
                default  -> 0;
            };
            resultLbl.setText("Result: " + r);
        } catch (Exception ex) {
            resultLbl.setText("Invalid input");
        }
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        SwingUtilities.invokeLater(() -> new CalcGUI());
    }
}
```

**Explanation of Key Lines (mark-wise):**
- `setLayout(new BorderLayout(10,10))` — applies the BorderLayout manager (**1 mark**).
- `JButton` creation inside a `for` loop with `addActionListener(this)` (**1 mark**).
- `actionPerformed` reading the button text using `e.getSource()` cast to `JButton` (**2 marks**).
- `try/catch` for `NumberFormatException` and `ArithmeticException` (**1 mark**).
- `SwingUtilities.invokeLater` to launch on EDT (**1 mark**).
- Proper compilation and output of result in `resultLbl` (**1 mark**).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]` — Alternative Choice

**(a)** List and explain any **seven key features** of Java Swing. Compare Swing with AWT in a tabular form. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java Swing program that demonstrates the use of `JTabbedPane`, `JScrollPane`, and a `JTable` populated with sample student data. Use `JOptionPane` to display a confirmation when a row is selected. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a) Seven Key Features (7 marks)

1. **Lightweight Components** — no native peer, drawn via Java 2D.
2. **Pluggable Look And Feel (PLAF)** — runtime re-skinning via `UIManager`.
3. **MVC Architecture** — separable Model, UI delegate for View+Controller.
4. **Rich Component Set** — `JTable`, `JTree`, `JSlider`, `JProgressBar`, `JTabbedPane`, etc.
5. **Double Buffering** — flicker-free repaint.
6. **Border, Icon, Tooltip Support** — standardized via `JComponent` bean properties.
7. **Accessibility API** — every component implements `Accessible`.
8. **Keybinding & Undo Framework** — `InputMap`/`ActionMap` and `UndoManager`.

**Comparison Table** (already covered in §2.2 — reproduce briefly in answer for full marks).

#### Model Solution — (b) JTabbedPane + JTable Program (7 marks)

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import javax.swing.table.DefaultTableModel;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class TabbedTableDemo extends JFrame {
    public TabbedTableDemo() {
        setTitle("Student Records");
        setSize(500, 300);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE);

        JTabbedPane tabs = new JTabbedPane();

        // --- Tab 1: Table inside JScrollPane ---
        String[] cols = {"Roll No", "Name", "Marks"};
        Object[][] data = {
            {1, "Anu", 89}, {2, "Binu", 76},
            {3, "Cyril", 92}, {4, "Divya", 81}
        };
        DefaultTableModel model = new DefaultTableModel(data, cols);
        JTable table = new JTable(model);
        table.addMouseListener(new MouseAdapter() {
            public void mouseClicked(MouseEvent e) {
                int row = table.getSelectedRow();
                if (row != -1) {
                    int confirm = JOptionPane.showConfirmDialog(
                        TabbedTableDemo.this,
                        "View details of " + model.getValueAt(row, 1) + "?",
                        "Confirm",
                        JOptionPane.YES_NO_OPTION);
                    if (confirm == JOptionPane.YES_OPTION) {
                        JOptionPane.showMessageDialog(
                            TabbedTableDemo.this,
                            "Roll: " + model.getValueAt(row, 0) +
                            ", Marks: " + model.getValueAt(row, 2));
                    }
                }
            }
        });
        tabs.addTab("Students", new JScrollPane(table));

        // --- Tab 2: Simple label panel ---
        JLabel info = new JLabel("Select a student from the first tab", SwingConstants.CENTER);
        tabs.addTab("Info", info);

        add(tabs);
        setVisible(true);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        SwingUtilities.invokeLater(() -> new TabbedTableDemo());
    }
}
```

**Mark Allocation:**
- `JTabbedPane` construction and `addTab` calls (**1 mark**).
- `DefaultTableModel` populated with data array (**1 mark**).
- `JTable` wrapped in `JScrollPane` (**1 mark**).
- `MouseListener` to detect row clicks (**1 mark**).
- `JOptionPane.showConfirmDialog` and `showMessageDialog` usage (**2 marks**).
- Proper `setVisible` and EDT launch (**1 mark**).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the `J` prefix** in `JFrame`/`JButton` (mixing AWT and Swing) → -1 to -2 marks.
> 2. **Not wrapping long-running code in `SwingUtilities.invokeLater()`** → -1 mark on the EDT question.
> 3. **Confusing AWT's `BorderLayout` constants with Swing's** — they are identical but the *import* differs.
> 4. **Calling `setSize()` and then `pack()`** — `pack()` overrides `setSize()`. Use only one.
> 5. **Forgetting `setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE)`** — app window won't close.
> 6. **Painting outside `paintComponent`** — always override `paintComponent(Graphics g)` and call `super.paintComponent(g)` first.
> 7. **MVC confusion** — saying Swing uses "three completely separate classes" per component is **wrong**; it uses a *separable model* with a compound UI delegate.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Swing Fundamentals – Key Features"**

- ✅ Swing is **lightweight, pure-Java, and skinnable**.
- ✅ Package: `javax.swing`; root class: `JComponent`; components begin with `J`.
- ✅ Top-level containers (only **4**): `JFrame`, `JDialog`, `JWindow`, `JApplet`.
- ✅ Key features: lightweight, PLAF, MVC, rich components, double buffering, borders/icons/tooltips, keybindings, accessibility.
- ✅ **PLAF API:** `UIManager.setLookAndFeel(String)`; update with `SwingUtilities.updateComponentTreeUI(window)`.
- ✅ **MVC mapping:** Model = data interface (e.g., `ButtonModel`); View+Controller = `ComponentUI` delegate; Glue = the `JComponent` subclass.
- ✅ **EDT rule:** All UI work on `SwingUtilities.invokeLater(Runnable)`.
- ✅ **Double buffering:** automatic in `JComponent`; no manual override needed for basic use.
- ✅ Swing is **not thread-safe**; never update UI from arbitrary threads.
- ✅ Always override `paintComponent(Graphics g)` — **not** `paint()` — for custom drawing.
- ✅ AWT = heavyweight + native peer; Swing = lightweight + no per-component peer.
- ✅ JFC = AWT + Swing + Accessibility + Java 2D + Drag-and-Drop.
- ✅ Common KTU exam traps: mixing AWT/Swing classes, missing `J` prefix, no EDT, wrong MVC description.
- ✅ For KTU 2024, expect 3-mark theory on **PLAF / MVC / Lightweight** and 7+7 marks on a **full GUI program** with event handling.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Model View Controller (MVC)
Swing Controls
Components and Containers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT414 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Components and Containers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What are AWT Components?

In the Java Abstract Window Toolkit (**AWT**), a **Component** is the abstract base class for all visual user-interface (UI) elements that can be displayed on the screen and can interact with the user. Every button, text field, label, checkbox, scrollbar, and even the top-level windows themselves are derived from `java.awt.Component`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):** A *Component* is an object having a graphical representation that can be displayed on the screen and that can interact with the user. Examples include buttons, checkboxes, scrollbars, and text fields. The `Component` class is the superclass of the AWT hierarchy, and any class that represents a UI control is a direct or indirect subclass of `Component`.

**The Three Core Properties of every AWT Component:**
- A **graphical representation** (it can be drawn on the screen).
- A **size** (width and height in pixels).
- A **location** (x, y coordinate of its top-left corner relative to its parent container).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Methods of the `Component` class** (to be remembered for the KTU exam):
> - `setSize(int width, int height)` – sets the component's dimensions.
> - `setLocation(int x, int y)` – sets the position relative to the parent.
> - `setBounds(int x, int y, int w, int h)` – combined position + size.
> - `setVisible(boolean b)` – shows or hides the component.
> - `setEnabled(boolean b)` – enables or disables user interaction.
> - `setBackground(Color c)` / `setForeground(Color c)` – colour management.
> - `repaint()` – requests the system to redraw the component.

---

### 1.2 What is a Container?

A **Container** is a special kind of `Component` that can hold (and manage the layout of) other components, including other containers. Containers are subclasses of `java.awt.Container`, which itself extends `java.awt.Component`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** A *Container* is a component that can contain other AWT components. Subclasses of `Container` (such as `Panel`, `Frame`, `Dialog`, and `Applet`) provide the actual physical containers that can be added to the screen. The rules governing how components are laid out within a container are governed by a `LayoutManager` object.

---

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **building (your screen)**. Inside the building you have:
- **Rooms** = `Containers` (e.g., `Frame`, `Panel`, `Dialog`). Rooms can hold furniture AND other smaller boxes.
- **Furniture** = `Components` (e.g., `Button`, `Label`, `TextField`). Furniture has a position, size, and appearance but cannot contain other furniture directly.
- **Nested boxes / shelves** = nested `Panels` (containers inside containers).
- **Interior designer** = `LayoutManager`, which decides where every piece of furniture sits inside a room.

> [!NOTE]
> **Hierarchy Intuition:** Just as a `Frame` is a top-level window, a `Panel` is a sub-region. You can place multiple panels inside a frame, and within each panel you can place labels, buttons, and text fields. The `add()` method is the act of "placing furniture into a room."

---

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Property | Standard Value | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Component coordinate origin** | `(0, 0)` | Top-left corner of the parent container |
| **Size unit** | **pixel (px)** | All dimensions are in screen pixels |
| **Default frame size (typical)** | **`400 × 400` pixels** | KTU exam default unless specified |
| **Java's `Dimension` unit** | pixels | Encapsulates width × height |

---

### 1.5 The AWT Class Hierarchy (Snapshot)

The fundamental relationship in AWT is:

```text
java.lang.Object
   └── java.awt.Component
          └── java.awt.Container
                 ├── java.awt.Window
                 │      ├── java.awt.Frame
                 │      └── java.awt.Dialog
                 ├── java.awt.Panel
                 │      └── java.applet.Applet
                 └── java.awt.ScrollPane
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Whenever asked to "draw the AWT class hierarchy", always start from `java.lang.Object` at the top, then `Component`, then `Container`, then branch into `Window`, `Panel`, and `ScrollPane`. Finally expand `Window` into `Frame` and `Dialog`.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of the `Component` Class

The `Component` class is the **superclass of all AWT UI elements** and itself is an **abstract class**. It provides the following groups of functionality:

1. **Appearance Control**
   - `setBackground(Color c)` – sets the background colour of the component.
   - `setForeground(Color c)` – sets the foreground (typically text) colour.
   - `setFont(Font f)` – sets the typeface, style, and size used for rendering text.

2. **Position & Size Control**
   - `setLocation(int x, int y)` – moves the component to the specified coordinates.
   - `setSize(int width, int height)` – resizes the component.
   - `setBounds(int x, int y, int w, int h)` – combined move + resize.
   - `getX()`, `getY()`, `getWidth()`, `getHeight()` – accessor methods.

3. **Visibility & State**
   - `setVisible(true/false)` – shows or hides the component.
   - `setEnabled(true/false)` – controls whether the user can interact with it.
   - `isVisible()`, `isEnabled()` – state checkers.

4. **Painting & Repainting**
   - `repaint()` – requests the AWT painting system to redraw the component.
   - `update(Graphics g)` – internal method called by `repaint()`.
   - `paint(Graphics g)` – the method you override to perform custom drawing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is `Component` abstract?** Because it is too generic. Java needs you to use a specific subclass such as `Button` or `TextField`. A bare `Component` has no useful appearance of its own.

---

### 2.2 Detailed Breakdown of the `Container` Class

`Container` extends `Component`, so a container **inherits all the capabilities of a component** (size, position, visibility, etc.) **and adds the ability to hold other components**. Key methods are:

| Method | Purpose | KTU Exam Importance |
|---|---|---|
| `add(Component comp)` | Appends a component to the container | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| `add(Component comp, int index)` | Inserts a component at a specified position | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| `remove(Component comp)` | Removes a component from the container | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| `removeAll()` | Removes every component from the container | ⭐⭐ |
| `getComponentCount()` | Returns the number of child components | ⭐⭐ |
| `getComponent(int n)` | Returns the n-th child component | ⭐⭐ |
| `setLayout(LayoutManager mgr)` | Sets the layout manager | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| `getLayout()` | Returns the current layout manager | ⭐⭐ |
| `validate()` | Lays out the container's children | ⭐⭐⭐ |

---

### 2.3 Major AWT Container Subclasses

#### A. `Frame` – The Top-Level Window
- A `Frame` is a top-level window with a **title bar**, an optional **menu bar**, and a **border** managed by the operating system.
- It is the **most commonly used top-level container** in standalone GUI programs.
- A `Frame` has its own layout manager (default is `BorderLayout`).
- **Constructors:**
  - `Frame()` – creates a frame with no title.
  - `Frame(String title)` – creates a frame with the specified title.
- **Common Methods:**
  - `setTitle(String title)` – sets the title shown in the title bar.
  - `setMenuBar(MenuBar mb)` – attaches a menu bar to the frame.
  - `setResizable(boolean b)` – allows or blocks the user from resizing the window.
  - `setIconImage(Image img)` – sets the icon shown in the title bar and taskbar.

#### B. `Panel` – A Generic Sub-Container
- A `Panel` is a **simple container** that does NOT have a title bar or border; it is intended to be embedded inside another container (typically a `Frame`).
- The default layout of a `Panel` is `FlowLayout`.
- Panels are very useful for **grouping related components** and applying a different layout manager to a sub-region of the screen.
- **Constructor:** `Panel()` or `Panel(LayoutManager mgr)`.

#### C. `Dialog` – A Pop-Up Window
- A `Dialog` is a top-level window (like `Frame`) that **typically depends on another Frame** (its "owner").
- Used for pop-up messages, login prompts, and modal dialogs.
- **Constructor:** `Dialog(Frame owner, String title, boolean modal)`.

#### D. `Applet` – A Web-Embedded Container (Legacy)
- An `Applet` is a `Panel` subclass that runs inside a web browser or applet viewer. Although deprecated, KTU may still ask conceptual questions about it.

---

### 2.4 Major AWT Components (Non-Container)

| Component | Purpose | Common Constructor |
|---|---|---|
| `Label` | Displays a single line of read-only text | `Label()`, `Label(String text)`, `Label(String text, int alignment)` |
| `Button` | A clickable push button | `Button()`, `Button(String label)` |
| `TextField` | A single-line editable text box | `TextField()`, `TextField(int cols)`, `TextField(String text)` |
| `TextArea` | A multi-line editable text box | `TextArea()`, `TextArea(int rows, int cols)` |
| `Checkbox` | A toggle box (used in groups to make radio buttons) | `Checkbox(String label)`, `Checkbox(String label, boolean state)` |
| `CheckboxGroup` | Groups multiple `Checkbox` objects so only one can be selected (radio behaviour) | `CheckboxGroup()` |
| `Choice` | A drop-down list | `Choice()` |
| `List` | A scrollable list with optional multi-select | `List()`, `List(int rows, boolean multiSelect)` |
| `Canvas` | A blank rectangular area for custom drawing | `Canvas()` |
| `Scrollbar` | A vertical or horizontal scroll bar | `Scrollbar(int orientation)` |

---

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Methods + Rules)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory Add Formula for KTU:**
> $$\text{Container Hierarchy} = \big\{ \text{Frame} \rightarrow \text{Panel} \rightarrow \text{Components} \big\}$$
> In code, this is realized as:
> $$\text{f.add(p);} \quad \text{p.add(b);} \quad \text{// b is a Button}$$

| Concept | Key Rule | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `Component` is abstract | Cannot be instantiated directly | `new Component()` ❌ |
| `Container` extends `Component` | All containers are themselves components | `Frame f = new Frame();` ✔ |
| Default layout of `Frame` | `BorderLayout` | North, South, East, West, Center |
| Default layout of `Panel` | `FlowLayout` | Left-to-right, top-to-bottom |
| Adding a component | Always done on the **container** | `c.add(comp);` |
| `setSize` on a Frame | Must be called **before** `setVisible(true)` | `f.setSize(400, 400);` |
| `setLayout(null)` | Disables the layout manager, manual positioning required | `f.setLayout(null);` |

---

### 2.6 Real-World Utility

AWT components and containers form the **foundation of every Java desktop application**:
- **Eclipse IDE's older versions** used AWT/Swing for menus, toolbars, and text panes.
- **NetBeans IDE's UI** uses Swing (which extends AWT concepts).
- **Banking enterprise GUIs** for teller machines were originally built on AWT frames, panels, and buttons.
- **Industrial Control Panels** for SCADA systems rely on the same component-container model for grouping gauges, indicators, and controls.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Analytical Matrix & Code Walkthroughs

### 3.1 The "Place Furniture in a Room" Algorithm

Every AWT GUI follows the same five-step construction algorithm. This is the **canonical pattern** that you will write over and over in your KTU lab and exam.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 5 Universal Steps of Building an AWT GUI:**
> 1. **Create the top-level container** (a `Frame` or `Dialog`).
> 2. **Set the layout manager** on that container (or `setLayout(null)` for absolute positioning).
> 3. **Create the child components** (buttons, labels, text fields).
> 4. **Add child components to the container** using `add()`.
> 5. **Set the size of the frame** and call `setVisible(true)` **at the end**.

Let us now derive a complete working program step by step.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 1: A Simple Login-Style Frame

**Problem Statement:** Create an AWT `Frame` titled `"Login Window"` with a `Label`, a `TextField`, and a `Button` "Submit". Set the size to **400 × 200** and display it on the screen.

#### Step 1 — Create the top-level container
We instantiate a `Frame` and give it a title. The title appears in the OS-managed title bar.

```java
Frame f = new Frame("Login Window");
```

#### Step 2 — Set the layout manager
The default layout of `Frame` is `BorderLayout`. For this simple example we will set it to `FlowLayout` (left-to-right) so the components line up neatly.

```java
f.setLayout(new FlowLayout());
```

#### Step 3 — Create the child components
We instantiate one `Label`, one `TextField`, and one `Button`. Each is a `Component` object.

```java
Label l = new Label("Username:");        // a non-editable text label
TextField tf = new TextField(15);        // 15 columns wide
Button b = new Button("Submit");         // a clickable button
```

#### Step 4 — Add the components to the container
We call the `add()` method on the container, passing each component as an argument. This is the act of "placing furniture in the room."

```java
f.add(l);
f.add(tf);
f.add(b);
```

#### Step 5 — Set the size and make the frame visible
The size and visibility settings must be made **after** all components are added, otherwise they will not be laid out properly.

```java
f.setSize(400, 200);
f.setVisible(true);
```

#### Complete Program

```java
import java.awt.*;

public class LoginDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Frame f = new Frame("Login Window");          // Step 1
        f.setLayout(new FlowLayout());                // Step 2
        Label l = new Label("Username:");             // Step 3
        TextField tf = new TextField(15);
        Button b = new Button("Submit");
        f.add(l);                                     // Step 4
        f.add(tf);
        f.add(b);
        f.setSize(400, 200);                          // Step 5
        f.setVisible(true);
    }
}
```

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 2: A Frame Containing a Panel (Nested Container)

**Problem Statement:** Create a `Frame` that contains a `Panel`. The `Panel` should hold two `Button` objects ("OK" and "Cancel") arranged in a `GridLayout` of **1 row and 2 columns**.

#### Step-by-Step Reasoning

1. **Outer Container (Frame):** we need a top-level window with a title bar.
2. **Inner Container (Panel):** we need a sub-region to hold the buttons, using a different layout from the frame.
3. **Components (Buttons):** we need two buttons.
4. **Assembly:** add buttons **to the panel**, then add the **panel to the frame**.

#### Complete Program with Step-Wise Annotations

```java
import java.awt.*;

public class PanelInsideFrame {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // 1. Create the top-level container
        Frame f = new Frame("Panel Demo");
        f.setLayout(new BorderLayout());        // Frame uses BorderLayout

        // 2. Create a sub-container (Panel) and set its layout
        Panel p = new Panel();
        p.setLayout(new GridLayout(1, 2));      // 1 row, 2 columns

        // 3. Create the components
        Button b1 = new Button("OK");
        Button b2 = new Button("Cancel");

        // 4. Add components to the panel (innermost first)
        p.add(b1);
        p.add(b2);

        // 5. Add the panel to the frame
        f.add(p, BorderLayout.CENTER);

        // 6. Size & show
        f.setSize(300, 100);
        f.setVisible(true);
    }
}
```

#### Order-of-Add Rule (Analytical Matrix)

| Layer | Object | Method Called On | Argument |
|---|---|---|---|
| Innermost | `Button` "OK" | `p.add(b1)` | component |
| Innermost | `Button` "Cancel" | `p.add(b2)` | component |
| Middle | `Panel` | `f.add(p, "Center")` | sub-container |
| Outermost | `Frame` | `f.setVisible(true)` | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Generalized Add Rule:** The argument of `add()` is always a `Component`. Because `Container` is itself a `Component`, you can legally pass either a leaf component (Button) or another container (Panel) to `add()`. This is the **mechanism that enables nested container hierarchies**.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 3: Using `setLayout(null)` for Absolute Positioning

Sometimes, especially in KTU practical exams, you are asked to place components at **specific x, y coordinates**. In that case you must disable the layout manager and use `setBounds()`.

```java
import java.awt.*;

public class AbsolutePositionDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Frame f = new Frame("Absolute Layout");
        f.setLayout(null);                              // disable layout manager

        Label l = new Label("Enter Name:");
        l.setBounds(50, 80, 100, 30);                   // x, y, width, height
        f.add(l);

        TextField tf = new TextField(20);
        tf.setBounds(160, 80, 150, 30);
        f.add(tf);

        Button b = new Button("Submit");
        b.setBounds(120, 140, 80, 30);
        f.add(b);

        f.setSize(400, 250);
        f.setVisible(true);
    }
}
```

**Coordinate Math (why these numbers?):**
$$\text{Component right edge} = x + \text{width} = 50 + 100 = 150$$
$$\text{TextField starts at } x = 160 \quad (\text{leaves a 10 px gap})$$

---

### 3.5 Component Lifecycle (How a Component Becomes Visible)

The lifecycle of a component follows a strict order. AWA **understanding this order** is essential for KTU viva questions.

1. **Instantiation** – the component object is created with `new`.
2. **Configuration** – properties (size, font, colour) are set using `setXxx()`.
3. **Attachment** – the component is added to a container using `add()`.
4. **Realization** – the AWT realizes the peer (the underlying OS widget) when the container is validated.
5. **Display** – `setVisible(true)` triggers the paint cycle and the component appears on screen.

$$\text{lifecycle} = \big\{ \text{new} \rightarrow \text{configure} \rightarrow \text{add} \rightarrow \text{validate} \rightarrow \text{visible} \big\}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 AWT Class Hierarchy (Block Diagram)

This block diagram shows the inheritance chain from `java.lang.Object` down to the major `Component` and `Container` subclasses.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["java lang Object"] --> B["java awt Component"]
    B --> C["java awt Container"]
    B --> D1["Button"]
    B --> D2["Label"]
    B --> D3["TextField"]
    B --> D4["TextArea"]
    B --> D5["Checkbox"]
    B --> D6["Choice"]
    B --> D7["List"]
    B --> D8["Canvas"]
    B --> D9["Scrollbar"]
    C --> E1["Panel"]
    C --> E2["Window"]
    C --> E3["ScrollPane"]
    E1 --> E1A["Applet"]
    E2 --> F1["Frame"]
    E2 --> F2["Dialog"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Every leaf on the right (Button, Label, Frame, Dialog, etc.) is a concrete class that you can instantiate with `new`. The intermediate nodes (`Component`, `Container`, `Window`) are abstract and cannot be instantiated directly.

---

### 4.2 Component-Container Relationship (Has-A Block Diagram)

This diagram shows the **runtime relationship** between a Frame, a Panel, and the child components inside it.

```mermaid
graph TD
    FR["Frame title Login Window"] --> P1["Panel p1"]
    P1 --> L1["Label Username"]
    P1 --> TF1["TextField 15 cols"]
    P1 --> B1["Button Submit"]
    FR --> P2["Panel p2"]
    P2 --> L2["Label Password"]
    P2 --> TF2["TextField 15 cols"]
    P2 --> B2["Button OK"]
    P2 --> B3["Button Cancel"]
```

**Interpretation:**
- The `Frame` (top-level container) **has** two `Panel` sub-containers.
- `Panel p1` **has** one `Label`, one `TextField`, and one `Button`.
- `Panel p2` **has** one `Label`, one `TextField`, and two `Button` objects.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Shortcut:** When drawing this on paper, label each arrow with the `add()` call that creates the relationship, e.g., `f.add(p1)`, `p1.add(L1)`, `p1.add(TF1)`, etc.

---

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The GUI Construction Pipeline

This diagram shows the **step-by-step build pipeline** that every AWT program follows.

```mermaid
graph LR
    S1["Step 1 Instantiate Frame"] --> S2["Step 2 setLayout"]
    S2 --> S3["Step 3 Instantiate Components"]
    S3 --> S4["Step 4 container add component"]
    S4 --> S5["Step 5 setSize and setVisible true"]
    S5 --> S6["OS draws the GUI on screen"]
    style S1 fill:#f9e79f
    style S2 fill:#aed6f1
    style S3 fill:#a9dfbf
    style S4 fill:#f5b7b1
    style S5 fill:#d7bde2
    style S6 fill:#f0e68c
```

---

### 4.4 Memory Model: How the JVM Stores Containers and Components

```mermaid
graph TD
    HEAP["JVM Heap Memory"] --> FREF["Frame f reference"]
    FREF --> FOBJ["Frame object title Login Window"]
    FOBJ --> LM["LayoutManager BorderLayout"]
    FOBJ --> CHILDREN["List of child Components"]
    CHILDREN --> POBJ["Panel p object"]
    POBJ --> PLM["LayoutManager FlowLayout"]
    POBJ --> PCHILDREN["List of child Components"]
    PCHILDREN --> LBL["Label Username"]
    PCHILDREN --> TFL["TextField 15 cols"]
    PCHILDREN --> BTL["Button Submit"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** Each `add()` call stores the child reference inside the parent container's internal list. Removing a component (via `remove()`) removes that reference, allowing the garbage collector to reclaim the memory.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the term *Component* in Java AWT. List any FOUR commonly used AWT components.**

**Model Answer:**

A *Component* in Java AWT is an abstract class (defined in `java.awt`) that represents any object having a graphical representation that can be displayed on the screen and can interact with the user. It is the root of the AWT UI hierarchy; every AWT visual element is a direct or indirect subclass of `Component`.

**Four commonly used AWT components:**

1. **Button** – a clickable push button (e.g., "Submit").
2. **Label** – a non-editable text display.
3. **TextField** – a single-line editable text input.
4. **Checkbox** – a toggleable box, also used to construct radio buttons via `CheckboxGroup`.

*(Alternative valid components: TextArea, Choice, List, Canvas, Scrollbar.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between a `Component` and a `Container` in Java AWT.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | `Component` | `Container` |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | The abstract superclass of all AWT UI elements | A subclass of `Component` that can hold other components |
| Ability to hold children | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| Examples | `Button`, `Label`, `TextField` | `Frame`, `Panel`, `Dialog` |
| Key method | `setBounds()`, `repaint()` | `add()`, `remove()`, `setLayout()` |
| Instantiation | Abstract (cannot be instantiated) | Also abstract, but its subclasses can be instantiated |

In short, **every `Container` is a `Component`, but not every `Component` is a `Container`.** Containers extend `Component` to add the capability of holding children.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**(a) Explain the AWT class hierarchy starting from `java.lang.Object` to `Frame`, `Panel`, and `Dialog`. List the constructors and any THREE important methods of `Frame`. [7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

The AWT class hierarchy represents the inheritance relationships among the AWT UI classes. The complete hierarchy is:

```text
java.lang.Object
   └── java.awt.Component
          └── java.awt.Container
                 ├── java.awt.Window
                 │      ├── java.awt.Frame
                 │      └── java.awt.Dialog
                 ├── java.awt.Panel
                 │      └── java.applet.Applet
                 └── java.awt.ScrollPane
```

- **`java.lang.Object`** is the root of every Java class hierarchy.
- **`Component`** is the abstract superclass for all UI elements that can be displayed.
- **`Container`** extends `Component` and adds the ability to hold and manage other components.
- **`Window`** is a top-level container (no parent other than the OS) that does not have a title bar or border by default.
- **`Frame`** extends `Window` and **adds a title bar, menu bar, and resizable border** — making it the standard top-level window for standalone Java applications.
- **`Dialog`** extends `Window` and is used for pop-up windows that depend on a parent `Frame`.
- **`Panel`** extends `Container` directly (not through `Window`) and is the simplest container for grouping components. It is intended to be placed **inside** another container.
- **`Applet`** is a legacy `Panel` subclass that ran inside browsers.
- **`ScrollPane`** is a container that provides automatic horizontal and vertical scrolling for a single child.

**Constructors of `Frame`:**
- `Frame()` – creates a frame with no title.
- `Frame(String title)` – creates a frame with the specified title.

**Three important methods of `Frame`:**
1. `setTitle(String title)` – sets the title displayed in the title bar.
2. `setSize(int width, int height)` – sets the dimensions of the frame in pixels.
3. `setVisible(boolean b)` – shows or hides the frame.

*(Other valid methods: `setResizable(boolean)`, `setMenuBar(MenuBar)`, `setLayout(LayoutManager)`, `setIconImage(Image)`, `setBackground(Color)`.)*

---

**(b) Write a complete Java AWT program to create a `Frame` titled "Feedback Form" containing a `Label` "Rate our service:", a `Choice` (drop-down) with options Excellent, Good, Average, Poor, and a `Button` "Submit". Set the layout to `FlowLayout` and the size to 400×200. [7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

```java
import java.awt.*;

public class FeedbackForm {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Step 1: Create the top-level container
        Frame f = new Frame("Feedback Form");

        // Step 2: Set the layout manager
        f.setLayout(new FlowLayout());

        // Step 3: Create the components
        Label l = new Label("Rate our service:");
        Choice c = new Choice();
        c.add("Excellent");
        c.add("Good");
        c.add("Average");
        c.add("Poor");
        Button b = new Button("Submit");

        // Step 4: Add components to the container
        f.add(l);
        f.add(c);
        f.add(b);

        // Step 5: Set size and display
        f.setSize(400, 200);
        f.setVisible(true);
    }
}
```

**Explanation of key steps:**
- `Frame` is the top-level container with a title bar.
- `FlowLayout` arranges components left-to-right, top-to-bottom.
- `Choice` is the drop-down list component; items are added with `add(String)`.
- `setSize(400, 200)` and `setVisible(true)` finalize the GUI; these **must** be at the end of the program.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a) Explain the `Container` class in AWT. List FIVE important methods of the `Container` class with a one-line description of each. [7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

The `Container` class (defined in `java.awt`) is a direct subclass of `Component` and is the **superclass of all AWT containers**. It adds the ability to contain other AWT components (and other containers), to manage them via a `LayoutManager`, and to dispatch events to them.

A `Container` maintains an internal list of child components, accessible through methods such as `getComponentCount()` and `getComponent(int)`.

**Five important methods of the `Container` class:**

| # | Method | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `Component add(Component comp)` | Appends the specified component to the end of the container's child list. |
| 2 | `void remove(Component comp)` | Removes the specified component from the container. |
| 3 | `void removeAll()` | Removes every child component from the container. |
| 4 | `void setLayout(LayoutManager mgr)` | Sets the layout manager that positions and sizes the child components. |
| 5 | `int getComponentCount()` | Returns the number of child components currently in the container. |
| 6 *(bonus)* | `void validate()` | Lays out the container's children (useful after adding/removing children at runtime). |
| 7 *(bonus)* | `Component getComponent(int n)` | Returns the n-th child component (0-indexed). |

---

**(b) Write a Java AWT program that creates a `Frame` with a `Panel` inside it. The `Panel` should contain a `Label`, a `TextField`, and two `Button`s ("Add" and "Clear") arranged in a `GridLayout(2, 2)`. The frame should use `BorderLayout` with the panel placed in the centre. [7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

```java
import java.awt.*;

public class PanelInFrameDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // 1. Top-level container
        Frame f = new Frame("Panel Demo");
        f.setLayout(new BorderLayout());

        // 2. Sub-container (Panel) with GridLayout(2, 2)
        Panel p = new Panel();
        p.setLayout(new GridLayout(2, 2));

        // 3. Components
        Label l = new Label("Enter Item:");
        TextField tf = new TextField(15);
        Button bAdd = new Button("Add");
        Button bClear = new Button("Clear");

        // 4. Add components to the panel
        p.add(l);
        p.add(tf);
        p.add(bAdd);
        p.add(bClear);

        // 5. Add the panel to the frame
        f.add(p, BorderLayout.CENTER);

        // 6. Size and display
        f.setSize(350, 150);
        f.setVisible(true);
    }
}
```

**Explanation:**
- The **Frame** uses `BorderLayout` (the default for `Frame`).
- The **Panel** uses `GridLayout(2, 2)` to arrange its 4 children in a 2-row, 2-column grid.
- Components are added first to the **innermost container (Panel)**, then the Panel is added to the **outer container (Frame)**.
- `setSize()` and `setVisible(true)` are placed at the end, which is a good practice for any AWT program.

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in the KTU Exam:**
> 1. **Calling `setVisible(true)` BEFORE adding components** → components added later will NOT appear. *Always add first, then make visible.*
> 2. **Forgetting `f.setLayout(...)` when using absolute positioning** → the default `BorderLayout` will override your `setBounds()` calls and stack all components in the centre.
> 3. **Writing `new Component()` or `new Container()`** → both are abstract. Always use a concrete subclass such as `Frame`, `Panel`, or `Button`.
> 4. **Adding a `MenuBar` to anything other than a `Frame`** → `setMenuBar()` exists only on `Frame`. Adding a menu bar to a `Dialog` is not allowed.
> 5. **Confusing `setSize()` and `pack()`** → `setSize()` uses **pixels**; `pack()` sizes the window to fit the preferred size of its components. Using them interchangeably will lose you marks.
> 6. **Mixing up AWT and Swing class names** → `java.awt.Frame` and `javax.swing.JFrame` are different. For OOP Module 4 of KTU, **always use AWT classes** unless the question explicitly mentions Swing.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **`Component`** is the abstract superclass of all AWT UI elements; it provides appearance, position, and size management.
- **`Container`** is a special `Component` that can hold other components and other containers.
- **Top-level containers** (those that can stand alone on the screen): `Frame`, `Dialog`, `Applet` (legacy).
- **Intermediate containers** (must be placed inside another container): `Panel`, `ScrollPane`.
- **Five universal steps** of building an AWT GUI: *Create container → Set layout → Create components → Add components → Set size & make visible.*
- The `add()` method always takes a `Component` argument, which is why containers (themselves components) can be nested inside other containers.
- `Frame` has a title bar and default `BorderLayout`; `Panel` has no title bar and default `FlowLayout`.
- Use `setLayout(null)` only when you need absolute positioning with `setBounds(x, y, w, h)`.
- The AWT class hierarchy begins at `java.lang.Object` → `Component` → `Container` → `{Window, Panel, ScrollPane}` → `{Frame, Dialog, Applet}`.
- Component lifecycle: *new → configure → add → validate → setVisible(true)*.
- **Exam mantra:** "Inside-out, then outside-in" — add leaf components to inner containers first, then add inner containers to outer containers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Swing Packages
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT414 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Swing Packages

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Swing** is a lightweight, platform-independent **Graphical User Interface (GUI) widget toolkit** for the Java programming language, packaged under the **`javax.swing`** package hierarchy (Java Extensions → Swing). It forms a major part of the **Java Foundation Classes (JFC)**, alongside AWT (Abstract Window Toolkit), Java 2D, and the Accessibility APIs. Swing was introduced in **Java 1.2 (1998)** as a complete replacement for most of the heavy AWT components and provides a richer, fully customizable set of UI components built on top of AWT's foundational classes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** "Swing is a set of GUI (Graphical User Interface) components built on top of AWT that provides a pluggable look and feel, lightweight components, and follows the MVC (Model-View-Controller) architecture. It is part of the Java Foundation Classes (JFC)."

### 1.1.1 The Swing Package Family

Swing is not a single package — it is a **family of related packages**, all rooted at the `javax.swing` namespace:

| Package | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `javax.swing` | Core components (JButton, JLabel, JFrame, JPanel) |
| `javax.swing.event` | Event classes and listener interfaces |
| `javax.swing.border` | Border decorations (LineBorder, TitledBorder, EtchedBorder) |
| `javax.swing.plaf` | Pluggable Look-and-Feel architecture (UI delegates) |
| `javax.swing.table` | `JTable` model and renderer classes |
| `javax.swing.tree` | `JTree` model and renderer classes |
| `javax.swing.text` | Text component document models |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The LEGO Block System"

Imagine you are building a **robot dashboard**:

- **AWT** is like a set of metal panels that you must hammer and weld together; each panel is heavy, tied directly to your workshop's (operating system's) tools, and once built, it looks exactly like every other robot in that workshop.
- **Swing**, by contrast, is like **LEGO blocks painted to look like a dashboard**. Each block is made of lightweight plastic (pure Java code), it does not depend on the workshop's metal tools (native OS widgets), and you can **swap the entire style** (Lego "Look-and-Feel" pack) — City style, Space style, or Castle style — **without rebuilding** the dashboard. The blocks (components) are designed to fit together in **containers** (the chassis), and an **invisible manager** (LayoutManager) decides where each block sits on the panel.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway for KTU Viva:** If asked "Why Swing over AWT?" — answer with three pillars: **(1) Lightweight (pure Java, no native peers), (2) Pluggable Look-and-Feel, (3) MVC architecture** for component design.

## 1.3 AWT vs Swing — The Foundational Distinction

> [!NOTE]
> **Heavyweight vs Lightweight:**
> - **AWT components** (`Button`, `TextField`) are **heavyweight** — each one is backed by a peer component in the host operating system (e.g., a true Win32 button on Windows).
> - **Swing components** (`JButton`, `JTextField`) are **lightweight** — they are **100% pure Java**, drawn directly onto a canvas by Swing's own painters, with **no OS peer**. The exception is the **top-level containers** (`JFrame`, `JDialog`, `JWindow`, `JApplet`), which *are* heavyweight because they must borrow an OS window to exist.

$$
\text{Swing UI} = \text{Top-Level Container (Heavyweight)} + \text{Mid-Level/Atomic Components (Lightweight)}
$$

## 1.4 Critical Terminology Bank

- **Pluggable Look-and-Feel (PLAF):** A subsystem that lets a program dynamically change the appearance of all its components without code changes.
- **MVC Pattern:** Model (data state) — View (rendering) — Controller (input handling). Swing's `ButtonModel`, `UIResource`, etc., embody this.
- **Delegation-Based Event Handling:** Events are forwarded by a source to registered *listener* objects, rather than the component subclassing-and-overriding (as in old AWT 1.0).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 The Swing Class Hierarchy

Swing's class hierarchy is built on a deliberate **chain of abstraction**. Understanding this tree is essential for KTU theory questions and is directly asked in module-end exams.

### 2.1.1 Component Hierarchy (Top-Down)

```
java.lang.Object
   └── java.awt.Component
         ├── java.awt.Container
         │     ├── java.awt.Window
         │     │     └── java.awt.Frame
         │     │           └── javax.swing.JFrame       ← Top-Level Container
         │     │     └── java.awt.Dialog
         │     │           └── javax.swing.JDialog      ← Top-Level Container
         │     ├── javax.swing.JPanel                  ← Intermediate Container
         │     ├── javax.swing.JScrollPane              ← Intermediate Container
         │     ├── javax.swing.JSplitPane               ← Intermediate Container
         │     └── javax.swing.JTabbedPane              ← Intermediate Container
         ├── javax.swing.JComponent (abstract)
         │     ├── javax.swing.AbstractButton
         │     │     ├── javax.swing.JButton
         │     │     ├── javax.swing.JToggleButton
         │     │     │     ├── javax.swing.JCheckBox
         │     │     │     └── javax.swing.JRadioButton
         │     │     └── javax.swing.JMenuItem
         │     │           ├── javax.swing.JMenu
         │     │           └── javax.swing.JCheckBoxMenuItem
         │     ├── javax.swing.JLabel
         │     ├── javax.swing.JTextField
         │     ├── javax.swing.JTextArea
         │     ├── javax.swing.JComboBox
         │     ├── javax.swing.JList
         │     ├── javax.swing.JSlider
         │     ├── javax.swing.JProgressBar
         │     ├── javax.swing.JTable
         │     ├── javax.swing.JTree
         │     └── javax.swing.JInternalFrame
         └── (AWT components: Button, TextField, ...)
```

### 2.1.2 Breakdown of the Hierarchy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Two Pillars of Swing's Inheritance:**
> 1. **`java.awt.Component`** — root for anything that can appear on screen (has `paint()`, `repaint()`, `setSize()`, `setLocation()`).
> 2. **`javax.swing.JComponent`** — abstract superclass of virtually *all* Swing widgets. It adds: tooltips, borders, double-buffering, key-binding infrastructure, and a pluggable UI delegate (`setUI()`).

## 2.2 Taxonomy of Swing Components

### 2.2.1 Top-Level Containers

These are the **heavyweight roots** of any Swing GUI. Every Swing application has at least one.

| Class | Description |
|---|---|
| `JFrame` | A standard, decorated OS window with title bar, min/max/close buttons, and a content pane. |
| `JDialog` | A secondary pop-up window (modal or modeless) typically used for input/notification. |
| `JApplet` | (Deprecated) Embeds a Swing GUI in a browser via the Java Plug-in. |
| `JWindow` | An undecorated OS-level window (no title bar). |

> [!NOTE]
> **The Content Pane Rule:** You never `add()` lightweight components directly to a top-level container. Instead, you call `frame.getContentPane().add(component)` or, since Java 5, simply `frame.add(component)` which is automatically delegated to the content pane.

### 2.2.2 Intermediate (General-Purpose) Containers

These are **lightweight** containers that organize components *inside* a top-level container.

| Class | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `JPanel` | The most generic, invisible container. Used to group components under a single LayoutManager. |
| `JScrollPane` | Adds scrollbars to a single "viewport" child component (e.g., `JTextArea`, `JTable`). |
| `JSplitPane` | Two components separated by a draggable divider. |
| `JTabbedPane` | A tabbed notebook interface. |
| `JToolBar` | A row/column of buttons/icons, often draggable. |
| `JInternalFrame` | A "fake" window that lives *inside* a `JDesktopPane` (MDI style). |
| `JLayeredPane` | A container that allows components to overlap with depth ordering. |

### 2.2.3 Atomic Components (Widgets)

These are the leaf-level UI elements the user actually interacts with.

| Category | Classes |
|---|---|
| **Display** | `JLabel` |
| **Buttons** | `JButton`, `JToggleButton`, `JCheckBox`, `JRadioButton` |
| **Text Input** | `JTextField`, `JPasswordField`, `JTextArea` |
| **Selection Lists** | `JComboBox`, `JList` |
| **Sliders & Progress** | `JSlider`, `JProgressBar` |
| **Menus** | `JMenuBar`, `JMenu`, `JMenuItem`, `JCheckBoxMenuItem`, `JRadioButtonMenuItem` |
| **Rich Display** | `JTable`, `JTree`, `JEditorPane`, `JTextPane` |

## 2.3 Layout Managers — The Invisible Architects

A Swing container delegates the sizing and positioning of its children to a **`LayoutManager`** object. The container calls `layoutContainer()` on the manager whenever it needs to re-arrange.

### 2.3.1 The Six Essential Layout Managers

| Layout | Behavior | Default Container |
|---|---|---|
| **`BorderLayout`** | Divides container into **North, South, East, West, Center**. Center gets leftover space. | `JFrame`, `JDialog`, `JApplet` content panes |
| **`FlowLayout`** | Lays out components **left-to-right, top-to-bottom** in their preferred size; wraps to next line. | `JPanel` |
| **`GridLayout(rows, cols)`** | Equal-sized rectangular grid; every cell same size. | none (must be set) |
| **`BoxLayout(axis)`** | Single row OR single column; respects `preferredSize`, `maximumSize`, `minimumSize`. | `Box` containers |
| **`GridBagLayout`** | The most flexible — cells can vary in size, span multiple rows/columns; controlled by `GridBagConstraints`. | none (must be set) |
| **`CardLayout`** | Stacks components like a deck of cards; only one visible at a time (like tabs). | none (must be set) |
| **`GroupLayout`** | Preferred by NetBeans GUI Builder; hierarchical groups in horizontal & vertical dimensions. | none (must be set) |
| **`null`** | Absolute positioning — you set `setBounds(x,y,w,h)` manually. | none |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** `JFrame`'s default layout is **`BorderLayout`**; `JPanel`'s default is **`FlowLayout`**. This is a 3-mark standard question.

### 2.3.2 The `Container.add()` Overloads Used With `BorderLayout`

$$
\text{add(comp, BorderLayout.NORTH)} \quad\text{or}\quad \text{add(comp, "North")}
$$

Accepted direction constants: `"North"`, `"South"`, `"East"`, `"West"`, `"Center"` (case-insensitive in older Java, but the typed constants `BorderLayout.NORTH` are preferred).

## 2.4 Pluggable Look-and-Feel (PLAF) — The Magic Skin

Swing components do not draw themselves directly. Each component has a **`UI delegate`** — a separate object from `javax.swing.plaf` that handles the painting. By swapping the delegate, the *same* `JButton` can look like a Windows button, a Mac button, or a Motif button.

```java
import javax.swing.UIManager;

try {
    UIManager.setLookAndFeel("javax.swing.plaf.metal.MetalLookAndFeel"); // Cross-platform
    // OR
    UIManager.setLookAndFeel(UIManager.getSystemLookAndFeelClassName()); // Native
    // OR
    UIManager.setLookAndFeel("com.sun.java.swing.plaf.windows.WindowsLookAndFeel");
} catch (Exception e) { e.printStackTrace(); }

SwingUtilities.updateComponentTreeUI(frame);
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Order matters!** Set the Look-and-Feel **before** constructing any component. After setting it, you must call `SwingUtilities.updateComponentTreeUI(frame)` to retroactively apply the new skin to already-created components.

## 2.5 MVC Architecture in Swing

| Role | Example Class | Responsibility |
|---|---|---|
| **Model** | `ButtonModel`, `ListModel`, `TableModel` | Holds the state (e.g., is the button pressed? what are the list items?) |
| **View** | `ButtonUI` (UI delegate) | Renders the visual representation. |
| **Controller** | UI delegate + Event Listeners | Translates user input (mouse/key) into model changes. |

> [!NOTE]
> **MVC Advantage:** You can change the visual style (View) without touching the data (Model). For example, the `JSlider`'s `BoundedRangeModel` can be reused in a custom widget while the user drags a completely different-looking handle.

## 2.6 Event Handling — The Delegation Model

Old AWT 1.0 forced subclasses to override `handleEvent()`. Modern Swing (and AWT 1.1+) uses **delegation**:

1. **Event Source** — the component that fires the event (e.g., `JButton`).
2. **Event Object** — describes what happened (e.g., `ActionEvent`).
3. **Event Listener Interface** — declares callback methods (e.g., `ActionListener` → `actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)`).
4. **Registration** — `source.addActionListener(myListener)`.

When the user clicks the button, the JVM **invokes `actionPerformed` on every registered listener**.

### 2.6.1 Common Event Listeners Cheat Sheet

| Listener Interface | Key Method | Triggered By |
|---|---|---|
| `ActionListener` | `actionPerformed(ActionEvent)` | Click of button / Enter in text field |
| `MouseListener` | `mouseClicked/Entered/Exited/Pressed/Released` | Mouse activity |
| `MouseMotionListener` | `mouseDragged / mouseMoved` | Mouse movement |
| `KeyListener` | `keyTyped/Pressed/Released` | Keyboard |
| `WindowListener` | `windowOpened/Closing/Closed/Activated/Deactivated/Iconified/Deiconified` | Window state |
| `ItemListener` | `itemStateChanged(ItemEvent)` | Checkbox/radio/combobox toggle |
| `ChangeListener` | `stateChanged(ChangeEvent)` | Slider value change, progress update |
| `FocusListener` | `focusGained/Lost(FocusEvent)` | Focus change |
| `ListSelectionListener` | `valueChanged(ListSelectionEvent)` | List/table row select |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Examples, Code Walkthrough & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The following sections walk through **canonical Swing programs** that are heavily repeated in KTU examinations. Every line is annotated, and the logic of each transition is explained.

## 3.1 Example 1 — Your First `JFrame` (Hello Swing)

**Problem Statement:** Create a `JFrame` titled "First Swing App" of size 400×300, with a `JLabel` saying "Welcome to Swing!" centered on it. Closing the window must terminate the program.

### 3.1.1 The Code (Fully Annotated)

```java
import javax.swing.JFrame;        // Top-level container
import javax.swing.JLabel;        // Atomic display component
import javax.swing.SwingConstants;// Constants like CENTER

public class FirstSwingApp extends JFrame {       // (1) Inherit JFrame

    public FirstSwingApp() {                       // (2) Constructor sets up GUI
        setTitle("First Swing App");               // Window title
        setSize(400, 300);                         // Width × Height in pixels
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE); // (3) Exit JVM on close
        setLocationRelativeTo(null);               // Center on screen

        JLabel label = new JLabel("Welcome to Swing!", SwingConstants.CENTER);
        add(label);                                 // (4) Added to the content pane (BorderLayout.CENTER)
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        FirstSwingApp app = new FirstSwingApp();
        app.setVisible(true);                      // (5) MUST be true to render
    }
}
```

### 3.1.2 Line-by-Line Logic

| Line / Token | Purpose / Why It Matters |
|---|---|
| `extends JFrame` | We inherit all window-management methods (`setTitle`, `setSize`, etc.). Could also use composition: `JFrame f = new JFrame(); f.add(...)`. |
| `setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE)` | Without this, clicking the X only hides the window; the JVM keeps running. The constant `EXIT_ON_CLOSE` (value = 3) tells the JVM to terminate. Other options: `HIDE_ON_CLOSE` (default), `DISPOSE_ON_CLOSE`, `DO_NOTHING_ON_CLOSE`. |
| `setLocationRelativeTo(null)` | `null` parameter means "center relative to the screen". Pass a component to center relative to that component. |
| `JLabel(text, horizontalAlignment)` | The second arg is the alignment within the label's bounding box. In `BorderLayout.CENTER`, the label fills the whole content area, so alignment is moot — but the second-arg constructor is the standard idiom. |
| `setVisible(true)` | Until you call this, the window is not painted. **Never forget it.** |

## 3.2 Example 2 — `BorderLayout` With All Five Regions

**Problem Statement:** Build a window with labels in all five regions of a `BorderLayout`. Observe how North/South get their preferred height, East/West get their preferred width, and Center takes the rest.

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;

public class BorderDemo extends JFrame {

    public BorderDemo() {
        setTitle("BorderLayout Demo");
        setSize(500, 400);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setLayout(new BorderLayout(10, 5));   // (hgap=10, vgap=5)

        add(new JLabel("NORTH", SwingConstants.CENTER), BorderLayout.NORTH);
        add(new JLabel("SOUTH", SwingConstants.CENTER), BorderLayout.SOUTH);
        add(new JLabel("EAST",  SwingConstants.CENTER), BorderLayout.EAST);
        add(new JLabel("WEST",  SwingConstants.CENTER), BorderLayout.WEST);
        add(new JLabel("CENTER",SwingConstants.CENTER), BorderLayout.CENTER);

        setVisible(true);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new BorderDemo();
    }
}
```

**Analytical Insight:**
- If you add **two components to the same region** (e.g., both to NORTH), only the **last one added** is visible (unless you wrap them in a `JPanel` first).
- A region with **no component** is simply skipped; the remaining regions redistribute their space.

## 3.3 Example 3 — `GridLayout` Calculator-Style Grid

**Problem Statement:** A 3×3 grid of `JButton`s labelled "1" through "9".

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;

public class GridDemo extends JFrame {
    public GridDemo() {
        setTitle("GridLayout Demo");
        setSize(300, 300);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setLayout(new GridLayout(3, 3, 5, 5));   // 3 rows, 3 cols, 5px gaps

        for (int i = 1; i <= 9; i++) {
            add(new JButton(String.valueOf(i)));
        }
        setVisible(true);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new GridDemo();
    }
}
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 3 rows × 3 cols works perfectly with 9 buttons:** The product `rows × cols` equals the number of components in this case. If you had 10 components, the 10th would be added but the grid would still display 9 cells (the 10th button takes the first cell of a new implied 4th column).

## 3.4 Example 4 — `JPanel` with `FlowLayout` for Button Row

**Problem Statement:** A toolbar-like row of buttons that wraps automatically.

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;

public class FlowPanelDemo extends JFrame {
    public FlowPanelDemo() {
        setTitle("FlowLayout Panel Demo");
        setSize(400, 200);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE);

        JPanel panel = new JPanel(new FlowLayout(FlowLayout.LEFT, 10, 10));
        // (alignment, hgap, vgap)

        for (char c = 'A'; c <= 'G'; c++) {
            panel.add(new JButton(String.valueOf(c)));
        }

        add(panel);   // adds to BorderLayout.CENTER
        setVisible(true);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new FlowPanelDemo();
    }
}
```

**Analytical Insight — Resize Behavior:** When you resize the window narrower, the buttons **wrap to the next line** — this is `FlowLayout`'s defining trait. With `GridLayout`, they would simply shrink. With `BorderLayout`, the row would stretch.

## 3.5 Example 5 — `JTextField` + `JButton` + `ActionListener` (Full Event Handling)

**Problem Statement:** Type a name in a text field, click "Greet", and a label below says "Hello, \<name\>!".

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class GreeterApp extends JFrame {

    private JTextField nameField;
    private JLabel    greetingLabel;

    public GreeterApp() {
        setTitle("Greeter App");
        setSize(350, 150);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout(FlowLayout.CENTER, 10, 10));

        add(new JLabel("Enter name:"));
        nameField = new JTextField(15);
        add(nameField);

        JButton greetBtn = new JButton("Greet");
        add(greetBtn);

        greetingLabel = new JLabel(" ");
        add(greetingLabel);

        // (1) Event source = greetBtn ; Listener = an anonymous inner class
        greetBtn.addActionListener(new ActionListener() {
            @Override
            public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
                String name = nameField.getText().trim();
                if (name.isEmpty()) {
                    greetingLabel.setText("Please enter a name.");
                } else {
                    greetingLabel.setText("Hello, " + name + "!");
                }
            }
        });

        setVisible(true);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new GreeterApp();
    }
}
```

### 3.5.1 Step-by-Step Event Flow

1. User types "Alice" into `nameField`.
2. User clicks `greetBtn`. The JVM detects a click on a button, creates an `ActionEvent` object.
3. The button iterates through its registered `ActionListener`s.
4. It calls `actionPerformed(e)` on each. Our anonymous inner class executes.
5. `nameField.getText()` returns `"Alice"`; `.trim()` removes surrounding whitespace.
6. `greetingLabel.setText(...)` triggers a repaint of that label only (efficient — no full window repaint needed because Swing uses **dirty-rectangle repainting**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why an Anonymous Inner Class?** It's the most compact way to provide an implementation of a single-method interface right at the point of use. Modern code (Java 8+) often prefers a **lambda**: `greetBtn.addActionListener(e -> { ... });`

## 3.6 Example 6 — `JCheckBox` & `JRadioButton` With `ItemListener`

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class ChoiceApp extends JFrame {

    private JLabel resultLabel;

    public ChoiceApp() {
        setTitle("Choices Demo");
        setSize(350, 220);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout(FlowLayout.LEFT, 10, 10));

        // --- Checkboxes (multi-select) ---
        JCheckBox javaBox = new JCheckBox("Java");
        JCheckBox pyBox   = new JCheckBox("Python");
        add(javaBox);
        add(pyBox);

        // --- Radio buttons (mutually exclusive) ---
        JRadioButton gradBtn  = new JRadioButton("Graduate");
        JRadioButton ugBtn    = new JRadioButton("Undergraduate", true); // default selected
        ButtonGroup levelGrp  = new ButtonGroup();
        levelGrp.add(gradBtn);
        levelGrp.add(ugBtn);
        add(gradBtn);
        add(ugBtn);

        resultLabel = new JLabel("Selections: ");
        add(resultLabel);

        ItemListener il = new ItemListener() {
            @Override
            public void itemStateChanged(ItemEvent e) {
                String msg = "Java=" + javaBox.isSelected() +
                             ", Python=" + pyBox.isSelected() +
                             ", Level=" + (gradBtn.isSelected() ? "Graduate" : "Undergraduate");
                resultLabel.setText(msg);
            }
        };
        javaBox.addItemListener(il);
        pyBox.addItemListener(il);
        gradBtn.addItemListener(il);
        ugBtn.addItemListener(il);

        setVisible(true);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new ChoiceApp();
    }
}
```

### 3.6.1 Critical Analytical Notes

| Concept | Why It Matters |
|---|---|
| `ButtonGroup` | Logically groups radio buttons so **only one can be selected at a time**. Visually, buttons are still added to the container — the group only enforces mutual exclusion. |
| `ItemListener` vs `ActionListener` | For `JToggleButton` subclasses (`JCheckBox`, `JRadioButton`), use **`ItemListener`** because they have a stateful toggle (selected / deselected). |
| Default selection | Pass `true` to the constructor to pre-select, e.g., `new JRadioButton("UG", true)`. |

## 3.7 Example 7 — Nested Containers (Composite Layouts)

The real-world trick is **composing layout managers** — a `BorderLayout` containing a `JPanel` that internally uses `GridLayout`, etc.

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;

public class NestedDemo extends JFrame {

    public NestedDemo() {
        setTitle("Nested Layout Demo");
        setSize(400, 300);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setLayout(new BorderLayout(5, 5));

        // North: a single button
        add(new JButton("Header"), BorderLayout.NORTH);

        // Center: a panel with a 2x2 grid of buttons
        JPanel center = new JPanel(new GridLayout(2, 2, 5, 5));
        for (int i = 1; i <= 4; i++)
            center.add(new JButton("Cell " + i));
        add(center, BorderLayout.CENTER);

        // South: a flow row of buttons
        JPanel south = new JPanel(new FlowLayout(FlowLayout.RIGHT));
        south.add(new JButton("OK"));
        south.add(new JButton("Cancel"));
        add(south, BorderLayout.SOUTH);

        setVisible(true);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new NestedDemo();
    }
}
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern Recognition:** Almost every 14-mark KTU coding question asks you to "Design a Swing application with a specific layout combining multiple regions." This nested-container pattern is the canonical answer.

## 3.8 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Layout?

| Goal | Recommended Layout | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Standard window with header/body/footer | `BorderLayout` | Natural mapping to 5 regions |
| Toolbar / button row that may wrap | `FlowLayout` (in a `JPanel`) | Wraps on resize |
| Calculator keypad, board game | `GridLayout` | Uniform cells |
| Form with varying field widths | `GridBagLayout` | Cell-spanning and resizing rules |
| Tabbed notebook | `CardLayout` (or `JTabbedPane`) | Only one "card" visible |
| Menu of dialog screens | `CardLayout` | Programmatic `next()`/`previous()` |
| Single row or column with sizes | `BoxLayout` | Respects min/pref/max sizes |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Swing Container/Component Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    OS["Operating System Window Layer<br/>(Heavyweight Peer)"]
    subgraph TopLevel["TOP-LEVEL CONTAINERS (Heavyweight)"]
        JF["JFrame"]
        JD["JDialog"]
        JW["JWindow"]
    end
    subgraph Mid["INTERMEDIATE CONTAINERS (Lightweight)"]
        JP["JPanel"]
        JSP["JScrollPane"]
        JTP["JTabbedPane"]
        JSP2["JSplitPane"]
        JTB["JToolBar"]
        JIF["JInternalFrame"]
    end
    subgraph Atomic["ATOMIC COMPONENTS (Lightweight)"]
        JB["JButton / JToggleButton"]
        JL["JLabel"]
        JTF["JTextField / JTextArea"]
        JCB["JCheckBox / JRadioButton"]
        JCB2["JComboBox / JList"]
        JT["JTable / JTree"]
        JM["JMenuBar / JMenu / JMenuItem"]
    end
    LM["LayoutManager<br/>(BorderLayout, FlowLayout,<br/>GridLayout, GridBagLayout,<br/>BoxLayout, CardLayout, GroupLayout)"]
    PLAF["Pluggable Look-and-Feel<br/>UIManager + UI Delegate<br/>(Metal / Windows / Motif / Nimbus)"]
    MVC["MVC Inside Each Component<br/>Model = Data, View = UI Delegate, Controller = Listeners"]
    EH["Event Delegation Model<br/>Source + Event + Listener"]

    OS --> TopLevel
    TopLevel --> Mid
    Mid --> Atomic
    LM -.governs.-> Mid
    LM -.governs.-> TopLevel
    PLAF -.paints.-> TopLevel
    PLAF -.paints.-> Mid
    PLAF -.paints.-> Atomic
    MVC -.embedded in.-> Atomic
    EH -.connects.-> Atomic
```

## 4.2 Event Delegation Model — Interaction Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User
    participant Btn as JButton (Event Source)
    participant JVM as Java Event Dispatch Thread
    participant L as ActionListener (Controller)
    participant Mdl as ButtonModel (Model)
    participant V as ButtonUI (View)

    U->>Btn: Click (mouse press + release inside bounds)
    Btn->>JVM: Fire ActionEvent
    JVM->>L: Invoke actionPerformed(e) on all registered listeners
    L->>Mdl: Query / update state (e.g., isSelected)
    Mdl-->>L: Notify state change via ChangeEvent
    L->>V: Trigger repaint of affected region
    V-->>U: Visual feedback (pressed/unpressed)
```

```mermaid

    subgraph BL["BorderLayout Container"]
        N["NORTH<br/>(preferred height, full width)"]
        W["WEST"] 
        C["CENTER<br/>(all leftover space)"]
        E["EAST"]
        S["SOUTH<br/>(preferred height, full width)"]
    end
    N --> W
    W --> C
    C --> E
    E --> S
    style N fill:#f9c,stroke:#333
    style S fill:#f9c,stroke:#333
    style W fill:#cfc,stroke:#333

```

## 4.4 Swing Component Inheritance Tree (Partial)

```mermaid
graph TB
    Obj["java.lang.Object"]
    Comp["java.awt.Component"]
    Cont["java.awt.Container"]
    JC["javax.swing.JComponent"]
    AB["javax.swing.AbstractButton"]
    Win["java.awt.Window"]
    Frame["java.awt.Frame"]

    Obj --> Comp
    Comp --> Cont
    Comp --> JC
    JC --> AB
    AB --> JB["JButton"]
    AB --> JTB["JToggleButton"]
    JTB --> JCB["JCheckBox"]
    JTB --> JRB["JRadioButton"]
    AB --> JMI["JMenuItem"]
    JMI --> JM["JMenu"]

    JC --> JLst["JList"]

    Win --> Frame

```

## 4.5 Swing vs AWT — Architectural Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph AWT["AWT (Heavyweight)"]
        A1["Java Button Class"]
        A2["Native Peer (Win32 Button, Cocoa Button, etc.)"]
        A1 --> A2
    end
    subgraph Swing["Swing (Lightweight)"]
        S1["Java JButton Class"]
        S2["Java UI Delegate<br/>(MetalButtonUI, WindowsButtonUI, ...)"]
        S3["Java Canvas / Top-Level Window"]
        S1 --> S2
        S2 --> S3
    end
    OS["Operating System"] --> A2
    OS -.only window decoration.-> S3
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagram:** In AWT, **every** component relies on the OS for its rendering. In Swing, only the **top-level window** touches the OS (to draw the title bar / borders); every other component is drawn by Java code onto a Java canvas.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between AWT and Swing. Mention any three advantages of Swing over AWT.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | AWT | Swing |
|---|---|---|
| Package | `java.awt` | `javax.swing` |
| Component type | Heavyweight (OS peers) | Lightweight (pure Java, except top-level) |
| Look-and-Feel | Fixed by the OS | Pluggable (Metal, Windows, Motif, Nimbus) |
| Component richness | Limited set | Rich, extended set (`JTable`, `JTree`, etc.) |
| MVC adherence | Minimal | Yes — Model / UI Delegate / Listener |
| Double buffering | Not by default | Built-in for smoother rendering |

**Three advantages of Swing:**
1. **Platform-independent appearance and behavior** through the Pluggable Look-and-Feel architecture — same code, different look on different OSes.
2. **Lightweight components** — drawn by Java, fewer OS resources consumed; faster rendering and easier customization.
3. **Richer component set** — `JTable`, `JTree`, `JTabbedPane`, `JSlider`, etc., are absent or primitive in AWT.

---

### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the roles of `ContentPane` and `LayoutManager` in a Swing `JFrame`.**

**Model Answer:**

**Content Pane:**
A `JFrame`'s `ContentPane` is an internal `JPanel` (a lightweight container) that holds all the visible child components of the frame. Lightweight Swing components **cannot be added directly to the `JFrame`** itself — they must be added to the content pane. Since Java 5, the convenience method `frame.add(comp)` automatically delegates to the content pane.

**Layout Manager:**
A `LayoutManager` is an object implementing the `java.awt.LayoutManager` interface that controls the **size and position** of components within a container. The default layout of a `JFrame`'s content pane is **`BorderLayout`**. Common implementations include `FlowLayout`, `GridLayout`, `BorderLayout`, `BoxLayout`, `GridBagLayout`, `CardLayout`, and `GroupLayout`. If you set `setLayout(null)`, you take full responsibility for sizing components via `setBounds(x, y, width, height)`.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B has sub-parts typically **7 + 7 marks**, with an internal choice between (a) and (b). Below, **Question A** and **Question B** are two **independent full 14-mark questions**; in the actual exam, a student picks *one* of the two complete questions.

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 (Model Paper)]`

**(a) Explain the Swing class hierarchy starting from `java.awt.Component` to `JButton`. List any four properties inherited by all Swing components from `JComponent`. (7 Marks)**

**(b) Write a Java Swing program to create a `JFrame` titled "Login Form" containing two `JTextField`s (Username, Password), a `JButton` "Login", and a `JLabel` to display a message.
