When "Login" is clicked, validate that the username is "admin" and the password is "1234"; show appropriate success/failure message. (7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a) Swing Class Hierarchy

The class chain from `Component` to `JButton` is:

$$
\text{java.lang.Object} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{java.awt.Component} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{java.awt.Container} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{javax.swing.JComponent} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{javax.swing.AbstractButton} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{javax.swing.JButton}
$$

**Step-by-step purpose of each level:**

| Level | Role |
|---|---|
| `Object` | Root of the Java class hierarchy. |
| `Component` | Adds the ability to be drawn (`paint`), positioned (`setLocation`), sized (`setSize`), and to receive events. |
| `Container` | A component that can hold **other components**; introduces `add()`, `remove()`, and `validate()`. |
| `JComponent` | Abstract superclass of almost all Swing widgets. Adds tooltips, borders, double-buffering, key bindings, accessibility, and a **pluggable UI delegate** (`setUI`). |
| `AbstractButton` | Common superclass of all button-like widgets; holds the shared button model (`ButtonModel`), icons, mnemonic, and keyboard shortcuts. |
| `JButton` | The concrete, most common push-button widget. |

**Four properties inherited from `JComponent`:**
1. **Tooltip support** — `setToolTipText(String)` shows hover help text.
2. **Border** — `setBorder(Border)` decorates the component edge (Line, Titled, Etched, Empty).
3. **Double-buffering** — automatic, reduces flicker during repaints.
4. **UI Delegate** — `setUI(ComponentUI)` swaps the appearance via Pluggable Look-and-Feel.
5. *(Bonus acceptable)* **Key bindings**, **autoscroll**, **accessible name/description**.

#### Model Solution — (b) Login Form Program

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class LoginForm extends JFrame {

    private JTextField userField;
    private JPasswordField passField;
    private JLabel messageLabel;

    public LoginForm() {
        setTitle("Login Form");
        setSize(350, 200);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setLayout(new GridLayout(4, 2, 5, 5));

        add(new JLabel("Username:"));
        userField = new JTextField(15);
        add(userField);

        add(new JLabel("Password:"));
        passField = new JPasswordField(15);
        add(passField);

        JButton loginBtn = new JButton("Login");
        add(loginBtn);

        JButton clearBtn = new JButton("Clear");
        add(clearBtn);

        messageLabel = new JLabel(" ");
        add(messageLabel);
        add(new JLabel(" "));   // placeholder to fill the 4x2 grid

        loginBtn.addActionListener(e -> {
            String user = userField.getText().trim();
            String pass = new String(passField.getPassword());

            if (user.equals("admin") && pass.equals("1234")) {
                messageLabel.setText("Login successful! Welcome, " + user + ".");
            } else {
                messageLabel.setText("Invalid credentials. Try again.");
            }
        });

        clearBtn.addActionListener(e -> {
            userField.setText("");
            passField.setText("");
            messageLabel.setText(" ");
        });

        setVisible(true);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new LoginForm();
    }
}
```

**Logic walkthrough:**
1. The 4×2 `GridLayout` reserves 8 cells: 2 label-textfield rows, 2 button cells, 2 message cells (one label + one filler blank label).
2. `JPasswordField` masks input as dots — access via `getPassword()` (returns `char[]`, *not* `String`, for security).
3. The login lambda compares both fields against hardcoded credentials and updates the `JLabel` accordingly.
4. The "Clear" button resets all fields.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 (Model Paper)]`

**(a) With a neat diagram, explain the Pluggable Look-and-Feel (PLAF) architecture of Swing. How is it set programmatically? (7 Marks)**

**(b) Write a Swing program demonstrating the use of `JCheckBox` (hobbies), `JRadioButton` (gender), grouped with `ButtonGroup`, and a `JComboBox` (city). On changing any selection, display the current selections in a `JLabel`. (7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a) Pluggable Look-and-Feel Architecture

**Definition:** Pluggable Look-and-Feel is an architectural pattern in Swing that **decouples a component's behavior from its appearance**, so the entire UI can be re-skinned at runtime without modifying application code.

**Architecture Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    App["Application Code<br/>(JButton, JFrame, JPanel, ...)"]
    Comp["Swing Component<br/>e.g. JButton"]
    UIDel["UI Delegate<br/>e.g. MetalButtonUI,<br/>WindowsButtonUI,<br/>MotifButtonUI"]
    UIRes["UIResource<br/>(colors, fonts, icons)"]
    LF["LookAndFeel Object<br/>(MetalLookAndFeel,<br/>WindowsLookAndFeel)"]
    UM["UIManager<br/>(singleton, holds current L&F)"]

    App --> Comp
    Comp -. delegates painting to .-> UIDel
    UIDel --> UIRes
    LF -.registers defaults in.-> UM
    UM -.provides defaults and factory.-> UIDel
```

**Three-tier Roles:**

1. **Component** — holds behavior & data; delegates **all rendering** to its UI delegate (`button.getUI().paint(...)`).
2. **UI Delegate** — a `ComponentUI` subclass responsible for painting and hit-testing for *one* L&F (e.g., `MetalButtonUI`).
3. **LookAndFeel** — a factory that constructs the correct UI delegate for every component type when a new L&F is installed.

**Programmatic Setup:**

```java
import javax.swing.*;

public class PLAFDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {

        // (1) Set the L&F BEFORE constructing any component
        try {
            UIManager.setLookAndFeel("javax.swing.plaf.nimbus.NimbusLookAndFeel");
        } catch (Exception e) {
            e.printStackTrace();
        }

        // (2) Now construct the GUI
        JFrame f = new JFrame("PLAF Demo");
        f.setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        f.setSize(300, 200);
        f.add(new JButton("Click Me"));
        f.setVisible(true);
    }
}
```

**Helper methods on `UIManager`:**
- `UIManager.getSystemLookAndFeelClassName()` — returns the L&F matching the host OS.
- `UIManager.getCrossPlatformLookAndFeelClassName()` — returns the L&F `MetalLookAndFeel` (works on every OS).
- `SwingUtilities.updateComponentTreeUI(frame)` — applies the new L&F to an already-constructed GUI.

---

#### Model Solution — (b) Selection Form Program

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class SelectionForm extends JFrame {

    private JCheckBox musicBox, sportsBox, readingBox;
    private JRadioButton maleBtn, femaleBtn, otherBtn;
    private JComboBox<String> cityCombo;
    private JLabel resultLabel;

    public SelectionForm() {
        setTitle("Selection Form");
        setSize(400, 350);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout(FlowLayout.LEFT, 10, 10));

        // ---------- Hobbies (multi-select) ----------
        add(new JLabel("Hobbies:"));
        musicBox   = new JCheckBox("Music");
        sportsBox  = new JCheckBox("Sports");
        readingBox = new JCheckBox("Reading");
        add(musicBox); add(sportsBox); add(readingBox);

        // ---------- Gender (single-select via ButtonGroup) ----------
        add(new JLabel("Gender:"));
        maleBtn   = new JRadioButton("Male");
        femaleBtn = new JRadioButton("Female");
        otherBtn  = new JRadioButton("Other");
        ButtonGroup genderGrp = new ButtonGroup();
        genderGrp.add(maleBtn);
        genderGrp.add(femaleBtn);
        genderGrp.add(otherBtn);
        add(maleBtn); add(femaleBtn); add(otherBtn);

        // ---------- City (JComboBox) ----------
        add(new JLabel("City:"));
        cityCombo = new JComboBox<>(new String[]{"Kochi", "Trivandrum", "Calicut", "Chennai"});
        add(cityCombo);

        // ---------- Result label ----------
        resultLabel = new JLabel(" ");
        resultLabel.setPreferredSize(new Dimension(380, 30));
        add(resultLabel);

        // ---------- One ItemListener for ALL controls ----------
        ItemListener updater = e -> updateResult();

        musicBox.addItemListener(updater);
        sportsBox.addItemListener(updater);
        readingBox.addItemListener(updater);
        maleBtn.addItemListener(updater);
        femaleBtn.addItemListener(updater);
        otherBtn.addItemListener(updater);
        cityCombo.addItemListener(updater);

        setVisible(true);
    }

    private void updateResult() {
        StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder("Hobbies: ");
        sb.append(musicBox.isSelected()   ? "Music "   : "");
        sb.append(sportsBox.isSelected()  ? "Sports "  : "");
        sb.append(readingBox.isSelected() ? "Reading " : "");
        sb.append(" | Gender: ");
        if      (maleBtn.isSelected())   sb.append("Male");
        else if (femaleBtn.isSelected()) sb.append("Female");
        else if (otherBtn.isSelected())  sb.append("Other");
        else sb.append("-");
        sb.append(" | City: ").append(cityCombo.getSelectedItem());

        resultLabel.setText(sb.toString());
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new SelectionForm();
    }
}
```

**Logic walkthrough:**
1. `JCheckBox`es are added directly to the container — no `ButtonGroup` — because multi-selection is desired.
2. `JRadioButton`s are grouped with a `ButtonGroup` so only one is selectable. The group does **not** affect rendering; it only enforces logical exclusion.
3. `JComboBox` is constructed with a `String[]` (or a `Vector` / `ComboBoxModel`) of cities.
4. A **single** `ItemListener` is reused for every control — each fires `itemStateChanged()` whenever its state changes; the helper `updateResult()` rebuilds the summary string.
5. Lambda expressions simplify the listener (Java 8+ feature).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Swing Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting `setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE)`** → window hides but program runs forever (-1 mark).
> 2. **Adding components directly to `JFrame` without realizing that since Java 5 this works via the content pane, but in older Java it's a bug** — always mention the content pane explicitly in theory answers.
> 3. **Not calling `setVisible(true)`** → blank screen at runtime. Examiners catch this immediately (-2 marks).
> 4. **Mixing `ActionListener` and `ItemListener` on toggle components** — `JCheckBox` and `JRadioButton` use `ItemListener` (state change), not `ActionListener`. Mixing them works in some cases but is *semantically wrong* (-1 mark).
> 5. **Confusing `FlowLayout`'s alignment constants** with `BorderLayout`'s region constants** — they are *different* enum-like sets. `FlowLayout.LEFT` is an `int`, `BorderLayout.NORTH` is a `String`.
> 6. **Not setting the LayoutManager's hgap/vgap carefully** — `new FlowLayout()` defaults to 5-pixel gaps; some exam answers accidentally pass position constants as gaps.
> 7. **Blocking the Event Dispatch Thread (EDT)** — calling `setVisible` from another thread without `SwingUtilities.invokeLater(...)` can cause rendering bugs. Mentioning EDT in long answers is a bonus differentiator.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision — print this section before the exam:**

- ✅ Swing is part of the **JFC** (Java Foundation Classes), all in the `javax.swing` package family.
- ✅ Swing components are **lightweight** (pure Java); only the four **top-level containers** (`JFrame`, `JDialog`, `JWindow`, `JApplet`) are heavyweight.
- ✅ The **root** of almost all Swing widgets is the abstract class **`javax.swing.JComponent`**, which itself extends `java.awt.Container` → `java.awt.Component`.
- ✅ **Top-level containers** host the GUI; **intermediate containers** (`JPanel`, `JScrollPane`, `JTabbedPane`, `JSplitPane`, `JToolBar`) organize components; **atomic components** (`JButton`, `JLabel`, `JTextField`, etc.) are the leaves.
- ✅ Default layout: `JFrame` content pane → **`BorderLayout`**; `JPanel` → **`FlowLayout`**.
- ✅ Always end main with `setVisible(true)`; set `EXIT_ON_CLOSE` so the JVM terminates.
- ✅ `add(component)` on a `JFrame` since Java 5 delegates to the content pane.
- ✅ **PLAF** = Pluggable Look-and-Feel; switch via `UIManager.setLookAndFeel(...)` *before* building components, then `SwingUtilities.updateComponentTreeUI(frame)` to refresh.
- ✅ **Event Delegation Model**: Source → Event object → Listener interface (`ActionListener`, `ItemListener`, `MouseListener`, `KeyListener`, `WindowListener`, `ChangeListener`, etc.).
- ✅ **MVC** inside every Swing component: Model (data), View (UI delegate — paints), Controller (listener — responds).
- ✅ **Buttons** → `JButton`; **Toggle** → `JToggleButton` (parents of `JCheckBox` and `JRadioButton`); for radio mutual exclusion, wrap in **`ButtonGroup`**.
- ✅ Use **`ItemListener`** for toggle state changes (`JCheckBox`, `JRadioButton`, `JComboBox`); use **`ActionListener`** for command events (`JButton`, `JTextField` Enter, `JMenuItem`).
- ✅ Layouts: `BorderLayout` (5 regions), `FlowLayout` (wrapping row), `GridLayout` (uniform grid), `BoxLayout` (single axis with min/pref/max sizes), `GridBagLayout` (flexible with `GridBagConstraints`), `CardLayout` (stacked deck), `GroupLayout` (GUI-builder friendly).
- ✅ Nested containers compose layouts — the standard pattern for any non-trivial screen.
- ✅ `null` layout = **absolute positioning**; you must `setBounds(x, y, width, height)` manually and re-layout on resize yourself.

---

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Swing Packages**
> **Course:** PEECT414 - Object Oriented Programming | **Scheme:** KTU 2024

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Event Handling in Swings
Swing Layout Managers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT414 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Swing Layout Managers # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition A **Layout Manager** in Java Swing is an object that implements the `LayoutManager` (or `LayoutManager2`) interface and dictates the size and position of components inside a container such as a `JFrame`, `JPanel`, `JApplet`, or `JDialog`. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus, layout managers are the mechanism by which the Java AWT/Swing framework automatically arranges GUI components in a platform-independent manner, decoupling the developer's positional logic from the underlying windowing system. The interface `java.awt.LayoutManager` is defined in the `java.awt` package and contains five key methods that every concrete layout must (or may) implement: | Method Signature | Purpose | |---|---| | `void addLayoutComponent(String, Component)` | Adds a component to the layout's internal bookkeeping. | | `void removeLayoutComponent(Component)` | Removes a component from the layout. | | `Dimension preferredLayoutSize(Container)` | Returns the ideal size of the container. | | `Dimension minimumLayoutSize(Container)` | Returns the smallest size the container can shrink to. | | `void layoutContainer(Container)` | Physically positions child components. | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Every container in Swing has a **default layout manager** assigned. For `JFrame` (via its `ContentPane`) it is `BorderLayout`; for `JPanel` it is `FlowLayout`. Setting a layout to `null` disables automatic positioning, making the developer fully responsible for component coordinates. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy Imagine you are furnishing a hotel room. You do not manually measure where each chair goes every time a guest of a different height arrives. Instead, you have a **housekeeper (the layout manager)** who follows rules: - "Put the bed against the North wall" — `BorderLayout.NORTH` - "Line up all chairs in a single row" — `FlowLayout` - "Divide the floor into 2×2 equal squares and put one item in each" — `GridLayout(2,2)` The housekeeper is the **Layout Manager**. Without one, you would have to place every pixel manually (which is what `setLayout(null)` forces you to do). Layout managers make the UI **adaptive** — when the window is resized, the components rearrange themselves automatically. > [!NOTE] > **Core Definition (KTU Board Standard):** A layout manager is a Java object whose responsibility is to compute the `bounds` (x, y, width, height) of every child component when `Container.validate()` or `Container.revalidate()` is invoked. ## 1.3 Why Layout Managers Exist - **Platform Independence:** A button has different native sizes on Windows, macOS, and Linux. Layout managers abstract this. - **Dynamic Resizing:** When the user drags the window corner, components re-flow automatically. - **Internationalization:** When a label's text changes from "Save" to "Speichern" (German), the surrounding components adjust. - **Separation of Concerns:** Logic is separated from presentation — the developer says *what* to add, the manager decides *where* to place it. ## 1.4 The Container-Layout Relationship A container holds exactly one active layout at any given time, set via `setLayout(LayoutManager)`. The container's parent (or the windowing system) calls `layoutContainer()` whenever the container is resized or validated. This triggers a cascade: each child component is queried for its `preferredSize`, `minimumSize`, and `maximumSize`, and the layout algorithm distributes available space. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Layout Managers Layout managers are broadly divided into three categories based on their behavior: 1. **Order-based Managers** — Add components in insertion order (`FlowLayout`). 2. **Region-based Managers** — Use compass directions (`BorderLayout`). 3. **Grid-based Managers** — Use mathematical cell coordinates (`GridLayout`, `GridBagLayout`). 4. **Constraint-based Managers** — Use a separate constraints object (`GridBagLayout`, `BoxLayout`). > [!TIP] > **KTU High-Yield Tip:** The `LayoutManager2` interface extends `LayoutManager` and adds support for *constraints-based* addition (`addLayoutComponent(Component, Object)`) and a `maximumLayoutSize()` method. This is the interface implemented by `BorderLayout`, `GridBagLayout`, and `BoxLayout`. ## 2.2 Detailed Analysis of Each Major Layout ### 2.2.1 BorderLayout **Operational Rule:** Divides the container into **5 regions**: `NORTH`, `SOUTH`, `EAST`, `WEST`, and `CENTER`. Each region can hold exactly one component (or a nested panel). The `CENTER` region receives all leftover space. **Constructor Variants:** - `BorderLayout()` — 0 pixel gaps. - `BorderLayout(int hgap, int vgap)` — Horizontal and vertical pixel gaps. **Algorithm Steps:** 1. Compute total height: `totalH = NORTH.preferredSize.height + SOUTH.preferredSize.height + CENTER.allocatedHeight`. 2. Width of `NORTH`/`SOUTH` = full container width. 3. `EAST`/`WEST` are placed between North and South, taking their preferred width. 4. Whatever remains is given to `CENTER`. ### 2.2.2 FlowLayout **Operational Rule:** Lays out components **left-to-right**, top-to-bottom, in the order they were added. When a row runs out of horizontal space, it "flows" to the next line. **Constructor Variants:** - `FlowLayout()` — `CENTER` alignment, 5px gaps. - `FlowLayout(int align)` — Alignment is one of `FlowLayout.LEFT`, `CENTER`, `RIGHT`, `LEADING`, `TRAILING`. - `FlowLayout(int align, int hgap, int vgap)` — Alignment plus custom gaps. ### 2.2.3 GridLayout **Operational Rule:** Divides the container into a **rectangular grid of equal-sized cells** (rows × columns). Each cell holds exactly one component. All cells have identical dimensions. **Constructor Variants:** - `GridLayout()` — 1 row, 0 columns (single row). - `GridLayout(int rows, int cols)` — Specified grid. - `GridLayout(int rows, int cols, int hgap, int vgap)` — With custom gaps. **Mathematical Constraint:** If `rows > 0`, columns are computed; if `cols > 0`, rows are computed. At least one must be non-zero; the other can be 0 to mean "any number." ### 2.2.4 BoxLayout **Operational Rule:** Stacks components either **vertically (Y_AXIS)** or **horizontively (X_AXIS)** in a single line, respecting each component's maximum, minimum, and preferred sizes. It is the foundation of `Box` and `Box.createXxxBox()`. ### 2.2.5 GridBagLayout **Operational Rule:** The most powerful and complex manager. Places components in a virtual grid of rectangular cells where each component can occupy **multiple rows/columns** with custom **fill, weight, insets, and anchoring**. Requires a `GridBagConstraints` object per component. ### 2.2.6 CardLayout **Operational Rule:** Treats each component as a **"card"** stacked on top of each other; only one is visible at a time. The developer navigates via `first()`, `next()`, `previous()`, `last()`, `show(container, name)`. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Formula / Rule | Description | Unit / Value | |---|---|---| | `totalComponents = rows × cols` | GridLayout must satisfy this (or use `rows=0` to mean "many") | integer | | `hgap ≥ 0, vgap ≥ 0` | All gap parameters are non-negative pixel counts | pixels (px) | | `BorderLayout` regions | Exactly 5 valid regions: N, S, E, W, CENTER | enum | | `Component.preferredSize` | Queried by the manager to size children | Dimension object | | `Container.validate()` | Forces re-layout. Called automatically after `add()`/`remove()` | void | | `Container.revalidate()` | Marks layout as invalid; layout happens on next paint cycle (Swing-safe) | void | | `setLayout(null)` | Disables auto-layout. Developer sets `setBounds(x,y,w,h)` manually | void | | `BoxLayout.X_AXIS = 0` | Horizontal stacking constant | enum | | `BoxLayout.Y_AXIS = 1` | Vertical stacking constant | enum | | `GridBagConstraints.weightx, weighty ∈ [0.0, 1.0]` | Distribution of extra space among components | ratio | | `GridBagConstraints.fill = NONE / HORIZONTAL / VERTICAL / BOTH` | How component fills its display area | enum | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Boundary Condition (KTU Favorite):** If you specify `GridLayout(0, 3)`, it means "any number of rows, exactly 3 columns." The 0 acts as a wildcard. The number of components added dictates the row count. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Layout managers are foundational in: - **IDE Development:** IntelliJ, Eclipse, NetBeans use `GridBagLayout` to construct their flexible tool windows. - **Banking Software:** Forms with input fields use `GridBagLayout` to align labels and text fields. - **Medical Imaging Software:** DICOM viewers use `BorderLayout` with center as the image canvas and sides as tool palettes. - **Industrial SCADA Systems:** Use `CardLayout` to swap between different screen dashboards. - **Mobile-Style Forms:** Android's layout XML is conceptually a descendant of `GridBagLayout` principles. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix This section provides exhaustive, step-by-step analysis of layout behavior. Every visual coordinate is derived to make the algorithmic logic completely transparent. ## 3.1 Derivation: BorderLayout Coordinate Computation Suppose we have a container of size `W × H` (Width × Height), and we add 5 components: `northBtn`, `southBtn`, `eastBtn`, `westBtn`, `centerLbl`. The default hgap/vgap = 0. **Step 1 — Establish Known Preferred Heights (top and bottom strips).** The North and South components take their full preferred height, capped at the container's total height. $$h_N = \min(\text{northBtn.preferredSize.height}, \; H)$$ $$h_S = \min(\text{southBtn.preferredSize.height}, \; H - h_N)$$ **Step 2 — Compute the Middle Vertical Band Height.** $$H_{mid} = H - h_N - h_S$$ **Step 3 — Establish East/West Widths within the Middle Band.** $$w_W = \min(\text{westBtn.preferredSize.width}, \; W)$$ $$w_E = \min(\text{eastBtn.preferredSize.width}, \; W - w_W)$$ **Step 4 — Compute Center Dimensions (the leftover region).** $$W_{center} = W - w_W - w_E$$ $$H_{center} = H_{mid}$$ **Step 5 — Assign Final Bounds (with gaps incorporated).** For non-zero `hgap` and `vgap` (`g_h`, `g_v`): $$\text{center.x} = w_W + g_h$$ $$\text{center.y} = h_N + g_v$$ $$\text{center.width} = W_{center} - 2 g_h$$ $$\text{center.height} = H_{center} - 2 g_v$$ **Logic Explanation:** The CENTER region is always the *remainder* after all other regions claim their preferred size. This is why putting a critical component (like a drawing canvas) in CENTER is the canonical pattern — it scales with the window. ## 3.2 Derivation: GridLayout Cell Geometry Given a container of `W × H` pixels, with `rows = R` and `cols = C`, with horizontal gap `g_h` and vertical gap `g_v`: **Step 1 — Compute Usable Space (subtracting internal gaps).** $$W_{usable} = W - g_h \cdot (C + 1)$$ $$H_{usable} = H - g_v \cdot (R + 1)$$ **Step 2 — Compute Per-Cell Dimensions (all cells are equal).** $$\text{cellWidth} = \frac{W_{usable}}{C}$$ $$\text{cellHeight} = \frac{H_{usable}}{R}$$ **Step 3 — Determine Cell `(i, j)` Origin (0-indexed).** $$x_{i,j} = g_h + j \cdot (\text{cellWidth} + g_h)$$ $$y_{i,j} = g_v + i \cdot (\text{cellHeight} + g_v)$$ **Logic Explanation:** Components are placed in *row-major* order (row 0 fills left-to-right, then row 1, etc.). If you add fewer components than `R × C`, the unused cells remain empty. If you add more, the additional components overflow gracefully in some implementations but should be avoided. ## 3.3 Exhaustive Code Walkthrough: Mixed Layouts via Nested Panels The following is a complete KTU-style demonstrable program. The nesting of panels is the single most important layout technique — the KTU board routinely asks students to construct a "form layout" using this pattern. ```java import javax.swing.*; import java.awt.*; public class RegistrationForm extends JFrame { public RegistrationForm() { setTitle("Student Registration"); setSize(400, 300); setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE); setLayout(new BorderLayout(10, 10)); // 10px gaps on the outer frame // -------- NORTH: Header Label -------- JLabel header = new JLabel("Register New Student", SwingConstants.CENTER); header.setFont(new Font("Arial", Font.BOLD, 18)); add(header, BorderLayout.NORTH); // -------- CENTER: Form using GridLayout -------- JPanel formPanel = new JPanel(new GridLayout(4, 2, 5, 5)); formPanel.setBorder(BorderFactory.createEmptyBorder(10, 10, 10, 10)); formPanel.add(new JLabel("Name:")); formPanel.add(new JTextField()); formPanel.add(new JLabel("Roll No:")); formPanel.add(new JTextField()); formPanel.add(new JLabel("Branch:")); formPanel.add(new JTextField()); formPanel.add(new JLabel("Semester:")); formPanel.add(new JTextField()); add(formPanel, BorderLayout.CENTER); // -------- SOUTH: Buttons using FlowLayout -------- JPanel buttonPanel = new JPanel(new FlowLayout(FlowLayout.CENTER, 10, 5)); buttonPanel.add(new JButton("Submit")); buttonPanel.add(new JButton("Reset")); buttonPanel.add(new JButton("Cancel")); add(buttonPanel, BorderLayout.SOUTH); setLocationRelativeTo(null); // center on screen setVisible(true); } public static void main(String[] args) { SwingUtilities.invokeLater(RegistrationForm::new); } } ``` **Step-by-step explanatory analysis:** 1. The outer `JFrame` uses `BorderLayout` so the three major sections (header, form, buttons) can independently scale. 2. The North label takes the full top width at its preferred height. 3. The Center uses a nested `JPanel` with `GridLayout(4, 2)` — creating 4 rows and 2 columns of equal cells, holding label-field pairs in alternation. 4. The South uses another nested `JPanel` with `FlowLayout.CENTER` to keep the buttons in a single row, centered. 5. `SwingUtilities.invokeLater()` is the thread-safe way to start a Swing UI on the Event Dispatch Thread (EDT). ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Layout | Scenario | Recommended Layout | Justification | |---|---|---| | A calculator's button grid | `GridLayout` | All cells are equal-sized; order is row-major. | | A chat window (messages + input) | `BorderLayout` | Top: header; Center: scrollable messages; South: input. | | A toolbar with action buttons | `FlowLayout` | Buttons can wrap to a new line on resize. | | A complex login form with mixed widths | `GridBagLayout` | Some fields span 2 columns; others are aligned. | | Tab-like view switcher | `CardLayout` | Only one screen visible at a time. | | A simple dialog with stacked labels | `BoxLayout` (Y_AXIS) | Vertical list with consistent spacing. | | A dashboard with side panels | `BorderLayout` + nested `GridLayout` | Outer + nested managers. | ## 3.5 Disabling Layout — The `null` Layout Pattern ```java JPanel p = new JPanel(); p.setLayout(null); JButton b = new JButton("Manual"); b.setBounds(50, 30, 120, 25); // x, y, width, height p.add(b); ``` **Logic Explanation:** With `null` layout, the manager does not interfere; the developer owns all coordinates. This is fragile but sometimes necessary for pixel-perfect custom widgets. KTU exams prefer automatic layout managers. > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** If you call `setBounds()` on a component whose parent still has a layout manager, the bounds will be **overwritten** at the next `validate()` call. The two are mutually exclusive. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 BorderLayout Region Map ```mermaid graph TB subgraph Frame[Container with BorderLayout] N[Region NORTH
Full Width, Preferred Height] S[Region SOUTH
Full Width, Preferred Height] W[Region WEST
Preferred Width] C[Region CENTER
All Remaining Space] E[Region EAST
Preferred Width] end N --- W W --- C C --- E E --- S style N fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0050b3 style S fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0050b3 style W fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724 style C fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404 style E fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724 style Frame fill:#ffffff,stroke:#000000 ``` **Reading the diagram:** NORTH and SOUTH span the **full width** of the container. WEST and EAST sit **between** them on left and right. CENTER occupies the **rectangle that remains** in the middle. ## 4.2 GridLayout Cell Topology (3×2 Example) ```mermaid graph TB subgraph Grid[Container with GridLayout 3 Rows 2 Cols] A[Cell 0,0
Component 1] B[Cell 0,1
Component 2] C[Cell 1,0
Component 3] D[Cell 1,1
Component 4] E[Cell 2,0
Component 5] F[Cell 2,1
Component 6] end A --> B C --> D E --> F style A fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24 style B fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24 style C fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24 style D fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24 style E fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24 style F fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24 style Grid fill:#ffffff,stroke:#000000 ``` ## 4.3 FlowLayout Wrapping Flow ```mermaid graph LR A[Add Button A] --> B[Add Button B] B --> C[Add Button C] C --> D{Width overflow?} D -->|No| E[Continue Row 1] D -->|Yes| F[Wrap to Row 2] E --> G[Add Button D] G --> D F --> H[Continue Row 2] ``` **Reading the diagram:** Each component is checked against the remaining horizontal space. If adding it would overflow, the cursor moves to a new line and the component is placed there. ## 4.4 Nested Layout Architecture (Block Diagram) ```mermaid graph TB subgraph JFrame [JFrame with BorderLayout] N[Header JLabel] subgraph CenterPanel [Center JPanel with GridLayout 4x2] L1[Label 1] --> F1[Field 1] L2[Label 2] --> F2[Field 2] L3[Label 3] --> F3[Field 3] L4[Label 4] --> F4[Field 4] end subgraph SouthPanel [South JPanel with FlowLayout] B1[Submit] B2[Reset] B3[Cancel] end end N --- CenterPanel CenterPanel --- SouthPanel style JFrame fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#383d41 style CenterPanel fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#0c5460 style SouthPanel fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724 style N fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0050b3 ``` **Reading the diagram:** The outermost `JFrame` is governed by `BorderLayout`, with a `JLabel` in NORTH. The CENTER holds a `JPanel` running its own `GridLayout` (which itself has nested labels/fields). The SOUTH holds a `JPanel` with its own `FlowLayout` for buttons. This is the canonical KTU nested-layout pattern. ## 4.5 Class Hierarchy of Layout Interfaces ```mermaid graph TB A[java.awt.LayoutManager
Interface] B[java.awt.LayoutManager2
Extends LayoutManager] C1[BorderLayout] C2[GridLayout] C3[FlowLayout] C4[CardLayout] C5[GridBagLayout] C6[BoxLayout] C7[GroupLayout] C8[SpringLayout] A --> B B --> C1 B --> C5 B --> C6 B --> C7 B --> C8 A --> C2 A --> C3 A --> C4 ``` **Reading the diagram:** The `LayoutManager2` interface extends `LayoutManager` to add constraint-based additions. Implementations like `BorderLayout`, `GridBagLayout`, `BoxLayout`, `GroupLayout`, and `SpringLayout` use constraints, while `GridLayout`, `FlowLayout`, and `CardLayout` stick to positional/sequential logic. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **Q: Define a Layout Manager. List any three standard layout managers provided by Java AWT.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **Model Answer:** A **Layout Manager** is an object that implements the `java.awt.LayoutManager` interface and is responsible for arranging the components inside a `Container` according to a specific algorithm. It automatically positions and resizes child components whenever the container is resized, validated, or when components are added or removed. The three standard layout managers are: 1. **FlowLayout** — Places components in a row, wrapping to the next line as needed. 2. **BorderLayout** — Divides the container into five regions: North, South, East, West, and Center. 3. **GridLayout** — Arranges components in a grid of equal-sized rectangular cells. *(Other acceptable answers: CardLayout, BoxLayout, GridBagLayout, GroupLayout.)* ### Question 2 **Q: Differentiate between `setLayout(null)` and using a Layout Manager. State one scenario where each is preferred.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Layout Manager | `setLayout(null)` | |---|---|---| | Positioning | Automatic, rule-based | Manual, pixel-based | | Resize behavior | Components re-arrange | Components stay at fixed coordinates | | Portability | High (platform-independent) | Low (brittle on resize) | | Code complexity | Less (declarative) | More (must set bounds for each) | **Scenario for Layout Manager:** Any production GUI that must adapt to different screen resolutions, fonts, or localizations — e.g., a multi-language login form. **Scenario for `setLayout(null)`:** A custom drawn game board, a pixel-perfect chart, or a fixed-size splash screen where components must stay at exact coordinates. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the `BorderLayout` manager in detail. With a neat diagram, describe how the five regions are arranged. Write a Java Swing code snippet to demonstrate placing a label in the NORTH, a text area in the CENTER, and three buttons in the SOUTH. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** The **`BorderLayout`** manager is the default layout for a `JFrame`'s content pane (and for `JDialog`, `JApplet`). It divides the container into **five geographical regions**: `NORTH`, `SOUTH`, `EAST`, `WEST`, and `CENTER`. Each region can contain at most one component (or one nested panel). The North and South regions span the **full width** of the container, while East and West take their **preferred width** in the middle vertical band. The CENTER region receives **all remaining space**, making it ideal for the main content area. **Diagram:** ``` +--------------------------------------+ | NORTH | +------+--------------------+----------+ | | | | | WEST | CENTER | EAST | | | | | +------+--------------------+----------+ | SOUTH | +--------------------------------------+ ``` **Code Snippet:** ```java import javax.swing.*; import java.awt.*; public class BorderLayoutDemo extends JFrame { public BorderLayoutDemo() { setTitle("BorderLayout Demo"); setSize(400, 250); setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE); setLayout(new BorderLayout(5, 5)); // 5px gaps // NORTH JLabel header = new JLabel("Feedback Form", SwingConstants.CENTER); add(header, BorderLayout.NORTH); // CENTER JTextArea feedback = new JTextArea("Type your feedback here..."); add(new JScrollPane(feedback), BorderLayout.CENTER); // SOUTH JPanel southPanel = new JPanel(new FlowLayout()); southPanel.add(new JButton("Submit")); southPanel.add(new JButton("Clear")); southPanel.add(new JButton("Close")); add(southPanel, BorderLayout.SOUTH); setVisible(true); } public static void main(String[] args) { SwingUtilities.invokeLater(BorderLayoutDemo::new); } } ``` **Explanation of Code Logic:** - The frame uses `BorderLayout(5,5)` to leave 5px breathing room. - The `JLabel` is added to NORTH, and `SwingConstants.CENTER` keeps the text horizontally centered within that strip. - The `JTextArea` is wrapped in a `JScrollPane` (a container with its own layout logic) before being placed in CENTER — this is a classic nested-layout trick. - The buttons in SOUTH are placed inside a nested `JPanel` with `FlowLayout` because `BorderLayout` allows only **one** component per region; the panel itself acts as that single component, internally managing the three buttons. --- **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the `GridLayout` manager. Write a Java program that creates a calculator-style 4×4 grid of buttons (digits 0–9, +, −, *, /, =, C). How does `GridLayout` handle gaps, and what happens if you add 17 components to a 4×4 grid? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** The **`GridLayout`** manager arranges components in a rectangular grid of **equal-sized cells**. The container is divided into `rows × cols` cells, and components are placed in **row-major order** (left-to-right, then top-to-bottom). All cells have identical width and height. **Constructor:** `GridLayout(int rows, int cols, int hgap, int vgap)` where `hgap` is the horizontal pixel gap and `vgap` is the vertical pixel gap between cells. **Calculator Program:** ```java import javax.swing.*; import java.awt.*; public class CalculatorGrid extends JFrame { public CalculatorGrid() { setTitle("Calculator"); setSize(300, 400); setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE); setLayout(new BorderLayout()); // Display at top JTextField display = new JTextField(); display.setEditable(false); add(display, BorderLayout.NORTH); // 4x5 grid (4 rows, 5 columns) to fit 17 buttons comfortably JPanel buttonPanel = new JPanel(new GridLayout(4, 5, 5, 5)); String[] labels = { "7", "8", "9", "/", "C", "4", "5", "6", "*", "√", "1", "2", "3", "-", "%", "0", ".", "=", "+", "?" }; for (String label : labels) { buttonPanel.add(new JButton(label)); } add(buttonPanel, BorderLayout.CENTER); setVisible(true); } public static void main(String[] args) { SwingUtilities.invokeLater(CalculatorGrid::new); } } ``` **Handling Gaps:** - `hgap` and `vgap` are passed to the constructor and represent **inter-cell** spacing in pixels. They default to 0. - The total gap space is subtracted from the available container area, and the remainder is divided equally among the cells. So all cells remain equal-sized regardless of gaps. **What if you add 17 components to a 4×4 (16-cell) grid?** - The grid's structural capacity is `4 × 4 = 16` cells. - Adding a 17th component causes the layout to **redistribute**: in some JVM implementations, the grid will add an extra row (so it becomes 5×4), keeping all cells equal. - In other cases, the 17th component may simply not be rendered or will overflow into a new row automatically. Best practice is to ensure the number of components matches `rows × cols`. --- ### Question B (Alternative Choice) **(a) [7 Marks]** What is the role of the `LayoutManager` interface in Java? Discuss the difference between `LayoutManager` and `LayoutManager2`. List the methods of `LayoutManager2` and explain any two with examples. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** The **`LayoutManager`** interface defines the contract for objects that control the size and position of components inside a `Container`. When a container's `validate()` (or Swing's `revalidate()`) is invoked, the layout manager is responsible for querying each child's preferred/minimum/maximum size and computing its `bounds`. **Difference between `LayoutManager` and `LayoutManager2`:** | Aspect | `LayoutManager` | `LayoutManager2` | |---|---|---| | Introduced in | JDK 1.0 | JDK 1.1 | | Component addition | By string name (legacy) | By constraints object | | Maximum size | Not supported | Supports `maximumLayoutSize()` | | Implementors | `FlowLayout`, `GridLayout`, `CardLayout` | `BorderLayout`, `GridBagLayout`, `BoxLayout`, `GroupLayout` | **Methods of `LayoutManager2`:** 1. `void addLayoutComponent(Component comp, Object constraints)` — Adds a component with associated constraints (e.g., a `GridBagConstraints` object or a `BorderLayout` region string). 2. `float getLayoutAlignmentX(Container target)` — Returns the X-axis alignment (0.0 to 1.0). 3. `float getLayoutAlignmentY(Container target)` — Returns the Y-axis alignment. 4. `Dimension maximumLayoutSize(Container target)` — Returns the maximum size the layout can produce. 5. `void invalidateLayout(Container target)` — Discards cached layout information. **Example of `addLayoutComponent` with constraints:** ```java JPanel p = new JPanel(new GridBagLayout()); GridBagConstraints gbc = new GridBagConstraints(); gbc.gridx = 0; gbc.gridy = 0; gbc.fill = GridBagConstraints.HORIZONTAL; p.add(new JButton("One"), gbc); // constraints-based add ``` **Example of `maximumLayoutSize`:** `BoxLayout` uses it to know how far a component can stretch before the container refuses to grow. This is critical in `JScrollPane`-wrapped layouts. --- **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the `GridBagLayout` manager. List any four properties of `GridBagConstraints` and explain how they affect the layout. Write a small Java program that uses `GridBagLayout` to arrange a label, a text field, and a submit button in a row. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** The **`GridBagLayout`** is the most flexible and complex layout manager in Java AWT/Swing. It places components in a **virtual grid of rows and columns**, but unlike `GridLayout`, each component can: - Span **multiple cells** (rows or columns). - Have **custom size** relative to siblings. - Be **anchored** within its cell. - Be **insetted** with custom padding. - Be **weighted** for extra-space distribution. Each component added with `GridBagLayout` is paired with a `GridBagConstraints` object that holds its layout rules. **Four Important Properties of `GridBagConstraints`:** | Property | Type | Effect | |---|---|---| | `gridx, gridy` | `int` | The cell coordinates where the component begins (0-indexed; `GridBagConstraints.RELATIVE` for next available). | | `gridwidth, gridheight` | `int` | Number of cells the component spans. `REMAINDER` = to the end of the row/column. | | `weightx, weighty` | `double` | Distribution of extra space in [0.0, 1.0]. Higher value = more space. | | `fill` | `int` | How component fills its display area: `NONE`, `HORIZONTAL`, `VERTICAL`, `BOTH`. | | `anchor` | `int` | Where to place the component if display area is larger: `CENTER`, `NORTHWEST`, etc. | | `insets` | `Insets` | External padding in pixels (top, left, bottom, right). | | `ipadx, ipady` | `int` | Internal padding added to the component's preferred size. | **Program: Label + TextField + Button in a Row** ```java import javax.swing.*; import java.awt.*; public class GridBagDemo extends JFrame { public GridBagDemo() { setTitle("GridBagLayout Demo"); setSize(450, 120); setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE); JPanel panel = new JPanel(new GridBagLayout()); GridBagConstraints gbc = new GridBagConstraints(); gbc.insets = new Insets(5, 5, 5, 5); // 5px padding all around // Label gbc.gridx = 0; gbc.gridy = 0; gbc.anchor = GridBagConstraints.WEST; panel.add(new JLabel("Enter Name:"), gbc); // Text field - expands horizontally gbc.gridx = 1; gbc.gridy = 0; gbc.weightx = 1.0; // takes all extra horizontal space gbc.fill = GridBagConstraints.HORIZONTAL; panel.add(new JTextField(15), gbc); // Submit button gbc.gridx = 2; gbc.gridy = 0; gbc.weightx = 0.0; // button stays at its preferred size gbc.fill = GridBagConstraints.NONE; panel.add(new JButton("Submit"), gbc); add(panel); setVisible(true); } public static void main(String[] args) { SwingUtilities.invokeLater(GridBagDemo::new); } } ``` **Explanation:** - All three components are in row 0 (`gridy = 0`). - The `JTextField` has `weightx = 1.0` and `fill = HORIZONTAL`, so when the window is resized, the text field stretches while the label and button remain at their preferred widths. - The `insets` property provides breathing room (5px) around each component. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Forgetting to set `weightx` or `weighty` — without it, components cluster in the center and don't expand on resize, leading to "ugly dead space" that examiners deduct marks for. > 2. **Common Mistake #2:** Reusing the same `GridBagConstraints` object across multiple `add()` calls without resetting fields. Java *passes by reference*, so old values leak into new components. > 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Confusing `ipadx` (internal padding, adds to component size) with `insets` (external padding, adds gap between cells). Examiners specifically look for the correct usage. > 4. **Common Mistake #4:** Writing `add(component)` without a constraints object when using `GridBagLayout` — this throws an `IllegalArgumentException` at runtime. > 5. **Common Mistake #5:** Using `setBounds()` together with a layout manager on the same parent. The bounds are silently overwritten on the next validation. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Swing Layout Managers** - **Default Layouts (must memorize):** `JFrame` content pane → `BorderLayout`; `JPanel` → `FlowLayout`. - **`BorderLayout` regions:** 5 total — `NORTH`, `SOUTH`, `EAST`, `WEST`, `CENTER`. Only **one component per region**; extra components need a nested panel. - **`BorderLayout` rule:** `CENTER` gets all leftover space; the rest take preferred size. - **`FlowLayout` rule:** Row-major flow with automatic line wrap. Default alignment is `CENTER`. Default gap is 5px. - **`GridLayout` rule:** All cells are **equal-sized**. Components are placed in **row-major** order. Constructor: `GridLayout(rows, cols, hgap, vgap)`. - **`GridLayout` capacity:** Number of components ideally equals `rows × cols`. - **`BoxLayout` rule:** Single-line, vertical or horizontal, via `BoxLayout(container, axis)`. Uses `Box` helper for glue and rigid areas. - **`CardLayout` rule:** Stack of components; only one visible at a time; navigated by `first()`, `next()`, `previous()`, `last()`, `show()`. - **`GridBagLayout` rule:** Most flexible but most complex. Requires `GridBagConstraints`. `weightx`/`weighty` control extra-space distribution. - **`setLayout(null)` rule:** Disables the manager; developer must use `setBounds(x, y, width, height)` for every component. - **Thread safety:** Always start Swing GUIs inside `SwingUtilities.invokeLater(Runnable)`. - **Layout Interface Hierarchy:** `LayoutManager` (JDK 1.0) ← `LayoutManager2` (JDK 1.1, adds constraints-based `addLayoutComponent`). - **Key Constraint Constants:** `GridBagConstraints.HORIZONTAL`, `VERTICAL`, `BOTH`, `NONE` (for `fill`); `CENTER`, `NORTHWEST` etc. (for `anchor`); `RELATIVE`, `REMAINDER` (for `gridx/gridwidth`). - **Re-validation:** Call `revalidate()` after adding/removing components dynamically; call `repaint()` to refresh the visual. - **Best Practice (KTU expects):** Always use a layout manager unless absolutely required otherwise. Nest `JPanel`s with their own layout to achieve complex arrangements. - **Memory Trick:** Border → "5 directions"; Flow → "water flowing in a pipe"; Grid → "Excel cells"; GridBag → "Tetris with weights"; Card → "playing cards stacked"; Box → "boxes in a line."

topics.title: Exploring Swings - JFrame
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT414 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Exploring Swings - JFrame

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **JFrame** is a top-level container class in the `javax.swing` package that extends `java.awt.Frame`. It is the foundational window upon which all **Swing-based Graphical User Interface (GUI)** applications are built. A `JFrame` provides the standard window decorations (title bar, minimize/maximize/close buttons) and serves as the root container for holding other Swing components such as `JButton`, `JLabel`, `JTextField`, etc.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, JFrame is the entry point for learning **event-driven programming** in Java. It is a **heavyweight container** (relies on the underlying OS windowing system) but uses **lightweight** Swing components for its children.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Window" Metaphor

Imagine you are constructing a physical house:
- The **JFrame** is the **outer structure of the house** — the walls, roof, and front door.
- The **title bar** is the **nameplate** above the door.
- The **content pane** is the **interior empty space** where you place your furniture.
- The **Swing components** (buttons, text fields) are the **furniture** — they sit inside the house.
- The **event listeners** are the **doorbell, light switches, and taps** — they react to user actions.

Just as a house cannot exist without walls, a Java GUI application cannot exist without a `JFrame`.

### 1.3 JFrame in the Swing Class Hierarchy

```
java.lang.Object
   └── java.awt.Component
         └── java.awt.Container
               └── java.awt.Window
                     └── java.awt.Frame
                           └── javax.swing.JFrame
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Point for KTU Exams:** Since `JFrame` extends `java.awt.Frame`, it is a **heavyweight component**. All components added directly to a `JFrame` are technically added to its **Content Pane** (a `JPanel` instance), not to the frame itself.

### 1.4 Common JFrame Constructors

| Constructor | Description |
|-------------|-------------|
| `JFrame()` | Creates a new, initially invisible frame with no title. |
| `JFrame(String title)` | Creates a new frame with the specified title. |

> [!NOTE]
> Calling `JFrame()` alone does **NOT** display the window. You must explicitly call `setVisible(true)`.

### 1.5 Essential JFrame Methods (High-Yield for KTU)

| Method | Purpose |
|--------|---------|
| `setTitle(String s)` | Sets the title bar text. |
| `setSize(int w, int h)` | Sets the frame's dimensions in **pixels**. |
| `setLocation(int x, int y)` | Positions frame at (x, y) on screen. |
| `setBounds(int x, int y, int w, int h)` | Combines location and size. |
| `setVisible(boolean b)` | Shows (`true`) or hides (`false`) the frame. |
| `setDefaultCloseOperation(int op)` | Defines behavior on close. |
| `setLayout(LayoutManager mgr)` | Sets the layout manager. |
| `add(Component c)` | Adds a component to the content pane. |
| `setResizable(boolean b)` | Controls if the user can resize the frame. |
| `getContentPane()` | Returns the content pane object. |
| `setIconImage(Image img)` | Sets the frame's icon. |

### 1.6 `setDefaultCloseOperation()` Constants

This is a **favourite KTU question**. The valid integer constants are:

| Constant | Behavior on Close |
|----------|------------------|
| `WindowConstants.DO_NOTHING_ON_CLOSE` (0) | Ignore the close click. |
| `WindowConstants.HIDE_ON_CLOSE` (1) — **default** | Hide the frame. |
| `WindowConstants.DISPOSE_ON_CLOSE` (2) | Close and free resources. |
| `WindowConstants.EXIT_ON_CLOSE` (3) | Terminate the entire **JVM** using `System.exit(0)`. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> `EXIT_ON_CLOSE` is **only defined in `JFrame`** (not in `JDialog` or `Window`). It is typically used in standalone desktop applications.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 Internal Architecture of a JFrame (Root Pane Concept)

A `JFrame` is more than a simple window — it is composed of several layered panes (introduced in Java 1.2 as part of Swing's pluggable look-and-feel architecture):

| Pane (Layer) | Role | Typical Use |
|--------------|------|-------------|
| **Glass Pane** | Topmost invisible layer. | Captures mouse events over the entire frame; used for custom painting/effects. |
| **Layered Pane** | Manages z-ordering of components. | Contains the content pane and optional `JMenuBar`. |
| **Content Pane** | The main "drawing area" where components are placed. | Holds `JButton`, `JLabel`, `JPanel`, etc. |
| **Menu Bar (optional)** | Holds `JMenuBar` at the top. | Contains File, Edit, Help menus, etc. |

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic for Creating a Basic JFrame Application

1. **Import** the required packages (`javax.swing.*`, `java.awt.*`).
2. **Declare** a class that extends `JFrame` (or instantiate a `JFrame` object).
3. **Set** frame properties: `setTitle()`, `setSize()`, `setLocation()`, `setDefaultCloseOperation()`.
4. **Create** Swing components (e.g., `JLabel`, `JButton`).
5. **Add** components to the content pane using `add()`.
6. **Register** event listeners (if needed) on the components.
7. **Call** `setVisible(true)` to display the frame.

### 2.3 Why Use JFrame Instead of AWT Frame?

| Feature | `java.awt.Frame` | `javax.swing.JFrame` |
|---------|------------------|----------------------|
| Package | AWT | Swing |
| Component Type | Heavyweight | Heavyweight (container) + Lightweight children |
| Look and Feel | OS-dependent | Pluggable (Metal, Nimbus, System) |
| Pluggable UI | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| MVC Architecture | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| Lightweight components | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (all components starting with `J`) |
| Default Close Operation | None | Provided |

### 2.4 The "Why" Behind Each JFrame Design Decision

- **Why extend `Frame`?** — `JFrame` reuses AWT's windowing system (OS-native) while introducing Swing's lightweight painting model for children.
- **Why use a Content Pane?** — To allow Swing components (lightweight) to be rendered **on top of** the heavy frame, enabling features like double-buffering and custom look-and-feel.
- **Why `setVisible(true)` at the END?** — Adding components before the frame is shown prevents visual flicker (a common interview/KTU question).

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Syntax / Value |
|---------|---------------|
| Package | `javax.swing.JFrame` |
| Inheritance | `JFrame → java.awt.Frame → Window → Container → Component → Object` |
| Default Close Operation | `HIDE_ON_CLOSE` (value = 1) |
| Common Close Operations | `EXIT_ON_CLOSE` (3), `DISPOSE_ON_CLOSE` (2) |
| `setSize(w, h)` unit | **Pixels** (px) |
| Standard `setLocation` origin | Top-left corner of screen at (0, 0) |
| Default Look and Feel (Java 8+) | **Nimbus** (or Metal on older JDKs) |
| Recommended Java version | **JDK 8 or later** (KTU lab standard) |
| `setLayout(null)` | Enables **absolute positioning** (rarely recommended). |
| `pack()` | Auto-sizes frame to fit preferred sizes of its components. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** JFrame-based GUI development is foundational for **desktop applications** such as billing systems, attendance trackers, banking software, IDEs, and embedded HMI tools. In IoT/Kerala industrial projects, Swing-based dashboards remain in legacy SCADA systems.

### 2.6 Common Pitfalls Students Face in KTU Exams

- ❌ Forgetting to call `setVisible(true)` — window never appears.
- ❌ Forgetting to set `setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE)` — JVM keeps running after closing window.
- ❌ Using `setLayout(null)` without setting component bounds — components are invisible (0×0 size).
- ❌ Adding a `JMenuBar` directly via `add()` instead of `setJMenuBar()`.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Code Analysis & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Line-by-Line Analysis of a Standard JFrame Program

```java
// Program 1: A minimal JFrame Window
import javax.swing.JFrame;          // Step 1: Import JFrame class

public class SimpleFrame {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        JFrame f = new JFrame("My First Window"); // Step 2: Create frame with title
        
        f.setSize(400, 300);                        // Step 3: 400px wide, 300px tall
        f.setLocation(100, 100);                    // Step 4: 100px from top-left of screen
        f.setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE); // Step 5: End JVM on close
        f.setVisible(true);                         // Step 6: Display the frame
    }
}
```

#### Step-by-Step Explanation

| Line | Action | Why It Matters |
|------|--------|----------------|
| `import javax.swing.JFrame;` | Brings the JFrame class into scope. | Without this, compilation fails. |
| `JFrame f = new JFrame("My First Window");` | Allocates a new frame in memory. | The frame is **not visible** yet. |
| `f.setSize(400, 300);` | Sets the window's dimensions in **pixels**. | Without this, size defaults to **0×0** (invisible). |
| `f.setLocation(100, 100);` | Positions the top-left corner at (100, 100). | If omitted, OS decides placement. |
| `f.setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);` | Ensures the **entire application** terminates on close. | Default is `HIDE_ON_CLOSE` — JVM keeps running. |
| `f.setVisible(true);` | Displays the frame on screen. | Must be the **last** call to prevent flicker. |

### 3.2 Program 2 — Adding Components to a JFrame

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;

public class FrameWithComponents extends JFrame {
    JLabel lbl;
    JTextField tf;
    JButton btn;
    
    FrameWithComponents() {
        setTitle("Login Window");
        setSize(350, 200);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());                    // Arrange components left-to-right
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        
        lbl = new JLabel("Username:");                  // Step A: Create label
        tf  = new JTextField(15);                       // Step B: Create text field (15 cols)
        btn = new JButton("Submit");                    // Step C: Create button
        
        add(lbl); add(tf); add(btn);                    // Step D: Add to content pane
        
        setVisible(true);
    }
    
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new FrameWithComponents();
    }
}
```

#### Analytical Matrix — Code Flow

| Step | Constructor / Method | Returns | Visual Effect |
|------|----------------------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | `JLabel("Username:")` | `JLabel` object | Static text on frame. |
| 2 | `JTextField(15)` | `JTextField` (15 columns wide) | Editable single-line input. |
| 3 | `JButton("Submit")` | `JButton` with text "Submit" | Clickable button. |
| 4 | `add(component)` | `Component` (chainable) | Appends to content pane. |
| 5 | `setLayout(new FlowLayout())` | void | Sets horizontal flow layout. |
| 6 | `setVisible(true)` | void | Displays the entire window. |

### 3.3 Program 3 — JFrame with Event Handling (ActionListener)

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class EventFrame extends JFrame implements ActionListener {
    JTextField tf;
    JButton btn;
    JLabel result;
    
    EventFrame() {
        setTitle("Action Listener Demo");
        setSize(300, 150);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());
        setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        
        tf     = new JTextField(10);
        btn    = new JButton("Greet");
        result = new JLabel(" ");
        
        btn.addActionListener(this);    // Register this frame as the listener
        
        add(tf); add(btn); add(result);
        setVisible(true);
    }
    
    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        String name = tf.getText();
        result.setText("Hello, " + name + "!");
    }
    
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new EventFrame();
    }
}
```

#### Detailed Logic Walkthrough

1. The class `EventFrame` **implements** `ActionListener` — it must therefore provide the method `actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)`.
2. The button `btn` is **registered** as a listener-source with `addActionListener(this)`. When clicked, the JVM calls `actionPerformed` on the registered object.
3. Inside `actionPerformed`, the text inside `tf` is retrieved with `getText()` and used to update `result` via `setText()`.
4. The entire chain demonstrates the **Event Delegation Model** — the source (`btn`) delegates event handling to a listener (`this`).

### 3.4 Program 4 — Using `pack()` and `setLocationRelativeTo(null)`

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;

public class PackedFrame extends JFrame {
    PackedFrame() {
        setTitle("Packed Frame");
        setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setLayout(new BorderLayout());
        add(new JButton("North"),  BorderLayout.NORTH);
        add(new JButton("Center"), BorderLayout.CENTER);
        pack();                                       // Auto-size to fit components
        setLocationRelativeTo(null);                  // Center the window on screen
        setVisible(true);
    }
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new PackedFrame();
    }
}
```

| Method | Mathematical Equivalent | Purpose |
|--------|------------------------|---------|
| `pack()` | $\text{width} = \max(\text{pref widths}); \text{height} = \max(\text{pref heights})$ | Auto-sizes the frame to its preferred component size. |
| `setLocationRelativeTo(null)` | $x = \frac{W_{screen} - W_{frame}}{2}, \quad y = \frac{H_{screen} - H_{frame}}{2}$ | Centers the frame on the screen. |

> [!NOTE]
> When `setLocationRelativeTo(null)` is called with `null`, it positions the frame **relative to the screen** (centered). When called with another component, it centers relative to that component.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — JFrame Internal Layered Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    OS[Operating System Window Native Frame]
    JF[JFrame Heavyweight Window]
    RP[Root Pane]
    GP[Glass Pane Topmost Invisible]
    LP[Layered Pane Z-order Manager]
    MB[JMenuBar Optional]
    CP[Content Pane Default Container]
    C1[JButton]
    C2[JLabel]
    C3[JTextField]
    C4[JPanel]

    OS --> JF
    JF --> RP
    RP --> GP
    RP --> LP
    LP --> MB
    LP --> CP
    CP --> C1
    CP --> C2
    CP --> C3
    CP --> C4
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — JFrame Class Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[java.lang.Object]
    B[java.awt.Component]
    C[java.awt.Container]
    D[java.awt.Window]
    E[java.awt.Frame]
    F[javax.swing.JFrame]

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Event Delegation Model in JFrame

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User
    participant B as JButton Source
    participant L as ActionListener this
    participant J as JVM Event Dispatch Thread

    U->>B: Click on Submit Button
    B->>J: Generate ActionEvent
    J->>L: invoke actionPerformed event
    L->>L: Read text from JTextField
    L->>B: Update JLabel via setText
    Note over B,L: Event Delegation Model
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Typical JFrame App

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Input_Layer
        TF1[JTextField Username]
        TF2[JPasswordField]
    end
    subgraph Control_Layer
        BTN1[JButton Login]
        BTN2[JButton Cancel]
    end
    subgraph Output_Layer
        LBL[JLabel Status Message]
    end
    subgraph Container
        JF[JFrame Content Pane BorderLayout]
    end
    TF1 --> JF
    TF2 --> JF
    BTN1 --> JF
    BTN2 --> JF
    LBL --> JF
    BTN1 -- ActionEvent --> TF1
    BTN1 -- ActionEvent --> TF2
    BTN1 -- ActionEvent --> LBL
```

### 4.5 Mermaid Diagram — JFrame Lifecycle State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Created : new JFrame title
    Created --> Configured : setSize, setLocation, setLayout
    Configured --> Populated : add JButton, JLabel, etc.
    Populated --> Visible : setVisible true
    Visible --> Hidden : setVisible false or HIDE_ON_CLOSE
    Hidden --> Visible : setVisible true
    Visible --> Disposed : DISPOSE_ON_CLOSE or dispose
    Visible --> Terminated : EXIT_ON_CLOSE
    Disposed --> [*]
    Terminated --> [*]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the role of `JFrame` in Java Swing. List any two constructors and two methods of `JFrame`.**

**Model Answer:**

`JFrame` is a top-level container in the `javax.swing` package used to create the main window of a Swing-based GUI application. It extends `java.awt.Frame` and provides a **content pane** for holding lightweight Swing components. JFrame supports the pluggable look-and-feel architecture and standard window decorations like minimize, maximize, and close.

**Two Constructors:**
1. `JFrame()` — Creates a frame with no title.
2. `JFrame(String title)` — Creates a frame with the given title.

**Two Methods:**
1. `setSize(int width, int height)` — Defines the frame's dimensions in pixels.
2. `setDefaultCloseOperation(int operation)` — Specifies the action when the user closes the window (e.g., `EXIT_ON_CLOSE`).

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**What is the difference between `DISPOSE_ON_CLOSE` and `EXIT_ON_CLOSE` in JFrame?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | `DISPOSE_ON_CLOSE` | `EXIT_ON_CLOSE` |
|--------|-------------------|-----------------|
| Action | Closes the current window and **frees its resources**. | Closes the window and **terminates the entire JVM** via `System.exit(0)`. |
| JVM Continues? | **Yes** — other windows/threads may keep running. | **No** — application ends. |
| Use Case | Multi-window applications (e.g., MDI apps). | Single-window standalone applications. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Many students write "both close the application" — losing marks. `DISPOSE_ON_CLOSE` does **not** end the JVM by default.

---

### 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 4 | 14 Marks]`

**(a)** Explain the internal layered architecture of a `JFrame` with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of the **Glass Pane**, **Layered Pane**, and **Content Pane**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java Swing program to create a `JFrame` titled *"Student Registration"* that contains:
- A `JLabel` "Name:"
- A `JTextField` for name input
- A `JButton` "Register"
- On clicking the button, display "Registered: <name>" in another `JLabel`.

Use an `ActionListener`. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

The internal architecture of a `JFrame` is managed through a `JRootPane`, which contains the following layered panes:

1. **Glass Pane** — The topmost layer. By default, it is invisible and transparent, but it can intercept mouse events for the entire frame. It is commonly used for custom painting or tooltips that float over all components.

2. **Layered Pane** — Manages the z-ordering (depth) of components. It contains the `Content Pane` and the optional `JMenuBar`. Components in higher layers appear above those in lower layers.

3. **Content Pane** — The default container where developers add their components (`JButton`, `JLabel`, etc.). It is an instance of `JPanel` and uses the layout manager set on the `JFrame`.

4. **Optional JMenuBar** — Placed at the top of the frame (between the title bar and the content pane).

**Diagram:**

```
┌─────────────────────────────────┐
│         Title Bar                │
├─────────────────────────────────┤
│       Optional JMenuBar         │  ← Layered Pane
├─────────────────────────────────┤
│                                  │
│   Content Pane (JPanel)         │
│   - JLabel, JButton, JTextField │
│                                  │
├─────────────────────────────────┤
│      Glass Pane (overlay)        │
└─────────────────────────────────┘
```

When `add(component)` is called on a `JFrame`, it is internally forwarded to `getContentPane().add(component)`.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class StudentRegistration extends JFrame implements ActionListener {
    JLabel lblName, lblOutput;
    JTextField tfName;
    JButton btnRegister;
    
    StudentRegistration() {
        setTitle("Student Registration");
        setSize(350, 150);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());
        setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        
        lblName     = new JLabel("Name:");
        tfName      = new JTextField(15);
        btnRegister = new JButton("Register");
        lblOutput   = new JLabel(" ");
        
        btnRegister.addActionListener(this);
        
        add(lblName);
        add(tfName);
        add(btnRegister);
        add(lblOutput);
        
        setVisible(true);
    }
    
    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        String name = tfName.getText();
        lblOutput.setText("Registered: " + name);
    }
    
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new StudentRegistration();
    }
}
```

**Output (after clicking "Register" with name "Anu"):**

```
Registered: Anu
```

---

#### **Question B (Alternative)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024 | Module 4 | 14 Marks]`

**(a)** Explain the significance of `setDefaultCloseOperation()` in `JFrame`. List all four valid constants with their integer values and behaviors. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program to create a `JFrame` that uses a `BorderLayout` with:
- A `JButton` in the **NORTH** region titled "Header"
- A `JLabel` in the **CENTER** region displaying "Welcome to KTU"
- A `JButton` in the **SOUTH** region titled "Exit" (closes the application on click).

Use the `pack()` and `setLocationRelativeTo(null)` methods. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

The `setDefaultCloseOperation(int operation)` method defines what happens when the user clicks the **close (×)** button on the window. The four valid integer constants are:

| Constant | Value | Behavior |
|----------|-------|----------|
| `WindowConstants.DO_NOTHING_ON_CLOSE` | 0 | The close action is **ignored**. The frame remains open. |
| `WindowConstants.HIDE_ON_CLOSE` (default) | 1 | The frame is **hidden**, but the JVM **continues running**. |
| `WindowConstants.DISPOSE_ON_CLOSE` | 2 | The frame's resources are **released**. JVM continues if other windows exist. |
| `WindowConstants.EXIT_ON_CLOSE` | 3 | The entire application **terminates** via `System.exit(0)`. |

If no operation is set explicitly, the default is `HIDE_ON_CLOSE`, which often causes confusion as the application appears to keep running in the background.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class BorderLayoutDemo extends JFrame implements ActionListener {
    JButton btnHeader, btnExit;
    JLabel lblCenter;
    
    BorderLayoutDemo() {
        setTitle("BorderLayout Demo");
        setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setLayout(new BorderLayout(10, 10));  // 10px horizontal & vertical gaps
        
        btnHeader = new JButton("Header");
        lblCenter = new JLabel("Welcome to KTU", SwingConstants.CENTER);
        btnExit   = new JButton("Exit");
        
        btnExit.addActionListener(this);
        
        add(btnHeader, BorderLayout.NORTH);
        add(lblCenter, BorderLayout.CENTER);
        add(btnExit,   BorderLayout.SOUTH);
        
        pack();                              // Auto-size to fit components
        setLocationRelativeTo(null);         // Center on screen
        setVisible(true);
    }
    
    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        System.exit(0);
    }
    
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new BorderLayoutDemo();
    }
}
```

**Explanation of `pack()` and `setLocationRelativeTo(null)`:**

- `pack()` calculates the frame's size based on each component's **preferred size**, removing the need to call `setSize()`.
- `setLocationRelativeTo(null)` centers the frame on the screen by computing $(x, y) = \left(\frac{W_{screen} - W_{frame}}{2}, \frac{H_{screen} - H_{frame}}{2}\right)$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting `setVisible(true)`** — Frame is created but never shown. (–2 marks)
> 2. **Not setting `setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE)`** — JVM continues after closing. (–1 mark)
> 3. **Adding components AFTER `setVisible(true)`** — Causes flicker or invisible components. (–1 mark)
> 4. **Confusing `add()` for JFrame vs. JPanel** — JFrame delegates to content pane automatically, but the concept must be mentioned. (–1 mark)
> 5. **Using `BorderLayout` without specifying region** — Component defaults to `CENTER`, replacing other components. (–2 marks)
> 6. **Mixing up heavyweight AWT (`Button`) and lightweight Swing (`JButton`)** — Must always use `J` prefix in Swing. (–1 mark)

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ `JFrame` is a **top-level container** in `javax.swing`, extending `java.awt.Frame`.
- ✅ Always call `setVisible(true)` **last** to avoid rendering flicker.
- ✅ `setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE)` is **mandatory** for standalone apps.
- ✅ Components are added to the **content pane** (a `JPanel`), not directly to the frame.
- ✅ JFrame uses a layered architecture: **Glass Pane → Layered Pane → Content Pane**.
- ✅ Default close operation is `HIDE_ON_CLOSE` (value **1**) — not `EXIT_ON_CLOSE`.
- ✅ `EXIT_ON_CLOSE` calls `System.exit(0)`, ending the **entire JVM**.
- ✅ `DISPOSE_ON_CLOSE` releases frame resources but **does not end the JVM**.
- ✅ `pack()` auto-sizes the frame; `setLocationRelativeTo(null)` centers it.
- ✅ Swing components start with `J` (`JButton`, `JLabel`, `JTextField`, etc.).
- ✅ JFrame supports **pluggable look-and-feel** (Metal, Nimbus, System).
- ✅ Event handling follows the **Event Delegation Model** (source → listener).
- ✅ The class implementing `ActionListener` must override `actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)`.
- ✅ `setLayout(null)` enables **absolute positioning** (requires manual `setBounds()`).
- ✅ JFrame is **heavyweight** (relies on OS), but its children are **lightweight**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: JLabel
topics.content: > **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT414 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** JLabel # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **JLabel (Java Swing Definition - KTU 2024 Scheme Aligned)** > > `JLabel` is a subclass of `javax.swing.JComponent` used to **display a short text string, an image, or both** on a Swing-based Graphical User Interface (GUI). It is one of the simplest and most fundamental non-editable display components in the Java Foundation Classes (JFC) Swing API, defined in the package `javax.swing`. Unlike `JTextField` or `JTextArea`, a `JLabel` is **passive** — it does not generate ActionEvents when interacted with, and its primary purpose is purely informational (descriptive labelling of other GUI components). The formal class hierarchy is: $$ \text{java.lang.Object} \rightarrow \text{java.awt.Component} \rightarrow \text{java.awt.Container} \rightarrow \text{javax.swing.JComponent} \rightarrow \text{javax.swing.JLabel} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Key Declaration (Java SE 17 / KTU 2024 Lab Compatible):** > > `public class JLabel extends JComponent implements SwingConstants, Accessible` ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of a `JLabel` like a **printed name tag or a sticker pasted on a notice board**. - Imagine you walk into a government office. On every counter, there is a small printed paper that says *"Cash Counter"* or *"Token Display"*. That paper does **not** do anything when you touch it — it only *informs* you. That is exactly what a `JLabel` does in a Swing GUI. - The **text on the sticker** → corresponds to the **String content** of the label. - A **small logo/icon** next to the name → corresponds to the **Icon** property of the label. - The **position where the sticker is pasted** → corresponds to the layout (e.g., `BorderLayout.NORTH`, `FlowLayout`). ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Properties | Property | Default Value (as per Java SE 17 Specification) | |---|---| | **Alignment** | `LEADING` (left-aligned in LTR locales) | | **Opaque** | `false` (background not painted by default) | | **Text** | empty string `""` | | **Icon** | `null` | | **Mnemonic** | `KeyEvent.VK_UNDEFINED` (no mnemonic) | | **Displayed Mnemonic Index** | `-1` | | **Label For** | `null` (no component linked) | > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PEECT414 - Module 4):** JLabel is covered under *"Swing Basics and Event Handling"* as a foundational GUI display class, alongside `JButton`, `JTextField`, `JCheckBox`, and `JRadioButton`. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet ## 2.1 Constructors of JLabel The `JLabel` class provides **six (6)** overloaded constructors: $$ \text{JLabel}() $$ $$ \text{JLabel}(\text{String text}) $$ $$ \text{JLabel}(\text{Icon image}) $$ $$ \text{JLabel}(\text{String text}, \text{Icon image}, \text{int horizontalAlignment}) $$ $$ \text{JLabel}(\text{String text}, \text{int horizontalAlignment}) $$ $$ \text{JLabel}(\text{Icon image}, \text{int horizontalAlignment}) $$ The `horizontalAlignment` parameter must be one of the constants from `SwingConstants`: - `SwingConstants.LEFT` - `SwingConstants.CENTER` - `SwingConstants.RIGHT` - `SwingConstants.LEADING` - `SwingConstants.TRAILING` ## 2.2 Most Frequently Used Methods (Board-Exam Essentials) | Method Signature | Purpose | Return Type | |---|---|---| | `void setText(String text)` | Sets the label's text | `void` | | `String getText()` | Retrieves the label's text | `String` | | `void setIcon(Icon icon)` | Sets the icon/image of the label | `void` | | `Icon getIcon()` | Retrieves the icon | `Icon` | | `void setHorizontalAlignment(int alignment)` | Aligns icon & text horizontally | `void` | | `void setVerticalAlignment(int alignment)` | Aligns icon & text vertically | `void` | | `void setHorizontalTextPosition(int pos)` | Position of text relative to icon | `void` | | `void setVerticalTextPosition(int pos)` | Position of text relative to icon | `void` | | `void setMnemonic(int mnemonic)` | Sets keyboard shortcut key | `void` | | `void setDisplayedMnemonicIndex(int index)` | Sets index of underlined char | `void` | | `void setLabelFor(Component comp)` | Links label to another component | `void` | | `void setEnabled(boolean enabled)` | Enables/disables the label | `void` | | `boolean isOpaque()` | Checks if background is painted | `boolean` | | `void setOpaque(boolean isOpaque)` | Sets opacity flag | `void` | ## 2.3 Logical Breakdown — The "Why" Behind JLabel - **Why does JLabel exist?** Swing designers needed a *non-editable*, *lightweight* component purely for output (display). They did not want to misuse `JButton` (which generates events) or `JTextField` (which is editable) for mere display. - **How does it work internally?** `JLabel` overrides the `paintComponent(Graphics g)` method of `JComponent`.
