When Swing repaints the screen, the label's `UI delegate` (a `LabelUI` object) draws the icon first (if any) and then the text string using the current Font and Foreground color. - **How does Mnemonic work?** When a `JLabel` is given a mnemonic (e.g., `KeyEvent.VK_N`) and is linked to a `JTextField` via `setLabelFor()`, pressing **Alt + N** automatically transfers focus to the linked text field. This is a critical **accessibility** feature. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet | Concept | Key Point | Board Exam Weight | |---|---|---| | **Package** | `javax.swing` | High (1 mark) | | **Parent Class** | `JComponent` | High (1 mark) | | **Editable?** | NO (cannot be edited by user) | High (2 marks) | | **Event Generation?** | Does NOT generate `ActionEvent` | Medium (2 marks) | | **HTML Rendering** | Supports HTML 3.2 subset in text | Medium (3 marks) | | **Mnemonic Support** | Yes (via `setMnemonic`) | High (3 marks) | | **Default Layout** | Managed by parent container's LayoutManager | Medium (2 marks) | | **Implements** | `SwingConstants, Accessible` | Low (1 mark) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-world Engineering Utility:** JLabel is the backbone of every form-based enterprise Java application — banking software, railway reservation systems, hospital management portals, and KTU lab evaluation portals all use `JLabel` to display static information such as field names, headings, and instructional text. Without `JLabel`, GUIs would lack proper context for user interaction. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Working Analysis & Code Matrix ## 3.1 Working Example — Step-by-Step Construction Let us walk through a complete working example step-by-step. This is the **canonical KTU lab pattern**. ### Step 1: Import the required Swing classes ```java import javax.swing.JFrame; import javax.swing.JLabel; import javax.swing.ImageIcon; import javax.swing.SwingConstants; import java.awt.FlowLayout; ``` **Explanation:** We import `JFrame` (the window), `JLabel` (our topic), `ImageIcon` (to display images), `SwingConstants` (for alignment), and `FlowLayout` (a simple layout manager). ### Step 2: Create the main class and the JFrame container ```java public class LabelDemo extends JFrame { public LabelDemo() { setTitle("JLabel Demonstration - KTU PEECT414"); setSize(400, 200); setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE); setLayout(new FlowLayout()); } } ``` **Explanation:** We extend `JFrame` because our class *is* a window. The layout is set to `FlowLayout` so labels appear left-to-right. ### Step 3: Create the JLabels ```java JLabel lbl1 = new JLabel("Simple Text Label"); JLabel lbl2 = new JLabel("Label with Icon", new ImageIcon("logo.png"), SwingConstants.LEFT); JLabel lbl3 = new JLabel("Disabled Label"); JLabel lbl4 = new JLabel("Bold HTML Label"); ``` **Explanation of each line:** - **Line 1:** Uses `JLabel(String text)` constructor — text only. - **Line 2:** Uses the most complete constructor — text + icon + alignment. The icon is positioned to the **left** of the text. - **Line 3:** Plain constructor; will be disabled later. - **Line 4:** HTML content. JLabel's `setText` automatically detects HTML if the string starts with ``. ### Step 4: Configure properties ```java lbl2.setHorizontalTextPosition(SwingConstants.CENTER); lbl2.setVerticalTextPosition(SwingConstants.BOTTOM); lbl3.setEnabled(false); lbl4.setForeground(java.awt.Color.BLUE); ``` **Explanation:** - For `lbl2`: text appears in the **center horizontally** and at the **bottom vertically** relative to the icon. - For `lbl3`: greys out the label (typical disabled state). - For `lbl4`: blue text color. ### Step 5: Add labels to the frame and display ```java add(lbl1); add(lbl2); add(lbl3); add(lbl4); setVisible(true); ``` **Explanation:** The `add()` method inherits from `java.awt.Container`. Finally, `setVisible(true)` triggers the Swing painting pipeline, and the window appears. ### Step 6: Main method (entry point) ```java public static void main(String[] args) { new LabelDemo(); } ``` **Explanation:** Creates an anonymous instance — standard Java idiom for Swing applications. ## 3.2 Complete Compilable Program ```java import javax.swing.*; import java.awt.*; public class LabelDemo extends JFrame { public LabelDemo() { setTitle("JLabel Demo"); setSize(400, 200); setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE); setLayout(new FlowLayout()); JLabel lbl1 = new JLabel("Simple Text"); JLabel lbl2 = new JLabel("With Icon", new ImageIcon("logo.png"), SwingConstants.LEFT); JLabel lbl3 = new JLabel("HTML Demo"); lbl3.setText("Italic & Bold"); add(lbl1); add(lbl2); add(lbl3); setVisible(true); } public static void main(String[] args) { new LabelDemo(); } } ``` ## 3.3 Mnemonic Linking — Detailed Walkthrough A common **Part B (14 mark)** question type is *"Demonstrate a label linked to a text field using a mnemonic."* **Step A:** Create the label with mnemonic **Step B:** Create the linked component **Step C:** Use `setLabelFor()` **Step D:** Set the displayed mnemonic index The mnemonic logic can be expressed as: $$ \text{Focus Transfer Rule} = \text{setLabelFor}(C) \land \text{setMnemonic}(K) \Rightarrow \text{Press } (Alt + K) \rightarrow \text{Focus moves to } C $$ Where $C$ is the target `Component` and $K$ is a `KeyEvent` virtual key code. **Code Implementation:** ```java JLabel nameLabel = new JLabel("Name:"); nameLabel.setDisplayedMnemonic('N'); // 'N' will be underlined nameLabel.setLabelFor(nameField); // 'nameField' is a JTextField ``` The `setDisplayedMnemonic` method (note the name differs from `setMnemonic`) automatically **looks for the character** in the label's text and underlines it. The character used here is the **letter keycode** for `'N'`, equivalent to `KeyEvent.VK_N`. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Class Hierarchy Diagram ```mermaid classDiagram classA_Object classA_Object : java.lang.Object classB_Component classB_Component : java.awt.Component classC_Container classC_Container : java.awt.Container classD_JComponent classD_JComponent : javax.swing.JComponent classE_JLabel classE_JLabel : javax.swing.JLabel classE_JLabel : +setText(String) classE_JLabel : +getText() String classE_JLabel : +setIcon(Icon) classE_JLabel : +getIcon() Icon classE_JLabel : +setMnemonic(int) classE_JLabel : +setLabelFor(Component) classE_JLabel : +setHorizontalAlignment(int) classE_JLabel : +setDisplayedMnemonicIndex(int) classA_Object <|-- classB_Component classB_Component <|-- classC_Container classC_Container <|-- classD_JComponent classD_JComponent <|-- classE_JLabel ``` ## 4.2 JLabel Functional Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[User Program main method] --> B[Create JFrame Container] B --> C[Set Layout Manager] C --> D[Instantiate JLabel Object] D --> D1[Choose Constructor] D1 --> D1a[Default Constructor] D1 --> D1b[Text Only Constructor] D1 --> D1c[Icon Only Constructor] D1 --> D1d[Text + Icon + Alignment] D --> E[Configure Properties] E --> E1[setText String] E --> E2[setIcon ImageIcon] E --> E3[setHorizontalAlignment] E --> E4[setMnemonic] E --> E5[setLabelFor] E --> F[Add to Container via add method] F --> G[setVisible true] G --> H[Swing Painting Pipeline Activated] H --> I[JLabelUI paintComponent executes] I --> J[Icon Drawn First] J --> K[Text Drawn Second on Top] K --> L[Window Visible to User] ``` ## 4.3 Mnemonic Focus Transfer Sequence ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant U as User participant L as JLabel participant T as Target Component U->>L: Reads text Name with underlined N U->>L: Presses Alt and N together L->>L: setLabelFor reference checked L->>T: requestFocusInWindow method invoked T-->>U: Caret appears in JTextField T-->>U: Label is now associated with the input field ``` ## 4.4 Horizontal and Vertical Alignment Matrix ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph HA[Horizontal Alignment] HA1[LEADING - default LTR] HA2[CENTER] HA3[TRAILING] HA4[LEFT] HA5[RIGHT] end subgraph VA[Vertical Alignment] VA1[TOP] VA2[CENTER - default] VA3[BOTTOM] end HA --> CMP[JLabel Component] VA --> CMP CMP --> OUT[Final Rendered Position] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks Total) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]** *List any three constructors of the `JLabel` class. Also state the package in which it is defined.* **Model Answer:** The `JLabel` class is defined in the package **`javax.swing`**. The three commonly used constructors are: 1. `JLabel()` — Creates a label with empty text and no icon. 2. `JLabel(String text)` — Creates a label with the specified text. 3. `JLabel(Icon image)` — Creates a label with the specified icon. *(Award full 3 marks: 1 for package name + 2 for correctly listing two constructors)* --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]** *Explain the purpose of `setLabelFor()` and `setDisplayedMnemonic()` methods of `JLabel` with a small example.* **Model Answer:** The `setLabelFor(Component c)` method **associates the label with another GUI component** (typically a text field). When the label's mnemonic is pressed, focus automatically transfers to the associated component. The `setDisplayedMnemonic(char ch)` method **automatically underlines the specified character** in the label's text, indicating it as the keyboard shortcut. **Example:** ```java JLabel lbl = new JLabel("Username:"); lbl.setDisplayedMnemonic('U'); JTextField tf = new JTextField(15); lbl.setLabelFor(tf); ``` Here, pressing **Alt + U** shifts focus to the `JTextField tf`. *(3 marks: 1 + 1 + 1)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (Module Internal Choice — 14 Marks Each) > **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Per the revised pattern, students answer **ONE full question** from a choice of TWO in Part B. Each sub-part is typically **7 marks**. ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Actual Pattern]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the class hierarchy of `JLabel` in Java Swing. List at least **four** commonly used methods of `JLabel` with their signatures and purposes. **(b) [7 Marks]** Write a complete Java Swing program to create a window containing three labels: one with only text, one with only an icon, and one with both text and an icon. The label containing both should have its text positioned at the **bottom-center** relative to the icon. #### Model Answer (a) — Class Hierarchy & Methods **Class Hierarchy (3 marks):** The class hierarchy of `JLabel` in Java Swing is: $$ \text{java.lang.Object} \rightarrow \text{java.awt.Component} \rightarrow \text{java.awt.Container} \rightarrow \text{javax.swing.JComponent} \rightarrow \text{javax.swing.JLabel} $$ **Key points:** - `JLabel` is a **direct subclass** of `JComponent`. - It implements the `SwingConstants` interface (provides alignment constants) and the `Accessible` interface (for accessibility support). - It is part of the **`javax.swing`** package, introduced in **JDK 1.2** as part of the Java Foundation Classes. **Four Commonly Used Methods (4 marks — 1 each):** | Method | Signature | Purpose | |---|---|---| | `setText` | `void setText(String text)` | Sets the text string of the label | | `getText` | `String getText()` | Returns the current text of the label | | `setIcon` | `void setIcon(Icon icon)` | Sets the icon displayed by the label | | `setHorizontalAlignment` | `void setHorizontalAlignment(int alignment)` | Aligns the icon and text horizontally | #### Model Answer (b) — Working Program ```java import javax.swing.*; import java.awt.*; public class ThreeLabelDemo extends JFrame { ThreeLabelDemo() { setTitle("Three Labels Demo"); setSize(500, 250); setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE); setLayout(new FlowLayout()); // Label 1: text only JLabel l1 = new JLabel("Plain Text Label"); l1.setFont(new Font("Arial", Font.BOLD, 16)); // Label 2: icon only ImageIcon icon = new ImageIcon("logo.png"); JLabel l2 = new JLabel(icon); // Label 3: text + icon, text at bottom-center JLabel l3 = new JLabel("Label with Text and Icon", icon, SwingConstants.CENTER); l3.setHorizontalTextPosition(SwingConstants.CENTER); l3.setVerticalTextPosition(SwingConstants.BOTTOM); add(l1); add(l2); add(l3); setVisible(true); } public static void main(String[] args) { new ThreeLabelDemo(); } } ``` **Explanation of the key line for sub-part (b):** The line `l3.setHorizontalTextPosition(SwingConstants.CENTER)` positions the text **horizontally centered** relative to the icon, and `l3.setVerticalTextPosition(SwingConstants.BOTTOM)` positions it at the **bottom**. This satisfies the requirement of *"bottom-center"*. *(Total: 7 marks — 1 for class structure, 2 for proper import, 2 for the three labels, 1 for the alignment configuration, 1 for setVisible and main method)* --- ### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Actual Pattern]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Differentiate between `setMnemonic(int)` and `setDisplayedMnemonic(char)` methods of `JLabel`. When would you prefer one over the other? Provide a code snippet. **(b) [7 Marks]** Write a Swing program to create a form with two `JLabel`s and two `JTextField`s. The first label "**N**ame:" should have mnemonic 'N' linked to the first text field, and the second label "A**g**e:" should have mnemonic 'G' linked to the second text field. Use proper layout. #### Model Answer (a) — Mnemonic Differentiation | Feature | `setMnemonic(int)` | `setDisplayedMnemonic(char)` | |---|---|---| | **Parameter** | Virtual key code (e.g., `KeyEvent.VK_N`) | A character literal (e.g., `'N'`) | | **Underlines text?** | NO — must call `setDisplayedMnemonicIndex` separately | YES — automatically underlines | | **Convenience** | Lower-level; more control | Higher-level; automatic search | | **Use case** | When you need explicit control over the underline position | When you want the standard underline behavior | **Preferred usage:** In most cases, **`setDisplayedMnemonic(char)`** is preferred because it is a one-step operation. Use `setMnemonic(int)` when you need to combine with `setDisplayedMnemonicIndex(int)` to underline a **specific occurrence** of the character (e.g., the second 'N' in "Name"). **Code snippet:** ```java JLabel lbl = new JLabel("User Name:"); lbl.setDisplayedMnemonic('N'); // underlines first 'N' JTextField tf = new JTextField(15); lbl.setLabelFor(tf); ``` *(4 marks for table + 2 marks for code + 1 mark for explanation)* #### Model Answer (b) — Form with Mnemonics ```java import javax.swing.*; import java.awt.*; public class MnemonicFormDemo extends JFrame { MnemonicFormDemo() { setTitle("Mnemonic Form Demo"); setSize(350, 150); setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE); setLayout(new GridLayout(2, 2, 5, 5)); // Row 1: Name label + text field JLabel nameLabel = new JLabel("Name:"); nameLabel.setDisplayedMnemonic('N'); JTextField nameField = new JTextField(15); nameLabel.setLabelFor(nameField); // Row 2: Age label + text field JLabel ageLabel = new JLabel("Age:"); ageLabel.setDisplayedMnemonic('G'); JTextField ageField = new JTextField(15); ageLabel.setLabelFor(ageField); add(nameLabel); add(nameField); add(ageLabel); add(ageField); setVisible(true); } public static void main(String[] args) { new MnemonicFormDemo(); } } ``` **Key Implementation Points:** - `GridLayout(2, 2, 5, 5)` creates a 2×2 grid with 5px gaps. - `setDisplayedMnemonic('N')` underlines the 'N' in "Name:". - `setLabelFor(nameField)` creates the focus-transfer link. - Pressing **Alt + N** moves focus to `nameField`; **Alt + G** moves focus to `ageField`. *(7 marks — 1 for layout, 2 for label configuration, 2 for text field creation, 1 for setLabelFor linkage, 1 for main method)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > > 1. **Mnemonic vs Displayed Mnemonic Confusion** (Most common — loses 2-3 marks): Many students write `setMnemonic('N')` instead of `setDisplayedMnemonic('N')`. Remember: `setMnemonic` takes an **int** (virtual key code), and **does NOT underline** automatically. For automatic underlining, use `setDisplayedMnemonic(char)`. > > 2. **Forgetting `setLabelFor()`** (Loses 2 marks): A mnemonic alone does nothing visible. You **must** call `setLabelFor(targetComponent)` for the focus-transfer to work. > > 3. **HTML tag typos** (Loses 1-2 marks): HTML content must start with `` (lowercase) and end with ``. Also, HTML in `JLabel` only supports the **HTML 3.2** subset — no CSS, no JavaScript. > > 4. **Missing `setVisible(true)`** (Loses 1 mark): Forgetting this final call results in a non-visible window. Always place it as the last statement in the constructor. > > 5. **Wrong package import** (Loses 1 mark): `JLabel` is in **`javax.swing`**, NOT `java.awt` (where `Label`, the AWT counterpart, resides). Confusing these is a common board-exam trap. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist** - **Package:** `javax.swing` *(not `java.awt`)* - **Parent Class:** `javax.swing.JComponent` - **Implements:** `SwingConstants`, `Accessible` - **Purpose:** Display non-editable text, image, or both - **Six Constructors:** Default, Text-only, Icon-only, Text+Icon+HAlign, Text+HAlign, Icon+HAlign - **Alignment Constants:** `LEFT`, `CENTER`, `RIGHT`, `LEADING`, `TRAILING` (from `SwingConstants`) - **Mnemonic Methods:** - `setMnemonic(int)` — sets key code, no underline - `setDisplayedMnemonic(char)` — sets char, auto-underlines - `setDisplayedMnemonicIndex(int)` — picks which occurrence of the char to underline - **Linking:** `setLabelFor(Component)` — **mandatory** for focus transfer - **HTML Support:** Yes, if string begins with `` (HTML 3.2 subset only) - **Opaque Default:** `false` — background is transparent by default - **Event Generation:** Does **NOT** generate `ActionEvent` (use `JButton` for that) - **Icon Class Used:** `javax.swing.ImageIcon` - **AWT Counterpart:** `java.awt.Label` (heavyweight, deprecated for new GUIs) - **Common Lab Pattern:** Extend `JFrame` → `setLayout` → create `JLabel` → `add()` → `setVisible(true)` - **Layout Manager for Labels:** Any LayoutManager works; `FlowLayout` is simplest, `GridBagLayout` is most powerful - **Board Exam Favorite Topics:** 1. Mnemonics + `setLabelFor` linkage (asked almost every semester) 2. Difference between `setMnemonic` and `setDisplayedMnemonic` 3. Constructor variations 4. Class hierarchy diagram 5. Form-creation program combining `JLabel` + `JTextField`

topics.title: The Swing Buttons
topics.content: > **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT414 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** The Swing Buttons ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In **Java Swing (javax.swing package)**, *buttons* are interactive **GUI components** that extend the `AbstractButton` class. They serve as the primary triggers for user-driven events in a graphical application. Swing supports four principal button types, all built upon a unified event-handling model based on the **ActionListener**, **ItemListener**, and **ChangeListener** interfaces of the AWT/Swing delegation event model. > [!NOTE] > **AbstractButton** is an *abstract* superclass defined inside `javax.swing`. It is the root of all Swing button-like widgets. Concrete subclasses inherit its state (pressed, rollover, selected, armed) and its event-firing logic. The four canonical Swing buttons are: 1. **`JButton`** — A push button that fires an `ActionEvent` when clicked. 2. **`JToggleButton`** — A two-state (pressed/released) button that maintains a sticky state. 3. **`JCheckBox`** — A specialized toggle used for multi-selection from independent options. 4. **`JRadioButton`** — A toggle restricted to single-selection within a mutually exclusive `ButtonGroup`. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Swing is **lightweight** (100% Java, peer-based rendering) unlike AWT's heavyweight peers. All Swing buttons inherit the **pluggable Look-And-Feel (LAF)** capability, meaning a single program can render buttons in Windows, Metal, Motif, or Nimbus skins without code changes. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition Imagine a **remote control** for your television: - A **`JButton`** is the **Power button** — you press it, the TV reacts *once*, and it springs back. The TV does **not remember** that you pressed it. - A **`JToggleButton`** is the **Mute button** — press it once, the TV *stays* muted; press it again, it un-mutes. The button visibly "latches down." - A **`JCheckBox`** is the set of **independent feature toggles** on a streaming app (Auto-play, HD, Subtitles). You can switch on *any combination* — checking HD does **not** uncheck Auto-play. - A **`JRadioButton`** is the **TV input source selector** (HDMI1, HDMI2, AV, Antenna). Selecting HDMI2 *automatically* deselects HDMI1. Only **one input** can be active at a time. > [!TIP] > In a single sentence: **JButton = momentary action; JToggleButton = sticky action; JCheckBox = independent sticky actions; JRadioButton = exclusive sticky actions (only when grouped).** --- ### 1.3 The Class Hierarchy at a Glance `AbstractButton` is the abstract base, but it inherits through this chain: ``` java.lang.Object └── java.awt.Component └── java.awt.Container └── javax.swing.JComponent └── javax.swing.AbstractButton ← root of all buttons ├── JButton ├── JToggleButton │ ├── JCheckBox │ └── JRadioButton └── JMenuItem ``` > [!NOTE] > **Constants in KTU expected answers:** Common `ActionEvent` modifiers such as `BUTTON1_DOWN_MASK`, the standard `int` keyboard codes for `VK_ENTER` (= **10**) and `VK_SPACE` (= **32**), and event-ID fields like `ActionEvent.ACTION_FIRST` (= **1001**) are sometimes quoted. Memorize `VK_ENTER = 10` and `VK_SPACE = 32`. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The `AbstractButton` Contract — Why It Matters `AbstractButton` provides **state** and **events** that every concrete button reuses. Understanding this single class lets you master all four button subclasses. **State Variables (instance fields, all `protected boolean`):** - `model` — A `ButtonModel` reference (the **MVC** "M"). The model stores `armed`, `pressed`, `rollover`, `selected`, `enabled`, `mnemonic` index. - `text` — The displayed label (a `String`). - `icon` — The default icon (`Icon`). - `pressedIcon`, `selectedIcon`, `rolloverIcon`, `disabledIcon`, `disabledSelectedIcon` — State-dependent icons. - `mnemonic` — A keyboard shortcut (`int` KeyEvent code). - `actionCommand` — A `String` the program reads in `actionPerformed()` to identify *which* button fired. - `verticalAlignment`, `horizontalAlignment`, `verticalTextPosition`, `horizontalTextPosition` — Layout hints. - `borderPainted`, `contentAreaFilled`, `focusPainted`, `rolloverEnabled` — Paint-flag booleans for styling. **Event Listeners (the "VC" in MVC):** - `ActionListener` — fired on a **successful click** (mouse release inside the button, or keyboard activation). - `ItemListener` — fired when the button's `selected` state **changes** (only meaningful for toggles). - `ChangeListener` — fired on **any model-state change** (armed, pressed, rollover, selected). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Tip:** The most commonly asked viva question is *"Which listener fires on a button click and which on state change?"* Answer: **`ActionListener`** → click; **`ItemListener`** → state transition of a toggle; **`ChangeListener`** → any model change (over-fires). --- ### 2.2 The Default Button Model: `DefaultButtonModel` `AbstractButton` delegates all state to a `ButtonModel` interface, whose default implementation is `DefaultButtonModel`. The model: - Implements `ButtonModel` (extends `ItemSelectable`). - Fires a `ChangeEvent` to every registered `ChangeListener` whenever **any** state bit flips. - Maintains five named bits in a single `int mask`: `ARMED = 1`, `SELECTED = 2`, `PRESSED = 4`, `ROLLOVER = 8`, `ENABLED = 16` (these are internal constants — KTU rarely requires the literal bit values, but know that they are bitwise). The model is obtained via the `getModel()` method and is the *recommended* way to query state, because it survives across LAF changes. --- ### 2.3 Mnemonics, Accelerators, and the `Action` Interface A **mnemonic** is a single key (combined with `Alt`) that focuses a button. Set with `button.setMnemonic(KeyEvent.VK_X)`. The character is **underlined** in the label automatically. A more general abstraction is the **`javax.swing.Action` interface**, which bundles together: - A name (`String NAME`) - An icon (`Icon SMALL_ICON`) - An enabled flag (`boolean ENABLED`) - A short description (`String SHORT_DESCRIPTION`) - A long description (`String LONG_DESCRIPTION`) - An accelerator (`KeyStroke ACCELERATOR`) - An `actionPerformed(ActionEvent)` method > [!TIP] > Use the same `Action` instance for a `JButton`, a `JMenuItem`, and a toolbar button. The button state (enabled, text, icon) will then update **automatically** across all three — a powerful MVC trick heavily emphasized in modern KTU modules. --- ### 2.4 The ButtonGroup Rule (Exclusive Selection) `ButtonGroup` is **not a Component** — it is a logical container (`Object` subclass) that enforces **mutual exclusion** among an arbitrary number of `AbstractButton`s (typically `JRadioButton`s, but `JToggleButton`s and `JCheckBox`es can also be grouped). Adding a button: `group.add(button)`. The `ButtonGroup` holds a `Vector` of buttons and a `ButtonModel` reference to the currently selected one. Pressing a new button calls `setSelected(button.getModel(), true)` and `setSelected(oldModel, false)` internally. > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** Forgetting to add `JRadioButton`s to a `ButtonGroup`. Without a group, every radio behaves like an **independent check box** — multiple selections allowed. --- ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Method / Field | Returns / Default | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | Create text button | `new JButton("OK")` | `JButton` | Constructor arg is `String`, `Icon`, or both | | Create icon button | `new JButton(icon)` | `JButton` | Uses `ImageIcon` typically | | Get label | `getText()` | `String` | Live, may be HTML | | Set label HTML | `setText("Bold")` | `void` | Enables basic HTML | | Set mnemonic | `setMnemonic(KeyEvent.VK_X)` | `void` | Alt + X triggers it | | Set shortcut | `setMnemonic(int)` | `void` | int = virtual key code | | Action command | `setActionCommand("SAVE")` | `void` | Read inside `actionPerformed` | | Read state | `button.getModel().isSelected()` | `boolean` | Preferred over `isSelected()` | | Toggle selected | `setSelected(true)` | `void` | Works for JToggleButton and subclasses | | Get state of group | `group.getSelection()` | `ButtonModel` | `getSelection().getActionCommand()` for which one | | Add listener | `button.addActionListener(this)` | `void` | Lambda also valid (Java 8+) | | Remove listener | `button.removeActionListener(l)` | `void` | Symmetric | | Fires on state change | `button.addItemListener(...)` | `void` | Toggle only | | Fires on any model change | `button.addChangeListener(...)` | `void` | Over-fires; rare | | Border paint | `setBorderPainted(false)` | `void` | Flat / web look | | Content area | `setContentAreaFilled(false)` | `void` | Transparent body | | Focus paint | `setFocusPainted(false)` | `void` | Hides focus ring | | Rollover enable | `setRolloverEnabled(true)` | `void` | Allows rollover icon swap | | Mnemonic index | `setDisplayedMnemonicIndex(2)` | `void` | If two candidates exist | | Action map key | `getActionMap().put("actionKey", action)` | `void` | Key-binding plumbing | | Pressed icon | `setPressedIcon(icon)` | `void` | Displayed while mouse is held | | Tool-tip text | `setToolTipText("Click me")` | `void` | Inherited from `JComponent` | | Default button | `getRootPane().setDefaultButton(btn)` | `void` | Activated on Enter key | | Key binding | `InputMap` + `ActionMap` | — | For custom accelerators | > [!NOTE] > **Mandatory note on `KeyEvent` codes used in KTU answers:** `VK_ENTER = 10`, `VK_SPACE = 32`, `VK_ESCAPE = 27`, `VK_F1 = 112`, `VK_A = 65`. Memorize the first two. --- ### 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering Swing buttons drive the **front-end of desktop HMI (Human-Machine Interface) software** used in: - **Industrial SCADA panels** for plant control (toggle, momentary). - **Medical imaging workstations** (PACS viewers) — radio buttons select anatomy view, check boxes toggle measurement overlays. - **CAD tools** (e.g., older NetBeans platform apps) — toolbar buttons wrap `Action`s. - **Banking back-office UIs** — `JButton` "Submit", `JRadioButton` "Account Type". Knowing the *exact* hierarchy (especially MVC) lets you write **testable, LAF-portable** UIs — a skill repeatedly rewarded in KTU project evaluations. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Code Walkthroughs & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > This section walks through the **construction, event-binding, and grouping logic** step by step — the same logical steps KTU expects in a 14-mark program answer. Each block shows the reasoning *before* the code. ### 3.1 Step-by-Step: Building a `JButton` That Logs Its Action Command **Goal:** Create a window with two buttons ("Save" and "Open"). When clicked, print their action command. **Step 1 — Reason about responsibilities.** The window must (a) hold the buttons, (b) attach a listener, (c) implement the listener's callback. We extend `JFrame` and implement `ActionListener` in the same class (allowed but discouraged; for a single-file exam answer, it is the KTU-idiomatic choice). **Step 2 — Declare the buttons as instance fields.** ```java private JButton saveBtn, openBtn; ``` **Step 3 — Initialize them in the constructor with text and unique action commands.** ```java saveBtn = new JButton("Save"); saveBtn.setActionCommand("SAVE"); openBtn = new JButton("Open"); openBtn.setActionCommand("OPEN"); ``` The *label* is for the user; the *action command* is for the program. Keeping them independent decouples **internationalization** from **logic**. **Step 4 — Register the listener on each button.** ```java saveBtn.addActionListener(this); openBtn.addActionListener(this); ``` **Step 5 — Place them in a container and configure the JFrame.** ```java setLayout(new FlowLayout()); add(saveBtn); add(openBtn); setSize(300, 150); setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE); setVisible(true); ``` **Step 6 — Implement the callback.** ```java @Override public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) { String cmd = e.getActionCommand(); if (cmd.equals("SAVE")) { System.out.println("Saving file..."); } else if (cmd.equals("OPEN")) { System.out.println("Opening file..."); } } ``` **The complete, compilable program:** ```java import javax.swing.*; import java.awt.*; import java.awt.event.*; public class ButtonDemo extends JFrame implements ActionListener { private JButton saveBtn, openBtn; public ButtonDemo() { saveBtn = new JButton("Save"); saveBtn.setActionCommand("SAVE"); saveBtn.addActionListener(this); openBtn = new JButton("Open"); openBtn.setActionCommand("OPEN"); openBtn.addActionListener(this); setLayout(new FlowLayout()); add(saveBtn); add(openBtn); setSize(300, 150); setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE); setVisible(true); } @Override public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) { String cmd = e.getActionCommand(); if (cmd.equals("SAVE")) { System.out.println("Saving file..."); } else if (cmd.equals("OPEN")) { System.out.println("Opening file..."); } } public static void main(String[] args) { new ButtonDemo(); } } ``` > [!NOTE] > **Line-by-line reasoning for valuation:** (1) The class *must* `implements ActionListener` (1 mark). (2) Each button needs `setActionCommand` *or* relies on label as the default command (1 mark). (3) The `actionPerformed` method must be present with the exact signature `public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)` (1 mark). (4) `setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE)` — without this, the JVM will not terminate on window close (1 mark). (5) Correct container-add with a `LayoutManager` (1 mark). --- ### 3.2 Step-by-Step: `JToggleButton` with `ItemListener` **Goal:** A `JToggleButton` whose background color flips between red and green whenever it is pressed. **Step 1 — Understand the event.** A toggle has a *selected* state. The right listener is **`ItemListener`**, fired *twice* per click: once for `DESELECTED` (when un-toggling) and once for `SELECTED` (when re-toggling). **Step 2 — Add the listener.** ```java toggle = new JToggleButton("On/Off"); toggle.addItemListener(this); ``` **Step 3 — Implement `itemStateChanged(ItemEvent e)`.** ```java @Override public void itemStateChanged(ItemEvent e) { if (e.getStateChange() == ItemEvent.SELECTED) { toggle.setBackground(Color.RED); } else { toggle.setBackground(Color.GREEN); } } ``` > [!TIP] > **Why not `ActionListener`?** `ActionListener` fires *once* per click — fine for a `JButton`. For a `JToggleButton`, it tells you **a click happened**, not the **new state**. The toggle's *state* is in `ButtonModel.isSelected()`. `ItemListener` gives you the state transition directly. --- ### 3.3 Step-by-Step: `JCheckBox` Multi-Select **Goal:** Three check boxes ("Java", "Python", "C++"). Print all currently selected languages whenever any is toggled. **Step 1 — Build the check boxes.** ```java javaCb = new JCheckBox("Java"); pyCb = new JCheckBox("Python"); cppCb = new JCheckBox("C++"); ``` **Step 2 — Add an `ItemListener` to each.** ```java javaCb.addItemListener(this); pyCb.addItemListener(this); cppCb.addItemListener(this); ``` **Step 3 — In the handler, iterate all check boxes and read their `isSelected()`.** ```java @Override public void itemStateChanged(ItemEvent e) { StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder("Selected: "); if (javaCb.isSelected()) sb.append("Java "); if (pyCb.isSelected()) sb.append("Python "); if (cppCb.isSelected()) sb.append("C++"); status.setText(sb.toString()); } ``` `status` is a `JLabel` at the bottom of the frame. --- ### 3.4 Step-by-Step: `JRadioButton` with `ButtonGroup` **Goal:** Three radio buttons ("Male", "Female", "Other") in a `ButtonGroup`. Display the chosen gender. **Step 1 — Create a `ButtonGroup` (logical, not visual).** ```java ButtonGroup genderGroup = new ButtonGroup(); ``` **Step 2 — Create the radio buttons.** ```java maleRb = new JRadioButton("Male"); femaleRb = new JRadioButton("Female"); otherRb = new JRadioButton("Other"); ``` **Step 3 — Add them to the group *and* to the container.** The group is **not** a visual container; you **still** must `add()` them to the `JFrame` (or a `JPanel`) yourself. ```java genderGroup.add(maleRb); genderGroup.add(femaleRb); genderGroup.add(otherRb); add(maleRb); add(femaleRb); add(otherRb); ``` **Step 4 — Read the selection with `getSelection().getActionCommand()`.** ```java ButtonModel sel = genderGroup.getSelection(); if (sel != null) { String chosen = sel.getActionCommand(); System.out.println("Gender: " + chosen); } ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **`setActionCommand` again:** It is the *model's* action command, not the button's. For radios, prefer `genderGroup.getSelection().getActionCommand()`. If unset, the *label* acts as the default. --- ### 3.5 The Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Listener | Button Type | ActionListener | ItemListener | ChangeListener | Why | |---|---|---|---|---| | `JButton` (click) | ✓ fires once | rarely useful | over-fires | Clicks are atomic actions | | `JToggleButton` | ✓ but no state info | ✓ state transition | over-fires | ItemListener reveals `SELECTED` / `DESELECTED` | | `JCheckBox` | ✓ but no state info | ✓ state transition | over-fires | Each box toggles independently | | `JRadioButton` (grouped) | ✓ but no state info | ✓ state transition | over-fires | Group ensures single selection | | All buttons | — | — | ✓ on armed/pressed/rollover | Custom renderers / model observers | --- ### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What | Scenario | Recommended Widget | Listener | Grouping | |---|---|---|---| | "Submit form" | `JButton` | `ActionListener` | None | | "Bold" toolbar toggle | `JToggleButton` with icon | `ItemListener` | None | | "Show toolbar" preference | `JCheckBox` | `ItemListener` | Independent | | "Choose font size: S/M/L" | `JRadioButton` | `ItemListener` | `ButtonGroup` | | "Mute" audio | `JToggleButton` | `ItemListener` | None | | "Choose payment: Card/UPI/NetBanking" | `JRadioButton` | `ItemListener` | `ButtonGroup` | | Top-level menu item | `JMenuItem` (subclass of `AbstractButton`) | `ActionListener` | None | > [!WARNING] > **Pitfall:** Using `JButton` for a "stays pressed" function leads to manually tracking state with a `boolean` field. Always prefer `JToggleButton` for sticky behaviour — it does it for you and integrates with the LAF. ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Swing Button Class Hierarchy ```mermaid graph TD objA[java lang Object] compA[java awt Component] contA[java awt Container] jcompA[javax swing JComponent] absBtn[javax swing AbstractButton] jbtnA[JButton] jtogA[JToggleButton] jchkA[JCheckBox] jradA[JRadioButton] jmenuA[JMenuItem] objA --> compA compA --> contA contA --> jcompA jcompA --> absBtn absBtn --> jbtnA absBtn --> jtogA absBtn --> jmenuA jtogA --> jchkA jtogA --> jradA ``` --- ### 4.2 The MVC Cycle for a Single Button Click ```mermaid graph LR userA[USER] viewA[BUTTON VIEW] ctrlA[CONTROLLER LISTENER] modelA[BUTTON MODEL] stateA[STATE BITS] userA -- click --> viewA viewA -- forwards --> modelA modelA -- updates --> stateA stateA -- fires ChangeEvent --> ctrlA modelA -- fires ActionEvent --> ctrlA ctrlA -- business logic --> userA ``` --- ### 4.3 Event-Listener Routing Block Diagram ```mermaid graph TD actA[ACTION LISTENER] itmA[ITEM LISTENER] chgA[CHANGE LISTENER] clickA[Mouse Click or Enter] stateA[State Transition Selected to Deselected] modelA[Any Model Bit Change] clickA -- fires --> actA stateA -- fires --> itmA modelA -- fires --> chgA actA -- handler --> h1[action Performed ActionEvent e] itmA -- handler --> h2[item State Changed ItemEvent e] chgA -- handler --> h3[state Changed ChangeEvent e] ``` --- ### 4.4 ButtonGroup Enforcement Flow ```mermaid graph TD rb1A[Radio Button A] rb2A[Radio Button B] rb3A[Radio Button C] grpA[Button Group] selA[Selected Model] rb1A -- belongs to --> grpA rb2A -- belongs to --> grpA rb3A -- belongs to --> grpA grpA -- tracks --> selA rb2A -- user selects --> grpA grpA -- deselects --> rb1A grpA -- selects --> rb2A ``` --- ### 4.5 Lifecycle of a JButton State (Block-Level Functional Architecture) ```mermaid graph LR idleA[IDLE] armA[ARMED] pressA[PRESSED] relA[RELEASED] fireA[ACTION FIRED] idleA -- mouse enters --> armA armA -- mouse pressed --> pressA pressA -- mouse released inside --> relA relA -- triggers --> fireA relA -- back to --> idleA pressA -- mouse dragged out --> armA ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading aid for students:** The model bit `ARMED` is set the moment the cursor enters the button while a mouse button is held. `PRESSED` is set when the mouse button is actually depressed *over* the button. If the user drags out before releasing, `PRESSED` clears and no `ActionEvent` fires — this is why **drag-out cancels** a click. ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *What is the difference between a `JButton` and a `JToggleButton` in Java Swing? Mention the listener interface that is most appropriate to detect state changes in a `JToggleButton`.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** A `JButton` is a **momentary** push button that fires an `ActionEvent` once per click and then returns to its unselected state — it has **no sticky** behaviour. A `JToggleButton` is a **two-state** button that *retains* its pressed/released state across multiple clicks; the same physical click flips the model between selected and deselected. The listener interface best suited to detect a state change in a `JToggleButton` is **`ItemListener`** (specifically the `itemStateChanged(ItemEvent e)` method), which reports the transition `SELECTED` ↔ `DESELECTED` in `e.getStateChange()`. *(1 + 1 + 1 = 3 marks.)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Explain the purpose of the `ButtonGroup` class in Swing. Why is it that placing three `JRadioButton`s on a panel without grouping them is logically incorrect?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** `ButtonGroup` is a **logical container** in `javax.swing` that enforces **mutual exclusion** among an arbitrary set of `AbstractButton`s (typically `JRadioButton`s). It maintains a reference to the currently selected `ButtonModel` and, whenever a new button is selected, programmatically deselects the previously selected one, ensuring **only one** button in the group is selected at any time. *(1 mark)* If three `JRadioButton`s are added to a panel **without** a `ButtonGroup`, the radio button widget still renders the typical round selector, but **its semantic guarantee of exclusive selection is lost** — the user can tick all three simultaneously, defeating the purpose of "radio" semantics. The default model behaviour is to treat every radio as an **independent** toggle in the absence of grouping. *(2 marks.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks — Internal Choice Option 1) **Q3A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper Adapted]** *Write a Java Swing program to create a simple "Pizza Order Form" with the following requirements:* *(a) [7 marks] Three `JRadioButton`s — "Small", "Medium", "Large" — placed inside a `ButtonGroup` to select pizza size, plus a `JCheckBox` for each topping ("Cheese", "Olives", "Mushrooms") to allow multi-selection.* *(b) [7 marks] A `JButton` labelled "Place Order" that, when clicked, displays the chosen size and the list of selected toppings in a `JTextArea` (or `JLabel`) at the bottom of the window. Implement both `ActionListener` and `ItemListener` in the same class.* --- ### Model Solution (Q3A) ```java import javax.swing.*; import java.awt.*; import java.awt.event.*; public class PizzaOrderForm extends JFrame implements ActionListener, ItemListener { private JRadioButton smallRb, mediumRb, largeRb; private JCheckBox cheeseCb, olivesCb, mushroomsCb; private JButton placeBtn; private JTextArea summary; public PizzaOrderForm() { setTitle("Pizza Order"); setLayout(new FlowLayout()); // --- (a) Radio buttons grouped --- smallRb = new JRadioButton("Small"); mediumRb = new JRadioButton("Medium"); largeRb = new JRadioButton("Large"); ButtonGroup sizeGroup = new ButtonGroup(); sizeGroup.add(smallRb); sizeGroup.add(mediumRb); sizeGroup.add(largeRb); add(smallRb); add(mediumRb); add(largeRb); // --- Check boxes (independent) --- cheeseCb = new JCheckBox("Cheese"); olivesCb = new JCheckBox("Olives"); mushroomsCb = new JCheckBox("Mushrooms"); cheeseCb.addItemListener(this); olivesCb.addItemListener(this); mushroomsCb.addItemListener(this); add(cheeseCb); add(olivesCb); add(mushroomsCb); // --- (b) Place Order button --- placeBtn = new JButton("Place Order"); placeBtn.setActionCommand("ORDER"); placeBtn.addActionListener(this); add(placeBtn); // --- Summary area --- summary = new JTextArea(8, 30); summary.setEditable(false); add(new JScrollPane(summary)); setSize(360, 320); setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE); setVisible(true); } private String selectedSize() { if (smallRb.isSelected()) return "Small"; if (mediumRb.isSelected()) return "Medium"; if (largeRb.isSelected()) return "Large"; return "(none)"; } @Override public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) { if (e.getActionCommand().equals("ORDER")) { StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder(); sb.append("Size: ").append(selectedSize()).append("\n"); sb.append("Toppings: "); if (cheeseCb.isSelected()) sb.append("Cheese "); if (olivesCb.isSelected()) sb.append("Olives "); if (mushroomsCb.isSelected()) sb.append("Mushrooms"); if (sb.toString().endsWith("Toppings: ")) sb.append("(none)"); summary.setText(sb.toString()); } } @Override public void itemStateChanged(ItemEvent e) { // Optional live preview: update summary whenever a topping changes if (summary.getText().length() == 0) { summary.setText("(topping selection pending — press Place Order)"); } } public static void main(String[] args) { new PizzaOrderForm(); } } ``` **Mark-by-mark valuation grid (KTU Examiner's pattern):** | Step | Marks | |---|---| | Correct class declaration with both `ActionListener` and `ItemListener` implemented | 1 | | Three `JRadioButton`s created and added to a `ButtonGroup` | 2 | | Three `JCheckBox`es with `addItemListener(this)` registered on each | 1 | | `JButton` "Place Order" with `ActionListener` registered | 1 | | `JTextArea` (or `JLabel`) created and added to the frame | 1 | | `actionPerformed` correctly reads `getActionCommand()` or `getSource()` to identify the button | 1 | | Correctly reads the **selected size** by checking each `isSelected()` | 1 | | Correctly iterates and concatenates the **selected toppings** | 1 | | `itemStateChanged` is correctly *overridden* with the right signature and uses `e.getStateChange()` or `e.getItem()` | 1 | | `setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE)` and `setSize`/`setVisible(true)` present | 1 | | `main` method present with `new PizzaOrderForm()` invocation | 1 | | Code compiles (no missing imports, balanced braces, semicolons) | 1 | | **Total** | **14** | --- ### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks — Internal Choice Option 2) **Q3B. [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]** *(a) [7 marks] Explain the `AbstractButton` class hierarchy in Swing. List at least four methods declared in `AbstractButton` and explain the role of the `ButtonModel` interface.* *(b) [7 marks] Write a complete Java program that creates three `JToggleButton`s labelled "Red", "Green", "Blue". When **any** of the three is selected, the background of the content pane should change to that colour; if multiple are selected, the last-clicked colour wins. Use the `ItemListener` interface.* --- ### Model Solution (Q3B) **(a) Theoretical answer (7 marks):** `AbstractButton` is an **abstract class** in the package `javax.swing` that serves as the **root of the Swing button hierarchy**. It inherits from `JComponent → Container → Component → Object`. Its concrete subclasses are `JButton`, `JToggleButton` (which in turn is the parent of `JCheckBox` and `JRadioButton`), and `JMenuItem`. By being the common ancestor, `AbstractButton` factors out **all state, all events, and all rendering logic** shared by every button in Swing — promoting reuse and consistent LAF support. **Four important methods of `AbstractButton`:** 1. `setText(String)` / `getText()` — Stores and reads the displayed label; HTML markup is permitted. 2. `setIcon(Icon)` / `getIcon()` — Stores and reads the default icon displayed alongside the text. 3. `setMnemonic(int)` — Associates an `Alt`-key shortcut using a `KeyEvent.VK_*` constant. 4. `setActionCommand(String)` / `getActionCommand()` — Sets a programmer-defined identifier read inside `ActionListener.actionPerformed`. (Alternative correct methods: `addActionListener`, `setSelected`, `setEnabled`, `setRolloverEnabled`, `setPressedIcon`.) *(1 mark each.)* **Role of the `ButtonModel` interface:** `ButtonModel` is the **M (Model) in the MVC** architecture Swing uses for buttons. It encapsulates the *state* of an `AbstractButton` — `armed`, `pressed`, `rollover`, `selected`, `enabled`, `mnemonic` index — independently of the view. The view (`AbstractButton` subclass) calls `getModel()` to query and `setModel(...)` to swap the model. The default implementation is `DefaultButtonModel`, which fires `ChangeEvent`s to all registered `ChangeListener`s whenever any state bit flips. The MVC split enables the **Pluggable Look-And-Feel** mechanism to render the same model under different visual skins without touching the state code. *(3 marks.)* --- **(b) Program (7 marks):** ```java import javax.swing.*; import java.awt.*; import java.awt.event.*; public class ColorToggleDemo extends JFrame implements ItemListener { private JToggleButton redT, greenT, blueT; private Container pane; public ColorToggleDemo() { setTitle("Toggle Background Color"); setLayout(new FlowLayout()); pane = getContentPane(); redT = new JToggleButton("Red"); greenT = new JToggleButton("Green"); blueT = new JToggleButton("Blue"); redT.addItemListener(this); greenT.addItemListener(this); blueT.addItemListener(this); add(redT); add(greenT); add(blueT); setSize(300, 150); setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE); setVisible(true); } @Override public void itemStateChanged(ItemEvent e) { JToggleButton src = (JToggleButton) e.getSource(); if (src.isSelected()) { String label = src.getText(); switch (label) { case "Red": pane.setBackground(Color.RED); break; case "Green": pane.setBackground(Color.GREEN); break; case "Blue": pane.setBackground(Color.BLUE); break; } } else { // If the deselected one was the active color, fall back to default pane.setBackground(null); } } public static void main(String[] args) { new ColorToggleDemo(); } } ``` **Valuation grid (Q3B part b):** | Step | Marks | |---|---| | Class extends `JFrame` and `implements ItemListener` | 1 | | Three `JToggleButton`s with correct text | 1 | | `addItemListener(this)` on every toggle | 1 | | `itemStateChanged(ItemEvent e)` correctly overridden | 1 | | `e.getSource()` cast to `JToggleButton` to read the source | 1 | | Uses `isSelected()` to apply the color (or `e.getStateChange() == ItemEvent.SELECTED`) | 1 | | `setDefaultCloseOperation`, `setSize`, `setVisible(true)` present + `main` invokes constructor | 1 | | **Total** | **7** | --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Common Deductions on Swing Button Questions:** > 1. **Forgetting `setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE)`.** − 1 mark; the examiner specifically checks for *correct window-closing behaviour*. > 2. **Adding `JRadioButton`s to the frame *without* creating a `ButtonGroup`.** The buttons compile and run, but the *exclusive-selection* requirement is not satisfied. − 2 marks. > 3. **Implementing `actionPerformed` without overriding the annotation** (`@Override`). Stylistic, but the KTU rubric often awards ½ mark for the annotation. > 4. **Wrong listener chosen.** A student using `ActionListener` to detect the *new* state of a `JCheckBox` cannot determine which checkbox changed; the examiner expects `ItemListener` and deducts 1 mark. > 5. **Not importing `java.awt.event.*`.** The `ActionEvent`, `ItemEvent`, `KeyEvent` classes live in this package. Missing import ⇒ compile error ⇒ 2 marks. > 6. **Adding the `ButtonGroup` itself to the `JFrame` instead of adding the individual radio buttons.** `ButtonGroup` is `Object`-based, not `Component`-based, so this is a runtime no-op plus a logical error. − 1 mark. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — print this before exams.** - **`AbstractButton`** is the abstract root in `javax.swing` of `JButton`, `JToggleButton` (parent of `JCheckBox`, `JRadioButton`), and `JMenuItem`. - **`JButton`** = momentary click. Use **`ActionListener`**. - **`JToggleButton`, `JCheckBox`, `JRadioButton`** = stateful. Use **`ItemListener`** to detect the transition; use `ButtonModel.isSelected()` to read the current state. - **`ButtonGroup`** is a **logical, non-visual** container. It enforces **mutual exclusion** among any number of `AbstractButton`s. You must still `add()` each button to a visual container. - **`ActionListener.actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)`** — single method, single `ActionEvent` parameter. Use `e.getActionCommand()` (preferred) or `e.getSource()` to identify the firing button. - **`ItemListener.itemStateChanged(ItemEvent e)`** — single method. Use `e.getStateChange()` returning `ItemEvent.SELECTED` (= **1**) or `ItemEvent.DESELECTED` (= **2**). - **Mnemonic codes to memorize:** `VK_ENTER = 10`, `VK_SPACE = 32`, `VK_ESCAPE = 27`. - **`setMnemonic(KeyEvent.VK_X)`** attaches an `Alt + X` accelerator. The character is auto-underlined. - **`setActionCommand(String)`** is independent of the visible label — decouples i18n from logic. - **Pluggable Look-And-Feel** — `UIManager.setLookAndFeel(...)` works uniformly on every Swing button, demonstrating the MVC architecture. - **The `Action` interface** lets one logical action drive a `JButton`, a `JMenuItem`, and a toolbar button **simultaneously** — preferred for KTU project work. - **Default behaviour without grouping radios** — they behave like independent check boxes; the round dot is purely cosmetic. - **Drag-out cancels a click**: if the mouse is pressed inside and released *outside*, no `ActionEvent` fires. Be aware when writing custom button subclasses. - **`setBorderPainted(false)`**, **`setContentAreaFilled(false)`**, **`setFocusPainted(false)`** — three booleans to produce a *flat*, *transparent*, *no-focus-ring* button often used in modern web-style UIs. - **Always close the JFrame properly** in exam code: `setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE)`. Marks are explicitly reserved for this line. - **Naming convention**: prefix button fields with their semantic role, e.g., `submitBtn`, `cancelBtn`, `boldToggle`, `italicsToggle` — not `b1`, `b2`, `t1`. - **Listener registration** is per-component: the **same** listener instance can be attached to many buttons, distinguished via `e.getSource()` or action commands. - **`ItemListener` fires twice per click cycle** (once for `DESELECTED`, once for `SELECTED` on next click) on a `JToggleButton`. Guard your code with `if (e.getStateChange() == ItemEvent.SELECTED)` if you only want the "on" event. - **Threading rule (advanced, for project work)**: All Swing component manipulation must happen on the **Event Dispatch Thread (EDT)**. Wrap the UI construction in `SwingUtilities.invokeLater(() -> new PizzaOrderForm())`. KTU viva may ask this; mention it if your answer has space.

topics.title: JTextField
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT414 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** JTextField

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **JTextField** is a lightweight Swing component (in `javax.swing` package) that allows the user to enter and edit a single line of unformatted text. It is a subclass of `javax.swing.JTextComponent` and inherits from `java.awt.TextComponent`. It implements the `javax.swing.SwingConstants` interface and is the standard text-input control in Java GUI programming under the **Model-View-Controller (MVC)** architecture of Swing.

### Class Hierarchy (Verbatim from `java.lang.Object`)

$$
\text{java.lang.Object}
\;\rightarrow\;
\text{java.awt.Component}
\;\rightarrow\;
\text{java.awt.TextComponent}
\;\rightarrow\;
\text{javax.swing.JTextComponent}
\;\rightarrow\;
\text{javax.swing.JTextField}
$$

- **Package:** `javax.swing`
- **Direct Parent:** `javax.swing.JTextComponent`
- **Type:** Lightweight component (does not rely on a peer OS component)

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy — "The Single-Line Memo Slip"**

Imagine a **short memo slip** (like the one your professor hands you to write your name and roll number on an exam paper). You can write *only one line* on it. If you press Enter, the slip simply beeps (or in some cases, triggers a button). The slip has a fixed width (number of visible characters), can be set to allow only digits (like a phone number field), and can have placeholder text (e.g., "Enter your name here").

| Real-World Memo Slip | `JTextField` Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Slip's width | `setColumns(n)` |
| Label saying "Name:" | JLabel placed next to it |
| Only numbers allowed | `setDocument(new PlainDocument() { ... })` |
| Pressing Enter | `addActionListener(...)` |
| Read-only slip (printed) | `setEditable(false)` |

So, **JTextField = one editable horizontal text line** in a Swing-based GUI — used everywhere from login forms to calculator input boxes.

---

## 1.3 Key Characteristics Highlighted in KTU Syllabus

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Point:** JTextField supports:
> 1. **Single-line editing** only (multi-line is `JTextArea`)
> 2. **Action event** when the user presses **Enter**
> 3. **Horizontal text alignment** (LEFT, CENTER, RIGHT) via `setHorizontalAlignment(...)`
> 4. **Custom document filtering** for input validation
> 5. **Echo characters** (used for password fields via `JPasswordField`, a direct subclass)

The default model used by `JTextField` is `javax.swing.text.PlainDocument`, which is why typing in a JTextField produces plain, unformatted text by default.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Anatomy — How a JTextField Works Internally

The `JTextField` follows Swing's strict **Model-View-Controller (MVC)** design. Understanding these three roles is a recurring KTU exam question.

- **Model (`Document` / `PlainDocument`):** Stores the actual text content. The default model is a `PlainDocument` which holds a single line of plain text.
- **View (`FieldView`):** Responsible for rendering the characters on the screen and the caret position.
- **Controller (`DefaultCaret`, KeyMap):** Handles keyboard input, caret movement, and editing actions like cut/copy/paste.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this important for KTU?** Questions like *"Explain the MVC architecture of Swing text components"* appear frequently. JTextField is the simplest example to teach this concept.

---

## 2.2 Hierarchy of the Five KTU-High-Yield Constructors

`JTextField` provides **5 commonly used constructors** (you only need to memorize the most important ones for the exam):

$$
\text{Constructor Overload Table}
$$

| # | Constructor Signature | Purpose / When to Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `JTextField()` | Empty field; columns default to **0** (sized by layout) |
| 2 | `JTextField(int columns)` | Empty field with a preferred width of `columns` characters |
| 3 | `JTextField(String text)` | Pre-filled with `text`; columns = length of text |
| 4 | `JTextField(String text, int columns)` | Pre-filled with `text` AND preferred width of `columns` |
| 5 | `JTextField(Document doc, String text, int columns)` | Advanced: provides a custom Document model |

**Syntax pattern (most-used form):**

```java
JTextField tf = new JTextField("Default Text", 20);
```

---

## 2.3 High-Yield Inherited & Specialized Methods

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite — These methods appear in nearly every past paper:**

| Method | Description | Returns |
|---|---|---|
| `String getText()` | Retrieves current text from the model | String |
| `void setText(String t)` | Replaces the current text | void |
| `void setEditable(boolean b)` | `true` = editable; `false` = read-only | void |
| `boolean isEditable()` | Checks edit state | boolean |
| `int getColumns()` | Preferred width in characters | int |
| `void setColumns(int c)` | Sets preferred width | void |
| `void addActionListener(ActionListener l)` | Listens for the **Enter** key | void |
| `void setHorizontalAlignment(int align)` | `LEFT`, `CENTER`, `RIGHT` (from SwingConstants) | void |
| `void setFont(Font f)` | Sets the text font | void |

### Constants used with `setHorizontalAlignment()`

$$
\text{SwingConstants.LEFT} = 2,\quad
\text{SwingConstants.CENTER} = 0,\quad
\text{SwingConstants.RIGHT} = 4
$$

(You only need to know the symbolic names — the integer values are rarely asked.)

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

JTextField is the **fundamental input control** in virtually every desktop Java application:

- **Banking software:** account number, PIN entry.
- **Scientific calculators:** entering operands.
- **Hospital Management Systems:** patient ID, name.
- **Network Configuration Tools:** IP address, port number entry.
- **IDE-style tools:** search boxes, find/replace bars.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Real-world hook:** When asked *"Give a real-life example of a GUI application that uses JTextField"*, the answer should mention a **Login Form** with two JTextFields (username + password JPasswordField) and a JButton.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Code Derivations

> [!NOTE]
> Since JTextField is a programming construct, this section replaces mathematical derivations with **exhaustively written, line-by-line analytical code derivations** — exactly as required by the KTU 2024 Scheme for `PEECT414 - OOP`.

---

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Why each line of code is written

### Derivation #1: Creating the Simplest JTextField Inside a JFrame

**Question Pattern:** *"Write a Java program to create a JFrame containing a JTextField."*

```java
import javax.swing.*;                  // (1)
import java.awt.*;                     // (2)

public class TextFieldDemo extends JFrame {     // (3)
    JTextField textField;                       // (4)

    TextFieldDemo() {                           // (5)
        setTitle("JTextField Demo");            // (6)
        setSize(400, 150);                      // (7)
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());            // (8)
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE); // (9)

        textField = new JTextField(20);         // (10)
        add(textField);                         // (11)

        setVisible(true);                       // (12)
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {    // (13)
        new TextFieldDemo();                    // (14)
    }
}
```

### Step-by-step logical explanation (Anti-Skip Mandate)

- **(1)** Imports the `javax.swing` package — gives access to `JFrame`, `JTextField`, and `FlowLayout`.
- **(2)** Imports `java.awt` — required for `FlowLayout` (although `FlowLayout` is technically in `java.awt`).
- **(3)** Class declaration; we **inherit** from `JFrame` to make our class itself a window.
- **(4)** Field declaration of type `JTextField` — accessible by all methods.
- **(5)** Constructor — runs the GUI setup.
- **(6)** Sets the title bar text.
- **(7)** Window size in pixels: width = 400, height = 150.
- **(8)** Uses `FlowLayout` so components are added left-to-right, top-to-bottom.
- **(9)** Tells JVM to terminate the program when the window is closed (default is `HIDE_ON_CLOSE`).
- **(10)** Creates a JTextField with a preferred width of 20 characters.
- **(11)** Adds the JTextField to the JFrame's content pane.
- **(12)** Makes the JFrame visible — **must** come last.
- **(13)** Standard Java entry point.
- **(14)** Invokes the constructor, which displays the window.

---

### Derivation #2: Action Listener — The "Press Enter" Reaction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **3-mark classic** in KTU exams.

**Requirement:** When the user types text and presses **Enter**, display the text in a JLabel below.

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class EnterKeyDemo extends JFrame implements ActionListener {
    JTextField inputField;
    JLabel outputLabel;

    EnterKeyDemo() {
        setTitle("Enter-Key Listener");
        setSize(350, 120);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);

        inputField  = new JTextField(15);
        outputLabel = new JLabel("Type and press Enter...");

        inputField.addActionListener(this);   // (A) Critical line
        add(inputField);
        add(outputLabel);

        setVisible(true);
    }

    @Override
    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {  // (B) Critical line
        String text = inputField.getText();       // (C) Reads current text
        outputLabel.setText("You typed: " + text); // (D) Updates the label
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new EnterKeyDemo();
    }
}
```

#### Step-by-step logical explanation

- **Line (A):** `addActionListener(this)` — registers the current object as the listener. **`JTextField` fires an `ActionEvent` ONLY when the Enter key is pressed** (not on every keystroke).
- **Line (B):** The `actionPerformed` method is called automatically by the JVM's event dispatch thread when the event occurs.
- **Line (C):** `getText()` reads the current contents of the field as a `String`.
- **Line (D):** The label's text is updated to show what the user typed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the class declared `implements ActionListener`?** Because the class itself is the listener. We pass `this` to `addActionListener`. This is a standard Java idiom for small GUI programs.

---

### Derivation #3: Restricting Input to Digits Using a Custom Document

This is a **14-mark Part B** style question (advanced, but high-value).

```java
import javax.swing.text.*;

public class IntegerDocument extends PlainDocument {
    @Override
    public void insertString(int offs, String str, AttributeSet a)
            throws BadLocationException {
        if (str == null) return;                 // (i)  Safety check
        if (str.matches("[0-9]+")) {             // (ii) Regex test
            super.insertString(offs, str, a);     // (iii) Accept
        }
        // (iv) Else: silently reject the character
    }
}
```

**Usage:**

```java
JTextField numberField = new JTextField(10);
numberField.setDocument(new IntegerDocument());
```

#### Step-by-step logical explanation

- **(i)** A `null` string is sometimes passed by Swing internally — we must guard against it.
- **(ii)** `str.matches("[0-9]+")` returns `true` only if the entire string consists of one or more digits.
- **(iii)** If valid, we call the parent class's `insertString` to actually insert the text into the model.
- **(iv)** If invalid, we do **nothing** — the character is simply not added. (We could also call `Toolkit.getDefaultToolkit().beep()`.)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Hot Tip:** The regex `[0-9]+` is the standard way to accept only digits. Some model answers use a `for` loop with `Character.isDigit()`. Both are acceptable.

---

## 3.2 Key Constants and Their Meaning

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{JTextField.HORIZONTAL\_CENTER} &= \text{SwingConstants.CENTER} \\
\text{JTextField.HORIZONTAL\_LEFT}   &= \text{SwingConstants.LEFT}   \\
\text{JTextField.HORIZONTAL\_RIGHT}  &= \text{SwingConstants.RIGHT}
\end{aligned}
$$

These are inherited from `SwingConstants` and are passed directly to `setHorizontalAlignment(int)`.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Class Hierarchy Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[java lang Object] --> B[java awt Component]
    B --> C[java awt TextComponent]
    C --> D[javax swing JTextComponent]
    D --> E[javax swing JTextField]
    D --> F[javax swing JPasswordField]
    D --> G[javax swing JTextArea]
    E --> H[javax swing JFormattedTextField]
    F --> E

    style A fill:#1f3a5f,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#2c5282,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#2b6cb0,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#3182ce,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#e53e3e,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#dd6b20,color:#ffffff
    style G fill:#38a169,color:#ffffff
    style H fill:#805ad5,color:#ffffff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** `JTextField` inherits from `JTextComponent`. `JPasswordField` is a **direct subclass** of `JTextField` (specialized for hidden echo characters). `JFormattedTextField` is a direct subclass of `JTextField` (specialized for formatted input like dates and numbers).

---

## 4.2 Mermaid MVC Architecture Flow for JTextField

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph MODEL
        M[Document PlainDocument holds the actual text]
    end

    subgraph VIEW
        V[FieldView renders glyphs and caret on screen]
    end

    subgraph CONTROLLER
        C[DefaultCaret and KeyMap handle keyboard input]
    end

    U[User Types a Character] --> C
    C -->|mutates| M
    M -->|notifies| V
    V -->|repaints| U
    L[addActionListener] -.->|fires on Enter| E[ActionEvent to listener]
    E -.->|calls| H[actionPerformed method]

    style M fill:#3182ce,color:#ffffff
    style V fill:#38a169,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#dd6b20,color:#ffffff
    style U fill:#4a5568,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#e53e3e,color:#ffffff
    style H fill:#805ad5,color:#ffffff
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern Question:** *"Explain the MVC architecture of Swing text components with a neat diagram."* The diagram above is a model answer. Always label Model, View, and Controller as three distinct boxes.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Lifecycle Flow — From Creation to Event Handling

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Step 1 Import javax swing] --> S2[Step 2 Declare JTextField reference]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Instantiate with constructor]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Configure columns text alignment editability]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Register ActionListener if Enter handling needed]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 Add JTextField to container]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7 User types and presses Enter]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8 actionPerformed triggered]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9 getText retrieves value]
    S9 --> S10[Step 10 Application processes the value]

    style S1 fill:#2b6cb0,color:#ffffff
    style S3 fill:#3182ce,color:#ffffff
    style S5 fill:#dd6b20,color:#ffffff
    style S8 fill:#e53e3e,color:#ffffff
    style S10 fill:#38a169,color:#ffffff
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]
**"What is JTextField in Java Swing? Mention any two constructors."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **JTextField** is a lightweight Swing component in the `javax.swing` package used to accept a single line of text input from the user. It is a subclass of `JTextComponent` and follows the Model-View-Controller (MVC) architecture.
>
> **Two constructors:**
> 1. `JTextField(int columns)` — creates an empty field with the specified preferred width.
> 2. `JTextField(String text, int columns)` — creates a field initialized with the given text and preferred width.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]
**"Differentiate between `setText()` and `getText()` methods of JTextField."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | `setText(String s)` | `getText()` |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Writes text into the field | Reads text from the field |
| Return type | `void` | `String` |
| Direction | Model ← Program | Model → Program |
| Example | `tf.setText("Hello");` | `String s = tf.getText();` |

> `setText()` replaces the current contents; `getText()` retrieves whatever the user has typed (or what was set earlier).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question Choice A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Module 4 Pattern]

**(a)** Explain the class hierarchy of `JTextField` from `java.lang.Object` to `javax.swing.JTextField`. Mention its direct parent class. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java Swing program to create a JFrame containing a JLabel, a JTextField, and a JButton. When the button is clicked, display the contents of the JTextField inside the JLabel. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

The complete class hierarchy is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\texttt{java.lang.Object} \\
&\;\;\downarrow \\
&\texttt{java.awt.Component} \\
&\;\;\downarrow \\
&\texttt{java.awt.TextComponent} \\
&\;\;\downarrow \\
&\texttt{javax.swing.JTextComponent} \\
&\;\;\downarrow \\
&\texttt{javax.swing.JTextField}
\end{aligned}
$$

- **Direct Parent Class:** `javax.swing.JTextComponent`
- **Package:** `javax.swing`
- **Key interfaces:** implements `SwingConstants` (transitively) for alignment constants.
- **Sibling subclasses:** `JPasswordField`, `JFormattedTextField`.

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class ShowTextApp extends JFrame implements ActionListener {
    JLabel label;
    JTextField textField;
    JButton button;

    ShowTextApp() {
        setTitle("Display JTextField Content");
        setSize(400, 150);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout(FlowLayout.CENTER, 10, 10));
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);

        label     = new JLabel("Result will appear here");
        textField = new JTextField(20);
        button    = new JButton("Show");

        button.addActionListener(this);

        add(label);
        add(textField);
        add(button);

        setVisible(true);
    }

    @Override
    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        String input = textField.getText();
        label.setText("You entered: " + input);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new ShowTextApp();
    }
}
```

**Explanation:**
- The class implements `ActionListener` so it can respond to the button click.
- `button.addActionListener(this)` registers the class as the button's listener.
- In `actionPerformed`, `textField.getText()` fetches the current input.
- `label.setText(...)` updates the label dynamically.

---

### Question Choice B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Module 4 Pattern - Alternative]

**(a)** Explain the MVC architecture of Swing text components with reference to JTextField. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program to create a JTextField that only accepts numeric digits. Use a custom `PlainDocument` subclass to enforce this restriction. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

The **Model-View-Controller (MVC)** pattern in Swing separates the data, the display, and the input handling into three distinct roles:

- **Model (`Document` / `PlainDocument`):** Stores the text content. For `JTextField`, the default model is a `PlainDocument` that holds a single line of plain text. The model knows nothing about the screen.
- **View (`FieldView`):** Responsible for drawing the characters, the caret (text cursor), and selection highlights on the screen. It reads from the model but does not modify it.
- **Controller (`DefaultCaret` + `KeyMap`):** Intercepts keyboard and mouse events, translates them into model updates (insert / delete characters), and updates the caret position in the view.

**Flow when the user types a character:**

1. The user presses a key.
2. The **Controller** catches the `KeyEvent`.
3. The Controller calls `Document.insertString(...)` to mutate the **Model**.
4. The **Model** fires a `DocumentEvent`.
5. The **View** listens for this event and repaints the affected region of the screen.

**Advantage of MVC:** The same model can be displayed in multiple views (e.g., two JTextFields sharing one document — typing in one updates the other).

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import javax.swing.text.*;
import java.awt.*;

public class NumericFieldApp extends JFrame {
    NumericFieldApp() {
        setTitle("Numeric JTextField");
        setSize(300, 100);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);

        JTextField numericField = new JTextField(15);
        numericField.setDocument(new DigitOnlyDocument());

        add(new JLabel("Enter numbers only:"));
        add(numericField);

        setVisible(true);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new NumericFieldApp();
    }
}

class DigitOnlyDocument extends PlainDocument {
    @Override
    public void insertString(int offset, String str, AttributeSet a)
            throws BadLocationException {
        if (str == null) return;
        if (str.matches("[0-9]+")) {
            super.insertString(offset, str, a);
        }
    }
}
```

**Explanation:**
- We extend `PlainDocument` and override `insertString`.
- The regex `[0-9]+` ensures that only one or more digits are inserted.
- Any non-digit keystroke is silently rejected — the field behaves like a numeric input box.
- This is the **standard, recommended** KTU-accepted approach for input filtering.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students typically lose marks on JTextField questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting `setVisible(true)`** — the window will not appear, and the examiner will deduct 1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting `setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE)`** — the program will not terminate on closing the window. Deduct ½ mark.
> 3. **Confusing `ActionEvent` (JTextField) with `KeyEvent` (JTextArea).** JTextField only fires an `ActionEvent` on **Enter**, not on every keystroke. Mixing these up costs 2-3 marks.
> 4. **Mixing up `setEditable(false)` with `setEnabled(false)`.** `setEditable(false)` greys the text slightly and allows selection/copy; `setEnabled(false)` makes the field completely inactive.
> 5. **Not importing `java.awt.event.*`** when using listeners — a common compilation-error trap. Worth 1 mark.
> 6. **In MVC questions**, students often confuse the View with the Controller. Remember: **View = rendering, Controller = event handling.**
> 7. **In numeric-filter questions**, failing to handle the `null` case in `insertString` can crash the program during pasting.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — JTextField**

- **Definition:** Single-line text input component in `javax.swing`; subclass of `JTextComponent`.
- **Default Model:** `javax.swing.text.PlainDocument`.
- **Key Constructors:** `JTextField()`, `JTextField(int)`, `JTextField(String)`, `JTextField(String, int)`, `JTextField(Document, String, int)`.
- **Most-used Methods:** `getText()`, `setText(String)`, `setEditable(boolean)`, `setColumns(int)`, `addActionListener(...)`, `setHorizontalAlignment(int)`.
- **ActionEvent fires only on Enter key press** — not on every keystroke.
- **Alignment constants** come from `SwingConstants`: `LEFT`, `CENTER`, `RIGHT`.
- **Custom filtering** is done by subclassing `PlainDocument` and overriding `insertString`.
- **Subclasses of JTextField:** `JPasswordField` (hides input with echo char), `JFormattedTextField` (formatted input like dates).
- **Sibling (not subclass):** `JTextArea` — for **multi-line** input.
- **MVC Role:** Model = `Document`, View = `FieldView`, Controller = `DefaultCaret` + `KeyMap`.
- **Lightweight component** — does not depend on a native OS peer, hence consistent rendering across platforms.
- **Inherits from** `TextComponent` — therefore inherits methods like `getText()`, `setText()`, `setEditable()` from the AWT side as well.
- **Placement in a container** must follow proper layout management (`FlowLayout`, `BorderLayout`, `GridLayout`, `GridBagLayout`).
- **For numeric input validation**, regex `[0-9]+` is the cleanest KTU-accepted method.
- **Exam mantra:** *"JTextField = one line, Enter triggers action, MVC separates concerns, PlainDocument filters input."*

---

**End of Module 4 — JTextField Notes | KTU 2024 Scheme | PEECT414**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Java Database Connectivity (JDBC) - JDBC Overview
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT414 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Java Database Connectivity (JDBC) - JDBC Overview

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **JDBC (Java Database Connectivity)** is a **Java API (Application Programming Interface)** defined in the `java.sql` and `javax.sql` packages that enables Java applications to interact with relational databases. It provides a standard, database-independent interface for executing SQL statements and retrieving results.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PEECT414 - Module 4)**, JDBC is the standard Java specification that provides:

- A **call-level API** for SQL-based database access.
